But my lads, it was right to put in federal officers last year, and the crew knew it; they saw what was brewing well enough, and by the soul of me, if the federal men hadn't done what they did, Old Davy Jones would have had his clutches into our quarters long ago. And now who d'ye think they want to put in instead of our present Commander C. GORE, and the Chief Mate COBB, an old weather-beaten Tar, and as stout a heart as ever broke bread—why I'll tell ye, d'ye see, one Gerry, who is too old and stiff to keep his legs in a calm, and in a gale of wind would want half of the crew to shore him up—aye, and one Billy Gray, as he's called, who to be sure, has been a clerk aboard some time, but never since his mother trotted him had any command, and by the powers never ought to have, for you all know, in squally weather, he'd skulk under the long boat, or turn in, and for the life of him daren't go aloft in a heavy sea to help reef a sail. Pretty business this! turn out old experienced officers to make way for them that can't rig a cat-tackle to get an anchor on the bow. No, it won't do. Faith my lads, we must keep a sharp look-out, and put a stopper on these fellows directly.

Excerpt of a letter to the Editor, dated, City of Washington, Nov. 8. “At present there is nothing very important before Congress, except the President’s communication respecting a probability of war between the United States and the King of Morocco. "It is now known that the Spanish Minister, residing in this city, did, previous to the convening of Congress, remonstrate with our government against the ratification of the treaty ceding to the United States the Louisiana country; why this information was withheld from Congress, at the time it was the subject of debate before them, may, I presume, be easily conjectured. It is generally believed that the Spaniards will not peaceably relinquish possession of Louisiana; if that shall turn out to be the fact, which by the by, is more than probable, it will most assuredly involve us advocates in difficulties, that by them was not heretofore contemplated. "The Senate has not yet acted upon the resolution contemplating a change in the Constitution. My own opinion is, that doubts have arisen in that body whether a constitutional majority can be had upon that subject, admitting it should be called up; one thing is certain, that one democrat (a senator) has declared himself against any alteration whatever.” Yours, etc. A Word to the Wise.

Mr. Shaw, agreeably to his notice of yesterday, offered a bill "providing for, and authorizing the call of a convention to draw up a constitution, preparatory to the admission of this territory into the union as an independent state." November 26. Call of a Convention —A bill is now before the house of representatives of this territory, for the call of a convention, to form a state constitution. The convention is to meet on the 4th day of July next, and proceed to the adoption of a constitution, which is to be presented to the next congress, and application to be made for admitting into the union. Hostile as we are to territorial government, we cannot view this step in our legilative.

Another Federal passenger.—On board the Prexage, which sailed from this port on Tuesday last for St. Salvador, went a passenger, Mr. E. Magruder, a “staunch Federalist.” Mr. Magruder is the bearer of dispatches from our government to the Portuguese government at the Brazils. For the honor of the government, we state that Mr. Magruder was favored with every information from the office of state, which he could desire to facilitate the mercantile speculations in which he was engaged. To save brother Colvin the trouble of explaining what he himself may not understand, we also state that Mr. Magruder was bound to the Brazils on his own business, and offered his services to carry any letters the government might wish to forward to that country. We hope the exclusive friends of the administration will not be offended because a good democrat was not appointed to this office with monthly wages and “additional rations” for the service. DUEL! On Saturday the 1st of March next at seven o'clock in the evening, at the Merchant's Coffee-House in the city of Philadelphia agreeable to the directions of the last will and testament of General THOMAS MIFFLIN, deceased, will be exposed to Public Sale and positively sold to the highest Bidder—that beautiful and elegant COUNTRY SEAT, situated at the Falls of Schuylkill, five miles from Philadelphia, and about two from Germantown; consisting of a large and commodious House finished in the modern Style, with a Barn, Stables and Out-houses, twenty-two acres of highly improved Meadow land; an Orchard of the best grafted Fruit Trees, and a Garden of Fruit and Vegetables in an excellent exposure. The premises (particularly the Garden, in which there are two Fountains) are watered with a never failing stream, and all the Buildings and Fences are in perfect order. THE HOUSE Is seated on an elevated hill, surrounded with willow and other ornamental Trees of the first growth, and commands an extensive view of the Falls and Meadows of the Schuylkill. Suppliers of Marketing of every description are constantly passing on the road in front of the house, and Fish may be obtained in the greatest abundance during the season, and at all times preserved in a Fish-Pond, on the Premises. The terms will be made known at the time of sale.

The state of our finances continues to fulfill our expectations. Eleven milli ons and a half of dollars, received in the course of the year ending on the 30th of September last, have enabled us, after meeting all the ordinary expenses of the year, to pay upwards of three milli ons six hundred thousand dollars of the public debt, exclusive of interest. This payment, with those of the two prece ding years has extinguished upwards of twelve millions of principal, and a grea ter sum of interest, within that period, and, by a proportionate diminution of interest renders always sensible the ef fect of the growing sum yearly applica ble to the discharge of the principal. It is also ascertained that the reve nue accrued during the last year, ex ceeds that of the preceding, and the probable receipts of the ensuing year, may safely be relied on as sufficient, with the sum already in the Treasury, to meet all the current demands of the year, to discharge upwards of three millions and a half of the engagements incurred under the British and French conventions, and to advance in the fur ther redemption of the funded debt. As citizens are the princi rich, I have thought it time to communicate for your consideration and attention.  Some others will be laid before you in the course of the Session.

This usurper it seems was a governor under the rul ing Bashaw, and his own brother, and had been prevailed upon to let up pretexts to the Throne, in which pious pro ject he was to be encouraged by the United States as far as might contribute to their success. It now appears that he has indeed contributed materially to their success, and we shall soon see how far the better of the United States has gone in procuring stipulations in his favor from his brother, or whether any other ar rangement has been made in his favor, which might be more acceptable to him, and if he has not been played in this “honorable peace” of the Devil takes the hindmost. NEW-YORK, September 5. IMPORTANT. We are indebted to the polite attention of Mr. Craig for the following important information, received yesterday by letter, from Mr. Harford, of Darien, Georgia. It reached this place, that an English fleet of nine sail of the line, with frigates and transports, are at anchor in the mouth of St. John’s river, and that their object is the conquest of Florida. The Spaniards are extremely alarmed, and all is bustle and confusion. Should this account be correct, and there is every reason to believe it is, the Flori das will probably soon exchange masters.

Immediately after his arrival, the Admiral demanded of the Portuguese Governor a surrender of the place, which was peremptorily refused. Nothing further respecting it than negotiations took place till the 20th, when about 300 soldiers, with 2 or 3 pieces of artillery, were landed and put in possession of one of the forts. Previous to the landing of the troops, Admiral Drury had made every preparation of the boats and men belonging to his squadron for taking the town by assault. To prevent this, the Governor consented that there should be no opposition to the landing of the troops, and that they might garrison the forts, upon which, however, they were not permitted to hoist English colors. The Chinese now interfered and ordered Admiral Drury to leave the place, alleging that he had made an encroachment upon the territory of the Empire which could not be submitted to. He persisted in keeping his troops on shore and declared his determination to hold the town at all hazards. The Vice-Roy of the province issued an order on the 25th of September, forbidding the landing of any cargoes from, and sending any on board the English ships, of which there were between 40 and 50 lying at Wampoa. An entire suspension of trade ensued and continued for 3 months, during which time the Admiral visited Canton, attended by all the boats and about 2000 men from the ships.

Several years have elapsed since it was first brought into view. It originated in the east; it has been echoed from the south, and the people of the west have also calling for its adoption. It has been advocated in turns by men of all political parties. A federal member from the federal state of New-Hampshire is I recollect right, first introduced it into Congress. A proclamation of the same in substance was afterwards renewed by a federal member from the then federal state of South Carolina. In the two last instances it has been brought forward by republican members from republican states, first by one of my colleagues from Pennsylvania, a sister republican state, and during the present session by a gentleman from Virginia, which is said to be also republican. New York, Vermont, Tennessee, Ohio, and I believe Kentucky, have, by their legislatures, expressed their approbation of the measure and invited its adoption. Federalists and republicans in every part of the country, thus uniting in recommending the measure, is conclusive evidence, What a majority of the people, however they may differ on other political questions, are in favor of the leading principle contained in the amendment. This circumstance led me to conclude, that it would have received but little support in this House. The members from those states that had been most forward in recommending it, I did expect would have given it their unanimous support.

On motion, the bill supplementary to the more speedy recovery of small debts was read a third time by paragraphs, Had the question been taken, and the house equally divided, it was decided in the affir- mative by the speaker—and the bill passed the Senate. Tammany's 1,0 ¢ That day will be peculiarly favorable to public festivity, it will be at the most cheerful period of the year, when nature herself seems to rejoice—the forests begin to shew their gay attire—the winged tribes enliven the spray by their songs—and the face of nature appears in gladness and gaiety. ¢ The day will be remote, alike from the severity of the winter cold and summer heat. The day will not interfere with the labor of the cultivator or the reaper—ploughing and sowing will have past, and reaping and mowing will not yet have commenced. At that season the various members of our several governments will be at their several homes—our Senators and Members of Congress and Members of legislature—information will be diffused, and so large and respectable a portion of our fellow citizens separated both by winter from their families upon the public service, will be enabled to partake of the general joy in the circle of society to which they particularly are attached. It is impossible to ascertain the quantity of lands granted, without calling out the claimants to exhibit their titles; the registry being incomplete and the maps made by the different surveyors general having been burnt in fires at New-Orleans of 1783 and 1794. No estimate has been obtained. All the lands on both sides of the Mississippi, from the distance of sixteen leagues below New-Orleans to Baton Rouge, are granted to the depth of forty acres, or near half a league, which is the usual depth of all grants. Some have double and triple grants—that is to say, they have twice or thrice forty acres in depth; and others have grants extending from the Mississippi to the shores of the lakes behind them. In other parts of the country, the people, being generally settled on the banks of creeks or rivers, have a front of from sixty to forty acres, and the grant almost invariably expresses a depth of forty acres. All the lands ungranted in the island of New-Orleans or on the opposite bank of the Mississippi, are sunken, inundated, and at present unfit for cultivation; but may, in part, be reclaimed at a future day by the efforts of the rich and enterprising. Cultivation of Sugar. The sugar cane may be cultivated between the river Iberville and the city, on both sides of the river, and as far back as the swamps.

The rise of the waters has been so long, that it has surpassed by four feet the design inundation of 1755. The aqueduct of Craponne, erected twenty-six feet above the level of the earth, is only two feet higher than the waters. Two quarters of our city are under water: several houses have fallen, and have buried under them merchandise, furniture, provisions, and everything that the violence or suddenness of the inundation did not permit us to snatch from their fury. THE CONCORDAT. It is said that the Concordat is forthwith to be published, by which the future system of the French Church is to be regulated. We hear that his Holiness the Pope gives Bonaparte the title of Le Consul et Chretien. The Concordat limits the number of Archbishops for the whole Republic to fifty. It passes an act of oblivion on all the errors and deviations from the discipline of the church which happened during the revolution; but the priests who have married, though they are not to be dispossessed of their benefices, are not to exercise the clerical functions. The ceremonies, the fees, processions, and holy days of the church, the regulations about diet, etc. are to be abridged and relaxed—and certain fixed revenues, independent of tithes, are to be attached to each see and parish.

- OPERATIONS AGAINST TRIPOLI - A letter from Malta, dated April 16, 1805, says, "Our preparations for another expedition against Tripoli, are making with great activity; and probably the operation will commence 1 June. A vessel has been dispatched to Egypt, with stores, to the assistance of the Bashaw, who says he has a legal right to the rule of Tripoli. - Mr. E. is there, to assist him to regain his rights; and to attack his brother Lys land, while the American squadron bombards it from the sea.  - FROM NEWBURYPORT, JULY 3, - We learn by Mr. Harwood, supercargo of the ship Hannah, from Trieste and Gibraltar, that the U. S. schooner Enterprise, capt. Robertson, has been entirely rebuilt at Venice, and would be ready to sail for Syracuse the 10th May. The United States frigate Boston (the Constitution being in convoy) captured a Tripolitan cruiser and also took two Neapolitan vessels, her prizes, and carried them into Malta. The Hannah brought dispatches for govern ment from our Mediterranean squadron. - American Silk.— In the year 1801, there were raised in the town of Mansfield, Con.

No public examinations had taken place from fear, as was stated, of alarming the armies; but the prisoners underwent daily interrogatories in the temple and other revolutionary bastiles, before Fouche, and the confidential members of the secret police of the French emperor. Strict search has been made after the duke of Angouleme, the presumptive heir of the French Bourbons, married to the daughter of Louis XVI. who was supposed to be concealed at Paris. This prince was however, safe in England with his uncle Louis XVIII. Ferdinand VII. and his brother, were reported to be confined in the state prison of Vincennes, now called Fort Vincennes, near Paris, where the duke of Enghien was murdered in 1804. Bonaparte’s journey to Germany is stated to have been retarded by these occurrences; and before he quitted his capital, he deposited his will in the senate, which the grand officers of state, as well as the members of the senate swore, in case of any accident to him, punctually to execute. A postscript to a letter from St. Bartholomews, dated the 28th of April, contains the important news which follows: "I open my letter to say, that by a vessel yesterday from Liverpool, we have news to the 28th inst."

A gentleman arrived in town last evening from New York, (via Newport) informs that the British Packet arrived there on Saturday last and brings intelligence of the defeat of the Austrian army in a battle fought with the French, under Gen. Moreau, [a transcript, we presume, of the above-mentioned action.] The London dates, brought by the Packet, we are informed, are to Dec. 15, and in speaking of the internal state of the kingdom, they lament the continued rise of provisions of every kind. By the Governor Strong, arrived yesterday, we have received Paris papers to the 19 Frimaire, (December 19, 1800.) The "Journal du-Commerce," says, "The American ship Polly Cary, captured by a Cherbourg privateer, had been tried in the Council of prizes, the 1st of December, and released without damage and interest, conformably to the Treaty concluded on the 8th Vendemaire, an. 9 (24th September, 1800) between the French Republic and the United States of America. Accounts from the West-Indies via Norfolk, state that Commodore Barry has ordered all the American cruisers in; and that the British had declared Guadaloupe in a state of siege. The federal party appear like an army that has sustained. They are all in confusion. They have not yet received their orders from the commander in chief, and are ignorant. This was the monster until before he was chosen. The people must also hear the. They know not. Some of. a defeat. for. J. want of the plan intended to be pursued. We shall patiently wait until they are organized, and will then endeavor to explain the plan of their operations. At present, they are without order, discipline or method; and may literally be said to be upon their beam ends. Boston Chronicle. JOHN ADAMS. The very important disclosures made by this gentleman in a series of letters lately published in the Boston Patriot have justly drawn forth general attention, admiration and applause. He unravels many points and traits of his administration; the intrigues of Hamilton, Pickering, and the rest of the British partisans in his cabinet; their endeavors to plunge us into war with France, and draw us into an alliance with England, his resistance, their disappointment, and the subsequent attack made upon him by Alexander Hamilton—these and other schemes he unfolds in the most forcible, sprightly and candid manner. The ascendency of the British faction had cast Mr. Adams into the shade of unpopularity. The alien and sedition bills drew him into odium.

There is a minute account of this event in a letter of Wallis, who makes use of a geometrical figure to illustrate the method of reasoning, and says, "But the true motion of each part of the earth's surface being compounded of these two motions, the Annual and Diurnal and in page 123 of the same book, Dr. Wallis applies a similar theory to the trade winds as well as to the tides. In the English Gentleman's Diary for 1748 we find a memoir by Mr. Wallis on the cycloidal path and unequal motion of a point on the surface of the earth arising from the annual and diurnal motions compounded together. Having thus shown that Mr. Wallis's principle for explaining the causes of the tides, trade winds, etc. has been long known, I beg leave to subjoin the following observations. In the first place, Mr. Wallis seems to me to be fairly entitled to the merit of having invented his system, notwithstanding its having been familiarly known long before; this will readily be admitted by such as are acquainted with his book, and his various essays on this subject; and as his principle has been admitted by those Eminent Philosophers and Mathematicians Galileo and Dr. Wallis, he deserves, in my opinion, the praise of ingenuity.

...ration of the French and Dutch colonies,  It appears from an article dated Breda, that four ships belonging to Gantheaume's squadron have returned to Toulon. By this it should appear that the attempt to relieve Alexandria is given up. The embargo has not yet been taken off the Swedish vessels in our ports. The Danes, who were shut up in Greenland dock, are preparing for sea, but some of them, it is reported, are unable to sail for want of hands, several during the embargo having entered on board different ships in the river. The movements of the French troops occasion considerable sensations in the empire. According to an article from the banks of the Main, the chief seat of war is reported to have passed from Ulm to Neustadt, and they have taken possession of Cassel and Ehrenbreitstein on the Rhine, opposite Cologne. A letter received from Antibes announces the surrender of Formio, and that Gantheaume had captured two English frigates. The subscription at Lloyd's for the widows and orphans of the men who perished in the Queen Charlotte, amounted to £9,000 on Saturday evening. Mr. Erskine just concluded a speech of two hours in length, by observing, that the question the Jury had to consider, was, whether the libel was written or published by the defendant, and if so, whether it was written for the purpose, and with the intention ascribed to it; and if they were of that opinion, they were bound to give a verdict of damages commensurate with the injury, and such as would furnish a wholesome lesson to the defendant for his future conduct in life. Evidence was then produced that Mr. Cobbett was the sole proprietor of the Political Register. Several witnesses were then called in order to explain the innuendos; these witnesses were Mr. Wickham, Mr. Barnett, and Mr. Foster, Speaker of the late Irish House of Commons. These gentlemen, on being shown the libels, agreed in thinking the innuendoes applied to Mr. Plunkett. Mr. Adam then rose and addressed the Court and Jury for the defendant. He began by stating that there were several particulars in the speech of his learned friend, of which it was his duty to complain. His learned friend had made very free allusions to the proceedings which had taken place on the former day, and it appeared, his principal efforts were di-

Mr. Morrow from the committee on public lands, reported unfavorably to the claim of certain officers and soldiers in the British war against France in 1763 for lands, which was read and accepted by the house without opposition. The bill for supplying the deficiency in the appropriation of 1810 for distressed seamen, etc. and the bill for continuing the Mediterranean fund, were read a third time & passed. The house went into a committee of the whole, Mr. Pitkin in the chair, on the bill authorizing the secretary of war, by the appointment of commissioners to ascertain the exterior line of the public lands at West Point, etc. The bill was so amended as to leave the ultimate decision on the report of the commissioners to congress. The committee reported the bill as amended, which was ordered to be engrossed for a third reading. The house went into a committee of the whole, Mr. Pitkin in the chair, on the bill providing for the final adjustment of claims to lands, and for the sale of the public lands in the territories of Orleans and Indiana. After some inconsiderable amendments to the bill, the committee rose and reported the bill, which was ordered to lie on.

The corrected paragraph: "A sum of fifty dollars reward will be given for apprehending and securing, in any jail, on delivery to the commanding officer of any military post in the United States; a DESERTER from the Marine corps, to wit: Jethro Whitman, who absconded on the 14th inst. from the Marine Barracks at Washington, taking with him a small bay mare, about 15 hands high, with a blaze face, bob tail, and hog matted. Whitman is a handsome, fair complexioned young man, about 20 years of age, 5 feet 8 inches high, a singer and French horn player. Having been sent by the officers to bank with checks, he is full of money and has carried but few clothes with him; his dress was white nankeen pantaloons and round white striped cotton jacket, vest unknown. He is a talkative, boasting fellow, with much ignorance and great pride. The above reward will be given for securing, and reasonable charges paid for delivering him at Headquarters, Washington City. N. B. He was a stage driver between Albany and Schenectady, and his parents live below Schenectady. E. S. LANE, Lt. Marines. June 18. C.P.S.T. LANDING: six]! Merchants' Wharf. Gentleman would hardly fail to correct a small representation, where his interest was concerned; is therefore incumbent on his friends to do it for him. The views which influence Mr. Waite on this occasion, are not those of emolument, but those of safety and health. His present employment exposes him to fatigue, and the rigours of the weather, more than is consistent with the feebleness of his constitution. Hence he is often indisposed; is in danger of suffering greater inconvenience from this cause as he advances in years. Every one therefore, who knows his worth will be desirous of removing him to a situation, where his health and life will be preserved from the evils incident to the business in which he is now engaged. His qualifications are not only with faithfulness, but with great popularity, the office which is soon to be disposed of, no one will doubt, who has had any opportunity to observe the fidelity of his character, and the amiableness of his manners. I hope, by means of the extensive circulation of your paper, to prevent the mistake under which some have labored from operating to the disadvantage of a man who deserves every encouragement from a generous and discerning public. I am, sir, yours, &c.

The lands thus subdivided (excluding the sections reserved by the above-mentioned act) shall be offered for sale in sections and half sections, sub-divided as before directed at the following places and times, that is to say; those below the Little Miami shall be offered at public vendue, in the town of Cincinnati, on the first Monday of April one thousand eight hundred and one, under the direction of the Register of the land-office there established, and of either the Governor or Secretary of the north-western territory. The lands east of Scioto, south of the military lands, and west of the fifteen range of townships, shall be offered in like manner for sale at Chilicothe, on the first Monday of May, one thousand eight hundred and one, under the direction of the Register of the land office there established, and of either the Governor or Secretary of the said territory. The lands east of the sixteenth range of townships, south of the military lands and west of the Muskingum, including all the townships intersected by that river, shall be offered for sale in like manner at on the last Monday of May, one thousand eight hundred and one, under the direction of the Governor or Secretary, or Surveyor- General of the said territory. The sales shall remain open at each place for three weeks, and no longer.

This statement demonstrates that the same democratic party ran the same Elbridge Gerry four years prior to the year 1804, and until they ran him down. They have now vamped him over again, by telling you how young he is, and at the same breath, say he graduated at Harvard University forty-eight years since; that he opposed the Federal Constitution, but that when it was adopted, he (because he could not help it) agreed to support it, in order to obtain, if possible, some of the loaves and fishes; this same Mr. Gerry is known to be haughty and domineering—intimate with few, and domesticates with none. Another Argus Lie. The last Argus states boldly, that “during Gore’s reign of one year, about 800 Commissions have been turned out of his political oven, for Justices of the Peace.” About 800! No— the truth is, that a large number have been turned out of the oven, but more than three quarters of the commissions issued in the course of the last year, were merely renewals of old Commissions—to Federalists and Democrats indiscriminately; and that is what the Jacobin administration would not do. The above facts will appear by the Council Records. TRUTH. Portland, Monday, March 26, 1810. Town Officers. Federalists of Portland, to your duty!

This government is certainly more active than they have been, but not so much as they ought to be; however, the period is fast approaching when the Cortes are to be assembled, on the 1st of March. I give you extracts of a letter from Mr. Bradley, who is fixed at Seville in a store for the sale of goods and produce. Seville, 30th Dec. "As to political affairs, we are here quite in the dark. All we know is there seems no immediate dread; and the French are retreating from such of their advanced positions as they cannot support when the rains set in. In March we shall have a struggle; if danger is expected, it is in that month and April, particularly the last. But then the arch-demon must lead at least 150,000 more Frenchmen, Germans and Poles and Italians will not do, and he has given all promise (which he dares not break, for on them principally rests his power) that they shall not be called into Spain! Per interim we have a good cause and a protecting Providence." Jakvabt 8th.

Ladies and gentlemen, I acknowledge, said he, Mr. Chairman, the truth of all the facts stated by the gentleman who has just spoken. I acknowledge that Spain has insulted and abused us; broken her treaties and violated our territory. This is manifest from the documents before us. But, I am opposed to the adoption of the report. My judgment, sir, is not to be misled. I trust the judgment of no member will be misled by vaporings about honor and spirit. Let us know how the national account stands. Who will be debtor and who creditor is the question that ought first to be ascertained. (Here certain estimates of the secretary of war were read from which it appeared that to carry the resolution under consideration into effect, supposing the blank number of troops to be filled up with 10,000, would require an appropriation of a few hundred dollars more than it was supposed would be necessary to purchase the Florida.) Mr. G. in continuation. Then, sir, I am against war measures. The proverbial economy of our ancestors long since taught that a penny saved was a penny gained, and, in the instance before us, a few hundred dollars saved will be a few hundred dollars gained. This is the motive that will sway my vote. Among individuals, money is, emphatically, called the main chance. I see not why, among nations, it should.

NEW-YORK, Attest ... Capt. Ely, who arrived here last evening from Amsterdam, informs, that admiral Verhuel, commander of the united Batavian and French fleet, sailed from Flushing about the beginning of June with a squadron of about 60 gunboats, some brigs and schooners for Offend—. that on his way, he fell in with a division of British ships, late under command of Sir Sidney Smith; and after a severe engagement beat them off and got safe into Offend with the loss of only three boats, after which Admiral V. returned to Flushing, where he received 21 swords mounted with gold and many handsome compliments for his bravery. The Emperor promoted him to the rank of Vice-Admiral, etc. The British lost a cutter, and one of their frigates lost two of her masts. Captain Ely further informs, that the Dutch would not suffer any American vessels to enter New Dieppe; but it was supposed this prohibition would soon be removed. We have received Dutch papers to the 23rd June, but they are barren of news. One of which, however mentions, that Talleyrand was appointed Arch-Chancellor of France. PARIS, June 1. In Virginia, Sir, the judges of the Supreme Court, in 1792, declared that the assembly of that State had not the power of imposing chancery duties on the district judge, and in delivering their opinions, descanted at large on the independence of the judiciary, and said that the assembly could not annihilate the office of a judge, which was secured to him by the constitution. If this is a true exposition of the constitution of that I ask gentlemen by what authority they now attempt to impose a different meaning on the same words, when found in the constitution of the United States? Are we to suppose that the whole people of America were less regardful of their rights, less solicitous for independent judges, than the people of a particular State? And unless this is conceded, the doctrine of gentlemen who advocate the passage of this bill must be incorrect. But it has been said that the powers of each congress are equal, and that a subsequent legislature can repeal the acts of a former. And as this law was passed by the last congress, we have the same power to repeal it which they had to enact it. This objection is more plausible than solid. It is not contended by us that legislatures who are not limited in their powers have the same authority. The question is not.

Among the strange inconsistencies of the leaders of the democratic party, none appear more contradictory than their conduct towards Great Britain. They are constantly making the same declaration towards that country that Cato did of Carthage, that “it must be destroyed yet, they are perfectly ready and willing to plunge the country in all the miseries of a war, for the patriotic purpose of affording protection to British subjects. How this conduct can be reconciled we know not unless it is to be qualified, because it promotes the views of republican Bonaparte. When the general government was administered by federalists, the democratic presses were constantly prating about the “sovereignty of the people,” the “voice of the people,” etc. How much the practice of these “friends of the people” when in office, accords with their preaching may be seen by the reception the late petition of the merchants in Philadelphia met with in the House of the representatives. Our readers will find this "disrespectful" memorial, so offensive to the Kennebec Sobs, on the first page of this paper. Mr. Randolph’s remarks on the question are pertinent and just. It will probably be that the ratification of Spain returned back from Madrid, which with the ratifications of France and Holland, may be expected here about the end of the week. In about ten days after Lord Whitworth will set out for Paris. In announcing that the final arrangement of general peace is so near at hand, it is with much pain we give to the public any information tending to damp their joy. We received private advices from the Continent yesterday, which, in addition to the contents of the public papers, leave very little doubt that the flames of war just extinguished in the West of Europe, are on the eve of being rekindled in the East. We are assured the event which every one has so plainly foreseen is on the point of taking place; that the PARTITION of the TURKISH EMPIRE is agreed upon, and that Bonaparte has succeeded in persuading Russia, Austria and Prussia, tempted by the prospect of aggrandizement, to enter into his views. It is possible that Mr. Basset, the messenger, brought official intelligence of this to our Minister a few days ago. We understand Russia and Austria will immediately invade the Turkish dominions. This intelligence comes by a private channel; but it rests on good authority, and is strongly.

The Epistle from the Friends' yearly meeting, signed "William Alexander," is, as usual, fraught with the purest spirit of true Christian morality; for which they, with the other classes of the Christian Church, will be bitterly arraigned by our Jacobins. It must be pleasing to our worthy President and to our southern brethren to learn that should danger increase, and their country call, the True Federal Blues of America, will again seize their muskets to defend their country—already do we see the uniforms preparing, and they say one to the other, "prepare, for our country ere long will invoke your aid." Duane says that fifty persons who were under arms in the Legion on Monday had a share in the battle of Germantown—but he doesn't say a word about whose army they were in; we know that many of them are recently from Britain and Ireland. "The End shall justify the Means," is a maxim of long standing, and universally practiced by the Jacobins. It was this favorite axiom that urged the charge of Deism against Mr. Ross, prior to the last election for Pennsylvania. The basest means were then used by the Demos and the end was obtained by the election of M'Kean.

Grotius took refuge in France, and was accused by some of his countrymen of meditating to change his religion and become a Catholic. "A man," replied he to one of his friends who had written to him on the subject, "whatever ad vantage there may be to quit a weaker party that oppresses me, to go over to a stronger one that would receive me with open arms, I trust that I shall never be tempted to do so. And since," added he, "I have had courage enough to bear up under imprisonment, I trust that I shall not be in want of it to enable me to support poverty and banishment." Louis XIII. gave Grotius a very con siderable pension. He was, however, no favorite with his Minister, Cardinal de Richelieu, whom it is said he did not sufficiently flatter for his literary talents, and the pension was soon stopped. Grotius, however, met with a protectress in Christina, Queen of Swe- den, who made him her Ambassador at Paris. Here again he was harassed by Richelieu, who was angry with him for not giving him that precedence as a Prince of the Church to which Gr.

The right to create and abolish inferior courts has heretofore been acknowledged by the legislatures of New-Hampshire, Massachusetts, Pennsylvania, Maryland and Virginia, who have similar constitutions; and no legislature ever adopted a contrary construction. As to the policy of the measure, the only questions are, whether the six judges will be able to perform the service; and whether an annual session of the supreme court is sufficient. To decide upon the ability of the judges to perform the service, let us consider the labors of one judge—judge Cushing; for instance.~The law requires of him to hold annually, in conjunction with the district judges, two sessions of a circuit court in each of the states of New-Hampshire, Massachusetts and Rhode Island.—Ten days may fairly be computed the full length of a session. —The courts will then require sixty days,—His travel in going to and returning from those courts cannot exceed 480 miles, which at one day for each 30 miles amounts to 24 days more.—A session of the supreme court will not require more than 21 days. His residence does not exceed 480 miles distance from the seat of government.—At the above moderate rate of traveling, there shall be added 48 days more, making the whole time while an expense and in public service, 264 p.

Whereas by an act of the State of New Hampshire, passed on the 19th day of June last, a tax of one cent per acre on all the lands in the township of Durham, public lands excepted, is ordered to be raised and laid out on the roads and bridges leading through said town, and the subscribers, being appointed a committee by said act, to assess, collect and appropriate the said tax, hereby give notice to the owners of said lands that according to the said act, they have liberty to work out their respective taxes of one cent per acre, at the rate of two-thirds of a dollar per day for an able-bodied man finding his own tools and diet, and for ox work at the rate of half a dollar per day for each yoke of oxen, provided said labor shall be performed before the first day of October next, and that by application to Stephen Jillson of said Durham, one of said committee, they may work out their said tax in the manner above mentioned, or otherwise to pay their said tax to the subscribers, or either of them. Signed, OBED HALL, JAMES BRIGHT, STEPHEN JILLSON. February 24, 1803.

Mr. Newton submitted the following resolution:— Resolved, That the president of the United States be requested to cause to be laid before this house, by the proper department, a return of the army of the United States, specifying the particular force of each regiment or corps, with the designation of the principal officers commanding the same, and particularly the condition of that detachment of the regular force allotted for the defence of New Orleans, the number sent there, the different disposi tions which have been made of the detachments, and the present effective force of the same, with such other information as he may deem necessary to communicate respecting the state of the army. The question was taken on the resolution, and decided in the affirmative by a great majority. Messrs. Newton and Shaw were appointed a committee to wait on the president with the resolution. Letters have been received by two of the first mercantile houses in this city; the one positively asserting that Ulmoss has quitted Vienna, and the other that the Austrian ambassador has taken its departure from Paris. We learn that the Portuguese Frigate has captured a French vessel, with dispatches on board for Buenos Ayres, and a large quantity of arms and ammunition. The prize with the dispatches, are on their way to Portsmouth. We are happy to find by the Copenhagen mail which arrived this morning, that the disposition both of the people and government of Denmark towards this country has undergone a very material change. By the judicious management of Sir James Saumarez an agreement has been entered into with the government of Denmark for the exchange of prisoners, which has already been carried into effect. The prisoners taken on board  the Prince Christian have been sent home to Denmark, and a considerable number of British prisoners have been sent to Gottenburgh.

The government were about to meet duties. PROVIDENCE, (R. I.) May 18. On Sunday morning last, between the hours of 11 and 12, the following melancholy and tragic facts occurred.

On the morning last a fire broke out in a new brick building at the corner of Wood and Liberty streets, which being in great forwardness before any assistance arrived, soon communicated with the adjoining houses, and in the course of two hours consumed four brick and three or four frame buildings. The common complaint at a fire in Pittsburgh, of the scarcity of buckets, and of numerous idle spectators, was very evident last Friday morning. Had there been one or two more engines, and a sufficiency of buckets, several valuable buildings might have been saved. Merino Sheep—A few days ago two men from Connecticut passed through this place with 15 Merino sheep they had purchased from Dr. Bass, of Butler county, which they were taking home for the purpose of increasing the stock of those valuable animals. Although Colonel Humphries, of that state, has more Merinos than are in the United States besides, yet these men inform that it is impossible to purchase one from him, having such an extensive woolen manufactory to supply with their valuable fleeces. They paid Dr. Bass upwards of four thousand dollars for fifteen, and say they could get a thousand dollars apiece for them, so great a value do the people in that state put upon them.

Of those professing the Christian religion, there are 50 millions of Protestants, 30 millions of the Greek and Armenian Churches, and 70 millions of Catholics. If we calculate with the ancients, that a generation lasts 80 years, in that space 896,000,000 will be born and die; consequently, 81,760 must be dropping into eternity every day, or about 3407 every hour, or about 36 every minute. It is truly laughable to hear the tale of "French influence" raised by Federalists to blast the well-earned honor and reputation of the late administration, and contrast with the hypocritical, flattering cant, bestowed in the form of praise on Mr. Madison, one of the members of that administration, who, while occupying a seat at the council board of Mr. Jefferson, received an ample share of federal abuse. The charge of being a dependant of France, conjured up in the character of a French citizen, is not forgotten. Can an editor, who walks through society and asserts a claim to honesty, have so little regard to his own feelings, as to appear in a character which among mankind would be sufficient so to stamp liar on his brazen front as to destroy forever confidence in him?

He, and his favored rival are the Edwin and Sir Topaz of Dr. Parnell- " He felt the charms of Edith's eyes. Nor wanted hope to gain the prize, Could Ladies look within; But, one Sir Topaz dressed with art And, if a shape can win a heart, He has a shape to win." The lady, Who transcribed and transmitted the beautiful Lines to Sensibility is entreated to crop more of the flowers of Poesy. In the intervals of domestic duty it is more illustrious for a woman to peruse the brilliant and the useful, than to flaunt in public places or to cheapen fans. Ah, friend to dazzle let the vain design, To raise the thought, and touch the heart be thine. " Lines from Florio" are inadmissible. The rhymes are not legitimate, and we discern neither plan, nor connection in His Poem; Florio may continue to scan verses with ten fingers, but he never can be a Bard. " But, lest you think I rally more than teach, Or praise malignly arts I cannot reach, Let me for once presume to insult the times, To know the Poet from the man of rhymes 'Tis he, who gives my breast a thousand pains, Can make me feel each passion that he feigns, Enrage, compose, with more than magic art.

establishment, as to the birth of our its stock of inhabitants, as to the institutions it was established, and as to the letter and spirit of its laws. The English materials in our composition were very considerable; but we derived much from other kingdoms, states, and church much from our own necessities, occasions, dispositions and faculties, it some of the families planted in the late American provinces were from the land of Newton, Milton, Bacon, Coke, Shakespeare and Marlborough, others were from the countries of Luther, Calvin and Men no, Grotius, Pufendorf and Vattel, De Wilt, and Gustavus and the great Frederick, Leibnitz, Boerhaave and Linnaeus, Swift, Burke, Hutche- son, Hume, Stuart, Ferguson, Napier, Knox, Buchanan, Hunter and Monro. Let us re vive, then, with attention, certain historical truths, remembering that it is no worse to have been derived from all the nations of the wise, the learned and the good, than to have been derived from one alone; and that salutary institutions and principles, obtained from the British kingdoms (as they are now called), or from the continent of Europe or from the re- sources of our own minds, are no derogation to the customs of those, which they have all contributed to the present conformation of our national character. The authors and founders of empires naturally command the public regard.

Speaking of the present doings in Connecticut, it will be only said such things were evident at a Mechanical Association. The increasing number and respectability of this Society is a pleasing omen of its future usefulness. A special meeting of this Association will be held on Thursday evening next, for the admission of several candidates. At the same time, an opportunity will offer for any others who may be desirous of becoming members, by their making previous application to the Secretary or either of the board of Directors. The Association will celebrate their anniversary the 2nd day of next month, when they will meet at Jefferson Hall, at ten o’clock in the morning, and form a procession, preceded by a band of music, and proceed to some place of public worship, where an address will be delivered by Mr. Joseph Clark, Jr., Secretary to said Society, attended by solemn instrumental and vocal music, adapted to the occasion; after which, they will proceed to Mechanical Hall, and partake of an elegant dinner, to be provided for the occasion. His Excellency the Governor, by advice of Council, has appointed Thursday the 27th November next, to be observed as a day of public thanksgiving and prayer throughout this state.

MARINE INTELLIGENCE. Charleston, Aug. 22. Arrived from Thursday last, schr Mercury, Pennington, from Martinique. She joined the American convoy at St. Kitts, and sailed from thence on the 28th July, under the Baltimore, Capt. Cowper, with 120 sail of Americans, among which were a French privateer schr. and French dogger from Cadiz, prize to the U. S. ship Connecticut. Left at St. Kitts the U. S. ship Merrimack, and the schooner Enterprise. The ship Horizon, Capt. White, lately built in this harbor, left our Bar on Monday last, bound to London. On Tuesday she fell in with a British Privateer, which after chasing her for 32 hours, brought her to off Bull's Island. A lieutenant came on board the Horizon and pressed two men, who were Americans.

John Adams, Esq., late Commissioner of the United States of America, at the Court of Versailles, late Delegate in Congress from the State of Massachusetts Bay, and Chief Justice of that State, have nominated and constituted, and by these presents do nominate and constitute, him, the said John Adams, our Minister Plenipotentiary, giving him full power, general and special, to act in that quality, to confer, agree, and conclude with the Ambassador or Plenipotentiary of His Britannic Majesty vested with equal powers, of and concerning any of commerce, and to transact everything that is necessary for completing, securing, and ratifying the same in as ample form and with the same effect as if we were personally present and acted therein; hereby promising in good faith, that we will accept, ratify, fulfill, and execute, whatever shall be concluded and signed by our said Minister Plenipotentiary, and that we will never act, nor suffer any irregularity or misconduct in the whole or in any part. In witness whereof, we caused these presents to be at Congress, at Philadelphia, the twenty-ninth day of September in the year of our Lord, one thousand seven hundred and seventy-nine, and in the third year of the independence of the United States. Signed by the President, and sealed with his Seal, SAMUEL HUNTINGTON, President, Attest, Charles Thomson, Sec'y. With these two commissions, I received special instructions in detail.

Should any vacancy at any time happen among the Directors, through death, resignation or otherwise, the remaining Directors shall elect a Director to fill such vacancy until the next election; and on the said first Monday in April, 1811, and on every succeeding year, a general meeting of the Stockholders of the said Bank shall be holden at such place as the Board of Directors shall appoint, by notice given in the most public manner the nature of the case will admit, at least fifteen days next antecedent to such meeting, for the purpose of electing nine Directors for the ensuing year; and the said Directors, thus elected, shall, at their first meeting after such election in each year, choose one of their number as President: Provided, that in case it should happen at any time that an election of Directors should not be made upon any day when, pursuant to these articles, it ought to have been made, it shall be lawful, and the Directors for the time being are hereby required, within ten days after such neglect to call a meeting of the stockholders, for the purpose of making such election of Directors, in such manner as a majority of them shall direct and appoint. A proposition has been made in a Rhode-Island paper to form an association for the purpose of producing a Nursery of Lombardy Poplars. A lot of 5 or 6 acres, it is conjectured, will produce 100,000 trees in three years, of a size sufficiently large for transplanting; at the end of which time they are to be distributed gratis to all such persons as would agree to place them in proper situations on their own land either for ornament or convenience. His Excellency the Governor of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, has issued a Proclamation, appointing Thursday, the 9th of April next, to be observed as a day of Humiliation, Fasting and Prayer. In the Gazette extraordinary of this day, (accompanying this paper) will be found notifications for the payment of the Direct tax in the towns of Charleston, Langdon, Ackworth, Haverhill, Piermont, Oxford, Wentworth, Warren, Coventry, Wakefield, Effingham, Ossipee, Gilmanton, Barnstead, Bridgewater, New-Chester, Grafton, Danbury and Alexandria, with sundry other advertisements. This is the fourth Gazette we have issued double to our customers since the 3rd of February, though at a great expense, yet we have not added one cent to the price of the paper. NECESSITY compels me to make further application to all those who are indebted to the trustees of the Richmond Academy, by sales of lottery tickets of the first and second class and voluntary subscriptions, and still such are hereby requested to make payment to the subscriber to enable the building committee to pay all arrears for work done, and to procure materials this winter in order to proceed with the work next spring, it being for the education of our rising progeny, which ornaments and attaches them to republican principles and gives a spring to liberty and independence. I have undertaken the collection by agreement with John Brockenbrough and William W. Hening, Esq's. the other members of the committee appointed by a board of trustees. As the building of the Academy has progressed considerably, I flatter myself there will be but little trouble in obtaining the money for the purpose of carrying into effect so laudable an institution. ROBERT MITCHELL December 8th, 1809.

Mr. Jackson to Mr. Smith, Washington, 1st October, 1801. Sir — I have had the honor of receiving your official letter of the 9th inst. towards the close of which you inform me, that it has been thought expedient to put an end to all verbal communication between yourself and me, in discussing the important objects of my mission. Considering that a very few days have elapsed since I delivered to the president a credential letter from the king my master, and that nothing has even been alleged to have occurred, to deprive me of the facility of access, and of the credit to which, according to immemorial usage, I believe there does not exist in the annals of diplomacy a precedent for such a determination between two ministers, who have met for the avowed purpose of terminating amicably the existing differences between their respective countries: but, after mature reflection, I am induced to acquiesce in it by the recollection of the time that must necessarily elapse before I can receive his majesty's commands upon so unexpected an occurrence, and of the detriment that would ensue to the public service, if my ministerial functions were, in the interval, to be altogether suspended.

** I really think the arch-demon, in finding that he sacrifices his armies by sending them to Spain, and wishing to realize his vast ambitious projects on that side of Turkey in Europe, will give us a breathing time here. It is well known that up to the 25th of Dec. last, not above 3,000 men reinforced his armies in Spain.” * Not at Cadiz. “New York, March 11. The ship Atlantic, left Cadiz on the 20th of Jan. By her we learn verbally, that a battle was fought the beginning of Jan. in which the Spanish army was dispersed by the French. That a large French army was approaching Andalusia; That the Supreme Junta had ordered apartments at Cadiz, where they were expected about the first of Feb. That the Spanish ships of war at Cadiz were bending sail, and in other respects preparing for sea. It was reported at Cadiz, that there was a serious insurrection in the south of France, that the leaders of it had solicited Marshal Massena to become their commander in chief; and that this state of things was communicated to Lord Collingwood, probably for the purpose of soliciting his aid in furthering their views. He is about 5 feet 9 or 10 inches of a thin, spare make; rather a round face, looks bold, and is about 20 years of age. He carried with him, some tow linen shirts and trousers, a short blue coat etc. This lad is a shoemaker, and it is more than probable will engage as a journeyman with some one of the trade. He is an artful, cunning fellow, reads his testament tolerably well, and plays occasionally upon the fife; is pert and saucy, has a vast share of pride, thinks highly of himself; very tenacious of his opinions, overbearing and disputatious and will chatter and dispute from sun to sun, rather than confess a fault.

The account given of the affair by Mr. Herriot, drew a challenge from Mr. Dickens, which Mr. Herriot declined upon the ground that the challenger was no gentleman. Mr. Herriot, in his relation of the transaction, charges Cobbett with having been "sent from America as a convicted libeller." In reply to the charge, Cobbett says, "The affair is not ended; you tried by civil and not by criminal law; a corrupt and base jury awarded to my adversary 5,000 dollars, the whole of which money together with the expenses of the law-suit, was paid by British gentlemen in Canada, (the Governor of that province being one,) and in the United States of America. Nay, so flagrant was the injustice of the case, that a number of Americans agreed to raise the sum required, and made me an offer of it, through one of the Aldermen of New-York; an offer which I should have accepted, had it come previously to that made by my own countrymen." Here then is Cobbett's own avowal that the fine incurred by his slandering the venerable character of Dr. Rush, was paid by British gentlemen, and was offered to be paid by Americans! What patriotic Americans must those have been, who would first encourage a foreign blackguard to libel the reputation of one of their best men, and then screen him from justice by paying the penalty of his crime! True Amer. From Washington, Dec. 27, 1803, Advices from New.

and met with no opposition, except Irmii our field pieces till they arrived near the top, when they were charged by the 29th, 2nd, 43rd, and 16th regiments, who drove them much quicker, now retired to the top of the mountain, &c dispatched a column of about 2000 to take possession of a tower on top, which I thought ought to be occupied by us before, as by means of the easy descent on the other side, we could with ease be cut off, in case of a reverse of fortune, from the road leading from Oropesa to Talavera, which was the same we came in the latter by. The 23rd Light Dragoons, and Dragoon Guards now came round the hill, in a valley between the hill and mountain, which kept this French division from attempting any further, till reinforced by two other strong columns; they now advanced, and commenced forming a line of one of those, while the other remained in column, ready to receive our cavalry. The 23rd were then ordered to advance, and did so very regularly, though under a very heavy fire of musketry. On commencing the charge, the column which had displayed, succeeded by double quick time in getting into column before the dragoons came up.

THE subscriber wishes to dispose of the FARM on which he now lives, situated in Berkeley County, Virginia, near Opequon Creek, about three miles from Martinsburg and seven from Shepherd's Town, on the main route leading from the former to the latter place— This farm contains about three hundred acres, a part slate and a part limestone land of the first quality, about two-thirds of the whole tract cleared, and now in a state of high cultivation, the balance clothed with excellent timber, which will always afford a greater quantity than the con sumption of the place will require— A sufficient portion of the land can be con verted into good meadow—The improve ments are a very comfortable and convenient dwelling house, a large frame barn and stables, with other useful buildings; there is a spring of good water quite con venient to the dwelling, and several other springs rise on different parts of the place, furnishing a sufficient and con stant supply of water throughout the year. The subscriber being desirous to re move to the state of Ohio, will take in exchange for the above farm, land well situated and of good quality in any part of that state; or if a greater part of the price can be paid in hand, a great bar gain will be given in it.

However, remaining unalarmed, the whale rushed, expecting if possible a more dreadful mischief than this day, to our great joy and satisfaction we found they had retired; not a single man was to be seen. “Our light infantry brigade with some artillery guns arrived this morning, and have pursued in order to keep them in view. Their numbers could not be less than 50,000 men, having been joined by Joseph and the army from Toledo. Joseph was present, it is said, during the whole time. Their loss is very great; but I am afraid not in proportion to ours, which cannot be less in killed and wounded than 5,000 out of not more than 22,000 men. We lost about 180 privates and 6 officers, for which we had no more satisfaction than in six rounds per man; the officers' wounds I believe are not dangerous. “Our town and field of battle at this moment, present the most dreadful scene you can imagine: some of the wounded people remained two days undressed, for want of medical assistance, and numbers who could not be cared for."

Having concisely stated the important facts, passed by the legislature, it only remains to give the public a general view of the reduction of national expenditure since the change of administration. These reductions are of two kinds—First—Of expenditures which are casual or temporary, and which ought not to be brought into the estimate in ascertaining our yearly burdens, or in forming an opinion of the different systems contended for. Of these are the erecting and repairing fortifications, the appropriations for the support of seamen, and the like. The expenditures of this kind for the current year have been lessened about one hundred and sixty thousand dollars. Second—Those expenses which according to the late system of politics were permanent and annual, and may be fairly taken into view to determine which political party is best entitled to national confidence. Of these expenditures, from the peace establishment of the late administration, the following reductions have been made by the executive and by congress at its last session. By the executive in reducing various offices whose existence depended on his will, and other economical arrangements made before the session of congress has effected an annual saving of 161,695 91. PARIS, MARCH 17. Yesterday’s Moniteur has the following:—General Androfsi, Ambassador from France to Vienna, is arrived at Paris on leave of absence— He left behind him, to transact his affairs, M. Dobrin, his first Secretary of Legation. On his arrival, he had an interview with the Emperor. This morning, Col Goigoiy, Aide- de-Camp of the Emperor of Russia, arrived here; he left Petersburg on the 1st instant.—The Emperor invited him instantly to accompany him to the chase, and also to dine with him. March 18.—The last news from Saragossa contradicts the report of the death of Palafox; they even state that he has quite recovered. The Count de Fuentes continues danger ously ill in consequence of his long confinement in a dungeon by the insurgents. Ulm, March 10. The first column of General Oudinot's army which consists of 30,000 men parted the Danube between the 24th and 27th of February, and pro- ceeded in the direction of Augsburg. The second column has been filing off through this city since the first instant and pursues the same route.

A London paper contains a letter from an officer at the Cape of Good Hope, dated in March last, wherein he states that he encountered a storm, in which he had "his shirt blown in pieces from his back!" And adds curiously enough, that it was great mercy it had not also "blown away his skin." This we suppose is a relation of Baron Munchausen. From the New York Gazette. CASE OF CONSCIENCE. I have heard the author of "Serious Considerations," etc. on the supposition of his being a clergyman, blamed for meddling with what did not belong to his profession. But in my opinion, this judicious and pious writer has evoked a new and important case of conscience. Suppose one of his hearers, who was a member of the legislature and expected to vote for electors, or who was himself an elector, should address him in the following manner; "I believe Mr. Jefferson to be a Deist, at least I have such suspicions of him that I cannot banish from my mind. I know that there is nothing in the constitution which prevents my voting for a Deist or a Mahometan."

RAN AWAY from the Subscriber, on Sunday 19th inst. late in the evening, an indented Mulatto Boy, aged 17 years, engaged by the name of Joseph Brown, saying himself to be from Lancaster or that neighborhood; he is ill shaped, has a round face, short hair, large mouth, smiling countenance, dull speech, big hands and feet, and he has no beard. He may dress himself with a genteel coat of light drab color, white buttons, and black cape. He wears a good round black hat. He has stolen from the subscriber upwards of 100 dollars in cash and value of other objects. Whoever will apprehend and secure him with as much value about him will receive the above reward, and 10 dollars if the young villain can only be brought to condign punishment. 6 FELIX PASCAL, No. 70 South Street. To point out the errors, or to ridicule the absurd superstitions, which have debased the worship of the present system of religion is certainly not only consistent with a thorough belief of that system, but is perhaps a duty, which that belief requires; but one should be extremely careful, lest in the zeal of reformation, a weapon should be afforded to the opponents of religion itself. To the Catholics, I think we owe no apology for the exposure of their failings. The bigoted intolerance and persecutions, which have marked the footsteps of the followers of papacy, from the burning of John Huss, to the massacre of St. Bartholomew, give them but feeble claims on the liberality of Protestants; and the evident contempt for all other Christians, which is still to be perceived at Rome, notwithstanding all their humiliations, give us a fair right to examine the ground of their imaginary claim to superiority. I shall devote the present letter to the narration of such facts as have fallen within my own observation only, relative to the superstitious opinions and observances, and pious frauds, existing in Italy at the present moment. The doctrine of indulgences is familiar to you. The pretended origin is the power given to St. Peter, and which the papists contend has descended like a heirloom, or like the mantle of Elijah upon his regularly anointed successors in the apostolic chair.

The Editor of the National Intelligencer, published at the City of Washington, has issued a supplement, containing, as he says, an Official Copy of the Convention between America and France. He states, as a reason for publishing it, that a mutilated copy has got into circulation. A gentleman who left the City of Washington yesterday, informs, that it was currently reported there, that the French treaty would not be ratified. I had been scarcely three months at Königsberg, torn from a former mission to the Court of Versailles, in which I had twice run the gauntlet across the Atlantic, so with women of war, neither of which were from a land embarkation on my family estate. As well founded as those of any man living, having a wife and four children, then young, whom I must either abandon in a country oppressed in all the horrors of war, or convey them, with myself to the dangers of the deep, anxious situation of my country, which I then felt deeply, was such as to engross my thoughts, for months. Extract from the valedictory address of the Hon. John Q. Adams, previous to his resignation of the Professorship of Oratory, at Harvard University. "Finally, gentlemen, though inclination still lingers with me, I reluctantly bid you one and all adieu. I have heard of two lovers, who, unwillingly separated from each other for a length of time, and a distance like that to which I am bound, among the contrivances which they ingenuity of affection devised, to bring fancy nearer to each other, maintained.

This at the moment could not be helped. Fortunately, while fields were on fire, at once, we each covered their advance. Want of time might prevent me continuing a de- tail which any moderate man might fill pa- ges on. However, one thing we were de- ceived in, the enemy’s number; really it was doubtful at a late hour yesterday what would be the result. “All we can boast of, after 17 or 18 hours’ hard fighting, is possession of the field of battle, and I believe 19 pieces of artillery. I have often heard of the great superiority of the management of their great guns, which I must say was far short of what it really is. Provisions are still very scarce. The army lost a number of men by the mistakes occasioned by the darkness of the night of the 2nd; several times one line has been found firing on another.

To your Majesty's recollection, the melancholy fate of a second army, assembled within the Peninsula under the gallant command of Sir John Moore; ignoring alike of the state and disposition of the Spaniards, and the force and designs of the enemy, this army being sent into the interior of Spain, was in imminent danger of being captured; in this critical emergency, and state of agonizing perplexity, abandoned to his own resources, this hapless but meritorious officer at length discovered that he had no safety but in flight; with the loss of his ammunition, horses, specie, and harassed and assailed on all sides, he secured the retreat of his gallant followers by the sacrifice of his own invaluable life. "Deriving no benefit from experience, a third well-appointed army under the command of Sir Arthur Wellesley, was hurried into the interior of Spain, alike ignorant of the force and movements of the enemy; where, after an unprofitable display of British valor, and a dreadful slaughter, this army like the former, was compelled to seek its safety by a precipitate retreat before (what we were led to believe) a vanquished foe, leaving thousands of our sick and wounded countrymen in the hands of the enemy."

Fanport men who met together were only made to serve them and raise money for them to spend. Shortly after Clark, who was a good friend to the people as he himself told me last spring, ran off with 31,000 dollars, and this convinced me that the rest of them who talked just as smooth as he did, would also act like him if they could get as good a chance. Town meeting is now pretty near, and we have got to go and vote for Governor again; and the democrats are electioneering now harder than ever:—And it is only two or three days since C-ts’ postmaster was at our house to tell me what I must do. He said there had been a caucus at Bernard’s, where Thatcher, and Keyzer, and Moody, and Granger, and Green, and Pike, and Storer, and Hatch, and Keeler, and Kettel, and Lane, and Liscott, and forty other office holders and office hunters, and they had all agreed to puff up Gerry and Gray all they could; and bought the Kennebunk paper for Thatcher and Storer to write in. And he told me in confidence (for I did not let him know I was not as good a democrat as ever) how the County Committee had written to the Town Committees, and how the Town Committees had divided all the towns into little squads, and that they should carry Woodman and Rice all hollow.

From an official notable personage in Campeachy, to a person of quality in this city: "Last evening entered here, three of our vessels from Vera Cruz, in 7 days. It is certain that our king Ferdinand, was proclaimed and sworn to in Vera Cruz, and the Supreme Junta of Seville acknowledged with universal joy and acclamation. The same has taken place in the city of Mexico. When this intelligence shall arrive in Spain, it will greatly re-animate the spirit and hopes of our friends there; added to the immense treasures which they expect from America. In fact it is surprising, the money which is every day deposited in the royal chests, to be sent to Spain. To his lordship Napoleon, this will not afford much gratification when he comes to hear it." There is a report in circulation that the United States ship of war, Warren, Captain Newman, has been taken by the British frigate Quebec, on account of carrying Spanish money, from Vera Cruz to Havana. We do not think there is any foundation for the report. Capt. N. had no right thus to employ his ship. We understand that three or four persons of good appearance have been arrested in this city, who are supposed to have belonged to the gang which committed such depredations in Baltimore. They had commenced their operations with great swiftness and adroitness. That these gentlemen have been arrested so soon in their projects, reflects great credit on the intelligence and activity of our municipal officers. A man (perhaps one of the above mentioned fraternity) yesterday entered a house in Stone street, and very leisurely proceeded to load himself with all the plate which the sideboard and closets contained, and had left the house unperceived by any belonging to it. Fortunately somebody passing at that moment, suspected him, from seeing him thus muffled up and loaded, and went instantly in the house to enquire if anything was missing. The discovery of the theft was immediately made, and the pursuit was fortunately successful. By this Day's Mails. BOSTON, August 26. Connecticut Banks. * i ROWLETT's Copy-Right. Little, prove, never had an existence in the mind of government, until the court had honorably acquitted him. They are contained in the letter of the secretary of the navy, dated September 25, 1801, approving the sentence of the court martial. Mr. Smith says, "In the foregoing case, among several important allegations there is a charge of pillage, exhibited against Captain Little; and it was for the trial of this charge principally that the court martial was constituted. Not only the honor of the nation, but the honor of this officer was deeply implicated, and therefore called for such a solemn investigation. In this trial it was the government, that was the party prosecuting. The foreigners were only the informing witnesses in a criminal prosecution." The observations of the council for capt. Little, will show, whether government were really and substantially the party prosecuting. "An attentive perusal of these charges shows, that they are all comprised in one, that of maltreatment of his prisoners. It might indeed seem upon a cursory view, as it has been intimated, that the charges contain something more, viz. an actual embezzlement by capt. Little of certain prize goods with an intent to defraud the government; but I must insist that no such charge is even specified by the government.

An American ship, It is said, with dispatches for General Armstrong at Paris, in attempting to go into Havre on the 6th instant, was taken possession of by a French ship, and sent into another port. Her dispatches were forwarded immediately to Paris, but her crew were not permitted to have any communication with the shore. This circumstance is of itself a sufficient proof that no definite arrangement has yet taken place between America and France. Private accounts have been received from Paris, to the 11th inst. A letter of the 11th states that, on the 6th, Bonaparte had a transit attack of epilepsy, which was so violent as to occasion considerable alarm for several days. A Bulletin of the 9th, however, pronounced him to be rapidly recovering from what was called only a slight cold. Hence, doubtless, originated the report of his death, as we mentioned on Thursday.

I lent my aid to make our Constitution. For my part, I recollected that Gerry was one of the three members of the Convention that refused to sign it—and that he came home, got into the Massachusetts Convention on purpose to oppose it—and that was the reason why I never would vote for him when he was set up against Governor Strong. And yet now we are told that he was a great friend to it. When I see such stories circulated among the people, by folks who hold offices and are afraid of losing them, I think, indeed, that the cloven foot does peep out in good earnest. For one then, I am determined to do all I can to keep in CHRISTOPHER GORE, AS GOVERNOR— and DAVID COBB, AS LIEUTENANT GOVERNOR—, And to vote for JOSEPH LELAND, AND SAMUEL A. BRADLEY, AS SENATORS, Because I believe them to be the real friends of the people. So no more at present from yours to serve, ICHABOD SMYTH. To the Hon. Wm. Gray, Esq. Sir, As you have permitted yourself to be considered a candidate for the office of Lieut. Governor, your character becomes a proper subject of investigation.
