Perhaps a more atrocious usurpation of power was never attempted in a government of laws, than that by which Mr. Jefferson claims the right of reviewing and controlling the proceedings of the judiciary. Speaking in his message to congress, of the late prosecutions in the district of Virginia, he says: “You will be enabled to judge whether the defect was in the testimony, in the law, or in the administration of the law; and wherever it shall be found, the legislature alone can apply or originate the remedy.” There is much important and alarming meaning in these few words. “You will be enabled to judge whether the defect,” etc. How does it appear that there was any defect? Why, simply thus. At the last session of congress, Mr. Jefferson usurped the prerogatives of a court and jury, by positively pronouncing upon the guilt of Aaron Burr. Afterwards he was put upon his trial by due process of law, and two separate juries pronounced him not guilty. Now Mr. Jefferson, assuming the position that his sentence pronounced without trial is right, and that the sentence of a court and jury, informed upon a patient and tedious investigation is wrong, infers that there is a defect and that this defect exists either in the testimony, in the law, or in the administration of the law.

Robert Tate, first mate, and 13 passengers landed on shore; immediately after their landing, they hung Mr. Tate and two of the Prince's passengers on the public wharf. Captain Gibson was ordered to be shot, but the corporal who was ordered to shoot him, making some delay by refusing to do it without the order of his commander, gave time for the intercession of some Americans and the captain’s life was saved. Several French regiments, that had formerly served under Moreau, in Germany, have, under a pretense of a mutinous disposition, been disbanded, and the men in them sent to the colonial depots, or incorporated with regiments that had been in Egypt. This despotic act caused a great deal of murmuring; and when Buonaparte, the night after, went to the theatre at Boulogne, he experienced proofs of the public opinion, such as he little expected, and which caused the theatre to be shut up—the drama of Peter the Great was represented; when this Emperor was announced by his heralds, the pit cried out, “No Emperor! No Emperor!”

The Austrian troops in Tuscany retired without giving any assistance to the levy in mass, which it was necessary to disarm. These Jacobin banditti committed all kinds of excesses, and respected no treaty. While General Dupont was conducting the town of Arrezo, and carried it by assault at Pistoia the Austrians took General Sommariva, obtaining permission to march with the corps under his command across the French army, and to rejoin the Austrian army at Ferrara. The levy en masse was so oppressive to Tuscany, that those persons who were most attached to the Grand Duke saw with pleasure the arrival of the French, which was conducted according to the manner of regular troops who came to deliver a country from the yoke of undisciplined barbarians. In other respects, the government was preserved, as it had been established by the authority of the Grand Duke. November 10. The report of the entrance of the French into Rome is untrue. Lucien Buonaparte set off yesterday. It is said that he is going to Spain. From Lunéville we learn that the young citizens Lecreurer, Portalis, and Simeon, have declared to the officers of Police of that city, that they are attached to the French Legation.

Hearing much said of "the rabble" of late, I was led to inquire after the origin and meaning of the word. Johnson gives the following account of it - "Rabble: (from rabula, Latin) a tumultuous crowd; an assembly as low people." This word is often wrongly applied to the common people, who are by no means the tumultuous nor the low. They are the most peaceable and the highest in rank. Who appoint men to office? The common people. Who are the men to choose town officers? The common people. Who choose men to compose the General Court? "The common people." Who choose the Governor, Lieutenant-Governor, etc.? The common people. Who choose the members of Congress?

We may be as irritable, as high-minded as we please, in maintaining abstract republican doctrines, and define estimable value they surely are, but we shall still find that as the constitution itself was, so the practice under it is, and must be daily a subject of mutual forbearance and compromise, especially when seventeen sovereign states, are to agree upon any one point of colossal common concern. The nomination of President by individuals who compose the Congress, may or may not be accepted by the people; at most it is but notice, coming from a general and respectable quarter; no authority is assumed, no coercion is attempted—but if in the progress of things, the members of Congress, so far forgetting themselves and their constituents, should aim at the usurpation of abusive powers, it is hoped that there never will be wanting in the republic, a chastening voice and an arm that will compel them, with contrite hearts, to retrace the path of duty. Mr. jltjccu (the Speaker) expressed his opinion of the incorrectness of the course pursued on this occasion, on which the Committee of Commerce and manufactures, contrary to the usual practice of the House, had reported a bill without assigning their reasons in an accompanying report. He wished that such a report might be made, and that it might stand as a precedent, in case either applications should be made, to show the peculiar grounds on which the House acted. It was further his wish that there had been a bill embracing the general principle involved in this case, in preference to one making a special provision. That a detailed report might be made, he moved that the committee should rise, in order to make way for a motion to recommit the bill to the Committee of Commerce and Manufactures. Mr. Newton opposed this motion. He represented the case as extremely hard; that the facts were all before the House and were indisputable; that the vessel had been detained since Oct. that nothing was asked but what was perfectly fair; that the necessity of making the request arose from orders recently issued by a King who, though he could do no wrong to his own subjects, practiced great injuries on American citizens.

The: enemy, who, astonished and, dismayed by the intrepidity with which they were assailed, began precipitately to retreat, leaving the field covered with carnage. Above 700 bodies of their dead have been buried on the ground. The wounded and prisoners already in our hands (among which are generals, an-aid de camp, the lieutenant colonel of the Swiss regiment, and a linguist officer of dissipated ranks) amount to above 1000. There are also above 1000 men lost in Montilcone and these seem to have mostly notified their readiness to surrender whenever a British force shall be sent to receive their submission, and so protect them from the fury of bringing in fugitives, who dispersed in the woods and mountains after the battle. In short, never has the pride of our presumptuous enemy been more severely humbled, nor the superiority of the British troops more gloriously proved, than in the events of this memorable day. His majesty may perhaps, still deign to appreciate more highly the achievements of this little army, when it is known that the same second division which the enemy were said to be expecting, had joined them the night before the action; no statement that I have heard of their numbers places them at a less calculation than 1200 men.

I have pleased myself, people of Louisiana, with drawing at length this pleasing picture, and of opposing it to the reproaches of abandonment, and the tender regrets uttered by many among you, who are attached to the country of their ancestors, France and its government, will hear of these regrets, with gratitude and affection. But ere long, you will by your own experience, prove the justice it has shown you by this eminent and most memorable of benefits. The French government recognizes in this event, the first example offered in modern times, of a colony voluntarily emancipating itself, after the example of those colonies of antiquity which we so greatly admire. In the present time, and in times to come, may an inhabitant of Louisiana, and a Frenchman, never meet upon any soil of the globe, without feeling as brothers. May this title, for the future, be the only one which will truly describe their eternal engagements, and liberal dependence! Let us know what advice you have received from Mr. King, and whether Pitt and Grenville are obliged to Mr. Ames, for securing the lease of the Rainbow, by our adopting the Treaty, If such favorable events have taken place, we are desirous to know them, and we are willing to participate in your good for- tune, by reciprocating our congratulations. But believe me, federal gentlemen, when I tell you that the whole is a delusion, and that you will find a Faction has deceived you. Time will convince you, that the opposers of the Treaty have been, and now are, your only friends. Mr. Jefferson and Mr. Madi- son’s political principles would have helped you, but a British Junto led you to spurn their proffered friendship. If you do not get compensation, you must blame the last ad- ministration. I did propose in this Number to point out the impositions on our Neutrality by Mr. Jay's Treaty, but finding them so nu- merable, I have thought best to call on its advocates to declare the Commercial benefits they so confidently contemplated, and even pledged themselves would ensue. If they are silent on this head, we are to presume they have never realized their expectations, that the whole was a series of fraud and deception, and solely calculated to root out, by means of Agents and Factors, every advantage of Commerce, heretofore enjoyed by our citi- zens.

"Sir Harry," Sir Harry, a handsome and one of the best racers, son of Sir Peter Teazle, (unquestionably the best stallion now in England & inferior to none ever bred in that coun try,) will stand the ensuing season at my stable, in King and Queen county, about 40 miles from Richmond, 7 from Dunkirk and 12 from Tappahannock, at 50 dollars the season, notes for which, and half a dollar for the groom, must be sent with the mares or before they are taken away. The 50 dollars may be discharged by the payment of 40 dollars cash, (and in the event of the embargo continuing, or a war, by 39 dollars) on or before the expiration of the season, which will commence on the 1st March and end 1st August. Extensive high and low land pasturage, as good as any in Virginia, await. Mares may be fed with corn at the market price, and every attention will be paid to them, but no liability for escapes or accidents of any kind. Mares and foals will be kept by themselves. Sir Harry was imported in the fall of 1804, from London, is now 12 years old, in high health and vigor, 15 hands 3 inches high, full of bone and muscle, fine points, elegant form and beautiful brown color.

Be a preter to the great Napoleon. "Tell him that the wishes of all the Senators for the Emperor and his family are wholly absorbed in their wishes for the welfare and glory of their country. In all our affections as well as in our thoughts the destinies of the Bonapartes and the fortunes of France are henceforth inseparable." His excellency then addressing himself to her Imperial Highness Princess Joseph, said, "Madam, it is a glorious day for the Senate, that in which it can express its love, its respect and its devotion to the august family of the worthy Emperor of the French. "Madam, your Imperial Highness cannot but remark, by the altered voice of him who has the honor of speaking to you, the deep tenderness which has been produced in him by the admirable answers of his Imperial Highness Prince Joseph to the discourse, or rather the very few words which I have been permitted just now to address to him.

Mr. Wolcott’s Address To the People of the United States. - No. II. “Yet the first object of inquiry relates to the construction, which has been given by the Treasury Department to various laws, for appropriating money in the Treasury, especially those in relation to the expenses of the War and Navy Departments.” This introduces the famous question concerning specific appropriations; the history of which question from the time when the small-minded rivals of our first financier started it as a weapon to assail his administration, until this report of the Investigating Committee, would comprise a most essential portion of our national history, and would unfold no small insight into the secret impulses of political jealousy, and the wayward effects of the human heart.—But we have not the time to enter into the necessary details of this history, and so far as relates to Mr. Wolcott’s publication the results alone are material.

The question whether the bill to repeal the JUDICIARY LAW of the last session, should pass, was again debated. Messrs. Nicholas, Bradley, Anderson, Breckenridge, Jackson, Wright, and Cocke spoke in favor of the bill; which was opposed by Messrs. Ross, Ogden, Morris, Dayton and White. The argument continued until half past five in the evening, when the question was taken. The whole number of Senators present was 31—16 made a vote. There were, for the bill 16—against it 15. For the bill—Messrs. Anderson, Baldwin, Breckenridge, Bradley, Brown, Cocke, Ellery, Th: Foster, Franklin, Jackson, Logan, S. T. Mason, Nicholas, Stone, Sumpter, Wright. Against it—Messrs. "Gentlemen of the House of Commons, Under the circumstances of the present meeting, I am desirous of asking of you such Supplies only as may be necessary for carrying on the public Service, in the parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland may conveniently be The Estimates for that purpose will be laid before you; and I have no doubt of your readiness to make such provisions as the public interest may appear to require. "My Lords and Gentlemen, I have directed Copies to be laid before you of those communications which have been recently passed between me and the French Government, respecting the commencement of negotiations for peace. You will see in them fresh and striking proofs of my earnest desire to contribute to the re-establishment of general tranquility. That desire on my part has hitherto been unhappily frustrated, by the determination of the enemy to enter on a separate negotiation, in which it was impossible for me to engage, consistently either with public faith, or with a due regard to the permanent security of Europe. "My anxiety for the speedy restoration of peace remains unaltered; and there will be no obstacles nor delay on my part to the adoption of such measures as may best tend to promote and accelerate that desire.

I further admit, that for all kinds of common faults, for affronts and disorders whereby the citizens and societies are injured, but without affecting the state or the sovereign, this regard is due to a character so necessary for the correspondence of nations, and to the dignity of the prince represented, that the conduct of his minister should be complained of to him, and reparation demanded; and if nothing can be obtained, and the importance of his bulls absolutely requires that a stop may be put to them, not to carry resentment beyond an abrupt dismission. But shall an ambassador with impunity cabal against the state where he resides, plot its ruin, stir up the subjects to revolt, and confidently foment the most dangerous conspiracies? Under the assurance of being supported by his master, if he behaves as an enemy, shall it not be allowable to treat him as such? The case is unquestionable with regard to an ambassador who takes arms and uses violence; for those whom he mocks may repel him, self-defence being part of the law of nature. Those Roman ambassadors who, being sent to the Gauls, fought against them with the people of Clusium, divested themselves of their character. Is it to be thought that in battle the Gauls were to spare them? - (Montesquieu's Works, Lib. -)

The critical circumstances in which the colonies of Spain in South America are placed, and the dispositions some of them have evinced, as well as the advances they have made to strengthen their independence by an alliance with the United States, are important auxiliaries at this moment to the lure which the French Emperor offers to your government. France has lost all her colonies, but she would doubtless prefer having the produce and the articles she wants through the medium of another, to that of the only power now independent of her control. Our orders in council were advised to be founded on the promulgation of the Berlin and Milan decrees; those being rescinded, we may expect the system will necessarily be given up, but much anxiety is naturally united in the question of the policy which our government may now adopt. Olivenza, on the frontiers of Portugal, is taken by the French, who sent in a flag, requesting the place to be given up, which was agreed to by the Portuguese, but as the French troops were marching in, some Frenchmen were blown up by the springing of a mine which was prepared for the purpose. The remainder of the French, who were exasperated to the highest degree, rushed forward, carried the place, and put every soul to death. They pursued their march to Chaves and Almeida, of which they possessed themselves, and put all the Portuguese in these three places to the sword." On these particulars we have no remarks to make—let the reader decide. Capt. Baxter, however, in a postscript to his Journal, says, "there is no doubt but the above may be depended on." “Sir, I have been informed by Dr. Cooper that you benevolently intend to lend me your curious Hebrew M.S. and when you have so done, I shall celebrate, in the most grateful manner, your public spirit and generosity. I will take the utmost care of it, and it shall be returned in twelve months, to the person in London you appoint to receive it; and for this, I will give a note of hand, or (if you require it) a bond of obligation. You will receive of Dr. Cooper the present of a book of mine, in which I have published your kind intention; and I hope you will accept that book as a mark of the gratitude of, Sir, your highly obliged, and very humble servant, B. KENNICOTT. Oxford, March 4, 1770." “In May following my father received the following note: “Mr. Cooper’s compliments to Mr. Simpson, returns him Dr. Kennicott’s letter, which he did not send for yesterday, but only for his own, and hopes that Mr. Simpson’s determination on the point in question, viz. the transmission of the M.S. to England, is favorable to the grand scheme of collation, which the learned gentleman has now in hand. King’s College May 8, 1770.”

He d his look was across a spacious plain, which gave him sicker opportunity of minutely observing my movements. After some looking firing of the flanks to cover the deployments of the two armies, by nine o'clock in the morning the opposing fronts were warmly engaged, when the prowess of the rival nations seemed now fairly to be at trial before the world, and the supremacy was greatly and gloriously decided to be our own. The corps which formed the right of the advanced line, was the battalion of light infantry commanded by lieutenant colonel Kempt, consisting of the light companies of the 20th, 27th, 35th, 58th, 61st, 81st and Watteville's, together with 130 chosen battalion men of the 88th regiment, under major Robinson. Directly opposed to them was the favorite French regiment the 15th Legere. The two corps at the distance of about 100 yards fired reciprocally a few rounds, when, as is by mutual agreement, the firing was suspended and in close compact order and awful silence, they advanced towards each other, until their bayonets began to cross. At this momentous crisis the enemy became appalled. They broke and endeavored to fly, but it was too late; they were overtaken with the most dreadful slaughter.

A CORRESPONDENT. MR. JENKINS, I take the liberty through the medium of your paper to introduce Captain SAMUEL STEPHENSON as a candidate for the office of Register of Deeds. Among the numerous candidates for this office, no person is more deserving the patronage of his fellow-citizens than this gentleman. He possesses all the qualifications for the office in the highest degree; and, unable to endure the hardships and changes of climate to which marines are subjected, he has relinquished his former profession, and is at leisure to discharge the duties of this office. CUMBERLAND, March 21, 1814 MR. If this principle had been introduced into the constitution in express words it would have formed an unfortunate contrast to all other parts of the instrument; yet gentlemen make no difficulty in introducing that principle by construction, which would have appeared stupid and absurd if written in express words in the body of the instrument. But there is no such language in the constitution. Let us see what is the language of that instrument. "The judicial power of the U. States shall be vested in one supreme court, and such inferior courts as congress may from time to time ordain and establish." Here then instead of cautioning the legislature that a law for the organization of courts when passed, can never be repealed, it contains an invitation to a revision from time to time. It contains an intimation that the subject is new and difficult, and no injunction to ordain and establish your courts from time to time, according to the results which an experience of the system alone could suggest. 30th Attack on the Chesapeake—Federal Editors say this outrage can no longer be justly 'a subject of collision! Why? Because Bri tain disavowed the act—and we took self-rep aration at the instant! How? Why, forsooth, after our frigate was disabled, part of her crew murdered, and a part dragged away to execution by British myrmidons, British ships of war were interdicted our harbors! == This is "an indignity and a punishment" "which federalists think should fully atone for that atrocious outrage! The disavowal "of the act by Britain is a mere farce—she "tells us she does not approve the liberties taken by her officer—that he has "exceeded his in structions"—but stamps falsehood upon the protestation, by regularly promoting the hero of the tragedy to a vice admiralty and com mander in chief of her naval forces on a most important station—The stipulations, by her authorized agent, for redress and reparation for the indignity and injury, were also disa vowed; the true reason for which will be found in the momentary expectation raised by the struggles of Spain to avoid subjugation to France.The refusal of our government to pass over in silence an outrage un paralleled, save in the history of British na val prowess, is to the advocates for British supremacy indubitable proof of hostility to Britain, and partiality to France! 4th The Orders in Council.—The Repertory repeats the assertion so oft.

In not relinquishing the trade of the United States with enemies colonies. With respect to this point it is not necessary at this time to discuss the right of that trade. It is sufficient to remark, 1st. that as the trade is admitted to have become, in the view of G. Britain, of little practical importance, why has it been made a ground of the disavowal, and, especially, as important considerations only could upon principles of public law have justified a measure of so serious a character? 2nd. that as the colonial trade is a subject no wise concerned either with the orders in council or with the affair of the Chesapeake, why has it been permitted to frustrate an arrangement relating to those subjects; and to those only? 3rd. that as this condition is allowed to have originated in supposition, that it would be agreeable to the American government, why has it been persisted in after the error was made known by the representation of Mr. Erskine to his government that neither this nor the other conditions of dispatch of the 23rd January were attainable here? 4th. Another point in the dispatch, and not in the arrangement, is that the British navy might capture our trade to ports prohibited by the United States.

Mr. Gardenier said if Mr. Newiwon had added the Northern states would not abandon their commerce with Great Britain for light and trivial causes, he would be correct. I am willing, Sir, to take this argument of the gentleman with this limitation, that the commercial intercourse with Great Britain should not be surrendered or abandoned for light and trivial causes. I have said enough on the conduct of Great Britain towards the United States, to show whether their complaints arise from, and whether the acts of the government are founded on light and trivial causes. The view which I shall take of this subject and the facts connected with it notwithstanding the explanation, will still be the same. Permit me, Sir, to express my regret, that arguments on great national subjects, instead of being general and comprehensive, should be narrowed down and confined to local considerations. It is certainly improper and impolitic to cut the United States up into districts and slips, and to legislate for each division. This checkered patchwork sort of legislation comports not with the design of this government, nor with the interest of this nation.

Accused by impeachment, and for the Senate to judge and pronounce sentence. The President is not known or heard of in the business, he is even divested, in this instance, of the ordinary prerogative of pardoning. What then must we think of a chief magistrate who, because he has been disappointed of the destruction of a devoted victim of his vengeance, has dared to usurp the prerogative of the House of Representatives in accusing a judge whose prerogative he had before usurped by pronouncing on the guilt of a person accused of the highest crime against the state! If such enormities can be practiced with impunity it is worse than idle to talk of the security of our rights or the freedom of our government. — o — Inscribed on an Irish tombstone in Belturbet Churchyard, Ireland, is this inscription:— Here lies John Digley, whose wife and meister were drowned in their passage from America. Had they both lived, they would have been buried here.

Take away or diminish their compensations, It is to be observed that there is not one of these powers specified in the constitution, they are therefore necessary inferences from the paramount power, "to ordain and establish," and the power of repeal to take away all the services to be performed, is as necessary an inference, as either of the others, and has uniformly resulted from every other specified power in the constitution. From this part of the sentence, therefore, it is deducible; that the only restraint upon the general power given to congress in the first part of the section to ordain and establish courts, is that the compensations of the judges, should not be lessened during their continuance in office—and that during good behavior. And in this part of the sentence, the correct phraseology of the constitution is worthy of observation. In speaking of the executive attribute (to wit) the appointing and commissioning officer, the term good behavior is used, so speaking of the legislative attribute, (to wit) the creation of offices and fixing compensation, the term, during their continuance in office, is used. The reason for this variation of expression is obvious. It was known that the office might be discontinued and the judge continue to behave well, the limitation was.

On the 27th November 1796, the French Uommif fiuners at Cape Francois, authorized the capture of all Ameri can Vcffels bound to or from Livith ports. February 111, 1797, Commissioners at Cape Francois ordered the capture of all vessels, bound to any ports, in the West Indies, delivered up to the English, and all vessels, cleared out generally for the West Indies. In January 1798, France declared that the condition of ships in everything, which concerns their character as neutrals or enemies, shall be determined by their cargoes, and every vessel found at sea, laden in whole or in part with merchandise, coming out of England, or its possessions, shall be declared good prize, whoever may be the proprietors of such commodi ties or merchandise, and this Decree is founded expressly on the Ordonnance of 1704. It is therefore an incontrovertible truth that France, from the earliest time, and certainly prior to the year 1796, has adopted, on principle, and in practice, broader rules for limiting the commerce of neutrals, not condemning their property, and with fewer and shorter intervals of regard to their rights, than has been assumed by her enemy, and of course could on no ground be justified by the pretense that the decree of November 1796 and the subsequent one of January, condemning the property of neutrals, because the vessels had been spoken with by British cruisers, were a retaliation for similar violations of public law by her foe.

This building one of the most magnificent in Italy. It is solidly lined with polished black marble, mingled with the artificial darkness, which contrived with fire, impresses the firm of minds with awe, and disposes the lightest of the volatile, VGU. 16th 1804- D. O. JL Defunctorum animae in novem dies in requiem aeternam votton. This edifice was erected to enclose the holy handkerchief, with which our Saviour is fabled to have wiped his face, as he was bearing the cross to the place of execution. A writer upon this subject remarks, that he has found seven different churches, all of which claim the honour of possessing this valuable relic, but he gives the preference to the claims of the church at Turin, because it has fourteen bulls in its favour. The veneration in which this relic is held is astonishing. A citizen of Turin thinks it one of the most solid foundations of its superiority over its sister cities, and the sovereigns of Sardinia and Piedmont annually assisted at the ceremony of exhibiting this wonderful handkerchief to the people. The cathedral at Milan, whose noble architecture really merits the admiration of the world, also shut up one of these precious deposits, a real nail of the cross.

When that of D’Yrujo made none, and that of Genert very little—to me this is inconceivable. A gentleman from Rhode Island, (Mr. Potter), who made a very elegant speech, said that the whole dispute between the two great parties, was that of the ins and outs, a contest who should have the loaves and fishes. If it really be so, I have been very unfortunate, having been here a long time, and have not got either loaf or fish; I do not doubt, but the gentleman believes it as he stated, nor can I doubt, what he says concerning his own party, he knows them better than I do, and I therefore must believe him as to them. But, sir, the men who were in opposition in the year 1798, did not want places, and they afterwards proved it by their acts. As soon as they got into power, they put down four or five hundred sough places, by repealing the laws under which they existed; if those in power now possess the same principles with the opposition of the year 1798, they do not want places. To my mind the cause of the disavowal is as clear as the noon-day sun.

The bill reported by Mr. Adams "for the preservation of peace, and maintenance of the authority of the United States, in the ports, harbours and waters under their jurisdiction," after undergoing a slight amendment, was ordered to a third reading without a division. The question was taken on striking out the 5th section, which interdicts the entrance of all British armed vessels into the ports and waters of the U.S. and vests the President with power to take off the prohibition, whenever, in his opinion, due reparation shall be made—and lost—Yeas 3, Nays 26.

You could not expect, sir, when you presented Mr. Rutledge’s challenge to Mr. Ellery, that he would accept of it under those circumstances. You could not expect that Mr. Ellery, unless you considered him as wicked and unprincipled as yourself, would do an act out of the State of Rhode Island, which was contrary to its laws, and repugnant to the morality of its citizens. For he, sir, has some respect to the sentiments of his constituents, and does not think himself authorized to commit an act when without the jurisdiction of his state, which, were he within it, might, according to its consequences, either subject him to capital punishment, or oblige him to sit upon the gallows with a halter about his neck. He is not capable of the meanness of doing an act as soon as he goes beyond the limits of the state he has the honor to represent, which he dare not commit under the eye of the laws sanctioned by his constituents, and which they have enacted for the government of their own conduct. But you, sir, may suppose that opposition to popular opinion is an evidence of spirit, and a refusal to submit to the vulgar rules of common propriety is characteristic of independence. For on what other principle can we account for it that you consented to be the bearer of Mr. Rutledge’s challenge?

An amendment was offered by Mr. Dana, but afterwards withdrawn, to make way for one offered by Mr. Crownshield to the same effect; by which the resolution was amended to read, as follows: "Resolved, That it is expedient to authorize the President of the United States to cause such fortifications to be repaired and erected as will, with the assistance of gun-boats, etc." Mr. Thomas enquired as to filling the blank. Mr. Blount said he had stated while up before, that 300,000 dollars in time of peace was as much as could be expended in the course of a year. But if they could not enjoy peace, more would be necessary; and from the uncertainty of this, the committee wished to decline filling the blank at the present time. When the bill should be laid before them, they might be better qualified to judge. MR. GARDENIER'S SPEECH. “Gardenier was not so much opposed to the not filling up the blank in the resolution at this time as to the reason which had been given for not doing it, or in other words, the reasons why the House should not now debate on that part of the resolution on which it was most important to deliberate; the sum to be allowed. It was hardly necessary except in point of form, to have gone into committee of the whole for any other purpose than this."

One hundred dollars will be given to any person or persons, who will apprehend and secure, in any jail in the United States, JESSE MOORE, who absconded from Canton, Ohio, having in possession and carrying with him about four thousand dollars' worth of Store Goods, containing a very general assortment, the property of the subscriber. He will endeavor to effect sales on the Ohio or Mississippi rivers, or the places adjacent, as he purchased a keel boat at Steubenville, and descended the river on the 20th of December last. Moore is from the Eastern States, and may probably go to New-Orleans and take shipping for Boston, New-York, or some other eastern port. He is of a sedate turn and diffident, but of pleasing and insinuating manners—fair complexion, grey eyes, dark hair—about 24 years of age—about 5 feet 7 inches high—and has formerly studied Physic. Any attorney at law in the place where said Moore may be apprehended is requested to attach according to law, the goods or property which he may have and hold them until advice is sent, which will be promptly attended to and the necessary proofs and instructions will be immediately forwarded. EDWARD JONES, Pittsburgh, Feb. 17 1816. The Editor of the N F. Patriot is requested to insert the above and forward his bill to this office. April 9.

It has fallen to my extraordinary lot, to be the jailor of the instrument of the misery that Europe had so long endured, and you will believe me, when I say, that far from allowing him to think that I bear in mind any animosity towards him, from a recollection of what my country suffered, I endeavor by my attention to quiet his uneasy mind. It appears to me like a dream, when I look back 18 months and see all Europe prostrate at his feet, and he now my prisoner. It is a glorious finish to my services, and leaves me nothing to wish for. As Count Klam, Aid de camp to Prince Schwarzenberg will set off immediately for Paris, and takes charge of my letter, I have only time to tell you that on the 11th of April, the white flag was displayed at Marseilles by the inhabitants. Anxious to shake hands with my former enemies, but now friends, I pushed into the anchorage before the town, but not without some opposition from the military; a battery having opened its fire and struck us—This appeared to me such an act of treachery, that I opened my broadside, and in 10 minutes, silenced the fire. I now saw the inhabitants assembling on the ramparts, waving white handkerchiefs.

Others, who, after the paroxysm is over, recover for a short time the use of their reason, are engaged in making translations from the English and French into the Italian language. Besides this, many are occupied with music, others with husbandry, and various other works, but particularly so in a manufactory of woollen cloth. By these means, this miserable class of people, once an incumbrance to their families and the state, become useful to society, and put many idlers to the blush. This kind of treatment is introduced not only in the hospital for male patients, but also in that of women. M. Lingutti manages the whole establishment with wise economy. The table is always served with silver, and the whole house is handsomely fitted up; so that wherever the inhabitants turn their eyes, they find something to relieve the mind. Formerly the windows were secured with iron bars; but instead of this, there are now flowers curiously made of iron, and painted from nature, as if growing; which remove every idea of forced confinement. A handsome domain adjoining the hospital, serves for the recreation of the patients. FROM NILES' REGISTER. The French can only become more powerful in the proportion of new troops they send across the rivers; and to make up their daily losses, and to augment their armies to an amount equal to the expulsion of the British will require more troops than the present state of affairs will permit to be sent. A greater concentration of their forces from the interior of Spain, than what was effected in order to create an army for Soult, when he marched for the relief of Badajos, and also to strengthen Marmont, does not appear practicable. The French cannot leave the provinces destitute of armies. The people are kept down only by their presence, and their absence gives both men and succors to the numerous guerrillas by which they are surrounded. Soult is gone to take the command of the French army in Murcia. A part of his army has been sent to reinforce Victor before Cadiz. Bessieres has detached 2000 men to Zamora, and is also moving on Salamanca, in consequence of Don Julian Sanchez having threatened that city. Bessieres gives out that he is advancing with his main body against Portugal. Two divisions of Lord Wellington's army, the 2nd and 3rd, are left under Generals Hill, and Picton, on the Caya, near Elvas. The rest of the army has proceeded to the North. General Spencer has left the army, and is on his way to England.

Land for Sale A tract or parcel of land, containing between two and three hundred acres, on the north side of Occoquan, in Fairfax county, about 5 miles west of Occoquan mills. It is well watered with never failing springs, it is also well timbered, and the great quantity of fur timber on it renders it the more valuable to any person in the coopering line. The land is of the middle soil, and produces corn, wheat or any other grain tolerably well— there is about 100 acres cleared and under good fence. The improvements are a log dwelling house & other out houses, and a good apple orchard of about sixty trees. ALSO, One other tract in the same county, within three miles of Occoquan, lying on Sandy Run, containing 47 acres—one half in cultivation and under good fence, the remainder heavily timbered. There are on the premises an old framed dwelling house and out houses and an apple orchard of eighty bearing trees. Any person inclining to purchase can view the premises and know the terms by applying to the subscriber who lives near them. Thomas Gossom. November 30th, 1800.

On the 9th and 10th March, there was a massacre at Cadiz. It appeared that Gen. Freyre revisited Cadiz on the 8th, when the people called for the Constitution. He promised it should be proclaimed on the 9th; on which day the inhabitants being assembled in a large public square to hear it, the troops of the garrison (who had been joined by a number of deserters from the army) fired upon them, and killed between 700 and 800, and wounded from 1100 to 1200! This, of course, created great sensation in Spain; but when the Red-Rover sailed, all was quiet. The Constitution was eventually proclaimed in Cadiz on the 9th March, in the evening. A plot to effect a counter-revolution was discovered in Madrid on the 11th March, and several persons implicated in it had been arrested and imprisoned. The Duke del Infantado had resigned all his employments. We add with pleasure, that the following Americans, who have been imprisoned in Spain nearly three years, have been liberated, viz: Messrs. Conklin, Thompson, Weston, Cochran, Smith, Fouché, Cushing, Lapham, Wyman, Milner, Payne, and Hyer. D. S. Hackley, Esq. bearer of despatches for government, from Mr. Forsyth, at Madrid, came passenger in the E. F. [Translation from Spanish papers] OFFICIAL ARTICLE. MADRID, MARCH 9.

A reason for the Government’s wanting 11 millions of dollars. It is calculated that the expense of our military establishment for the ensuing year, without taking into consideration the question of war, will exceed TWENTY MILLIONS of dollars. Supposing the subscription for the loan of eleven millions should get filled up, where are the other 9 millions to come from? A late act of Congress furnishes the answer, viz. By a tax on Lands, a tax on Carriages, a tax on distilled spirits, a tax on refined sugar, a tax on salt, a tax on stamps and on tavern licenses and retailers.

I object now, said Mr. K. as I have before done, to this amendment, because it declares that, in the admission of Missouri, the Senate have not considered, and do not pronounce any opinion, concerning the clause of the Missouri constitution which makes it the duty of the legislature thereof to pass laws to exclude free negroes and mulattoes from coming to, and settling in, Missouri. This declaration ought not to be made, because it would exhibit the Senate in this singular situation, (if his construction of the constitution of Missouri were correct) that, in passing the act of admission, the Senate or its to consider and to allow its due weight to the only provision on that constitution upon which the obligation to admit, or not admit, Missouri depends. Mr. K. said he considered this proposition of much more importance than the mover of it appeared to do; and, he was not willing to decide on it instanter at any rate. Mr. Eaton replied at some length. The Senate then divided on the amendment, and there rose in its favor 23 members, and it Was agreed to. The question then being on ordering the resolution to a third reading, as amended. Mr. Morrill, of N. H. rose and delivered a speech of nearly two hours' length against the resolution.

She left the ship on Sunday night, and anchored at the mouth of Spermaceti Cove; and during the gale, she cut her cables and stood for Amboy. When the wind shifted to the westward, she changed her course, and with much difficulty, reached the Quarantine dock last evening. By the arrival of this vessel, we learn that considerable damage was sustained yesterday at the Quarantine. The bridges of the public store dock were carried away, and the stone wall on the bank was undermined and overthrown by the violence of the sea. The bridge of the steam-boat dock was also destroyed; and the steamboat Nautilus unshipped her rudder, broke her wheels, and sustained other injury. The brig Newbern, Capt. Cushing, of Philadelphia, from St. Barts, via Turks Island, came inside the Hook on Sunday at 4 o’clock P. M. and arrived at the Quarantine dock at 7 in the evening. While lying at the dock yesterday, at 12 o’clock, the fast slipped off the head of the post, and she grounded, lost her rudder, carried away the main boom, and stove the quarter-board—N. Immediately after the cession of 1762-3, Great Britain took possession of all the country on the east of the Mississippi, except only the town and island of New-Orleans, and, in the year 1763 or 4, erected old Florida, Pensacola, the river and port of Mobile, etc. into two distinct provinces, under the name of East and West Florida, names which they have borne ever since. In 1783, at the close of our revolutionary war, Great-Britain ceded to Spain East and West Florida, which, from that period to the present time, have been held by Spain under those names, as separate provinces from Louisiana. In the year 1800, when Spain was in possession of East and West Florida and Louisiana, as three several and distinct provinces, the famous treaty of St. Ildefonso was concluded, whereby Spain "retrocedes to France the colony or province of Louisiana, with the same extent that it now has in the hands of Spain, and that it had when France possessed it; and such as it should be after the treaties subsequently entered into between Spain and other states." This treaty likewise has not been published but the part referred to is cited in the treaty between the United States and France of the 30th of April, 1803, whereby France cedes to the United States, Louisiana, as fully and in the same manner as she acquired it of Spain, by the treaty of St. Ildefonso.

Our carriage was then stopped, and ladies came forward begging to be permitted to shake hands— we were soon almost suffocated with kisses. We then made a speech, which was cheered by the loudest huzzas from immense crowds of people. At Church, at Concert, the Opera, all places were alike, you could hear nothing but Vivent les Anglois, Vive Louis dix huit. When I entered the Opera, in the evening, they huzzahed for half an hour. I harangued them and called out, everlasting peace and friendship with our brothers, the French; they called my ideas sublime, and cheered me with the loudest acclamations. What a nation! Now for Napoleon. On the 25th, Col. Campbell drove into Marseilles, being commissioned by Lord Castlereagh to attend Napoleon; he said he came by the express wish of Napoleon himself; to request I would go round to St. Tropez, where it was intended he should embark, as he did not consider himself safe on board a French frigate, which had been ordered by the French Government to convey him to Elba. Next day I arrived at Tropez, but found that he had altered his route, and was at Frejus; (it was at Frejus he landed on his return from Egypt.) At 1 o’clock I arrived, and was introduced to the Russian Commissioner, Count Shouvaloff, the Austrian General Koeller, Prussian Count Truxoq, English Colonel Campbell, and Count Klatn. Soon after my arrival.

The taxes imposed on lands, by this law, are as follows:— Lands of the 1st quality and class, £2 per 100 acres, or £3.20 per quarter section. Lands of the 2nd quality and class, £1.50 per 100 acres, or £2.40 per quarter section. Lands of the 3rd quality and class, £1 per 100 acres, or £1.60 per quarter section. This tax is required to be paid by non-residents to the state treasurer, on or before the first day of October in each year. A failure either in entering his land with the Auditor by the 1st of August, or in paying the tax to the Treasurer by the 1st of October, subjects the owner to the payment of three times the amount of the tax, and in such case his lands are taxed as being of the first quality. This will make the tax amount to £6 per 100 acres, or £9.60 per quarter section—which sum, together with the sheriff’s fees and costs of advertising, may be paid to the sheriff authorized to sell the land, before or at the time appointed for such sale.

It is 328 years since the first discovery of North America, by John Cabot; 236 since it was more perfectly explored by Sir Walter Raleigh, when an attempt, which proved abortive, was made to establish a settlement in Virginia; 210 since the first permanent colony was planted in Virginia; 208 since the settlement of New-Amsterdam, now New York, 200 years, 30th of December next, since the progenitors of New-England landed at Plymouth, in Massachusetts; 44 years since the commencement of our national existence, and 31 years since the adoption of our present system of national government. It must afford the most pleasing contemplation, that, notwithstanding the bloody wars in which the first settlers of this country were involved, both with their savage neighbors, and different European nations; yet, in the short period of 210 years, from a small number of persecuted exiles, aided by subsequent emigrations from the parent country have sprung (as we doubt not the result of the approaching census will announce) the vast number of more than ten million souls; constituting 24 independent, confederated states, exclusive of 2 territorial governments, and comprising a greater extent of territory than is united under any one form of government on earth, the Russian empire excepted. In whatever point of view we survey our country, we may behold the most alluring prospects. Its local situation is the most pleasing and delightsome.

He further says, the general average depth of corn ground, as bro ken up and planted, is about three and a half to four inches; and that one week's hot sun, after the ground be comes droughty, will dry the ground and scorch the corn, so as to stop its growth. But to plough up your ground eight inches deep, your corn will stand growing a three week's drought, and if you plough twelve or fourteen inches deep, it will grow every day during a six or seven week's drought. I have made an experiment, and I think with success, and have saved a large portion of the labour of deep ploughing, and gained some bits of advan tages. I break up and strike out my corn ground in the old usual way ? the furrow I plant in, I run a single coulter ten or eleven inches deep in the centre of the furrow, and plant on that mark. ? When my corn is up, I run a harrow one round in each corn row, and make my coulter plough follow in the same furrow as deep as formerly. Thus every corn row has three coulter furrows, fourteen or fifteen inches deep around the whole. The Doctor asserts, that corn roots run no deeper than you break and cul tivate your ground, and this gives pas ture for the roots.

R P N, PRINTER Mr. Calhoun’s Remarks AT THE LATE MEETING ON SLAVERY. (Concluded from our last) National policy forbids the extension of slavery. Look down through the vista of suffering — see one of the vast Territory of Missouri perhapst five or six new states erected — and a population (perhaps half slaves) exceeding that of the eastern and middle states, spread over them. What would be the moral and political conse quences! The millions that would inhabit them would be indolent, feeble, ignorant and enslaved. The morals of the people would be depraved. The history of nations, where slavery has been tolerated, proves that these are its effects. In war, could the government derive strength and support from such a population?

V. V. Daily advertiser, 21st. A small work has recently been published in this city, by Messrs. Wmkle & Wiley, entitled "Memoirs of a French Lady, written by herself, from St. Louis." This work excited considerable attention in England when it first made its appearance there. The question—whether it is the product of Bonaparte himself or of some other hand—has, as far as we have seen, been left to the judgment of individuals and must probably be decided in a great measure upon internal evidence. PjÉC; this book be genuine or spurious is a singular performance. We do not so much to the character of its style and composition, as to the sentiments it contains, and the motives which it ascribes, have influenced and governed the hero of it. If it could be proved to be genuine, we should consider it as a very important production, because it would satisfy so many interesting curiosities relative to the character and conduct of a man who, for fifteen years, has commanded a greater share of public attention than all others on the human theatre. His extraordinary system is exhibited in all its energy.

M'Ilheny, arrived here this morning from Wilmington, N. C. informs us that the brig Juno, arrived at that port on Saturday last, in a short passage from Aux Cayes, bringing the important information that a rebellion had broken out against the government of King Henri, (Christophe,) who was marching to attack Geo. Boyer, when his troops revolted, seized upon their commander, and cut off his head, which was placed upon a pole and exhibited at various places; after which, the troops went over to Gen. Boyer, the rival chieftain of Hayti. A bloody civil war was still raging in various parts of the Island when the Juno sailed but it was expected that both sections of the Republic, which were formerly under the separate commands of Christophe and Petion, would now be united, under one head, in the person of General Boyer.

The Farmer is published weekly, on a sheet the size of a large newspaper, and folded so as to make eight pages—and to admit of being conveniently bound up and preserved in volumes. Each volume will consist of fifty-two numbers, a title page, and an index—and numerous engravings to represent new implements, and approved systems of husbandry. Each number gives a true and accurate statement of the then selling prices of country produce, live stock, and all the principal articles brought for sale in the Baltimore market. Terms of subscription $4 per annum, to be paid in advance. But for the sum of Five Dollars, the actual receipt of every number is guaranteed.—That is, when numbers fail to come to hand, duplicates shall be sent until every number shall have been received.

Monday, July 1, 1816. Extract of a letter from the American Consul, dated St Pierre, Martinique, May 26, 1816: "I take the liberty to advise you of the change which has taken place in this market in the price of flour; owing chiefly to the demand for the different islands near this, most if not all being in want. For the last three months we only have two cargoes from the United States. They have sold at $11 per bbl. and $5.25 per bbl. for corn. The first arrivals will obtain the same or possibly $12 for flour, in the road for exportation. Our fresh flour would command more was it permitted for consumption, for good flour is very scarce, and even of bad the supply is very short, and if we have no arrivals soon from France, American flour must be admitted for consumption. Fresh French flour retails at $20, old at $16 and none in first hands. The first cargo goes from the U. States will command $14 up. I think our flour must soon be admitted here for the consumption of the island. “The regulation of 1784 being in force will cramp our commerce. At present salt provisions and naval stores are a glut here, and no sales."

The editors of the Gazette have only time to notice the following: Much is said in the French papers relative to the late conspiracy in Sweden, which it appears, grew out of disaffection to the present rulers, amongst which are a great number of the nobility and persons in the higher ranks. The mischief threatened was prevented by a timely discovery— troops surrounded the capital, and the hydra crushed at its vein. Hu elle vaut kim- md'phes, was said envy to this plot. There was a report in Paris that the Chambers of Peers was to be dissolved—the report is treated as idle by the Paris-Messenger of the 24th ultimo. The princess of Wales had left Vienna, for the Lake of Como. She moved incog, and permitted but few to see her. The French officers have subscribed for a monument to the memory of Marshal Massena. A steam boat arrived at Hamburg from Berlin, in 13 hours, 72 leagues. It was to ply constantly between the two places. The count de Blacas, ambassador from France to the Holy See, arrived in Paris yesterday. It is thought his journey has some affairs of negotiation in view from the court of Rome. The first column of Prussian troops returning from France, has passed by Coblentz, and the first Austrian column by Ratisbon. French funds—5 per cent. 97f. Ipe. —Actions of Bank 1300f. Oe.—Exchange on London 2f 70e.

A letter from a very respectable gentleman in Bordeaux, to his correspondent in this city, dated the 2nd March, says, “I have just heard that your Plenipotentiaries (American) have been met at Gothenburg, by those of England, and it is expected a peace will be the result.” Another letter of the 5th of March, says, “Lord Wellington's army had a battle with Soult’s, at Orthes, on the 28th of February, in which the latter was defeated with the loss of 6000 men.” British fleet out, on Lake Champlain. A gentleman arrived in town last evening, who left Vergennes on Tuesday last, and informs us that Lake Champlain is free from ice, and that on Monday last the British fleet (9 sail) made their appearance off Burlington, and on Tuesday were about ten miles below that place, standing for Vergennes. That about a thousand militia had assembled at Vergennes.

The other appropriations in the bill were then agreed to, until the house came to the appropriation for fortifications; for which object the committee of ways and means proposed the sum of eight hundred thousand dollars. Mr. Smith of Md. explained the views of the War Department, and of the committee of Ways and Means, on this subject. The officers having charge of that branch of the service had recommended the appropriation of a considerably larger amount, as being necessary under existing contracts; but the committee of ways and means was of opinion that a part of the proposed amount might be dispensed with without great prejudice to the service, and had so reported. This item gave rise to some brief debate, which had not terminated when a motion was made for the committee to rise (about 4 o’clock) and agreed to. When the house adjourned. Thursday, March 9. They filled a large pair of saddle bags and a pocket handkerchief with letters, and then took to their heels. We understand that on examining the mail at our post-office, there appears to be two Philadelphia packages of unpaid letters for New York missing, and probably many single packages from south of Philadelphia were taken out. This is the second time that the mail has been robbed within a short time, and unless the government will furnish a guard for its protection, there is no security in trusting money in it. Since the above was written, we learn that one of the passengers had in his charge $33,000 in bills for one of our banks. As soon as he discovered the unpleasant predicament he was placed in, he contrived to slip the package out of his pocket onto the floor of the coach, and covered it over with straw, by which means he saved it. When they searched him, all they found in his pockets were a few shillings in silver.

A fierce division was to follow them in a few Apostate Federalists are all the rage at present with the democratic party, whilst the demos of the old school and of long Standing are completely hustled out of place and confidence by interlopers composed of disappointed, and of federalists. Look at the President and Secretary of the caucus for nominating democratic candidates for Governor and Lt. Governor, and the mouthpiece of the party in the Senate of this State. Look at the Collectors and Tax gatherers, together with a thousand office holders, Custom House pimps and spies and army loungers scattered up and down in every town and village, and who live on the bread drawn from the labor of the Farmer and Mechanic. These are the blessings which the people are reaping at the hands of Democratic Rulers. Fellow citizens, if you like these times and these measures, cease for a moment in laboring to promote the elections of the War Candidates, that you may experience taxes and poverty to your heart's content. But when the License and Land Tax Collector comes, do not complain at being compelled to pay the uttermost farthing, even if it obliges you to part with your last Cow or Last farthing and thereby your children should rendered destitute of food and raiment. He is a mulatto fellow, about 30 years of age, 5 feet 10 inches high; is remarkable on account of having red curly hair and grey eyes which generally appeal to be fore; one of his legs somewhat shorter than the other, though scarcely to be perceived without nice observation; when stamping, is very apt to stand fast on his right leg, and rather extend the left. In pronouncing the word whiskey, which he is very fond of, and apt to call for at a public house, he pronounces it whiskey, also biscuit, inlet; waistcoat, weskit. He is a very humble, obedient fellow, and when spoken to, has a down look. It is not improbable that he may obtain free papers, to endeavor to pass as a free man, having absconded from his boat in the basin at Richmond, with about $150 in cash. I will give the above reward, together with all reasonable expenses, to any person who will deliver him to me, on my farm in Albemarle county near Scott’s Landing, or secure him in any jail so that I get him again. MARRIED, On Sunday last, by the Rev. Dr. Uzal Ogden, Mr. Isaac G. Ogden, of New-York, to Miss Margaret W. Darton, of this town. Astronomical Lectures. "MR. DAVID YOUNG proposes to deliver Four Lectures on the science of ASTRONOMY, in the School room of Mr. Jabias Ross, in the Adelphian Academy, in this town; in which, among other things, will be shown the figure and motion of the Earth, together with those of the Celestial Bodies; the causes of the various phenomena of the visible heavens; the method by which the distances and magnitudes of the planets have been determined, and the motion of light ascertained; the causes of the ebbing and flowing of the sea, and the reason why there are two flows and two ebbs in one lunar day, etc. He will attend every Tuesday evening at the room above mentioned, for four weeks, commencing THIS EVENING, at half past 6 o’clock, at which time and place, all who may feel themselves interested, whether Gentlemen or Ladies, are respectfully requested to attend. Admittance, 12 1/2 cents per evening. Eliz. Town, Oct. 31, 1820. CONCLUDING EVENING.

The Supreme Court of the United States commenced its Session in Washington today. Among the decisions expected to take place at this term, perhaps there are none which has excited more interest in this part of the country than the question respecting Dartmouth College, which was argued last term, and continued for advisement. A decision in this case may be expected among the first acts. We hope it will afford a illustration of Justice triumphing over party. We understand a Post-Office has lately been established in the town of Brooks, in the County of Hancock, and that Phineas Ashmun, is appointed Post Master. Mr. Clay's Speech.—A correspondent at Washington informs us, says the editor of the New-York Herald that Mr. Clay spoke more than two hours, in the house of representatives last Wednesday, on Gen. Jackson’s conduct in taking Pensacola and executing Arbuthnot and Ambrister—That he was very temperate and discreet, and singularly happy in manner and in selection of his topics, and part of the time was taken up in answering interrogations made the preceding day by Mr. Whitman of Massachusetts. He alternately wielded the weapons of wit and argument, and left his antagonist stripped, wounded, and disarmed. It is a matter of most perfect astonishment, that so important an article should have been not only so perfectly and wantonly abandoned by the present tariff, but pointedly selected for reprobation by a strange policy— which, whilst it raised the duties on most other articles, reduced that on iron nearly 100 per cent.—From 1804 until 1815, it was at seventeen and a half per cent, and until 1816 at 15—a duty which might have saved these interesting establishments thus apparently destroyed by design. Pigs and castings in 1816, paid fifteen per cent, ad valorem; bar iron nine dollars a ton, equal to, say nine per cent, ad valorem; in 1818 the duties were increased to fifty cents a hundred on pigs, seventy-five on castings and bar iron: in this house it was raised to twenty dollars a ton by a majority of forty-seven, but reduced in the senate to fifteen. Had the duty been a proportionate one in 1816, a rate lower than the one now proposed would have been sufficient to have insured a domestic supply; but the reports of the treasury present us with facts which call for immediate and efficient interference. In 1818 the importation of bar iron exceeded sixteen thousand tons—in 1819 it amounted to near twenty thousand. The decrease of ad valorem importations in this year has exceeded $19,000,000, while the increase of bar iron has been near four thousand tons.

Mr. Kinney, from the committee on the letter of the Treasurer relative to the School Fund, reported, that they have examined the laws on the subject referred to, and in their opinion the Trustees of that fund are entitled to one-tenth of the annual taxes commencing with the year 1817, and as the Treasurer has requested instructions on the subject, the committee recommend the following resolution: Resolved, That the Treasurer be instructed to pass to the credit of the Trustees of the School Fund one-tenth part of all monies raised by tax for the use of the state since the year 1817, and to continue that credit on the annual taxes in future. It afforded great satisfaction to see the venerable John Gilman, aged 122 years, appear at the Poll on Monday and give his vote for the patriot and statesman CALEB STRONG. Augusta (Maine) Herald From the Connecticut Courant. * DAME EXPERIENCE. Experience has been schooling mankind constantly for thousands of years. Some few who are docile and sharp-sighted, she gently leads by the hand, or points them onward with her finger; so that they escape the traps and snares which are laid for their feet in the path of life. Others, who are forward and purblind, she whips unmercifully; yet the greater part of them grow never the wiser either for her lessons or her lashes. All history is as it were one great chart drawn by the hand of experience; on the which are distinctly marked down, the numerous eddies, rocks, shoals and quicksands, that are met with in the voyage of life. We need but look over this chart, or map, carefully, and we see how some who began the voyage of life prosperously, have been dashed against this rock, and others swallowed up in that whirlpool; how some have plunged headlong into this particular pitfall, and others have been caught in that particular snare—And thus we might profit from the disasters of those who have gone before us, and by shunning their course, might avoid their ruin.

Gentlemen, it was by one of those accidents which so often accompany and occasion the development of guilt, that we have arrived at this evidence. It was very natural that she did not wish to reveal it; very natural that she did not wish either to expose her mistress, or afflict her unconscious master with the recital; very natural that she did not desire to be the instrument of so frightful a discovery. However, when she found that concealment was out of the question; that this action was actually in progress, and that the guilty delinquent was publicly triumphing in the absence of proof, and through an herd of slanderous dependents, cruelly vilifying the character of his victim; she sent a friend to Mr. Browne, and in his presence and that of two others, solemnly discovered her melancholy information. Gentlemen, I do entreat of you to examine this woman, though she is an uneducated peasant, with all severity, because, if she speaks the truth, I think you will agree with me, that so horrible a complication of iniquity never disgraced the annals of a court of justice. He had just risen from the table of his friend—he left his own brother and that friend behind him, and even from the very beard of his hospitality, he proceeded to the defilement of his bed! Of mere adultery I had heard before.

When your Excellency is pleased to terrify the inhabitants of Boston (for assembling to oppose your election), by threatening to remove the seat of government, you say, that “ It is remarkable likewise that such have “ been the politics of almost every metropolis of our Sister States as to have driven “ from it the State Legislature—the reasons “ assigned have been the influx of foreign “ ers and a collection of the discontented of the National and State Gov “ ernments *’  Now your Excellency knows, that to make an assertion with the expectation of being believed, and with intent to prejudice the Country gentlemen against the Metropolis, which you did not know to be true, is as inexcusable as to assert what you know to be false.—  Now for the facts. Did the Legislature of New-Hampshire remove from Portsmouth to Concord on account of the politics of the former place ? Was that town filled with malcontents against the National and State Governments ? Was that the reason of the removal of the New-York legislature to Albany? On the other hand do you not know that until this year New-York has harmonized with its democratic rulers, but Albany, to which they removed, is opposed to democracy and to our present national rulers ? Is not the same true of Pennsylvania?

The king of Saxony is said to have been repeatedly invited to consent to give up the fortresses of Wittenburg and Torgau, on the right bank of the Elbe, including the two Lusatias, the south part of which is to fall to the side of Austria, the territory extending from Torgau as far as the borders of the Saale, with the exception of Leipzig and the possessions of the king of Saxony in Thuringia. If he should persist in refusing to consent to this arrangement, they will carry it into execution without him. PARIS PAPERS Vienna, Dec. 6. The Duke of Wellington appeared on the 2nd on the redoubts, where above 7000 people were assembled to greet him. He was plainly attired, and in company with Lord Stewart, with a masked lady hanging at his arm, supposed to be either Lady Castlereagh or Lady Cathcart. — The crowd pressed so closely upon him that he requested a little room for the lady to pass — The arrival of this heroic officer has caused a lively sensation, and to it is attributed the improved state of our exchange. Lord Castlereagh will set off for London on the 10th. Everything will probably be settled by that time. Trade is said to be quite at a stand. The preparations for the departure of the sovereigns are going forward with redoubled activity.

That the love of gaming has a foundation in avarice is an undoubted truth; that it proceeds from a species of covetousness, differing from every other. Sallust, in his character of Cataline, has given us an exact definition of it: "Alii am apertens, sui profusus; profuse of his own, greedy of the property of another." The destructive consequences of this vice will be evinced by the melancholy history of Lysander. This unfortunate youth was the only son of Hortensius, a gentleman of high worth, who with a paternal eye watched over his education, and suffered no means to be neglected, which might promote his future usefulness, honor and happiness. Under such tuition he grew up improving in every amiable accomplishment."

A Farm for Sale, The subscriber offers for sale the farm on which he now resides, situated in Mount Vernon Prairie, (Marine settlement) near Silver creek, Madison county, Illinois; 22 miles north-east of St. Louis, and 9 miles from Edwardsville. It consists of four quarter sections of first-rate prairie, and two quarter sections of timber. The improvements are a dwelling house, kitchen, stables, etc.; a never-failing well of excellent water; a good horse-mill; a distillery, and about 100 acres under cultivation. This property is susceptible of division, and would make two or three good farms; and will be sold entire, or divided to suit purchasers. The situation is high and healthy, and in no respect inferior to any in the state.

LONDON, OCT. 20. The Queen &c. —Yesterday, in the House of Lords, was consumed almost wholly in technical arguments about the manner of getting at the conspiracy against the Queen, of which every man in England has long had a moral conviction. Witness after witness having deposed to facts clearly proving it; some of which witnesses are unimpeached and unimpeachable, as the builder, Giuseppe Carolini, and Pomi. The counsel for the Queen then were, at least, not allowed to interrogate a witness named Maoni, respecting certain acts of subordination, of which one Zangla, a person not produced, might it was supposed, by Maoni’s evidence, have been proved guilty. The hardship of this case is peculiar—We are not now reasoning on the law; respecting which the House has no doubt decided justly; but the hardship of the case, we still say, is peculiar.

Trial for Piracy.—At a special Court of the United States now sitting in this city, their Honors Judges Johnson and Drayton presiding, Henry Roberts, alias De Wolf, was yesterday found guilty of Piracy. He was carpenter on board the piratical ship Louisa, commanded first by Almeida and afterwards by George Clarke, and was on board when the Asia, of New-York, was robbed, near the Isle of Bonavista. Clarke is now in confinement, having been convicted of the same crime. - Charleston Times. LOUISVILLE, FEB. 16. Fire.—A fire occurred in Boston on the morning of the 26th ult. in which the Printing-Office of Messrs. Truk & Weston, publishers of the Boston Yankee and Christian Watchman, with their Books of Accounts, and upwards of $500 worth of paper were consumed. Inhuman Conduct.—Captain Winslow, of the schr. Sisters, from Havana, states, that on Saturday night while on soundings standing in for the land, in company with the brig Trader, from St. Salvador, he discovered a light at 11 o'clock, which he supposed to be Sandy Hook light, and shortly after he saw two more lights which are made in coming into Sandy Hook. At 2, when close in, he saw the breakers, and was only enabled to tack ship and stand off. At 4 o'clock the lights disappeared entirely, and at daylight men were seen on the beach. Capt. Winslow is confident that they were arranged for the purpose of decoying vessels on shore. The lights were seen 18 miles south of Sandy Hook.

Ten, fifteen, and twenty guineas have been offered for seats. The Elephant.— The Montreal Courant states, that last Sunday morning at about 1 o’clock, A. M. he was led to the lady Sherbrooke steam-boat, for the purpose of being embarked for Quebec; but apprehending some disaster from the narrowness of the pontoon thrown from the shore to the vessel, (which had been constructed of double the ordinary thickness, in order to bear his great weight) he obstinately stood still, and neither force nor persuasion could induce him to advance. Once or twice indeed, he put one of his fore feet on the pontoon, apparently to ascertain whether he could trust himself to it; but seeming to be convinced of the contrary, as often receded. Another attempt was made about two hours after, but with no better success. Irritated by the strokes employed to compel him, he took up with his proboscis stones and dust and flung them at his conductors.

The conduct of Spain, during the war of our revolution, was calculated to make a lasting impression on her colonies. This result was then foreseen by intelligent politicians; many were surprised that she could be so blind to her own interests, after she had, on one occasion, manifested the strongest suspicion of Paraguay; for to her scrupulous jealousy of this power, the expulsion of the Jesuits from that country, in 1750, is to be attributed. The wars that arose from the French revolution have produced in Europe changes of the greatest magnitude, which have had an immense influence on the affairs of South America. When Spain joined France against the combined princes, she exposed her distant possessions to British hostilities. The great naval power of England gave her ready access to the American colonies. Engaged in an arduous contest, she was prompted by her feelings and interest to retaliate on Spain the conduct she experienced from her during the war of our independence. Encouraged, perhaps, by the counsels of her enemies, the first symptoms of insurrection in the continental possessions of Spain were exhibited in the year 1797, in Venezuela.

This will be the last pledge of our usefulness, and the best se curity of our happiness. In 50 days after leaving Boston, we reached Malta, tarried there 17 days, and came thence to this place in 6 days, arrived here today; of course I cannot tell you much about our prospects here. What information we gained at Malta was highly favorable and encouraging. This place was once blessed with a church which was highly commended by the Saviour. The same Gospel may triumph again. We must not be impatient, however, if a considerable time elapse before we see much fruit of our labors; and the friends and patrons of missions will not be wearied in praying and contributing, if the seed lie some time before it grows. CONNECTICUT. Extract from Gov. Wolcott's Speech. The legislature of Connecticut met in New-Haven on the 5th inst. for the first time under the new constitution. LOST from Fairfield, in the county of Frederick and Commonwealth of Virginia, on or about the 7th day of March last, a negro slave named ANTHONY the property of the late William B. Page deceased. Anthony is about 5 feet 9 inches in height, of a substantial form, a light yellow complexion, and rather of an effeminate voice. He is about 28 years of age, was brought up a house servant and has been accustomed to that course of employment all his life. He is a slave of great capacity and considerable ingenuity—he reads—he plays the fiddle—and possesses a great mechanical turn—he can do rough carpenters' work and make combs. The above reward will be given if Anthony should be apprehended at the distance of 100 miles from Fairfield, and brought to the subscribers living in Alexandria. But if apprehended at the distance of less than 100 miles.
