MISCELLANY. EXTRACTS FROM A LONG TALK, Delivered in Philadelphia, for the Tammany Society, or Brethren of principle, at the celebration of their anniversary, on May 13, 1815, by Dr. Michael Leib. Brothers, let us ask ourselves this one question, are we in a better condition now as a people and a political party, than we were under the administration of President Ad ams? A little inquiry will demonstrate to us that we are not. It is true, that individu als who were in opposition to his adminis tration, and who call themselves republicans, have profited by the change; but has our country been lifted? Have our princi ples been improved? Has our Constitution sustained no rude shocks? Have those abuses which were then reprobated been corrected? Have the enormities complain ed of in former times been avoided or di minished? Has the purity and simplicity of republican manners been preserved or chastened by the example or the practice of the public agents? President Adams did not pretend to be the friend of a representative democracy. He had been too long at the court of St. James, to feel much reverence for the sa cred rights of the people. It was there that he renounced his revolutionary theories, and wrote that memorable satire, which he sarcastically stated to be a defence of the American Constitution!

To the Colonial Department, under date Feb. 14, 1809. See the remainder of the aforesaid letter. See document No. 1, herewith submitted. Extract of the official letter of Sir James Craig, signed by Mr. Ryland, dated Jan. 1809, accompanying Lord Liverpool’s dispatch. Most secret and confidential Quebec, 26th January, 1809. My dear sir—The extraordinary situation of things at this time in the neighboring States has suggested to the governor in chief the idea of employing you on a secret and confidential mission, provided an arrangement can be made to meet the important end in view, without throwing an absolute obstacle in the way of your professional pursuits. The information and political observations heretofore received from you were transmitted by His Excellency to the Secretary of State who has expressed his particular appreciation of them and there is no doubt that your able execution of such a mission as I have above suggested, would give you a claim not only on the governor General but on His Majesty's minister, which would eventually contribute to your advantage. At present it is only necessary for me to add that the Governor will furnish you with a cypher for carrying on your correspondence and in case the leading party in any of the States wish to open a communication with this government, their views might be communicated through you.

These persons soon became very intimate with the princess; but, in 1804, in consequence of some misunderstanding that took place, her royal highness dismissed Lady Douglas from her society. This proceeding so exasperated Sir John Douglas and his lady, that they immediately set to work to do the princess all the injury in their power, or which revenge could dictate. Sir John, in particular, waited upon the Dukes of Kent and Sussex, brothers to the present king, and informed them of some circumstances, connected with the conduct of the princess, injurious to her reputation. On investigating the matter, however, at that period, the royal brothers concluded not to mention any of the circumstances to the Prince of Wales, as they conceived them to "rest entirely on the misapprehension of both parties." Notwithstanding this resolution on the part of the two brothers, measures were taken, by some one, to make the prince acquainted with the stories which had been told about his wife; for, in November, 1805, an investigation of the matter was gone into by his authority. In December following, Lady Douglas and her husband gave in, as she stated, in consequence of commands to that purpose from the Prince of Wales, a written statement of facts, relative to the language and behavior of his wife, and particularly relative to the birth of a child, which she asserted the princess to have brought into the world in 1802.

It is credible; does it not rather exceed belief, that a single murmur should be heard, an unpleasant sensation indulged, because my soldier, who devoted the bloom and vigor of life to save his country from oppression, should receive from that country a small boon? Is it manly; is it generous; does it comport even with equity, to take from him this boon, and leave him nothing to show for his prowess and toils, but poverty, wretchedness, and scars? Let no such injustice, sir, stain your journals. Let it never be recorded by history's golden pen. Does it become those who, privileged with a seat within these magnificent walls, who behold the splendors of the capitol, who solace themselves in the elegant pleasures, the refined luxuries of the city, whose every sense is regaled with its brilliant scenes: does it become those who, by the courtesy of the people, are clothed with the robes of office, and by their bounty fare sumptuously every day: does it, sir, become such to grudge the plain morsel, the homely meal, to the war-worn soldier, by whose sufferings and blood they are enabled to participate in those elevated enjoyments? Honor, and every ennobling sensation of the generous mind, must recoil from the attempt.

This determined me at all hazards to enter—soon after the Mayor and the Municipality came off, forced by the people to apologize for the act of hostility, and until they were assured that I was satisfied with the apology, the town was in a state of insurrection. I immediately went on shore with Capt. Napier, of the Euryalus, under my orders, and we were received by upwards of 50,000 people, who literally carried us to the Town Hall, when a speech was made by one of the Municipality, after which we were carried to the Governor's, and with him and all the authorities, went to hear Te Deum chanted—after which we went in procession round the town, amidst shouts of the loudest joy and enthusiasm. Such a mixture of mad joy and melancholy was never before witnessed. I assure you I saw thousands of women with their hands clasped, extended to Heaven, bewailing the loss of husbands, brothers, sons, but partaking in the general joy of deliverance from a tyranny that cannot be conceived, much less described. When we returned to the Governor's the mob assembled round his house; he requested we would drive out in his carriage, to satisfy curiosity, which we did, and arrived at a part of this magnificent city, where none but royalty are allowed to enter in a carriage. The mob tore down the iron rails, and we drove in.

We have received a general bill of all the Christenings and Burials in the city of London, from Dec. 15, 1818, to Dec. 14, 1819, as reported to the King by the company of Parish Clerks. From this statement, there were christened between those periods:—males, 12,574; females, 11,726; in all, 21,300. Burials within the same period;—males, 9,671; females, 9,557; in all, 19,228; making 5,072 christenings more than burials. By the bill of mortality, among other complaints, we observe that 187 died of apoplexy and suddenly; 799 of asthma; 3,839 of consumption; 3,076 of convulsions; 1,244 of dropsy; but two of dysentery; 8 of St. Anthony’s fire; 1,150 of fever; 750 of whooping cough; 1,314 of inflammation; 240 of insanity; 81 of jaundice; 695 of measles; 399 of mortification; 1,860 of old age and debility; 202 of palsy; 712 of smallpox; 310 suddenly; 501 teething; 97 drowned; excessive drinking but four; killed by accidents 65; executed 28; suicides 36.

Curious Occurrence.—A few days ago, was shot near the entrance of Kilkenny harbour, a large sea fowl, having through its neck an arrow, such as those described by Captain Cook to be used by the natives of the islands of the Pacific Ocean. The shaft of the arrow, which is about eight inches long, is of a kind of wood resembling bone, and is rudely bearded with iron. The beard and shaft shot at least four inches through the neck; and the flesh round the shaft is not only healed, but perfectly hard and callous. ANECDOTE. During the administration of Sir Robert Walpole, the transportation of convicts to this country proved a very great grievance. Dr. Franklin wrote to the Minister the thanks of the colonists for the maternal care of Britain to the country, so strongly manifested in this instance; and as a satisfactory proof of American gratitude, sent him a collection of rattlesnakes, which he advised him to have introduced into his majesty’s gardens at Kew, in order that they might propagate and increase—assuring him that they would be as beneficial to his majesty’s English dominions as the British convicts were to America. Aphorism— A gentle reply to scurrilous language is the most severe revenge.

RAN away from the subscribers on the night of the 26th instant, a negro man named PETER, (commonly calls himself Peter John son,) of black complexion, about thirty years old, about five feet 9 or 10 inches high, stout made and clumsy in his motions; pretends to be religious, and can read a little. Has on one of his arms a deep scar, occasioned by a burn; when closely interrogated, stammers very much. His clothing was a dark brown cloth coat and pantaloons with gilt buttons; scarlet cassimere vest; shoes nearly new, the soles of which are fastened on with pegs. Also, a ne gro woman named ELLEN, about twenty-sev en years old, black complexion, of good size and well formed, answers very pleasantly when spoken to. Had a considerable quanti ty of clothing, particularly of fine articles.— Also, a negro woman named SYLVIA, of black complexion, about twenty-five years old, of good size, stout made, and pleasant counte nance; took with her many articles of clothing, particularly fine articles. Also, a negro boy, black complexion, about ten years old, named MARTIN, lisps much when speaking; had on a plain linsey roundabout and flesh-colored cassimere pantaloons. They will probably di rect their course for the state of Illinois.

Ile writes to a shipmate, whom he hoped might receive his letter, and come to his relief; but it appears his hope was fallacious, for another American, a stranger who knows him not, receives from a good-hearted English seaman, which else, would have been returned to the broken hearted writer, and publishes on his arrival at Newport, to the friends of the four enslaved, that some time since, they were on board such a ship. Perhaps ere they can be restored to the blessings of freedom, they may fall on a British deck, combating against their Fathers and Brothers! Fellow-Citizens—To assert the right of crossing the ocean, unmolested, has our government appealed to arms against the Tyrant of the Seas. To procure the release of thousands of our Citizens from the floating hells of Britain, and to prevent you, your brothers, and your sons, from being entombed alive, the present war was waged. Will you then hesitate to support it? No! You will cheerfully lend your aid to effect the great and glorious work which alone can render the United States really independent. Whales, and the Sea-Serpent.—Captain Lewis, just arrived from Portland, saw on his passage here a number of large Whales; and we have assurances of gentlemen of veracity, that the Sea Serpent was distinctly seen from Nahant cliffs, on Saturday last, for more than half an hour. These animals are doubtless in pursuit of the same food—the schools of herrings and menhaden, which now abound on our coast. Twenty Guineas to see the King!—Preparations are now actively carrying on in Westminster Hall, London, for the Coronation of the King. Arrangements are making to suspend magnificent chandeliers from the roof, and next week the building at the extremity of the Hall, in which the Courts of Chancery and King’s Bench are held, will be taken down, to afford an additional space for the ceremony. The prices given to secure views of the ceremony in August, surpass those of previous occasions. The front of a house in Great George-street, which faces the grand entrance into Westminster Abbey, is let for the occasion for 2000L. The neighboring house is fixed at 2500; and it is calculated that 80001.

These, with the line of battleship building at this place, and the materials of live oak on hand, and those procured from Blackbeard and Grover's Islands, which are estimated as equal to the frames of two frigates, exceed the whole number authorized by the act for the gradual increase, three frames for line of battleships, and four frames for frigates. This excess, however, is only nominal, for, of the contracts made, two, viz: those with Mr. Livingston and Mr. Snow, which embrace the frames of three line of battleships and four frigates, have, as was expected and intimated in our letter of 20th January last, been forfeited by the contractors. This failure on the part of the contractors compelled the navy commissioners, in the months of June and September last, to enter into contract with Messrs. Swift, Green and Grice, for the frames of three line of battleships and four frigates, to supply the place of those which Messrs. Livingston and Snow had failed, agreeably to their respective contracts, to deliver. Hence, the contracts now existing, including those which have been completely executed and the timber procured at Blackbeard and Grover's Islands, embrace the frames of nine line of battleships and ten frigates only, which is the number authorized by the act for the gradual increase of the navy.

Mitchell—which describes a stone fort, situated at the confluence of two of the most southern forks of Duck River, on the northwestern corner of Franklin County, Tennessee—The fort covers about 32 acres of ground; it is surrounded by a stone wall from 16 to 20 feet in thickness at the base, and about 5 feet at the top. The letter which describes the outlines of this work mentions that within 7 years, a fine piece of thin glass was ploughed up in the area, about one inch in thickness and of remarkable transparency; it appeared to be part of a bowl neatly polished and fluted on its sides. A small stone, carved and ornamented, was also found. Evident marks of the hammer and chisel are perceived upon the walls; though they are rough and uncouth from the lapse of ages. About forty miles from this fort in Maury County, a cave has been discovered containing a variety of earthenware bricks of a form different from those of a modern make, and an iron sword resembling the sabre of the Persians or Scythians. On the 27th of last January, Mary Hines, one of the domestics, received directions from Mrs. Browne, to have breakfast ready very early on the ensuing morning, as the defendant, then on a visit at the house, expressed an inclination to go out to hunt. — She was accordingly brushing down the stairs at a very early hour, when she observed the handle of the door stir, and fearing the noise had disturbed her, she ran hastily down stairs to avoid her displeasure. She remained below about three quarters of an hour, when her master’s bell ringing violently she hastened to answer it. He asked her in some alarm where her mistress was? naturally enough astonished at such a question at such an hour, she said she knew not, but would go down and see whether or not she was in the parlor.

Finding, however, that the enemy had only light artillery, and being well convinced that it could make little impression upon the works, and that any attempt to storm it would be resisted with effect, I waited for the arrival of 250 mounted volunteers, which on the evening before had left Upper Sandusky. But as soon as I was informed that the enemy were retreating, I set out with the dragoons to endeavor to overtake them, leaving Generals M’Arthur and Cass to follow with all the infantry (about 700) that could be spared from the protection of the stores and sick at this place. I found it impossible to come up with them. Upon my arrival at Sandusky, I was informed by the prisoners that the enemy’s forces consisted of 490 regular troops, and 500 of Dixon's Indians, commanded by General Proctor in person, and that Tecumseh, with about 2000 warriors, was somewhere in the swamps, between this and Fort Meigs, expecting my advancing, or that of a convoy of provisions. As there was no prospect of doing anything in front, and being apprehensive that Tecumseh might destroy the stores and small detachments in my rear, I sent orders to General Cass, who commanded the reserve, to fall back to this place, and to General M’Arthur with the front line to follow and support him.

And the question stated "Shall the resolution pass?" Mr. Trimble observed, in reference to some remarks between himself and Mr. Smith yesterday, that he had not voted for the admission of Alabama, because he could not reconcile the provision in relation to banks (with all the checks and guards which had been introduced into the constitution of Alabama on that subject) with the federal constitution. In relation to that provision, he had entertained doubts which were at the time expressed to some of his friends. Mr. T. said it was true that he had not made a formal opposition to the admission of Alabama because he had just taken his seat in the Senate, and was unaccustomed to legislative proceedings; nor did he then suppose that it was so important that he should record his name in opposition to the measures which he thought violated the spirit and true meaning of the federal constitution. But, had the gentleman, said Mr. T., no other defense to set up for that article of the constitution of Missouri? If, said he, the federal constitution has been violated, in one instance, is that any reason that it should be violated in another? Can precedent sanctify a violation of the constitution which we are sworn to support? The question being then put, the Resolution was passed and sent to the House of Representatives for concurrence; and the Senate adjourned. HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES.

A MAP of the state of Illinois will soon be published.  Dec. 25—3itf PROPOSALS  For printing a weekly paper in the town of St. Charles, Missouri Territory, to be entitled THE MISSOURI GAZETTE.  BY ROBERT M’CLOUD.  THE public naturally expect printers establishing new papers, to give a detail of their principles, and the line of their future conduct. We shall merely say that we are na tive Americans, and duly appreciate the privileges of our happy government. The only pledge we give is, that we will promote with our best abilities the interest of our common country, and particularly the interests of the territory and future state of Missouri, leaving to our fellow citizens and patrons to judge of our future conduct, and our claim to a continu ance of their confidence. Upon these condi tions we solicit their patronage.  St. Charles, Oct. 39, 1819.  CONDITIONS. The MISSOURI GAZETTE will be printed on the largest sized super-royal paper, and good type, at Three Dollars per annum, payable in advance; or Four Dollars at the expiration of the year. Subscriptions received at this office.

We do not proclaim that our hero was safely on board the commodore’s ship, while Gen. Pike was capturing Little York, nor boastfully exhibit the scalp and mace which he bore off as trophies of his triumph. He never marched into Canada and then—marched out again. He did not devote Sackett's harbour to destruction; He did not allow the enemy to retire unmolested from Fort George except by Lewis, whose only object seems to have been to secure the retreat of his own commander; He did not detach Chandler to be kidnapped in the dark, nor Buetsler to be cut off in the daytime—the only fate for which he was fit—In fine, he was never placed on the list of Yellow Admirals by envy or intrigue—rated as a Harbor Admiral—nor reduced to a razee. We care no more for Armstrong or Madison than we do for Dearborn.

"Lieutenant Wilmer, after fighting most gallantly throughout the action, had been knocked overboard by a splinter while getting the sheet anchor from the bows, and was drowned. Acting Lieutenant John G. Cowell had lost a leg. Mr. Edward Barnewell, Acting Sailing Master, had been carried below after receiving two severe wounds, one in the breast and one in the face; and Acting Lieutenant Wm. H. Odenheimer had been knocked overboard from the quarter an instant before and did not regain the ship until after the surrender. I was informed that the cockpit, the steerage, the wardroom, and the berth deck could contain no more wounded; that the wounded were killed while the surgeons were dressing them, and that unless something was speedily done to prevent it, the ship would soon sink from the number of shot holes in her bottom. On sending for the carpenter, he informed me that all his crew had been killed or wounded, and that he had once been over the side to stop the leaks, when his slings had been shot away, and it was with difficulty he was saved from drowning."

I hear War is considered a pastime, and I lute it as a chess board for political game triers to play upon.— This short analogy will satisfy you, my brethren, that instead of a republican, we have the rule of oligarchs, of political prostitutes, who profess one thing and practice another, and who make public functionaries as George III makes Knights of the Bath, or a Minister of his members of Parliament; and that in such hands we shall soon finish our republican career. What has become of economy, the boast of democrats, nay the pass word to power? Have we seen any glimmerings of it through the darkness of the times? Still, we look for it in a late act of Congress to increase their pay to more than twelve dollars a day? Commerce is languishing, manufactures are at a stand, the currency embarrassed, taxes heavy, and no people in difficulties; and yet, at such a moment, and with an enormous public debt upon our shoulders, our economical and democratic administration, so called, more than double the congressional pay! The people to be sure will no longer have cause to complain of long sessions; for as members of Congress are not ruled by economy, the course of legislation will be as rapid as impatience itself would desire.

"WHEREAS, by an act of Congress, passed on the 17th of February, 1818, entitled "an act making provision for the establishment of additional Land Offices in the territory of Missouri," the President of the United States is authorized to direct the public lands, which have been surveyed in the said territory, to be offered for sale: Therefore, I, James Monroe, President of the United States, do hereby declare and make known, that public sales for the disposal (agreeably to law) of certain lands in the territory of Missouri, shall be held in Franklin, in said territory, viz: - On the first Monday in January next, for the sale of: Townships No. 46 to 52 inclusive, and fractional township 53 - On the first Monday in March next, for the sale of: Townships 48 to 55 inclusive, in ranges 24 and 25 - On the first Monday of May next, for the sale of: Townships 51 to 54 inclusive, in ranges 11 and 12 excepting the lands which have been, or may be, reserved by law for the support of schools, and for other purposes. Each sale shall continue as long as may be necessary to offer the land for sale, and no longer, and the lands shall be offered in regular numerical order."

Between the towns of Lisbon and Durham, which are believed to be incorrect, it is there stated that the aforesaid bridge is upwards of 500 feet in length, which assertion is true, but it is not the whole truth for it is 627 feet,—it is also stated that said bridge is only 20 feet wide, which is not true, for the width is 22 feet,—it is also stated that it is very inconvenient for teams meeting on the bridge to pass. Now it is apprehended that the writer of the aforesaid notice is much wider in his ideas than our common country folks, for we have never yet experienced any inconvenience in passing one another with our widest carriages or sleds loaded with the widest loads of hay, they never lay over 11 feet in width.

Let it therefore be well considered before it passes. In point of time, I think we are particularly unfortunate. Recollect the important crisis of this country in relation to the belligerents of Europe.—France, it is said, has revoked her edicts, and now it becomes for Great Britain to say whether she will repeal hers. Let it be remembered, that Great Britain is now the ally of Spain, and, for aught we know, may have guaranteed her colonies. Would it not at least have been prudent, as was done on a former occasion, in relation to another power, to have enquired what part she would take? If she is to act the part of an ally offensive and defensive or of an ally at all, can it be expected that she will revoke her orders in council or even remain indifferent? It is a singular circumstance, that the proclamation reviving the non-intercourse with Great Britain and the one for taking possession of Florida were upon the anvil at the same time. There is only five days' difference in their dates, and the Florida proclamation is the first. Sir, to me they look a good deal like twin brothers. One moment more will close my remarks. The honorable gentleman from Kentucky has told us, that Europe is now in a state of barbarism, and has emphatically asked, are we to sit here and cavil about questions of right?

John Randolph spoke five days on the Missouri Question, and his “Indian Arrow” was aimed at all sides. At the close of the debate, after ridiculing the compromise, he said, in allusion to the seceders: “I knew these men would give way. They were scared at their own doublings—yes, they were scared at their own doublings! We had them, and if we had wanted three more, we could have had them; yes, and if these had failed, we could have had three more of these men, whose conscience and morality and religion extend to thirty-six degrees and thirty minutes north latitude. You can never find any difficulty in obtaining the support of men whose principles of morality and religion are bounded by thirty-six degrees and thirty minutes north latitude!” POLITICAL VIEWS. M. De Pradt, a French political writer, has made the following remarks on the United States:— “It is North America that England will fear in future; for it is the country where there exists most of the elements that render her formidable. “North America is a second England, descent, language, manners, natural bias towards commerce and maritime pursuits, all are English in America. “Fable has had its rival brothers; England and the United States will realize the fable. Sprung from the same blood, swayed by the same inclinations, instead of uniting them, these circumstances will tend only to place them in perpetual opposition.

The Speaker, no doubt draws his checks as usual, the money, I suppose is put into his hands by the treasurer, and the Serjeant at arms calls at the bank, receives the money and pays it over to the members. Unless then this method be altered, the mode remains the same; how unworthy then of a National Legislature to resort to a contemptible artifice to cover their outrage upon the public purse—Why not call the brat by its proper name and say at once, that it was an act to double or treble the pay of members of Congress, and to place them upon the pension list? This would have been honest, and an indication of their conviction, that the deed would bear the light. Brothers, with real sorrow I say it, our republic is rapidly on the decline, that Constitution which was made by the patriots and sages of our country, by the best heads, and the best hearts in it, is already little more than a cadaver mortuum; and yet a little longer in such hands, we shall understand how Augustus amused the Roman people with the shadows of the Commonwealth, while the substance was held by the hands of Caesar.

Let every man keep such as he likes; but I repeat, let no man keep more than he can keep well, nor any, but such as are likely of their breed. With regard to shearing; I am inclined to believe that our farmers generally shear too early. A sufficiency of time between washing and shearing ought to elapse, to produce from fresh and nutritious pasture, a new mass of oil, to be infused into the whole texture of the fleece. In this interval, especially, ought the flock to be kept in a rich and clean pasture: besides, the fleece ought not to be taken off until the N G. storms and winds of June are past, and summer really commences. As to neat cattle, there is much in the breed. This is a subject worthy the farmer’s attention. But, after all, nothing short of good pastures and good feeding will support and keep up long, even the best breed; without these, the best will soon degenerate. In a rich country, and on well cultivated farms, we generally see one stock, handsome and well proportioned. If the land be not naturally rich, the farmer cannot, without much labor and high cultivation, expect a profitable stock. Stock takes its quality much from the farm where raised; indeed, it would not be extravagantly hazardous to purchase a farm on the sample of its stock.

The return of the regular force now in service, laid before you will show how many men will be necessary to fill the present corps; and the return of the numerical force of the present military establishment, will show how many are required to complete it to the number proposed. The next and most important inquiry is, how these men shall be raised? Under existing circumstances, it is evident that the most prompt and efficient mode that can be devised, consistent with the equal rights of every citizen ought to be adopted. The following plans are respectfully submitted to the consideration of the committee. Being distinct in their nature, I will present each separately, with the considerations applicable to it. FIRST PLAN: Let the free male population of the U.S. States, between 18 and 45 years, be formed into classes of 100 men each, and let each class furnish men for the war, within 30 days after the classification, and replace them in the event of casualty. The classification to be formed with a view to the equal distribution of property among the several classes. If any class fails to provide the men required of it, within the time specified, they shall be raised by draft on the whole class: any person thus drafted being allowed to furnish a substitute.

Four or five attempts have recently been made to set fire to the town of Augusta, Georgia. One of them succeeded but partially, owing to the calmness of the weather. Patrick Magruder has resigned the office of Clerk of the House of Representatives, and Thomas Dougherty, of Kentucky is appointed in his room. Alexander H. Everett, Esq. of Boston, is appointed Secretary of the American Legation to the Court of Holland. A soldier was lately shot near Lexington, (Ken.) for desertion. A bill has passed the Legislature of Virginia, authorizing the erection of a line of Telegraphs. The Supreme Court of Massachusetts have decreed, says the Chronicle, that a minor having a parent and master (to whom he is bound as an apprentice) must have the consent in writing of both of them, if he wishes to enlist as a soldier; because the parent and master both have rights either natural or conventional, which neither of them can compromise as it respects the other. The Grand Lodge of Massachusetts have appropriated 500 dollars for the relief of Masonic brethren, British prisoners of war at Salem. Robert B. Taylor and Armistead T. Mason have been chosen by the Legislature of Virginia as Major Generals in the state corps, which that state is about raising. EARTHQUAKE—On Saturday, the 7th ult.

The day after Lieutenant Gregory arrived on the coast, he discovered a vessel beating up, but just as he shoved off to board her, a large gun boat hove in sight a little below him. This boat made a signal to the vessel in the offing, upon which she tacked and stood for the gun boat. Lieutenant Gregory secreted his boats as well as he could, but was apprehensive that he had been discovered. The gun boat and her convoy (which was full of troops) stood into "Presque Isle." Lieutenant Gregory was determined to ascertain whether he had been discovered—he accordingly sent one of his boats in the next night, and took off one of the inhabitants, who informed him that it was known that he was on the coast, and that two expresses had been sent to Kingston in consequence. He therefore determined upon executing the latter part of his instructions, and made his arrangements accordingly; landed, placed sentinels at the houses to prevent alarm, and set fire to the vessel, which was nearly ready to launch; she was a stout well-built vessel to mount 14 guns, and would probably have been launched in ten days. A small storehouse which contained stores for the vessel was unavoidably burnt, as it was so near the vessel that it took fire from her.

A wealthy Swiss farmer, much affected with hypochondria, came to Lausanne to consult the celebrated Michael Schuppach, better known by the appellation of the 'Mountain Doctor.' 'I have seven devils in my belly,' said he; 'no fewer than seven.' 'There are more than seven,' replied the doctor, with the utmost gravity, 'if you count them right you will find eight.' After questioning the patient concerning his case, he promised to cure him in eight days, during which time he would every morning rid him of one of his troublesome inmates, at the rate of one louis d'or each. 'But,' added he, 'as the last will be much more obstinate and difficult to expel than the others, I shall expect two louis d'ors for him.' The farmer agreed to these terms, the bargain was struck, and the doctor, imposing upon all present the necessity of secrecy, promised to give the nine louis d'ors to the poor of the parish. Next morning the imaginary demoniac was brought to him and placed near a kind of machine which he had not seen before, by means of which he received an electric shock. The farmer roared out lustily. 'There goes one,' said the doctor, with the utmost gravity. Next day the same operation was repeated; the farmer bellowed as before, and the doctor coolly remarked, 'another is off.'

At Portland, on Sunday last, the privateer brig Grand Turk, Birced, from a cruise of 4 months, having manned 4 prizes, made a cartel of 1, and burnt 1. She has on board a cargo of dry goods, and has brought in 30 prisoners. The British ship Upton, of 16 guns, from Cork, for Newfoundland, prize to the Diomede privateer of Salem, are at Wiscasset, 3d st cargo pork, flour, butter, biscuit, soap, candies, &c. captured May 20, off Newfoundland, after an action of an hour and 20 minutes, in which she had 1 killed 7 wounded. The D. none hurt. The prize had on board at the time of her capture, 104 men, (most of them passengers,) who were sent on shore in a small fishing schooner. She is 270 tons burden. A letter from Wiscasset states, that the Upton is a fine ship, and formerly belonged to Philadelphia. Two days after leaving the D she fell in with the British letter-of-marque ship Hero of Cork, loaded with provisions, which bore down and engaged her 20 minutes when she struck to the Upton, and having a sufficient number of men to man her, permitted her to proceed. The Hero had a large number of passengers on board. The British sch. Prickle, from Dartmouth, Eng. for the St Lawrence, with Teas, Dry-Goods, &c. has been taken and destroyed by the pr. Pike, of Baltimore, after taking out the cargo.

That depredations have been committed on the woodlands, and the wood and timber, nearly all taken off so that the lands are daily decreasing in value; but if they could be sold and the money put to interest and that interest applied to the support of their minister, the Parish would derive great benefit from it—where they now comparatively receive none. We, your petitioners, therefore, in the name and on behalf of said Parish and with the consent of their Rev. Pastor, humbly request your honors, to appoint, to be incorporated by the name of the Trustees of the ministerial fund in the first Parish in Northampton, and that they be empowered to sell all the ministerial lands belonging to said Parish as soon as they find it for the interest thereof, and vest the proceeds of the sale in a fund, the income of which shall be duly applied to the support of a settled minister in said Parish and for such other purposes and as money bound will ever pray. AMMI R. MITCHELL, HIRAM GILMAN, Committee. WILLIAM D. BROWNE, Northampton, June 7, 1816. Commonwealth of Massachusetts. IN SENATE, June 3rd.

The President of the United States, yesterday received, at the executive mansion, the congratulations of a large concourse of ladies and gentlemen, consisting of members of congress, the heads of departments, foreign ministers, private citizens and strangers. The uncommon fineness of the day, added to the performance of the marine band, imparted an exhilarating and pleasing feeling to the numerous visitors, who were highly gratified with the hospitality and affability of the President and his lady. Mr. Elmer from committee to whom was referred the report of the Treasurer relative to the several banks in the state, made a report that the Bank of New-Brunswick had failed making payment of the tax levied on their capital subscribed and paid in, to the amount of $994.42 including the years 1817, 1818, 1819, and 1820—alleging that their capital had been reduced by losses; and that some other banks had also failed to a large amount. That the Treasurer be directed to call upon the President and Directors of the defaulting banks, for payment, and in case they neglected or refused to make payment to proceed to recover the same according to law. In the British House of Lords, the Earl of Liverpool was asked whether the country was at war with France, to which his Lordship replied that any hostile acts committed against Great Britain would be considered as an act of hostility towards Great Britain. INLAND NAVIGATION. CONCORD, (N.H.) JUNE 27.— Arrived in the Merrimack company’s Boats. There arrived at this port on Friday last, three boats, making a passage from Boston, of only three days. It is expected that in future, the arrivals from Boston at this place will be frequent and merchants and others wishing to transport from, or to, Massachusetts, will find this a safe and expeditious mode of conveyance. COMMERCE OF THE MISSISSIPPI. During the month of May, there arrived in the Mississippi, ninety-seven vessels, of which sixty were ships, twenty brigs, and the residue sloops and schooners. Persons have been appointed to execute an order of the government, that each disbanded soldier shall receive 160 acres of land, and each officer 320—with suitable provisions and implements of husbandry. Mr. Romayne, of New-York, a Republican, has been chosen professor of natural history in the Kentuckey University. A committee of the Legislature of New Hampshire have reported in favor of the erection of a State House, to be built of stone, and estimated to cost $30000. A dividend of interest on the U.S. 3 per cent. stock becomes due on the 1st of the present month. All the creditors of the Treasury Notes payable at Philadelphia.

BY EDSON HALL, Agent for the Foreign Mission School, FOR publishing by subscription, MEMOIRS OF HIS EXCELLENCY OBOOKIAH, A NATIVE OF OWHYHEE, And a Member of the Foreign Mission School; WHO DIED AT CORNWALL, CONN. FEB. 17, 1818, AGED 20 YEARS. This work will contain, in addition to the Memoirs, a Sermon delivered at the funeral of Obookiah, and another deliv ered at the Inauguration of the Principal of the Foreign Mission School, entitled, The Banner of Christ set up; also, two Inaugural Addresses. There are at present twenty-three schol ars in the Foreign Mission School at Cornwall, eight of whom are hopefully pious. This School is dependent upon the charity of the Christian public for its support, and it is hoped that they will be induced to subscribe liberally to this work, as all the profits of it are to be devoted to the funds of the institution. CONDITIONS. The work will contain about 200 pa ges, with a portrait of Obookiah. It will be printed on good paper, with a fair type, and be handsomely bound and let tered. The price to subscribers will be 75 cents per copy. Jan. 8, 1819. Subscriptions received at the office of the Elizabeth-Town Gazette. The debate on the Slave Restriction Bill was continued the same day in the House. On the 17th inst the Senate agreed to exclude slavery beyond the latitude of 36° 30’ N. The vote was 34 to 10; this amendment to the Maine and Missouri Bills, with no restriction affecting them as states, was passed 24 to 20, and sent to the House for concurrence. The above amendment appears to have been adopted in the spirit of conciliation or Hr. For the amendment—Messrs. Inglis and Major Scott, of the 57th, were killed, with many other officers, the command of this regiment devolved on the adjutant; the 29th, after the most gallant behaviour fell into the great; having only 91 men left; this regiment and the 48th are commanded by subalterns. All the field officers of the Fusiliers are killed or wounded. Col. Ellis of the 23rd was wounded. The result of the action was, that Marshal Soult was compelled to retreat back to Seville, without having been able to secure Badajoz, which was expected to fall in a few days. Thus far the allies were victorious; but never was victory more dearly purchased. On the 19th of May, Lord Wellington arrived, with a part of his army, at Elvas; and as he is said to have gone in pursuit of Marshal Soult, another action was daily expected. Gen. Hill had arrived at Lisbon from England, and set out immediately to resume his command. Our letter of the 26th also states that a convoy was then off the harbor from England, which is said to have a reinforcement of from 12 to 14,000, chiefly cavalry and artillery. It was said that Massena had quitted the army having been ordered to Paris by the Emperor, whose displeasure he had incurred. The latest letter states that Marshal Beresford had resumed the siege of Badajoz in person, and that Soult continued his retreat towards Seville.

The Louisiana Legislature have resolved, "That the memorable 8th of January, shall, in future be observed as a day of public thanksgiving; that a Te Deum shall be sung at the Cathedral Church in the city of New-Orleans, and the civil and military officers of the State and of the United States be invited together with the ministers of every denomination, to join in a procession from the government house to the place of ceremony." Washington, Feb. 9, CONGRESS. The House of Representatives sat at 12 o'clock, an hour yesterday, that we can only, from that circumstance; and from the want of room; state a few of the prominent results of the proceedings. In the Senate, some business was done, of which an account will be hereafter given. The proceedings of most immediate interest, was a motion, by Mr. Morril, of New-Hampshire, to adopt a proposition to the following effect: Resolved, by the Senate, that the President of the United States be requested to strike from the rolls of the Army and Navy the names of all such officers thereof, as were, directly or indirectly, concerned in counseling, aiding, or abetting the late duel between Armistead T. Mason and J. M. McCarty, citizens of Virginia. This motion lies on the table. House of Representatives.- We have great satisfaction in announcing that the discussion in respect to the incidents of the Seminole war, is at length brought to a close.

Sir—As money, the representative of labor, both mental and corporeal, and of the productions of nature, besides being the medium of fiscal regulation, and of commercial pursuits, (with its proper substitute) has of late become a matter of public concern, as well as of curious speculation, it may not be amiss, occasionally, to bring into view the sentiments of those venerable sages who have heretofore meditated profoundly on the subject. Among those sages appeared Doctor John Witherspoon. He was president of the college at Princeton, in the state of New-Jersey; and represented that state, as a delegate in congress; at which time he became one of the Patriotic group who signed our inestimable Declaration of Independence! The following, given as a specimen of the solidity of his judgment, is selected from the 1st volume of “The American Review, and Literary Journal,” page 425.—If you think this article deserving of a re-publication, be so good as to give it an insertion in your Gazette. I am, &c. M. An essay on money as a medium of commerce, with remarks on the advantages and disadvantages of paper admitted into general circulation.

RAN away from the subscriber, on the 10th of July last, a negro man named SAM, twenty-four or twenty-five years old, near six feet high, stout made, of a black com plexion, and very likely. It is expected that the next account would cement their assertion, that every thing had been arranged, we find doubts again expressed, and a recurrence of those speculations and conjectures with which we have been fatigued for the last two months. The Duke of Wellington has been received at Vienna with the highest distinction. Crowds hang upon his steps, and Princes and Peasants, are equally anxious to behold the General who can say what no other General in Europe can, that he never was beaten. Lord Castlereagh’s departure from Vienna was to take place on the 10th, by which time it was thought everything would be settled. There is a statement in one of the French papers which we must believe to be unfounded, that the congress have agreed to cede to France the departments of the Protestants, Gurthc, Rhine, and Moselle, and the Roer. Talleyrand is expected to return to Paris soon. A Dutch mail arrived this morning — It has not brought any information of a positive nature relative to the congress at Vienna Feb. 5. The determination of the frontiers of Holland, or rather of Belgium, on the side of France, seems to become one of the most important points of the negotiation at the congress.

During the war of the revolution, Washington had honored him with a high command. His military merit was highly esteemed by Washington who selected as Adjutant General to the commander in chief and the ability with which he discharged the duties of that office are fresh in the recollection of the people of this state. Suffice it to say that his conduct was most approved where it was understood and that the whole credit of which we were indebted for our security at a critical and portentous period belonged to Isaac Brooks. Such was the character and such were the abilities of the man who most fitly, was selected with universal approbation as his successor. The experience of years, in which he has performed the functions of that high and important station, has shown that the expectations entertained of him have not been disappointed—and he proved to be abundantly adequate to the execution of the office to which he was elected. The purity with which he has passed through the duties of that period has been without a spot. The integrity with which he has acted.

FOREIGN. IMPORTANT AND AUTHENTIC NEWS FROM FRANCE— Hostilities will commence again with Russia and Prussia, and no doubt some des perate conflict will take place in a few days. The Emperor is determined if it is in his power to force his enemies to his own terms. He has raised again a most power ful army, some say amounting to 500,000 men; on his present success depends all our hopes of raising prices and relieving the market of the immense quantity of produce which has been imported to supply his continental friends. We are happy to state that three of the four persons concerned in this transaction are in custody. Robert B. Watson, on being brought before the police, voluntarily testifies that he presented the check and received the money. That he was persuaded to do it by Grove Miller, Isaac M. Woolley, and Frederick Shaw. Shaw told him he had personated the signer of the check (John Jones) and had made the deposit at the Newark Bank. The deponent received $850, Woolley received $1100, and the remainder was kept by Shaw and Miller. The detection of these adroit rogues, we are told, reflects credit on our police. Col. Elizabeth-Town, Tuesday, November 24, 1818. Seventh Annual Report of the Board of Managers of the Elizabeth-Town Female Humane Society, Nov. 9, 1813. The members of the society are again called on to unite with the managers in the expression of gratitude to the Author of every good and perfect gift for his continuance of the institution, and for the degree of usefulness to which it is believed the association has contributed. The managers cannot avoid calling upon the members generally to take into particular and serious consideration the necessity of an increased attention and solicitude for the affairs of the society.

Nov. 11, captured English brig Eagle, from Bristol for Newfoundland, with a full cargo of dry goods, hardware, stationary, wine, brandy, paints, &c. ordered in. 17th, captured English brig Edward, from Teneriffe to London, cargo wine, ordered in. 19th, captured English brig Peter, of 8 guns, fr. Same day, captured English cutter Eliza & Peggy, from Malaga to London, cargo raisins—gave her up to 33 prisoners. 20th, captured English brig Good Intent, from Corunna to England, in ballast, burnt her. 23rd, brought to an English brig from Messina for London, cargo sulphur—owing to increasing gales could not board her, and she escaped in the night. Dec. 1, captured English cutter Dart, cargo wine, raisins and nuts, from Malaga, took out a sufficiency for the ship’s use, and burnt her. 6th, captured English brig Christian, from France to London, with a full cargo of cork, dog fish skins, almonds, baskets, &c. burnt her. 9th, captured English sch.

The Committee on the Judiciary reported a bill for establishing a uniform system of Bankruptcy throughout the U. States. Read twice, and committed. The Committee of Claims reported unfavorable to the petition of Samuel G. Adams. This report occasioned a debate. The petitioner commanded a detachment of militia during the war and received the due bill of a pay-master for the amount of their pay, etc. and he asks Congress to assume the payment of it, as the act of a public agent. This was opposed on the ground, that it would establish a precedent for endless frauds. Messrs. Tyler, Williams, Pinkney, Rich, Brush, Holmes, and Gross took a part in the debate, but eventually the report of the Committee of Claims was concurred in. Mr. Williams, of N. C. in the course of his observations remarked, that it was due to the state of the finances at this time—which he understood from the report of the Secretary, were now nearly exhausted, to guard against all lavish disbursements. Adjourned. FRIDAY, DEC. 17.

Inson 17 days from Aux Cayes, states that 3 or 4 days before he sailed an order arrived from Port au Prince for all the troops to repair to that capital for its defence. Christophe, it was said, was approaching it with all his force. The militia were doing garrison duty at Aux Cayes. Mr. Benjamin O. Tyler, who lately published an elegant edition of the declaration of Independence, has announced his intention of publishing a facsimile copy of the first commission given by congress to Gen. Washington, also the articles of confederation of the thirteen United States with facsimiles of the signatures of all that signed those instruments with several other national publications. V. I. Col. es, DIED, In this Borough, on the 27th ult. after a short but severe indisposition, Mrs. Sarah Brown, wife of Mr. Isaac Brown, in her 34th year. In this dispensation of providence is found loss of an amiable and beloved companion, a fond and tender mother, a kind and agreeable friend. She was highly exemplary in her walk, and evinced in her whole demeanor the feelings and conduct of a sincere and ardent believer in the Gospel of Christ. In her distressing illness she manifested such a strong confidence in the merits and intercession of the son of God, as gives full assurance of her resting in the embrace of that savior to whose service her life was eminently devoted.

Feb. 29, 1820. The editors of papers throughout the United States, are requested to give circula- tion to the above advertisement. of various kinds, for sale at this office. Republican Eloquence. Observations of Mr. Cushmg, of Mas- sachusetts, on the amendment to the bill virtually to repeal "an act to pro- vide for certain persons engaged in the land and naval service of the Uni- ted States, in the revolutionary war." Mr. Chairman: I am opposed to the amendment now under consideration, as well as to most of the provisions of the bill, as reported by the committee of ways and means; for, as much as I re- gret that the revenue should be bur- dened with a list of pensioners, of al- most every description, I should more deeply regret that the law granting aid to the revolutionary soldier, in reduced circumstances, should be repealed, or even modified to his detriment. This repeal, sir, or modification, would be at- tended with consequences to be depre- cated.

We have just closed a treaty with the Chickasaw Indians for all their claim in the states of Tennessee and Kentucky, containing about seven millions of acres of the best lands in the western country, and washed by the Tennessee, Ohio and Mississippi rivers, for at least 350 miles; for an annuity of $20,000 for 15 years. I am respectfully, &c. ANDREW JACKSON. From the Norristown Herald of Nov. 11. “Disguise thyself as thou wilt, still, slavery, still thou art a bitter draught.” Sterne. On Saturday night last, between the hours of 9 and 10, two white men, with a horse and cart, two negro men, a woman and two children (one an infant) arrived at the house of a certain innkeeper, who shall for the present be nameless, and put up for the night.

Piris, Feb. 15. Extract of a letter from Bayonne of Feb. 12. According to letters from Cadiz received this day, all is tranquil in the city; but it is the tranquility of a 111 agony. You will judge whether the insurgents are not in some force, when the chief of the national army has dispatched a division of 3000 men for Algeciras, to accompany from thence to the headquarters seve ral members of the Cortes who had landed there. It is said there have been some skirmishes between the royal and insurgent troops, in which the latter had the advantage. The troops have left Vittoria for Galicia, where some troubles have broken out. February 17. When Louvel was interrogated on Monday, by the minister of the interior, why, if he did not fear death, he had fled after assassinating the prince? He replied cooly, "In order that he might be able to kill another." Boston, March 18. Arrival of the Falcon. The fine regular trading ship Falcon, capt. Lewis, arrived here yesterday from Liverpool in 25 days passage. By the Falcon we have received regular files of London papers to the 18th February—same date as those by the Union.—Patriot.

If our accounts are correct, the unprovoked assault by a military force on an unarmed meeting, deliberating on their distresses and their grievances, was a stain on the banner of the army, and an outrage not to be endured, if it could be resisted, by a people claiming the shadow of freedom. The stain becomes more deep, when we find that women became the subjects of military assault. The Texas Expedition.—We would be led to suppose, from the pompous letters from the adventurers in this enterprise, that their numbers at least were respectable, and their prospects brilliant. We have authentic accounts from that quarter which represent them to be directly the reverse. They have not the least prospect of succeeding in obtaining possession of the territory they are so liberal in offering to dispose of to recruits and proselytes. Their actual numbers, as late as the middle of August, did not exceed one hundred, and they were said to be without funds, and ill supplied with provisions.

The soldiers were then let slip on the enemy, as if they were prey, and the contest over, they remained to rights mutilated like their persons; with laurel on their heads, but with chains on their limbs; but with it Was such, he would ask, the treat ment which they ought to receive? The religion of the Irish was unknown to him, and he knew what the reli gion of mankind had been—It was fre quently marked by bigotry, persecution and interest. But he contended injustice was done on his country when in religious sin was compared to what she was 200 years ago; and he would ask, if, they would think themselves fairly treated if their tenets were to be judged by the events which took place anterior to the Reformation? On the subject of Annual Parliament and Universal Suffrage, he had to say he respected the liberties of the peo ple as much as any man could do, and he thought they ought to be ex tended as far as this could be done with a view to their benefit; but if the words universal suffrage were acted upon to the extent of their mean ing (which, in his conscience he did believe the people understood,) he was satisfied there would be no repre sentation at all.

They amounted to about 5,000, and with their friends who accompanied them, formed a line that filled the street from Temple Bar to the Adelphi. There were three other similar flags, each carried by a seaman in the procession. The first was inscribed—“Heaven protect the Innocent.” The second—“God save the Queen.” And the third—“Non Mi Ricordo.” Several naval flags were also carried in the procession; in the midst of it was a small cart with two men seated on it, sustaining a pole, on the top of which was the figure of a sailor, with a roll in one hand, and a hat in the other, in the act of cheering. The procession was closed by a considerable number of hackney coaches, full of company, male and female.—The windows in the line of the procession were filled with spectators. The ladies waved their handkerchiefs, and the sailors occasionally cheered as they passed. They conducted themselves in the most orderly manner. Buenos Aires; that Chile has declared itself independent, and is closely connected with Buenos Aires; that Venezuela has also declared itself independent, and now maintains the conflict with various success; and that the remaining parts of South America, except Montevideo, and such other portions of the eastern bank of the La Plata as are held by Portugal, are still in the possession of Spain, or in a certain degree under her influence. By a circular note addressed by the Ministers of Spain to the Allied Powers, with whom they are respectively accredited, it appears that the allies have undertaken to mediate between Spain and the South American provinces, and that the manner and extent of their interposition would be settled by a congress, which was to have met at Aix-la-Chapelle, in September last. From the general policy and course of proceeding observed by the allied powers, in regard to this contest, it is inferred that they will confine their interposition to the expression of their sentiments; abstaining from the application of force. I state this impression, that force will not be applied, with the greater satisfaction, because it is a course more consistent with justice, and likewise authorizes a hope that the calamities of war will be confined to the parties only, and will be of shorter duration.

Openckon Sorrel, Will stand this season, at Jacob Shartles' stable at The Sulphur Springs, Four miles from Harrodsburg, every day in the week (public days excepted) at the moderate price of four dollars the season, if paid during the season, if not paid in the season, five dol lars will be demanded; one and a half dol lars the single leap, to be paid when the mare is put to the nurse, and eight dollars for insurance, if the mare is regularly brought; parting with or removing the mare forfeits the insurance; the insurance money to be paid as soon as the mare is known to be with foal. Strict attention will be paid, but no responsibility for accidents. Openckon Sorrel is seventeen hands high, handsomely formed, has a blaze in his face and one of his hind feet white, is strong and active, he is six years old this spring, and is remarkably well calculated for either saddle or draft. There are a number of his colts now to be seen, that are allowed by good judges to be equal, if not superior to the colts of any other horse that ever stood in this County.

On Friday night last, some villain broke into one of the houses of John L. Vernier, Esq. and cut open every of goods in the store, the only thing missed as yet was fearnought cloth. An earthquake was felt on the 3rd ult. over all the Isle of Grand Marian—and has been thus described to us: a Reporter was awakened just after daybreak by the of a loud sound, and a violent shaking of the house, at the same instant. The shaking ceased very soon—but it and, he thinks, continued from 10 to 13 seconds after he awoke, lessening gradually till it entirely died away, some of the inhabitants say it was perceived much longer. The general opinion was that it lasted a full min All agree in describing the motion as violent, and to have been very loud. The weather was re markably fine and serene, with a light breeze of wind at the Weatherward. The previous day it had been un usually mild for the season. During the day the weath er turned every fine and warm—No wind being easterly light. It is a loud and solemn note from the cavern of the hills, this swelling sound of the Voice of the God of heaven and earth, awfully uttered through his works, speaking from an extensive Region, how far yet we know not— but we already trace it from Boston to Fred erickton, nearly four hundred miles.

His Majesty, Ferdinand VII, by the Grace of God, and by the Constitution of the Span ish Monarchy, King, etc. having determined to take the oath to support the Constitution be fore a Provisional Junta, until the Cortes shall be assembled, has appointed The Cardinal Louis de Bourbon, to be President, Lt. Gen. Balederos, Vice President, and the Bishop of Valladolid, Don Francisco Cebadilla, Yecemoros, Sanchio, Crespo de Tejada, Tarrus, Ignacio Pezuela, and the Count of Tavorada, to be Members; and who are to exercise the pow ers of the Cortes, provisionally, until that body has assembled. Signed by the King, March 5, 1814, and directed to Don Joseph Garcia de la Torre. MARCH 6. Yesterday his Majesty issued a royal order directing the immediate liberation of per sons imprisoned for political reasons, and that the order be communicated by circu lar. CATALINA.

a little before 11 o'clock, the shock of an Earthquake was distinctly felt in Milledgeville, Georgia. It lasted only a few seconds, but was so severe as to create a tremulous motion of objects and rapid jarring of the windows. The state house bell struck repeatedly from the violence of the shock. The Indian bowed assent and set out upon his journey; but had not gone far before he met another Indian belonging to the Governor, to whom he gave the letter, and told him his master had sent him to meet with him, and bade him return with that letter to Boston as soon as he could. The poor Indian carried it with great diligence, and received a sound whipping for his pains. The other Indian came no more, but at meeting with some of his nation, the Governor saw this fellow among the rest, and asked him, very severely, 'how he could dare to serve him such a trick?' The Indian looked him full in the face, placed his fore-finger on his forehead, and replied, 'head work, Governor, head work.' James F. The argument drawn from the danger of slave population, during critical emergencies, frequently receives practical illustration. In the great fire in Savannah, for instance, and in similar calamities in slave-holding States, that part of the population, which is the most useful and active, is required to protect the property and the lives of the citizens from the plunder and insurrection of the Blacks. So it has been and would be in a foreign invasion; and instead of being of any utility at such times, the slaves may be ranked, in one case with the destructive fury of the element, and in the other with the sweeping hostility of the enemy. Their knives, instead of being drawn to resist invasion or rebellion, would be whetted with cannibal satisfaction, to revenge their wrongs upon the hearts of their oppressors. It is only by ignorance and want of concert, that they are now kept in subjection; but the ignorant have feelings and passions, which some master spirit, even among the Blacks, may arouse and direct; and if so, that day will be one of awful retribution! African School. - There are seven young men of colour, now pursuing a course of Theological studies in New-Jersey, under the instruction of a respectable clergyman. Their design in obtaining an education is to become qualified for the gospel ministry. They appear to possess good talents, and exemplary piety, and are making great proficiency in their pursuits. We understand that there are others of equal promise, who wish to obtain an education for the same purpose, if sufficient funds could be procured. This object, in connection with the colonization of the people of colour on the coast of Africa, must appear to the benevolent mind as highly worthy of patronage. The Rev. Mr. Huntington, from New-Jersey, visited this city the last week, on his way to Boston, for the purpose of soliciting aid in this benevolent object. We are happy to state that the citizens of New-Haven, with their usual liberality, contributed about two hundred dollars—Relig. Intelligencer. Spanish Treaty—We have (says the N. Y. France at that time had insulted and injured us more than Spain has done; and France then was equally as incapable of affording protection to that island as Spain now is in her colony of West Florida.  Sir—What has been your conduct hereto fore towards Spain? When she denied to you the right of navigating the Mississippi, which may be considered one of the natural rights of this country—a right too consecrated by treaty; did you go to war? When she denied to you the right of deposit at New Orleans, in violation of a recent treaty, did you go to war? When she subjected you to a duty of tribute, as some would call it, on the Mobile; did you go to war? Nay, sir, when she committed spoliations on your commerce, and suffered them to be committed within her jurisdiction by another power, did you go to war? No, sir. And why? Spain was then the ally of France. What has Spain recently done to provoke this act of aggression upon her territory? What new offence has she given to the U. States? Is it her determination to resist the usurpation of France, or is it, that she has lately sent a minister to express her friendly disposition, to treat with you for both the Floridas, and pay what she owes us for spoliations?

No. XIII.—BRITISH NOTE. From the British to the American Ministers, Oct. 31. The undersigned have the honor to acknowledge the receipt of the note addressed to them by the American plenipotentiaries on the 24th inst. in which they object to the basis of uti possidetis proposed by the undersigned as that on which they are willing to treat in regard to part of the boundaries between the dominions of his Majesty and those of the United States. The American plenipotentiaries in their note, of the 13th inst. requested the undersigned to communicate to them the project of a treaty embracing all the points insisted on by Great Britain, engaging on their part to deliver immediately after a contra projet as to all the articles deemed material by the United States, and omitted in the project of the undersigned.

Sacheuse told them that he was a man, that he had a father and mother like themselves; and pointing to the South, said that he came from a distant country in that direction. To this they answered, ‘That cannot be, there is nothing but ice there.’ They again asked, ‘What creatures these were?’ pointing to the ships; to which Sacheuse replied, ‘that they were houses made of wood.’ This they seemed still to discredit, answering, ‘No, they are alive, we have seen them move their wings.’—Sacheuse now enquired of them what they themselves were; to which they replied, they were men, and lived in that direction pointing to the north; that there was much water there; and that they had come here to fish for sea-unicorns. It was then agreed that Sacheuse should pass the chasm to them, and he accordingly returned to the ship to make his report, and to ask for a plank. “During the whole of this conversation, I had been employed with a good telescope in observing their motions, and beheld the first man approach with every mark of fear and distrust, looking frequently behind to the other two, and beckoning to come on, as if for support.

But, in truth, the heart of human life is but imperfectly discovered from its countenance; and before we can know what the summer, or what the winter yields for enjoyment or trial to our country's peasantry, we must have conversed with them in their fields and by their firesides; and made ourselves acquainted with the powerful ministry of the seasons, not over those objects alone that feed the eye and the imagination, but over all the incidents, occupations, and events that modify or constitute the existence of the poor. I have a short and simple story to tell of the winter-life of the moorland cottager—a story but of one evening—with few events and no signal catastrophe—but which may happily please those hearts whose delight it is to think on the humble under-plots that are carrying on in the great drama of life. Two cottagers, husband and wife, were sitting by their cheerful peat fire one winter evening, in a small lonely hut on the edge of a wild moor, at some miles distance from any other habitation. There had been, at one time, several huts of the same kind erected close together, and inhabited by families of the poorest class of day-labourers who found work among the distant farms, and at night returned to dwellings which were rent-free, with their little gardens won from the waste.

Still there was no trace of poor Hannah Lee; and one of his dogs at last came close to his feet, worn out entirely and afraid to leave its master, while the other was mute, and, as the shepherd thought, probably unable to force its way out of some hollow or through some floundering drift. Then he all at once knew that Hannah Lee was dead; and dashed himself down in the snow in a fit of passion. It was the first time that the youth had ever been sorely tried; all his hidden and unconscious love for the fair lost girl had flowed up from the bottom of his heart; and at once the sole object which had blessed his life, and made him the happiest of the happy, was taken away and cruelly destroyed; so that sullen, wrathful, baffled and despairing, there he lay, cursing his existence, and in too great agony to think of prayer. "God," he then thought, "has forsaken me: and why should he think on me, when he suffers one so good and beautiful as Hannah, to be frozen to death." God thought both of him and Hannah; and though his infinite mercy forgave the sinner in his wild turbulence of passion. William Grieve had never gone to bed without joining in prayer; and he revered the Sabbath-day and kept it holy.

About the year 1801, the Hessian flies first made their appearance in the neighborhood of the city of Richmond; we saw but little mischief that year. But in 1802, they were much more destructive—1803, they swept whole fields—about the same in 1804— and then for some time, I spent in the western parts of North Carolina—and heard but little about them for they did not trouble us there. In 1807 I returned to Virginia, and have continued here ever since. In 1809, the Locusts (as we call them) made their appearance—this is the third time I had been where they were numerous. But they excited my attention more than ever before—and I was determined to find out their history if possible—and therefore watched them for hours together at a time; and finally found that the males made all the noise, being prepared with proper organs—and the females did all the mischief, being prepared with proper weapons—having in their hinder extremity, a weapon that opened out somewhat like a lancet, with which they made incisions in the limbs, and young growth, and there deposited their eggs. And upon examination, I found the Hessian fly possessed every shape, and form of the locust; and I felt fully bent to find out this destructive insect—and caught and examined many, and found they had the like weapon in their hinder parts, that opens like a lancet.

Franklinton, (Ohio) Dec 23 1812, Captain Hite has just arrived, ex- press from Col. Campbell’s detachment which it will be recollected, left this place on the 18th ult on a secret expedition. From him we learn the following account of a most obstinate and hard fought battle, in which the valor, intrepidity and firmness of the American troops shone with a lustre which has never been surpassed during the present war. On the 17th, after marching all night Col. Campbell with his com- mand, arrived at one of the Maumee towns, and instantly charged upon the town drove the savages a- cross the Maumee river, killed seven of them and took 37 prisoners— only two of our men were killed in this skirmish. While contending with the enemy at this town, they sent a runner to another of the towns about three miles distant, which was immediately evacuated.

The Courier of the 8th contains an account (by the Anhalt Mail) of a severe conflict having been fought at Coburg, between the Prussians and French in which the latter were beaten. The French wished to pass through but Gen. Blucher, the commandant of Coburg, would not let them—hence the action, in which the French had 200 killed, and 300 wounded. The Courier of the 9th, says that the report of the action, produced a very great sensation in London, and obtained general credit— but throws a damper upon the information, by saying, "that government have received intelligence from that quarter to the 27th, which takes no notice of the event mentioned." The subscriber, formerly of the city of Albany, and now residing at Edwardsville, (Illinois) will act as agent for such gentlemen as may think proper to confide to him their business in relation to the recording of deeds for Bounty Lands, and the payment of taxes upon the same and other lands. All deeds of Bounty Lands in Illinois are required by law to be recorded at the Recorder's office in Edwardsville; and, in order to hold against a subsequent sale of the land, it is necessary to have them filed for record within one year from their dates. The original patent from the United States is not required to be recorded. Letters, free of postage, enclosing deeds for recording, and three dollars (Land Office money) for each deed of the usual length, will be promptly attended to. By a law of the state of Illinois, it is required that every non-resident owning land, “whether by deed, entry, bond for conveyance, patent, grant, or otherwise, or where the same has been entered in any land office established for the sale of lands lying within this state,” shall, on or before the first day of August in each year, enter the same with the Auditor of public accounts, by presenting to him a list of his lands, the quality and class of each tract of the same, the number of acres in each tract, and the county in which it is situated.

The stars were in thousands—and the full moon was risen. It was almost light as day, and the snow, that seemed encrusted with diamonds, was so hardened by the frost, that his daughter’s homeward feet would leave no mark on its surface. He had been toiling all day among the distant Castle-woods, and, stiff and wearied as he now was, he was almost tempted to go to meet his child—but his wife’s kind voice dissuaded him, and returning to the fireside, they began to talk of her whose image had been so long passing before them in their silence. “She is growing up to be a bonny lassie,” said the mother, “her long and weary attendance on me during my fever last spring kept her down awhile—but now she is sprouting fast and fair as a lily, and may the blessing of God be as dew and as sunshine to our sweet flower all the days she bloometh upon this earth.” “Aye Agnes,” replied the father, “we are not very old yet—though we are getting older—and a few years will bring her to woman’s estate, and what thing on earth, think ye, human or brute, would ever think of injuring her? Why I was speaking about her yesterday to the minister as he was riding by, and he told me that none answered at the examination in the kirk so well as Hannah.

Much is forgiven to the human heart by him who so fearfully framed it; and God is not slow to pardon the love which one human being bears to another, in its frailty; even though that love forget or feigns his own sleeping providence. His voice has told us to love one another; and William loved Hannah in simplicity, innocence and truth. That she should perish was a thought so dreadful, that in its agony, God seemed a ruthless blow. "Blow, blow, blow, and drift us up forever; we cannot be farther asunder. O Hannah! Hannah! think ye not that the fearful God has forsaken us!" As the boy groaned these words passionately through his quivering lips, there was a sudden lowness in the air, and he heard the barking of his absent dog, while the one at his feet hurried off in the direction of the sound, and soon loudly joined the cry. It was not a bark of surprise, or anger, or fear; but of recognition and love. William sprung up from his bed in the snow, and with his heart knocking at his bosom ever to sickness, he rushed headlong through the drift with a giant’s strength, and fell down half dead with joy and terror beside the body of Hannah Lee.

In such a state of things, the chances of renewed affection became every day still more remote. A veil of mystery was thrown over the whole affair, which has never been completely removed, and in the end, the princess, after being delivered of her late lamented daughter, became the inhabitant of a separate establishment on Marlborough. This final separation took place in April, 1796, twelve months after the marriage, and three months after the birth of the princess Charlotte of Wales. It was preceded by some negotiation, in which lord and lady Cholmondeley took part, by conveying to his royal highness a desire on the part of the princess, to know the terms on which they wish to live. This produced the following letter from the prince: ? Windsor Castle, April 30, 1796. Madam - As lord Cholmondeley informs me that you wish I would define, in writing, the terms upon which we are to live, I shall endeavour to explain myself upon that head with as much clearness, and with as much propriety, as the nature of the subject will admit. Our inclinations are not in our power; nor should either of us be held answerable for the other, because nature has not made us suitable to each other.
