Extract of a letter from St. Francisville, La J to the Editors, dated May 5, 1811. "There was a very serious and distressing accident happened nearly opposite this place yesterday morning about 5 o'clock. The steamboat Constitution (formerly the Oliver Evans) passing down the river from Natchez to New Orleans, burst her boiler, and every person in the cabin, 11 in number, scalded to death—some lived two or three hours, some five or six, and two or three lived about 20 hours. The captain of the boat, the engineer, and one or two sailors that were in the after part of the boat, were the only persons that escaped uninjured. I annex a list of the persons killed, which you may publish if you please: William Yarnell, Virginia. E. Frazier, merchant, Gibson Port, M. T. Thomas Brown, Scotland. W. M'Farland, Washington county, Ky. Joseph Carpenter, Alex. Philpot, Henrico county, Va. Wm. Steel, merchant, Warrenton, M. T. Peter Hubert, New Orleans and Baltimore. Larkin, silversmith, Natchez. A List of the Titles of all the Acts, Resolutions, and Treaties, approved and ratified at the first session of the Sixteenth Congress, which was begun and held in the city of Washington, on Monday the sixth day of December, 1819, and ended on the 18th day of May, 1820; with a brief abstract of the provisions of each law; together with the branch of the Legislature in which each act, etc. originated, the dates of approval, and the dates, respectively, at which they were published in the National Intelligencer. (Concluded.) LIGHTHOUSES. 1. An act to authorize the erection of a lighthouse on one of the Isles of Shoals, near Portsmouth, New Hampshire. [Senate. 15th May, 1820.] 20th May, 1820. Besides the lighthouse designated in the title, this act provides for other lighthouses, buoys, light vessels, piers, and bells, in other situations, and makes appropriations of money for them. LOAN. 1. An act to authorize the President of the United States to borrow a sum not exceeding three millions of dollars. (House. 15th May, 1820.) 17th May, 1820. The President empowered to borrow not exceeding 3,000,000 dollars, at five per cent, reimbursable at any time after 1st Jan. 1832; or at six per cent, reimbursable at the pleasure of the United States.

On the subject of the fisheries, the undersigned expressed with so much frankness at the conference already referred to, the views of their government, that they consider any further observations unnecessary at the present time. On the question of the boundary between the dominions of his majesty and those of the United States, the undersigned are led to expect from the discussion which the subject has already undergone, that the North Western boundary from the Lake of the Wood to the Mississippi (the intended arrangement of 1503) will be admitted without objection. In regard to the other boundaries, the American plenipotentiaries in their note of August 24th appeared in some measure to object to the propositions then made by the undersigned as not being the basis of uti possidetis. The undersigned are willing to treat on that basis, subject to such modifications as mutual convenience may be found to require; and they trust that the American plenipotentiaries will show by their ready acceptance of this basis, that they duly appreciate the moderation of his majesty’s government in so far consulting the honor and fair pretensions of the United States as in the relative dwelling of the two countries, to authorize such a proposition. The undersigned avail themselves of this opportunity to renew to the American Plenipotentiaries the assurance of their high consideration. Signed, GAMBIER.

John W. B. Thompson, The subject of impressment has been so repeatedly mentioned that many people pass it over without a moment's reflection. So difficult is it for the unfortunate captive to forward to his native country positive proof of his citizenship, at the same time he calls for the protection of his government, that a long time must elapse before his release can be obtained, even should his enslavers deliver him up on his being identified. The British have so extensive a navy that it is very convenient for them to pass a man over from ship to ship, and thus elude the search of his friends and government after they are prepared to prove his citizenship. In one of the cases before us, it appears that Geo. Herrick was impressed on board the Impetuous, while there refuses to fight against his country or to aid and assist her enemy, and demands to be considered a prisoner of war. For this manly and patriotic conduct, himself, with three others equally noble Americans, are seized by the ruffian hand of violence, dragged to the gangway, and there unmercifully flogged! We afterwards find him shuffled on board another ship, the Stately, where the Captain, perceiving that the poor man is a stranger, very kindly offers to release him, if he can find someone to make oath that he is an American.

Draguignan, (near the Alps) July 5. A field of forty thousand plants of cotton, in the isle of Porquerolles and Hyères all promise well. Fifty thousand yellow and white beets have been sown between the rows of cotton. The fields of indigo, potatoes and sugar cane, indicate that our climate and soil are suitable to their growth. LIVERPOOL, July 16. A Boy from an American vessel was received into the public infirmary here a few days ago, under very peculiar circumstances. While yet on the American coast, the legs of this unfortunate youth which had been very badly frostbitten, exhibited such symptoms of gangrene that nothing but immediate amputation seemed to have chance of saving his life. In this desperate extremity, with no surgeon at hand, an Italian sailor offered his services, which after some deliberation were accepted. He cut off the poor boy’s legs with a common case knife, and sawed the bones with a large carpenter’s saw, and bound up the stumps in the best manner he could.

Tecumseh Mills, Hanover, June 3. 182— WFEY’s MlLLS &C.—This valuable estate is in Albemarle County, on the main road from Char lottesville to Staunton about 5 miles from Rock fish Gap, and 3 from James River—the situation beautiful and healthy. The Tract of Land contains 200 acres, equal in quality to any in the county a part of which is well set in Clover. The improvements are a Brick Dwelling House, two stories high, 40 by 20 with a rear the whole size of the house, a Smokehouse, three stories, 20 by 18, the first, stone 30 by 34—two pair stones 6 feet, with Raines’s and Evan’s improvements on them, a never fails. It is well known, this Mill is equal to any in the state for making good flour: a Distillery with three kettles and mash-tubs &c. A Grist-Mill, to which is attached a Store and Counting House. Two Stables, well finished and substantial, Overseer’s Home.

It is evidently his intention to press the war along; the whole extent of our seaboard, in the hope of exhausting equally the spirits of the people, and the national resources. There is also reason to presume, that it is the intention to press the war from Canada on the adjoining states, while attempts are made on the city of New York, and other important points, with a view to the vain project of dismemberment or subjugation. It may be inferred likewise to be a part of the scheme, to continue to invade this part of the union, while a separate force attacks the state of Louisiana, in the hope of taking possession of the city of New Orleans, and of the mouth of the Mississippi, that great inlet and key to the commerce of all that portion of the United States, lying westward of the Alleghany mountains— The peace in Europe having given to the enemy a large disposable force, has essen tially favored these objects. The advantage which a great naval superiority gives to the enemy, by enabling him to move troops from one quarter to another, from Maine to the Mississippi, a coast of two thousand miles extent is very considerable. Even a small force moved in this manner for the purpose avowed by the British commanders, cannot fail to be sensibly felt, more especially by those who are most exposed to it.

Passports were signed by his excellency Prince Paul Esterhazy, on the application of the Queen, for one of her Majesty's couriers to pass through the Austrian dominions in Italy. Carlos is the courier appointed to this service; and it is said, we know not how correctly, that one of the objects of his mission is to accompany the celebrated Bergami to London. The courier left London on Monday morning. Addresses to the Queen were daily and hourly received by her majesty. The Ladies of Edinburgh have sent the Queen an address. The females of Exeter have also sent the Queen an address. On the 12th of September, her majesty received addresses—three from Exeter; one from the parish of St. Sidwell; one from Montrose; from the males and females of Leeds; from Ross; one in verse from the ladies of Bristol, with 1,047 signatures; from Carlisle, Hereford, Ipswich, etc. On the 13th of September, the Captains, Mates and Seamen of the British merchant service whose vessels were lying in the river, presented to her majesty addresses at Brandenburgh House by a numerous deputation. The procession arrived in the Strand, about one o’clock, from the city. It was headed by a gentleman on a white horse holding in his hand a blue flag, fringed with white, inscribed with “The Seamen’s Address.” Then followed the seamen, walking four abreast, all decently dressed, and wearing white flowers.

In consideration of the cession aforesaid, the United States agree to pay to the Chippewa nation of Indians, annually, forever, the sum of one thousand dollars in silver; and do also agree that all annuities due by any former treaty to the said tribe, shall be hereafter paid in silver. Art. 5. The stipulation contained in the treaty of Greenville, relative to the right of the Indians to hunt upon the land ceded, while it continues the property of the United States, shall apply to this treaty; and the Indians shall, for the same term, enjoy the privilege of making sugar upon the same land, committing no unnecessary waste upon the trees. Art. 6. The United States agree to pay to the Indians the value of any improvements which they may be obliged to abandon, in consequence of the lines established by this treaty, and which improvements add real value to the land. Art. 7. The United States reserve to themselves authority the right to make through any part of the land reserved by this treaty. Art. 8. The United States engage to provide and support a blacksmith for the Indians, at such time as the President of the U. States may think proper, and to furnish the Chippewa Indians with such farming utensils and cattle, and to employ such persons to aid them in their agriculture, as the President may deem expedient. Art. 9.

The British have brought against us the Tomahawk and the scalping knife, and now cause it to come forth against us with the flaming torch, Let her come if she dare, with this British Traveller for a guide, and we will soon convince her that we can carry the torch of war farther and longer than she can. Is London Liverpool, and Bristol cities entirely of rock, or made entirely of iron? Are their cities made of incombustible materials? Is there nothing about them inflammable? And are they all surrounded with walls nine g? Is *this burning and destroying game that one alone can play ? The terrified British traveller says that never was there a foe, until now, that possessed her language, that key to her counsels”—and he might have added that detector of her likes. “Never could a foreigner, until now, introduce himself, unnoticed, into her parliament, army or navy or manufactories’—and might have added, never until now could a foreigner set our cities on fire so snugly as could these Americans, were they so diabolically disposed, as to copy the example of the Bulwark of our religion. We would advise my Lord Castlereagh to shut his British Traveller up in a mad-house, and never send him across the Atlantic again, lest he should put “toys of desperation,” into the hands of the humane and merciful Americans.

If you give it the construction the gentleman contends for, to wit, that the second member of the passage is an extension of the description given by the first, then the second includes the first, and of consequence the first would be nugatory and superfluous; which would be doing violence to the gentleman's own rule of construction. But if the gentleman will insist on giving to the second member an enlarged or extended sense, it may be done by applying it to the western boundaries of Louisiana. It is said that when France ceded Louisiana to Spain in 1762, the country extended on the west to the river Sabine, and that Spain, prior to the treaty of 1800, detached from Louisiana the territory south of the waters emptying into the Red River, and erected it into a new province under the name of the "Province of Texas." Sir, the operations on the Sabine are memorable. It is well known how mysteriously they were suspended by an arrangement in 1806, by which it was agreed that the Spaniards should recross the Sabine, and that the Americans should not extend their settlements so far as that river. And for this purpose, to prevent collisions, until the difference should be settled, instructions were given that no surveys should be made west of a meridian passing by Natchitoches.

The Commissioner adds:—"The ships now building under the act for the gradual increase of the navy," are regular two-deck ships, and unless guns be mounted on their gunway, which is not usual, they will mount the same number of guns with our present seventy-fours. Hitherto no ship having put two decks, has rated higher than an eighty; and we beg leave to observe, that the ships now building, are of inferior capacity to some ships of other nations; having but two decks. For some weeks past we have been visited with all the horrors, and at the same time been favored with all the advantages which attend the winter season in this climate. While we have been shuddering with the severity of the weather, we have been delighted with the appearance of our country friends, who by the excellence of the traveling have been enabled to avail themselves of the high price and ready sale of their produce in our market. Indeed, considering all advantages, we do not hesitate to avow our preference of the situation of this state to that of the more renowned regions of the south and west, whose superior fertility of soil is here counterbalanced by facility of transportation to ready and convenient markets, and whose mildness and equability of climate is here compensated by the vigor and energy, both mental and corporal, produced by our sudden transitions. Copy of a letter from Rear Admiral Sawyer, Commander in Chief of his Majesty’s ships and vessels on the Coast of North America, to John Wilson Croker, Esq. dated on board the Africa, at Bermuda, the 11th June, 1811. Sir—Inclosed I transmit to you for the information of the Lords Commissioners of the Admiralty, a copy of a letter from captain Arthur Batt Bingham, commander of his majesty’s sloop Little Belt, received this day from lord James Townsend, captain of his majesty’s ship Aeolus, and Senior Officer at Halifax; by which their Lordships will perceive that he was attacked on the evening of the 16th May last when cruising between Cape Henry and Cape Hatteras, by the U. S. frigate President, of 44 guns, commanded by Com. Rodgers; and that after a close action of three-quarters of an hour the American ship made sail from him.

The commissioners have found it necessary to send these pieces from the southward. They have accordingly been ordered, and the keel of a line of battle ship will, therefore, be laid at Portsmouth, (N.H.) early the ensuing spring. Of the line of battle ships now on the stocks, the one at Washington will be ready to launch the ensuing spring. Those at New York and Norfolk can, if required, be prepared for launching next fall; and during the ensuing year, considerable progress will be made in building those at Philadelphia, Boston, and Portsmouth. All the tarred rope, excepting the standing rigging, required for the line of battle ship building at this place, has been contracted for. Canvas being a perishable article, the commissioners have not deemed it expedient to contract for any for the gradual increase. They have, however, satisfactorily ascertained that canvas of a superior quality, of American manufacture, can be procured on moderate terms, and in sufficient quantities. Owing to the imperfection in the manufacture of heavy anchors, which have hitherto been supplied from private shops, and the high price asked for them, the navy commissioners have established at this yard an anchor shop, where all the anchors for the ships authorized by the act for the gradual increase of the navy will be made.

Confidence in him was never alienated. No arm was raised for their defence; the nation and the army flew to meet their deliverer. Thus then, in lamenting that throne to which the people had raised him, the Emperor re-established the people in their most sacred rights. He now but recall to their execution, those decrees of the representatives of the nation; he returns to govern by the only principle of legitimacy which France has acknowledged for twenty years, and to which all the authorities were bound by oaths, from which the will of the people only could have freed them. The Emperor is called to guarantee anew by institutions, (and he has engaged to do it, in his proclamations, to the nation and to the army) all the liberal principles: individual liberty and equality of rights, the liberty of the press, and the abolition of censors, freedom of worship, the vote for contributions and taxes by the representatives of the nation, freely elected, the national property of every origin, the independence and inviolability of the tribunals, the responsibility of ministers, and all the agents of authority. The better to preserve the rights and the obligations of the people and the monarch, the national institutions will be revived in a grand assembly of representatives, as already announced by the Emperor.

It was not nine o'clock when the storm came down from Glen Scrae upon the Black moss, and now in a pause of silence the clock struck twelve. Within these three hours William and Hannah had led a life of trouble and of joy, that had enlarged and kindled their hearts within them; and they felt that henceforth they were to live wholly for each other’s sakes. His love was the proud and exulting love of a deliverer, who, under Providence, had saved from the frost and the snow the innocence and the beauty of which his young passionate heart had been so desperately enamored; and he now thought of his own Hannah Lee ever more moving about in his father’s house, not as a servant, but as daughter; and when some few happy years had gone by, his own most beautiful and most loving wife. The innocent maiden still called him her young master; but was not ashamed of the holy affection which she now knew that she had long felt for the fearless youth on whose bosom she had thought herself dying in that cold and miserable moor.

This country had, in fact, become the theatre of every species of lawless adventure. With little population of its own, the Spanish authority almost extinct and the colonial governments in a state of revolution, having no pretensions to it, and sufficiently employed in their own concerns, it was in a great measure derelict, and the object of cupidity to every adventurer. A system of buccaneering was rapidly organizing over it, which menaced, in its consequences, the lawful commerce of every nation, and particularly of the United States; while it presented a temptation to every people, on whose seduction its success principally depended. In regard to the United States, the pernicious effects of this unlawful combination, was not confined to the ocean; the Indian tribes have constituted the effective force in Florida. With these tribes, these adventurers had formed, at an early period, a connection, with a view to avail themselves of that force to promote their own projects of accumulation and aggrandizement. It is to the interference of some of these adventurers, in misrepresenting the claims and titles of the Indians to land, and in practicing on their savage propensities, that the Seminole war is principally to be traced. Men who thus connect themselves with savage communities, and stimulate them to war, which is always attended on their part with acts of barbarity the most shocking, deserve to be viewed in a worse light than the savages.

The design of this malicious suggestion is, first, to prejudice the public against the British government as aiming to reacquire the dominion of these States, a thing, which you, Sir, do not believe, though you are willing to impose upon your more ignorant partizans;—and, secondly, to connect the odium of such a project with the inhabitants of Boston, and the federalists generally, whom you would represent as aiming to divide the Union, for the wicked purpose of throwing themselves under the dominion of Great-Britain. This you know to be untrue, and the suggestion is as mean as it is malevolent. — I have had access to the files of British papers at the “Athenaeum.” I have inquired of merchants, who in the way of business can see them more than you can do—but I can find no one who ever met with such a sentiment. It cannot then be true, as you have stated, “that of late years it has been an avowed object in the ministerial functions." Of a minor species of distortion, misrepresentation, and departure from facts, there are numerous examples in your Excellency’s speech, of which I dare say you will excuse me for selecting only a few—perhaps I shall not stand so readily forgiven by the public for thus dishonoring the State by proving such charges against the First Magistrate to be true.

In that view, a soil to be perpetually fertile, must be endowed with power to retain air and water sufficient for its plants; and at the same time must be of a nature that will not harden by moisture. Salt promises to answer all these different purposes; for it will prevent the soil from being hardened by water, and also invigorate it by its retentive alkaline and acid qualities. These suggestions almost amount to a proof, that common salt is that desirable object, which when properly used will be found the true acid solvent, so essentially necessary to prepare matter proper for the food of plants. Salt, if generally used, would probably be the cheapest, best, and most universal manure in nature. When the English farmer intends to turn his land to tillage, in autumn he sows a double quantity of salt in order to destroy grass, rushes, weeds, fern, worms, soils, etc. The whole is by that means converted into a rich manure, which supports three successive crops, and leaves the soil, after all, in good condition. This mode of preparation appears to be superior to any other. Some farmers have sown 100 lbs. of salt on one acre of land as soon as ploughed, in order to meliorate the soil, before the seed is sown. They have also laid on meadow grounds, as soon as mown, and pasture lands, in winter, about the same quantity.

A message from Council informed that Council have passed the bill to prevent Justices of the Peace from issuing blank process; and have agreed to the resolution for prosecuting the commissioners appointed to superintend the management of the Milford and Owego turnpike lottery, with an amendment; which was read, and agreed to by the House. The message further informed that Council had receded from part of their amendments to the Ordinary and Surrogate bill, but insisted on the residue; whereupon, the House receded from their disagreement to the latter, and ordered the bill to be re-engrossed. The message further informed that Council had passed the bill relative to printing a new edition of the public laws, with amendments; which were read, part agreed to, part disagreed to, and the Clerk ordered to inform Council thereof. The message further informed that Council had disagreed to part of the amendments to the bill regulating the descent of real estate, and have agreed to a part of said amendments, with an amendment: whereupon, the House insisted on their amendment, and appointed Messrs. Griffith, Elmer, and Ten Eyck, a committee of conference. Messrs. Newbold and Thompson were appointed by Council.—Adj’d. Tuesday, June 13—nine o’clock. A message informed that Council had passed the bill to provide for the distribution of the public laws, with amendments—Read, the first agreed to, the other disagreed to, and the Clerk ordered to inform Council.

The undersigned, having declared in their note of 21st of August, that although instructed and prepared to enter into an amicable discussion of all the points on which difference or uncertainty had existed, and which might hereafter tend to interrupt the harmony of the two countries, they would not make the conclusion of the peace at all depend upon a successful result of the discussion, and having since agreed to the preliminary article proposed by the British government, had believed that the negotiation, already so long protracted, could not be brought to an early conclusion, otherwise than by the communication of a projet, embracing all the other specific propositions which Great Britain intended to offer. They repeat their request in that respect, and will have no objection to a simultaneous exchange of both parties. This course will fairly bring into discussion the other topics embraced in the last note of the British plenipotentiaries, to which the undersigned have thought it necessary to advert at the present time. And the superior liberality has been asserted of southern gentlemen over those of the north, in all contributions to moral institutions, for Bible and missionary societies. Sir, I understand too well the pursuit of my purpose to be decoyed and drawn off into the discussion of a collateral subject. I have no inclination to controvert these assertions of comparative liberality. Although I have no idea they are founded in fact, yet, because it better suits the object of my present argument, I will on this occasion, admit them to the fullest extent. And what is the result? Southern gentlemen, by their superior liberality in contributions to moral institutions, justly stand in the first rank; and hold the first place in the brightest page of the history of our country. But, turn over this page and what do you behold? You behold them contributing to teach the doctrines of Christianity in every quarter of the globe. You behold them legislating to secure the ignorance and stupidity of their own slaves! You behold them prescribing by law, penalties against the man that dares teach a negro to read. Such, sir, is the statute law of the state of Virginia. Mr. Hascett and Mr. Tyler said that there was no such law in Virginia. No, sir, said Mr. T. I have misspoken myself: I ought to have said, such is the statute law of the state of Georgia.

The resistance of the Bourbons to the wishes of the people brought on their fall and their banishment from the French Territory. Twice have the people consecrated by their votes, the new form of government established by their representatives. In the year 8, Bonaparte, already crowned with victory, was raised to the government by the national sentiment; a constitution created the consular mandate. The Senatus Consultum of the 16th Thermidor, year 10, named Bonaparte consul for life. The Senatus Consultum of the 28th Floreal, year 12, conferred upon Napoleon the imperial dignity and made it hereditary in his family. These three solemn acts were submitted to the acceptance of the people, who sanctioned them by nearly four million votes. Thus for twenty-two years the Bourbons had ceased to reign in France; they were forgotten by their contemporaries, were strangers to our laws, to our institutions, our manners, our glory; the present generation know them only by the recollection of the foreign war which they had excited against the country, and by the intestine dissensions which they had kindled. In 1814, France was invaded by foreign armies, and its Capital occupied. Foreigners created a pretended government. It assembled a majority of the senators, and forced them against their mission and against their will, to cede.

But neither the report of the soldiers, nor that of the bribed Henry answered the purpose, for not one federalist was implicated. The firm and long-tried patriot, Gov. Strong, has been an object of vulgar abuse by those who style themselves the friends of the people. He is abused for not obeying the mandate of a subordinate military officer, when he must have violated the constitution and his oath of office had he complied and permitted the citizens of Massachusetts to be led beyond the limits of the United States to the field of disgrace and death. The vile intrigues and mean artifices, which have been practiced in order to bring odium upon the federalists, merit the contempt and indignation of every honest citizen. A democratic Printer of Newburyport forcibly entered his office in the night, and committed outrage on his types, books, and furniture, and then charged the infamous deed to the federalists of that place, with a view of raising his sinking cause. This was proved by the solemn oath of a witness who saw the outrage, and to whom the stratagem was communicated by the perpetrator. By falsehood and intrigue sin entered into this world; and by the same instruments, the dominant party gained, and still hold their ascendancy; the former brought death and ruin, and the latter, war and poverty.

The Life of Titian: the whole va lued at upwards of 100,000/. This distinguished artist was born at Springfield, in Chester county, Pennsylvania, on the 10th of October. By his father's side he was lineally descended from lord Delaware, who distinguished himself in the wars of Edward the Third, and at the battle of Cressy, under the Mark Prince. Colonel James West, the friend and champion in arms of the celebrated Hampden, was the first of the family who embraced the tenets of the Quakers. The maternal grandfather of the artist, Thomas Pearson, was the well-known confidential friend of William Penn. The object which first called forth West's art and discovered the genius of West, was that of a sleeping infant, whom he one day was placed to watch in the absence of its mother, he being then about seven years old. The child happened to smile in its sleep, when he was so forcibly struck with its beauty, that he seized pens, ink and paper, which happened to lie by him, and endeavoured to delineate a portrait, though at this period he had never seen an engraving or a picture. The year after he was sent to school in the neighborhood. During his hours of leisure he was permitted to draw with pen and ink.

The lands occupied in the country, remote from the cities, are generally converted by their users, into estancias, or large grazing farms for cattle, and chacras for growing grain. The small farms, or quintas in the neighborhood of cities, are in fine order. Those around Buenos Aires, which furnish their market with an ample supply of fruit and vegetables, are, by irrigation, in the highest state of culture. The population, exclusive of the Indians, is now calculated at about one million three hundred thousand: but adding the civilized Indians only, who are of great importance, it would in all, probably exceed two million. The whole population consists of natives of old Spain, and their descendants born in the country, or, as they style themselves, South Americans; of Indians civilized, of unreclaimed, with different casts; of mixed blood, of Africans, and their descendants, or negroes and mulattoes. I could not ascertain, with satisfaction, the population of the different provinces: the province of Buenos Aires, contains about one hundred and twenty thousand, whilst the population of Entre Rios and Banda Oriental is computed at fifty thousand. The city of Buenos Aires contains a population of sixty thousand. The inhabitants of this place appear to be amiable and an interesting people.

Capt. Z. came into the Hook yesterday morning at one o'clock, without a pilot, and anchored in the bay. We have no further intelligence of the outward bound vessels. Two or three small vessels are ashore below. In addition to the account of damage in the city, given in our last, we understand that a number of casks of flaxseed at the foot of Broad street were exposed to the tide, and considerably injured, that a sloop was sunk at New slip: that a large quantity of lumber of various kinds floated from the yards in the vicinity of Corlear's Hook; that the ships Asia and Trident and the brig Minerva, at the docks on the North River, sustained considerable injury; and that nearly a thousand barrels of naval stores, and a considerable quantity of mahogany and other timber, were washed from the wharves and lumber yards at Brooklyn. About two o'clock, the southern mail boat, with five men, attempted to convey the mail over the North River. She had not proceeded, however, more than two or three hundred yards, when she had nearly filled, and was obliged to return. The mail-bags were completely soaked; and in that condition were returned to the Post office, where they were opened, dried, and detained until this morning. The lighter Two Brothers arrived this morning, with dry goods, steel, arms, etc. from the ship General Gadsden which was driven ashore in a former gale.

With these explanatory remarks, sir, I proceed to answer specifically the questions which you have proposed.  1. I am of the opinion that a considerable issue of treasury notes, with the quality of being receivable in subscriptions to a national bank, will have an injurious effect upon the credit of the government and also upon the prospects of a loan for 1815. Because, it will confer gratuitously an advantage upon a class of new creditors, over the present creditors of the government, standing on a footing of at least equal merit. Because, it will excite general dissatisfaction among the present holders of the public debt; and general distrust among the capitalists, who are accustomed to advance their money to the government. Because, a quality of subscribing to the national bank attached to treasury notes exclusively, will tend to depreciate the value of all public debt not possessing that quality; and whatever depreciates the value of the public debt in this way must necessarily impair the public credit."

H. Rice & Co. Oct 28 St Plaster Land for Sale. THE subscriber offers for sale, a Farm, in the County of Fauquier, and state of Virginia, containing 450 acres, one third of which is of the strongest quality, and covered with a heavy growth of red oak and hickory—The residue, (being cleared land,) is in good heart, two thirds of it having been laid down in red clover, and no tract, in the state has afforded higher evidences of its adaption to the plaster of Paris.—There is, on this tract, and at the confluence of the two never failing streams of Broad and Mill Runs, a site for a Merchant Mill, with a fall of 25 feet, surrounded by every material for building, and by the side of a well built Saw-Mill, recently erected. A law has passed for a turnpike road, which will pass by or near this site. For the above property, I will take the moderate price of 20 dollars per acre, with a reasonable allowance, for the water advantages.

There cannot be a doubt but that the result of the Gubernatorial Election in this State has had a very considerable effect in checking the war partisans at Washington, in their mad career. The Friends of Peace in Massachusetts will recollect that though their exertions have been crowned with success as to Governor, still they have an important duty to perform in the choice of Representatives. Every man, without distinction of party, who is op posed to War and Embargoes ought to unite in the election of such men as have been im- partially opposed to these measures. There is every reason to believe that a large proportion of the democratic party are honestly and decid edly opposed to WAR and TAXES;—let such men reflect that by giving their votes for the advocates of Madison and Gerry, they virtu- ally vote in favor of embargoes, war, loans, and taxes. The writer of this article is a Mechanic, no seeker for office and only wishes that the usual channels of business and trade may be opened again, that the sound of the hammer and anvil may be heard in our workshops. "Truth" is assured that the friends of Peace are fully apprized of the arts and in- trigues employed by a few democratic of- fice holders and office seekers, to gull the public.

Aug. 5. The Royal visits on board the Franklin.—On this subject, which has been already more than once noticed in our paper, the Democratic Press remarks "that the statement heretofore made of one of the Princes having fallen down the main hatchway is an error. It was the grand Chamberlain to the Empress, who fell down. His leg was dreadfully broken; it was set by Dr. Salter, of the Franklin, to whom the Emperor sent a rouleau of doubloons, the acceptance of which was politely, but positively declined. "No person holding any office of profit or trust under them, shall, without the consent of Congress, accept of any present, emolument, office or title, of any kind whatever, from any king, prince or foreign state." U.S. Const. Sect. IX. Art. 7. Of sudden and awful deaths, the mails of this morning furnish us with the following cases:— On the 21st ult. Mr. David Langston, a respectable Merchant of Augusta, Georgia, was instantly killed, by a fall from his horse. On the 24th ult. Mrs. Susannah Dunkin, an aged and respectable widow from Chester county, Pennsylvania, having finished a visit to her daughter in Buckingham, started for home in company with a young lady; and having proceeded a short distance, her horse took fright, her wagon was upset, she was thrown with her head and shoulders against a fence, was rendered speechless, and survived only about two hours.

Virginia is entitled by her slaves to seven Representatives more than she otherwise would be—while the state of New-Hampshire elects but six. Whatever may have been said of the slave representation in times of party animosity, we can now, when the hydra of party slumbers in silence, claim a higher motive for introducing the subject than that of exciting invidious dis tinctions. It is the political effect it would have upon the liberties of unborn millions—upon the future destinies of the nation, which ought to ex cite the fearful solicitude of all who would per petuate our republican institutions. We would not foment local jealousies by the stale story of the "Slave-Representation" of the southern states. Having a constitutional power over their slaves, they may enjoy the rich boon without ex citing our envy, or our molesting them in a con ceded privilege, although arbitrary and oppres sive. But it is the undue influence it would give the future states beyond the Mississippi, which now demands the united reprobation of the friends of freedom. That Slavery is subversive of the fundament al principles of a free government—repugnant to every moral and religious precept, and to every benevolent and social feeling—that it de grades its wretched victims almost to a level with the brute creation—that it deprives them of all intellectual enjoyment and the means of happiness, none will have the hardihood to deny.

There is little need to speak of returning recollection, and returning strength. They had all power to weep, and power to pray. The bible had been lying in its place ready for worship; and the father read aloud that chapter in which is portrayed our Saviour’s act of miraculous power, by which he saved Peter from the sea. Soon as the solemn thoughts awakened by that act of mercy so similar to that which had rescued themselves from death had subsided, and they had all risen from prayer, they gathered themselves in gratitude round the little table which had stood so many hours spread; and exhausted nature was strengthened and restored by a frugal and simple meal partaken of in silent thankfulness. The whole story of the night was then calmly recited; and when the mother heard how the stripling had followed her sweet Hannah into the storm, and borne her in his arms through a hundred drifted heaps; and then looked upon her in her pride, so young, so innocent, and so beautiful, she knew, that were the child indeed to become an orphan, there was one, who, if there was either trust in nature, or truth in religion, would guard and cherish her all the days of her life.

From Bermuda.—We learn by the arrival of the brig Rapid, in ten days from Bermuda, that, about the middle of December, the schr. Hiram of New-York, loaded with salt, was taken in tow by the schooner Delight, Capt. Trot, of Bermuda, who tried to get her into port. When discovered she was about 20 leagues E. by S. from that Island, but not a soul on board. At the same time was also discovered a brig, with bright sides, deep waists, both her masts gone, and full of water. Storm in Jamaica.—In our last, we mentioned, that a severe storm had been experienced in the Island of Jamaica, which continued with great force from the 7th to the 11th of November. In addition to the facts then stated, we find it stated in a Charleston paper, that "immense damage was done to the plantations, buildings, &c. throughout the whole extent of the Island. The cane and coffee trees were in particular very seriously injured, and the crops are expected to be reduced in consequence fully one half. A great number of vessels were driven on shore, and many lives lost. Its effects were also very serious at St. Jago, and other ports of the Island of Cuba." The houses of Persia are very slightly constructed, and generally consist of but a few rooms, mostly open towards the north; that is to say, instead of a wall there is a window with panes of variegated colors. It is shut during the night, but left open during the day. In the rooms are a number of cornices and niches, which the poor people generally paint white, but the rich decorate with beautiful flowers and gilding. In every room there is a chimney piece, generally opposite the window; the floor is of stone, and covered with carpets by the affluent, and with mats by the poor. Such, in a few words, is the description of Persian houses. Their rooms are provided with neither chairs, tables, mirrors, nor any article of furniture whatever. The Persians sit cross-legged on the ground; they leave their slippers at the doors of their apartments. Their meals are served upon trays; and if a gentleman does not retire for the night to his harem, a pillow is brought to him, and he sleeps on the same spot. To take a walk is considered highly ridiculous, and even vulgar. When the Persians see a person walk fast, they conclude it must be on business, or they look at him with astonishment, and even think him out of his senses.

Brothers, an accurate observer of the rise and fall of ancient republics, exclaims, that "there cannot be a more certain symptom of the approaching ruin of a state than when a firm adherence to party is fixed upon as the only test of merit, and all the qualifications requisite to a right discharge of every employment, are reduced to this single standard."—What then must be our condition, when men are measured, not by their virtues, not by their talents, not by their adherence to republican principles but by their devotion to their country, but by their attendance at an evening room, their subserviency to an Executive magistrate, and an adherence to his party!

There was a general movement of the allied army towards the French frontier on Monday and Tuesday last. An allied army is collecting to act on the Southern frontier of France. A squadron with sealed orders sailed from Plymouth on Sunday. Ships are fitting to fire rockets and red hot shots. Two French vessels from Brest for Dunkirk with stores are detained by order of Government as is found. The French Consul has advised French vessels to withdraw from British ports. A Declaration of War against France was expected to be promulgated on Wednesday. The ratification of the Treaties have been received from Vienna. Paris papers to May 9, have been received. Murat has offered to retreat to his own territories on condition of a suspension of arms, but this was not acceded to. His troops are almost disorganized. At which time flaxseed oil was accidentally spilled into the ashes; in about 24 hours the ashes were found to be on fire, and wishing to have it fully ascertained, whether it was the oil which occasioned the ashes to take fire, I filled a kettle of cold dry ashes, in which I poured a pint of flaxseed oil, and in 24 hours I examined it, and found that as far as the oil had penetrated, the ashes were in a state of combustion, and on applying some shavings and chips of wood, it immediately caused them to blaze. From an apprehension that many buildings have been consumed by fires from the foregoing cause, I have been induced to give publicity to the fact. JOSEPH ATKINSON. Ellicott's Patapsco Mills, 1 mo. 22nd, 1820. Extract of a letter from a gentleman in Petersburg, (Va.) dated January 22. "Last evening the inhabitants were very much alarmed by a fire on the north side of the town, called Old street. The fire commenced about 8 o’clock, in a stable in the rear of a number of brick buildings. There were upwards of sixteen houses burnt, and a great deal of furniture stolen, broke and destroyed by removing, In fact I never saw the inhabitants in such an alarm: for, if the wind had shifted to the N. W. instead of the S. the destruction of property would have been much greater.

We are so common in this part of the country, as to a great degree of curiosity—but the one before us is something of extraordinary interest. I wish to point out, in the 4th range of the Holland Land Company’s Lands, there is an ancient fort, innate in a marsh or swamp; it covers about five acres of ground —three trees are standing upon it—the earth appears to have been brought from a distance, as its site is dry sand and loam—without the embankments it is great proof of its origin it appears the last resort. At the distance of half a mile from it, on the margin of the swamp, has recently been discovered, on opening the earth, a quantity of human bones of an unusual and large size; the thigh bone appears to be about two inches long er than a common sized man’s—the lower or chin bone covers a large man's chin—the skull bones appear of enormous thickness, and the breast and hip bones are large; on being exposed to the air, they moulder . The bodies appear to have been deposited there by their murders, as they were laid at right angles to each other in every instance; no appearance of bullets were discoverable among them. North of the mountain or great slope, there is no ap pearance of ancient fortifications or tumuli—this appears a strong circumstance in favour of the idea that the mountain once bounded the shores of Lake Ontario.  (Niagara Public Advertiser.  St. John's, June 11.

A sufficient quantity of tow or linen sheets (not woollen) should be brought to put the rolls in. Merino wool can be carded, if those who have it will prepare it in the following manner: Take rain or river water, boil it, to which add an equal quantity of cold water; stir the wool in this until the grease is extracted from the body of the wool and rises to the top, then take it out, rinse it in clear water, dry it, and it is ready for carding. The same preparation will do for the next and succeeding parcels. If the above directions cannot be attended to (which may happen), wash the wool well in a strong soap suds. Work cannot be well done unless these directions are observed. The advantage of a never-failing stream will enable me to accommodate all who may favour me with their custom. As they bore Hannah into the house, this fear gave way to worse, for there upon the hard clay floor lay the mother upon her face, as if murdered by some savage blow. She was in the same deadly swoon into which she had fallen on her husband’s departure three hours before. The old man raised her up, and her pulse was still; so was her heart; her face pale and sunken; and her body cold as ice. "I have recovered a daughter," said the old man, "but I have lost a wife;" and he carried her, with a groan, to the bed, on which he laid her lifeless body. The sight was too much for Hannah, worn out as she was, and who had hitherto been able to support herself in the delightful expectation of gladdening her mother’s heart by her safe arrival. She, too, now swooned away, and as she was placed on the bed beside her mother, it seemed, indeed, that death, disappointed of his prey on the wild moor, had seized it in the cottage, and by the fireside. The husband knelt down by the bedside, and held his wife’s icy hand in his, while William Grieve, appalled and awe-stricken, hung over his Hannah, and inwardly implored God that the night’s wild adventure might not have so ghastly an end.

He had seen Hannah every day, at the fireside, at work, in the kirk, on holidays, at prayers, bringing supper to his aged parents, smiling and singing about the house from morning till night. She had often brought his own meal to him among the hills; and he now found that though he had never talked to her about love, except smilingly and playfully, that he loved her beyond father or mother or his own soul. "I will save thee, Hannah," he cried with a loud sob, "or lie down beside thee in the snow; and we will die together in youth." A wild whistling wind went by him, and the snowflakes whirled so fiercely round his head, that he staggered on for a while in utter blindness. He knew the path that Hannah must have taken, and went for ward shouting aloud, and stopping every twenty yards to listen, for a voice. He sent his well-trained dogs over the snow in all directions, repeating to them her name, "Hannah Lee," that the dumb ani mals might, in their sagacity, know for whom they were searching; and as they looked up in his face, and set off to scour the moor, he almost believed they knew his meaning, and it is probable they did, and were eager to find in her bewilder ment the kind maiden by whose hand they had so often been fed.

To the Humane. It is requested that persons residing near, and those coasting up and down the Delaware, friendly to the cause of Humanity, will use their exertions for the recovery of the body of the late Mrs. COX, who was unfortunately drowned on Sunday morning the 12th instant, while endeavoring to get on board one of the steam boats in the river Delaware, after the same had left the wharf. The exertions of the friends of the deceased in searching for her body at the place and the vi cinity where she was drowned, having proved fruitless, it is supposed the body has been swept down the river by the current, and may probably be between Philadelphia and Wilmington. The deceased when drowned had on a white muslin dress, trimmed with white satin ribbons, large merino shawl with wide border and plum colored shoes, on one of she has a plain gold ring. Her hair is very dark brown, inclin ing much to black.—Her family are very anxious that the body should be found, it is presumed that by the a bove description it will be known to be the body of Mrs. Cox. Information left at No. 114, South Eighth St. will be thankfully received by her distressed relatives, and any expense if ship borne.

Camp among the Comanches, October 20, 1818. Dear Sir—I received yours, bearing date of Oct. 1, 1818, requesting information concerning the animal which has lately been discovered by the natives of the province of Texas, and duly take my pen to answer it. On the 10th August, 1818, there was a report by two chiefs of the tribe of Indians called Lapazsy that a tremendous animal had been discovered in their neighborhood—it was represented by them as an animal so different from what naturalists had delineated in their descriptions; but knowing the veracity of these chiefs, my curiosity was excited to make further enquiry. I went to the place where it was said to have been seen, which was called the Prairie del Grande Ajo, or the Prairie of the Great Spring. I found the place entirely desolate. Every native had retired to a considerable distance. They told me it was a fact that the Caiman de Tierra ruled predominant in that part of their country. They said if I did not believe them, they would take me to a precipice from under which the spring flowed, where I could re.

Some days ago a motion was made before Judge Hall, by the United States’ Attorney, Mr. General Jackson to shew cause why a writ of attachment should not issue against him for a contempt of court, in not returning a writ of Habeas Corpus directed to him, and for the arrest and imprisonment of the Judge. The General appeared in court with a written defense but this was objected to by the attorney of the U. States. The Judge, after consideration, confirmed the objections of the district attorney, and refused to hear the defense read. The motion was made absolute; and the General appeared this morning to receive sentence, which was a fine of One Thousand Dollars. The populace, who are devoted to Jackson, on his leaving court, hurried him into a carriage, and conducted him to both of the Coffee Houses, where he harangued them in an able and suitable manner. They then escorted him to his quarters.  At the mere suggestion of the measure a subscription was opened, limited to one dollar each person, for the purpose of paying the fine; before 8 o’clock the whole sum was raised in this small way; but if the limitation to one dollar each had not been adopted, it would have been raised in two minutes.

The inventor of some improvements in coaches in England, has found an advantage in hanging the carriage, and placing the baggage low, and mentions the following experiments: the patentee suspended 31 lbs. to a line which, passing over a pulley, moved a stage coach which weighed 17 cwt. Seven half hundred weights were then placed on the roof, when it required 55 lbs. more to move it. Three hundred weights were then placed down in the boot, when it required only 15 lbs. thereby proving, that to place the weight nearer on a level with the line of draft, is a saving of labor to the horse of 18 lbs. to every 53 of draft. If a person was to fix a pound weight at the top of a ten foot rod, and run with it at the rate of seven miles in the hour, what would show how much more labor it required than if brought down on a level with the hand." From his subsequent conduct, however, it would seem that he had acted under a different feeling. When the regency came to be established in the person of the prince; when the husband came to be exalted to the rank, the splendor, and power of a king, the princess was still left in her former comparatively obscure and penurious state. His conduct on this occasion excited at the time a considerable degree of animadversion. He had relieved the princess from the consequences of a foul calumny, but her husband being now exalted, her non-exaltation operated with regard to her character, in nearly the same way as her exclusion from court had formerly operated. Her royal highness was again exposed to the malevolent shafts of time-serving malice, but still she preserved a dignified silence, and would probably have remained in retirement, were not her maternal feelings deeply wounded by the increasing restraints which were daily imposed upon her intercourse with her daughter. At length her indignation burst forth. She could no longer exist in silence under the reiterated charges which were preferred against her, and under the mortification of being almost exposed to her daughter's contempt. She wrote a letter to the prince regent, characterized by considerable force and eloquence, in which she challenged a full public disclosure of everything connected with the investigation of 1806. She emphatically said in this letter.

The territorial limits of the state* where slavery is permitted, are already far more extensive, and their lands probably more fertile, than in those where it is interdicted. Slavery is unfavorable to a compact population, and to improvements in the arts; and the mode of cultivation usually practised, tends to promote a rapid expansion of new settlements greatly exceeding a just proportion of the comparative masses of population. An immense territory west of the Mississippi either belongs to the aboriginal tribes of Indians, or is separately settled by the subjects of Spain, where slavery is already established; and much of this territory will doubtless be annexed to our nation by amicable arrangements, and the natural progress of civilization. “ It has been urged as a compensation for the admitted evils of slavery, that the spirit of liberty is more elevated and persevering among the masters of slaves, than in states where liberty is a common blessing. We may admit that our southern brethren are as firmly attached to liberty as ourselves, but we cannot concede that they are in any respect our superiors, without submitting to humiliation and reproach. Probably the claim has no other just foundation, than in the well-known ardor, tenacity of opinion, and strict concert of action, with which the members of a privileged order invariably pursue a separate and exclusive interest.

He eyed him? would speak? but how hit on the plan Long pondering, at length he thus calmly began: "Will you favor me, pray, with your name, sir?" said he; "My name," said the other, "why what's that to thee?" But it is a well-known fact, that all the Royal and Loyal blood which remained in this country at the close of the Revolution, and all that has since emigrated hither, whether by importation or otherwise, remain with the Federal party; and it would be happy,— it would be honorable for the country if those hundreds, and thousands, and tens of thousands, and hundreds of thousands of true-hearted, patriotic, republican-federalists would forget their defeat in '98,—and their struggles since; struggles for a mere form,—an empty name,— a hollow, and unmeaning victory;—forget the things which are behind, and reaching forth to those which are before, press forward, in union with their Republican brethren, toward the mark,—for the prize of the high calling of our august and happy political destinies;—leaving the Monarchist, the Aristocrat, and the bad blood of both parties to the salutary restraints of law and the redeeming spirit of Liberty. The latter part of the administration of President Adams was decidedly aristocratic; and the Republicanism of the country became highly and justly alarmed. How far the President voluntarily participated in the unprincipled measures of his administration is a matter of doubt. He was surrounded by daring and aristocratic spirits; and it is probable that any exceptionable conduct on his part during his administration, proceeded rather from perplexity and want of firmness than from anything adverse to republican principles.

A large quantity of timber at the works of the River Company at Enfield was carried off, a part of which, however, was secured. The main works which were in an unfinished state, we understand, have been injured, though we do not know to what extent. Indeed in the present state of the water, it is difficult to ascertain the degree of injury. We shall doubtless be able to give further particulars of the ravages in our next. We fear it will prove a melancholy record. The following account from a Vermont paper, contains the only information we have received of the effects of the freshet north of this place. [Conn. Courant.] GREAT FLOOD. Disastrous consequences have followed from the long and heavy rains of the 3rd and 4th. The streams had risen considerably upon the 4th, but the constant and heavy fall of rain on Thursday night produced the principal part of a freshet which is unexampled in this part of the country. Yesterday morning, at an early hour, Connecticut River and its tributaries were found to be rapidly rising. The river continued to swell until about four, P. M. when its rise, as supposed, had attained to 18 feet above its ordinary level, and at the time of writing this article had not perceptibly subsided. During the whole day, innumerable quantities of all kinds of valuable timber in rafts and pieces passed along.

We felt desirous, therefore, that the Sovereign People, from whom all power, under our happy government is derived, should have been left to the free and unbiased exercise of their own will in the selection of one of them for President—Not doubting that if left to themselves, they would select the man for that highly dignified and important station, who in the language of Mr. Jefferson, should be deemed by them the most * honest, capable, and faithful to the Constitution.” We should not have departed from our neutral course, had not recent events rendered it in our opinion, in some measure, necessary, to protect and preserve the principles of the Republican Party. The principles on which our government is founded, is, that a majority shall govern. This is a sound republican principle, and ought not to be departed from when it can be ascertained and known. We regret, therefore, to find, after it had been ascertained and published to the world, that, out of 261—(the whole number of the members composing both Houses of Congress) 181 were opposed to holding a Caucus for the nomination of President and Vice-President, that the minority should have persevered in their determination to hold one notwithstanding. And for what purpose was this caucus got up?

British Ministry.—Those who pretend to know all the mysteries of the cabinet and the palace, state that Messrs. Canning and Huskisson, are no great favourites with the King and the Duke of York. They are, however, amply compensated by the favor of the nation. The King has given them the title of "The Two Consuls." His Majesty adds, "I am consulted about nothing. I am no longer what I should be." The Times newspaper observes, "It would be curious, at some future era of our history, if posterity were to discover a stone with this inscription: Cus; Georgio Canningio et Gulielmo Huskinnano mercatura liberahs, frustra olimtente liege intra nationes fieri incipebal. (Commerce, which began to be free amongst nations, in spite of the opposition of the monarch, during the consulship of George Canning and William Huskisson, dedicates to them this stone.)

My motto is, and ever has been: Timeo Danaos et dona ferentes. I never can exist in harmony with any, whose favorite theme is the abuse of Thomas Jefferson. There are many who call themselves republicans—whenever you wish to ascertain whether metal is base, you apply a powerful acid - the very name of Thomas Jefferson, when pronounced in the hearing of these people, effects at once a chemical analysis, and exhibits their component parts. I say with truth, and without fear, that no man who differs with Mr. Jefferson in politics, is either a Democrat, or a friend to the people of this country. Who is the representative of this District? He has enjoyed the confidence of Jefferson, Madison and Monroe—he first brought about the revolution in 1810 in the Northern Neck, which made it truly and emphatically republican. His talents are without any question far superior, not only to those of any other in his District, but equalled by very few in his native State.

Prospectus Of a new semi-weekly Paper, to be published in BOSTON, entitled the AMERICAN STATESMAN. In order more effectively to promote the re publican interest it has been deemed necessary to establish a new semi-weekly paper in this metropolis. An arrangement has been made between True & Weston, late publishers of the Yankee, in this town, and P. N. Greey, late publisher of the Essex Patriot, at Haverhill, to commence the publication of a new semi-weekly paper. In offering to the public a prospectus of a new paper, we do not conceive it necessary to enter into a very minute detail of our political principles. Among our republican friends, at least, we presume it is well understood, that they who have long been concerned in publishing the Yankee and the Essex Patriot, are devoted from feeling and principle, to the republican cause. But, it may not be amiss to state, that by the republican cause, we mean those fundamental views and principles, which were the birth of our revolution, and which have grown up in the era of Jefferson.

Mr. Bayard was a high-toned federalist, at the commencement of party strifes under the federal constitution: He continued a federalist to the day of his death: He was a patriot of the most virtuous and honorable stamp: His country was his idol; and he would have scorned to participate in any corrupt political proposition whatever.

And a second ringing, with equal violence, I brought him to the door, to ascertain whether, peradventure, some of his constituents might not be in a suffering condition. On opening the door, no person was to be seen, but only a parcel wrapped in a blanket, and lying upon his doorstep. On bringing it to the light, it was found to contain an interesting infant, about five weeks old. It was a pretty little innocent, with black hair, and eyes as bright as buttons. The poor foundling was sadly benumbed with cold, and would have perished in a very few minutes. A nurse was humanely procured, in whose bosom it shortly revived. It has been sent to the alms-house. Jefferson said they should be, "all Federalists and all Republicans." Such, however, are the diversities of human opinion on the multiplied rela tions of civilized society under a compli cated government, that it is perhaps impossible for a pleasing uniformity to pre vail for any length of time. There will be some who approve, and some who condemn every scheme of policy. Hence has arisen what now exists in this coun try, and what is probably destined to ex ist as long as our government endures, an Administration party, and an Opposi tion party—the Ins and the Outs, which will occasionally supplant each other, as the party administering the government happens to be wise and virtuous, or cor rupt and corrupt. Under Mr. Madison’s administration, the Federalists lost their real ground of opposition to the government, and under his successor, in proportion as the last dying echoes of federalism have ceased to reverberate, the murmurs of an anti- administration party have become aud ible. No attentive and candid observer of political events can deny this fact, al- though the complainants have not, until very recently, acknowledged themselves a party. Not twelve months since, the existence of an opposition or radical par- ty, waging "war in disguise," on the ad- ministration, was positively denied by their own prints; but during the last session of Congress, the skeleton assumed at Washington its flesh, form, and complexion.

The date and circumstances of its origin determine the point beyond controversy. It commenced with the existence of the present cabinet; before any step had been taken, or any measure developed, the torch of discord was lighted and the messenger had gone forth, formed between the vanquished partisans of all the other candidates, and its first effort was to blast the reputation of Mr. Adams and Mr. Clay, by the charge of corrupt bargaining for office. A combination was formed, George Kremer, a member of Congress from Pennsylvania, a friend of General Jackson, first put forth the offensive charge in the House of Representatives. A committee was appointed at the request of Mr. Clay, to examine the subject; and though that was composed of a majority of the friends of General Jackson, yet Kremer and his coadjutants, when called on to substantiate the charge, shrunk from the investigation. The same charge was subsequently iterated by General Jackson himself. Mr. Clay again met it with manly indignation, and conscious of his integrity called for the proof. To those friends of the Administration, not immediately conversant with the Roman virtue of this estimable Statesman, it was a period of painful anxiety. The charge was made boldly, by a man who had stood high in the estimation of his country.

"It is found from observations made on the New York canal, during the last summer, that one horse of a medium value, is capable of moving on the canal a boat containing 25 tons of freight at the average rate of 25 miles per day. When we consider the number of horses, wagons and men, required to move the same weight an equal distance on the best roads, we shall cease to wonder at the diminution in the price of conveying produce to market by canal navigation, and its consequent increase in value where it is produced. Wheat, of which there were near 500,000 bushels flourished at the mills in & near Rochester on the Genesee river, during one year ending in November last, was worth no more than 50 cents per bushel, before its price was affected by canal navigation. During the past season it has commanded in Rochester, from 94 to 106 cents per bushel, owing entirely to the canal navigation, between that place and the Hudson river. We are apt to consider works with which we are unacquainted as fraught with difficulty in their construction, and of doubtful utility, when completed."

BE IT ENACTED, by the Common Council of the town of Alexandria, in Council assembled, That the Board of Health, and each of the members thereof, in the particular district assigned to that member, of which notice shall have been given in the newspapers, shall have power, which is hereby granted, to order and direct the superintendent of Police to have removed, in the manner prescribed by the various laws of the Corporation in those cases made and provided, all nuisances, which in their opinion might endanger the health of the inhabitants. Sec. 2. And be it further enacted, That the superintendent of Police shall promptly obey every such order given to him in writing as aforesaid, and if he should neglect or refuse the performance of any duty assigned to him as aforesaid, for a longer space of time than twenty-four hours after receiving the same, he shall forfeit and pay one dollar per hour, for every hour thereafter, that he shall so refuse or neglect the same, without a reasonable excuse being given. Sec. 3.

All that we can gather from our latest files of English papers is highly favorable to Greece; far more so in some respects than we had permitted ourselves to hope for. With regard to the intentions of the allied sovereigns towards Turkey, we are most gratified to find that they show every disposition to adhere strictly to the terms of their treaty of interference, made in July last. The members of the new British ministry, in their explanation to the House of Commons on the 18th of February, distinctly expressed such a resolution; and the Duke of Wellington was very clear and decided on this point. The Emperor has addressed a highly complimentary letter to Admiral Codrington, and has also announced that there will be no renunciation of the firm position taken by the powers above referred to. The Greeks themselves are comparatively happy. They have seen no moment of rest they have known since the commencement of the war. They are still fighting, but it is with a disheartened force. The contributions of food and clothing now making in this country will help to support many of their houseless and starving poor until they can sow and reap their next harvest. With one season of peace, their industry will place them beyond the reach of suffering.

From the Albany (N. Y.) Gazette. PRESENT SITUATION OF THE STATE OF NEW-YORK. The following remarkably well written article on this interesting subject, we have copied from a late number of the Ithaca Chronicle. The present situation of the state of New-York must be a subject of felicitation to every citizen who has the honor and interest of his country at heart. Our increase in wealth, population and resources has been unprecedented. During the revolutionary war, Long Island and a narrow strip along the Hudson comprised our whole inhabited territory. New-York was then in the second or third class of states; yet she was the theatre of the most important operations, and some of the most brilliant exploits which distinguished that memorable era. Her sages, her patriots and her soldiers hold an elevated rank among the great architects of the temple of American liberty. In the short space of 40 years New York has attained an undisputed preeminence in the republic.

Both Houses of Congress sat from 10 o’clock in the morning to a late hour last night. In the bustle of so much business, we find it difficult to keep pace with the business transacted. Referring our readers generally to our Congressional Head, we particularly note, for their information, the following incidents, which occurred in the course of yesterday: The bill to erect toll gates on the Cumberland road, with the objections thereto by the President, having been reconsidered in the House of Representatives, it was rejected, by a majority of 4 votes, though it had before a majority of 11 votes in its favor. The Senate put into the military appropriation bill an appropriation of 9000 dollars, to be applied to the repair of the Cumberland Road; which appropriation was rejected by the other House. The bill to regulate the emoluments of the officers of the customs, and to abolish certain collection districts, which passed the Senate some weeks ago, was taken up in the House of Representatives, and passed its final reading with some amendments, however, which yet require the concurrence of the Senate.

The two islands were distant from each other, upwards of three leagues; and the inhabitants being thus collected, and having lived together three weeks, the means of living began to become more slack, and it was agreed upon, that five of the number should go back to the island to live upon the produce there, while those who remained where they were, should build a vessel of the materials of the huts and the planks of some houses which the Americans had formerly erected, and were buried in the sand. After the most dreadful fatigue, they succeeded in building a stone house, and were at the same time proceeding with the ship, in which, wretched as it necessarily must be, they were determined to attempt to return home. In about five months, a lugger of about twelve tons was completed. They launched her, and intended to depart the day after—but, to their dismay, on the very night of the launch, she was blown amongst the rocks, and her stern was beaten in. Scarcely, however, had this calamity occurred, when an old fellow, who was constantly employed on the lookout, cried out "a sail." They looked in the same direction, but the general opinion was, that the sail was no other than a large bird, which had often led them into errors of the same sort.

CHAMBERS' REMEDY FOR INTEMPERANCE From the Philadelphia Monthly Journal of Medicine and Surgery, edited by N.R. Smith, M.D., Professor of Surgery in the University of Maryland: "Chambers' medicine for the relief of drunkenness has been used with success near Louisville, in the cases of several negroes. It completely reformed them of their vice." The following is from a circular signed by John Rogers, John Kimball, and Freese Dearborn, Esqrs. Selectmen of Exeter— "We would suggest the propriety of making an appropriation from the license tax for the purchase of Chambers' Medicine. We have placed a quantity of this article in the hands of our Physicians, who administer it free of expense to all who will take it; we are happy to have it in our power to state that more than twenty of our most confirmed drunkards have taken it, and those who have completed their course, have been cured by it, and restored to their friends and to usefulness in society." In every case where it has been tried in Portsmouth and its vicinity (and 30 to 40) it has not been known to fail of effecting a cure.

Good; that no oath is heard in Heaven; that secret crimes have no witness but the perpetrator; that human existence has no purpose, and death is total, everlasting extinction—once let them thoroughly abandon religion, and who can conceive or describe the extent of the desolation which would follow. We hope, perhaps, that human laws and natural sympathy would hold society together. As reasonably might we believe, that, were the sun quenched in the heavens, our torches would illuminate, and our fires quicken and fertilize creation. What is there in human nature to awaken respect and tenderness, if man is the unprotected insect of a day? and what is he more, if Atheism be true?—Erase all thought and fear of God from a community, and selfishness and sensuality would absolve the whole man. Appetite, knowing no restraint, and poverty and suffering, having no solace or hope, would trample in scorn on the restraint of human laws. Virtue, duty, principle, would be mocked and spurned as unmeaning sounds. A sordid self-interest would supplant every other feeling, and man would become in fact what the theory of Atheism declares him to be, a companion for brutes. Love and Faith. LAFAYETTE rose and expressed his affectionate acknowledgments for the very kind welcome he had received today from the people of New-Hampshire, at the seat of Government, particularly for the toast which had just been given, and for the pleasure he felt, to be now at this social table, with all the Representatives of the State in every branch, with his numerous beloved revolutionary companions in arms, and other respected citizens; to the whole of these, he begged leave to propose the following sentiment:— "New-Hampshire, its Representatives in every branch, and this seat of Government, may they forever enjoy all the blessings of civil and religious liberty, which their high-minded ancestors came to seek on a distant land, and which their more immediate fathers have insured on the broader basis of national sovereignty, and the rights of men." 2.
