The Dein.—At the late session of the Legislature of Vermont, a law was passed in addition to an act against disturbing the remains of the dead,” which provides that “if any person or persons shall, hereafter, be convicted of either, or all of the offenses described in the act to which this act is an addition, such person or persons shall be confined to hard labor, in the state prison, for a term not exceeding ten years, nor less than three years, and be fined a sum not exceeding one thousand dollars, nor less than three hundred dollars, at the discretion of the Court, before whom such person or persons shall be convicted.  The Secretary of War.—The Coalition papers affect to make the absence of this gentleman from the Seat of Government a just ground of complaint. It seems that he was not in Washington to reply under his own proper hand to a letter from the Governor of Massachusetts! Prodigious!! These truth-loving Editors take especial care not to inform their readers that though the Secretary of War was absent from Washington, he has been nevertheless constantly engaged in the discharge of public and official duties of the greatest magnitude, and highest importance to the country.

In the evening the General was introduced to the ladies on the floor of the State-House, and afterwards attended an Oratorio by the N. H. Musical Society at the upper Meeting-House. The next morning he left Concord for Dover. The concourse of people who attended upon this joyous occasion was very large, estimated at about ten thousand. The militia ordered out for the escort made a splendid appearance and were highly complimented by Lafayette. There were eight companies of light infantry and two of artillery, in elegant uniform. The day was uncommonly pleasant, and the proceedings were conducted in such a manner as to give universal satisfaction. Gen. Lafayette arrived in Dover on Thursday afternoon. He was met at Durham by the Committee of Arrangements. At the line, a very respectable procession was formed, consisting of gentlemen on horseback and in carriages. On his approach, near the compact part of the town, a salute was fired, and the procession escorted through the principal streets by the Rockingham Guards, of this town, and the Strafford Guards, of Dover, to Wyatt’s Hotel. After being introduced to several gentlemen, he partook of an excellent entertainment, provided at the Court-House, which was handsomely decorated for the occasion. The next morning the General left Dover for Portland, where he arrived on Saturday. We understand he passed through Dover on Sunday afternoon on his way to Vermont.

There are persons in all parts of our country who speak in terms of utter abhorrence of the course now pursued with the Indians. From the most unquestionable sources, it is ascertained that great numbers of well-informed individuals in South Carolina, Alabama, and Mississippi perfectly agree on this subject with the mass of the people in Pennsylvania, Ohio, and the western, middle, and eastern States. I say the mass of the people; for excepting those who are misled by false statements, and including all who are acquainted with the subject, it will be found that nearly every individual is on the side of the Indians. It is extensively supposed that petitioning does no good, and owing to the prevalence of this supposition, many thousands neglected to petition in behalf of the Indians last winter. But this is a total mistake. A few more spirited memorials, supported by the personal influence of men distinguished in the community for their integrity and patriotism, would probably have defeated the Indian bill.

Immediately discharged his pistols, which were double-barrelled, two of the bullets passing through the door, about six or seven inches above the lock and close to Haag’s head; the other two passing through the opposite side of the door, one of them grazing the door-cheek. Mrs. Haag, who was standing in the door which opened into the children’s room, which is immediately opposite to that which opens from that room into the kitchen, received one of the balls in the center of her breast, which occasioned her death in the course of a few moments. The neighborhood was then alarmed and the Mayor and other officers sent for, who proceeded to force an entrance into the house of Mr. Lechler, who lived immediately opposite to Mr. Haag. Upon entering Lechler’s house, his children were found in bed, and upon further search, Mrs. Lechler was found on the garret, hung by the neck with a rope to one of the beams. From the bruises on the arms, breast, and back of Mrs. Lechler, it is almost certain that she must have been murdered in her chamber (on the ground floor) and then carried up two pairs of stairs to the garret and there hung up, in order to induce a belief that she had hung herself! ! !

The Great Naval Experiment—At the appointed hour yesterday morning, all the arrangements having been completed, the experiment was commenced which was to test the practicability of hauling up, on an inclined plane, upon the plan invented by Commodore Rodgers, a large ship of war. The new frigate Potomac, of the class of 44 guns, and weighing, with the apparatus attached to her, about 1600 tons, was the ship with which the interesting and important experiment was to be tried. Though a light wind prevailed, the ship was introduced without accident between the ways on which she was to ascend, and at 10 o’clock the power of three windlasses, worked by 40 or 50 men each, was applied to the immense floating castle, and she began slowly to ascend. The operation was continued successfully, until the ship was drawn almost out of the water. At this moment, the lashings which connected the block of the center purchase with the large cable that passed around the ship, fore and aft, and drew her on, parted. This accident, however, had been guarded against, and means taken, in case of such an occurrence, to prevent the ship from running back. The vessel, therefore, remained firm in her place; but, as it required some hours to repair the damage, the remainder of the operation was deferred until this morning.

The strain of his very brief petition, was such as became the elevated spirit of an finest soldier, incapable of an ab ject for a whining supplication. He stood until as a respectful subject, to be Missed to re-enter his country and embrace his family, leaving his ulterior fate to the justice of the king. The French ambassador at Wash ington, M. Hyde de Neuville, not only forwarded his application to the ministry at Paris, but seconded it in two letters in his own, one addressed to them, and another directly to the king, in which he urged in the strongest terms, the general merits of the exile, and bore testimony to the propriety of his deportment in this State. The warm and liberal repre sentations of Mr. de Neuville, and the instances of the gen eral's wife at last, prevailed with the government so far that it invited him to proceed to the Netherlands, there to await a speedy compliance with all his wishes, and instructed the minister of France in that country, to give him the kindest welcome and fullest protection. He availed himself as soon as possible of this in dulgence, and was on his way to Holland by Liverpool, confident of soon being indemnified for all the sufferings of his exile, in a tranquil existence in the bosom of his country and family, when he found a watery grave on the Irish coast.

Hearne states that he has kept various individuals about his house, during his residence at Hudson's Bay, and remarks, 'they made not the least dirt, though they were kept in my own sitting room where they were the constant companions of the Indian women and children, being so fond of their company that when the Indians were absent for any considerable time, the beaver dis covered great signs of uneasiness, and on their return showed equal marks of pleasure by fondling on them, crawling into their laps, laying themselves on their backs, sitting erect like a squirrel, and behaving to them like children that see their parents but seldom." In general, during the winter they lived upon the same food as the women did, and were remarkably fond of rice and plum pudding. They would eat fresh venison and partridges very freely, but I never tried them with fish, though I have heard they will at times prey on them. It is well known that our domestic poultry will eat animal food; thousands of geese that come to London market are fattened on tallow scraps, and our horses in Hudson's Bay would not only eat all kinds of animal food, but also drink freely of the wash or pot-liquor intended for the hogs.

Pupils of 5 to fifteen years of age are admitted by the Teachers at the school, at the rate of twenty-five cents to one dollar per quarter according to the circumstances of the parents, and the children of such as cannot afford to pay anything are admitted free of expense, and enjoy the same advantages as those who. Each school is visited weekly by a committee of the trustees, in addition to which a committee of ladies pay regular visits to the female schools. Care is taken to impart moral instruction, and such have been the happy effects of the system pursued in these schools, that although several thousand have been taught in them since their establishment (now more than thirty years) there has never been an instance known to the trustees where a pupil having received a regular education has been convicted of any crime in our Courts of Justice. The Hon. Speaker of the House of Representatives. Copy of a letter from the Board of Navy Commissioners to the Secretary of the Navy, dated Navy Commissioners' Office, 31st October, 1828. The Commissioners have had the honor of receiving your communication of the 11th instant, calling for "a plan and estimate for connecting with two or more of the Navy Yards as many establishments for purchasing, water-rotting, and preparing for manufacture, American hemp and flax, and also cotton as for manufacturing the same into cordage and canvas, for the use of the Navy." Never having contemplated such establishments as necessary appendages to Navy Yards, none such having been connected with either of ours, or those of any country in Europe; the Commissioners have not extended their inquiries to the subject, and are not prepared to afford satisfactory information upon the various points involved in the call. To obtain such information, it would be necessary to consult practical men, engaged in the erection of such works, in making the machinery required, and in all the various processes of preparing and manufacturing hemp, flax, and cotton; and would unavoidably occupy a considerable length of time, probably so long as to exclude any report upon the subject during the ensuing session of Congress.

The longitude of the points embraced by Mr. Wood's contract have been made, although the calculations and results have not been completed. In February last, Mr. Wood had returned all the maps of the country, except six; the survey of which have since been returned to him, and the cost of making out the maps for these countries, it is believed will not exceed £500. One fifth part of the general map, it is understood, is completed. The surveys of the roads, mountains, etc. have been finished, and Mr. Wood anticipated in February last to have complied with his contract by the first of August; unfortunately, however, both for himself and the commissioners, his death will prevent the realization of his expectations. Of the sum £10,000, Mr. Wood had received £3, leaving a balance in the treasury of £7,000, to be applied to the completion of the work, and for which sum, it is said, the general map in every respect done by Mr. Wood can be furnished. By the original contract with Mr. Wood, the executive stipulated in the event of his death, that they should appoint some competent person to complete what part of the work remained unfinished by him. - The compensation bill was lately before the House of Representatives.

This falls far short of the 15 dollar estimation, even admitting the very kind of taxation so much deprecated by him to be true; but as I said before, it is the wealth of a country which must maintain it, and not its poverty. "Henry" asserts that the formation of a State (Government will not induce one single individual more to emigrate to our country. We have instances to prove that he has taken a position which cannot be justified—and these instances are not of an ancient date, but within the recollection of all of us. The Territory of Illinois must have been organized with a population of 10,000; she continued as a Territory about 12 years, and went into a State Government with less than 37,000 souls; 13 years have since elapsed, and she is computed to have a population of 350,000. When she went into a State Government, her population was proverbially poor, but she has surmounted every difficulty, and in the last mentioned period of time, must have increased nearly 15 to 1. Missouri had been a Territory 13 or 14 years, went into a State Government in 1820 with a population of not exceeding 40,000; she has now been a State 10 years, and is supposed to have a population of 230 or 300,000 souls.

Governed solely by these correct views, we were “pursuing the event of our own way,” when the puerile editor of the Times, entirely without provocation, undertook to instruct the people that this press was owned by a “federal Junto,” that the individual whose name stands at the head of the paper as its editor, was the mere tool of this “Junto,” and that he never pretended to dictate what should be published, this business being entirely governed by others. This same boyish blackguarding has been kept up for the last year, even down to his paper of the 1st instant, when he says, “It would not be difficult for us to ascertain the cause of the unrelenting attacks made on this paper, by the little junto on Litchfield hill, through their creature the Post.” When we read this last paragraph, we really felt like laughing fifteen minutes by a stop watch. Poor boy—his paper must be run by a “little junto on Litchfield hill,” when there is no other paper printed in either of the three counties bordering upon this, but what is read here ten times as much as his. Indeed, when we offer it for perusal to our neighbors, the almost inva riable answer we receive is, “I never read the Times.”

The following mixture, it is asserted, will make writing which has been obliterated, faded or sunk, either on paper or parchment, immediately legible. Bruise two or three nut galls, infuse them in half a pint of white wine, and let the bottle stand for two days in the sun or any other equally warm situation; then wash the parchment or paper which is wanted to have the writing recovered, by means of a sponge or soft brush dipped in the vinous infusion; and the purpose will be immediately answered, if it be sufficiently strong. Should that not happen, its powers must be increased by an additional quantity of galls; and perhaps, in some instances, stronger heat and even stronger wine, may also be necessary. FOREIGN. The king of Spain had arrived at Seville, and the Government had officially declared war against France, and had ordered out many public and private armed vessels against her commerce. The enthusiasm of the Spanish people continued unabated. All was tranquil at Madrid. The French army was marching slowly towards Madrid, and it was reported had committed many excesses. The national troops are represented as having fought bravely in the Province of Bilbao, but had retreated before superior forces. It was added that the French had levied a contribution of 8000 dollars on Bilbao. The French papers say, Gen. Quesada had levied a contribution of 400 mils of clothes on this city.

Mr. Monroe, the late president, made some very sensible remarks, in which he was desirous of uniting the various interests of the State by a compromise, viz.:—by having the basis of the Senate upon population and taxation combined, and that of the House upon white population exclusively. We mention as rather a singular circumstance that Gen. Taylor, of Norfolk, has resigned his seat in the Convention because he has received particular instructions from his constituents how to vote on the question; but as his conscience is the other way, and he cannot therefore vote in conformity to the instructions, he has taken this course that his Constituents may supply his place with one who will vote according to their wishes. What if they should choose another of the same opinions with Gen. Taylor? What if then, similar instructions should be given? Quaere. How many of his constituents gave the instructions? Gen. Taylor is, advisedly, a distinguished man, but he seems in a degree possessed of the strange notions in relation to instructions that beset many of our southern politicians, although not to the same extent.

A STEVENSON, Speaker of the House of Representatives.  1. C. CALHOUN, Vice President of the United States, and President of the Senate.  JOHN QUINCY ADAMS.  for as the Thursday March 3.— The following message from the President of the United States, received & considered the third inst. in legislative session, was at that time, on motion of Mr. Tazewell, ordered to be transferred, with the accompanying documents, to the Executive Journal: To the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America: Washington, 3d March, 1829. I transmit herewith to Congress a copy of the instructions prepared by the Secretary of State, and furnished to the Minister of the United States appointed to attend the Assembly of American Plenipotentiaries, first held at Panama, and thence transferred to Tacubaya. The occasion for which they were given has passed away, and there is no present probability of the renewal of those negotiations; but the purposes for which they were intended are still of the deepest interest to our country and to the world, and may hereafter call again for the active energies of the Government of the U. States. The motive for withholding them from general publication having ceased, justice to the Government from which they emanate, and to the people for whose benefit it was instituted, require that they should be made known.

With all the aid of the philosophical glare, which a political chancery has shed upon this matter, we are still at a loss to see under what pretext this objection is made, unless it is that Mr. Adams had not the greatest number of votes when brought to the House of Representatives. But the constitution, by its unqualified and direct injunction, requiring the Representatives to choose a President "from the persons having the highest numbers, not exceeding three," without a shadow of reference either to the foremost or to any particular one of them—The three candidates with the highest numbers stand before the Representatives House as if tied, upon a perfect equality, to be judged of by them as by a new body of electors, who are to decide upon "the man who, in their opinion, may be best qualified for the office." To require the Representatives to be governed by the will of the people in this case, is to require them to be governed by what they have no means of knowing—for when the electors of a state have not voted for one of the three highest candidates—or when the electors of a state are equally divided, perhaps between two, neither having the highest numbers, how can the representatives know the popular preference.

Maine could not have been formed and admitted to the Union without the consent of the Legislature of Massachusetts. Congress, without State Legislatures, could not connect New Hampshire and Vermont into one State, nor dismember Rhode Island and annex its parts to Massachusetts and Connecticut. And possibly the clause, in the spirit of it, would forbid a colony of French or Russians settling in any State and there erecting a monarchy independent of the State government, and being admitted by Congress to the Union. But the case of the Indians is altogether a different thing, anomalous in its character, by no means embraced in either the letter or the spirit of the clause referred to. In the next place, it is not true that the Indians have "lately attempted to erect an independent government within the limits of Georgia and Alabama." What could tempt the Executive thus to outrage all historical facts? These tribes were independent and had a regular government of their own, long before Georgia had either name or existence, either as a State or a province, and before the white man placed his proud foot upon her soil. The Indians have never surrendered their sovereignty or been in bondage to any man since that time. Neither Georgia nor the U. States have ever claimed the sovereignty over them. They have remained in their own way.

Yet the highest law character in the state, delivering an opinion before the Senate, sitting as the highest court of law in the state, did not apprehend an impeachment for sacrificing state rights, when he declared, that, if an arrangement should be made on this subject, it should be made "with the entire approbation of the government of the United States." And the Senate, consisting of thirty or forty members from all parts of the state, supported the reasoning of the Chancellor, with but a single dissenting vote. How different a spirit is there, from that which prevails in Georgia! At the close of the paragraph which I have quoted, the Chancellor recommends this course, not only as the most prudent course, and "not only for conscience sake but for the reputation of our justice."

Thousands of Americans wore the French national cockade, and vilified Washington publicly in the most virulent terms. It was believed by many, (or pretended at least) that our government must and would enter into all the views of the French revolutionists; and all this under the specious name of republicanism. Was it genuine republicanism? Let its termination in France, under Napoleon, 1st Emperor of the Oanh, answer the question. Were the rights and liberties of the people secured by the accomplishment of the views of these societies in France? Let the blood of slaughtered millions, and the exile of an equal number, have a voice to answer this question. Has Christianity been advanced—or has the moral and political state of mankind been made better by the triumph of these societies? Let the French official document which proclaimed atheism by law—the demoralized state of the French people under the revolution, and the acts of the midnight soldiery all over France, dictate. All the enormities of the French revolution were committed under the false pretence of republicanism; and it was intended by the French party among us, in the time of the French revolution, in opposition to Washington, to effect a like revolution in religious and political principles. They were however obliged to abandon the hope of success, during the life of Washington.

The following Address of the Editor, will show the object and design of the above work: TO THE PATRONS OF THE CHRISTIAN ALMANAC. The liberal patronage hitherto bestowed on the Christian Almanac is pleasing and satisfactory proof of the estimation in which it is held by the public. Not far from 230,000 copies have been circulated since it was commenced in 1821, by the American Tract Society; and it is thought the increasing demand will warrant the publication of a larger edition for the present year, than has hitherto been published. Of this we are glad. Not because it is a source of individual profit; but because great good to the temporal and spiritual interests of the community is anticipated from such a general distribution. The Christian Almanac is not a scheme for private pecuniary benefit. It is a project of Christian benevolence, and designed only for public utility. It is a Tract, published by the American Tract Society; and while it is intended to answer the purposes of a common family Almanac, it is also intended to be a vehicle of religious truth, and a register of interesting facts and important statistics connected with the prosperity of the church.

Firmly attached to republican institutions and the doctrines proclaimed by the illustrious Jefferson, we shall adhere to them with constancy and steadiness, but without rancor or bitterness of feeling against those who differ from us—believing the truth of the maxim, that “error of opinion may be safely tolerated, where REASON is left free to combat it.” If it be asked, to what school we belong? Our answer is—to that in which Jefferson and Madison taught—in which Bartlett, Langdon, Plumer, and Bell were educated.—And even among the humblest pupils to the doctrines of such men, it is a source of pride that we can conscientiously maintain their principles, sanctioned as they have been by the lapse of years. Believing that the administration of the government of this Union for the last twenty-four years has fixed on durable foundations the prosperity of the country and its citizens—we shall support with our feeble but sincere efforts the administration of Mr. Adams, whose talents and services have during most of that period been devoted to his country’s welfare, and essentially contributed to that result.

It is providential that so few passengers were on board. A person on board has expressed astonishment that a single individual could escape. BOSTON, DEC. 16. In the United States, Circuit Court, on Wednesday last, before Judges Story and Davis, came on the trial of John Duncan White, alias Charles Marchant, charged with the murder of Thomas Paine Jenkins, mate of the American schr. Fairy, when upon the high seas, on the 28th of August last. The prosecution for government was managed by the District Attorney, Mr. Blake, and the defense was conducted by Sidney Bartlett and William A. Warner, Esqrs. Counsel appointed by the Court. It is known that Edward Selfridge, captain, and Mr. Jenkins, mate of the schr. were both killed on the same evening, and that the prisoner was indicted for the murder of the mate, and Curtis alias Colson, an American, for the murder of the captain. Their trials were ordered to be separate. It appeared in evidence that the Fairy was an American vessel, and sailed from Boston in August, on a voyage to Gothenburg, with a common cargo on board, without any specie. — Her crew consisted of Colson, the prisoner, and two other men John Hughes and John Murray, witnesses in the case. On the night of the 28th of August, when the murder was committed, Capt. Selfridge, Hughes, and Murray were below—the mate, the prisoner, and Colson on deck.

[from THE NANTUCKET ENQUIRER.] A FRAGMENT, ON THE THEORY OF CHEMISTRY INVERSE, BY ST. PIERRE. Translated by L. U. U. V. JOHNSON, and of the U. S. Navy AV 91 Supposing that the first of the polar ices depart from the frozen zone, at the vernal equinox;—and that they are entirely dissolved by the time they reach 40th degree of latitude, about the summer Solstice;—they must have traversed in three months a thousand leagues, or, to say, twelve leagues per diem, the usual velocity of the stream of rivers. But as I have already said it is necessary to distinguish well between this particular motion, and the motion of the seas, which is incomparably more prompt. In so distinct a manner our icy ocean, the most elevated, pushes back and replaces the water of the temperate zone which it chills in the spring; those of the temperate traverse in part the seas of the torrid zones, which they refresh by their coolness, and these last take the place of the southern temperate zone, to which they lend their warmth, whilst the waters of this zone, driven through the channel of Mozambique, after making almost the entire circuit of the globe, flow to the southern pole to congeal in ice.

The act of our State Legislature directing the mode of choosing and appointing electors of President and Vice-President of the U. States, passed June 14, 1824, is now in force. It is a standing law, and unless the House of Representatives, and the Senate, and the Governor should all agree to repeal that law, the question is settled, that the Electors shall be chosen by the people. Our republican citizens may therefore understand the strong motive, which has led to the violent opposition to the re-election of Governor Pierce. He could not be prevailed upon to approve a bill to repeal that law, and thus deprive the people of their rights. That act provides, that the Electors shall be chosen by the people on the first Monday of November next. CAMILLUS. TO THE REPUBLICAN CITIZENS OF DISTRICT NO. 1. On the 11th March you will be called upon to elect a Senator to represent your interests and political sentiments in the Legislature. But two candidates, Amos Greenleaf, Esq. and Hon. John W. Parsons, are presented for your choice. They both sustain good moral characters. They are both personal friends to us. For which candidate ought we to vote? It is, at this momentous crisis, a very important question, as the political character of the Senate may depend upon your decision. According to the republican maxim, "rotation in office," Dr. Parsons has been long enough in the Senate.

Samuel Adams—A native of Boston, a fine scholar, a merchant by profession, was born 22nd September, 1722. He reported the first resolution to the citizens of Boston, denying the right of the mother country to tax the colonies against their consent for purposes of revenue; a political writer of eminence; a member of Congress eight times; a member of the convention which formed the constitution of his State; and of the convention which adopted the Constitution of the U. States; Lieut. Governor and Gov. of Massachusetts; one of the proscribed patriots, whom the British authorities proclaimed beyond the reach of pardon. Died, 3rd Oct. 1803, in his 82nd year. John Hancock—A scholar and a man of immense wealth, was born near Quincy, Mass. 1735.

The circumstances of the case, as they are presented to us, seem to be these: it was made known to governor Jackson that certain papers relating to the private property of individuals in Pensacola, and which ought by treaty to have been delivered up, were in the possession of the Spanish lieut. Domingo Sousa, and after being formally demanded of him, they had been carried in a box by Sousa and a negro to the house of the late governor col. Calava, and delivered into the hands of his steward, named Fulvia. It being made known to governor Jackson, he issued an order directed to certain individuals to apply to col. Calava and his steward Fulvia for the papers which were particularly designated, and in the event of their not being delivered up by one or the other, to take them both and convey them before the governor, there to answer interrogatories to be put to them relative to the premises and to abide such order as the justice of the case should require.

Her father, though a member of the Society of Friends, was by no means strict, and suffered his children to enjoy greater freedom than is usually permitted among individuals of that sect. Elizabeth Gurney was accustomed to mix much with society, and she enjoyed all the advantages of birth, fortune, and education. She was about seventeen years of age when she first visited London—was anxious to see everything, and having participated, for a period, in all the gay amusements of the capital, she returned to Norfolk. A short time after her return, some members of the society (as is customary among the Friends) came to Earlham to make a family visit. This suddenly wrought a transformation in the habits of the whole family—all became more serious and seemed to feel the influence of the holy visit—Elizabeth, in particular, was deeply penetrated by the evangelical lectures which she heard. In a mind like hers, a religious impression was not likely to be transitory—too pious, and too well-informed, to confine herself to useless forms of devotion, she proved her faith by her labors, and soon prevailed on her father to convert one of the apartments of Earlham Hall into a schoolroom. Here she daily received four and twenty poor children, to whom she read and explained the Bible. She assumed the simple garb of the Quakers, and renounced all kinds of amusement.

The London Times of June 30, contains the following paragraph: A Brussels article of June 20th says,—"the Prince of Canino, son of Lucien Bonaparte, and his young wife set out this afternoon for Antwerp, where they will embark for the United States of America. They intend to remain two or three years in that country." The Minister of the United States of America, the Ambassador of the Netherlands, and the Ambassador of Sweden, still remain at Seville. PORTUGAL. Yesterday advices were received from Oporto to the 6th last. An extraordinary meeting was held on the 4th of June, in the Municipal Chamber of the city of Oporto, at which his Excellency the Marshal Jose Joaquim de Sousa Coelho attended, with 25 of the most distinguished Aldermen and inhabitants of the city, for the purpose of taking the oaths of allegiance to the King. After having given three rounds of vivas for the King, John IV., the Queen, and all the Royal family, Marshal Sousa was requested, by deputation, to assume the command of the city forces until the directions of his Majesty should be known. The troops received the announcement of the restoration of the Royal Authority with the liveliest acclamations, in which they were enthusiastically joined by the entire population of the city. The Bishop of the Diocese attended at the request of the Council.

I repeat, that leading gentlemen from South Carolina were first and foremost in behalf of the doctrines of Internal Improvements, when those doctrines first came to be considered and acted upon in Congress. The debate on the Bank question, on the Tariff of 1816, and on the Direct Tax, will show who was who, and what was what, at that time. The Tariff of 1816, one of the plain cases of oppression and usurpation, from which, if the Government does not recede, individual States may justly secede from the Government, is, sir, in truth, a South Carolina Tariff, supported by South Carolina votes. But for those votes, it could not have passed in the form in which it did pass; whereas if it had depended on Massachusetts votes, it would have been lost. Does not the honorable gentleman well know all this? There are certainly those who do, full well, know it all. I do not say this to reproach South Carolina: I only state the fact, and I think it will appear to be true, that among the earliest and boldest advocates of the Tariff, as a measure of protection, and on the express ground of protection, were leading gentlemen of South Carolina in Congress. I did not then, and cannot now, understand their language in any other sense.

A private letter from Paris, dated April 5th, says, that actual hostilities had not then commenced; but that no doubt existed they would begin very soon. It is said in the London Globe that an Austrian minister is to accompany the French army during its operations in Spain, to see that it does not overstep the bounds marked out by the Congress of Verona. Marshal Victor, Duke of Belluno, French minister of the War Department, left Paris about the middle of March, to join the army as nurse to the Duke of Angouleme. Victor is said to be a fine officer, and an excellent disciplinarian. Bonaparte called him the favorite of fortune, for under him he rose from the lowest office in a marching regiment to those of Marshal and Peer of France. The corps of the French army of the western Pyrenees which were to have been at Bayonne, and in the vicinity by the 24th April, consist of 47,420 infantry—10 cavalry—and 3500 artillery—total 60,230 men. Another large force is collected in the eastern Pyrenees, whose headquarters are at Perpignan. PORTLAND BRIDGE. A meeting of the proprietors of the Portland Bridge corporation, is to be held today at Mitchell's Hotel. The stock we understand is nearly all taken up, and it is considered now that the project will succeed, there being but 10 shares left.

Casimir Perier is a very distinguished man, and has long been before the French public as a financier as well as debater. He was born in Grenoble in the year 1777, and is of course fifty-three years of age. He is one of the bankers of Paris, and when we last had the pleasure of being there, his office was at No. 27 Hue Neuve du Luxembourg. But it is as an orator that Casimir Perier has distinguished himself.

They are weak; we are strong. They have the possession; we have the words of a treaty. Mr. B. would state the case as fairly as he could for both parties. In 1804 the Sacs and Foxes ceded the country between the Ouisconsin and Rock river (including the mines in question) to the United States; the Chippewas, Ot towas, and Potawatomi, disputed their title. In 1816 a treaty was lived with the three filler tribes, and the dispute with the United States was compromised by a division of the disputed tract: a line was agreed upon to run west from the south end of Lake Michigan to the left bank of the Mississippi; the United States taking what was to the south, and the three tribes taking that which was to the north, with the exception of one reserve of three leagues square, to include the Fort at Prairie-du-Chien, and with the reserve of “such other tracts at or near the Ouisconsin and Mississippi as the President may think fit to reserve; such tracts not exceeding five leagues square.” (6 vol. Laws U. S. page 310.) The question, as it concerns the Indians, said Mr. B. arises out of the last reserve. The tracts are not designated. It is left to the President. The Indians deny that they intended that the mines should be taken, and have often threatened to kill any white people who should attempt to work them.

There can be no doubt that in every instance in which a boiler has burst, it has been owing to an original defect in the machinery, which might have been detected when it was first made, and its use consequently prevented, if it had been properly tried and proved by competent judges, or else to the incompetency or carelessness of the engineers. Why is it, otherwise, that scarcely a year elapses in this country, in which we do not hear of more than one of these frightful explosions, accompanied with a most distressing loss of life, and by the most agonizing of all the tortures which the human body can endure, whilst, we are told, “since the introduction of Steamboats into England but a single boat (the Norwich) has burst her boiler?” The paper which gives us the information remarks: “The explosion (of the Norwich) was more than twelve years since. Immediately after this, there was a parliamentary enactment on the subject, and no disaster of the kind has since taken place. We are glad to hear that this subject has at last been taken up by Congress. The Chamber of Commerce of Cincinnati has also appointed a committee to investigate properly upon this matter. We hope investigation will be pursued until such measures are adopted as will effectually check the recurrence of these dreadful accidents.

It gladdens one’s bosom to know that this earth can be made such a semblance of heaven, And to see a confiding sweet circle of worth deserving each good God hath given; But there are things yielding more exquisite joy Than Mirth’s smile, or bewitching Love glancing—’Tis the Tear for distress gemming Sympathy’s eye, And the Hand that Relief is advancing! One sight of a rich tear so holily shed, One view of a hand so extended Is worth days and nights of the happiness spread Around Friendship with plenty attended. List! ye merchants and ball-men, ye matrons and belles, Who postpone doing good till tomorrow, Would you wish for enjoyment no pleasure excels? Claim the Tear and the Hand—seek out sorrow. February 4th, 1829. F. Y. DIED, At the Navy Yard, in Washington, about 10 o’clock, A. M., yesterday, aged 79 years, Commodore Thomas Tingey, Commandant of that Yard, and for twenty-eight years a resident of that city in that capacity, for his exalted worth everyone who has ever known him will bear testimony. His unapproachable character must be to his bereaved widow and affectionate children an invaluable legacy. His commission in the Navy was nearly coeval with its existence. For almost fifty years he has sustained the reputation of an Officer of the Navy with unsullied reputation. As the head of a family, he was a venerable and.

It is true that they have "lately" changed the form of their government, from monarchical to the republican; but an independent government had been erected long before that of the U. States was framed, and has been in uninterrupted operation till now. The states of Maine and New York, of which the President speaks, never dream of exercising sovereignty over the Indians within their bounds. These tribes have, to this day, their laws and their chiefs. So it is in the other States and Territories. Even the President himself shows, that the extension of a State Government over Indians has been but "lately" made; and with admirable inconsistency he adduces this act of Georgia and Alabama as subsequent to the late attempt of the Indians to erect an independent government, and as done in consequence of it. Whereas, to give the least force to his argument, Georgia should have "extended her laws over the Indians," and have placed her yoke upon their necks at least before the adoption of the federal constitution. Again, Georgia and every other State which embraces a tribe of Indians, has consented to their separate and independent national existence within her territory. The Provinces made treaties with them, and in various ways recognized their independence. The General Government only has now the treaty-making power, and she has recognized their sovereignty.

MORE DUELLING—and with new weapons. A rencontre of a singular nature has engrossed much conversation in the town of D-ier several days past. Mrs., and Mrs.—, (tenants of the same house) having for a considerable time been in controversy, and high words having passed between them (by way of challenge) had a meeting near the hog-pen in their back yard, when the lady commenced the combat by seizing a handful of manure from the pen, and throwing it into his ear. The compliment was immediately returned, when a desperate contest ensued, in which they were both covered from head to toe with the unsavory ammunition; victory finally declared in favor of the lady, by depriving her antagonist of his eye-sight. The "affair" was conducted throughout with the greatest firmness; neither of the parties wore a silk dress, nor could either be charged with any unfairness.

Here are facts well authenticated, and so much in point, that they ought to serve to the U.S. States, as individual experience. But, we caution the Southern agriculturist against the double delusion, which the "rumor" alluded to, is calculated to produce. We caution him against believing either that England is going to relax her duties on wheat and tobacco, in consideration of any boon, that the U.S. States can offer, or that the relaxation would be of the smallest advantage to him, if there were. That England will not do it is evident from many circumstances, and if the Cabinet at Washington believe any such thing, it proves only by what trickery a great nation is ruled. She would not do it if she could, but what is a still more cogent reason, she cannot do it if she would. The corn laws of England are the prop of her agriculture. Without the exclusive privilege of feeding the population, and thus of always securing a market for corn, that class could not stand under the enormous burdens which the necessities of Government and of imperialism impose. Great efforts have been made by the manufacturing interest for years past to effect a repeal of the corn laws.

Plenty showered its abundance in our lap; our treasury was overflowing; our army well employed, and clothed with economy; our navy gradually increasing; and our late administration with forbearance and mildness. Three years have not revolved around, and behold the contrast! With England, the most powerful nation of Europe, difficulties of an alarming nature have occurred. With the rising Republics of South America, alliances of an entangling description, have been attempted; and in our bosom, rebellion is rearing its crest; our treasury is empty and our resources daily declining; our army, with its bayonets, turned against the bosoms of our brethren; our national ships rotting in our own waters, and some of our most valuable officers driven into foreign service; and our laws perverted and held up in terror to our peaceable and unoffending citizens. Our wealth has been wasted in useless projects, or worse than useless, used to subserve the ambitious and electioneering views of the Executive. Every means that the ingenuity of man could devise, have been sought and seized with avidity, to increase the patronage of the President at the expense of the rights of the States, and in defiance of the constitution, the charter of our liberties. With these facts, disagreeable but incontrovertible, staring us in the face, we can no longer repose our confidence in J Adams.

The following lines are given in Moore's Life of Byron. They were intended to be added to Byron's well known Ode to Napoleon. They contain a beautiful and just tribute to the memory of him who acted so gloriously a part in the enfranchisement of his country: "There was a day—there was an hour When earth was Gaul's—Gaul thine— When that immeasurable power Hesitated, to resign, 'Twould been an act of purer fame Than gathers round Marengo's name And gilded thy decline, Through the long night of crime! Despite some passing clouds of crime! But thou forsooth must be a king And don the purple vest, As if that foolish robe could wring Remembrance from thy breast. Where is that faded garment—where The gewgaws thou wast fond to wear, The star—the string—the crest? Vain, forward child of empire! say, Are all thy playthings snatched away? Where may the wearied eye repose When gazing on the great, Where neither guilty glory glows, Nor despicable state? Was—one—the last—the best— The Cincinnatus of the West; Whom envy dares to hate, Bequeathed the name of Washington, To make man blush there was but One!" MISCELLANEOUS. THE NEW WORK. On our arrival at the scene of horror—and it was at the door of that little seven by nine dwelling, where is quartered a whole army of Irishmen—a little jolly-faced plump gentleman from the land of the Bogs, opened his jaws, and said to us, "good masters if you'll only be quiet a bit now, I'll tell you all about and about it." He was in a sad predicament, and the March winds, as they whistled around him, blew at full length, the small quantity of linen that served as a covering to his person. Stop, stop, he continued, "good master, and you shall know the whole matter of it, that’s what you shall by the pipes of St. Dennis. You must know then, that last night I had a bit of a jollification—I drank a good deal of the creature; and after I had danced a wee bit, Teage O' Neal said to me—that is to myself—Larry O' Bog, you're a spalpeen, you are, for drinking so much of the creature, and now do you see, let's take a night-cap of the whiskey of old Ireland, and then get to bed, and take a wee bit of a cocktail snooze. With all my heart, says I, that’s what you know, and so, you see I put under my shirt, a bit of a flask, of the creature.

When we review what has been accomplished by the American Bible Society during the eight years of its existence, and by the many other Bible Societies which have been organized in the twenty years that have now elapsed since the British and Foreign Bible Society was formed; when we recollect that these Institutions have been countenanced and aided by Emperors and Kings, and Rulers, by Noblemen, Statesmen, and Prelates, by those who are venerable for their age and their piety, by those who are eminent for their talents and learning, by all ranks and conditions and denominations; —when we reflect that, in these Institutions, Christians without controversy or suspicion, and laying aside the panoply of the polemic for a time, they have united in testifying their love to God and their love to men—when we call to mind that every year has witnessed new multitudes joining the holy brotherhood, revenues to a vast amount raised and expended, and many, very many thousands, and hundreds of thousands of copies of the scriptures sent forth to the needy,—who can forbear to cry out in holy astonishment, and fervent gratitude, ‘what hath God wrought!’ who will not feel desirous to do far more than he has yet done, to aid the hallowed cause at home and abroad?

MISS HASTINGS will commence a School in the town of Sitka, on Monday the 21st instant. This School is exclusively designed for the instruction of Young Ladies, in all the branches usually taught in common English schools; also, in Geography, Natural and Ex perimental Philosophy, Plain and Ornamental Needle Work, Drawing and Painting. Miss Hastings is recently from the state of New Hampshire, where she has taught school for a number of years. By order of the Trustees, ISAAC WATERS, Secretary. May 15—14 EDUCATION. THE Public school at Alton, which was in stituted by an act of the General Assem bly, at their last session, is now open in this town, for the instruction of youth of both sexes, in any or all the different branches of English education that may be required. — The principal branches to be taught in this school are, Orthography, Reading, Writing, English Grammar, Geography, Arithmetic, and Mensuration of Surfaces and Solids—also, Trigonometry and the Art of Surveying. The Trustees have erected and fitted up for the purpose, a large and commodious building, and have employed Mr. Henry H. Snow to take charge of the school. Mr. Snow is a gentleman of urbane manners and unexceptionable morals, and from his ample literary attainments eminently qualified for the charge.

The petition being explained to the Bashaw by Lexington, (or Hu met American, his new name) the Bashaw forbade the Turks striking the prisoners. August 11.—The Bashaw sent for me, and agreeably to his orders, I took my seat by his side. He began a conversation about my country and our squadron, which was then in sight, and consisted of eighteen sail. He said for two dollars he could repair all the damages that the bombardment did to this town—that but one man was hurt by the shells—that what he had been offered for the American prisoners was about fifty dollars per man—that he would make them earn that sum in two months. He asked me what I thought my country would give for me. I told him I did not know.—He said he would not take twenty thousand dollars for me; to which I replied, that I might then expect to remain in slavery for life. He patted me on the shoulder, and said I must then content myself to stay with him. I asked to go and see our men, but he refused, saying the Moors and Arabs would kill me if they could catch me. August 12.—Our squadron hoisted a flag of truce, sent in a brig and schooner, and fired a gun.

Sir—Your Vegetable Cerate having prevented my hair from coming out, I recommended it to Dr. Divine, who was nearly bald; he had not used it more than four weeks, when a vigorous vegetation of young hair made its appearance. From a motive of curiosity I have paid much attention to its action for fifteen months, and from accurate observations, I can safely say, I have known as many as fifty persons, whose hair has been reproduced, in this city. I am induced to believe, from the above observations, that it is the only thing ever yet discovered that reproduces the hair. With respect, WILLIAM CHAMBERS. Copy of a letter from George Hubbard, Esq. of Berlin, Hartford County Conn. to the proprietor of the Vegetable Cerate, or Hair Restorative. Sir—For the satisfaction of those who have lost their hair, and become bald, I will state, that I was completely bald, for nearly five years: on hearing of the many cures effected by your Vegetable Cerate, I was induced to give it a trial. And now, after using three boxes, and in the short space of six weeks, I find my hair is reproduced with all its original vigor. I was also afflicted with an almost constant head-ache—since using the Cerate it has entirely left me. "Your ob't servant, George Hubbard" Hundreds of Certificates might be added; but the acknowledged virtue of the Cerate renders that unnecessary.

The friends of Col. Barbour, in his behalf, make the following propositions, to the friends of Capt Davis—That these gentlemen shall each choose one, two or more gentlemen - that the gentlemen thus selected shall choose another, not a resident of the county (to be selected by lot if they cannot agree upon one) to act as umpire upon all questions upon which the referees do not agree—that they shall scrutinize the polls taken (at the Court-House and the precincts) for a delegate to represent this county in the Legislature at the approaching session of the General Assembly—that they shall expunge from both polls all the votes which they shall decide to be illegal, and that the Sheriff shall return the candidate, which shall, upon such scrutiny, have the greatest number of good votes. Each candidate shall furnish the other, within days, with a list of the votes to which they respectively object—and the burden of proof shall, in all cases, lie upon the candidate for whom the objectionable votes were given. The referees and their umpire shall meet at such times and places as they shall appoint, and have power to adjourn from time to time at their discretion. The referees are to have the same power to transfer votes from the back to the front of the polls, that is given by law to the Sheriff.

In compliance with the act of the last session, directing the board of public works to appoint an able engineer of another state, and three experienced commissioners from this state, to assist the civil engineer of this state in making a new survey of the James and Jacksons river, from Maiden’s Adventure Falls to Dunlap’s creek, the board was convened for that purpose and met in the month of May last, when Benjamin Wright, Esq of the state of New York was chosen as assistant engineer; and Col Philip R. Thompson, Col George Love, and Richard N. Venable, Esq as commissioners for the purpose aforesaid. It was understood by previous correspondence with Judge Wright that his engagements would not admit of his coming to this state before the month of October, when he would review the line which had been previously surveyed by an engineer and commissioner. The high character of Judge Wright induced the board to accept his services on those conditions, though his personal assistance throughout was desirable. Mr Venable, one of the commissioners, declined accepting his appointment. Vexatious Litigation. — Judge Irving says he has tried 238 actions of slander and assault and battery in that city, beginning with the winter of 1824. The aggregate costs and expenditures he estimates at $5,000. Of this number, 91 verdicts were for defendants, and 60 for the plaintiffs with only six cents damages; showing that two-thirds of the actions were frivolous, and two-thirds of the expense thrown away. Potatoes. — Mr. Wm. Wilson states in the N. Y. Farmer, as the result of 20 years experience, that the driest and best-flavored potatoes, and the most abundant crops, are produced on strong heavy loams. He tried 7 years in succession to raise good potatoes on sandy soil. He thinks that thorough cultivation is necessary to raise potatoes of good quality. Canada Thistle — Mr. Winchell of Duchess county, N. Y. in an article published in the N. Y. Farmer, describes several experiments which he has made to exterminate the Canada Thistle. He tried strong brine, frequent plowings, covering with boards and barks, cutting down, etc. and as the result of his experiments concludes that if the stalks are frequently destroyed, by any means whatever, for one or two seasons, in the months of August and September, the thistle will be destroyed. Mr. Johnson of Louisiana submitted for consideration the following resolutions, which lie over one day of course: Resolved, That the president of the United States be requested to lay before this house such information as he may possess, with regard to the piratical depredations upon our commerce in the West Indies during the last six months, what naval force is employed now in that service, what seizures and captures have been made, and what additional force can be ordered on that duty, and what other means are necessary to suppress these practices and to give entire protection to our commerce. Resolved, That the president of the United States be requested to lay before this house any particular acts of violence and outrage that have been committed upon the persons and property of American citizens in the port of Havana, how far they are permitted or acquiesced in by the authorities of that place; how far they have refused the aid of their laws to restrain and punish them, and what measures have been taken by this government in relation to this class of injury. "Perpetual motion seems to be a greater desideratum in politics than in the arts. For certainly the machines (conventions, caucuses, etc.) which worked admirably a few years since, are out of joint, rusty, and useless. The very sound of their operations "grates horrible" on the public ear, as they labor without relief from appeasing oil. Each member grinds, toils and clatters over its own work in total disregard of harmony, and in utter repugnance to combination, and the results are as slovenly and ill-shaped as an awkward manager, with a rickety machine, can contrive to produce out of poor and unmanageable materials.

We never shall forget the noble declaration which was uttered at Ghent, at the gloomiest period of the negotiation with the British commissioners, in the late war: He firmly and manfully declared, that his country rose in proportion to the pressure of the war upon her, and that she never would yield to the demands of the enemy, as long as a stick of wood was left in Kentucky. The sentiment ought to be inscribed in letters of gold, and worn at the heart of every American. It was uttered by a statesman who would have been the pride and ornament of any nation; by a statesman who nobly labored, and sacrificed his health and life in the service of his country. — Phil. Inq. John Marshall. It must give every American pleasure to witness the voluntary homage that is paid to virtue, patriotism, and talents, in the person of Chief Justice Marshall. This eminent man has the singular felicity of having passed through a long public life blameless and unreproached. No whisper is ever heard against a single action of his life, and there is no other man in the nation who enjoys to such an extent its confidence. His words are considered almost the oracles of wisdom, and long before his death, his statue has been erected in the temple of Fame. "May he live longer than I have time to tell his years, ever beloved and loving!"

He returned to the stable, got on his horse with a saddle, and was proceeding to leave to Annapolis. The stable; it was with much difficulty that Hewson, junior, assisted by the other lad, could remove him from the horse; his strength was great, and it was with difficulty he was brought within doors. Mr. Hewson, seeing him at this time, sent for Mr. Benn, a most eminent practitioner in Bridge-road, who stood by him for a quarter of an hour, during which time the lad considered stopped at the turnpike gate and took a sixpence out of his pocket to be changed, and holding out his hand for the change, the sixpence was returned to him. He immediately observed, "none of your nonsense, that is the sixpence again, give me my change," when 3 shillings and 2 pence was given to him. He counted it over and said "None of your gammon, that is not right. I want a penny more," making the 4 shillings and 2 pence which was his proper change. He then said, "Give me my castor," (meaning his hat) which slang terms he had been in the habit of using, and then began to whip and spur to get his horse on; his pulse at the time was 180 full and hard; no change of countenance could be observed, nor any spasmodic affection of the muscles, the eyes remaining closed the whole time.

San Martin wishes the fleet to be under the direction of the patriot governor of Peru; but Cochrane will not consent to it, which has caused some little difference between the two governments. San Martin must ultimately submit to that of Chile. The war appears to be at an end in this part of the world, and the prospect is that in a short time a considerable business will be done. The English are flocking here, and large quantities of goods are arriving daily. Four English ships with full cargoes arrived this week: they do not go to Lima, on account of the heavy duty of 20 per cent, which amounts to a prohibition. This country at present is in a very distressed condition, owing to the failure of crops. There is not enough wheat raised this season, for the city of St. Jago. There will not be enough saved in the country for seed another year. The inhabitants of Peru are also in a very distressed state. Bread was selling in Lima a short time since, at five dollars a pound. [Here Mr. Washington was shown a paper, submitted to the Committee by Mr. Edwards, as the enclosure contained in the letter of the Secretary of the Treasury to the Cashier of the Bank of Edwardsville, dated 1st of November 1819.] Do you know the handwriting? Answer. I do. Q. Will you state whose it is? A. It is the handwriting of the late Edward Post, formerly a Clerk in the Office of the Secretary of the Treasury. Q. Do you know who copied the letters of the 1st of November, above referred to, for the purpose of being communicated to the House, with the Report of the Secretary of the 24th of March last. A. I do—it was copied by myself. Q. Did the original copy of the enclosure accompany the original letter, when copied by you? A. It did not. The enclosure was a letter to a Receiver. The letters called for, were letters to and from Banks. Q. Do you, or do you not, know that the Secretary of the Treasury could have commanded the same means during the recess of Congress, in 1822, for the purpose of preparing the documents to accompany the report made on the 22nd of March, 1824, as were employed during the succeeding year, to prepare the same documents; and, if so, do you know what means.

The envoy arriving triumphant at the gates of his capital.— Little could be expected from his presence near the scene of active operations, whereas it was of the highest importance that his person should be far from the reach of the Russian. The city of Broussa, on the Asiatic shore, about 190 miles from Constantinople, across the Propontis, was therefore the place supposed to have been se lected by the Sultan as a retreat in the first in stance. Tranquility prevailed in the capital up to the departure of the mail, notwithstanding the uneasiness of the inhabitants. Letters of the 31st have been received today from Odessa. They state that in consequence of the prompt and energetic measures adopted by the local authorities on the manifestation of the first symptoms of the plague near that city, the contagion had been so completely checked, that no fears were entertained of its making any progress. The city of Odessa itself had escaped all danger of contagion, and its inhabitants had sufficiently recovered from their late alarm to resume their habitual avocations. The Allgemeine Zeitung, which arrived yesterday by the Flanders mail, is full of interesting intelligence from the theatre of war.

On the morning of the 30th December, the Turks were seen advancing in three heavy columns in the direction of the George Franklin and Bofzaris batteries, led on by German officers, under a tremendous fire of artillery and mortars; at the same time their fleet commenced a most furious attack on the port. They were received by a destructive shower of grape and musketry from the troops and batteries; at the same time a discharge of shells and rockets added to their dismay, but in spite of a heavy loss, they advanced and carried two of the outposts. The conflict now raged with the greatest fury; shot and shells spread death in every direction. Our gallant artillery and riflemen excited terror around them. It is impossible to describe the horrors of the scene; all around was obscured with clouds of smoke. At this moment, two Turkish vessels blew up with a tremendous explosion; still they persisted, and after two hours’ fighting, they succeeded in obtaining possession of the Franklin battery. After a heavy loss, an officer planted the Turkish colors on the rampart, when our gallant leader, St. Aubyn, rushed forward and hurled him from the walls.

I ask in behalf of the Indians only for a pittance of these enormous sums, to be expended in establishing schools among the Indians, in teaching them the pursuits of agriculture and the mechanical arts, and in establishing proper regulations for their government, and for the distribution and security of their property. Sir, in the language of the late Secretary of War, let "the system "which has been adopted, be put "into extensive and active operation," and the result will be infinitely more honorable to us; the prosperity and happiness of the Indians will be more effectually promoted and secured, than by any new invention for their benefit. Before we carry the eighty thousand Indians, now on this side of the Mississippi, over that river, I conjure gentlemen to look at the situation of the two hundred thousand which are already there.

His anxious parents were firm for a while, reluctant at parting with this promising son; but relying upon the goodness of the author of all good, and believing that in the dispensations of his providence he will do right, they at length yielded and cheerfully submitted to his call. The subject of this notice was six years younger than William, and had, during his absence, and for some time after his death, followed, with equal perseverance and fidelity, the former laborious employment of his brother. The gentleman above alluded to, perceiving in Lyman the same natural activity and manly deportment which had characterized William almost from his infancy, solicited and obtained the consent of his parents that Lyman should supply his place. He did so, and having remained in this situation, giving and receiving the most perfect satisfaction for about six months, was while boarding in the same family in which his brother died, and just five days before his own death, taken ill of a complaint, the symptoms of which appeared to correspond with those in William's case. Although his attack was by no means so violent, and the remedies administered at first had a flattering effect, and although he appeared irreconcilable to the idea that this disease must terminate his earthly existence, still he seemed as if by intuition, to be certain that he should never recover.

The Ferry.- The ferry-boat plying between the eastern and western sections of the town, commenced operations, last Sunday, under the most favorable auspices. In the course of the day, she made about seventy-five passages, and realized something like two dollars for her own cri, The passengers were composed principally of the members of the different religious societies, who availed themselves of this easy and convenient mode of conveyance to carry them to their respective houses of worship. The establishment of the Ferry will prove of vast convenience to the public, and save an almost incalculable amount of land travel, which has hitherto been performed by those who have business to attend to in the different sections of the town, or who worship in churches situated at remote points, and at unequal distances. We learn that the proprietors of the ferry, in order to render every facility to passengers of every description, have authorized their agent to convey such mechanics as cross the river necessarily, in pursuance of their vocations, at a very moderate rate, which will supersede the necessity of their keeping boats of their own.

The day before, the Queen had been officially reported to the Cortes to continue in the same state; the King in that of a person gradually recovering from a fit of the gout; and Cardinal Bourbon had breathed his last. His Majesty's person and suite are escorted by a respectable column of foot and horse, which will be successively relieved by troops stationed all along the road for this purpose. "The inhabitants of Madrid (says the Universal) are sorry for the departure of the King and Government; but they are sensible that the sacrifice to which they submit, is of the greatest importance, and that this measure alone will contribute, more effectually than many victories to defeat the plans of our enemies." The cortes have lately been engaged in discussing certain instructions for the administration of the Spanish Provinces beyond the Seas. SLAVERY. Sir Robert Wilson, in submitting a petition to the British Parliament on the 27th of March on the subject of Slavery, remarked that in their West India Colonies there were about one million of human beings, who every morning as the sun rose, were awakened by the echoing lash of the whip, and knew but too well that they were to be punished for the remainder of the day, like cattle, at the caprice or discretion of the overseer.

A debate of considerable duration took place on this bill (principally on a difference of opinion as to the probable operation and consequences of the bill, as well as on the question of its expediency) in which Mr. Barbour supported, and Mr. South opposed the bill.

Horses alone will enable them to make that appearance. Gen. Ashley, with his mounted men, traverses the continent in safety—goes to the Buena Ventura and Multnomah, and returns in safety. For want of horses our soldiers are pursued, surrounded, insulted, harassed, and assassinated. Read the report of Major Riley, and see what his detachment suffered for want of horses. The mounted force is indispensable: it is the appropriate, and the only appropriate, defense for the West; it is the true and adequate, and the only true and adequate, protection for the Fur trade, the Mexican trade, and the whole line of the Western frontier. It is the security, and the only security, for the tranquility of the frontiers, the preservation of lives, and the protection of two great branches of western commerce. This being shown—the great fact of the necessity of this species of force being established—I feel confident that the consideration of the expense that may attend it, which is the next point to be examined, will present but little difficulty to the Senate. The mounted force being necessary, the cost of it I feel persuaded, will not be an overruling consideration, although that cost should be large, when, in point of fact, it will be small, and, in comparison to its object, inconsiderable and insignificant. Five hundred horses will be wanted for ten companies of fifty men each; the price of these horses, and their maximum price, will be $50 each.

It has obtained its present great distinction by the extraordinary successes which has attended it in the healing Art, while every avenue and every track have been searched in vain for its parallel; its discovery is one of the most sacred boons that can be afforded to the unfortunate martyr of disease, and it is most sincerely hoped, that the sympathy of the public will be aroused to the promulgation of inestimable merits. Proofs of the value of Potter’s genuine Catholicon—its healing powers—its usefulness as a general restorer of health, the city of Philadelphia alone could furnish thousands of witnesses, to contradict the base and malicious fabrications which daily emanate from the jealous. What, we would ask, can be stronger proof of great utility, than its success in the PENNSYLVANIA HOSPITAL, PHILADELPHIA ALMSHOUSE, and the PHILADELPHIA and NEW YORK PENITENTIARIES, and in other public institutions alike serviceable, where the eminent skill which presides over them had been exhausted in vain? "These facts speak trumpet tongued" and cannot be gainsaid.

A grand reservoir, which will be brought into use before winter, is now building in New York, at a cost of near $50,000. It is of cast iron, and placed in such high ground, that water will run from it in pipes to any part of the city—and in some, at such an elevation that it can be discharged on the tops of houses and stores. Essay on Man.—Two footpads lately attempted to rob Major Samuel Mann on the Chesterstown turnpike in Maryland. The Major fortunately having a pair of new boots, put one of his assailants out of breath by a kick in the bread-basket, and the other took leg bail. 'Will nothing immortalize me?' said a conceited blue-stocking to one of her male friends, after having vainly endeavored to procure a publisher for some of her 'loose thoughts.' 'Yes, madam,' replied he, 'making a funeral pyre for yourself with your own manuscripts.' 'But think,' said the lady, 'what the world would lose.' 'Aye, but think also what the world would gain,' said her tormentor. Mr. Eli Bunk advertises in the Schoharie Advocate a caution against one David Sandys who has run away with his three daughters. A Pennsylvania canal recently called on a surgeon at Easton, with a broken arm, which, having set and bandaged, secundum artem, the surgeon advised him to drink no ardent spirits until the fracture healed.

A procession to be formed at Jefferson-Hall, under a military escort, to proceed through some of the principal streets to the Universalist Meeting-House. The Rev. Mr. Turner to be requested to perform the religious services. The Chairman of the Board of Selectmen to read the Declaration of Independence. Dr. Charles A. Cheever is appointed to deliver an Oration, and has accepted. Music at the Meeting-House to be under the direction of Mr. Lewis Elliott. The following gentlemen are requested to act as Committee of Arrangements, viz: Messrs. ICHABOD BARTLETT, N. A. HAVEN, JR., TIMOTHY UPHAM, SAMUEL GOOKIN, GEORGE SPARHAWK. Salutes to be fired and the bells to be rung at sunrise, noon, and sunset. THOMAS P. BROWN, Sec'y. The Committee appointed as above, beg leave to announce to their fellow-citizens the following arrangements: Salutes will be fired and the bells rung at sunrise, noon, and sunset. The procession will move from Jefferson-Hall, precisely at half-past 10, those who intend to join it will assemble as early as 10, A. M. Col. Samuel Gookin is appointed Chief Marshal of the day. The order of procession, etc. will be published by him. The procession will be escorted by the 1st L. I. Company under Capt. Robert LeFavour, through Congress-street, Middle Road, Jaffrey and Pleasant-streets, to the Meeting-House, and return through State and Daniel-streets to Jefferson-Hall.

Sent: I have the honor to acknowledge the receipt of your communication of the 23rd instant, stating the advantages in reference to the selection of a route for the contemplated National Road from Washington to New Orleans, presented by a line passing through the four Western counties of North Carolina; and requesting that this line may be surveyed, and the result laid before Congress previously to the location of the route. The examination of the routes which have been made, and that are now in progress, it is believed, will enable Congress to fix upon a general line of location at the present session; but, before the actual location takes place, minute surveys will be required, and the features and advantages of the various sections of country through which the road will pass, accurately ascertained. It will not, however, be practicable to have an examination made, this season, of the section of North Carolina mentioned in your communication; but, should Congress decide upon running the general direction of the route through the Southern or Atlantic States, the part of North Carolina referred to will be taken into proper consideration, and be surveyed, if, upon a correct estimate of its importance, a survey of it should be required.
