I was deceived; yet the phantom which my own fancy raised continues to haunt my memory, and though I am now aware I loved an unworthy nothing, yet, like Gibbon, I am proud that I was once capable of feeling so pure and exalted a passion. The celebrated and virtuous John Wesley, in the course of his voyage to America, hearing an uncommon noise in the cabin of General Oglethorpe, Governor of Georgia, with whom he sailed, stepped in to inquire the cause of it on which the General immediately addressed him— "Mr. Wesley, you must excuse me, I have met with a provocation too serious to bear. You know the only wine I drink is Cyprus, and it agrees with me the best of any, I therefore provided myself with several dozen of it, and this villain, Grimgribber (his foreign servant, who was present and almost dead with fear) has drank up the whole of it. But I will be revenged on him. I have ordered him to be tied hand and foot, and to be carried to the man-of-war, that sails with us. The rascal should have taken care how he used me so, for I never forgive!"

From the Hon. W. B. Calhoun, for several years Chairman of the Committee on Education in the Legislature of Massachusetts, and now Speaker of the House of Representatives. In reference to the Franklin Primer, and the Improved Reader, and the General Class Book, he says:— “I gave them a very thorough examination and found them so peculiarly adapted to the wants of children, in the early stages of education, that I recommended their introduction into schools at once. They are just the kind of books that are needed, as I think, to make the business of education pleasant and easy.” “We can say with truth that they (the Franklin Primer, the Improved Reader, and the General Class-Book) are certainly well adapted to the purpose for which they are intended by their highly deserving author, and may be recommended to the attention of parents and teachers.”— North-American Review, April, 1829. “These books have been examined by the best judges in our State and have received unqualified approbation. They are beginning to be introduced into the Boston schools, and will do there, what they have done in Hampden County, reform the present false modes of instruction.”— Hingham Gazette. From the American Journal of Education. “They are intended to obviate prevailing evils in the manner of teaching the English language, and though susceptible of some improvements, are, on the whole, excellently adapted to their object.

Mr. Russ offered for consideration the following resolution: Resolved, That the Secretary of the Treasury be directed to lay before this House a statement of the precise amount of special deposits to the credit of the Treasury of the United States, which is referred to in his supplementary report of the 28th ultimo, as not being available the current year; stating, particularly, of what such deposits consist—if of depreciated bank paper, the bank or banks which issued the same, with the amount by each, and the present current specie value thereof, and the year or years respectively when the same was received. A letter was presented by the Speaker from the Secretary of the Treasury, transmitting a list of Clerks employed in that office, and their pay; which was referred to the committee on that subject. On motion of Mr. Eustis, the House proceeded to the consideration of the resolution submitted by him, for the conditional admission of Missouri into the Union; and, on his motion, in order that it might not interrupt the discussion of the Army bill, it was referred to a committee of the whole on the state of the Union.

Who that has during the winter spent a part of a day near our parade, and witnessed the harangues of our would-be-great men, will ever doubt the ability of the coalition to make the most noise and parade, or rather noise on the parade? But to proceed, the writer says, the federalists “are continually getting up caucuses and laying plans to deceive the republicans.” This is truth. “But all their exertions will accomplish but little.” This cannot be doubted. Again,— The republicans here are actuated by the same de- termined spirit which animated them to stand or fall with their country’s cause at the commence- ment of the last war.” To this truth our souls can bear witness. “They are ready again to contend for their rights and privileges, which they consider to be involved in the result of the March election.” Truth again. “There are three federal papers contending against the only republican paper we have, the old New-Hamp- shire Gazette.” This we suppose, will rub hard on a certain nameless thing, which pretends to be republican, but we know no difference between federalists and those who think the former party distinctions are useless. Those who are fond of federal company, and cling to them as their dear allies, should be ranked as federalists. “But we think that falsehood and deception cannot triumph, if truth and reason are left free to combat them.”

Beans and Pork. Beverly is famous for beans and Charlestown folks for pork. The Beverly stage was standing at the door of the Charlestown Hotel, when the Charlestown stage drove up. "Take away your lean pot," quoth the Charlestown Jehu; "I will," said Beverly, "as soon as I have got my pork in." Rising of the Netherlands. A village politician in France told his wife that the Netherlands had risen. "That's lucky," replied the good old woman, "it will no longer be exposed to inundation." Maxim. Men show particular folly on five different occasions: when they establish their fortune on the ruin of another; when they expect to excite love by coldness, and by showing more marks of dislike than affection; when they wish to become learned in the midst of repose and pleasure; when they seek friends without making any advances of friendship; and when they are unwilling to succor their friends in distress. — Galaxy. Sleep has often been mentioned as the image of death. Their resemblance is indeed striking and apparent; they both, when they seize the body, leave the soul at liberty, and wise is he that remembers of both, that they can be made safe and happy only by virtue.

The success of his operations will greatly further that of the army of the Danube, and that of the whole army. A letter from the Russian Admiral Heyden to the Consul General at Smyrna assures the Pasha that his Majesty does not desire to molest the peaceful inhabitants of the Ottoman towns and villages, or to impede the commerce of neutral powers. It is said that a brig belonging to the Egyptian Pasha, which sailed from Modena, with a cargo of Greek slaves had been taken by a Russian frigate. Two other vessels of the Pasha had shared the same fate. GREECE. It seems that a conspiracy against the life of the President has been foiled in Greece and that the recent successes of the Russians have raised the tone of the Greeks. An article dated Smyrna June 18th says that Count Capo D’Istra will be obliged to have recourse to more energetic measures to govern the Greek nation, especially since the discovery of the conspiracy of Colocotroni, who has been arrested with 25 of his accomplices. The President, while he was at Paros, gave a formal audience to the Greek bishops sent by the Sultan to propose an amnesty, and dismissed them with an admonition to leave the Greek territory because their addresses, in which they call upon the people to return to their obedience to the Porte, cannot be tolerated by the Greek Government.

It is intended, that our readers will see by a plan of arrangements, that the procession shall move forward at 17 o'clock. As some changes have been made in the order of procession, we republish it today in its corrected form, Funeral of Gen. Brown.—On Wednesday the remains of this brave and lamented veteran were consigned to the grave. At an early hour in the morning, the troops began to assemble on their different parade grounds, and about 11 o'clock, the companies formed on that part of the Pennsylvania avenue which runs North of the President’s House. The carriages containing the heads of Departments, the Supreme Court, and the two Houses of Congress, formed into line; and about 12 o'clock, the body of the deceased General was brought out of the house, and placed in the hearse, which was drawn by four horses. The procession began its solemn and slow march, various bands performing the funeral dirges usual on such occasions. The old war horse of the General, said to be upwards of thirty years of age, was led before the hearse, the General's boots fastened to the saddle, reversed in the stirrups.

"Hello there, Master Job! I've traveled half the globe trying to find some reasonable dog to do my dyeing. Get up—if you've a soul," the Frenchman doubted, "but thought it best upon the whole to cut head." Just then another luckless wight, who came that night, commenced as usual at the door to cry, "Job! Job! over and over. Confound you, Job, why don't you come?" was still the cry. But Job was slow. "I tell you, Job, I want to know when you intend to dye!" Monsieur felt very sick—his tight grew thick. "Die! Die! Messieurs—mon dieu! I mean no die—parbleu! Die blue?" another cried. "There, Job, you lied. Come, no excuses borrow—you promised me, you devil, yes, you would dye black tomorrow." The Frenchman felt unpleasantly numb; he thought his time was come. Another fellow with his bundle came; a witty one—and lame. "Good Mr. Fustick," said this one, "I've hither run, half limping and half flying, to know what time tomorrow by the sun you will be dyeing!" The Frenchman shook his unfeeling head to see if he might dead or living be. For then they screamed good-naturedly and somewhat civil. Just at that worthy hour, they had the power.

Burdine had been ordered to meet the lessees at the Great Crossings, in Kentucky, on the 1st March, and proceed with them to select the mine. A small detachment of regular troops were to give them support and countenance. Three Indian agents have been directed to hold themselves in readiness to proceed with the lessees to explain to the Indians the views of the government in making the leases, and securing the lessees in the uninterrupted prosecution of the work. Mr. B. took two objections to these leases: 1. Disadvantageous to the Republic. 2. Illegal. First, Disadvantageous, he said, because the mines intended to be covered by the lease were worth an immense sum, and were parted with for a song. The leases would be located on the left bank of the Mississippi, between Wisconsin and Rock river. Mr. B. showed a table of the Indian trade at St. Louis, in 1816, which exhibited the quantity of 180,000 lbs. weight of lead sold by the Indians, from these mines, to the fur traders. He referred to Mr. Brackenridge’s Views of Louisiana, page 65, to show that, in 1811, the same item amounted to 500,000 lbs. weight. At five cents a pound, the average price at St. Louis, the first of these quantities would command 9,000 dollars, and the second 25,000 dollars.

There were more votes in favor of that law in the House of Representatives, not only in each of a majority of the Western States, but even in Virginia herself also, than in Massachusetts. It was literally forced upon New England, and this shows how groundless, how void of all probability any change must be, which imputes to her hostility to the growth of the Western States, as naturally flowing from a cherished policy of her own. But leaving all conjectures about causes and motives, I go at once to the fact, and I meet it with one broad, comprehensive, and emphatic negative. I deny that in any part of her history, at any period of the government, or in relation to any leading subject, New England has manifested such hostility as is charged upon her. On the contrary, I maintain that, from the day of the cession of the Territories by the States to Congress, no portion of the country has acted, either with more liberality or more intelligence, on the subject of the Western lands, in the new States, than New England. This statement, though strong, is no stronger than the strictest truth will warrant. Let us look at the historical facts. So soon as the cessions were obtained, it became necessary to make provision for the government and disposition of the Territory—the country was to be governed.

M." which begins by stating, I am charged to transmit to you, with a request to insert it in your next Number, the following document, which, after deliberation, was adopted this day by a numerous assembly, and Spontaneously in order to concert the measures which circumstances render # # * necessary and indispensable for the preservation of our rights, and the establishment of a true Constitutional Government.”  Manifesto to the French—to all people and to all governments.  A solemn Act had, in 1816, laid the basis of a reconciliation between the French nation and the ancient dynasty, and fixed the conditions by which the Chief of the Bourbon family should resume and preserve the exercise of the Royal authority—reiterated oaths have at different epochs rendered more imperious the obligations contracted by the chiefs of this family, and had made their Charter the sole title to the obedience of the French. All these oaths have been violated during the last sixteen years, by the establishment of a great number of laws, opposed in their spirit and letter to the spirit of the Constitutional Charter: but each of the attempts made against this fundamental law had an appearance of legality, and had not exceeded legislative forms, which, while they had been preserved, offered the means of reparation.

During his administration, a bill was passed through both Houses of Congress, conferring the jurisdiction and prescribing the mode by which the Federal Government should exercise it, in the case of the Cumberland Road. He returned it, with objections to its passage, and in assigning them, took occasion to say that in the early stages of the Government, he had inclined to the construction that it had no right to expend money except in the performance of acts authorized by the other specific grants of power, according to a strict construction of them, but that, on further reflection and observation, his mind had undergone a change - that his opinion then was, "that Congress has an unlimited power to raise money, and that, in its appropriation, they have a discretionary power, restricted only by the duty to appropriate it to purposes of common defence and of general, not local - national, not State benefit: and this was avowed to be the governing principle throughout the residue of his administration. The views of the last administration are of such recent date as to render a particular reference to them unnecessary. It is well known that the appropriating power, to the utmost extent which had been claimed for it, in relation to internal improvements, was fully recognized and exercised by it.

A poor, drunken sailor, being asked, if he was sure of being gratified in three wishes, what they would be, replied—“My first wish would be all the brandy in the world.” Your next, Jack? “All the tobacco in the world.” Now for the third? “Why I’d have more brandy.” A lady, who was desperately fond of play was confessing herself. The priest, among other arguments to dissuade her from gaming, said that she ought to consider the loss of time. “Ah father,” said she, “it is always what vexes me: so much time is lost in shuffling the cards.” Lord Townsend, whose good humour was proverbial, was dining one day at a friend’s where among other servants, the coachman waited on the table. In handing a sauce tureen, honest John unluckily spilt part of its contents over his lordship’s clothes, who instead of showing any displeasure, jocosely said, “John, you should take care never to come anything but your wheels.” A clock-maker of St. George’s in the East, London, has invented an apparatus, which, by the sole agency of a clock, wakes the workmen, and lights a candle, at any desired time, night or morning! Ludicrous Anecdote. The Nottingham Mercury tells a good story, which it entitles the “march of humanity.”

From St. Domingo. —Capt. Wise, of the brig Essex, arrived at Charleston on the 10th inst. from Port-au-Prince, which place he left on the 21st ult. informs, that 3 days previous to his departure, President Boyer arrived there from Cape Henry, with 16,000 troops, among whom were the bodyguards of the late Christophe. His return was succeeded by great rejoicings and illuminations, during which all business was suspended. The tranquility of the Island was deemed to be completely established, and the consolidation of its government under Boyer was hailed with universal satisfaction. Markets were dull—American produce freely admitted. Capt. Wise brought despatches for government. Mr. Daniel V. Brown, of this town, has been appointed Deputy Sheriff. Accident. —Pauling’s Bridge, over the river Schuylkill, above Norristown, Pa. on the night of the 10th inst. gave in while a wagon loaded with salt, plaster, &c. and drawn by five horses, was attempting to pass over it.

Sept. 21, 1830. istf WAR DEPARTMENT, Washington, November 17, 1830. Pension and Bounty Land Requisitions. THE many impositions which are attempted in relation to Pension and Bounty Land Claims, has caused the Department of War to establish a regulation which declares that no attention will, in future, be given to applications from persons who act as Agents, unless they are known at the Department, or are vouched for as respectable persons by some one who is known. Notice of this regulation is hereby given; and that all may be informed thereof, it is requested that publishers of the laws of the United States, in the respective States, will insert the same, on the front page of their respective papers, for three months.

The Franklin Bank of Alexandria to the United States was made, for the purpose of securing certain loans made to it by the Treasury, and all the circumstances connected with such transfer. I was applied to as the Attorney of the United States for the District of Columbia by the Secretary of the Treasury, sometime in the summer or fall of 1821, to carry into effect an agreement which had been entered into between the Secretary of the Treasury and the Franklin Bank of Alexandria, in relation to a debt of about £18,000 due from the Franklin Bank to the United States. The agreement appeared to have been entered into on the 23rd of August, 1821 and the Bank bound themselves to transfer to Wm. H. Crawford, and his successors in office, all the debts due to that bank, and all the real estate of every description belonging to the bank, and all the notes given by the stockholders of the bank, on account of stock by them severally subscribed thereto. The real estate was to be disposed of on the most advantageous terms, and the whole amount of the moneys collected was to be placed in the Bank of Alexandria, to the credit of the Treasurer of the United States.

Tuesday, December 15. The following gentlemen were elected as board of internal improvement for the next year, viz: Isaac T. Avery, Burton Yancey, John D. Hawkins, Thomas Turner, Durant Hatch, Jr. and Win. J. Cowan. The house unanimously resolved that the public treasurer be directed to pay Denison Olmsted one hundred dollars, to defray his expenses in making a geological and mineralogical survey of the state. A number of bills passed their second and third readings. Wednesday, December 26. The following bills were passed: Mr. Macon's bill to amend an act passed in 1796, to remedy certain inconveniences arising under the present land laws. Mr. Stanly, a bill regulating the fees of notaries, and a bill to amend the acts making provision for the poor. Mr. Hassell, a bill to alter the time of holding the general assembly; and Mr. Boykin, a bill to increase the rate of toll at the big bridge in New Hanover county. Which bills passed their first reading. Mr. Stanly, from the judiciary committee, reported on a resolution referred to them, that it is inexpedient to change the law relative to executions on judgments in the supreme court. He also reported unfavorably on the petition of sundry inhabitants of Burke; which reports were concurred with. The house went into a committee of the whole on the bill to provide for internal improvements, Mr. Buckell in the chair.

At the upper end of York-street, there are two Albions, natives of this city; the one being about 10 years old, and the other four 6. The parents are perfectly black, and have four children of as deep a color as themselves; one of whom is intermediate, with respect to age, between the subjects of these remarks. They have light hair, resembling lamb’s wool—bluish eyes—and, a very delicate complexion though the whiteness is rather of the cast that belongs to a dead body than a living one. Their eyes are weaker than those of any other variety of men in this section of country; but by moonlight, they see as well as other persons commonly do in the brightest daylight. Buffon, Saussure, and Blumenbach, have, each of them, attempted a solution of this phenomenon, but in neither instance, in our opinion, is it satisfactorily accounted for. The writer says, that it is generally observed in all warm-blooded animals, but, that he has never seen it exhibited in those with cold blood. The mother of these children had also a brother and sister born Albions; who, with herself, were natives of Savannah; she now belongs to a Mr. Zettler, of Effingham County, in this State.—Sav. Geo.

At or near this place heads the Luchkadee, or California Stinking Fork, Yellowstone South fork of Missouri, and Henry's fork-all those head at one angular point; that of Yellowstone has a large freshwater lake near its head on the very top of the mountain, which is one hundred twenty miles in diameter, and as clear as crystal. On the South border of this Lake is a number of hot and boiling springs, some of water and others of moist heat, fine clay, resembling a mush pot, and throwing particles at the immense height of from twenty to thirty feet. The clay is of white, and of a pink color, and the water appears fathomless, as it appears to be en tirely hollow underneath. There is also a number of places where pure sulphur is sent forth in abundance. One of our men visited one of these while taking our recreation—there at an instant the earth began to tre 1 V *' ,_„,mble and he with difficulty made his escape, when an explosion took place resembling that of thunder. During our stay in that quarter I heard it every day. From this place by a circuitous route to the Northwest we returned. Two others and myself pushed on in advance for the purpose of accumulating a few more beaver, and in the act of passing through a narrow confine ...

By dint of ironing, new stretching, and cleaning, has rescued it for some years. If I mistake not, it was formerly in the Dancing Room at Annapolis, which it was presented for the time that should be the resort of the gay. A fashionable Whig has become of the national portrait, whereof others, perhaps, are prouder. The color of the frame is greatly from the merit of this painting—The prickly white imparts a portion of dead flatness to a picture that could be fuller than the tints of the count, under the right arm. The face, although good, wants relief— we think it may have faded. There is great dignity in the contour of the head and right arm. The draperies, although well managed, and the keeping of the whole picture is very fine. [Note by the Editor of the Baltimore Federal Gazette] "It is esteemed as very probable that this picture now in the Museum is believed to be the portrait of Cæcilius Calvert, Baron of Baltimore, in Ireland, to whom Charles I. granted by charter, the province of Maryland, in the year 1632, being the eighth year of his reign. If the impression is correct, it is the work of Van Dyck, who about that period resided in England, and was the most celebrated portrait painter in Europe."

As a citizen of Maine, it is a mortifying reflection that a large proportion of the Senate of the present political year are totally destitute of those talents and acquirements which those who compose so important a branch of our Legislature ought to possess. A large majority of them are not only inexperienced and ignorant as to the manner and matter of legislation, but they are incapable of learning; they are men of small talents, destitute of one of the important requisites for the office, capability. It is with extreme regret, therefore, that I observe some of the same weak men in nomination for the next Senate. As an elector of Hancock, I feel more anxiety, more responsibility, that none may be put in nomination for Senators for this County, but such as are "honest" not only, but "capable." The equal rights of the citizens, the reputation of the County, and the welfare and safety of the people of the State require this of us. It is not enough that a Senator may be inoffensive and harmless; it is important, it is necessary, that he have the talents, the information and the experience, which will enable him to do good—to know the right, and approve and pursue it. An ignorant man, with the best intentions, must often do wrong.

When rapacious and unprincipled men get a little brief authority into their hands, virtue and worth either retire, or are driven into exile. May God in mercy avert from our country such an evil as this. It will behoove the New England States to look about themselves for the most talented and patriotic of her sons, to be her delegates in the next Congress. Never has there been a time when talents and firmness of purpose, in our Senators and Representatives, was more needed. Let the people have an eye upon this subject, and wisely act as wisdom dictates. SPECULATION. Flour is now quoted in Baltimore, Philadelphia and New York, at $10 a barrel; why it is thus high, does not seem to be distinctly known, or agreed upon. At one time we are told that the "Ports are open in England," for the admission of grain; but if we understand the subject, the ports there are always open, subject however to duties which very nearly equal the market value of the produce. This duty is graduated by the price of flour in the English market. If the price be $7 the barrel, the duty is about $4,—but if the price should be $11, the duty is only 31 cents. No indication is given by late arrivals, as far as we have seen, that our merchants can make a profit by giving $10 for the article here, and paying the insurance and freight to England.

On the 11th of February the royal assent was given to the Irish Insurrection Bill, and to the Habeas Corpus Suspension Act. The insurrection bill, says a London paper, gives to Magistrates a right to enter into the most refined and delicate part of any dwelling house, and, if refused admittance, to force the chambers even of A man's. By this Act, any man found out of his house between sunset and sunrise, any person found drinking in a public home in the same period, whether a traveller or not—any owner or occupier of a house, absent from home within the same period—any person in whose possession either arms or ammunition are found—all these are liable to transportation for seven years; and this too without the interference of a Grand Jury by bill—without the Petty Jury by their verdict, and without affording to either party any satisfactory appeal. On the other hand, he who arrives at manhood in vigorous health, and with an unshaken constitution, commonly retains these till old age creeps gradually on, or till he is swept away by some disease or accident that had no connection with the formation of his habits. Remonstrate with the intemperate man in his sober moments, on the misery and guilt attending the course he pursues, and he will tell you with tears it may be the habit is fixed, and I cannot help it. He dies as he lived, a drunkard. The same may be said of all those addicted to vicious habits of any kind. "Can the Ethiopian change his skin, or the leopard his spots? Then may they learn to do well, who have been accustomed to do evil." If then our moral and physical habits possess such an influence in the formation of human character, and if, as we have attempted to show, these habits become fixed in the morning of life, what immense importance is attached to the early period of our existence! Our destiny is then most irretrievably fixed! To be continued. THE ROCKY MOUNTAINS. After it has stood some time, pour off the liquor, just as you would tea, and fill up the pot again with boiling water; in a similar manner pour that off, and so go on filling up and pouring off, till the taste in the pot is tasteless, which will be the case when all the virtue is extracted. The liquor, or malt tea, thus extracted, must then be boiled with a few hops in it, and when it becomes cool enough, that is, about blood heat, add a little yeast to ferment it, and the thing is done. This is the whole art and process of brewing, and to brew a larger quantity requires just the same mode of proceeding as it would to make a tea breakfast for a regiment of soldiers. A peck of malt and four ounces of hops will produce 10 quarts of ale, better than can be purchased in London, and for which purpose a tea-kettle and two pan mugs are sufficient apparatus. Dissection of an Ourang Outang. A Scientific Journal published at Boston, gives an account of the dissection of an Ourang Outang, from the island of Borneo, which died in that city on the day of its arrival. During a considerable part of the voyage it had been cheerful and fond of amusement, very docile and clean in its habits, cleaning out a house made for its residence every morning.

Saleratus is a chemical preparation from the well known article pearl-ash purified and highly charged with carbonic acid gas (fixed air). This valuable process discovered by the venerable Dr. Holyoke, of Salem, Mass. His communications upon this subject may be seen in Dr. Thatcher's Dispensatory, and in other medical and scientific works. A cooling and agreeable saline draught is made by dissolving a teaspoonful of these salts in water, and adding thereto a tablespoonful of lemon juice, or good vinegar, to neutralize the same, producing a strong effervescence. The salutary effects of this medicine are well known to the faculty, in allaying the preternatural heat and thirst attending fevers.

NATHAN Robertson, Augusta, Georgia. Col. James Turk, Bellefonte, Ala.- bama. From the National Intelligencer PRESENT CRISIS IN THE CONDITION OF THE AMERICAN INDIANS. NO. XXIII. There are in our country not a few benevolent individuals, who theor etically admit that the Indians have a perfect right to the possession of their country; that we are bound by trea ties to defend this right; and that the forcible seizure and division of their lands would be an act of enormous injustice; who yet suppose, that the continuance of the Cherokees where they now are, would be extremely in convenient to Georgia and to the Uni ted States. These persons are inclined to think, that the inconvenience will be found so great, as to amount to a sort of moral necessity: and that, there fore, the sooner the Cherokees con sent to a removal, the better it will be for them, as well as their white neighbors. An acquaintance with the real state of facts, would convince these benev olent individuals, that they are quite mistaken, in regard to the best man ner of promoting the permanent good of all parties. The inconvenience which appears so formidable, is all imaginary.

The select men of Brunswick have offered a reward of a hundred dollars for the apprehension of the perpetrators; and at a general meeting of the citizens on Monday, a strong abhorrence of the deed was expressed. The beautiful and singular phenomenon which was visible on the evening of the 28th ult. is noticed by all the papers from different parts of the country, and the descriptions given of it agree with the observations made here. The following notices of its appearance and departure are from the N. H. Chronicle. The venerable Dr. Holyoke, of Salem, Mass, aged 100, remembers twice before having witnessed similar phenomena. He thinks they happened more than fifty years ago; but declares the light on Tuesday evening exceeded both of them in brilliancy. "A very brilliant Aurora was visible on Tuesday evening last, and witnessed by many of our citizens— It appeared at first between the hours of 9 and 10, as a cloud of light, lying on the northern horizon, with a slight corona investing its whole border. This arch of light gradually arose above the radiations from the northern horizon, and moved as a separate belt towards the south, till it became nearly vertical in its center, and its terminating pillars rested in the eastern and western points of the horizon.

Such is Spain! The description of the Spanish police at present shows to what a degree the Constitution of Portugal has inspired the Government with terror. Independently of the Manifesto issued by the king for the purpose of giving confidence to the friends of absolute power, a Manifesto, which affords a kind of text here for a thousand conjectures, the severity of the Police is strict in the extreme. In Madrid, no person is admitted into the city unless he bears with him a passport, or bill of protection; and a man coming a distance of six leagues must provide himself with the same. If you want to leave the city, you must apply to a Commissary of Police, who points out on a piece of paper called lazar the gate of the city though it on you are to take from your departure. Neither the labourer nor horseman, he who rides in a carriage, or he who walks on foot, is not obliged to submit his papers for examination at the police office established in the neighborhood of the capital. I saw some poor people sent home from the gate of Buen Retiro, because they had not their Carta de Seguridad. Foreign travelers who were

To the General Assembly of the State of Delaware, Gentlemen of the Senate, and of the House of Representatives, The general prosperity of the United States, as represented in official communications to Congress, during their present session, is a source of great felicitation; and more especially so, as we have risen from a state of great depression, without the aid of adventitious circumstances, but from causes essentially inherent in the country and the government. It must, in fact, be remembered that after the conference broke up, it was generally reported that the state of adverse feeling prevailing against the government in France has been a subject of discussion at Johannesburg, but that no definite measures had been resolved upon. It is also said that Prussia and Austria promised to assist Charles X, in case he should come in contact with his people, with 160,000 men. It is added that, when this was mentioned to the Duke of Wellington, he replied "160,000 men will be a mere drop in a bucket of water." If the Duke ever made this observation we can only say that he entertains a just opinion upon the subject. In case of a foreign invasion, France might, in three months—nay, in three weeks—have at least a million of men in arms. — Men are easily transformed into soldiers where there is no want of good officers. France at this moment possesses in abundance the ablest officers in Europe. The grand Army is merely scattered, not destroyed. People have no need to be alarmed lest a change of Government in France should be med, and the Continental Monarchs dare not attack her. As for England—she is out of the question. London, (Courier,) April 8. The advices from Constantinople are to March 6th. The Divan assembled on the 28th of February, to take into consideration the note of the Ambassadors. The result was a reply not so favorable to peace as was expected. “The Assembly unanimously resolved, that the propositions contained in the Russian ultimatum were of a nature that could not be accepted.” Subsequently, repeated interviews took place between Lord Strangford and the Reis Effendi, which terminated abruptly; and on the 3rd a note was delivered to the English and Austrian Ambassadors, of which the following has been given as the substance: “The Divan witnesses with pleasure the efforts which its friends the Ambassadors of England and the Austrian Internuncio made to adjust the differences which have arisen between the Sublime Porte and the Czar of the Muscovites; but the Divan views, as useless to the proposed object, all further discussion of the proposition contained in the Russian ultimatum—propositions which are incompatible with the sovereignty of his Highness.

Those children are now in bondage, or their dead bodies floating on the waves.—The parents destroyed—the city in ruins. The contrast is awful! The lesson deeply instructive to all free governments! Retain your dignity, your self-respect; cultivate the arts, and honor virtue—and we may bid the tyrants of the world defiance. But lose these preserving and elevating attributes, divide your power, and forget in indolence or interest, the great bonds of principle that hold us together, and we shall one day exhibit the spectacle of modern Greece. Once, like her own Apollo, with the curling lip that mingled the smile of victory with the consciousness of power—in attitude as graceful as it was firm— the glorious beau-ideal of perfection, noble, intellectual, enviable, free. Now, a mutilated, down-trodden image, scathed and blackened by the lightning, defiled in the dust, and trampled on by Barbarians. Charleston Gazette. A young man named Richard David, was lately bitten by a rattlesnake, in Jackson county, Indiana, while in the search of his horse in the woods, on which he intended to proceed a little way to be married. The bite was so fatal, that he appears to have died on the spot. Meanwhile the bride had made the necessary preparations for the union; the marriage party were assembled, and in merry mood, looking out for the approach of the expected bride- groom, when they received the intelligence of his death.

The Legislature of Massachusetts assembled at Boston on Wednesday last. Hon. N. Hill was re-elected President of the Senate, and Paul Willard, Esq. Clerk. The House of Representatives was called to order by Mr. Russell, of Boston. Win. C. Jarvis, Esq. and Pelham W. Warren, Esq., the Speaker and Clerk last year, were re-elected with great unanimity. Edward D. Bangs, Esq. was chosen Secretary of State, and Nahum Mitchell, Esq., was re-elected Treasurer. The whole number of votes for Secretary was 261. Mr. Bangs had 161—Mr. Bradford, 112, and some votes were given for Mr. Bacon and others. The following gentlemen were chosen counsellors to the Governor—Thomas L. Winthrop, Nathan Noyes, Joseph Richardson, Levi Thaxter, James L. House, Sherman Leland, James Fowler, Rodman Hazard, and Braddock Dimmick, Esquires. The Democratic County Committee, in Lincoln, have requested the electors in the several towns and plantations to choose delegates to meet in convention at Myrick's Tavern in Nobleborough, on Tuesday, the 29th of June next, for the purpose of agreeing on a suitable person to be supported as an Elector of President and Vice President, another to represent the District in Congress, four to represent the County in the Senate of this State, and a suitable person for Treasurer of said county.

The Mineral District of Missouri, comprising parts of the Counties of Washington, St. Genevieve, Jefferson, St. Francis, and Madison, extends from the headwaters of the St. Francis, to the Meramec river, a distance of about seventy miles in length, and from the Mississippi - in a Southwesterly direction, a distance of about fifty miles in breadth, and abounds with minerals of various descriptions, but is particularly characterized by the abundance and richness of its Lead Ore. Iron, Manganese, Zinc, Antimony, Arsenic, Plumbago, and other Minerals of minor importance, are said to be found in this District. The Townships above designated, embrace a portion of this district, and include about sixty leases, granted by the Government, for the purpose of procuring Lead Ore, some of which have produced from six to eight hundred thousand pounds of Mineral; and they also include some of the most productive mines in the State, either claimed by individuals, or belonging to the United States; among others, the very rich ones known as “Mine Shibboleth,” “Mine a Martin,” “Gray’s Mines,” “Hollfontaine Mines,” “Valle’s Mines,” and the “Old Mines.” The ore is of the finest quality, and yields from fifty to seventy-five per cent. Labor is abundant and reasonable. Two Shot Factories are established and situations are afforded by the high rock hills on the Mississippi, for erecting others at an expense of about fifteen hundred dollars.

Young Villains.—It will be remembered that there have been a great many stores broken open and robbed in this city, within the last six months, without any detection of the thieves. A few days since the arrest of one of the gang led to the discovery of the whole. The Police once in the track soon found the rogues, and in two days, seven of the ten were safely lodged in prison. They were indicted on Thursday last, and convicted on Wednesday. Another has been taken since, and probably will be indicted and tried at the present sitting of the Court Sessions. They are the same gang that broke open the stores in Front, Pearl, Wall, Fletcher, Pine, and other streets, by getting into the scuttles from the tops of new unfinished buildings. A more desperate and depraved set of boys was never seen. They are all under 15 years of age and it appears that they have entered and robbed twenty-eight stores, here and in Philadelphia.

Never was there a clearer case of right—never a stronger case of conventional obligation—never a case in which the ultimate decision will more certainly receive the hearty reprobation of the future millions of America, if that decision should disregard the cries of the poor, and the entreaties of the oppressed. There should be one loud, general overwhelming note of remonstrance, sounding forth from Georgia to Maine, and repeating admonitions like those: Abstain from robbery—oppress not the poor—perform your vows—respect the rights of all. There should be a vehement and universal outcry, as against a great crime on the eve of being perpetrated, and which can be prevented only by a voice of alarm and decision. If the importance of this subject were seen in its true light, there is no presumption in saying that the friends of the Indians, in all our great cities, and in all the States of the Union, would send a deputation to Washington to see that a proper representation of the views and feelings of the people was made to the executive and legislative branches of the Government. Men of the first respectability.

Where in Rome, the eternal Rome! shrunk down and shriveled beneath the paralysis which papal supremacy inflicts upon the minds and consciences of men. Year after year the Propaganda have endeavored to extend their benumbing influence beyond the 'Bridge of Sighs', and Mis prisons of Lisbon as if Italy, Spain and Portugal, were not enough to be cursed. The last year saw the papal largesses distributed with no unsparing hand in the United States; but are we not to hope that the splendid domes of St. Peter will be appropriated to a more noble purpose than the flattery of the papal Sees? Is the voice of Rome’s ruins never to be heard? - Did Caesar bleed at the foot of Pompey’s statue and bleed in vain, and Brutus’ spirit be a redeeming power for all the world except degraded Rome? Had the temporal power of Rome been less great or her ecclesiastical ambition less aspiring, liberty and order might now prevail where the Bigot whets the knife of persecution and the oppressed points the dagger of the assassin. Greece! the clime in which the schoolboy's imagination first learns to revel—whence are drawn prolific examples of all that is noble and patriotic—Greece!—long hast thou kept the onward path to national honor and prosperity.

The undersigned having obtained letters of administration on the Estate of THOMAS BRADY, deceased, from the Judge of Probate for the county of St Clair, will, on Thursday the 24th of August next, it being the first Thursday of the next term of the Circuit Court for the county aforesaid, at the town of Belleville in the said county, present a petition to the said Circuit Court, for the sale of all the real estate of which said Brady died seized, situate in said county, for the payment of the debts of said estate, his personal estate being insufficient: All persons, therefore, who are interested in said real estate, are requested to come forward at the time and place aforesaid, and show cause, if any they have, why the same should not be sold.

Newspaper excerpt: By arrivals from Liverpool, accounts to July 1 are received, from which we make the following extracts: The dissolution of Parliament was expected to take place on the 14th, but would not be prolonged, in any event, beyond the 19th of July. The funeral of George IV was to take place on the 15th. The Coronation of William IV is to take place in October next. The new King had pardoned thirty-seven convicts in Newgate prison, who were under sentence of death. In the British House of Commons, on the 6th of July, a long and interesting debate occurred on a motion made by Mr. Grant to appoint a Regency. It was finally negatived by a vote of 93 to 247. The proceedings of Parliament appear to be of a local character exclusively. A letter from Constantinople states that the exchange of the treaty of amity and commerce concluded with the United States of North America took place on the 30th of May. A dreadful fire took place at Constantinople on the night of the 30th May, which destroyed nearly 400 houses. France and Algiers: The French papers of Wednesday (July 6th) contain the detailed accounts of the operations of the French army up to the 28th June inclusive, and a telegraphic dispatch from Sidi Feruch, dated the 30th, giving an account of the operations on the 29th.

The Senate and House of Representatives of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania have resolved "that the Tariff of eighteen hundred and twenty-eight accords with the spirit of the constitution of the United States, and that it maintains the true principles of protection to the industry of the country against foreign policy and legislation." Substantially the same opinions have been expressed in resolutions adopted by the respective legislatures of the States of Delaware, Vermont, and Ohio. Copies of these resolutions have been transmitted to me by the Governors of those States respectively, and in conformity to their requests are now communicated for your information. Like sentiments were contained in Resolutions of the two houses of the General Assembly of this State, at its last session. Agreeably to the request therein made, copies thereof were forwarded to the Executives of the states of Georgia, South Carolina, and Virginia, respectively, to be submitted to the several Legislatures of those States, their proceedings and remonstrances declaring the law of Congress, for the protection and encouragement of domestic manufactures, inexpedient and unconstitutional, having been previously laid before the Legislature of this State.

Mr. Peel was called to the bar in order that the observations should be tendered in turn again. Previously to the order of the day being read, Mr. Peel was to be seen leaning on the elbow of the speaker's chair in deep conversation; those who knew the right honorable gentleman's person, will be able to estimate the gratefulness of his attitude. The private conversation between the minister and the speaker might have continued much longer, had not the solicitor-general whispered something in the right honorable gentleman's ear, that induced him to look up to the gallery; we thought the action was a commentary upon a hint, that the members of the press, who were at the moment busied in arranging their note books, had observed the minister's private conference. Mr. Peel withdrew to his seat, and O'Connell was called to the bar. The answer of the member for Clare to the question, whether he would take the oath, was short but emphatic, and uttered in a voice of subdued and solemn intensity. "I swear this oath one assertion of which I know is not true; and I swear it under protest, as a matter of opinion, which I believe is not true, I therefore refuse to take this oath."

The Trustees of the Worcester County Agricultural Society are hereby notified that there will be a Meeting of the Board, on Wednesday, the 18th day of June, current, at the Society’s Room, in Worcester, at 7 o’clock, P. The shape it in general took was the miraculous cure of diseases. When the miracle did not work well, it was always owing to want of faith in the subject. An inspired brother approached the bed of his sister, who had been confined to it a long time, and commanded her in the name of the Lord Jesus, to rise and walk. Her faith was perfect; she arose and walked about her room, and slept better that night than she had done a long time before, and continued well ever since, though thin and emaciated—a circumstance which causes a want of faith in many, who cannot believe that God would work half a miracle but think He would at once remove all traces of the complaint. Other similar miracles have been performed, and some have failed for want of faith. The effect of the imagination in the cure of many diseases is well known. Miss Campbell’s miraculous tongues, understood by no one, can be accounted for; but the real miracle is that men of education—that members of the Scottish Bar, and Ministers of the Scottish Church—should be found among the believers. Their number exceeds two thousand, and it is still increasing.

A day was fixed for his execution, and all Ferrara thronged to see it. The servant appeared on the scaffold, and after protesting that he held no other motive than the cure of his master, he laid his head on the block, and gave the fatal signal.—The executioner, according to previous orders, at that instant poured cold water on his neck, and this was no sooner done, than the color left his cheeks, his eyes sunk into his head, and he died in a few moments without speaking a single word. [from the Cheraw Intelligencer.] From the Editor in New-Jersey. Exemplary Punishment by Women. While at Patterson, a circumstance occurred, which to me was of a most novel and extraordinary nature. I was told however, it was a custom of that place. A man flogged his wife severely. He was arrested and taken before a jury of 21 women. They sentenced him to be whipped until he should appear perfectly penitent, and beg his wife’s pardon on his knees. He refused. Seven cow skins were accordingly provided, and were actively employed by seven of the twenty-one women, on the back of the criminal. He still continued stubborn. Seven others then took the cow skins and lashed him well. Still he remained refractory. The remaining seven, with much energy, then commenced operations; they fleeced him so severely, he was at length compelled to render at discretion.

Whereas a contest exists between Thomas Davis and James Barbour, as to the right to a seat in the House of Delegates. James Barbour agrees with Thomas Davis to scrutinize the polls lately taken. When the votes are agreed to by both parties to be good, they shall be admitted. When it is agreed by both parties that the votes are bad, they shall be excluded. When any doubt is expressed by either, they shall be placed in a column. As these doubts must rise from the Constitution or facts, as to the right of voting, the parties agree to refer the legal questions to Chapman Johnson, Benjamin Watkins Leigh, and Robert Stanard, whose decision or a majority of them the parties agree shall be sovereign between them. Anecdote.—During the late cold weather, one of the gentlemen in Boston, who was engaged in visiting the poor, and supplying their wants, entered one of the cellars in Broad-street, which was occupied by an Irish family. In one corner of the room there was an old straw bed, in which was a little boy, whom the mother was covering with some rags, on the top of which she placed an old door. ‘Mother,’ said the boy, ‘how do poor folks make out this cold weather, who have no doors to lay on their beds?’—Salem Ob. As the soldiers were returning home from the ever memorable campaign of New Orleans, they met with no trifling difficulty in getting across the Tennessee river: one of them, getting entirely out of patience with the Indian ferryman, bestowed upon him abuse accompanied with a volley of oaths rarely, if ever, equaled—which the intrepid man of the forest heard without interrupting him, when he quickly replied, “The same you say to me, I say to you back.” Thus it is with our “bad English.” “butchering". We do not pretend to anything more than Common—of the humble grade—sufficiently honest to tell the truth; which, we hope, will be heard throughout the state. But as we do not intend to take up much time in answer to this brilliant display of these button-making functionaries, (LL. D. and D. A.) we’ll cut the matter short. “Charity urges,” &c. BIG folks; truly able to bestow “charity” in point of language, etc. Great critics. At this, we beg to be excused, for laughing. One circumstance seems to vex them, for which we are sorry; that is, our being "lilth:"—But, as we did not form ourselves, we hope, they, BIG folks, will have still some “charity.” We are compelled to acknowledge one mistake in our statements—that is respecting the watering of the city of Jackson; but as it's a trifling one, we still hope for.

Of this performance it is sufficient to say that the speaker equalled the expectations of his friends; his discourse was classical, ingenious and eloquent;—containing much valuable information, and indicating a liberal and cultivated mind, a fine imagination and sensitive feelings. After a beautiful exordium, which was very naturally a comparison between New Hampshire in the 17th and 19th centuries, Mr. Haven gave a rapid but distinct sketch of the characters of our forefathers, and enumerated the circumstances wherein the "merchant-adventurers of Pascataqua" differed from the Pilgrims of New-Plymouth. He portrayed the character of John Mason, the original projector of New-Hampshire, and rescued him from the obloquy with which the prejudice and injustice of his contemporaries had partially covered him: the puritan characters of our ancestors were next adverted to, and their industry, sublime piety and stern integrity were eloquently delineated. The present character of the people of New Hampshire, as it is formed by their occupations, climate and localities, and as it has been exhibited by her distinguished sons in war, in science and literature, was the fruitful topic of discussion; and the orator claimed and substantiated for his native state a place among the first for patriotism and intelligence, and in proportion to its extent for moral and physical energy.

The Norman chieftains were attended by minstrels and harpers, who composed and sung in rude verse to their harps, the romantic adventures of imaginary heroes, recited the valorous deeds, and eulogized the characters of the ancestors, or praised the achievements of their patrons themselves. The wild and warlike melody roused the soul of the daring and haughty warrior; the fire of triumph gleamed from his eye, and illuminated with martial enthusiasm, he shook the trusty weapon at his side. Their strains gave hilarity to the feast and boldness for the combat. It was not to be expected that much attention would be paid to refinement of diction, when strength and vividness of expression were most desired by the auditors. With rough majestic force they moved the heart, and strength and nature made amends for flaw. New poems, also, of a religious and sacred nature, were composed about this period. The rise of the crusades, whose origin was fanatical, but whose effects were beneficial for literature and manners; the intercourse between the minstrels of England and those of France, together with other causes, produced a fondness for tales of chivalry, and amatory romances; and with them a taste for foreign ornament of language. The character of poetry was greatly improved by their influence. We now come to the consideration of a new era in the annals of English literature.

POETRY. From Parry’s Welsh Melodies. OWAIN GLYNDWR'S WAR SONG. BY MRS HEMANS, Saw ye the blazing star? The ocean's look down on freedom's war, And lift her torch on high! Bright on the dragon-crest It tells that glory's wing shall rest, When warriors meet to die! Let earth’s pale tyrants read despair And vengeance in its flame, Hail ye, my bards! the omen fair Of conquest and of fame, And swell the rushing mountain air, With songs of Glyndwr's name. At the dead hour of night, Mark’d ye how each majestic height Burn’d in its awful beams? Red shone the eternal snows, And all the land, as bright it rose, Was full of glorious dreams! Oh! eagles of the battles rise! The hope of Gwynedd wakes; It is your banner in the skies, Through each dark cloud which breaks, And mantles, with triumphal dyes, Your thousand hills and lakes! A sound is on the breeze, A murmur, as of swelling seas! The Saxon on his way! Lo! spear, and shield, and lance, From Deva's waves, with lightning glance, Reflected to the day! But who the torrent wave compels A conqueror’s chain to bear! Let those who wake the soul that dwells On our free winds, beware! The greenest and the loveliest dells May be the lion's lair!

Resolved, That it is the opinion of this meeting, that the pressure of the times, and the scarcity of money, the unsalableness of produce, afford the strongest reasons for lessening instead of increasing the taxes, and that a few repairs on the old jail will answer every necessary object. Resolved, That in our opinion the proposed jail is altogether too large and expensive, and that there is no necessity for a jail with magnificent rooms for a jailor to inhabit. Resolved, That if the commissioners should persevere against the general wishes of the people in causing the erection of the proposed building, and levying the taxes for the payment thereof, it will be our right and a duty we owe ourselves, to resist by every proper means, measures so oppressive and useless. Resolved, That the farmers who concur with us in opinion are hereby invited to assemble without delay, in their respective townships, for the purpose of adopting such resolutions as they may deem proper on the subject, and to appoint two delegates from their respective townships, to present the same to the Commissioners, at the courthouse in Edwardsville, on the fourth Monday in this month. Resolved, That Jacob Gunterman and Joseph Bartlett, be, and they are hereby appointed delegates on the part of this meeting, to attend at Edwardsville, on the 4th Monday in this month, and present the foregoing resolutions to the Commissioners. The fountains of the sky, And on the expectant thirsty crop Pour down the rich supply: The Farmer, when the seed time's o’er, Joys in the mercies given — Thinks on the promised harvest, And, smiling, looks to Heaven. God of the Sheaf! to thee alone Are due our thanks and praise, When Harvest’s grateful labor's done, On plenty glad we gaze: Then shall our thoughts on Heaven rest, Thy grace we will adore, And that God, whose mercy's blest Our basket and our store. Female Child with two Heads. M. Geoffroy St. Hilaire presented to the Academy of Sciences of Paris, on the 25th May, 1829, a drawing of a monster, which was living at Turin in the beginning of last May, and was two months and a half old. The individual represented is a girl with two heads. The lower parts alone are common to the two individuals: the rest is separated, and presents the conformation common to the normal state. The Priest, seeing in this creature two distinct individuals, baptized each of them separately: one received the name of Ritta, the other that of Christiana. They were born at Sassari in Sardinia, in the beginning of March. The father has the intention of carrying them to Milan, whence he is going to Geneva. There have been examples of such monsters living to a pretty advanced age. In the reign of James VI.

To conclude—on March 13th, about a month after our disaster, we spoke with the Guilford, Captain Johnson from China, homeward bound. Cap. Johnson, with the greatest humanity, relieved our wants, as far as he was able. He lent us two of his sailors, gave us new sails, also a chronometer, sextant, etc. wine and provisions; and after experiencing some rough weather off the Western Isles, to our great joy, we arrived at Gravesend on April 17th 1828. My readers will perhaps conclude, that the owners of the Morning Star have handsomely rewarded the poor sailors and soldiers, not only for their extraordinary labor, but also for the loss of their grog and their scanty supply of bad food. To the first mate (Mr. Busby) and Mr. Smith, they were indeed indebted for the safe return of their vessel; but it will be learnt, with no little surprise and disgust, that so far from this being the case, Mr. Smith, has not even received their thanks. The soldiers received 1s. 6d. each as a compensation for the loss of their grog, and the sailors, also, had some trifle. (The cabin passengers, however, subscribed, and presented Mr. Smith with a handsome snuff box, in token of their esteem for his meritorious conduct and services.)

The late events in Portugal have excited among the Spanish people immense enthusiasm; and there appears to be a general disposition to extricate themselves from the misery of their own unhappy condition by removal within the territories of Portugal. This, however, is strictly prohibited by the beloved Ferdinand. GREECE. The accounts from this country, though contradictory, are something more favorable. The aid of Lord Cochrane, whose immediate arrival at the Archipelago was looked for, is expected to effect much; Athens, Corinth, and other citadels, are said to be in a complete state of defense; and the brave Miaulis lately gained some naval victories. TURKEY. The insurrectionary disposition of the Janissaries is not yet finally extinguished; but little doubt is entertained of the final success of the government. The resolution and activity of the Sultan are exerted with great effect. From the New York Enquirer, Sept. 4. GREAT BRITAIN. Distress. — We do not perceive that the papers contain anything of great importance. They are chiefly filled with disastrous accounts of the state of the manufacturing districts. The facts of suffering and want of employment cannot be concealed, but it is almost impossible to form any opinion from the newspapers as to the permanence of these distresses.

Soon after Mr. I proposed to the Earl to make a private adventure of it, in which he (Mr. L-) offered to be concerned with Kid one fifth in vessels and outfits, and moreover, become surety for Kid's faithful execution of his trust. On a communication of this new arrangement to the King, he very readily gave his sanction to it, and aided its popularity by taking himself one tenth of the joint fund, which was now agreed to extend as far as the gross sum of 524,840, to which (with others) Lord Horners, the Earl of Rumney, Sir Edmund Harrison, the Duke of Shrewsbury, and the Earl of Oxford, Belmont, L-and Kid were subscribers, the whole being under the direction of Belmont. Kid sailed from Plymouth for New York, in April of 1696. How long he cruised on the American coast in execution of his commission is not known. Mr. L- was, however, the only one of the concern in America, until the arrival of the Earl two years afterwards, that is in April, 1697. Kid, in the meanwhile, went to the Indian Ocean, and establishing himself somewhere in the Island of Madagascar, lay like a shark in those remote seas, pillaging and plundering with impunity the commerce of all nations, at his pleasure.

We admired above all the sublime and religious emotion, the decency and harmony which reigned in this sacred place when the Rulers brought forth from the Tabernacle the Five Books of Moses (Pentateuch) richly ornamented and surmounted with Crowns of Silver, and walked in procession seven times round the Interior of the Temple, stopping each time at the Altar; after the seventh one, the President of the Community with a distinct and audible voice and with a characteristic warmth of love for our august Sovereign Frederick the 6th, elevated his hands towards the Omnipotent, addressing to Him the Prayer of the Hebrew Community for the prosperity of the King, the Royal Family, the Governor General of the Danish Islands, the Governor and other Magistrates of St. Thomas; the idea of reciting the same in a language (English) more generally known than Hebrew, in which it is ordinarily read on Saturdays and holidays ordained by the Laws of Israel, is worthy of applause. The august ceremony of the Consecration was terminated by Offerings which were abundant. — EXTRACT FROM THE N. V. PATRIOT. These Indians are our brethren, made of the same blood, possessed of the same inherent rights, as keenly alive as ourselves to their infringement; and because we have acquired some rights which they have not, shall we madly assume the additional right of depriving them of those they do possess, to gratify either our pride, ambition, or avarice? Mercy, Christianity, the principles of reciprocal justice, all equally forbid it. Now that they have just begun to taste of the sweets of civilization, and many of them of the "honey from the Rock" of our "common salvation" who can avoid weeping at the prospect of seeing the "cup dashed from their lips," and they driven to feed again among the wild herds of the forest? We cannot, however, but anticipate better things, even though we thus speak, and hope that the fair inheritance with which God has blessed us may never be wrested from us by the hand of violence, as a punishment for our violence towards the original proprietors of our soil. Our most ardent prayer is, that neither the president of these United States, the congress, nor any state government, will assume the awful responsibility of forcibly ejecting any one, Indian or white man, or Indian tribes, from his or their rightful possessions. The Indians; a new Theory.

Mr. Smith of Md., the bill making further appropriations for the military service of the United States for the year 1822, was again taken up for consideration. The amendments of the committee on the whole were concurred in by a general vote, subject to specific exceptions that were named. Mr. Cooke proposed to disagree to the appropriation of $50,000 for Fort Calhoun; and he stated a variety of considerations in support of the proposition. The question of agreeing to the report of the committee of the whole was then put and carried—ayes 69. Mr. Smith, of Md., proposed to add to the appropriations for the Indian Department, for the pay of sub-agents, the sum of 9,538 dollars. Vance said, the sum proposed was not sufficient to pay for so many sub-agents as the law provided for, and he moved the sum of 11,338 dollars. The appropriation was agreed to. Mr. Smith, of Md., moved to amend the appropriation for the contingencies of the Indian Department, by striking out $40,000 and inserting in lieu thereof $75,000. Mr. Vance proposed to make this disbursement of contingencies as specific as we can. He therefore moved the sum of $14,992 for interpreters.

During divine service at Lea Church, near Gainsborough, on Sunday week, a servant entered with hurried step and melancholy air, and whispered in the ear of Squire S., who immediately left the church, accompanied by the servant. Great was the sensation, for the congregation apprehended some dire calamity. In a short time, Squire S. returned to his pew, "when," says our authority, "to such a pitch had the excitement reached, that even the Rev. Sir Charles Anderson himself, who was the officiating minister, could not refrain from inquiring, from the pulpit, what calamity had happened. Everyone was on his legs, the tear of pity glistening in each eye, every bosom felt a sympathetic throb, every ear was open, the most breathless and deathlike silence prevailed—while Mr. S. replied, that "his dog had hurt one of his feet!" The Liquor of Life. When the art of distilling spirits, generally attributed to Raymond Lully, was discovered, the secret of longevity was supposed to have been brought to light, the mercurious volatilis to be at length fixed, and the pernicious product received the name of aqua vita—the liquor of life. A discovery concerning which, says a learned physician, it would be difficult to determine whether it has tended most to diminish the happiness, or shorten the duration of life.

How often do we see the people of a place compelled, for years, to pay exorbitant prices for the goods they purchase, or to be at the expense of obtaining them from a distance, because, if anyone is adventurous enough to enter into competition, he is driven from the undertaking, by the established capitalist putting his goods for a time at a price which must prove ruinous to a beginner. Yet who will say, it would not be for the interest of that people to patronize and sustain the person who would continue to supply them at fair prices, till he should be able to enter into successful competition with his more powerful neighbor. MISCELLANEOUS ITEMS Brown University. - At an adjourned meeting of the Corporation of this institution, on Wednesday last, the Rev. The editors of the Quarterly Review mention that they saw a Manuscript speech which Mr. Wortley Montague (or rather the husband of Lady Mary) delivered in the House of Commons in which there were certain notable hints for the delivery, arranged along the margin, such as— "pause for a minute" — "cough" — "look round" — "slow" — "loud" etc.— What an idea some men must have of oratory... OF LANGUAGES. The following rules have been given by critics, in reference to the languages of Europe. If you would address the Deity, make use of Greek or Latin, for their antiquity, purity, and majesty—if to kings, speak in Spanish; it is slow and grave—if to men, Italian; to women, French; to dogs, Welsh; but if you would affright an enemy, or the devil himself, make use of High Dutch. A RICH LEGACY. An old Italian, on his deathbed, left little to his widow except a fine horse and a favorite cat; desiring, however, that the horse might be sold, and the price employed in masses for his soul.

At Harper's Ferry, on the Island in the Potomac, opposite the village—The officers of the United States Armory, Gen. George Rust, the Superintendent, Major William Broadus, Paymaster, Capt. John H. Hill, of the Rifle establishment, Major Benjamin Moore, the Master Armorer, and also Major James S. Henderson, formerly a representative in Congress from this district, were invited to attend; however, only the two first were able to comply. The Declaration of Independence was read by Major William Broadus, who is a revolutionary officer, with great feeling, accuracy and judgment. Major Broadus likewise acted as President of the day, assisted by Hon. George Rust and Wm. Anderson, and Philip Huffman as Vice President, assisted by Col. Henry Ward and Noah S. Braden. The armory at Harper's Ferry: by fostering it, the government will obey an injunction of the father of his country, “in peace prepare for war.”

We hesitated to publish the following communication, inasmuch as we wished to avoid any allusion that might be considered personal, while we were treated with common decency by the editor of the Journal; but a thought has occurred to us, that the respect so repeatedly mentioned, being at one time a reptile crawling on the face of the earth, and at another time a “winged seraph,” may with some degree of aptness, admit of general political application to that party of trimmers, yclept the “Great New-England Adams party,” who are sometimes one thing, sometimes another, as best suits the seasons, the signs of the times, and their convenience. We therefore give it a place, though we admit the Bat would be a better symbol. "Masks off"—Flap, flap, go the wings of the “Butterfly,” tribe, because forsooth, the “pew county of Sullivan” have had a Democratic Caucus. Don’t be alarmed, Gentlemen Butterflies; with the blustering, dapper little fellow for a leader, you have nothing to fear. Republicans will not interfere with any policy you may have the vanity or hardihood to pursue. Hold as many Caucuses, and pass as many Resolutions as you please, you will not be disturbed. To the honor of the Democratic party, be it said, they never yet were known to attend a federal Caucus. “O my little hero, and tell the public, that, * there is no such thing as principle in politics.”

The laborious nature of such an undertaking may easily be conceived, when it is known that the tops of the houses are generally from four to six feet thick at the apex of the cone. Heat tie relates having seen one instance in which the crown or roof of the hut was more than eight feet in thickness. The door or hole leading into the beaver-hut is always on the side farthest from the land, and is near the foundation of the house, or at a considerable depth under water. This is the only opening into the hut. The large houses are sometimes found to have projections of the main building thrown out, the better to support the roof, and this circumstance led to all the stories of the different chambers or apartments in beaver huts. But these larger edifices, so far from having several apartments, are either double or treble houses, each part having no communication with the other, except by water. — Upwards of twelve such dwellings have been seen under one roof, and, excepting two or three of them, the whole of the remainder had no communication unless by water, each having its own door into the dam, which is doubtless well known to the inmates, who may have comparatively little intercourse with each other.

The fifth possessor of the Ciarvali estate, who derived his Christian name from the surname of his mother's family, the Stratford-Knolls of Aldinovolt; his eldest son died in his father's lifetime leaving an only son the Right Honorable George, who, though representative of the elder branch of the Galvail family was missed by in the will of his grandfather who left the estate to his second son Paul Canning, whose only son George Canning was created Baron Ciarval in 1818. The premier, however, succeeded to a small estate in the county of Kilkenny, worth about £200 per annum, either by bequest, or in consequence of his father's bankruptcy. Mr. Canning was born at Fullham near London, in 1770. His paternal ancestors were only seated at Foxcote in Warwickshire, where a branch of the family was settled in the reign of Henry VIII, by Thomas Canning, a younger son of the Foxcote family, who thereupon assumed the name of Stratford.

Now, if we suppose that at the commencement of the Millennium the inhabitants of the earth will amount to only one thousand million, a little more than exist at present, and double only once in fifty years, (which is much less than the present increase of our own country,) then at the end of 1,000 years, we shall have more than ten millions of people for every square mile of land on the earth. But if they should double once in a hundred years, (which is probably less than any civilized nation has done) then at the end of a thousand years we shall have more than ten thousand inhabitants for every square mile. Query: where are all the inhabitants to dwell at the end of Dr. Nott’s 365,000 years if they only increase as fast as they do at present in our own country? As the opinion that the duration of the Millennium will be 365,000 years, is not, so far as we know, very prevalent among Theologians, its defense may be properly relinquished to those who embrace it. To us it appears inconsistent with the many passages in Scripture which speak of the Judgment day as near at hand, and also which represent the general course of the world as evil and wicked: to say nothing of the difficulties it involves in respect to population.

OLD LETTERS I know of nothing more calculated to bring back the neatly faded dreams of youth—the al- most obliterated scenes and passions of our boy- hood—and to recall the brightest and best asso- ciations of those days— When the young blood ran riot in the veins, And hot blood made us sanguine— nothing that more easily conjures up the alter- nate joys and sorrows of maturer years—the fluctuating visions that have floated before the restless imagination in times gone by, and the breathing forms and inanimate objects that wound themselves around our hearts, and be- came almost necessary to our existence, than the perusal of old letters. They are the memo- rials of attachment—the records of affection— the speaking-trumpets through which those whom we esteem hail us from afar. They seem hallowed by the brother's grasp, the sister’s kiss, the father's blessing, and the mother’s love.

“I have now only time to give you a brief sketch of a lelancl nly occurrence which took place on Friday last about 3 o'clock; A few minutes after I had been seated in a front room, (with a couple of strangers who had called for shelter during a light shower of rain) a sharp flash of lightning appeared, accompanied with loud thunder, though apparently at a distance. In two minutes after, a general tremendous blaze appeared—lo every one whose eyes were towards the street, balls of liquid fire were seen falling on the earth, which for a moment destroyed the power of vision. The explosion, which was apparently at the same time with the flash, raised me quite erect. Fearing the consequence, I ran to a room in which my wife lay sick; on opening the door my eyes were blinded with smoke and with a strong sulphurous vapor. Seeing no fire I supposed the store was struck, and that the powder had exploded. When I got near the door I was met by one crying “yonder! yonder!” pointing towards the East end of the North porch. I then turned my course and ran with my eyes elevated, expecting every step to meet with a column of flame, until I almost trod upon the lifeless body of my son William.”
