The wool bill, (this measure so important to the great interest of our country) was, in effect, rejected by the casting vote of Mr. Calhoun, the Vice President. 2. A committee appointed by the majority of the Senate made a long report on the subject of the Georgian controversy, of which three thousand copies were ordered to be printed for distribution among the people of the United States, countenancing and encouraging the Governor of Georgia. 3. The bill for converting a portion of the six per cent debt into a debt bearing an interest of five per cent, which passed the House of Representatives was lost in the Senate. By the failure to pass a bill last year, in the consequence of the now acknowledged error of Mr. Louis McLane, the country lost about ninety-thousand dollars. By the failure of the Senate to pass this bill, it loses a like sum. 4. The Colonial bill was lost by the refusal of the Senate to agree to an amendment of the House of Representatives, without which the measure would have humiliated the country. And now Great Britain is left in possession of the monopoly of the navigation concerned in the intercourse, unless the President feels himself authorized to close our ports under the act of March, 1823.

The Constitution of the United States authorizes the State Legislature to direct the mode in which the electors shall be chosen, but it has ever been considered as against the spirit and true meaning of that instrument for the Legislature to usurp into their own hands that power. It is the highest prerogative of the people. It never ought to be exercised except by the whole people in their sovereign capacity. Such is the prevailing opinion throughout the whole Union, except in South-Carolina and the little State of Delaware, the only States in which the servants of the people choose the electors. But we are not ignorant that the federalists do intend to choose the electors by the Legislature, should they succeed in turning out the Revolutionary Patriot Gov. Pierce, and placing a majority of "Adams men" in the Legislature. Before the people, they dare not avow their real design, because if they did, the people would indignantly frown upon them and their candidates. With the boldest effrontery they then turn round, and accuse the republican party of secretly harboring the black design of cheating the people out of their dearest rights. But it so happens that republicans, who are the great body of the people, are very careful to give their support to those only who are their friends and will maintain their principles. The suggestion therefore that a republican Legislature would choose the electors, has no foundation in truth.

The request was immediately complied with by Mr. Miller, who gave up all the copies within his reach, and the papers were destroyed. On the next day, however, the letter appeared in print, and Hunter at once accused Miller with duplicity, and challenged him. Mr. Miller asserted that the publication was made without his knowledge, and refused to accept the challenge, alleging the insufficiency of the provocation. Upon this, Hunter posted him in handbills, declaring at the same time that he held himself ready to meet Mr. Miller. Thus goaded, Miller accepted the challenge, and the fatal meeting took place. The acts of Assembly are express in their provisions against dueling. For accepting or sending a challenge, there is prescribed a penalty of $500, one year’s imprisonment at hard labor, and a forfeiture of rights of citizenship for seven years. For carrying a challenge, or acting as a second, the same fine and imprisonment, and incapacity of ever holding an office of honor or trust in the commonwealth. For publishing handbills abusing a person for not fighting, the same punishment as if the duel had taken place. All these laws have been openly violated: the challenges have been published and acknowledged by the parties; publications instigating to duels have been widely disseminated, yet no officer interfered, — none of the offenders were indicted and held to bail. And shall they yet escape?

Letters from Bucharest state that, at a council of war held at Karassu, it was resolved to employ all means to penetrate to the Turkish empire as soon as possible, in consequence of the serious preparations making by the Turks. The Imperial Guards received orders to proceed by forced marches to join the army of Prince Wittgenstein. The same letters say news has been received from Adrianople of the arrival of Hussein Pacha at Schumla. The Silikdnr of the Grand Vizier has been sent with Hussein Pacha to watch over his conduct; for the Porte, though it has great expectations from his talents, entertains some suspicions of him, and the command would have been given to another, but for some political considerations. — The reinforcements which arrived by the way of Adrianople at the beginning of June, on their way to Schumla are of little importance, consisting at the most of 60 cannon, 500 artillery men, 800 cavalry, and 400 infantry; the camp can hardly amount to 10,000 men. The Grand Vizier was expected at Adrianople, where apartments are prepared for him. It is to be observed that about 20 surgeons, most of them German, had arrived in that city to enter into the service of the Turks. Letters from Vienna, of the 17th ult. state that the Russians have effected a landing near Varna.

An honest man, remarkable for the singularity and doggedness of his opinion, had been several times on a jury, and always entertained notions of law and justice totally at variance with the judge and his brother jurors. One of the judges asked him how it was possible that he always gave so much trouble to the court. He replied with the utmost gravity, “I don’t know how it is, but it always happens to be my misfortune to be on a jury with eleven obstinate men. Smart Repartee. Some person whom Quin had offended, met him one day in the street, and stopped him. “Mr. Quinn,” said he, “I—l—understand, sir, you have been taking away my name!” “What have I said, sir?” “You—you—you called me a scoundrel, sir!” “Keep your name,” replied Quin, and walked on. “I have no more doubt that I have killed a hundred men, than if I had taken a gun and shot them!” This was said by a person who formerly bought and sold spirituous liquors, and with reference to that traffic. “THE LIBERTY OF THE PRESS IS ESSENTIAL TO THE SECURITY OF FREEDOM.” WORCESTER, WEDNESDAY, DECEMBER 15, 1830. From the New England Weekly Review. Beautiful Extract.

‘This should deserve in arms be crown’d.’ A sound, stern print after mentioning the late duel with muskets and buckshot, at 33 paces, between Col. Gibson and Mr. Ross, in Mississippi, adds “we are happy to learn, that both were wounded, and hope that the peppering they received will do them good.” Last week a charcoal fire had been kindled in a tight cabin of a vessel lying at Pratt’s ferry, near Hartford. The captain and a boy slept there. The boy first discovered the noxious effects of the coal, and waked the captain who could but just succeed in getting the cabin door open and the boy out. He then fell back on the furnace and was much burnt before he was revived by the fresh air. The worm that gnaws and never dies. — We have an instance of the power of an accusing conscience, in the case of Silas D. Reed, who was arrested in Charleston, S. C. several months since, and conveyed to the jail in Raleigh, N. C. charged with having forged certain papers, for the purpose of receiving from the War Department the pensions allowed to individuals by government. While in prison, he was very desponding, and abstained from food for fifteen days, which occasioned his death. He died on the 3rd inst. The Brazilian Slave Trade.

As the ground was originally cursed on account of sin, so when man shall possess a character resembling that which he has lost, why should it be deemed improbable that the curse shall in any part be restrained? If this view of the subject be correct, it is easy to see how terrible must be the state of the world, when the thousand years being fulfilled, "Satan shall be loosed a little season." The evil passions of men being again predominant, this immense population will become like a raging sea. The curse upon the ground returning in its full force, famine will urge the nations on to deeds of untold violence, in order to possess each other's territory and means of subsistence. A population so dense and withal so desperate, will be fit instruments for the designs of ambition, and the malice of her persecutors. Satan will "go out to deceive the nations which are in the four corners of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them to battle, the number of whom is as the sands of the sea." IMPORTANT MESSAGE. The following Message from the President of the United States, received yesterday, was read - To the Senate of the United States. Recent information of the multiplied outrages and depredations which have been committed on our seamen and commerce by the Pirates in the West Indies and Gulf of Mexico, exemplified by the death of a very meritorious officer, seems to call for some prompt and decisive measures on the part of the government. All the public vessels adapted to that service, which can be spared from other indispensable duties, are already employed in it; but, from the knowledge which has been acquired of the places from whence these outlaws issue, and to which they escape from danger, it appears that it will require a particular kind of force, capable of pursuing them into the shallow waters to which they retire, effectively to suppress them. I submit to the consideration of Congress the propriety of organizing such a force for that important object. JAMES MONROE. Washington, December 6, 1822. Sundry petitions were read and much other business though not of general interest, transacted in the Senate. Astonishing Performance.

A Frankfort paper states that in the canton of Zurich, in Switzerland, a young woman pretended that Bonaparte had appeared to her, and inspired her with the resolution to die, to save several thousands of souls. The sacrifice was immediately resolved upon. Men, women, and young girls prepared instruments for the execution, listened to the young woman to a board, and, amid her cries of joy, drove nails into her hands and feet, tore her breast, dashed her heart to pieces with a mallet, and sang pious hymns to celebrate her death. Six of the perpetrators were in custody. A sister of this woman had also perished in the most cruel tortures, inflicted by the hands of her brother-in-law, who boasted of having committed this horrid atrocity in fulfillment of the will of God!  Telegraphy—The method of communicating intelligence by signals has been brought to great perfection in France. News can be conveyed from the northern extremity of the kingdom to the southern, a distance of 600 miles, in 23 minutes. A new line of telegraphs is to be erected from the Pyrenees, to intersect the old line from Toulon to Paris. A novel project.—Capt.

The Indians inhabiting the countries •Lewis and Clark relate an instance which fell under their observation, of one beaver being caught in two traps belonging to different owners, it having one paw in each. The proprietors of the traps were engaged in a contest for the beaver, when the above-named distinguished travellers arrived, and settled the dispute between them by an equitable arrangement. Watered by the tributaries of the Missouri and Mississippi, take the beavers principally by trapping, and are generally supplied with steel-traps by the traders, who do not sell, but lend or hire them, in order to keep the Indians dependent upon themselves, and also to lay claim to the furs which they may procure. The name of the trader being stamped on the trap, it is equal to a certificate of enlistment, and indicates, when an Indian carries his furs to another trading establishment, "that the individual wishes to avoid the payment of his debts. The business of trapping requires great experience and caution, the senses of the beaver are very keen, and enable him to detect the recent presence of the hunter by the slightest traces. It is necessary that the hands should be washed clean before the trap is handled and baited, and that every precaution should be employed to elude the vigilance of the animal.

Take a Newspaper!—A gentleman came into our office a few days since, and subscribed for our paper, saying that he had not been in the habit of taking a newspaper, but had accidentally a few days previous, taken up one, and on looking over the list of letters advertised in it, found one for himself. He had taken it from the office and found the contents relating to a certain farm of his, some distance from here, which, in consequence of some legal proceedings against it, would have been past recovery, had the letter remained but a few days longer in the post office. As it is, it will cost 60 or 70 dollars more to retain his property than it would, had he obtained his letter when first advertised.—Shekels Harb. Adv. He removed from here to go into the post office department, then at Philadelphia, In the year 1789—and has continued in the faithful discharge of his duties in that department, through all the vicissitudes of party, and under every successive administration, from that of Washington to Jackson—and would still have continued, if unyielding integrity and a prompt and diligent discharge of duty were any guarantee for such continuance. But the “besom of reform” has been put in requisition—and he among others must be swept from office. We should be glad to have our aged friends greet him again as a fellow townsman; we have understood however, that if he removed from Washington, he would probably take up his residence in New Haven. We have not yet learned whether it is the intention of the President, or Mr. Barry, or Duff Green, to remove Dr. Phinehas Bradley, the other assistant postmaster general. Probably they will wait until they can witness the effect of removing his brother. The successor of Mr. Bradley we believe is, saving his Jacksonism, a clever sort of man enough—but we detest the principle of removing every honest man because he doesn’t 'throw up his greasy cap’ and hurrah for the powers that be.

The Book of Jasher, it seems, has been preserved by the Jews in the East, and some few copies were printed in Poland twenty years ago. It is written in that plain beautiful style that will sufficiently testify its great antiquity, and which is the chief cause of my publishing it, with the Hebrew text attached to it: and however much I venerate the sacred Scripture, and however infinite I consider the distance between this Book and the inspired Volume which we possess, I am still bold to declare that its language is equally beautiful, and throughout one hundred and sixty pages it keeps up the same chaste, elegant, and historical style as that much admired part of Scripture - the History of Joseph. It commences with the creation of man, containing very copious accounts of Jewish records, not at all mentioned in the Scripture, and reaches as far as Joshua. The two places in Scripture wherein the Book of Jasher is mentioned, are beautifully cleared up throughout this Book, particularly that in 2 Samuel, chap 1, v. xviii. "Also, he bade them to teach the children of Judah the use of the bow, behold it is written in the Book of Jasher." It also elucidates many other parts of Scripture, and will set right some of the most perplexing parts of chronology.

Mr. Wright treated the scurrilous note of Mrs. as every honorable and moral man should treat such contemptible notes—by declaring it to be “of a character that requires no answer.” The combined opposition do indeed carry on business with a high hand; they make use of the most abusive epithets when speaking of the administration and its friends; but if the most distant allusion is made against their republican purity, their “honor” is insulted, and the insult must be washed away with blood! “A guilty conscience needs no accuser.” The friends of theatrical amusements, and consequently the friends of morality, correct taste, and decency, will be gratified to learn that an improvement has recently been introduced into one of the principal theatres in New York, the cornerstone of which was laid with great pomp by His Hon. Philip Hone, late Mayor of the city, and chief guardian of the laws and morals of the place. This improvement consists in the introduction of certain French actresses, who, discarding the formula of modern prudish manners, have made their debut before an American audience in the full character of French opera dancers! Whatever may be said about cloaks of hypocrisy, there is nothing of the kind used about these “frail sisters,” there is no concealment suffered to prey upon their charms—no disguise—all is candor and frankness—naked truth itself—exhibiting all the unadorned simplicity of a Grecian statue!

Lift A Day Eli; and the people of all religious denominations are requested to abstain from all labor and recreation inconsistent with the religious solemnities of the day, and to assemble in their respective places of public worship, and unitedly to supplicate the favor of their Supreme Ruler: that he would in mercy forgive them their sins and errors, and not requite them according to their merits, but according to his own parental kindness; that he would bless them in their various personal and political interests; that he would prosper the industry of the husbandman, and cause the earth to yield a plentiful increase; that our fisheries may continue to be prosecuted with success; that a gainful trade may reward the enterprise of those who are engaged in mercantile pursuits; that he would bless our colleges, academies and schools, and make them instruments of diffusing useful learning and pure morality; that the ministers of religion may instil into the minds of their people the love of order and peace, and teach them by their own example, that a difference in unessential speculative doctrines should make no difference in the bonds of Christian charity; that he would bless the governments of this State and the United States; and that they may be administered with wisdom and moderation, and preserve the peace of the country and harmony of the union.

Defeat he was never called to endure: but in the crisis of difficulty and danger he displayed untiring patience, and fortitude not to be overcome. Such was the great and accomplished Captain whose loss the Army has now, in common with their fellow citizens of all classes, to deplore. While indulging in the kindly impulses of nature, and yielding the tribute of a tear upon his grave, let it not be permitted to close upon his bright example, as it must upon his mortal remains. Let him be more nobly sepulchered in the hearts of his fellow soldiers, and his imperishable monument be found in their endeavors to imitate his virtues. "To think of summer yet to come, That I am not to see, To think a weed is yet to bloom, From dusty earth. It shall be." The next morning she found penciled on the back of the same card: "To think when heaven and earth flee, And time's sand streams o'er, When all I thought to be shall be, That I must die no more. Ah! where will then my portion be? How shall I spend eternity? A decisive answer - The Rev. Armstrong of Yorkshire, England, once engaged in a dispute which terminated in a challenge. "Name your place, time and form!" said his opponent, to which he received a prompt reply: "The graveyard, Thursday, 8 o'clock, P.M. - the sexton of the parish." It is said that Austria has been invited by Russia to take temporary possession of Bosnia and Serbia, for which purpose a levy of 100,000 is spoken of. According to French papers received this morning, there was a general talk at Paris of a Congress, which is to be held at Laybach. Its object is said to be the regulation of the existing interests concerned in the Turkish war, as well as the consequences likely to result from the treaty of the 6th July. It is affirmed that all the powers will send their Ambassadors. What necessity has Russia to send one? Is not her army sufficient? The Nuremberg Correspondent of the 22nd inst. says it was reported at Vienna that an alliance had been entered into by England, France, and Austria, to prevent the threatened aggrandizement of Russia. Accounts from Bucharest of the 1st of July announce that the Russians had begun to bombard Silistria, to which place the Turkish garrison of Braila had been permitted to retire. The garrison, which consisted originally of 12,000 men, had sustained considerable losses both in killed and wounded. The Grand Duke Michael arrived at headquarters on the 5th July and appeared with the Order of St. George of the 2nd class, given to him by the Emperor for the conquest of Braila. A German paper states that 100,000 Russians are advancing to force the passage of the Balkan mountains.

He pretended to be reconciled, altered his behavior and on holidays invited her to walk with him into the fields for pleasure and recreation. One summer evening, after a very hot day, he carried her to cool and repose herself on the borders of a spring in a place very shady and solitary. He pretended to be very thirsty. The clearness of the water tempted him to drink. He laid himself down all along upon his belly and swilled large draughts of it, highly commending the sweetness of the water, urging her to refresh herself in like manner. She believed him and followed his example. As soon as he saw her in that posture, he threw himself upon her and plunged her head into the water, in order to drown her. She struggled hard for her life, but could not have prevailed but for the resistance of a dog who used to follow and was fond of her and never left her; he immediately flew at the husband and seized him by the throat, and made him let go his hold, and saved the life of his mistress. A Scotchman and an Irishman falling in company as they were traveling, continued together about two days journey. The former being quite bald-headed, the latter frequently diverted himself by jesting him on that account.

"FOREIGN". Liverpool Papers of April 8, have been received. Mr. Canning, appointed Monday, April 14, for communicating to Parliament the documents respecting the mediation of England to preserve peace between France and Spain. tanner*. April 4.—Sun office, 2 o'clock, P.M. Evening Edition.—We stated in a Second Edition of the Sun of Wednesday last, that the French Declaration of War had been received by our Government. Yesterday was the day appointed for its discussion, with our Foreign Minister and French Ambassador; and we can state from authority that his Excellency and Mr. Canning were closeted for two hours at Gloucester Lodge yesterday, on this important subject. We can also state that the French Government are exceedingly alarmed lest privateering should be allowed by the British Government, and by the probability that our seamen will volunteer in great numbers into that part of the Spanish service. These measures we are all assured, the French Government is most anxious to prevent; and urgent representations have been made on the subject to the British Cabinet. The same paper states, as the last unofficial news from Paris, that in consequence of intelligence from the frontiers of Spain, it had been resolved in the French Cabinet, to make an increase to the army of from 40,000 to 60,000 men.

In respect to the last objection, its probable effect upon the dignity and independence of the State Governments, it appears to me only necessary to state the case as it is, and as it would be if the measure proposed were adopted, to show that the operation is most likely to be the very reverse of that which the objection supposes. In the one case, the State would receive its quota of the national revenue for domestic use, upon a fixed principle, as a matter of right, and from a fund to the creation of which it had itself contributed its fair proportion. Surely there could be nothing derogatory in that. As matters now stand, the States themselves, in their sovereign character, are not infrequently petitioners at the bar of the Federal Legislature for such allowances out of the national treasury as it may comport with their pleasure or sense of duty to bestow upon them. It cannot require argument to prove which of the two courses is most compatible with the efficiency or respectability of the State Governments. But all these are matters of discussion and dispassionate consideration. That the desired adjustment would be attended with difficulty, affords no reason why it should not be attempted.

The apprehension thus betrayed in rejecting Col. Barbour's propositions is confirmed by the proposition which Capt. Davis makes to that gentleman. Col. Barbour is required to resign his seat—to resign the Sheriff a certificate—to resign the vantage ground which these circumstances give him, and what is he tendered in return? Why that which the law gives every candidate—the privilege of contesting the seat of a member returned! This is really a tremendous attempt at compromise. We mean nothing disrespectful to Capt. Davis—we do not mean to say that he is capable of taking a seat which he knew the People had not assigned him—but insensibly, involuntarily, the scenes at Washington in the contested elections between Washburn and the Jackson competitor from Maine, and Newton and Loyall, in which right was almost confessedly sacrificed to might, may have influenced his imagination. Hence our allusion above. A Virginia Assembly has never tarnished its legislative honour; never done collectively, that which its individual members would disdain to do singly; and hence in our opinion, the precedents furnished last winter at Washington will have no influence this winter at Richmond.

It is also known, and I believe has been before stated, that while Gov. Pope was in Kentucky, he wrote to Sevier, then at Washington, to get him an order from the State Department, calling him (Pope) socially to the seat of government. Sevier applied to the President for the invitation requested by Pope. The President remarked, that if Gov. P. could leave the “Plantation” conveniently, and had business at Washington, that he might consult his own pleasure; that for his part, he had no business with him, &c. The Governor waited in Kentucky for Col. Sevier’s answer, and when he found that answer unfavorable, and learnt that government would not pay his expenses, he determined to visit Washington under any circumstances. Now the question arises, as Governor Pope did not visit Washington officially, as Governor of Arkansas, what did Mr. Pope do, which was so beneficial to the Territory? Why, he appeared before one or two Committees, and Col. An extensive window glass manufacture, which supplies the market, and exports to a considerable amount; five very extensive taverns, with accommodations equal to any of a similar nature; three banks, and a branch of the United States bank; a Fire Insurance Company, nine Printing Offices, two daily papers, two tri-weekly, and two weekly. A steamboat runs regularly to Aquia Creek, one to Alexandria, and another to Norfolk to the city. Five stages depart daily to Baltimore, and several others to other places. The seat of government was removed here in the year 1800, during the presidency of John Adams. The city was incorporated by an act of Congress, passed on the third of May, 1802, by which act the appointment of the Mayor was vested in the President, yearly; and the two branches of the Council elected by the people, in a general ticket. By a supplementary act, passed May 4, 1812, the corporation was made to consist of Mayor, a Board of Aldermen, and a Board of Common Council; the Board of Aldermen to consist of eight members, elected for two years, two to be residents of, and chosen from, each ward; the Board of Common Council to consist of twelve, three from each ward; and the Mayor, by the joint ballot of the two boards, to serve for one year.

A Judicious Tariff." ( From the Harrisburg Argus. 'General Jackson's Letter in reply to the enquiries of Governor Ray, of Indiana, made under a resolution of the senate of that State. We have received a copy of another letter from Gen. Jackson, on the subject of the Tariff, dated Feb. 28th. Where it has been for near two months we cannot say, but it fully confirms our opinion. He intends to adhere closely to the Southern policy. His present letter amounts to nothing more than a declaration that his "opinions at present, are precisely what they were in 1833—when they were communicated by letter to Dr. Coleman," at the time referring us to that letter. Gov. Ray's enquiries of Gen. Jackson are plain and unequivocal."

A privateer under Spanish colors had appeared off Guayaquil, and taken and plundered a Dutch ship and turned the crew on shore at Cape Blanco, entirely destitute—with some other small captures and damages; but it is believed she has returned to some port at the south. Reports are in circulation that others have been seen on the coast. Also, an brig Orono, Gorden, 26 days from Tabasco. On the 14th June, being about 8 leagues E. of Tabasco, the Orono was chased by a piratical schooner, but outsailed her—next morning hove in sight, she boarded a Spanish brig, and next evening boarded the Catherine, —took out all the crew, confined them in the hold of the piratical boat—robbed her of all her provisions, clothes, watches, &c. of seamen—stove the cabin to pieces—took all her spare rigging, and were about unbending the sails, when a small sail hove in sight, which deterred them. The Captain swore that he would have the Orono, if he cruised after her a month, and would cut the crew to pieces. The Orono escaped the barbarian by entering the port in the night. Two days after, the pirate appeared off the port, and was taken near the shore by an armed brig, the Captain and crew, however, escaped to their rendezvous, which was at St. Peter and Paul, where they have a droger.

It is probable that the partisans of Wilkes had promulgated by insinuations the charges made by Junius, and therefore it became desirable to have them categorically stated that he might answer in like manner. Junius, - in reply, modifies his direct charge of Horne’s supporting the ministerial candidates, to his neglect of rendering aid to Mr. Wilkes, which was notorious, and expected by Wilkes himself, three lists of candidates being canvassed for that election. “To exclude Wilkes (says Junius) it was not necessary you should solicit votes for his opponents. We incline the balance as effectually by lessening the weight in the one scale, as by increasing it in the other.” By a strict examination of the whole correspondence of Junius and Horne, it will appear evident that the object of it was to clear the character of Mr. H. from the aspersions thrown out against it, and to exhibit Wilkes to the public in his true colors, as an unprincipled demagogue; and it is very apparent that but one hand had any concern in the transaction. The open rupture between Horne and Wilkes took place in January 1771, although Mr. Horne accused his adversary of having made anonymous attacks upon him previously. The election for charter officers for the city of London occurred on the latter part of March following, and terminated in favor of the Wilkites.

On Saturday last an old she bear and three cubs were seen, about a mile from here, on one tree, picking chestnuts, by a boy who was out on the Annie errand. The bears would stand on the large branches, bend in, or break the small ones with their paws, and pick the chestnuts from the burs with their teeth. As soon as they saw the boy, Mrs. Bruin alighted from the tree, and took French leave; while the young Bruins, nothing abashed, continued picking nuts. The boy having neither arms or ammunition, came home to obtain both, as well as some assistance in killing the bears. But before his return they had made their escape—however, the old bear and two of the cubs were killed on Monday morning.

Besides many minor considerations, there are two prominent views of the subject which have made a deep impression upon my mind, which, I think, are well entitled to your serious attention, and will, I hope, be maturely weighed by the people. From the official communication submitted to you, it appears that, if no adverse and unforeseen contingency happens in our foreign relations, and no unusual diversion be made of the funds set apart for the payment of the national debt, we may look with confidence to its entire extinguishment in the short period of four years. The extent to which this pleasing anticipation is dependent upon the policy which may be pursued in relation to measures of the character of the one now under consideration, must be obvious to all, and equally so, that the events of the present Session are well calculated to awaken public solicitude upon the subject. The convention consisting of delegates from 39 counties, and from the towns of Norfolk, Williamsburg, Petersburg, Richmond, Fredericksburg, and Lynchburg, met at Charlottesville on the 14th inst. to take into consideration the subject of Internal Improvement of the state. The convention was called to order by Mr. Hugh Nelson. On motion of Mr. Nelson, Mr. Madison was called to the chair, to preside in the deliberations of this body. On the 15th, a committee was appointed to report to the convention such measures as they might think ought to be pursued in order to effect that improvement, and the course of this convention ought to adopt in order to ensure success to those measures. On the 16th, the committee made a report in the form of a memorial, addressed to the speakers.

Under this brightening prospect of better times which has recently burst upon our vision, under the hopeful auguries of the future, which we may thus derive from the past, let us have philosophy enough, yet to bear and forbear; Let us remember that if we cannot feel patience under oppression, it behooves us, as we love our country, yet to check our impatience, so as to do nothing which might hereafter be the subject of regret. Let us try further argument, further remonstrance, I had almost said entreaty. And is it, can it be, too sanguine a hope to be indulged, that if the one party shall practice moderation, which will bear with their brethren, though several times offended, and the other shall remember that there is a point at which forbearance ceases to be a virtue, we may yet have a happy issue out of all our afflictions? I trust that this will be the result, and that the union of these states will be as durable as the everlasting hills. ON THE LIFE AND CHARACTER OF CLEOPATRA. by MADAME DE STAËL HOLSTEIN. Cleopatra, Queen of Egypt, was the daughter of Ptolemy XII. (Auletes.) By her father's will, she became, at the age of seventeen, heiress to the throne, con jointly with her brother Ptolemy XIII. to whom, according to the Egyptian custom, she was to be married. Being older than her brother, she thought herself entitled to wield the sceptre alone but the young king, instigated by his courtiers, attempt ed to exclude Cleopatra from the throne; and the princess was under the necessity of retiring to Syria, where she levied an army to march against her brother. About this period Pompey was assassinated by order of Ptolemy; and Caesar, though he had little cause to regret being deliver ed from so powerful an adversary, conceived the deepest hatred and contempt towards the Egyptian prince. Caesar pos sessed virtues and passions, which fre quently carried him away even from the views of his own interest; and he succeeded in his enterprises rather through genius than calculation. Ptolemy Auletes had ap pointed the Roman people tutor to his children."

The Colonization Society has been arraigned before the legislature of their country, as incendiaries and cut-throats? I am one of them; I bless God that he permitted me to live at a period when an institution, existing for nothing less than his glory, by doing good to fallen man, is in existence, and will pursue its objects until my life shall end, or my opinions of their tendency be changed; equally unmoved by the sneers of the scoffer and the invectives of the gainsayer. But to the testimony—Let us briefly review the history of colonization, compare its effects induced by colonization upon the mother country and the colony. Tyre, the chief city of Phoenicia, was among the first nations of which we read, as having been remarkable for sending out colonies. Phoenicia itself was but a narrow strip of land on the borders of the Mediterranean. Yet it attained a degree of wealth, of refinement and of power, which fell to the lot of few of the nations contemporary with her. An inspired writer tells us that "her merchants were princes and her traffickers the honourable of the earth." It is universally admitted that "her commerce which raised Tyre to such a pitch of greatness is universally admitted, and that her colonies contributed to the extension of her commerce, I suppose no thinking man will deny. Accounts from Lisbon to the 6th of August state, that some disturbances have taken place in the Alentejo, and at Lyra, between the Constitutionalists and the partisans of Don Miguel; at the latter place they were of a serious nature, the Constitutionalists having lost, in a rencontre with the troops, thirty wounded and four killed. The new Ministry in France had produced an extraordinary feeling, and roused the greater part of the journals, both in the metropolis and in the provinces, to the bitterest hostility. Some of the accounts from Paris consider the whole affair of the change of the Ministry as resulting from religion alone without any influence of foreign politics but the general impression in France among the liberals is, that the English Ministry had something to do with the affair, probably from its desire to have a Ministry in France which will go more readily along with it in favoring the usurpation of Don Miguel, and also one that would harmonize with it respecting Russia, and be more ready to engage in a silly attempt to uphold the integrity of Turkey. Under date of London, 24th August, it is mentioned that the state of the weather still continued to excite apprehensions respecting the safety of the crops, and to prop up or raise the prices.

I have been informed that at the time Cortez conquered the city, it contained two hundred thousand inhabitants, and at the commencement of the revolution, one hundred and seventy thousand; at this time the population is calculated at one hundred & twenty thousand, two-thirds of which are Indians, the most ignorant, superstitious and indolent people I have ever seen—they have been priest-ridden, until they are neither fit for soldiers, sailors, or citizens. I have frequently visited the old palace of Montezuma, and have been in the room which he filled with gold to purchase peace with the Spaniards. I have likewise seen the large stone that Montezuma and his people worshipped—this stone is made to resemble the sun, and is about eight feet in diameter, of solid stone, on which is carved the figure of all the animals inhabiting this country—it is now put in front of the Cathedral as an ancient ornament. At this time I inhabit a part of the Viceroy's palace: it is a splendid building—it is calculated to quarter twenty thousand dragoons; the lower story is all stables and granaries—the second, quarters for soldiers—the third, officers of the government.

In order that the farmer may be convinced that the pomace should remain some time before it is pressed, let him mash a few apples and force out the juice immediately. He will see that it is nearly as colorless. Let him again mash a few quite fine, and let them in that state be exposed to the air for twenty-four hours, and pressing out the juice he will find it a rich cider color. If the experiment is carried further by putting the juices into bottles, and permitting them to undergo the usual fermentation, he will be still more convinced of the impropriety of pressing out the juice immediately after the apples are ground. Many farmers in the United States get from four to ten dollars per barrel, but the majority do not get two. The latter should buy one or two barrels of their neighbors, and keep it to treat their friends. They should continue this practice until they are able to make such as will bring at least three dollars per barrel. SUGAR CANE IN NORTH CAROLINA. Newbern, (N. C.) November 1. Sugar Cane.—We have seen a specimen of the Sugar Cane raised in the neighborhood of this town, which proves, we think, beyond a doubt, that this valuable article may become one of the staple products of the State. The Cane we saw green on the plantation of George Pollock, Esq.

African Females.—I take this opportunity (says Lander, in his Record of Clapperton's Ml Expedition) of expressing my high admiration of the amiable conduct of the African females towards me; in sickness and in health, in prosperity and in adversity, their kindness and affection were ever the same. They have danced and sung with me in health, grieved with me in sorrow, and shed tears of compassion on the recital of my misfortunes. When quite a boy, and suffering from fever in the jungle, women of the same race used to take me in their arms, or on their knees, sing and weep over me, and tell me not to die, for that my mother would break her heart to hear the news; and pointing to the ocean, they cheered my spirits by saying that it laved the shores of England, and would shortly bear me on its bosom to my distant home. In fine, through whatever region I have wandered, whether slave or free, I have invariably found a chord of tenderness and trembling pity to vibrate in the breast of an African woman; a spirit ever alive to soothe my sorrows and my afflictions;—and I never in my life knew one of them to bestow on me a single unpleasant look or angry word.

I I^H Cochrane has taken his leave of the officers of the Navy of Chile, from which place he has retired for a time, in a letter addressed to them on the 10th January. The following is an extract from his letter:—“ Gentlemen—By our united exertions, the naval power of the enemy in these seas, though superior to our own, has been annihilated, and the commerce of the Pa- cific is everywhere carried on under the protection of the Independent Flag. To me it is highly gratifying to render, that those services have not been sullied by any act of illegality or impropriety on our part; and that while you have asserted the rights of Chile, and maintained and confirmed her independence, you have so conducted yourselves as uniformly to preserve the strictest harmony and good fellowship with the officers of the ships of war of all neutral states." The Admiral gives no reason for his present retirement. The affairs of Chile are in a disturbed state. A strong opposition has been organized by General Freire, Gov. of the Province of Concepcion to the government of the Director, O'Higgins. The constitution is declared to be tyrannical, and it is the object of Gen. Freire to establish a new one: he says his only purpose is to set Chile at liberty.

Early in the morning, Le was awakened by some one in his room, when on looking around, he recognized the person of Pat O'Hannon, but taking him for one of the servants of the house, he paid no attention to his movements. When summoned to breakfast, the sailor boy, to his utter astonishment, discovered that his doubloons had been stolen, and suspecting Pat O’Hannon to be the thief, he went in pursuit of him, but could find no traces of his tracks, until he reached Hodges’ Franklin House. The sailor told so plain a story, and was with all, so interesting in his appearance and manners that he gained belief, and the persons present volunteered their services to assist him in regaining his money from the grasp of the Irish depredator. To accomplish this, it was proposed that the stage agent or barkeeper should consent to receive payment for Pat’s passage to Boston, when an opportunity would be offered to discover if he had stolen the sailor boy’s property. Mr. O'Hannon was accordingly informed that the Steamboat was near at hand, and that if he wished to take a seat in the coach, bound for Boston, he could do so, by paying his passage money.

Baltimore, Feb. 20. A Fortunate Rescue—We understand that a German lad, about ten or eleven years of age, broke through the ice about midway in Jones’ Falls, at the foot of Horse Market Square, yesterday afternoon, between four and five o’clock, and must have drowned, but for the noble exertions of Lieut. Adams, of the United States Navy, who happened very opportunely to be passing at the time. Several efforts had been made by the spectators to rescue the youth from his perilous situation, all of which had failed.

What is fair and politic for the owner of a manu factory who derives his profits from the labors of some two or three hundred people, to whom he gives work and subsistence, is surely as fair and politic for the farmer who lives in a great portion of our country by the sweat of his brow, and that of his immediate family. The policy which protects the farmer is, indeed more beneficent, because its effects are more equally distributed amongst those on whom it operates, and because it contributes to the prosperity of that species of occupation which most conduces to the healthiness of the body and to the soundness of the private and the public morals. If the account is to be relied upon, and the Courier seems to give it credit, the affairs of the eastern borders of Europe may soon assume an interesting aspect. At present in his Asian dominions, the Grand Seignior will find himself placed in a situation of extreme difficulty and peril between Persia and Russia. The latter, we have supposed abundantly able to crush him, if once seriously engaged in the undertaking; but we had expected that the object of Alexander's policy would lie within the boundary of Europe. A war in Asia may have a much more extensive effect - it may crumble the whole fabric of the Ottoman empire into ruins. A SONG, INTENDED TO HAVE BEEN SUNG BY THE AUTHOR. YE firm sons of freedom, who visit these boards, To commemorate the birth day of heaven's chosen nation; Like brothers enjoy what its bounty affords, Leave care and blue-devils to next generation. For we have here met, To laugh, not to fret, And for this time at least, all our troubles forget; For this is the day, that all parties combine, To boast of their freedom, and swallow their wine. Old Europe may quarrel, and keep in a pother, Submit like poltroons to the holy alliance; And tell us their story; but we’ll tell another Which is—to their systems, we bid e’m de fiance— To the Poles, we’d soon brush a Whole legion of Prussia, France, Austria, Sweden, Spain, Denmark or Russia, Should they in a phalanx, their forces combine To infringe on our freedom, or swallow our wine.

The first charter was granted by Charles the second, one hundred and sixty-three years ago, and embraced all that part of North America which lies between 29 and 36 1-2 degrees of north latitude; that is, a tract of country more than five hundred English miles broad, extending from the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific. It granted the territory, "together with all ports, harbours, bays, rivers, soil, lands, woods, lakes, and other rights and privileges therein named." So far as appears, the charter said nothing of the native inhabitants. Whether it said anything in regard to them, or not, is immaterial to the case now in hand: for, as I have already observed, no man will undertake to maintain the proposition, that the unknown tribes and natives between the Atlantic and the Mississippi, and thence westward to Mexico and the Pacific, could have their rights and property justly taken from them by the signature of the British King, in his Palace of Whitehall. The rights derived from this charter were surrendered to the British crown in the year 1729.

The poor fellow, seeing his countrymen seated in a row, and fastened in their chairs, began to apprehend, he hardly knew what—for he bore a protection, as a friend of liberty, signed by Bolivar himself, and granted to him, because of the great sacrifices he had made in the cause, not of Spain, but of the colonies.  “Bring another chair!” said the General.  Another chair was brought; and the protection which had been offered to the General, by the poor man, was returned to him with these words—“Very well—keep it; you may find it of great use to you by and by. Gentlemen, this man will just make up the twelve.” The poor man locked his hands together as well as he could—the fingers would but just touch—and prayed the General to spare him for the sake of his family : but the General, whose beloved wife had been captured a short time before by the royal troops, and put to death, because he would not exchange a pri- soner for her, made no other reply than— “Prepare!”  Again the muzzles were presented.  “The twelve apostles, gentlemen!—a pleas- ant voyage to them—fire!”  A general discharge followed, and not a man of the twelve was ever seen to breathe or stir afterwards. A Sad Misfortune.—A farmer from — County, Va. a few weeks since, embarked from the mountains for the low country with a cargo of whiskey, intending to furnish the good people of the eastern shore with their annual quantum sufficit of this universally approved Panacea, and fill his pockets with cash, into the bargain, by the speculation. He "wagoned it" many a weary day—found no sale, or but a very poor one, at best, for his cargo, and finally returned much disheartened at his ill success. He complained sadly that he could do nothing, for he had fallen, he said, every where he went, upon a string of these Temperance So cieties.—Vis. if Tel. By J. Holliman—The National and State Administration—They merit the approbation of the democratic party, as long as they are conducted upon democratic principles. By J. R. Lattimore—The democratic party of old Northampton—May they at all future elections, stand by their old landmarks—regular nominations. By Charles Ray—Bolivar, the Hero of the South—His actions proclaim him a republican in principle. By David D. Wagner—Gen. Samuel McKean, Secretary of State—A firm, consistent and unwavering republican; an honest, capable and virtuous officer. By Samuel Taylor—Hon. John McLean, late Postmaster General, and now one of the Judges of the Supreme Court—His talents and principles, united with indefatigable industry, fit him for any station in the government. By the first Easton Troop of Cavalry, Captain James M. Porter, president, Lieutenant Abraham Millar, vice president, William G. Ihrie, Esq. reader of the Declaration of Independence. The President of the United States—His station more honorable than that of the throned monarch—A freeman chosen by freemen. The Governor of Pennsylvania—Our townsman and commander-in-chief—May his administration be as prosperous as his motives are honest. By Harrisville—Andrew Jackson—He has proved his powers as a statesman equal to his prowess in the field: His veto on the "Maysville road" is more important to his country—more durable in its consequences, and more honorable to himself than the victory of New Orleans.

In Portugal, an unavailing attempt was made to force upon the people a system of reform in opposition to its wishes, though for its own benefit. But the talents of a Pombal, with the most unlimited authority ever enjoyed by a minister in Europe, the power that was able to crush the Jesuits and disable the opposition of an inveterate nobility, were unable to carry into healthy operation one lasting improvement in agriculture, commerce, finances, the army, or the Government. The opinions, the usages of the nation were unyielding; though death was the penalty of one look of dissent, of one word of disobedience, the clamor of discontent was not stilled; though at the command of the minister the vineyards ceased to be purple with the grape, yet the valleys yielded not their increase nor did the hills grow yellow with the golden corn. For Spain likewise, this was the eventful period. The everlasting hills formed no barrier to the onward march of liberal principles. The sacred fire, without diminution of its intenseness, was multiplied and spread, till in every valley and on every hill in the most bigoted country of Europe, many a peasant bosom was cherishing the germs of that liberty and loyalty which were afterwards to give the Guerilla bands power to offer the first check to the career of universal usurpation.

One opinion on the conduct of the Brazilian government to our citizens. By the polite attention of Mr. Elisha M. Sink, late first officer of the schooner Beauty of Baltimore, who came passenger in the Virginia, the editors are indebted for the following full report of the state of affairs in the La Plata. Mr. S. left the city of Buenos Aires on the 9th February, and Montevideo on the 10th of the same month. He states that on the 15th, Admiral Brown had an engagement with the Brazilian squadron in the Uruguay, in which he captured six sail and sunk two. The rest, about eleven in number, escaped up the river. Brown's loss was one small vessel. It is certain, however, that the Brazilian vessels cannot long hold out, as they have but little to eat, and can derive no supplies from the shores which are occupied by Patriot soldiery. All hope of supply from the main squadron is cut off, as Brown has strongly fortified the Island of Martin Garcia, and has placed his vessels across the channel of the river. A few days previous, he had captured a small Brazilian schooner containing gunpowder and 700 Patagonians.

Civil, military, and naval officers of the U. States, civil and militia officers of this State, and citizens of the town and vicinity, are respectfully invited to join in the procession. By order of the Committee. GEORGE SPARHAWK, Sec'y. ORDER OF THE DAY. A procession will be formed at Jefferson-Hall, at 12 past 10 o’clock, A. M. which will move under escort of the company of Rockingham Guards, through Congress Street, Middle Road, Jaffrey and Pleasant Streets to the Universal Meeting-House, in the following order:— Military Escort. Marshal. Committee of Arrangements. Selectmen and Secretary of the Board of Selectmen. Orator of the Day. Chaplain, Reader of the Declaration of Independence. Reverend Clergy. Judges of the United States and State Courts. Present and past Members of Congress. Members of the State Legislature. Civil Officers of the State and of the U. States. Foreign Consuls and Commercial Agents. Officers of the U. States Army and Navy. Officers of the Militia. Strangers and Visitors from the adjacent towns. Municipal Officers of the Town. Citizens. Order of Performance at the Meeting-House. Music. Prayer. Ode. Declaration of Independence. Ode. Oration. Ode. Benediction. The Meeting-House will be opened at 10 o’clock, A. M. for the admission of Ladies, for whom the wall pews will be reserved. No gentlemen will be admitted until the procession enters the Meeting-House. After the services, the procession will again form and proceed.

A telegraphic despatch, sent by the Prefect of the North, from Lille this day, October 12, announces that the Belgians have refused to admit into their territory the various detachments of volunteers who are endeavoring to effect an entry. Their gendarmerie and the Urban Guard are stationed on the frontiers for that purpose. A letter from Auxerre states, that on the 10th of this month between 100 and 150 persons assembled at the market-place, and carried off all the corn that they found there, paying for it at a rate below the price fixed by the merchants, who were compelled to submit to the violence. The same mob afterwards proceeded to the house of a corn merchant, under the pretext that he was a forestaller, and forced him to sell his corn at a price equally below the current rate. The National Guard immediately took up arms, and its presence has since prevented any disorders. SPAIN. Bayonne, October 6. General Mina visited Colonel Valdez yesterday morning. This interview, if I can believe information which appears to me to be authentic, was of the most violent description. It is stated that Valdez expressed to Mina his displeasure, that he, being at the head of 3,000 men who wished to enter Portugal at the time of the usurpation of Don Miguel, had preserved a neutrality.

Though he found it necessary at one period of his administration to recall Mr. Monroe from his ministry at the "terrible republic," for misunderstanding or misusing the powers with which he was clothed, it only proved that the individual who had been entrusted with the concerns of his country, at a place where even his best feelings in favor of the rights of man led him astray, was not the most suitable character to meet events and circumstances that took place subsequent to his appointment. It cannot be denied that this recent disclosure must produce very considerable mortification to the distinguished personages, who had for some time previous courted concealment by quibble and equivocation, even if they can escape the deeper shame and condemnation of direct and deliberate evasion of truth. The advice of General Jackson to Mr. Monroe, relative to selecting his cabinet counsellors, and conducting his administration generally, though not precisely in totidem verbis, the same as reported by Mr. Lowrie, is yet proved to be substantially so, —and had it been regarded by him with a magnanimity becoming the chief magistrate of a free people, might have rendered him a more useful as well as a wiser President. The other remarks as to what the General might have himself done, in the plenitude of power, are equally unfortunate in affording materials for an estimate of his civil or moral character.

The Spanish government has never been ignorant that the institutions spontaneously adopted by Spain would excite jealousy of several of the cabinets of Europe, and that they would be the object of deliberation at the congress at Verona. But, firm in its principles, and resolved at every sacrifice to defend its present political system and the national independence, it has tranquilly awaited the result of this contest. Spain is governed by a constitution promulgated, accepted, and sworn to in 1812, and recognized by all the powers who assembled themselves in congress at Verona. Perfectly loyal counsellors prevented his Catholic Majesty, Ferdinand VII, from swearing, on his return to Spain, to this fundamental code, which the whole nation desired, and which was destroyed by force, without any remonstrance on the part of the powers who had recognized it; but an experience of six years, and the general will, engaged his majesty in 1820 to conform to the views of Spaniards. It was not a military insurrection that established this new order of things at the commencement of the year 1820. The courageous men who so decidedly declared themselves in the Isle de Leon, and successively in other provinces, were only the organs of general opinion and of the desires of the whole nation.

House of Delegates. Tuesday, Jan 12.— The House of Delegates received a communication from the Senate, stating their agreement in the resolution for proceeding on that day in the election of a member of the Board of Public Works, in place of George Newton, Esq. A variety of bills were received from the Committees and laid upon the table. On Mr. Anderson's motion, the Committee of Schools, &c. were instructed to enquire into the expediency of amending the act making provision for opening a Road from Union's Court House to the Sweet Springs, passed in Jan. 1839, so that the powers conferred on the Commissioners may be exercised by the Survivors. On Mr. Parrott's motion, the Committee of Roads and Internal Navigation were instructed to inquire into the propriety of amending the act to raise by lottery a sum of money for the purpose of opening a road from Muldrough in Tyler county to the Pennsylvania line, passed Feb.

The King of Spain has promoted his Ambassador at Paris, who was unwilling to mount the French National Cockade. France is said to declare that she seeks not to retain her African conquests! A large restraining force is kept up at Paris. Water has been thrown on the threatening fire, but it is not extinguished. The London Morning Herald, says—“This happy result is the joint fruit of the devotion and vigor of the National Guard, and the personal firmness of the King. The crisis was, by all accounts, one which required all the energies of the friends of order and the existing state of things. For several days—indeed from the day that it became generally known that the Chambers, the Ministry, and the King had declared themselves friendly to a mitigation of the ex-ministers—a feeling had begun to develop itself amongst the populace at Paris, strongly depreciatory of the contemplated indulgence. This feeling found its first vent through the Revolutionary Journals; but at length it became the cry and rallying word of the mob of the capital.

Rockingham County. At a meeting of Republican Members of the Legislature and gentlemen from several towns in the county not represented by republicans, Dec. 19, 1820, at the State House, Thomas Robinson, Esq. was appointed Chairman, and Gen. Joseph Low, Secretary. Resolved, That it is inexpedient to call a County Convention for the purpose of nominating County officers, and that it is expedient to nominate County officers at this place before the adjournment of the Legislature. Adjourned to Dec. 26th. Dec. 20, 1820. Met according to adjournment. Proceeded to ballot for a candidate for Counsellor for the county of Rockingham, and Hon. John Bell, junior, was unanimously chosen. Proceeded to ballot for a candidate for County Treasurer, and William Pickering, Esq. was chosen. Proceeded to ballot for a candidate for County Register, and Seth Walker Esq. was chosen. STRAFFORD COUNTY. At a meeting of the Representatives of the County of Strafford, hidden at Concord Dec. 19, 1820; Hon. Nehemiah Eastman, was called to the Chair, and Henning M. Bean, Esq. elected Secretary. Voted, that a Convention of Delegates from the several towns in the county of Strafford be holden at Dover on the first Wednesday, being the seventh day of February, 1821, at six o’clock, P.M. consisting of delegates in the ratio of one to each representative, and town.

A minute or particular description of the general devastation among the shipping, stores, wharves, lumber, &c. on both rivers, is not easily given. "On the south shore of the North River, for a mile or more in length, we had presented to our view, firewood, timber, boards, shingles, plank, staves, barrels, hogsheads, canoes, boats, &c. the property of a great number of persons, so promiscuously thrown together as not to be easily distinguished by the respective owners. About 50 cords of wood, and 15 or 1600 bushels of floor sand, were carried off Mr. Barr’s wharf, at the North Bridge. A schooner of considerable burthen, having broke her fast, was thrown so high as successively to beat against the edge of the Bridge, and it was with difficulty she was kept off the top of it. The Bridge itself was considerably damaged. Another schooner, just below it, broke her fasts, drove away from the wharf onto a beach, and at length quite over it on the grass; a third schooner, not far from this last, and near 80 tons burden, late from the West Indies, was driven up in much the same manner, and now lies so high as that her keel is considerably above common high-water mark. On the P n I? 16 side of the town, being the principal seat of business, the confusion and destruction was much greater than that above related.

For those, who are laboring, and planning, for a new candidate and a new administration, do not disguise their hostility to this wise system. Nothing, since the establishment of this government, has come before the people, more interesting and more important to them. Who then will, beyond doubt, support John Quincy Ad ams for the next Presidency? All New England will answer in the affirmative. To these States may be safely added New Jersey, Delaware, Ohio, Indiana, Missouri and Louisiana. Here are twelve States. At an extraordinary meeting held in Mexico on the 19th March, 1823, Congress having recognized and approved the proceedings and acts of Casa Mata, the causes of my remaining for years in the vicinity of the capital have ceased to exist; and as my only view was to sustain and protect that body, the division heretofore existing on my account is therefore at an end. I accepted the crown with great reluctance, and only acquiesced through a desire to serve my country. But from the moment I perceived that to retain it would serve as an excuse or pretext for an intestine war, I determined to abdicate it. I did not conclude on this course before, because there was not a body to represent the nation generally recognized; and I considered that any step taken by me, unless there was, would have been both useless and injurious to the country; there now exists one, and I accordingly absolutely abdicate. My presence in this country would always be a pretext for disturbances, and projects of which I have never thought might be attributed to me. In order therefore to avoid the most remote suspicion, I will leave this country and direct my steps to a foreign one. Ten or fifteen days will be sufficient to regulate my domestic affairs and take the necessary measures to conduct my family and myself away.

Harrisburg Convention. - Speaking of the contemplated Convention of Delegates from the Manufacturing States, to be held at Harrisburg, Penn, the Democratic Press holds the following language. “Some persons have affected to entertain fears of the possible mischievous application of the power which this concentration of popular opinion will give. That it will create power is certain, and that all such power is liable to be abused is equally certain. But this is in fact the old question of republicanism against despotism. If the people cannot be trusted with the use of their power, the principle of self-government is a fallacy. So long as the apparent object of popular associations is good, it is only the rankest spirit of tyranny that would wish to prevent them from exercising the power. It is made a question in the United States whether any branches of business need be cherished by the legislature. Surely it behooves those who feel an interest in that question to take the most effectual mode of ascertaining the extent of that exigency.

THE president’s VETO. Mr. Governeur delivered the following message in writing from the President (with the bill for the preservation and repair of the Cumberland road,) which was read as follows: To the House of Representatives. Having duly considered the bill entitled "An act for the preservation and repair of the Cumberland road," it is with deep regret, approving as I do the policy, that I am compelled to object to its passage, and to return the bill to the House of Representatives, in which it originated, under a conviction that Congress do not possess the power, under the constitution, to pass such a law. A power to establish turnpikes with gates and tolls, and to enforce the collection of the tolls by penalties, implies a power to adopt and execute a complete internal improvement.

The unfinished business of yesterday was then resumed, and the bill for the trial of land claims in sundry States and Territories was taken up, the motion of Mr. Johnston of Louisiana, to strike out the 6th line of the 6th section, and insert “French and Spanish Claims,” the effect of which would be to exclude other claims than French and Spanish from the adjudication contemplated by the bill, still pending. Messrs. Smith of S. Carolina, and Johnston of Louisiana, and Berrien, addressed the Senate, when the motion was agreed to, by the following vote: Yeas 31, Nays 9. Mr. Chandler moved to amend the bill by adding a clause to the last section, making the decision of the Commissioners final as to the claimants, and directing that each claimant, previous to the adjudication, sign an agreement to abide by the decision, and making it a bar to any further investigation in a court of justice, or otherwise; which was agreed to. Mr. Eaton offered an amendment, making it necessary that the person claiming lands should have resided in Louisiana or Florida at the time the lands were granted. The motion was opposed at great length by Messrs. Kane, Reed, Johnston of Louisiana, Berrien, and Smith of South Carolina, and supported by Messrs. Eaton, and Macon; when it was adopted by the following vote— Yeas 22, Nays 20.

Mina has a perfect knowledge of the whole country, and all the passes of the mountains, and whenever it is necessary he disperses his people, appoints a particular spot at some distance off the to meet at; during the former invasion of Spain by the French he practiced this maneuver frequently, and with great success; his corps was by no means numerous, but he was not anxious to increase its numbers—he is not vain, and says fairly, but, that he can manage a select few, better than a larger number. When a youth wishes to enlist in the cavalry, after being examined by Mina and questioned, he calls for the commanding officer of the infantry, and says, "This boy wishes to serve in the cavalry, take him first with you, and let me know how he behaves. The first action they are engaged in, this captain who commands the infantry, and is on horseback, keeps the boy close to him during the whole action, and watches narrowly.

From chill November till blushing April, a morning’s walk for health and exercise, for the enjoyment of a bracing and keen air, may with satisfaction be enjoyed; but it is in the summer, when the dews of night sparkle like diamonds on the grass, and melt away at the beams of the rising sun, when the feathered songsters escaping from their beds of moss, carol their matin hymn; when the freshness of the morn and the fragrance of the meadow flowers, the honeysuckle and bluebells, the pinks and magnolias steal o’er the scenes; when even a solitary ramble through a populous city presents novelty, creates interest, and imparts health and cheerfulness to the early riser; while pallid indolence continues to doze and stretch his lazy limbs o’er the heated surface of a feather bed, and with eyes yet scaled by the heavy sleep of night and mouth extended with many a frightful yawn, he kills the brightest hours of his life. A few mornings ago, when “jocund day stood trembling on the misty mountain’s top,” I sallied forth to see the rising world of our city. 'Twas just dawn—the streets were silent. I first entered the subterranean vaults of the City Hall and Watch House, just at the moment when the sleepy sentinels of the night were about being dismissed from duty, and after roll call, had deposited their thick caps and clubs, and prepared for their daily labor.

Our author, having read this description of the phenomenon of the Euripus, pronounced inexplicable, applied to it the theory of the motion of the seas by the melting of ice, and resolved the problem without further difficulty. He examined a chart of the Archipelago, made by the learned Lasville, and there found that the mound which hemmed in the Euripus, separated it also from the extensive marshes of Thessaly; that these marshes were more or less inundated by the melting of the ice of the high mountains of the Great Olympus, at the base of which they are situated. The summits of these mountains, formed of everlasting snows, melted in part, according to the various aspects of the sun. These intermittent meltings of the snows produce the waters at the mouth of those caverns which are disgorged into the Euripus. This explication is undoubtedly more reasonable than the one cited by Spon, as given by a Jesuit, who traced their cause in the influence of the mountains, but who frankly acknowledged that he knew nothing of the matter. As for the rock pierced with seven or eight enormous openings, they are, without doubt, the consequence of the currents which dug them out at a period when the rock had not yet become hard. We see frequent examples of this in the mountains of Switzerland.

The grand Duke who, as is well known, had conferred upon the Jews the full enjoyment of civil and political rights, called him to a seat in the electoral college. A distinction by which the Prince in a special manner evinced his wish to reward Rothschild for the many favors which he had communicated to his fellow-citizens in times of distress. In 1812 he was taken from his family by death, after he had blessed his twelve children, and enjoined upon the five sons in particular, the command of inviolable unity and affection. And never has a paternal legacy been more fully or conscientiously discharged. It was a singular trait in the character of this family, that all its members appear like counsel of the shade of their father, in every important affair, often mention him in discourse, recall his maxims of prudence and experience, and never breathe his name without veneration. In 1813 occurred those political events which introduced the house of Rothschild, by an unbroken series of cash and credit negotiations, to that!

But, he avowed, the inhabitants of the country through which this road was intended to pass, were unable to raise the means by uniting in a joint stock company; it could only be done in a commercial community; not among plain farmers, who had not surplus income for such purposes.—He believed joint stock companies were at an end throughout the state: they rarely proved profitable, and individuals would not unite in them; and they had been put down by the repeal of the law which secured to them the six per cent interest on their capital advanced. If the road be made at all, it must be done at the expense of the State;—the tolls he thought would pay a handsome profit, and fully reimburse the State;—the Engineer pronounced unequivocally that this will be the result;—The two Governors and the Board of Public Works had united in the same opinion.—But admit that the profits will not pay the interest,—the funds of the commonwealth were in a flourishing state at present. SENATORIAL ELECTION.—We deem it unnecessary to say much relative to the Senatorial Election, to the publications which have passed between the Hon. AsHUR ROBBINS, and Mr. E. R. Potter. Each have already occupied a sufficient space in the public eye; the one as the accuser; the other as the accused;—Mr. Robbins has triumphed, he has refuted the calumnies of an unprincipled political enemy and aspirant. Whatever may be public sentiment abroad, it is certain that Mr. Robbins retains the entire confidence of the people in this quarter, and the public voice, if called for, would give him a spontaneous suffrage.

Richard S. Taylor, Thomas C. Nelson, Lewis Temple, and John Lumpkin, were appointed a committee, who, after retiring for a short time, reported the preamble and resolutions offered by Mr. Spiller, which were unanimously adopted by the meeting, and are as follows: Among the political maxims of the day, we hold none more self-evident and important, than that "magistrates are trustees or servants" of the people, and "at all times amenable to them."—Acting under the influence of this principle, we consider it not only a right, but a sacred duty, to examine and scrutinize the conduct of public "agents" impartially but firmly, fairly but deeply. If, upon this examination, we find that these "agents," clothed with authority by the people, for their welfare and happiness, shall have neglected or betrayed their interest, it is our duty to withdraw our confidence and select more faithful "servants." We believe that the conduct of John Q. Adams, elevated to the most important office in the gift of a free people, has been calculated to forfeit our confidence and awaken our fears. Let facts be submitted to the candid. At the time of his elevation to the Presidency, we were at peace with all the nations of the earth, and leaping the fruits of a free, liberal and profitable commerce. Concord and harmony reigned in every quarter of our beloved country, and

The Indians have not refused to permit you to establish schools in their country, to educate their children. They have not refused to permit you to send farmers and mechanics among them, to teach them husbandry and the mechanical arts. They have sanctioned and approved of the system commenced by the annual appropriation of $10,000 for these objects. This is, in my opinion, the only correct system which we can pursue. The sums expended under the act of 1819, for the civilization of the Indians, have been productive of more beneficial effects than the whole sum of $250,000 paid to them in annuities. The expenditure of this small sum is more honorable to the nation than five times the amount paid for the support of your Military Academy, and many other objects of appropriations. Sir, by this expenditure, more than one thousand two hundred Indian children are taught whatever is valuable or necessary to be learned by the common classes in society. They are taught to read and write, to plow and reap, and all the branches of business necessary for the prosperity of a new country. The females are taught all the domestic duties which belong to their station. The advantages derived from this small appropriation have been much enhanced by the "auxiliary aid" of the Missionary Establishments existing in the Indian country.

Jackson's conduct is referred to, it is believed that no unprejudiced man would ever suppose after that, that he could have been induced to vote for Gen. Jackson as the Chief magistrate of the country. Nor is there any fair ground to believe that he supported Mr. Adams from personal motives of any sort, but from a conviction, such as influences ourselves, that he was obviously and decidedly the best qualified man, and as such, he was compelled by duty to vote for him. As little do we believe that Mr. Adams nominated Mr. Clay to the Senate as Secretary of State from personal attachment or obligation; but knowing that no man in this country had passed through a more brilliant public course for the last twenty years than Mr. Clay, in which he had acquired great popularity and evinced a Statesmanlike ability.
