It was a shaggy dog of the St. Bernard breed, and of uncommon size and strength; and distracted as was the mind of Franz, he thought he recollected having seen him before. The sight of the snow seemed to delight the noble animal, and divert it even from its post of watchfulness; and while it unrolled its huge body in the familiar element and seemed rather to enjoy than defy the tempest, Franz cautiously entered the hut and, seizing precipitately a flask of kirschwasser, which he saw on a shelf, flew down the slope, rather mechanically whistling for the dog as a relief to his solitude, than aware of the powerful ally he was enlisting in his service. There was a lull in the tempest as he entered the defile. The moon struggled with a sickly glimmer through the driving wrack, and he could see far before him over the now nearly uniform sheet of snow. But not a trace of a human being was visible, nor did the spot where he thought he had left Clara present even a vestige of the tall staves which he had set up to ensure recognition. A shudder crept over his frame, and he felt as if in leaving Clara he had been her murderer! He ran with frantic impatience, racking his memory for the spot he sought in vain to recognize, and in his despair called loudly on the insensible and buried Clara.

The following anecdote of Huber, the celebrated naturalist, in the Memoires sur Josephine. M. Huber's work on the natural history of bees is well known in this country, but we believe it is little known that his observations relative to the habits of this insect were made through the medium of his wife, at a time when he was totally blind. "We frequently visited," says the authoress, "an interesting and remarkable man, M. Huber, nephew of the friend of Voltaire. He had been blind since the age of seventeen. At that period he fell in love with a rich young lady, who returned his affections, but their parents opposed their union and they were separated. A few months after he was afflicted with gutta serena, which deprived him entirely of sight, which he regretted the more because he was unable to see the object of his affection. He was sent to Paris with the hope that a cure might be effected, but he obtained no relief, and returned in despair to Geneva."

Murder no longer continued a licensed crime on the plea of retaliation, was to yield itself to the rules of civilization, and to be examined by a Jury of the country, before the accused should be made to atone—crime whomever committed was to be inquired into; and savage laws and savage habits, to give place to the more enlightened principles of experience and of reason. Should the effect of this measure be to induce Indians to remove from the territory in question, on receiving from the United States, all that they are justly entitled to, the President is satisfied that a result in the highest degree desirable will be effected, and by means the most unexceptionable. Their removal west of the Mississippi, and beyond the range of state authority, would, he believes, greatly tend to better their condition, that it would carry with it, benevolence to them, and justice to the state of Georgia. His long and large experience in Indian affairs leaves no doubt with him on this point; and however much he respects the motives which have induced missionary societies and others, to ameliorate the condition of the Indians, he nevertheless feels it a duty to employ all the light but means which have been placed at his disposal, to produce more enlarged and beneficial results towards this peculiar race of people.

Torrigiano had undertaken to carve a Madonna and child of the natural size, at the order of a Spanish Grandee; it was to be made after the model of one which he had already executed; and a promise was given him of a reward proportioned to the merit of his work. His employer (the Duke de Anas,) was one of the first Grandees of Spain; and Torrigiano, who conceived highly of his generosity, and well knew what his talents could perform, was determined to outdo his former work. He had passed a great part of his life in travelling from kingdom to kingdom in search of employment, and, flattering himself with the hope that he had now found a resting place after all his labors, the ingenious artist, with much pains and application, completed the work; and presented to his employer a matchless piece of sculpture, the utmost effort of his art.

The papers have abounded with numerous details of the late duel between Cumming and M Duffie,—we have scarcely looked at them, having no desire to read, or to present them to our readers;—the following article is of another description, and will, probably, afford amusement:— From the Aurora. “They paced with rapid steps the way to greatness. Female dueling.—On the 5th inst. in Chester county, Pennsylvania, two young ladies determined to imitate Cummings and M’Duffie; the challenge was given—the time of meeting fixed—seconds were chosen to make the necessary preparations for the sad affair—the mediation of friends was of no avail—the battle was to be fought—the fair damsels were to encounter each other on the field of honor, or their reputation was to suffer—the whole union would make it a public business, and it would be a general topic of conversation, unless one of the daughters of Eve shed her blood nobly in a duel. Miss Lillyroach had already bequeathed part of her personal estate, the beautiful flowers plucked from the mountain's brow, were to become the property of her lover. Miss Iroldfinch had sighed with her last breath, at the prospect of losing her tender passion in the arms of death. The meeting was to take place at six o’clock, A. M., the 6th inst.

The good people began to stir—the maids opened the street doors, and wash brush in hand prepared for the morning’s duty—the sleepy apprentice and shop boy, with naked feet and uncombed locks, were yawningly taking down their shutters—the little black sweep with his sooty blanket, had just begun his shrieking cries—the milkmen with their ponderous tin kettles and falsetto voice—the baker with his long one-horse cart and ammunition baskets—the laborers sauntering for employment had just given animation to the scene, at early dawn, when I made my way to the battery. Novelists and poets have differed as to that period of the day, when women are seen to the most advantage—some prefer the bustle of domestic duty—others the head of the well-stored table—some the social circle, while there are many, who by the glare of torchlight, in the mazes of the dance or the crowd of the drawing room, make the most useful or the most brilliant exhibition. But, if you wish to see nature unsophisticated, lowering o’er the blandishments of art, with powerful majesty, you must rise very early and see our ladies take a morning walk, no stiffly laced bodices, no hair thickly curled, no zephyr shawls or a frightful sized dress bonnet, crushed with the weight of artificial roses.

The horse then became frightened, quitted their rank, and were carried away by the violence of the waves. They no longer swam, but floated about in scattered groups. The riders struggled in vain; at length their strength gave way, and they resigned themselves to their fate. Their destruction was certain; but it was for their country: it was in her presence, and for the sake of their deliverers, that they devoted themselves; and even when on the point of being engulfed forever, they suspended their unavailing struggles, turned their faces towards Napoleon, and exclaimed, "Vive l'Empereur." Three of them were especially remarked, who, with their heads still above the billows, repeated this cry and perished instantly. The army was struck with mingled horror and admiration. "As for Napoleon, he prescribed with anxiety and precision the measures necessary to save the greater number; but without appearing moved; either from the habit of subduing his feelings; from considering the ordinary emotions of the heart as a weakness in times of war, of which it was not for him to set the example, and therefore necessary for him to suppress; or because he foresaw the advent of greater evils, compared with which that above described appeared of trivial importance." D. O. Ourane Ouraxc.

On both occasions steamboats were chartered. The movement of Major Riley's detachment, last summer, must also have involved some expenditure for transportation. I know these various items to be considerable, and that they have been incurred, and must be incurred, just so long as we remain without horses. The exact amount of what has been expended heretofore is unknown to me; the amount that may be expended hereafter cannot be foreseen. It will depend upon the frequency and magnitude of the alarms on the frontiers; alarms which must arise upon every point of a line of more than a thousand miles in length, covering the settlements of Louisiana, Arkansas, Missouri, Illinois, and the new Territory upon the Upper Mississippi. The amount of these casual and unforeseen expenditures may be safely assumed to be equal to the expense of keeping up the ten companies proposed in the bill. I make this assumption advisedly, and would be willing to stake the passage of the bill upon the Quarter Master General's opinion of the fact, if the passage of such a bill, in the judgment of any statesman, ought to depend upon such a fact.

Restaud was a man of low origin, who had by a course of honest and persevering industry, acquired an immense fortune; to inherit which he had only one child, a son, at that period about eighteen, who was a fine promising young man, and Restaud, who was extremely fond of him, had spared no expense on his education. The Marquis, after opening his business, began to talk of the length of time it would be before he could repay the money, and the security which he proposed giving. "Monsieur Marquis," said Restaud, interrupting him, "I have a plan to propose, which will, if you choose to accede to it, entirely obviate all the inconveniences you must be put to, to repay this sum. But my plan may perhaps bear a little hard upon your pride, if so, monsieur marquis, speak freely; I shall not be less ready to lend you the money." "What is your plan, my good friend," said De Moncey. Restaud now with some little circumlocution, unfolded it, and De Moncey listened with considerable surprise to the proposal of an alliance between his only daughter and the son of Restaud. Like deepest crimson flushed the cheek of the Marquis at an offer which he was at first inclined to regard as an insult, but a few minutes' reflection changed his ideas.

and ten or eleven feet in width, drawing something less than three feet of water. They mostly rate at forty tons, although I saw one of sixty. They are provided either with a tarpaulin or boarded ceiling to keep out rain. Each boat requires one man in the stern to steer, and another in the bow with a boat hook or pole. The tow rope is of the size of a common trace cord, and is about one hundred and twenty feet in length. This rope is fastened about fifteen feet abaft the bow, to the side of the boat next to the path. A single horse generally draws the market boat. If the beast is very good, he may advance from 30 to 40 miles a day—always walking. The packets or passenger boats I think, now, are six in number—They are not quite so long as the market craft, but are something wider. I passed four miles in one of them (the Montezuma) and found her a very convenient vessel. Her cabin was forty feet long and handsomely furnished. Thirty persons might sleep commodiously—the provisions are quite equal to those in our steam ships or sloops. These packets are provided with relays of horses, and make no halts except to land and take in passengers. They run at night as well as by day, and make a voyage of ninety or ninety-five miles in 24 hours.

From thole wells winch he acquainted, containing in the bed of our ninety-six cubical feet, being twelve feet long, four feet wide, and two feet in depth; it was supposed, that to give one horse the fourth of the load of four, it would only be necessary to give them the space of twenty-four cubical feet, or to make them four feet by three, with a depth of two feet; but it was soon observed that the power of a horse might be enlarged so as to have the dimensions of five feet one inch in breadth, two feet in depth, and to contain thirty-five cubical feet and a fraction, making the proportion of the one horse cart to the four horse wagon as thirty-five to ninety-six— more than one to three. This places in a striking point of view, the advantage such small carts have over large ones in the quantity of work performed. The great superiority of these carts is rendered still more obvious and striking by the observations, that two horses with single horse carts will draw as much as three horses with one cart; that a common carrier at Carlisle, who many years employed a wagon, has laid it aside, and now uses single horse carts only; as he finds he can, by these means, carry much greater weights.

This is nothing less than an impression which has been artfully made upon the public mind, too generally, that the good will and honor of our state is not to be confided in. It must be doubly revolting to the just pride of the state, if it shall be ascertained that any portion of its own, hitherto, respectable citizens have contributed to give to it this inimical character. Two statements have been principally relied upon to establish this most degrading charge against the state. One of which is known to be utterly destitute of truth, and the other I must presume to be equally so. The 1st is, “that the members of our late legislature caused to be re-issued, in payments to themselves, for their services, the one-tenth part of the notes of the State Bank, which by law was required to be unconditionally withdrawn from circulation.” This statement is notoriously false. The subject was brought before, and fully discussed by the legislature, which positively refused to permit those notes to be re-issued, nor did a single member receive any portion of them, in consideration of his services. The 2d statement is, “that, notwithstanding this solemn refusal on the part of the legislature, the President and Directors of the State Bank, have determined to re-issue those notes, upon their own authority, for the purpose of rebuilding a bank house.”

With what part of all these is it possible, that the other man, either by reading, or practice, hold any acquaintance? If he has any merit, any skill? If he can work at any trade, it is at the trade of arms; and in the labors of the camp. For this kind of labor we have not now, nor shall we soon have, unless we make occasion for it ourselves, any serious demand. What would the nation, what New-England, what would all men say of John Quincy Adams, if he had, by the same practices, acquired the same skill, as Andrew Jackson has acquired, at the dirk and pistol? What if he had, at any public rejoicings, or at a horse race, quarreled with his neighbor, called him to a duel, reserved his fire, and then deliberately shot him through the heart? What would men say of New-England, had she nurtured, and brought up a man, who could, at noon day, at an inn, and in a city, when men were quietly at their table, rush upon them, with sword and pistol, at the head of a band selected for the purpose of assassination? This was done by Andrew Jackson to the Bentons. Well may his advocates boast of his skill at his weapons. No man has used them more. It is with truth they say he can make his mark. He has indeed made it on many a gory bosom.

When the wings are spread, they measure sixteen paces (40 feet) in extent, from point to point; the feathers are eight paces (40 feet) in length, and the quill part two palms (8 inches) in circumference. It is said to have powers sufficient to carry off a live rhinoceros." Edmund Temple’s Travels in Peru. Lofty Flight of the Condor.—The region which may be considered as the habitual abode of the Condor, begins at a height equal to that of Etna, and comprehends strata of air at an elevation of from 9,000 to 16,000 feet above the level of the sea. The largest individuals that are met with in the chain of the Andes of Quito, are about 14 feet from the tip of one wing to that of the other, and the smallest only eight. From these dimensions, and from the visual angle under which this bird sometimes appears perpendicularly above our heads, it may be judged to what a prodigious height it rises when the sky is clear. When seen, for example, under an angle of four minutes, it must lie at a perpendicular distance of 8,876 feet. The Cave of Antisiana, situated opposite the mountain of Chussulongo, and from which we measured the bird soaring, is situated at a height of 19,958 feet above the level of the Pacific Ocean.

But lest, this view of the subject should not be sufficient to satisfy you, I beg leave to refer you to the concurring opinions of the executive and legislative departments of the general government. Neither of those departments have ever doubted, and both have affirmed that the office of postmaster is “an office held under the government of the United States.” By the 6th section of the 1st article of the constitution of the United States, it is declared that, “No person holding an office under the United States, shall be a member of either house during his continuance in office." To prove that the exposition given by the executive department of the general government to this section is, as I have stated, I need only refer you to a report made by the Secretary of State to the President, on the 24th December, 1817, and the message of the President to the House of Representatives, on the 29th December, in the same year. I also refer you to the recorded facts that at different intervals, since the year 1800, the Hon. Mr. Turner, Benjamin Tallmadge, Erastus Root, Matthew Lyon, Thomas Gholson, Samuel McKee, S. Dana, E. Wickes, H. Tracy, and Elias Earle held the appointments of postmasters at the times of their respective elections to Congress, and had to resign those appointments previous to taking their seats in the House of Representatives.

Deputations of this character might call on the President, and explain to him fully the wishes of their constituents. The Indian bill leaves almost everything to the discretion of the President; and this makes it particularly proper to address him personally. Caution and delay cannot injure the United States, or the rightful claims of any State; but haste may destroy the Indians, and inflict tremendous evils upon ourselves. There is the more reason to hope.

Report says that he owes about $100 for board in one place, $40 for board in another; to his tailor, and numerous smaller bills to the other people, not even excepting the friend of whom he borrowed money. All are left to suffer by him. So the world goes. A worthless, brainless fellow in whiskers and gloves, will be welcomed into good society, while an industrious citizen, who dresses within his income is discarded or overlooked. A dashing fop can gull almost everybody. He runs in upon the tailor, he runs upon the landlord, he runs about upon the labor of the shoemaker, he runs his face at the billiard board and his presence into good society; all by the fascinations of his dress, his whiskers and a borrowed gold watch; and at last he runs away, leaving his creditors and his dupes unpaid, and perhaps unpitied. It is with extreme regret that we have to announce the death of H., Assistant Engineer of the Delaware Canal, who, on Wednesday 25th, leading his horse, was thrown down and trodden upon by the animal, causing almost instant death. Mr. W. was an able and competent master of his profession, and to his firmness and integrity those who have an interest in the prosperity of that work. The following resolution, passed by the board of Directors, is but a just tribute to the merits of that distinguished unfortunate. Immediately on the receipt of the intelligence of Mr. W's death, the Directors of the Chesapeake and Ohio Canal Company met, and with heavy hearts, passed resolutions bearing testimony to his honorable, frank, and upright character, as an individual—and that they sympathize in the distress of his family.

Ever since the separation of the United States and the mother country, the West Indies have in one way or another drawn from this country their principal supplies. They must do it still; and it does not make so great a difference as many people imagine, whether our produce reaches them directly or indirectly through the ports of St. Thomas or Cartagena. Indeed, the more intelligent of the British shipping merchants are well apprised of the effect of the Order in Council which has closed their ports to the vessels of the United States; and they already feel that the indirect trade is stripping them in a great measure of the advantages which they hoped to derive from that measure. We have before us the Nova Scotian of the 9th ult., containing some extracts from a letter on the denial policy of Great Britain, addressed to the Ministry, which originally appeared in the London Morning Chronicle, and has been attributed to Mr. Carter, of London, a gentleman well known in the mercantile world. The writer tells ministers that it is true the Colonists did express by addresses, etc. their gratitude for the boon which they were told was to be conferred on them, by opening the trade of foreign nations to their industry and enterprise.

Mr. O'Connell.—Whatever relates to this extra ordinary man is read with extraordinary interest. He is the reigning lion of the Anglo Saxon world of Eu rope and America; the darling of the multitude, the terror of ministers, and a man whose great force of character and enthusiasm and disinterestedness of pa triotism, seem to have impressed even his enemies with high respect. Though circumstances were working together long before, to produce the effect, and sooner or later must have produced it, yet to Mr. O'Connell is certainly due the glorious honor of having been the immediate instrument of the emancipation of Ireland.

A large body of Mamelukes were charged by our cavalry, headed by Colonel Herbert, and totally routed. Our riflemen in front picked out the officers as they advanced to their post. Our artillery was served by French and English volunteers, who had orders not to fire till the Turks were within 200 yards. The enemy now endeavoured to turn our left wing, but were received at the point of the bayonet by General La Fayette, our second in command. — The battle now raged along the whole line with the most deadly fury. The village of St. John was three times taken and retaken, with great slaughter; their second in command fell in the last attack, when they gave up the contest, leaving 800 dead on the spot, besides prisoners and wounded. Gen. Gouras, at the head of the Corinthian brigade, carried the village of St. Anne’s, and ordered a general attack, with the whole force, when the Turks were completely routed, and fled in every direction, leaving 3000 dead, 900 prisoners, 400 wounded, 2 Generals killed, 14 officers wounded, 25 taken, with 9 cannon, the Pacha’s tent, 14 baggage and ammunition wagons, &c. Our loss was 800 killed and 700 wounded. A young man of the name of Herbert took two standards, for which he was made Captain on the field; three other standards were also taken.” Letters from Col. Herbert.—“Camp at Lepanto, Jan 25, 1826—Dear”.

When the printed from high visited the corrupted church of Christ and the religion of the Gospel was once more preached in its primitive purity, literature began also to awake, and the invention of the art of printing gave her blessings a more general diffusion than in the days of Greece and Rome, they had ever known. But the nations of Europe had yet their literature to create. Their languages, except the Italian (which was early refined) were rough and uncultivated, and those who sought for the treasures of literature and science found them in the Moré houses of Greek and Roman lore. As the knowledge of these ancient tongues became more generally diffused, they imparted their refinement to the languages of modern Europe. From this remarkable movement, it appears that to Greece we are indebted for our English literature, as well as for whatever we may possess of taste, skill in the fine arts. Estimating, as I do, all these blessings next after those of true religion and genuine liberty, what bounds can I place on our obligations to the land from whence we derive them? To go a step further back: How can we adequately estimate our obligations to him who first introduced them into that land?

[Morning Journal] SIMILARITY OF TASTES.  Jack Prime could eat no fat.  His wife could eat no lean.  And so between them both,  They licked the platter clean.  WILL SOMO.  It is the prevailing opinion, that the man and woman, in order to be happy in married life, should possess the same or similar tastes. But that this opinion is very far from being correct, it requires very little observation or force of reasoning to decide. Apart from the quaint and venerable stanza which we have quoted above, it is evident that where the tastes of two persons are lived on the same article, and there is barely enough for one, there may be quarrelling and jangling for its possession. On the contrary, where the tastes are different, there is a greater chance of both being gratified.  In a turkey or chicken, there are two kinds of meat, the white and the brown.

J. C. S. M. VARIETY. EGYPT ILLUSTRATED. Mr. Buckingham's lectures on that Country. In speaking of Alexandria, the Lecturer described the large column lying there, called Cleopatra’s Needle; and to show the great power of the ancient Egyptians he mentioned that the united skill and talents of the English and French armies, when both were in Egypt, and when the latter generously proffered their services to the former to assist in conveying this monument of antiquity to England—the united skill and power of both armies were unable to move this Column, which the Egyptians must have transported beyond the Cataracts—a distance of 600 miles, and placed there on its pedestal. He also referred to the Monolithic Temple of Amasis, at Sais, which was brought from the same spot, a distance of 500 miles, and employed 3,000 pilots of the Nile, three inundations, or three years, to convey it to the place where it was to be erected; as well as to the Pyramids, and the enormous Statue of Memnon, to show that the Egyptians had a method of moving and raising large masses, now unknown to us. To illustrate the size of the large Pyramid, the Lecturer stated that it would, within a few feet, fill the whole area of Lincoln's Inn Fields, its height being equal to its base.

He thought it aot partic'ler, To mention at what time he'd dip the clothes So at all times of sight As well as day, His customers would come and make such fray As any common man would fright, Tho' dull-ear'd, Merely to see, Who should have precedency In being colored. Brown patient of the noise. Job never chid, Or ever woke; Or if he did, He never spoke. At length, by some reverse In pot and purse, Job, feeling rather cm kith, Determined to relinquish, for, hark'ee! not to flatter, This rainbow kind of life, Together with his wife, As plain as any glass could speak, Began to take the coloring matter From either cheek! So, meeting by good chance A little, nervous man of France, He took an opportunity to let His house, And still as any mouse, After the hire, Without a notice by Gazette, Or crier, Or any other way, He took his leave one day, And moved up street, a few doors higher. Job felt a wicked fun To think he had not advertised: Thought he, as sure as a gun They’ll make a clatter! He’ll be most cursedly surprised— Right! Lord; how they will bawl At this poor devil of a Gaul— No matter! It happened about twelve o'clock, Or thereabouts, Monsieur awoke; He listened—there were shouts— And then a knock— And after, A rattling peal of laughter, At last one spoke. * Halloo!

Dr. Sir Falward Stanley may suitably claim respect from others.'' Again Fab .la promised to behave with more calmness. The vessel reached the harbour, and they proceeded to a hotel, from whence they determined to dispatch a note to inform Isabella's family of their arrival. The time which intervened before an answer could arrive, was spent by Stanley in endeavoring, by mingled arguments and soothing, to restore his wife to some degree of composure. But tranquility is not for those who, like poor Isabella, have forsaken the paths of duty, and who, by following the impulses of passion, have entailed on themselves regret and remorse. Those only may expect tranquility, who adhere to principle, and take reason to their guide. Isabella had learned this too late, and though united to the man without whom she had imagined she could not live, yet she was miserable. Four years prior to this second voyage, young Manly had visited America. This voyage was one of curiosity and pleasure. His parents were dead, and he inherited from them an ample fortune. NEW YORK AMULET THE primary object of this work will be, to check the rapid progress of two alarming evils, so fatally prevalent in our country, viz: Intemperance and Infidelity, which, like the cankerworm, are stripping the walks of life of all its flowers and leaving the moral world a leafless desert. To do this the more effectually, we shall portray in the most vivid colors, the deformity and deleterious consequences of these two deadly evils, by interesting moral tales, sketches, fragments, essays, and scriptural illustrations.

His servant who loved him with the utmost tenderness, having heard that sudden fear was a sovereign cure for that complaint, resolved to try it on his master; wherefore, having observed that the Marquis walked every day on the banks of the Po, and knowing it was not deep, he resolved to push him in. He acquainted a miller who lived over against the place with his design, and having ordered him to be ready with his boat to take his master up, if there should be occasion, the next morning he threw him in, after which he fled to Padua. In the meantime, the miller took up the Marquis, who was indeed thoroughly frightened, and vowed to be revenged. So extraordinary a case was the subject of general conversation; the Marquis caused his servant to be summoned before the courts of justice, and not appearing, he was declared an outlaw, and condemned, if he should ever return, to be beheaded. This news soon reached Padua; notwithstanding which the servant in a few days came back to Ferrara, and desired admittance to the Marquis, which was denied, and instead thereof, he was apprehended, and ordered to prepare for execution. The Marquis, however, finding himself cured of his ague, his resentment began to abate, and he was determined to save him, but seemed to resolve to let the law take its course.

We copy the above paragraph from the last Richmond Enquirer. We are disposed to give credit to the assertion of the Editor that the letter referred to was not forged by him. We believe Mr. Ritchie to be too honorable a man, and to have too much of the Virginian chival- ry about him, to descend to such an act. We hope he will have at least manliness enough to avow himself, and thus rescue Mr. Ritchie from the situation of delicacy and mortification in which he has placed him.

Churchill raised his hand for a moment from his face, and then looked towards Heaven. A smile of rapture was on his face; he again covered his eyes; George read on in a broken voice, but he betrayed no further emotion. His hand at last fell gently from his face. — Mrs. Churchill uttered a scream of horror on seeing the pale and fixed look; he did not hear it; the pure spirit was at last free. * * -■* * It was morning when the confusion of ideas began to subside, and the dreadful truth gradually returned to my recollection. I started from the bed where I had been laid, and perceiving my poor worn out servant asleep, I softly left the room, determining to look once more on that beloved countenance, in which I had so long read the soul of Churchill. I saw a door half opened, and entered the room, but could scarcely believe that I was in the apartment of my departed friend. There was none of the mockery of death—all was as if nothing unusual had happened. A window was open, into which had strayed the flowering branch of a white lilac that grew against it, and now filled the apartment with perfume. I hoped I had only been dreaming of misery, and approached the bed.

"Question. Mr. Fuller, in order to India, receive the different papers sent out to you by the Secretary, is it necessary that you should read them? A. To read them all would occupy more time than I can spare; but on merely opening them, I can immediately perceive whether they are from banks or Receivers of Public Moneys, or what is their general character. Question by Mr. Forsyth: Who marks the endorsements on these letters? A. If they require an immediate answer, I usually draw the answer, and in that case I endorse them myself; but, if they do not require immediate attention, or do not need any answer at all, I hand them over to a young man who assists me, and he makes the endorsement. The greater part of the letters received are of the description first mentioned. I do not draft all the answers, but most of the letters are of such a kind that there are precedents for the answers required. Question by Mr. Forsyth: When checks are made upon the Secretary of the Treasury for papers, does the direction usually pass through your hands? A. Yes, it usually does. (The direction is generally endorsed on the back.) In some cases it has been sent immediately by the Secretary to the person having charge of the papers. Question by Mr. Forsyth: To which of the Clerks go the letters from the Receivers of the Public Moneys? A."

In 1800, she married Mr. Fry, whose amiable and generous character fully justifies her choice. Far from opposing her benevolent labors, he facilitates them, and affords her ample means of relieving the unfortunate, by annually placing at her disposal a considerable sum which she applies entirely to the benefit of the poor. Mrs. Fry's life is devoted to acts of virtue, and her time is almost wholly occupied in charitable missions. She makes no distinction—the unfortunate are brothers, whatever be their country or religion—sorrow is everywhere the same, and benevolence should be universal. Mrs. Fry is at once a physician to the body and the soul; she comforts and feeds the poor, and supplies them with clothes and with Bibles—and thus she explains and teaches the gospel. She even administers succor to criminals—she regards vice merely as a disease, and never withholds assistance from the sick. Mrs. Fry, on being informed of the deplorable state of the female prisoners in Newgate, resolved to relieve them. She applied to the governor for leave of admittance—he replied that she would incur the greatest risk in visiting that abode of iniquity and disorder which he himself scarcely dared to enter—he observed that the language she must hear would inevitably disgust her, and made use of every argument to prevail on her to relinquish her intention. Mrs. Fry said she was fully a-!

The proper ground to be assumed, being as it is considered a mere inquiry as to the boundary, is whether the Indians, without the consent of the United States and of the State of Georgia, were capable to alter or change the boundary from what it was understood to be in 1802, and to refer the decision of the respective demands hence arising to the result to be arrived at on a final inquiry and examination into this question. These being the views entertained by the President on this subject, it is concluded by him to send some competent and faithful representative to ascertain as far as practicable what is to be considered the actual and true boundary between the Creek and Cherokee nations, the precise period at which the line from Buzzard Roost to Will's Creek was made, and the circumstances under which it was agreed upon, with whatever evidence calculated to disclose certainty, may be obtained.

I am a Clerk in the Office of the Secretary of the Treasury. I never heard of the letter of Mr. Stephenson to the Treasury, until I read the memorial of Mr. Edwards to Congress. I have been in the office three years. I had charge of the letters in the apartment where Mr. Washington now is, for a time. I never saw such a letter as that said to have been written by Mr. Stephenson, in the Department. It is now nearly two years since I left that room. I have heard nothing of such a letter’s being found in the Department, since Mr. Edwards’s memorial was presented to Congress.

Abandoning the position which he had just taken, and admitting that in law a dissolution of the charters does produce an extinguishment of all debts and from the banks, the gentleman nevertheless insists that his second position remains unshaken – still contends, that by an act to be now passed, we can change this penalty for past misconduct into a forfeiture of the property to the state. And I had already attempted to show that the state could not thus impair the obligation of contracts by adding new conditions or terms to its own grant; that it could not thus divest its citizens of vested rights by partial legislation; and that it could not thus, by an ex post facto law, create new penalties for past misconduct. Military uniforms, gold and silver embroidery, fringe, epaulets, etc. cleaned and polished to equal their original brilliancy. As to his piety, astonishing were his applications of Scripture upon occasion, and his sense of God. He had learned all his catechism early, and understood the historical parts of the Bible and New Testament to a wonder: —how Christ came to redeem mankind, and how, comprehending these necessities himself, his godly father wrote unusual truth in the hour of his wise. These and the like illuminations, far exceeding his age and experience, considering the piettiness of his address and behavior, cannot but leave impressions on me, at the memory of him. When one told him many days a Quaker had fasted, he replied that was no wonder, for Christ had said that man should not live by bread alone, but by the word of God. He would of himself select the most pathetic psalms and chapters out of Job, to read to his maid during his sickness, telling her when she pitied him, that all God’s children must suffer affliction. He declaimed against the vanity of the world before he had seen any. Often he would desire those who came to see him to pray by him, and a year before he fell sick to pray with him alone in some corner. How thankfully would he receive admonitions! How soon be reconciled! How different, yet continually cheerful. He would give a grave advice to his brother John; bear with his impertinences, and say that he was but a child.

This divided into two rooms would give 14 feet by 12 to each room, and leave the jailor's room 16 feet by 24, all upon the lower floor. These rooms of confinement are all that your plan provides for, and are certainly sufficient for the purpose intended; the room for the jailor is surely large enough—where then is the necessity for the second story? I can see none, unless it be to make an excellent parlour, and two lodging rooms for the jailor on the second floor. Which would be providing four rooms for him on the two lower floors, besides two excellent rooms, which a house of that width would admit of, in the garret, making in the whole six excellent rooms for a jailor. It seems to me that this would be making most extravagant accommodation for him at public cost. — The people will not submit to it. Your plan has been hastily adopted—think of it again.—If on further reflection you think it inexpedient, let not the suggestions of pride prevent you from changing it. Can the second story be necessary? Are not two rooms sufficient for the confinement of prisoners? Your plan does not contemplate more.

It is the people that govern the press; and experience every day teaches us, that no newspaper whatsoever can long head against the public sentiment. Two thirds at least, of the newspapers of the United States, were opposed to the election of Gen. Jackson. Yet the people elected him. An independent Press, that is to say, a press independent of certain political obligations which it cannot depart from without losing its influence and its patronage, could not, and I doubt if it ought to exist in a free country. It is the organ, and except in very extreme cases, ought to be the creature of the public opinion, when that opinion is free. A President of the United States, chosen by the free voice of the people of the United States, is little else than the executive of the will of that people. The people elect the conductors of newspapers to office in their gift, and the President of the United States occasionally appoints them to office in his gift. And for what? For having expressed and enforced the will of the people, in advocating the election of their favorite candidate. It is not so much to gratify himself, as to gratify the people that he appoints them.

Singular secret Murder. —“Mr John Kello, minister of Spot, in East Lothian, had an extraordinary talent for preaching; and was universally held to be a man of singular piety.—His wife was handsome, cheerful, tender-hearted, and in a word, possessed all the qualities that can endear a woman to her husband. A pious rich widow in the neighborhood tempted his avarice. She clung to him as her spiritual guide; and but for his little wife, he had doubts of obtaining her hand in marriage. He turned gradually peevish and discontented. His change of behavior made a deep impression on his wife, for she loved him tenderly and yet was anxious to conceal her treatment from the world. Her meekness, her submission, her patience tended but to increase his sullenness. Upon a Sunday morning, when on her knees, offering up her devotions, he came softly behind her, put a rope about her neck, and hung her up to the ceiling.

It was the work of the nation, and the sympathies of every true son of Germany made him a brother, to all who could participate in his exultation in the literary glories of his country; he could not look with feelings other than those of generous affection, on his associates in the splendid work of calling into existence a new world of intellect. Thus, though the political union of the German states was daily weakened, the subversion of the system was for the present averted by the moral union, created by the progress of their common culture. Though Frederick lent not his princely favor to encourage the first efforts of the German Muse, within his own states he was the patron of every useful art and liberal science.

January 3. NATIONAL ARMORY. Mr. J. T. Johnson, of Kentucky, in introducing the following resolution, observed that there were various reasons which urged him to offer a resolution, which had for its object the establishment of a national armory in the western country. He was apprised that this subject had been before congress on several occasions, and had been rejected; but when he considered its intrinsic merit and justice, he would not anticipate an unfavorable result. He said there were considerations connected with the nature which were dear to every American bosom. It had been conceded that the militia constituted our surest guarantee in times of danger; they are our shield for protection. In order then, to enable them to act efficiently, they must be armed and disciplined. This subject, he observed, was not novel.

Monticello, Saturday, September 27, 1823. We some weeks since informed our readers that we should discontinue the publication of this paper unless a more liberal support was given—since then, we have not received any additional encouragement of consequence—consequently, in consulting our interest, we are compelled to relinquish the hope of making the Gazette a permanent establishment; therefore, the next number will close the half year, and the publication of The Monticello Gazette. All persons are respectfully requested to come forward and settle their accounts immediately after to save trouble. We copy into our columns today Stephen F. Austin's address to settlers in the Province of Texas, to which we particularly invite the attention of our readers, and more particularly those who have seen the many miserable wretches who were duped to emigrate to that province and succeeded in getting back, naked and worn down with fatigue and hunger—and also those who were able to get back at all, and who have an idea of the number that perished in that inhospitable country. The London Courier of the 8th says, "Joseph Hunton, the Quaker, was taken out of the American packet ship Leeds, windbound at Portsmouth, by a Bow Street officer, on a charge of forgery, at the house of Sir William Curtiss & Co." Captain Sprague wrote from Portsmouth to London on the 7th, that he came on board the Leeds at London, by the name of Wilkinson, and stated that he was a merchant in Ironmonger-lane; his family resident about four miles from London. Capt. S. adds: "I understand that his real name is Joseph Hunton, and he is now lodged in jail. They proceed for London to-night. It is fortunate for the ends of justice that the vessel was detained by contrary winds."

It is evident that now, as during the first years of the revolution, the enemies of liberty would wish to see it disgraced by anarchy, sullied by crime, and misunderstood by the peaceable citizens, who would greatly err in confounding it with the causes of those disturbances which are the means of destroying their quiet, interrupting their industry, and interfering with public order. Certainly it is not by such means that what we have gained by the glorious revolution of the great week can be consolidated, and that we shall obtain what is necessary to complete the regeneration of France on the basis of perfect freedom, without restriction as without alloy, on a foundation worthy of the revolution effected by a generous people, acquainted at once with their rights and their duties. This people have now become too enlightened to allow themselves to be made the dupes of their disguised adversaries—too well informed to be trained on by men who would mislead them—too just not to be horrified at those vacillations which would disturb the course of justice, and would dishonor us among nations accustomed to the principles and the forms of true liberty—and too proud of their present glory to descend from the high station to which we have been raised by our new revolution.

In a private letter to Wilkes, Junius says, “A man who honestly engages in a public cause must prepare himself for events which will at once demand his utmost patience, and rouse his warmest indignation. I feel myself, at this moment, in the very situation I describe, yet from the common enemy I expect nothing but hostilities against the people. It is the conduct of our friends that surprises and afflicts me.” But if any doubts could remain respecting the identity of Junius and Horne, they must be dispelled by the exordium of Junius’s forty-fourth letter, dated April 22, 1771, addressed to the Printer of the Public Advertiser. This introduction has not the least connection or relation to the subject treated of in the letter, and appears to be lugged in merely with a view of explaining his situation and feelings at the time, which was very soon after the (letter to Mr. Horne) unfortunate termination of the city election. It is as follows: “Sir—To write for profit without taxing the press, to write for fame and to be unknown, to support the intrigues of faction and to be disowned as a dangerous auxiliary by every party in the kingdom, are contradictions which the minister must reconcile before I forfeit my credit with the public. I may quit the service, but it would be absurd to suspect me of desertion.

Those who now talk loudest against what they term proscriptions, but which are changes and reforms demanded by the unequivocal and overwhelming voice of the people, are themselves of the most intolerant case. They never have voted, nor will never vote, for anyone whose political opinions they dislike. In their bigoted and factious conceit, they have trampled down the principles of honor and moral honesty to reach those who have opposed their opinions. The last year the editor of the Boston Statesman was refused a bid of printing to which he was fairly entitled, under the plighted faith of the city, avowedly because his political opinions were obnoxious, and the most boisterous now against what they term the proscriptions of your administration, are those who voted for this unjust and dishonorable act, and those who approved of the measure. That men who have left no effort unessayed to prevent the great civil revolution which the people have decreed, that they who in their intolerance have been unrestrained by love of country or love of justice, should now step boldly forward to denounce salutary official changes, under the plea that they are proscriptive, if it cannot move our anger, must at least excite surprise.

Nations which establish themselves by acts of injustice towards others, which extend over them a cruel dominion merely because a stronger arm enables them to do so, need expect nothing less than the reaction of a retributive providence as a punishment for all such deeds of injustice. That "might gives right" is a maxim of such an odious character, so abhorrent to every feeling of justice and humanity, that few have the hardihood openly to avow it as the rule of their action; while, such is the inconsistency of erring man, there are thousands who daily exemplify it in their practice. Hence the tyranny of despots, the oppressions of men in power, the extortions of the rich, the cupidity and perfidiousness of the cunning dealer, and the high-handed measures of the strong, and the exorbitant prices set upon his wares by the monopolist. The maxim of our Saviour above quoted, we believe, will apply with equal force to every instance and to every species of injustice, whether it be perpetrated by nations, by communities, by secret combinations, by monopolists in company, or by individuals. Neither is it a less infringement of the paramount authority of inflexible justice, because the motive is plausible. Will an honest man steal his neighbor's property that he may be charitable to the poor?

Extraordinary Accident.—We are informed, by a very respectable gentleman, that about seven days since, a stagecoach, containing nine passengers and the driver, turned over, at the north end of the town, near the Blackstone Canal, and was so completely dashed in pieces, that hardly a vestige of it remained after the accident. Several of the passengers were severely injured, and one of them dangerously wounded. The coach was then on its way out of town, and procuring a new carriage as soon as practicable, the passengers were huddled into it, and hastened on their journey. What is most extraordinary in this affair, is that an accident, which, it is known, originated in the carelessness of the driver, should be hushed up, and kept from the notice of the press. It must have been known; but we presume that that disposition, which leads to the suppression of facts, when patronage is in danger, deterred some from giving publicity to the fact. Our informant, who is a man of the first standing in society, says that the carriage was so completely crushed, that no part of it, even the spoke of a wheel, remained whole.

To this high and honorable purpose, General Brown may be truly said to have sacrificed his life. For the disease which abridged his days, and has terminated his course at a period scarcely beyond the meridian of manhood, undoubtedly originated in the hardships of his campaigns on the Canada frontiers, and that glorious wound, which, though desperate, could not remove him from the field of battle till it was won. Quick to perceive—sagacious to anticipate, prompt to decide, and daring in execution, he was born with the qualities which constitute a great commander. His military coup d'etat, his intuitive penetration, his knowledge of men, and his capacity to control them, were known to all his companions in arms, and commanded their respect, while the gentleness of his disposition, the courtesy of his deportment, his scrupulous regard for their rights, his constant attention to their wants, and his affectionate attachment to their persons, universally won their hearts, and bound them to him as a father. Calm and collected in the presence of the enemy, he was without tender of human life; in the hour of battle more sparing of the blood of the soldier than his own; in the hour of victory, the vanquished enemy found in him a humane and compassionate friend; not one drop of blood shed in wantonness or cruelty, sullies the purity of his fame.

My dearest Antony, I once performed your funeral honors with free hand, but now I am a prisoner; satellites watch over me and prevent me from dying, in order that this captive form may be exhibited in the triumphal procession which Octavius has prepared to celebrate your defeat. Henceforth hope for no funeral honors, these are the last that Cleopatra can bestow. We both lived, nothing on earth had power to separate us; but after death we are in danger of making a sad exchange of sepulchers. You, who are a Roman citizen, have found a grave in Alexandria; while I, unhappy queen, must find one in your country. But if the gods of your nation have not forsaken you as mine have done, let me find an asylum in your tomb, that I may thus escape the disgrace which is prepared for me. Oh! receive me, dearest Antony! For of all the misfortunes I have endured, the greatest is separation from you." Her prayer was heard. She obtained permission to have some flowers conveyed to her, among which an asp was concealed, and the sting of the reptile at once freed her from life and the outrage which the pride of Octavius had prepared for her. Her women, Iras and Charmion, stabbed themselves and died with her.

The Chancellor says, also, that this change should be effected, if at all, with the most scrupulous regard to the weaknesses and prejudices of the Indians. He would not justify the use of cold and unfeeling language, such as: "Indians must always retire from the march of civilization. It is in vain to attempt to save them." He would much sooner lament the frauds and impositions, which have been practiced on them by profligate and interested white men, and the deficiency of benevolent feeling towards them, on the part of many, who would by no means tolerate fraud or oppression. Justice requires that it should be said, however, that most of the legislatures of the older States framed laws for the protection of Indians, with a most benevolent regard to their good, and on the genuine principles of Christianity. The Chancellor says again, that the change should be effected "with the entire approbation of the government of the United States." This change, be it remembered, had reference to the little tribes, in the State of New York.

Descending the hill still farther (about ten rods distant,) is another Factory, which is 207 feet long, and 94 wide, including the pack, if upon the whole length. In the southern part of the works are temporary buildings, erected for the purpose of the copperas (sulphate of iron). The ore by the following process is converted into copperas:—First, with the use of a drill, a perforation is made from 10 to 30 inches in depth, and by means of powder large portions are separated from the mine, which are broken into small pieces and conveyed to a suitable spot, until a sufficient quantity is accumulated for a heap, which in the space of a few days will ignite spontaneously. In this condition it remains burning without cessation for about two months, which mostly consumes or expels the sulphur with which the pyritous rock is saturated.

A decree will speedily fix the number of Deputies to be elected by each administrative district, the day of election, and the course of electoral proceedings. From London Courier, Oct. 15. The Paris papers of Wednesday arrived this morning. The only important part of their contents is a telegraphic dispatch from Lille, announcing that the Belgians had refused to receive any more volunteers from France, and stationed guards on the frontiers to prevent their entrance. This determination, while it will tend to the more speedy settlement of affairs in the Netherlands, by diminishing the number of persons interested in prolonging the present state of anarchy, will also prove of great value to the French Government, by removing all cause for offence at an intervention which it had not influence enough over popular opinion to prevent. For some time past the French Ministry have been endeavoring in vain to prevent the march of volunteers to Belgium; and they must, therefore, have received with pleasure a communication which affects all French persons who desire to assist the Revolutionists in Belgium, equally with the native Belgians, who are hastening to join in the struggle against the Government of the Netherlands. The expedition to Greece has nearly failed. The Greeks who embarked on board the frigate Hellas commanded by the Bavarian Colonel Heydeck, refused to land. They confined themselves to burning two Turkish boats loaded with provisions, and demolishing a little fort which was on that coast. Kiraiscaqui, whose corps consists of about 700 men, has ravaged everything on his way from Salamis to Phalerum. He reduced Megara to ashes, without any reason being known until this time for so cruel conduct towards unfortunate countrymen who had already suffered so much. The position of Lavicier becomes every day more critical. Every day he causes guns to be fired for aid, but no chief is willing to undertake a mission which cost the unfortunate Bourjacqui his life, that is, to attack with soldiers who take to flight at the first shot. News from Napoli and Hydra offer nothing interesting. The first of these places has become a sort of hospital, where the women and children flock, who are driven from their homes. Misery is there at its height. Constantinople, March 12. A motion was made by Mr. Floyd to lay the communication on the table. Mr. Kramer rose amid some excitement, and called for the reading of it. The Speaker decided that it was in order for any member to demand the reading. After some conversation, Mr. Kramer withdrew his motion, when Mr. Wakefield renewed it, believing that the House could not act on any communication, correctly, without an understanding of its import. The clerk then proceeded to read the communication, and after he had completed about one half of it, Mr. Wakefield withdrew his motion, which Mr. Moore, of Alabama, renewed, when the whole of it was read. Mr. Tucker, of Va. moved the printing of the communication, and went on to make some remarks upon its language and spirit. Mr. Webster observed, his first wish was to preserve the dignity and character of the House in a matter so delicate as the present. He said one of two courses presented itself to the consideration of the House, either to order the printing of the communication, or to refer it to a select committee. He was in favor of a reference of the whole subject to a select committee, as the communication had already been read. If any gentleman would move such a reference, he would give it his support.

I avail myself of this mode of calling the attention of Preceptors of Academies and Instructors of schools in general to a little volume lately published at Keene, N. H. entitled “A history of the United States."—It is designed for the use of schools: and is certainly well calculated for the purpose and is by far the best which has ever been published.—It deserves the public attention. The history of each of the states is given from its discovery and first settlement; and that of the United States from the formation of the general government to the present time. All provincial and important facts are preserved—wisely selected—well arranged and clearly stated. The volume also contains a brief compendium of the principal Empires and States in the world: with a series of historical ques- tions, &c.

We understand that the subject of connecting the waters of Lake Winnepissiogee with those of the Piscataqua by a canal, is in serious contemplation, and will probably be taken into consideration at the next General Court. It would be of immense advantage to the citizens of this State generally, and particularly to the extensive factories established at the head of our navigable waters. New-York has set us a noble example, and it is hoped that our State Legislature will liberally patronize an undertaking, which would be of so great public utility. Portf. Gaz. Adventures of Lt. Baker- — He writes from Trinidad de Cuba, stating that he had been a lieutenant in the Colombian service; that he sailed from Charleston, last Oct., on a cruise, and captured several Spanish vessels, one of which he was ordered to carry to Cartagena; that off Cape Antonio he was captured by the pirate schooner Clara.

It only remains for us to declare the sentence of the law—which is, and this court doth accordingly adjudge, that you be carried from hence to the prison from whence you came—and from thence to the place of Execution—and there be hanged by the neck until you shall be dead. And may God of his infinite grace have mercy upon your soul. After pronouncing the last word, the austerity and firmness of the Judge gave way to the feelings of the man; he was most sensibly affected; for some moments he was absorbed in tears, and his sobs were audible to those who were near him. Many of the audience were equally affected. But the prisoner stood, steadfast and immovable—there was not the slightest change of countenance—no tears—and not the least emotion. He appeared like a statue of adamant. When about leaving the bar where he had received the awful sentence, which doomed him to death on the very next time he would be permitted to leave his gloomy dungeon, he took from his pocket his quid of tobacco, and placed it in his mouth, with as much apparent coolness as if he had been the disinterested spectator of the solemn scene. The prisoner was remanded, as soon as the sentence was pronounced, and the Court was adjourned to the second Tuesday of November next, when the trial of Joseph J. Knapp, Jr. and George Crowninshield will take place.

The fifth demand is the complete restoration of the Greek churches, and re-establishment of the Greek religion. What the expense that would attend a compliance with this demand would be we have no means of conjecturing. But that out of the question, the nature of the demand, we should imagine, would be very unpalatable. The faith of the Koran prohibits all communion between the Mussulman and the Infidel. It would certainly be a strange spectacle to see the Turks engaged in building Christian churches in the midst of their own capital, and extensively over their dominions; or even furnishing the means for such an unauthorized purpose. Distinction, of everything opposed to the Mahometan creed, whether sacred or profane, is much more congenial to the Ottoman character, and we should think such a demand as this would be submitted to with great reluctance. The sixth and last item of the catalogue of tergiversations is ?, public apology to Baron Stroganoff for the continued insults which the Porte has offered him. The last exhibition of contempt and insult which the Turkish government show to a hostile power has usually been to imprison, or otherwise ill-treat.

Now was a noble opportunity for the great and powerful to throw off the fetters of luxury, indolence and dissipation, and resume the vigorous mind and manly bearing of their ancestors. The chain of superstition, which had bound them hand and foot to Roman domination, had suffered many jarring shocks. Though closely welded, the iron links had begun to yield. By a determined effort they might have been entirely severed. Nor was Spain destitute of great men, but their projects were mostly thwarted by the effeminacy of a degenerate nobility and corrupted people. France, in this period, was in the highest state of internal fermentation. The commotions produced by the Bull Unigenitus were not yet subdued. By one party it was still regarded as an article of faith and a law of the state. In defense of it they dared to assail the very principles of civil society, and to refuse the protection of the laws and the sacraments of religion, to all who appealed from its authority. But the time was passed when the "Sacred Congregation of the most eminent and most reverend cardinals of the Holy Roman Church—inquisitors general in the whole Christian Republic against heretics" could pronounce with effect the sentence of the greater excommunication against all those enemies of the Church who should dare to pray in French or read the life of an appellant deacon.

In speaking of Mr. Adams, I tread on party ground; but although my principles and feelings upon this ground have always been definite and decisive, yet I have never been conscious of any political virulence of spirit, or even uncharitableness. As a disciple of the Jeffersonian School, I have ever viewed, and do still view, the Federal Party with a suspicious eye; but, in relation to a vast majority of that party, I thus viewed them, not as men, but as partisans; aiding, through the deceptive influence of party spirit, at first, an unreasonable, then an unnatural, and finally a cruel opposition to their own government. But still, I have never doubted that the great mass of the Federalists were patriotic, and, in heart, Republican; and in view of both parties, I have ever made a distinction between the real friends of our Republican institutions, and the advocates of aristocratic principles of government. I have known Republicans who were Aristocrats, and Federalists who were Republicans.

REPORT ON NAVIGATION LAWS. Mr. King, of New York, from the committee on Foreign Relations, presented the following Report: The committee on Foreign Relations, to whom were referred the Memorial of R. Appleby and others, of the Colleton District, South Carolina, and the Resolutions of the Chamber of Commerce of the City of Baltimore, praying for the repeal of the laws closing the ports of the United States against British vessels employed in the trade between the United States and the British Colonies in the West Indies, Report: That referring to the period between the completion of the Revolution and the adoption of the Constitution, it cannot be doubted that the embarrassments of the agriculture, trade, and navigation of the several states were truly ascribed to the want of power in Congress to make adequate laws for their encouragement and protection; and no motive in favor of the adoption of the Constitution, was more strongly or more generally felt than the opinion, that the vesting of power in Congress to regulate trade, would serve to promote the welfare and prosperity of the Union. The new government, under the Constitution, very soon experienced the influence created by the extraordinary changes that were taking place in France, and which, in the sequel, engaged all Europe in arms.

In the course of my travels, I have seen many a promising young man gradually led to dissipation, gambling, ruin, merely by the want of means to make a solitary evening pass pleasantly. I earnestly advise every youth that quits that abode of purity, peace and delight, his parental home, to acquire a taste for reading and writing.

We were considerably struck at first, both of us; and when he found out I was going to eat dinner with Mr. ----- and the General Court, he thought it queer kind of doings— but says he, I guess it will be as well for both of us not to know each other a bit more than we can help. And says I with a wink, you’re half right, and in I went. There was nobody in the room but Mr. and his wife, and not a sign of any dinner to be seen anywhere—though I thought now and then when a side door opened, I could smell cupboard, as they say. I thought I should be puzzled enough to know what to say, but I hadn't my thoughts long to myself. Mr. has about as nimble a tongue as you ever heard, and could say ten words to my one, and I had nothing to do in the way of making talk. When he found out that I was from Hiram, he seemed to be at a loss to know what part of Massachusetts that was, and I didn't think it worth a while to tell him without consulting Stephen. Just then I heard a ringing, and Stephen was busy opening the door and letting in the General Court, who all had their hats off and looking pretty scrumptious, you may depend. I didn't see.

Under date of the 20th of August, we learn from Panama that an Envoy from Greece to the Liberator had arrived in that city, to offer him two hundred vessels, the greater part of them armed, 4000 sailors, and to request of him a grant of land in return, to be occupied by the families who are about to emigrate, in consequence of their recent ill success, and the cruelties of the Turks. The behavior of the Envoy is so perfectly moderate, and he describes the distress of his country so pathetically, that the greatest interest has been excited in his favor. A decree signed by Francisco de P. Santander, dated Bogota, September 22nd, has been issued, enjoining the army and navy and public officers of Colombia, to wear a knot of crape on the left arm, out of respect to the memory of Thomas Jefferson and John Adams. M. D. Professor of Surgery and the Theory and Practice of Physic; John F. Wells, M. D. Professor of Anatomy and Physiology; and Parker Cleaveland, A. M. Professor of Chemistry and Materia Medica.—The Professor of Anatomy is now in Europe, and will make extensive purchases for the benefit of the School. His return is expected in season for the next Course of Lectures. Several boxes of preparations and books have already been received from France; and the Library will be very much enlarged this autumn. The particular management of this School is committed to the Faculty of Medicine, who, in connection with a Board of Censors from the Medical Society of Maine, examine candidates for the Degree of Doctor in Medicine, and recommend to the Corporation of the College for that degree. In order to obtain it, two full courses of Medical lectures must have been attended, the last of which, at least, must have been at Brunswick; three years must have been devoted to professional studies; and some knowledge of Latin, and of Experimental Philosophy must have been acquired. The price of a ticket, admitting to the Lectures of one Professor, is fifteen dollars; or forty-five dollars for all the Lectures. The course begins about the middle of February, and continues three months.—The usual price of board is one dollar and seventy-five cents a week. NEWS SUMMARY.—FOREIGN.

A reward of $100 had already been offered by the Mayor for the detection of the incendiaries.

MT 14 HURLBERTON & SONS, vkt 11 f.J!!.ING ACADEMY.—The fall term of the Wheeling Academy will commence on Monday the 14th of September. Owing to the difficulty experienced in obtaining school-rooms suitable for a large number, the principal will be obliged to restrict his number of pupils to those pursuing the advanced studies. The subscriber regrets that he will be unable to receive those of his former scholars in the male department, who are pursuing only the common branches of an English education, as his arrangements make it necessary for him to confine his attention to the higher studies. He has associated with him Mr. Irving, under whose direction, will take charge of the Mathematical and Scientific department. A plan has been contracted, and every facility will be furnished to those who wish to qualify themselves for entering College, or to pursue any of the Collegiate studies. Tuition—for the Latin, Greek and French languages; Algebra, Geometry, and the higher Mathematics; Moral Philosophy, Chemistry, and Political Economy, etc., $10 per term of eleven weeks; those wishing to take both of these studies will pay per term. In the Female department pupils will be received in Arithmetic.

There is no doubt that the depositions upon which the prosecutions against the Spaniards were found, were procured by gross fraud—whether by collusion with the Huzzard interpreter, or in some other way, remains to be shown. But the crime of perjury has been committed, and justice demands that the offender should be punished. A circumstance occurred among the Africans a few evenings since, that is worth relating, as going to illustrate the peculiar system of ethics that prevails with them. As Col. Pendleton was about locking up for the night, he observed an extraordinary excitement among the blacks. They were all talking at once, and gesticulating with hands and feet with astonishing energy and effect. They clustered around the jailor, and in their eagerness to explain the trouble, jabbered so fast and composedly that he could not understand them at all. After enforcing silence, he inquired of Hannah what was the matter? "Tiefy, tiefy, in de cellar," was the reply. Col. Pendleton then descended to the cellar, taking along with him Hannah and Kaen. Nothing looked suspicious here.

Maine.—The Bangor Advertiser states that on Thursday last it snowed there through the day without cessation, but the snow melted as fast as it came down. The stage, however, from Charlestown on Friday came on runners, and the driver reported that in the vicinity of Charlestown, there was twenty inches of snow. Large quantities of snow had fallen at Houlton. An accident happened in the Iron Foundry, on Thursday afternoon, in consequence of some damp sand getting into the mold in which two men were casting a wheel. The moment the hot iron touched the sand it exploded, throwing the iron into their faces, injuring them badly. It is thought each of them will lose the sight of one of their eyes. — Bath Me. In the course of a debate in the Senate, a few days ago, Mr. Forsyth took occasion to say that Elihu Chauncey, Esq., the Chairman of the Philadelphia Committee who lately made a report of their interview with the President, was one of the most bitter and cantankerous politicians that ever existed. The epithet was misunderstood by Mr. Clayton, who called Mr. Forsyth to account for terming Mr. Chauncey a cankered or rancorous politician. Mr. Forsyth replied that the gentleman had grossly misrepresented him: that cankered was a very strong phrase, and he had not employed it. The expression which he had employed was that made use of by Tony Lumpkin to his sweetheart — he had said that Mr. Chauncey was a cantankerous politician. He had known Mr. Chauncey for many years, and knew that no man was more amiable in private life. Nevertheless, he was a cantankerous politician.

And, by a reference to the names of those who voted on the motion made on Sunday, the 3rd of March, it will be seen, that of those who voted in the affirmative, there were SIXTY WHIGS, three Conservatives and ELEVEN VAN BUREN MEN and, that of those who voted in the negative, there were FIFTY-TWO VAN BUREN MEN, five Conservatives, and TWELVE WHIGS. This statement, taken from the record, requires no comment to give it significance. Those who have charged the Whig party with a design to defeat the bill for political purposes, will see in it, as in a mirror, not only the refutation of their base calumny, but the reflected image of their own shameless profligacy, will, doubtless, astonish the supporters of the Administration, in this quarter of the country, to learn for the first time that their friends in Congress are opposed to the National Road, in the proportion of nearly five to one. And, when they remember the number, which will not soon be forgotten, they cannot fail to be as much surprised as disgusted, at the indiscretion and audacity of their organs, in charging their adversaries with the loss of the bill. The vote, on the final passage of the bill, last year, was ayes, 100; noes, 63.

Mr. Calhoun pretends to be quite enamored of the principles avowed in Mr. Madison’s Report, and in the decision of the Supreme Court of Pennsylvania, in the case of Cobbett —which, as is well known to all who know anything of the political history of the period in which they had their origin, are diametrically opposed to the doctrine of constitutional construction by implication, by the admission of which only can the charter of the U. States Bank, and the great schemes of Internal Improvement by the General Government be sustained. We have now before us two speeches of Mr. Calhoun’s; one delivered in the House of Representatives in 1816, in opposition to the motion made by John Randolph to strike from the land bill the minimum valuation of Cotton Goods, and the other delivered the year afterwards (1817) on a bill introduced by himself, "to set apart and pledge, as a permanent fund for Internal Improvement, the bonus of the National Bank, and the U. States’ share of its dividends." We beg leave to submit a few extracts from each—and thereby to make Mr. Calhoun his own assailant: I excerpt from Mr. Calhoun's Speech, in 1816, against Mr. Randolph's motion to strike out the minimum valuation of Cotton Goods.

I simply drop you a line to say all is right. I take it that all fear, or doubt, or apprehension on the part of our friends in Washington and all the State, are dispelled. Look at the estimate of the Loco Foco. It is a cognovit. You and I can count their counties, and change their majority into minority. I begin to think the Whig majority in this State will be nearer 20,000 than 15,000. As to this district, I am confident it will increase its majority over 1838 from 600 to 800, and in this opinion I am below most of our friends. The people do not seem to care about men it is principle for which they are contending. And they are determined to hold all those who have deceived them to a rigid and strict account. The days of Benton, Calhoun, Van Buren, and the whole crew, are numbered. This State would give a majority of 15,000 against Van Buren if every other State in the Union were to go against him. A Sign in Georgia: An election lately took place in Savannah, Georgia, for City officers. The contest was a very animated one. The highest Whig candidate came within 52 votes of the lowest on the Loco Foco ticket; and the average majority against the Whigs was 81. In 1838, the Loco Foco majority was 139 votes.
