At sunset a gentleman arrived, and gave information that he had seen the stage across the road, having no horses attached, at the 7 mile bridge, and stated that on riding within about 100 yards of it he saw several articles in the road in disorder; at the same time his horse became restive, snorted, and exhibited symptoms of terror, and justly concluding that all was not right, turned his horse's head and came to the city by another road. The gentleman did not mention this fact, fearing he might give rise to unnecessary alarm until some anxiety had been expressed at the cause of the non-arrival of the mail from St. Mary's via Pablo, when he mentioned what he had seen to the Postmaster. This did not become generally known until about 10 o'clock, when it was communicated to the Mayor T. L. Doggy, who promptly ordered Corporal Cereopon and 10 men (who had been placed under his command by Col. Twiggs) to proceed immediately to the spot to ascertain the facts, and to proceed onward, should there be necessity to give protection to the mail road. The detachment on reaching the 7 mile branch, discovered the carriage in the position described, and the body of the driver named Geo. W.

They go in gangs of 100 or less, have a secret understanding by signs, they live in their locks' bond without shedding one drop of blood. The unsuspecting traveler who has been lured by their pretended hospitality, is suddenly strangled by an expert cord puller seated at his elbow, and in converse with him. Every traveler of the group is thus despatched the same instant by concerted signal, and the bodies are concealed. This garroting or strangling, and the concealment, partakes of the Spanish or Indian The Thugs riot on the plunder, and dress gorgeously. They disdain the name of robber. They are sometimes known as Phansigars and Budhcks. If a traveler's path is crossed by a snake while accompanied by a Thug the sign is bad; and the murder suspended till a favorable sign. Maine gives a bounty of five cents on each pound of cocoons raised in the state, and 59 cents for each pound of silk reeled in the state. Connecticut gives a bounty of $1 for each 100 mulberry trees of five years' growth, and 50 cents on each pound of reeled silk, the growth of the state. Vermont gives a bounty on silk of 10 cents on each pound grown in the state. Pennsylvania gives a bounty of 20 cents on each pound of cocoons, and 50 cents on each pound of reeled silk, the growth of the state. This is our remedy for the pressure—encourage production. From Ecauri. If then the bituminous coal from Cumberland can be delivered at tide, for this sum, of course it can be transported coastwise to all our Atlantic ports and towns cheaper than it can be obtained from any other part of the world; and if so, can there be any assignable limit to the demand? Let us see whether this position is sustained by facts. The cost of transporting coal from Philadelphia to Washington, (as a regular business and not a ballast,) is $1.50 per ton, 5 cents per bushel; to Baltimore, coastwise or by railroad from Point of Rocks, 4 cents per bushel; to Boston $2 per ton, or 7 cents per bushel, and it may be carried to Charleston, or the most distant of our seaports for 8 cents per bushel, which is more than is received by importers from Liverpool. Apply these facts, and the cost of Cumberland coal will be in our principal cities as follows, viz: In Washington, Alexandria, and Georgetown, (per bushel) 12.5 cts. Present price $7 per ton, or 25 Saving. 12.5 Add, then, to this price, in the District, freights to Baltimore, 4 cents; Philadelphia, 5 cents; and Boston, 7 cts: and there will be a saving in the price, from the present rates in those cities, of 8.5 cents to Baltimore, 7.5 to Philadelphia, and 12.5 cents to Boston. Coal brings in the latter place $9 per ton, or 32.5 cents per bushel.

Gw56 THREE RIVERS MILLS. The proprietors inform the public that they have enlarged, and thoroughly overhauled and repaired their mill, and that no expense has been spared in producing a first-rate establishment, worthy the patronage of the community, and one which they believe, will prove entirely satisfactory. Three new run of the largest Burr stones have been put in, with new screens, fanning mill, bolts, smut machine, and all other necessary machinery of the latest and most approved kind, under the superintendence of Mr. John Beaumont, a Millwright of high reputation from Rochester. The company have also permanently engaged Mr. John Morton, a first-rate miller from Rochester, from whom customers may expect general satisfaction. The Mills being now in full operation, are ready to do packing or custom work to any extent. D. V. BELL, Agent St. Joseph Manufacturing Company. Centreville, July 19, 1837.

HORCLINE is a beautiful bay, full 15 hands 3 inches high, and is a sure foal-getter; has run and won many races in this State, Virginia and Maryland. When he left the turf, he was re garded as one of the best three-mile horses in the State, and two miles unequalled. At three years old, after winning the great stake at Balti more. (See Turf Register,) his owner was offered and refused five thousand dollars for him. His colts are generally very promising, partaking of the old Archy stock. His sire, are extremely docile and gentle, nearly all making good family horses. PEDIGREE. HER-CLINE was got by old Sir Archy, out of Georgiana. He is much indebted to Mr. MacGillivray's humanity, who had the kindness to send for a Surgeon who dressed his wounds, by which means he revived a little before he was carried to jail. This humane conduct on the part of Mr. MacGillivray offended Mr. Forbes very much, and of course displeased the tender-hearted Editor of the Herald. 29th April: The prisoners of the old War are brought here and shut up with us. On their way to this place they were greeted by some kind and brave Canadians, who, in spite of bayonets, feel yet sympathy for their fellow-countrymen, who suffer captivity for the common cause of liberty. Our joy is inexpressible, yet melancholy, at seeing our old friends. But we can hardly recognize them, so much are they altered by the sufferings of their long captivity. Tears are shed by both parties, but still our courage is undaunted, and our animosity against British slavery is still the same as ever. One of the new comers is said to have written the history of his captivity. If we can judge by their miserable appearance, the recital of their sufferings must certainly excite the sympathy of the readers. 9th May.

INCREASE OF POPULATION - In the last ten years the increase of population in Cincinnati has been 50 per cent, in Boston 35 per cent, and in Baltimore, 25 per cent. The population of Charleston has actually diminished. These facts indicate, by comparison, the rapid growth of the West, and its increasing power and influence in the Union. In twenty years from this, the Cincinnati Chronicle supposes that Cincinnati will contain a larger number of people than Baltimore. This calculation is based upon the fact that each of the cities has increased 20,000.

Sec. 2.—And be it enacted, That any person who shall make a donation, at any time, of a sum not less than fifty dollars, to the funds of the said Corporation, shall be a member of said Corporation for life, and any person who shall subscribe and pay to said funds a sum not less than five dollars annually, by such subscription and payment shall become a member so long as he con tinues to pay the same.  SEC. 3.—And be it enacted, That there shall be a meeting of the members of the said Corporation, in the city of Baltimore, on the first Monday of March annually, after the year of our Lord eighteen hundred and forty-one, for the purpose of electing, by ballot, from their number, a President, Vice President, Secretary, Treasurer, and nine Directors, who, collectively, shall be called the Board of Directors; which officers shall hold their respective offices for one year, and until others shall be elected to succeed them, except in case of vacancy by death, or resignation: in which case it shall be the duty of the Board of Directors to fill the vacancy by an election from the members aforesaid, so soon as conve niently may be.

Hide us—aye! crush us from the face of Him Who sitteth on the throne, and from the Lamb. For, lo! his day of vengeance is arrived, And who can hope to stand?’ MONSIEUR CHAUBERT—THE FIRE KING. Who has not heard of the Fire King?—the moral lover of poisons? The epicure in arsenic and phos phorus? the tippler in boiling Florence oil? The celebrated Monsieur Chaubert who uses melted lead to wash his hands? and warms himself in an oven alongside a beefsteak? Who has astonished all England, has arrived in this city, and is exhibiting his experiments in Clinton Hall. He is certainly the eighth wonder of the world—the real salamun der to whom fire, heat, poison, &c. are perfectly innocuous. On Thursday evening last, he gave a private ex hibition of his wonderful powers to a select audience of Scientific and literary gentlemen at the lecture room of Clinton Hall. On entering the Hall the first thing that strikes the eyes of the spectator is a large oven built of bricks and resting on the floor of the building. In the front of the oven is a small platform with a table, lights, &c. where Monsieur Chaubert performs his experiments.

The following anecdote of the Duke of Wellington, not generally known, exhibits in a strong light the indomitable perseverance and foresight of the duke, and especially exonerates his grace from the charge raised against him by some writers, of allowing himself to be outpaced by Bonaparte while amusing himself at Brussels. At a dinner, a short time ago, the duke was asked, "Has your grace seen the pamphlet published in America by General Grouchy, in answer to General Foy's attack on his report on the operations of the French army on the day previous to Waterloo?" "I have," answered the duke, "and Grouchy has the best of it; He could not move without orders, and orders he certainly did not receive. As to his leanings, none knew them. I was a witness to them." "You," exclaimed one of the party, "every one thought that your grace was in Brussels." "I know they did; but they were wrong; for on the evening in question, I and Gordon, (who was killed at Waterloo) left Brussels, took a position on the as escort, no one knowing us, and joined the Prussian headquarters. I passed the whole night in conference with Blucher, York, and Kleist. In the morning I observed to Blucher, "If I had an enemy army in the position in which yours now is, I should expect to be attacked immediately." The attack of Grouchy soon after commenced, and the Prussians were defeated.

Extract of a Letter received at Miami City, dated St. Augustine, Feb. 2, "Since my last, nothing has occurred here which is in the least degree worthy of communicating. Yesterday, Captain Merchant of the United States army, arrived here in command of his Company, and has superseded Capt. Porter in command of the troops now here. This has of course added to the security of the inhabitants, and I believe that the force now here has almost quieted the apprehensions of the people in this vicinity. We have occasionally some flying reports of the Indians hovering between this and Picolata, but their habitual caution keeps them off from a post as well defended as this, and if they were mad enough to make an attack, it would dispense with the force now collecting from all quarters, for most certainly we should give such a good account of these troublesome gentlemen, that no further danger would be apprehended from them. Since our arrival, we all have received the most marked politeness and attention from the inhabitants." So that the question is narrowed down to this point, - how can the right of making the representative of value and of dealing in it be thrown open to ensure the greatest good? The New York law, to our mind, contains the essence of this matter with but few exceptions. The simpler the restrictions the better, and we doubt not, but that the general law of 1838, in that state will be modified, so that every child who can read, may understand it. The defect of that law, to us, appears to be in the regulation of admitting bonds and mortgages on real estate as cash securities at so high a rate. The appropriation of property at the market value should not cover more than one-third the amount in cash. Suppose the legislature at its present session, were to adopt a general Bank law, what ought to be its principal features? In the first place, the right to form associations under it ought not to be limited as to the duration of them. Let any man or set of men say, that he or they will continue to bank for ten, twenty, one hundred or one thousand years under this law, only make it imperative on him or them to state the period of termination.

We cannot refrain from giving our readers the entire, the following heart-stirring account of the late Convention of the people at Columbus, Ohio. It is estimated that more than 20,000 persons were present on the occasion. Thus progresses the good cause in the whole west. [Correspondence of the Boston Atlas.] Columbus, Ohio, Feb. 24, 1840. THE OHIO STATE CONVENTION of the friends of HARRISON and TYLER, came off here on the 21st and 22nd. There was probably never before so great a turn out, such unanimity of thought, such universal enthusiasm, at any political meeting in the U.S. In giving a description there is no danger of exaggeration. You must bear in mind that Columbus is near the center of the State, from 100 to 175 miles from either boundary—that the weather was in transition from winter to spring—that the roads were all kind, deep mud, nothing but interminable unmitigated mud—that money was hard to be got for any purposes—that Harrison has been derided as the "LOG CABIN CANDIDATE," fond of "HARD CIDER," and so forth. On the morning previous to the first day of the Convention, it began steadily to rain, and the people began to come in by delegations. The Hotels were all filled by 10 o’clock in the forenoon. Soon crowds collected along the streets and about the public houses.

The soldiers were engaged, man to man, up to 10 o’clock at night and the slaughter was immense. The Poles are said to have lost 4,000 men, and the Russians 14,000 to 16,000. In the results, the Russians were repulsed; and the Poles, not deeming it necessary to their purpose to recommence the strife, rested for the night of the 26th upon the field of battle. Next day Skrzynecki led his troops to Pultusk, and thence to Praga, where they can take some repose after such great labors. In their retreat they were unmolested by the barbarians, and the cannon, and other munitions of war, were brought off in the best order. No prisoners have been lost. The Imperial Guards, part of the Archduke Michael's division, were the troops principally engaged and they are admitted by the Poles to have fought with great bravery. The Polish General, in his dispatch, frankly admits the losses which he has sustained, but his tone is anything but despairing. The loss of men, and of two valuable general officers, is considerable; but his retreat was pre-intended, and the object with which he set out was already accomplished. The two generals are understood to have a successful lodgment in Lithuania. The people of Warsaw were prepared for the worst, and had not given themselves up to despair. The next arrival from these quarters must bring intelligence of importance.

Splendid Steel Engravings, prepared by Mr. A. Dick, ornament the work one of which accompanies each number. These plates are entirely new, and are engraved at a heavy expense by one of the best artists in America, "expressly for the magazine. It is with pride the proprietor announces that the Ladies' Companion is the only magazine published, in which new and elegant steel plates appear regularly. Those accompanying other monthly periodicals, are generally first worn out in annuals. In addition to the engravings mentioned, a correct plate of the Quarterly Fashions for Ladies will appear in the June, September, December, and March numbers, independent of the usual embellishment. It is the determination of the proprietor, that these fashion plates shall appear in a style hitherto unknown. Its literary character will undergo no change, as it will remain under the charge of the same Editors as heretofore.

In expressing our determination to support Mr. Clay, we must not be understood as derogating in any degree from the high merits of the other gentlemen named. Their characters, their services, their actions are before the world, and speak out for them in a voice that cannot be silenced. But, it is undeniable, that at the present juncture, Mr. Clay is the favorite of the South, and so far as North Carolina is concerned, is the only one, of the three, who can command her electoral vote. Raleigh Register. The Emancipator says that "many of the supporters of the Administration are abolitionists." This is not true, if he means that many in proportion to the whole Democratic Party at the North are so. Recent events have confirmed our opinion that the Presidential contest would be carried on with abolition principles as a test, and the few of the democratic party, joined to the conservatives, have given the "whigs" majorities, where they would have been defeated but for this question. All New England, except New Hampshire, will probably go for Clay and the abolitionists.

The fact is that the revenue will not be wanted for the constitutional use of the Government will be no restraint. We saw, from 1831 to 1832, that the duties were almost annually raised, notwithstanding the charges upon the treasury were continually decreased, and, notwithstanding, too, the active and ever angry opposition waged by the South. With these convictions, Mr. Speaker, I cannot vote in favor of an absolute and unconditional distribution. I would sooner at once vote in favor of an increase of the Tariff, because the effect would be the same, and the latter course would be more open and manly. Besides, the resolution of the Committee will be more likely to produce the effect, because the measure it proposes stands a better chance of success, as it addresses itself to the cupidity of the States. Now, Sir, I ask if there is be any one here who is willing to see the Tariff controversy opened again? When it was the only source of discord between the different sections of this country, the Union was scarce strong enough to stand it. But since then another and a far more direful one has arisen.

For a while my whole attention was absorbed by the just and glowing picture of infamy and avarice and more than savage cruelty held up to the Senate by the orator, and an inexpressible sympathy for the oppressed and afflicted Indians had choked my own utterance and bedimmed my vision; when, seeking relief for my fullness from the painful contemplation of dishonor and suffering, I turned my eyes across the Chamber, and lo! there sat two of the Cherokee females whom I described to you some weeks since, striving to restrain their tears, and to listen with dignified composure to the story of their wrongs. They were not always successful, for the tears would flow in spite of them. But distressed as they were—agonized as they were, I felt for the time, and so expressed myself to an acquaintance, that I would rather be those two Cherokee women, participating as they did, in the sympathies of all who heard Mr. Clay, than the two Georgia Senators, whose State he exposed to the abhorrence and detestation of the civilized world. — New-York Commercial Advertiser. The Archbishop, half afraid of the indignation of the French Ministry, by whose order a more liberal bishop was sent to perform the sacred rite in question, and by whom arrangements have been made to receive the body in the church after death, but, unable to conquer the prejudices of education and habit, calls upon the clergy under his control to enable him to reconcile the duties of his office, and those required by his country, by uniting with him in entreating from the mercy of God one of those marks of grace (in favor, not presume, of the dying Constitutionalist,) which touch, calm, and tranquillize. Mr. Chittenden, of New York, moved a resolution, calling on the Post-master General for information in relation to the transportation of the mails between Washington and the city of Philadelphia, since the 31st day of December, the way they have been brought, and the causes of delay that have attended them. Mr. Monroe, of the city of New York, offered a separate resolution, calling on the Post-master General to know if any action, and if any, what action, was necessary to facilitate the progress of the mails between the cities of Philadelphia and Washington. Mr. Wm. Pinkney, of Maryland, moved a resolution to instruct the Committee on Public Lands to enquire into the expediency of distributing the public domain among the states, for the purposes of education. Mr. Waddy Thompson moved a resolution, calling on the Secretary of State to furnish information in relation to the causes that led the American domestic slaver, the Utopia.

The affairs of Canada are in a sort of abeyance, except when temporary attention is moved to the subject by some petty discussion about the exact quantum of despatches or documents which shall be produced to or withheld from Parliament. It is believed that ministers have no definite project prepared with respect to Canada, and that they wish to delay promulgating any as long as possible, in order to take advantage of the chapter of accidents. The affair of the Maine Boundary has not created much excitement, and it is thought may be amicably settled, as far as two supreme governments can settle it on paper, for it must be confessed the borderers seem disposed to do almost as they please. We are as a nation not at all disposed to quarrel with the United States on this or any other matter, without very sufficient cause, the commercial relations between us being too large and too important to be lightly jeopardized. We have, as you will perceive, increased our naval and also our military establishments, (the latter by 5000 men) but this is only to provide for the increased demands in Canada and India, and not from any apprehension of war. The Corn Law question is settled for the present; the averages have been gradually declining for some weeks, and it is expected that the duty will be in a week hence 2s. 8d., and then 4s. 8d., if not 10s.

It is also agreed that the same liberty be extended to persons who are on board a free vessel, so that, although they be enemies to either party, they shall not be made prisoners, or taken out of that free vessel, unless they are soldiers, and in the actual service of the enemy. By the stipulation that the flag shall cover the property, the two contracting parties agree that this shall be so understood with respect to those powers who recognize this principle; but if either of the two contracting parties shall be at war with a third party and the other neutral, the flag of the neutral shall cover the property of enemies whose Governments acknowledge this principle, and not of others.  ARTICLE XVII.  It is likewise agreed that in the case where the neutral flag of one of the contracting parties shall protect the property of the enemies of the other, by virtue of the above stipulation, it shall always be understood that the neutral property found on board such enemies’ vessels shall be held and considered as enemies’ property, and as such shall be liable to detention and confiscation, except such property as was put on.

A Fourth of July speech begins with - "Ladies, Soldiers, and Citizens!" The speaker would have done better by prefixing to each of these words the appellation of "Fellow." Prentice says: - "Is it not rather a curious fact that, in nine cases out of ten, a slanderer of the Blacks is a swindler of the Blacks?" Nevertheless, the fact is well substantiated. One example will suffice - the Washington Whig received a letter from the family of L. McHargue, in Dutchess County, New York, inquiring whether this person, who was scalded on the George Collier, is still living, or whether he died. This is to ask for any information that can be given relative to him. Was he carried to the hospital, or where was he placed after the explosion? A reply is earnestly requested for the sake of his mother and sister.

Silver sixpence.—"Do you see here," said Theodore to a group of gaily dressed urchins, as he came up from Market Street wharf in Philadelphia, "do you see here, I've got a silver sixpence!" said Jeremiah Budd, whose father was a wealthy shipmaster, "I have six dollars to spend on Christmas, and that fellow is proud of a sixpence!" Theodore heard, and looking thoughtfully upon the ground for a moment, then recollecting himself, "six dollars to spend"—muttered he, "but sixpence to keep is better than that." He did keep his sixpence in his pocket, carefully wrapped up, for several weeks; when one day his uncle, who keeps a fruit shop at the corner of the alley where he lived, said to him, "Theodore, your sixpence don't grow in your pocket, you know." The boy understood him better when he told him he might buy some fruit in the market with it, and stand in his shop and sell it out again. He embraced the offer, doubled his money the first day, and went on until he had as much fruit to sell as he had room for in his little corner. His uncle, observing the thrifty, and withal honest turn of the boy, finally took him into his store as an assistant, and allowed him to trade sundry specified articles on his own account.

He also states that he then came to advise me against accepting a seat in the cabinet, admitting that the charges made against my family were false, but representing "what use the opposition would make of it," and that "the enemies of the President would not fail to make a handle of it." He says that he placed Mrs. Jackson and Mrs. Eaton on the same footing, and desired to save the President "from recollections which would be painful and distressing." Mr. Branch has a treacherous recollection. He kept no notebook, or, like his copartner, Mr. Ingham, he has accommodated his notes to emergencies. I can put him right in this affair, not doubting his admission of the truth of the narration I offer, if honor be left him, although he may deny the motive which I feel persuaded influenced him at the time. Failing in the attempt to prevent my appointment, and to dissuade or rather deter me from accepting, Mr. Branch was next made the instrument of a piece of secret management, having in view the same result. It was suggested to the President, after the first arrangement of the Cabinet was made, that Mr. M'Lean entertained objections against remaining in the Post Office Department. It was known that the President was disposed to gratify him by placing him in another Department, if he could do so with a proper regard to others previously selected.

The Neptune. - We have had an opportunity of conversing with several gentlemen, who were on board the Neptune during the severe gale she encountered on Saturday. We learn from them that the scene was the most affecting and heartrending that they ever beheld. From noon on Saturday till 12 at night scarcely any hope of life remained. Death seemed inevitable. The sea was running mountains high, and the boat was sometimes lifted to an angle of forty-five degrees. A gentleman with whom we conversed, contrary to the received acceptation, attributes the preservation of the Hunt to her great length astride of three seas at one time.

Who Killed Cock Robin? An interesting debate is now going on in some of the news papers, whether Col. Richard M. Johnson of Kentucky, killed Tecumseh at the battle of the Moravian towns? It is admitted that he shot an Indian with his rifle, but whether it was the great native warrior of the northwest is disputed. The reason why the question is important just now, is that the Jackson party are to have a caucus on the 4th of March next, to nominate a Vice President in place of Mr. Calhoun; and Col. Johnson, besides several other gentlemen, including Van Buren, has been named for the office. Gen Jackson’s qualifications for the Presidency are fully established. He killed some hundreds of unresisting Indians at the bend of the Tallapoosa, and fought two duels and the two Scotch traders whom he mistook for prisoners, besides shooting a lot of militiamen for going home when their time was out. This certainly proves that he is a man of course qualified for the highest civil office. Now if it can be proved that Col. Johnson actually killed Tecumseh, then it shows that he also is a hero, and ought to be placed in the second office. His mulatto daughters would not probably relish with Bellona, [Mrs. Eaton] as the proud family of Calhoun did. But how is Van Buren to manage?

Notwithstanding however much we would all espouse the improvements, I have recently noticed in the public prints, yet, as the facts there presented, I should consider it utterly impossible to get a dollar subscribed here at present. But upon the contrary, these efforts may not only turn out to be premature, but by bringing before the public, works that would detract from the profits of the one that has so long engaged the attention of the State, place it for years beyond our power to accomplish either. Everything here now is prosperous in relation to the main work, to the full extent of our most sanguine expectations; because they believe it will be the exclusive work of the State for very many years. Had we not better get one work under a full headway first? and by projecting thus early others, may we not be jeopardizing the whole? I will not decide; but on this ground will venture in frankness to say—beware—let us not be thought chimerical. I write this from a deep conviction of the danger to be apprehended here where we only can look for aid. No reports were made, but a Special Council of Physicians appointed, consisting of seven, whose duty it was to examine and ascertain the existence of cholera in the city. On Wednesday, (that is, for 24 hours to noon, and so each day,) the Board reported 9 cases and 4 deaths. On Thursday, 20 new cases and 11 deaths. On Friday, 37 cases and 22 deaths. On Saturday, 53 cases and 12 deaths. On Sunday, 42 cases and 21 deaths. And on Monday, 105 cases, including 57 at the Bellevue Hospital, and 27 deaths. We are sorry to learn, from private sources, that the public confidence in the Board of Health in the city is very greatly impaired in consequence of their difficulty with the medical profession. In Albany, also, the cholera made its appearance as early as Tuesday of last week. There have been from 5 to 13 cases per day since reported, and about half as many deaths. On the latest day from which we have accounts, Monday last, there were 15 cases and five deaths. The disease here, however, as well as in New York, appears rather more manageable than in Canada. Reports are current that there have been cases at Troy, Catskill, and some other places—but as yet we have no positive accounts of its being in any of them.

New “Holy Alliance."—The late accounts from Washington inform us that last week "Pilate and Herod were made friends"—in other words that the princes of locofocoism and nullification, Martin Van Buren and John C. Calhoun, formed a new “Holy Alliance;" offensive and defensive, to carry on their operations in Congress, and secure the passage of the sub-treasury. This alliance is to continue as long as the interests of the chiefs and their vassals will enable them to keep together without quarreling with unusual bitterness. It is said that the day the treaty was signed, the Prince of nullification, attended by those of his subordinates and retainers who are considered transferable, made his grand entree into the White House, where, over sundry bottles of good wine, and other good liquors, the pipe of conciliation was smoked and the hatchet buried. All present then shook hands round and parted as closely as other rogues when combined for the purpose of promoting their mutual aggrandizement at the expense of honest men. This is the first time Calhoun has darkened the doors of the Executive mansion since his quarrel with Gen. Jackson in 1830.

In order to do away with the effect naturally produced upon the public mind in consequence of disappointment in a scheme so important in its influences as that proposed to be carried out by the British and American Steam Navigation Company, several of the Directors and Proprietors determined upon chartering the steamship Sirius, a vessel of 700 tons and propelled by engines of 351 horsepower. She was a new ship - had been only six months at work - and had proved herself an excellent sea goer. On the 28th of February 1838, the Sirius, Steam Ship was accordingly advertised to leave London on the 28th of March and Cork on the 2nd of April for the port of New York. She was consigned to the care of Wadsworth & Smith, Merchants, Jones Lane, New York, and Lieutenant Richard Roberts, R.N. was assigned to the command of her. [We here omit the details of the departure of the Sirius, her passage, her arrival in New York and her return, as they are already well known to the public.] It was observed, when the Sirius returned, that all interest in the arrival of the letters had suddenly ceased. A month nearly was to elapse before they could bring any news. Steam and steam alone filled the public mind. A great demand was made for the British and American Steam Navigation Company's shares. The directors sold a large part of those they had reserved.

I suppose a man, on the whole, isn't hardly fit to be a democrat after he gets to be fifty years old. And here is old uncle Joshua in the Post Office, he's got to be about sixty, and he's hanging on to the democratic side yet, like the toothache, and it begins to worry me a good deal. I think it's high time he went over. You know Downieville has always been a genuine republican town, and I should always go according to the dictates (I think that's what they call it) of the democracy party. When it gets to be time for an old democrat to go over on the federal side, I believe the Argus always puts them over. You remember there was old Mr. Insley in Portland, and old General Wingate in Bath, as much as a dozen years ago, big republicans as there were some as were anywhere about. Well, they got to be considerable old, and had been in office some time, so the Argus took and clapped 'em right over on the federal side. And you know there was Mr. Holmes, he was a whopping great republican. But he began to grow old, so the Argus put him over. And there was Mr. Sprague; he was such a nice democrat everyone said it was a pity to put him over.

It was formerly the practice to estimate the value of animals by the size of their bones. A large bone was considered to be of great merit; a fine-boned animal always implied great size. It is known now that this doctrine was carried too far. The strength of the animal does not depend on the bones, but on the muscles; and when the bones are disproportionally large, it indicates in Cline's opinion, an imperfection in the organs of nutrition. Blakewell strongly insists on the advantage of small bones, and the celebrated John Hunter declared that small bones were always attended with corpulence in all the various subjects he had an opportunity of examining. A small bone, however, being heavier and more substantial, requires as much nourishment as a hollow one with a large circumference. Eloquence. The following is an extract from a speech delivered in the Indiana Legislature, on a bill to encourage the killing of wolves, which in sublimity has seldom been surpassed: "Mr. Speaker: The wolf is the most ferocious animal that prowls in our western prairies or runs at large in the forests of Indiana."

Late from England.—By the British barque Mary Catherine, arrived at Charleston, March 26, from Liverpool, whence she sailed on the 11th February, we have received London papers of the evening of the 12th, and Liverpool papers of the morning of the 11th ult. Matters appear to be yet quite unsettled on the continent. The Duke de Nemours, second son of Philip King of the French, had been elected King of Belgium, but the French Government have rejected the offer, thus throwing back on the Belgian Congress the question which they supposed was settled. The place was not filled at our latest dates. The British Parliament assembled on the 3rd of February. The Chancellor of the Exchequer has proposed to Parliament to lay a duty of one penny per pound on all raw Cottons imported, with a drawback duty to the same amount on all manufactured Cotton exported. In consequence of this proposition, the raw article had advanced 3-8d. in the Liverpool market. The great question of reform was to have been brought forward in Parliament on the 1st of March, by Lord John Russell, with the unanimous approbation of the Government. Although not a Cabinet Minister, he was selected for the task on account of his assiduous advocacy of the cause in unfavorable times.

Nov. 5, 1830. From the Rev C C P. Crosby, late agent of the American Colonies. To Dr. D. Jayne, Dear sir; I have made use of the Indian Expectorant, personally and in my family for the last six years with great benefit. Indeed I may consider my life prolonged by the use of this valuable medicine, under the blessing of God, for several years. I may say almost as much in the case of my wife and also of the Rev. Mc Fis, of the Island of Jamaica. For all cases of cough, inflammation of the chest, lungs and throat, I do not unhesitatingly recommend this as the best medicine I have ever tried. My earnest wish is that others afflicted as I have been, may experience the same relief, which I am persuaded they will by using the Indian Expectorant.

An army of 500,000 Mongols, under the sons of Genghis, so completely laid waste the provinces to the Indus in the hand of the barbarians into the Chinese empire, 12,000,000 are computed to have perished. During the invasion of Timour, twelve of the most flourishing cities of Asia, including Delhi, Isfahan, Baghdad and Damascus, were utterly destroyed, and pyramids of human heads, one of which contained 90,000 skulls, erected on their ruins. During thirty-two years of the reign of Justinian, the barbarians made an incursion into the Grecian empire, and they carried off or destroyed at an average on each occasion 200,000 persons. Nor was the depopulation of the southern and western provinces less during the same disastrous period. In the wars of Belisarius in Africa, 6,000,000 of its inhabitants are computed by a contemporary writer to have perished, and during the contest between that illustrious warrior and his successors Narses, and the barbarian armies in Italy, the whole Gothic nation and nearly fifteen millions of the natives of Italy disappeared.

Commercial. Wall Street, Monday, April 3. We are still in an anomalous condition. Some are running round the area crying out "all's well-all's well." Others, with gloomy faces, are positive we have not seen the worst. The agitation and fluctuation continue. On Saturday the Mechanics' and a few other banks opened their purses airing to those in want of discounts. The crowd-the jam-the squeeze at the Mechanics' Bank, baffled all description. It resembled the crowd at a fashionable suite sale, or a meeting at Tammany Hall in the days of its glory or a rush for a taste of the roasted ox when the Erie canal was opened. At the Bank of America and the United States Bank agency, large amounts of foreign exchange were sold on London, Paris, and Amsterdam. The United States Bank will make nearly 10 to 15 per cent a month on its post notes, used as exchange-the other banks a like proportion. The measures of relief, as they are called, will be measures of profit to those who have brought them forward. The next question is, "what credit will be attached to these post notes in Europe?" By the returns made to the state of Pennsylvania, it appears that on the 1st of March, 1837, the United States Bank stood thus, Loans, $50,000,000, circulation, $28,470,170.

"A RIVAL TO THE ASTOR HOUSE.—It is in contemplation to build a hotel at the west end of London, on the most gigantic scale, so arranged as to be capable of dining 1,200 persons. It is intended to resemble Maurice's, at Paris, as regards the management. Attached, will be extensive gardens, with fountains and jets d'eaux for the pleasure of the frequenters. Carriages will be in constant readiness to run to and fro to the terminus of the great railway. The building will be erected by a company having a capital of £100,000. Melancholy Event.—During the severely cold night of Wednesday week, two widowed females who were sisters, were frozen in death at Nantucket. The one was Mrs. Phebe, widow of Sylvanus Coleman, aged 79 years, the other Mrs. Priscilla, relict of Capt. Jo seph Cartwright, aged 66 years. They had retired in usual health, but were found in the morning lying up on the floor of their apartment dead and frozen.

Resolved, That each member of the House be at liberty to order for his use, at the expense of the territory, any number of copies not exceeding fifteen each week, during the session, of any paper appointed to contract for printing. On the Speaker announcing this result, Mr. Cathin returned his thanks to the House for the honor conferred, but at the same time declared that his experience had convinced him that he could not properly represent the citizens of his town and at the same time perform the duties of Clerk; he therefore respectfully declined the appointment. The House then proceeded to a second ballot, which resulted as follows: J. A. Welch, 59; Lafayette S. Foster, 38; E. C. Birdseye, 47; E. C. Bacon, 10; Wm. F. Brainard, 6; Mason Cleaveland, 9; Chas. Chapman, 16; E. C. Herrick, 1; J. Olney, 1; Blank, 2—189. There being no choice, the House proceeded to a third ballot, when the number of votes given was 185, as follows: For J. A. Welch, 86; E. C. Birdseye, 46; L. S. Foster, 37; Mason Cleaveland, 7; Chas. Chapman, 3; E. C. Burgess, 2; E. C. Herrick, 1; Scattering, 3—183. There still being no choice, a fourth ballot was taken, when the whole number of votes given was 189—of which Jonathan A. Welch, Esq. of Brooklyn, received 106, and was chosen. The House now proceeded to the election of an Assistant Clerk. On the 1st ballot 174 votes were given—necessary to a choice 88.

Through the medium of the records of the Russian Greek Church, we learn the death of a man in Russia, in 1831, aged 165 years. About three years since, a woman died in Tennessee, aged 154 years. We could add many more to this list, but forbear. Those we have mentioned are drawn from records that do not admit of a doubt, & prove to us almost conclusively, that our lives may be prolonged much beyond the present ordinary term. - Dr. Fitch. SOME OF THE CONSEQUENCES. - A correspondent, writing from Landisburg, Perry county, Pa., under date of the 14th inst., says: "Two men have been frozen to death in this county this present winter, with no companions but their bottles. The last was on his way from the distillery, full, and when found, he was on his hands and knees, with his pantaloons, skin, and flesh, torn or worn off in his struggles. It chills my blood to relate it." This bill is the complete separation of the affairs of the Government from the affairs of the Banks. The great vault to hold the public money is to be at Washington, and the lesser vaults in the mints in Philadelphia and New-Orleans, and the custom houses at New-York, Boston, Charleston and St. Louis—all treasuries of the United States. The mint, collectors, receivers at land offices and postmasters, (without exception,) are required to keep safely the revenues, without lending the same. The Secretary may make deposits in certain Banks, provided such deposits are special, and the same not to be touched. Whenever the revenue on hand shall exceed four millions, the Secretary is empowered to invest the surplus in United States or State stocks, and when the surplus is less he may sell out the same. All debts due the Government shall, after the 31st December, 1838, be paid one-sixth in specie—1839, one-third in specie—1840, one-half in specie—1841, two-thirds in specie—1842, five-sixths in specie—and 1843, the whole in gold and silver or Treasury notes. These embrace the leading traits of the bill, and all the others are details.—N. Y. Express, Review of the New York Market, Jan. 20.

The said Frederick Huth & Co. undertake and hereby promise to advance said Bank two hundred thousand pounds sterling, payable in London as early as they can conveniently do so after the recovery of the money market in London, in instalments. It is agreed in good faith, by said party, that said advance shall be made with the least practicable delay, and sixty thousand pounds of which are to be paid to S. Jaudon, Esq. in the city of London, for account of the Bank of the United States, and his receipt therefor to be taken and transmitted to said Bank in St. Louis, Missouri; but it is expressly declared that the said Frederick Huth & Co., shall not be called on to make any advance till it is convenient for them to do so. The Bank agrees to pay all said advances within twelve months after notice recalling the same. The said parties are to be allowed for all advances to said Bank in the premises, the same rate of interest that the Bank of England charges at the time, not being less than five per cent, per annum until said advances are cancelled by sales of said bonds; and one per cent, commission. The said Frederick Huth & Co. hereby undertake to introduce for sale, the said bonds, and sell them with all practicable dispatch. The said Frederick Huth & Co., are at liberty to sell the said bonds at ninety-six per cent, but not below that price.

Resolutions, Unanimously adopted at a meeting of the Whig freemen, held in Newport, on Monday evening, July 10th, 1839, Resolved, That the Whigs of Rhode Island are called upon, by a regard to the best in terests of their country, to use the most vig orous exertions in their power, to secure the election of Representatives to the next Con gress of the United States who are opposed to the oppressive and injurious policy, and to the destructive measures of the present Na tional Administration. Resolved, That the manner in which our late Representatives in Congress have dis charged their trust as members of that body, entitles them to the highest praise, and that we have the fullest confidence in their tal ents, integrity, and faithfulness, and believe their re-election will promote the most valua ble and important interests of our State and country. Resolved, "That we hereby sincerely pledge ourselves to support the Hon. Roswell B. Cranston and Col. Joseph L. Tillinghast for Representatives to the 26th Congress of the United States, and that we will use all honorable means to secure their elec tion. Resolved, That we cordially unite with our Whig brethren in Providence, in the patriotic sentiments contained in their “Cir cular to the Whigs of Rhode Island,” and also in their urgent solicitation to the Whig freemen to give to Messrs. Cranston and Tillinghast, the zealous and undivided support of the Whig party throughout the State. MICHAEL FREEBORN, Chairman. JAMES ATKINSON, Secretary.

In occasional relief, the white paupers receive about three times as much as the colored. -r Dominica. The white population is estimated at about 900; the free black and colored population was ascertained, in 1825, to amount to 3122. During the five years ending in November, 1825, thirty of the former class had received relief from the poor fund, and only ten of the latter, making the proportion of more than nine white paupers to one colored one in the same number of persons. Grenada. This colony contained in 1825, 3486 free black and colored persons, and it does not appear that any relief whatever has been given to them during the five years for which returns have been sent. Honduras. The free black and colored population, in 1824, amounted to 1750. The return of the Treasurer of the Colony is, 'that there are no funds raised for the support of the poor, there being so very few persons who are literally in distress from poverty; but such as are so are readily relieved from the public funds. The number of free persons thus relieved has varied from 6 to 8 during the five years. Jamaica is supposed to contain 20,000 whites, and double that number of free black and colored persons.

Resolved, That, as it would be inexpedient for the United States to proceed, upon their separate authority, to survey and mark out the North Eastern Boundary, until all reasonable means of effecting that object by the consent and concurrence of both parties shall have been exhausted, the "bill to provide for surveying the North Eastern Boundary Line of the United States, according to the treaty of seventeen hundred and eighty-three," ought not to pass; and it is therefore ordered that it be laid on the table. Duel Tragedy.—A lieutenant in the Navy, while ascending the river in a steamboat, became involved in a quarrel with three persons from Arkansas, two of them brothers. He was challenged by one of the brothers, and endeavored to "back out," but the other party insisted upon a fight, and a gentleman volunteering to be second for the officer, they went ashore to settle their difference. At the first fire, the lieutenant received his adversary's ball in his hip, asked if he was satisfied, to which his antagonist replied, "no not until one of us is killed." They took another shot, and the lieutenant's adversary fell dead. His brother then insisted on a fight, which the second of the lieutenant endeavored in vain to prevent. They fired and then BURGESS DE WARDER JER Y second of the second, shot and killed him, and was in turn shot dead by the lieutenant, who was conveyed from the field much exhausted by loss of blood, and is now at Louisville, recovering, under the care of his physicians.—Thus four men were left dead upon the field.

Bammerick amused himself by walking about till it began to grow dark, when not finding the dean return, he at last ventured to follow him, and on arriving at the gate, was surprised to find it locked. He knocked and called several times, but no one came; and he found himself securely confined between high walls on a dark and cold night in the month of March. Knowing the character of the dean for occasional absence, he set down the dilemma as an accident, for he had not the least suspicion of his being confined intentionally. At the usual hour the elderly servants went to bed, and the dean remained in his study till two in the morning. He then went into the hall, drew the charges from a blunderbuss and other firearms, returned to his study and rang the bell; which was immediately attended by his butler. "Robert," said he, "I have been much disturbed by a noise on the garden side, I fear some robbers must have broken in; give me a lantern, and call up Saunders." Having procured a lantern, the dean went into the hall and stayed with the firearms till the men came down. "Hush," said Swift, "arm yourselves and follow me." Having unlocked the gate they entered the garden, where the light soon attracted poor Bammerick, who came running towards them.

In Wall Street, there was a considerable buzz on the beautiful morning, and the stockholders were some blowing up, some swearing, some praying, and some selling out. The public generally were laughing till their ribs ached, at the sad plight of this great locofoco institution. It seems that no committee is sitting, nor any one likely to sit—Bob White is going on as usual, mixing his water with the usual proportions, and moistening his clay with the usual libations. The principal inquiry seems to be about the loan of $19,000 to the Hon. Barnabas Bates. "How did he get a loan?" "How the devil did Harry contrive to get $19,000 out of Bobby?" This we shall now reveal. In the year 1815, the post office was in the old Exchange. Then Barney Bates was installed sub-treasurer and watch-dog over Sam Gouverneur, the postmaster. It was antecedent to the great fire. Barney, at that time, had just started the famous system of insuring money sent through the post office, and took the premiums regularly until the first loss was incurred. He had also set up a splendid house and establishment uptown, fitted up with elegant furniture, ottomans, pier glasses, and everything. All that was wanting now to make Barney a fashionable leader of the beau monde.

Could he have succeeded in this project, he would have taken not less than four million dollars from the national Treasury annually; for it should be borne in mind that the public lands are managed by the government at an expense of not less than one million dollars a year; so that, where the people should have three million of their own money returned to their State Treasuries, for every dollar of the people's own money would be taken from their national treasury, and which would have to be supplied from the pockets of the people, in duties, for the benefit of the government. We repeat, the proposition was absurd, and such only as the mind of a political gambler would have conceived. It was nothing more nor less than a project to make the people pay four million dollars in taxes into the national treasury, in order that three million of it might be returned to their State Treasuries.

This year. I have so often referred to the subject of education in my former communications to the legislature, that, important as the subject is, I feel indisposed to dwell upon it at any great length. I here, sensible, that your feelings and interest are alive to its success, and that your unremitting exertions will be directed to its advancement throughout the state. Every free government is called on by a principle of self-preservation, to afford every facility for the education of its people. The liberty of a people cannot be forced beyond their intelligence; the two go hand in hand. The South American Republics exhibit but alternate scenes of anarchy and despotism; and France in the day of her bloody struggle for freedom, was overwhelmed and plunged in misery by the very attempt to make her free. In the United States we witness the advantages of education, in the virtue, intelligence and liberty of the people. History points out the ignorance and degradation of other countries, and we are admonished of the duties before us. If our own country is ever to fall from her high position before the world, the cause will be found in the ignorance of the people; if she is to maintain where she now stands with her glory undimmed, educate every child in the land. It is now satisfactorily ascertained, that our common school fund will be abundant for all the purposes of our extended system of education.

Having made any pie arrangements for the bestowal of immense attention to the legislative department of their newspaper, they cherish the conviction that they will render entire satisfaction in this respect not only to the members of the legislature but to the reading community generally. As it is more than probable that the all-engrossing subject of the derangement of the currency under which the community is now laboring, and the condition of the several banking institutions of our state, will constitute topics of speedy legislative action, the proceedings of the approaching session will be fraught with unusual interest and render a paper published at the seat of government, which bestows upon them constant attention, a vehicle of intelligence "most devoutly wished!" We would also call to the minds of our readers that the great Presidential contest of 1840 is approaching with rapid stride, which is to decide whether our National Government shall continue to be administered upon the truly republican principles of its present illustrious head, Martin Van Buren, or whether the reins of power shall be committed to the hands of ultra Federalists and friends of a great and blighting monopoly in the shape of a National Bank. The Keystone, being indissolubly wedded to the support of the republican cause, will in this momentous conflict, battle manfully on the side of Van Buren and a Constitutional Treasury, and contribute its mite to the advancement and ultimate triumph of the democratic cause.

Mr. Venable said, on offering the above Resolution, that he considered it due to this House to make a brief statement, showing the importance of acting at once on the subject, and showing the present state and condition of the existing Banks, as regards the increased capital of $5,000,000 provided for by the law passed the 25th day of March, 1837, as well as to show that the just expectations of those portions of the commonwealth which were provided for in that act ought to be at once complied with. The act above referred to provides in the first place for the incorporation of the Exchange Bank of Virginia located at Norfolk, the capital of this institution to be $1,800,000—half to be subscribed by individuals, half by the State; this bank has gone into operation, and has established its branches required by the law—and has received a subscription from the State out of the surplus revenue. The Northwestern Bank by the same law had $800,000 added to its capital, it has already received a subscription from the State which has been paid out of the surplus revenue of $101,000, and said bank Mr. V. believed has established its branch or branches required by law.

Among all the numerous constitutions of Anti-Slavery Societies, which we have read and they have not been a few we do not now recollect which of them did not, in one shape or another, place " the great law of love" at the foundation of its operations. If there should be one here and another there that has not embodied it in so many words, we have produced a refutation of the Chronicle's statement, which ought to put it to the blush, and will, if it is susceptible of being shamed by being most clearly convicted of what is shameful. If the Chronicle was acquainted with the facts now presented, how does it reconcile its statements with them?

THE INTELLIGENCER, WEDNESDAY, MARCH 30, 1831. The present number closes the second volume of the Intelligencer, and we cannot so far depart from the established custom among printers, as not to tender our grateful thanks to our patrons for the very liberal support they have extended to us. It is a source of pleasure to look back upon the dreary waste from which we emerged, and now to behold the bright and cheering prospects they have spread before us; and though at best, the path which conductors of political journals are destined to travel, must be thorny and uneven, yet when cheered by the approbation of a large portion of the intelligent community, it is rendered comparatively smooth. It will be our great object to merit a continuance of the confidence of the public. We dislike to mar these pleasant reflections by hinting to our friends, that we are in want of the "one thing needful," but our circumstances render it necessary that we should. We therefore request those who are indebted to us to make early payment, particularly those who are owing for two years' subscription. Mr. Henry Totwiler, the lately appointed professor of ancient languages in the University of this State, has just arrived from Charleston, to undertake the exercises which have been assigned to him. President Woods, from Transylvania University, and Dr. Saltonstall, the present Register of the Land Office at Cahawha, may be looked for in a few days.

The galleries are supported by 160 cast iron pillars. The length of the exterior wall is 1200 feet, breadth 46 feet, thickness 4 feet, height 33 feet. It is warmed by 4 Lehigh stoves and lighted by 16 lamps. There are 5 ventilating and four flue chimneys—all of which, together with all the windows and skylights are securely grated. The cookery is provided with a steam boiler from the Sing Sing Prison, and the Chapel is warmed by steam from the boiler. The entire cost of the new prison—which was first occupied in Oct. 1829—including the cookery, chapel, schoolroom, &c., was $86,252.25, (including the labor of the convicts.) Bunker Hill Aurora.   Distressing Calamity. On Sunday afternoon, about 4 o’clock, the Widow E. Rumney, residing with her brother-in-law, Mr. Richard Rumney, in Allen street, was burnt to death in a most afflicting manner. Her brother having gone out, she locked the door, as was her constant custom when Mr. Rumney was absent, and in going towards the fireplace, her foot caught in the carpet and threw her into the fire before she could recover herself, being a very stout woman.

Barstow arrived at Boston on the morning of the 19th inst., bringing London papers of the 18th July, and Liverpool of the 19th, which contain the annexed account of the trial and execution of Alibaud, the person who attempted to assassinate the King of France in the streets of Paris, on the 25th of June last.  Trial and Execution of Alibaud—This trial took place in the court of Peers, on Friday the 8th ult. Shortly after 10 o’clock, the prisoner was introduced into the court, in custody of five municipal guards, two of whom held him under the arms, and led him to the bar prepared for him. The prisoner walked to the bar with a firm step. His carriage was erect, though his face was pale and his eyes cast down. As his advocate advanced, he rose and shook hands with him. The Peers entered immediately afterwards. The President asked the accused the preliminary question; to which the prisoner answered that his name was Louis Alibaud, aged about twenty-six years, and that his birthplace was Nimes. The remaining answers of the prisoner were candid and animated. He attempted not to deny for one moment the motives by which he had been actuated.

I certify that I have, by way of experiment, used the Hygeian, and most of the various kinds of Pills, in my practice, which have borne the highest repute in the public estimation, that have been offered for sale in this vicinity for the last five years, including those called the Resurrection or Persian Pills; and the public may rest assured that none among the whole catalogue has answered a better purpose, as an easy and effectual remedy, than the Resurrection or Persian Pills, in most cases of disease. Charles B. Beck, M. D. Rochester, N. Y. Sept. 21, 1837. TO MOTHERS. Messrs. E. Chace & Co. Gents. Hearing much said about the extraordinary effects of the Resurrection or Persian Pills, upon those about to become mothers, we were induced to make a trial of them. My wife was at that time the mother of two children, and had suffered the most tedious and excruciating pains during and after her confinement of each. She had tried every means and taken much medicine, but found little or no relief. She commenced taking the Persian Pills about three months before her confinement (her health being very poor about this length of time previous) and in a short time she was enabled by their use to attend to the cares of a mother to her family until her confinement.

In the House of Representatives, the bill from the Senate for the compensation of the witnesses and others attending the trial of Judge Peck, was read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary. Numerous other bills from the Senate were also passed through the preliminary stages of legislation. A message was received from the President, transmitting an interesting report from the Board of Inspectors of the Penitentiary of the District of Columbia, respecting the state of that establishment. It was referred to the Committee on the District, and directed to be printed. Various bills were afterwards reported and acted upon. Mr. Wayne, from the Committee on the Library, introduced a Joint resolution authorizing a subscription on the part of Congress, to a proposed stereotype edition of the laws of the United States; which was, on motion of Mr. Taylor, committed to a Committee of the Whole House, and made the order of the day for tomorrow. The bill reported on Monday by Mr. Davis, of South Carolina, from the Committee on the Judiciary, to repeal the twenty-fifth section of the act establishing the Judicial Courts of the Union, passed in the year 1789, was then taken up.

THE Medical Lectures of Harvard University, delivered in Boston, will be commenced in the Autumn, at the usual period, viz., on the third Wednesday in October. They will be continued four months. This extension in the term of the Lectures has been thought necessary to afford time for such a course of instruction and demonstration, as is deemed by the Faculty to be requisite, under the advantages which have recently accrued to the School.

Ministers of the Gospel should not be made to do as other people do in all matters of government, is to us inconceivable. They enjoy the privileges of our government, why not contribute for its support? FEDERAL MOVEMENTS. The old federal party is again in the field, and have called a State Convention to be held at Concord on the 21st of the present month, to organize their forces preparatory to the Spring election. We are not sorry for this; an organized opposition keeps political affairs in a healthy state. The great hobby on which the leading characters are to mount, will be the Banks and a paper currency; but they will hitch on their train the Abolitionists by way of auxiliaries. This is perfectly in character. The old war federalists have never yet given up their favorite Hartford convention scheme of severing the union. They have ever since the defeat of their plans in 1814 and 15 been fostering the idea of a separation, by insidiously goading on the South to commit an overt act, that they may obtain by indirection, what they dared not cross the Rubicon to effect openly. The indiscretion of the South had nearly effected their purpose by producing a revolt in the South, but was happily prevented.

The paragraph we copy today from the Daily Press, coincides with the remark of ours last week, respecting the admission of our state that congress having transferred the disputed ground to Ohio, the question submitted to us is not, whether we will let Ohio keep it, but whether we will go into the Union or not. We have no better answer for the editor of the Ann Arbor State Journal; nor can we better inform him why Michigan is asked to vote on the subject at all, than to refer him to the proviso in the second section of the 'act to establish the northern boundary of Ohio, and to provide for the admission of the state of Michigan,' etc., and to the compliance with the fundamental condition of admission,' required in the third section; which is, for Michigan, in a convention of delegates, elected for the sole purpose of giving the ASSENT not dissent therein required, may comply with the requisition of congress, so as to be proclaimed by the President a State of the Union, soon enough to give her a voice in the election near at hand of President and Vice President, and to entitle her Senators and Representatives to their seats at the next session of Congress soon enough to secure her five per cent,um of the public land sales and her apportionment of the United States surplus revenue.

We learn from St. Louis, that the three Potawatamie Indians charged with participating in the murder of the Hall family, last June, have been started on their way to La Salle to be delivered over to the authorities of that county for trial. Gen. Atkinson informed Gov. Reynolds a short time since, that these Indians had been identified by the Misses Hall as three of the party who murdered their father and mother, and that they had also acknowledged on examination that they were of the party, but denied any participation in the murders. Gov. Reynolds thereupon with characteristic promptness took measures for bringing them to trial before the state courts.  They left Jefferson Barracks, the 5th inst. in the custody of Messrs. J. D. Whitesides and A. W. Cavender, who will doubtless see them safely committed to the care of the proper officers. The Misses Hall are now residing in Putnam county, and will attend as witnesses at the trial.  What will Mr. Owen say now in defense of the loyalty and friendship of his Potawatomi charge? Evidence seems to be fast accumulating against him—evidence, too, which it will be difficult for him to refute. And by the same evidence also we apprehend the public will be convinced, if they are not already, that the governor’s opinion as to the hostility of the Potawatomi tribe was by no means wide of the truth.

Mr. Owen, the said Receiver, then, in a very long speech, attacked nearly the whole of Mr. Lewis' political course, both in the State Legislature and in Congress. After he had concluded, Judge Crawford made a few remarks in regard to the utility of the Coosa Canal, the constitutional power of Congress to make internal improvements, and adverted to a resolution passed by the State Legislature denying that such a power existed in the General Government. The Judge did not make a stump speech as is alleged by the correspondent of the Inquirer, and he expressly stated that he did not wish to influence a single vote. Judge Crawford did not in any of those remarks mention the election of Governor, nor the name of any candidate for that office, nor did he in any manner allude to them. He did not, as the correspondent of the Inquirer states, advocate the repeal of the 25th section of the Judiciary Act, either then or at any other time; but on the contrary, he has been uniformly opposed to the repeal of that section, and has expressed his opposition on all proper occasions. The communication in the Inquirer is undoubtedly from a bitter personal enemy of Judge Crawford; and if his name were given to the public, it would most probably carry with it a sufficient corrective of its own misrepresentations.

The same letter states that the Editor of the Telegraph did attempt to induce him, (the Editor of the Times) to come out at once in favor of Mr Calhoun. That letter we expect to command in a few days. The Key.—In a note to his book, Mr Calhoun has unluckily for himself, given a key to his character and plans. How did the people of the United States understand this gentleman in the canvass for the Presidency between Mr Adams, General Jackson, and Messrs Crawford and Clay? Did not General Jackson’s friends understand him to be decidedly with them? In an answer to a charge made by Mr Crawford, that he (Mr Calhoun) was for Mr Adams until Mr Clay went over, Mr Calhoun acknowledges that he was a neutral between General Jackson and Mr Adams, until he ascertained who was the most popular candidate, and then his choice was determined on a mere abstract principle, which, by a mere alteration of attitude, would have determined him for Mr Adams. There are many volumes in this note.—Frank. Argus. That such, to be, were the opinions of General Jackson, who came into office in the March succeeding the quote from his first message, as follows: "I would therefore recommend such an amendment of the Constitution as may remove all indirect influence in the election of President and Vice-President. The mode may be so regulated as to preserve to each state its relative weight in the election; and an inducement in the first attempt may be ordained for, by confining the second to a choice between the two highest nominees. In connection with such an amendment, it would seem advisable to limit the service of the Chief Magistrate to a single term, of either four or six years. "There are perhaps few men who can for any great length of time enjoy office and power, without being more or less under the influence of feeling, unfavorable to the faithful discharge of their public duties. But however much, we may wish prosperity and independence, to Poland—poor bleeding Poland, yet we cannot but hope for the same boon, to nations, on this continent, that suffer yet, under an infinitely greater tyranny, than ever disgraced the system of an European despot—a system, that can best be described, by the sobriquet of ‘stupid ferocity,’—and which I may emphatically say, is a living libel upon civilization, I mean, of course, the British Tory rule of Canada. Americans may well shudder at the tyranny to which the brave, but unfortunate Poles, have been subjected, — but Nicholas lost 100,000 of his best troops, in the campaign of 1631, and yet, not one political execution, took place. In the last twenty years, the number of Political executions in France, England, Sweden, Russia, Austria, Italy, Germany, Denmark and Holland, with a population of 200,000,000 does not amount to over twenty-seven. In Canada, with a population of not quite a million, in the short space of one year, thirty-five have been executed upon the gibbet, and hundreds murdered in cold blood. But perhaps, I have already trespassed upon your patience! I will only add, in concluding my remarks, a sentiment, which from extensive observation, in different nations, I am quite confident, should never be omitted, upon such an occasion, as the present. My Old Friends or Howard: Being a candidate to represent you in the Senate of this State is the cause of my addressing you at this time; and having no disposition to conceal my principles, but believing it the duty of every candidate to lay them before those he offers to represent, I will proceed, in a very brief manner, to state such of mine as I am told are interesting the minds of the people. Of the manner in which I will fulfill the following pledges, if elected, and stand by the interests of my county in all other respects, you will have to judge from my conduct in private life having been a citizen, farmer, and trader among you for nearly thirty years. I am in favor of the election of General Harrison as President, for a single term of four years, he standing pledged to the people not to be a candidate for re-election, and to set the important example of one term. I consider the establishment of this principle of the utmost importance, as it will do away with all electioneering among the office holders, and tend to the promotion and advancement of honesty and virtue in the administration of our public affairs.

STATE OF NEW HAMPSHIRE. BY THE GOVERNOR, A PROCLAMATION, For a day of Thanksgiving and Praise. By the advice of the Council, I appoint Thursday, the 24th day of November next, a day of public THANKSGIVING and PRAISE throughout this State. Deriving from the Supreme Ruler of the Universe all the blessings of this life and the expectation of a better; it is at all times proper that his intelligent creatures should render to him their grateful homage, adoration, and praise. The multiplied proofs of kindness and mercy by which we have been so conspicuously blessed as a people call upon us for especial offerings of love, thanksgiving, and obedience. He who guarded our nation in its birth and its infancy still smiles upon its matured strength, sustains and protects it by his Almighty arm, and enriches it with all the precious gifts of his bounteous Providence.

Harrison had not a hundred friends and supporters in all Georgia. The prejudice against him in that State was universal. But his life and character have since been rigorously canvassed by her citizens, and now she is for him by a majority of thousands. What a signal and glorious instance of the triumph of truth and virtue! OHIO. AN IMPORTANT LEGAL DECISION. —The Superior Court, now in session in this city, says the Cincinnati Gazette, has recently expressed the opinion that where a bill of exchange is drawn in this state upon a person in another state whose residence is here, the holder of the bill cannot recover damages upon a protest provided he knew the fact of residence when he took the bill; and the parol evidence is admissible to prove the knowledge. When, in such a contest as that which recently prevailed in New York, when the fate of such an administration depended upon its issue? Can anyone doubt that millions, if necessary, would have been expended, to ensure to the men in power their menaced places? Can anyone doubt that, after the lapse of a few years, when the money in the sub-Treasuries came to be counted, a deficit of at least two-thirds would have been discovered to exist, as Bonaparte found to be the case when that in the bank of Amsterdam was counted? We know that in the bill lately before Congress, there was a provision requiring the speedy presentation of Treasury drafts for payment in specie; and hence, it might be argued, that they could not remain out so as to form a common circulation. But, who does not know, that such a provision could not be enforced; and that probably it would have been repealed at the very first session after the commencement of the measure? What I say to a man in Arkansas or Louisiana, if you do not apply for payment of your Treasury draft or check by a given day, you shall lose the amount!

DROPSY CURED.- In the month of May last, Mrs. Robhina was taken sick with a violent inflammation about her breast, and then was with a cough and fever. She had good medical attendance, but continued to grow worse until her situation became alarming. The inflammation and cough finally abated, but dropsy ensued. Her body and limbs became much swollen, attended with great debility and severe pains. Scarce any hopes of her recovery were entertained. At this time Dr. J. was called to see her, and attended upon her. In a few days, his medicine had a very powerful and salutary effect, in reducing her swelling and relieving her pains. Her health soon began to mend, and in ten days she was able to walk about the house, and continued to improve until she has been entirely relieved from every appearance of dropsical affection, and her health otherwise much improved. JAMES ROBHINS, 17th street, near 8th Avenue New York, October 26th, 1856. Persons wishing to make inquiries in regard to Mrs. Robhina's case, are at liberty to call on her. Or on Miss Hlimia, 15th Avenue, who has had some knowledge of the case, and who has received essential benefit herself in a pulmonary affection of many years' standing, from Dr. Sear's treatment.
