It was stated by the gentleman from St. Joseph that there could be no such office created in the city of Detroit by the legislature. He, Mr. M'P. contended that whatever the constitution does not prohibit, it is competent for the legislature to enact. He had consulted with several gentlemen who have extensive legal information, and who were members of the convention which framed the constitution, and they are unanimously of opinion that it is competent for the legislature either to abridge or extend the powers and duties of County Register, or to create or abolish the office of City Register, as may seem to them most expedient. There are several matters which the constitution has prohibited the legislature from doing. They are prohibited from authorizing money to be expended except by an act of appropriation. They are prohibited from granting any bills of divorce. They are prohibited from passing any law to authorize lotteries. They are prohibited from passing any bill of Attainder, or ex-post facto law, or any law which shall impair the obligation of contracts. But they are not prohibited from defining the powers and duties of all officers named in the constitution, either to limit or extend such powers as may to them appear most advisable.

Mr. Bell and Mr. Turney, both members of Congress from Tennessee, took a boxing match in the House of Representatives, whilst in session in committee of the whole, on the 1st in stant, Mr. B. applied, during a speech of his, many insulting epithets to Mr. T. calling him "a scavenger," "a tool of tools." Mr. T. who was sitting near Mr. B. said, "it is false, you are a liar," when Bell struck him, and they had a regular "set to" until separated by the other members. We do not learn that any bones were broken or much blood drawn. We recollect when Matthew Lyon made an assault upon Griswold in Con gress hall many years ago, although the house was not in session, the great efforts made by the federalists to have him expelled. But the aggressor in the a bove matter belongs to the same party. And although one member, Mr. Mercer, declared, that "unless the house would enforce its laws he would not remain a member of it," the laws remain unen forced, and we have not heard of the gentleman's resignation.

To preserve, therefore, that health by natural means is a grand moral and political scheme, to fulfill which requires our utmost attention. Dear Mr. Crittenden, You are at liberty to make what use you please of this letter. Your friend, TUGS. W. NEWTON. 1 Robert Crittenden, Esq. Little Rock, A. T. To His Excellency John Pope, Governor of the Territory of Arkansas: Sir—As yet, I believe your Excellency has had no biographer, who has written the history of your eventful life; that your Excellency’s name may de scend to posterity, I will do all in my power to make it immortal. Your Excel lency’s stewardship, as Governor of this Territory, for the last three years, affords ample materials for a few num bers in the columns of a perishing news paper. It would be a waste of time to give a description of your person, age, parentage, and manners; they are al ready advantageously known to the pub lic. To analyze the qualities of your head and heart, and show your Excel lency’s fitness or unfitness to rule over and govern the free and independent yeomanry of Arkansas, is what we are most interested in knowing. Your per sonal and political friends claim for your Excellency a high order of talents, “great political experience, and emi nent qualifications for high and respon sible stations.” If this description of your Excellency’s qualifications ever was true at any period of your life, judg ing from the success of your administra tion in Arkansas, it must be admitted by all candid men, that it is so no longer.

On the morning following one of the cold nights of last week, Abijah Merritt, Esq. of the village of Sandy Hook, Newtown, found his room filled with dead chimney birds. He had the curiosity to count them, and as certained the number to be eleven hundred and twenty-three. By measurement, there were two bushels and a peck. The same chimneys are still the resort of hundreds and perhaps thousands more.—N. Haven Herald. Huntsville, (Ala.) May 1.—Great Damage by Frost—The damage sustained by the frost on Sunday night, has, perhaps, never been greater in this section of the country, so late in the season. For some weeks past the weather had been mild, just such as the planter could desire, and the stand of cotton was exceedingly fine; but the flattering prospect has been sadly reversed. Saturday night was attended with some frost—the weather was cold throughout Sunday, the wind from the north, and a killing frost was the consequence at night. We fear the injury has been general throughout North Alabama, if not the whole cotton growing country. In our immediate vicinity, we hear of some planters who have lost whole fields, others, one half, etc.

The following thrilling anecdote of a young South Carolina girl is copied from a work entitled "Tales of Marion's Men:" Sally St. Clair was a beautiful, dark-eyed Creole girl. The whole treasure of her love was freely poured out to Sergeant Jasper, who, on one occasion, had the good fortune to save her life. The prospect of their separation almost maddened her. To sever her long jetty ringlets from her exquisite head, to dress in male attire, to enroll herself in the corps for which he belonged, unknown to him, was a resolution no sooner conceived than taken. In the camp she attracted no particular attention except the night before the battle, when she was noticed bending over his couch, like a good and gentle spirit, as if listening to his dreams.

Mr. Ogle arrayed the very same statement against the Branch Mints the last session, and made a speech in support of it; and as it was warmly applauded by his friends, I suppose he is anxious to pursue the subject this session. If he can fully establish the facts that he presents, why then, the branch mints ought to be abolished. Unfortunately, this subject of the Mints, is one that will unite the opposition and administration parties of the South, in a common array in defense of the Southern branches of the establishment, and hence Mr. Ogle must not hope for success, no matter how good his case may be. Today, when he made his attack, he at once called up Mr. Stanley, of North Carolina, Mr. Dawson, of Georgia, both whigs, to the rescue of the Southern branches of the mint. The whigs of the South could not think of letting Mr. Ogle, a whig of the North, destroy their coin factories for the good of the people, or for the good of aught else. It can scarcely be doubted, however, that the Southern branch mints are nuisances to the country. One Mint is quite enough for this country—a single one is as good as a dozen.

The said government adds furthermore, that by the laws of the state more lands in property are left granted to the empresarios or grantees than those allotted by the law of the 24th of March 1825; and thus, after having introduced the number of families expressed in their contract, not being allowed them the right of selling said lands unless it be to Mexicans, and in no case to subjects of foreign powers. Neither have the grantees the right of transferring their contracts to Mexicans or foreigners, without the knowledge and approbation of the state government, and consequently, the sales of lands made by the grantees, shall be considered null and void, and all transactions made contrary to the rules above referred to.

And his eye brightened, as it answered mine; But soon an anxious shade passed o’er its light, And looking steadfastly, he said, “And brother too?” My child, my precious child! Let it be ever thus. Still crave to share All happiness, reward, and holiness With him, and we, your parents, will be blessed. Greenwood. Let Time speed on his hasty way And steal my sweetest hours, I care not for his night or day, His sunshine or his showers,— So I, my Mary, be with thee, There will my day and sunshine be. Then let my friends all proudly scorn The love my heart has chosen, Then let the world’s contempt be borne Though every look be frozen, I'd choose thy love with pride, ere mirth With the best love of all the earth. The Kings of Europe.—There are forty-nine reigning sovereigns in Europe, exclusive of the who was elected to his present rank. Nineteen of these are Catholics, seventeen Lutherans, five reformed Dutch, five Evangelical, one offshoot Han, one of the Greek Church, and one the Mahometan. The oldest of them all is Anthony of Saxony, who is 77—the youngest, Ferdinand of the two Sicilies, who is 24, and Donna Maria, who is 15. Frederick, Grand Duke of Saxe-Altenburg, has been on the throne longest—53 years.

The opposition parties in Virginia, North Carolina, and Georgia, have nominated Judge Williamson candidate for President. A Whig State Convention in Indiana has nominated Gen. Harrison for the same office. An amusing incident happened on Tuesday morning last, in the House of Representatives, just before they convened, of which the following account is given by the reporter for the Baltimore Chronicle: "A Quaker lady in the gallery rose and commenced an exhortation to the members to attend more to the principles of religion and morality, as a duty they owed to their God and country, being representatives of the freest government in the world, and concluded by requesting permission to preach in the Capitol this evening. Mr. Carr was sent up by the Speaker to cut short her delivery, but the members on the floor cried "go on, hear, hear;" but someone near her prevailed on her to sit down, when a member arose and observed that he hoped, as the lady in the gallery had concluded, the clerk would proceed to read the journal of the House." - Nat. Intel.

All questions relative to the government of foreign nations, whether of the old or the new world, have been treated by the United States as questions of fact only, and our predecessors have cautiously abstained from deciding upon them, until the clearest evidence was in their possession, to enable them not only to decide correctly, but to shield their decisions from every unworthy imputation. In all the contests that have arisen out of the revolutions of France, out of the disputes relating to the crowns of Portugal and Spain, out of the revolutionary movements in those kingdoms, out of the separation of the American possessions of both from the European Governments, and out of the numerous and constantly occurring struggles for dominion in Spanish America, so wisely consistent with our just principles has been the action of our Government, that we have, under the most critical circumstances, avoided all censure, and encountered no other evil than that produced by a transient estrangement of good will in those against whom we have been, by force of evidence, compelled to decide.

It has been for some time made known to the public that the subscribers are engaged in preparing for the Irreversible and enduring edition of the Louisiana Civil Code. They were, in the first, aware of the great difficulty in producing a satisfactory publication of the work, and it was not without great hesitation that they consented to the undertaking. But the present edition, amounting to about three thousand copies, and which cost the State more than thirty thousand dollars, is entirely different. For more than two years last, the only price of the work has been from thirty to fifty dollars. It is a system of written rules which to be immediately operates upon every individual in the state, interested either in agriculture or commerce, and which governs the disposition of so much property coming into it from other states, but - unlike all other treaties of law - it is as much the text-book and manual of the merchant and the planter, as it is of the private gentleman and the professional man. Zeitung. It has caused incredible joy in the Turkish capital, where, however, perfect tranquility already prevailed. Vienna, Feb. 20.—An English Courier from Constantinople has passed through Vienna hastening to London. The situation of the Porte seems to be very critical. Letters from Odessa say that part of the Russian fleet at Sebastopol sailed on the 10th to join the Turkish fleet. SPAIN. London, March 6.—According to our correspondent, the most perfect tranquility prevails at present, not only in the Spanish capital, but over the Spanish portion of the peninsula. The seditious movements in the province of Leon, which had indeed never excited any great alarm, had been put down without difficulty or delay. In the capital a greater spirit of freedom prevailed than on any similar occasion for some years past. — Mask balls and all the grotesque amusements of the carnival season had not only been permitted, but encouraged by the Court and Ministry. In fact, during the last days of the carnival, the entire population of the capital had the appearance of being engaged in one grand and noisy masquerade. But in the meantime the Ministers did not seem to imitate the harmony, or to share in the gaiety, of the people.

The following is a brief specimen (says the Journal of Commerce) of an argufmentation which took place a couple of days back at the Police Office in New York. An Irish man came up to answer the complaint of his wife for being about to abandon her and go to New Orleans. The lady herself was in the Police office when her husband came into it, and one of the officers good naturedly brought them both into an inner room, with the intention of arranging the matter amicably between them. The wife again stated the cause of her grievance, namely that her loving lord was about to leave her and go to New Orleans. What have you to say to that?

Times. Hard times in consequence of the Specie Circular. Not a hundred miles from the village of Ann Arbor, a gentleman called on a cobbler for his boots, which he had left with him to be mended. The cobbler, after a few moments hesitation, told the gentleman that he had been under the disagreeable necessity of making an assignment of his effects to his creditors, all in consequence of the Specie Circular, but if he would wait a short time, the assignees were investigating his affairs, and he would see what they could do for him. Accordingly, the gentleman waited patiently for the result, when to his great satisfaction the cobbler returned fetching one boot, saying that the administration of Van Buren had not made quite so much difference in his business as he expected, he could pay a dividend of fifty per cent, delivering at the same time to his customer one of his boots and assuring him that he should have the other next fall; provided the Whigs gained their election, and he obtained the office they had promised him.

Mr. Danforth resides in a very handsome house in Tenth below Spruce street, and purchased a pew at a high rate in Rev. Mr. Todd's Church. His transactions has not added to his wealth - he has good business capacity, but like many others, instead of making slow and sure, he was desirous of grasping a fortune at once, and became engaged with others in pork selling, etc., but it has not enabled him to increase his means as rapidly as he anticipated. Henry Toland, a Philadelphian - son of the late Henry Toland, and brother of Robert Toland a director of Girard Bank, - he formerly was of the firm of Toland & Koons, very good merchants, in Market street - he retired from that firm with a very handsome competence - he afterwards went into the East India trade, and there unfortunately for him, he had to make a stoppage - after that he got considerable into political life - and as a reward, General Jackson made him navy agent at Philadelphia - was supplanted about two years since, by M. W. Ash - Mr. Toland has most excellent business capacity, but he has been so deeply engaged in stock speculations with H. M. Whitney, C. M. Leisler and W. D. Lewis, that it may be considered as a very wrong act to have placed him in the board. Jas.

In fact, there are certain points on the coat which cannot be affected by self-varying principles in any other way, nor by any other means, than those laid down by the subscribers. Unlike any other of the kind, this system now offered to the trade is conducted without any breast measure, and effects every point and every part of the coat by self-varying principles, in a way calculated to convict the senses of any reasonable man, that should it ever fail to produce a good fit upon any shape whatever, the charge must be laid to a careless and incorrect measurement. It may seem mysterious that this rule is said to be self-varying, and yet a square rule and yet conducted without a breast measure; but the whole mystery will be unfolded at once by examination. Unwilling to have it said that the subscribers are attempting to palm this system upon the trade with misrepresentations, they avow their willingness to submit it into the hands of an impartial committee for an examination of its principles, in comparison with any other ever invented in the United States, at Philadelphia or New York, which committee shall make the decision known through the press.

The fifty new Lodging rooms opened last season, there has been added a large new separate building, containing 28 Lodging Rooms, alluding in the whole establishment, ample accommodation for 200 visitors. There are spacious Ball Room, Billiard Room, Bowling Alleys, Quoit Yards, &c., the whole fronting the river to the south, within a hundred yards of the clean wide beach. There are provided, also, two beautiful and commodious Yachts, under the charge of an experienced and skillful seaman. There are bathing houses for those who prefer them to the open surf; also a substantial wharf for the steamboats to come up to, instead of landing and taking off passengers in the small boats as heretofore; which moreover enables visitors to bring carriages and horses, if they choose. Besides the salt water luxuries above named, everything will be supplied for the table which the markets of the District, Baltimore, and Norfolk can afford, to which the steamboat lines furnish regular access; and the house will be amply provided with the best wines and other liquors. The establishment has been well and plainly furnished throughout, including new mattresses and bed furniture. The steamers which ply between the District and Baltimore and Norfolk furnish to the inhabitants of those cities regular opportunities for visiting and departing from the Pavilion.

Your constituents do not demand the creation of a sinking fund out of the receipts derived from roads and canals, as they may arise, and from such other sources as may be under the control of the state, for the payment of the interest on our debt, and the ultimate redemption of the principal. In the acceptance of the proposition from the Government of the United States, contained in the act supplementary to the act providing for the admission of Michigan into the Union, the Legislature submitted to Congress for their consideration, the proposition, "that five hundred thousand acres of unappropriated public lands be granted to the State for purposes of internal improvements within the peninsular counties;" and "that there be granted for the construction of a road from the mouth of the Ontonagon river of Lake Superior, to some point on Green Bay, one section of land for each mile. Also, "that all roads commenced by the United States within the State, and remaining unfinished, should be completed by them. This proposition is too reasonable in its charter to need enforcing by any system of argument. The neighboring states of Ohio, Indiana and Illinois, have received repeated donations in land from Congress for roads and canals, and no reasonable objection can exist, why the same liberality should not be extended to Michigan.

After which Mr. Adams called for the reading of a portion of the journal which contained the facts in relation to a petition presented by Mr. Moore yesterday from citizens of this District praying Congress to give countenance to abolition memorials. The Speaker stated that, from the absence of sufficient physical force in the Clerk's office, the journal on that day could not be, and never had been, prepared in time to be read on the following day. Mr. Adams demanded the reading of the journal; and persisting in the demand, much uproar ensued. Several gentlemen endeavored to obtain the floor, but the Speaker declared that he would recognize no member until order was restored. And order having been restored, some minutes elapsed in the preparation of the journal, when it was produced, and the entry having been read as follows: "Mr. Moore presented a petition of citizens of the District of Columbia, praying that Congress will give neither support nor countenance to a band of fanatics embracing men, women, and children, who cease not day or night to crowd the tables of Congress with their seditious memorials upon the subject of slavery, having a tendency to disturb the integrity of the Union. A brief statement of the contents of this petition was made by Mr. M, when it was laid on the table."

Delegates.—The remarks we lately made on this subject, and the recommendation which accompanied them, have, we are happy to see, excited public attention to a consideration of the subject; and if they are instrumental in effecting a union of the Whig strength, at the meeting of the Legislature, upon any one gentleman, we shall be satisfied with our success. The name of Mr. Janney has been everywhere favorably received; and however much other gentlemen may be preferred in other quarters, every confidence is expressed in his patriotism and abilities. In addition to Mr. Janney, we have seen the names of Thomas Marshall, of Fauquier, James McDowell of Rockbridge, John T. Brown of Petersburg, Edward Colston of Berkeley, Geo. W. Summers of Kanawha, and Joseph C. Cabell of Nelson, mentioned as suitable persons to be elected. Either of these gentlemen would do for us. We hope the members of the Legislature will be on the ground two or three days in advance of the opening of the session. The propriety of this will be obvious upon an examination of the matter. Maine Election.—The Portland Advertiser of last Friday evening says: By our returns thus far received, the Whig candidate for Governor has 13784 more votes than last year, showing a net gain over our opponents of six thousand one hundred and eighty-six votes.

The following extract, which we have been kindly permitted to copy, of a letter from an intelligent citizen in Maryland to his friend in this city, shows that the vote of Maryland is certain for Martin Van Buren in November. The letter is dated Deer Park, Oct. 12, 1840, and the writer says: I cautioned you the other day in my letter against drawing any inference from the state elections unfavorable to the vote of this State in November. In the counties where large ma. jorities are in our favor, such as this (Baltimore County) many of our friends could not be prevailed on to go to the polls on the 7th instant, because as they alleged, they were satisfied that our ticket would succeed. In counties where the majorities are large against us, our friends could not be persuaded to go to the polls, be. cause they said they had not strength enough to carry their ticket. Indeed in some of the counties on the Eastern shore, where we usually poll from 2,500 to 2,800 votes, we had no ticket out. If we had had a proper organiza. tion—which I regret to say we had not, this would not have been so. The whigs who were drilled like regulars, no doubt brought out every man they could claim, and yet with all this perfect discipline their aggregate ma.

Time was when senators wished to witness a political gladiatorial conflict, they went to the other house of congress, where a younger, a more enthusiastic and a fiercer spirit prevailed. Now, sir, we see, day by day, members of the house crowding in to witness our spasmodic exhibitions. Is it wonderful, Mr. president, in the exciting circumstances of party conflict that even the spirit of senators should effervesce so violently as to overflow the just measure of their authority, and lead the senate directly to the adoption of measures trenching upon the constitutional rights of others! I believe most sincerely, that we have proceeded against the executive department of the government and against the president of the United States, in such a mode as to impair the just constitutional powers of the one and the personal and political rights of the other—that it was the duty of the president, in defense of the rights and powers of the executive department and his privilege as an officer and a free man, to send us the protest. Notwithstanding, Mr. president, the matters of grave import, contained in the several resolutions submitted by the senator from Mississippi, the first question presented for decision, will be upon the amendment offered by the hon. gentleman from Kentucky, (Mr. Bibb.)—That question is, shall the paper be rejected?

In a free, or self-government, the col lected wisdom, the aggregate will of the whole, or at least of a majority, mould and direct the course of public af fairs. In despotism, the will of a single individual gov erns. In a practically free government, the nation controls bank and other payments and receipts. In one day amounted to two millions of dollars. How many men and how many days would be necessary to count such a sum? A young, growing, and enterprising People, like those of the United States more than any other, need the use of those credits which are incident to a sound paper system. Died, in Ludlow, on the first day of the month, Moses Mayo, aged 93 years. Dea. Mayo was an officer in the Baptist church more than 40 years, during which time he was one of the brightest ornaments of the church. Always scrupulously pious, he accomplished great good in his day, both in his own church and in others where he was called a counsellor and an exhorter, effectively laboring to bring sinners to Jesus Christ, and promote union and prosperity in the church.

The most brilliant and original writer in this species of literature, was the great original Joe Strickland, whose writings were short, brief, and witty. They were written by George W. Davis, of this city, the second Jack Downing—now some person caricatures them under the name of Jonathan Slick. "We shall review all these imitators, and place the great Joe Strickland in his proper position. Ville de Lyon.?No intelligence has yet reached us of this ship. A Havre packet was announced be low last evening and it was said to be the Ville de Lyon, but it proved to be the Silvie de Grasie. The news was too good to be true. The V. de L. sailed from Havre on the 6th of October, and has therefore been sixty-one days on the passage, but has probably put into Madeira, Lisbon or Fayal, most likely the latter, for provision and to add a spar or two.

The trial of Louis Bonaparte is going on before a court of 170 Peers. He answers with boldness and firmness the questions propounded to him. He was asked why he had made the attempt to get up a revolution. "Because," said he, "the country has been badly governed for the last 10 or 15 years. I wished to see France restored." The Jews. The persecutions of the Jews at Damascus is likely to be brought to a close. Sir Moses Montefiore had an audience with the viceroy of Egypt, who has granted him a firman to visit Damascus; but advised him not to meddle politically with affairs there, but to confine himself to comforting his people. At the same time he has issued orders for a full investigation of the whole affair; and decreed that the absent Jews be permitted to return to Damascus, and put in possession of their property. The Rev. Mr. Samuel, of Bombay, states that he has discovered, and for several months lived among, the remnant of the ten tribes of Israel, located on the southwest shores of the Caspian Sea, and surrounded by a circle of mountains.

While a party contest the succession was thus organized in Congress and in the Cabinet, and of the prominent friends of Mr. Calhoun introduced a resolution, which, contemplated by a retrospective provision, to amend the Constitution, so as to exclude Gen. Jackson from being eligible to a re-election, those who urged this measure in the House of Representatives, did not hope absolutely to disfranchise the President obtaining such an amendment, they moved it as a means of bringing a general principle to operate on him alone, and by obtaining a vote on the abstract proposition, to urge it as the sense of the representatives of the people against his re-election. In this mode was the war waged against the fame and influence of the man who was elevated by the voice of the people, and who was again summoned by them to become a candidate, because he had realized all their hopes, as the reformer of abuses in government, and was securing the rights of our citizens and adjusting difficulties of the country. The situation of the President was now easily to be perceived. With a Cabinet politically divided, and personally, as may be presumed, not very friendly, it was impossible for him to move along in the arduous duties of his station, with satisfaction to himself, or advantage to the country.

We are of opinion that a great proportion of these sad occurrences originate, not in what may be properly termed accident, but in culpable neglect or carelessness, occasioned either by the competition and rivalry of the proprietors of public conveyances, or the restless spirit of travelers, who, rather than be a moment behind a certain time, or than be outdone in their peregrinations, sacrifice safety to speed, and would rather hazard life itself, than submit to be beaten in the race. Our countrymen have become proverbial for their impatience of delay. If they move at the rate of ten miles per hour, they are anxious to increase their speed to twelve. This attained, and the next object is fourteen; the next sixteen; and so on; and even should it be raised to an hundred, still they would complain of the want of despatch, and a little faster yet, would be the general cry. Thus the proprietors and conductors of travelling establishments are driven into rivalry for speed with each other to obtain patronage, and, by so doing, are prevented from using that degree of caution necessary to ensure safety; and to satisfy this propensity to go ahead, hundreds of lives are yearly sacrificed, and an incalculable amount of suffering inflicted. And who is to blame for all this?

Three assessors, viz: B. B. Fontaine, Wat. R. Colgin, and H. P. Cochran, are hereby appointed for the present year. Vacancies may be filled at any time after resignation or non-acceptance. The Secretary shall deliver to one of the assessors a table with blank columns according to the forms used last year, and the assessors shall advertise the time and place at which they will attend to receive the tax lists of every person subject, or owning property subject to the corporation taxes, who shall, at some one of the times advertised, give or have given by an agent on oath, to the assessors, a full list of his, her, or their property and polls, as aforesaid, and an estimate of the value of the personal property as aforesaid, under the penalty of ten dollars; and from the statements so furnished, and such other information as they may obtain, the assessors shall make out a full tax list and deliver the same to the Secretary on or before the 15th day of May next, for which they shall be entitled to, and receive three dollars each for every day's attendance.

Comrades—Circumstances connected with the battle of the 21st, render our separation for the present unavoidable. I cannot express to you the many painful sensations which that necessity inflicts upon me. I am solaced however, by the hope that we will soon be re-united in the great cause of Liberty.— Brigadier General Rusk is appointed to command the Army for the present. I confide in his valor, his patriotism and his wisdom— his conduct in the battle of San Jacinto was sufficient to ensure your confidence and regard.

Fayrer, arrived at New York on Monday morning last. She brought London papers to the evening of the 21st July, Liv- erpool to the 1st of August, and Paris to the 17th July. She left Liverpool at 2 o’clock on the afternoon of the 1st of August, being her fifth voyage to New York. The berths had all been engaged for some time previous to her departure, and so great was the de- mand that sixty, seventy, eighty and a hun- dred pounds had been offered for persons to give up their berths. The number of pas- sengers being limited to a hundred and thir- teen, no others of course were taken. Among the passengers were Signor and Madame Guibilei, Mr. Charles Kean, and several per- sons of distinction.

The Senator laments that appeals have been made to party feelings on this side of the Senate; to parties which have long been extinct. Sir, the Senator from Maryland, on this point, is mistaken. Whatever changes of name they may assume; however variable individuals may prove in their politics, the old parties in this country have not only not become extinct, but never can be extinguished. Their principles remain the same. It is the Aristocracy perpetually warring with the Democracy, which originated the two parties into which the people of the United States have always been divided, and which will continue to distinguish their history to the end of time. I regret, sir, that the name of Democracy has lately lost its charms, its magic influence, over my friend from Maryland. The fact is a sad presage of his future political career. The Senator says, that the American people are crying to us for bread, and we give them vinegar; we plunge them into woe. Sir, I have heard no such cries. Clamors, indeed, have reached us from Wall Street, and kindred scenes of speculation and panic. A few petitions from two or three other cities have been presented to us.

Resolved, That we heartily concur with the friends of that "measure of deliverance and liberty," the Independent Treasury System, which if established on a firm basis, as a permanent law of the nation, will make our public funds independent of all banks, or any other corporations or power on earth, except Congress, who are the immediate representatives of all the people of these United States, whose property the public funds are, and not that of bank directors or stockholders. Resolved, That the establishment of a National Bank "Of discount and Deposit," with a capital of fifty millions, allowed to have numerous branches, and made the depository of all our national pecuniary resources, will place a financial lever in a single hand, (irresponsible to any department of Government,) one touch of which, whether prompted by motives of interest or ambition, can trample on those sacred revered rights of the States, (in which we much confide) by giving foreign and domestic exchanges, the notes of our State banks, property, produce and labor, a value to suit its purposes from the location of the principal Bank to the very borders of the remotest State, besides, having the custody of our Government funds, can (by suspending specie payments at the pleasure of the directors) change their character after being deposited, by collecting officers, before disbursed.

At length the sense of the words, which at first fell indistinctly and dull on her perceptive sense, I became apparent to her. Again, and yet again, she read it. “Father, ah here, he has not addressed this? you did not say he had confessed this? it is false, it! is impossible!—do not believe it!—if an angel from Heaven-” “My child, my child! Do you imagine that if one ray of hope had existed, your father would have detained it from you? Do you suppose that he would have suffered such a blow to fall upon you, if one chance, however remote, had existed of warding it off? Sophia, as I hope for mercy —it is true! he has confessed it all.” “Then father,” said Sophia, rising from her seat, and with a firm, calm, fixed countenance and tearless eye, laying the letter on the table, “then father, the die is cast—all is over between him and me.” Her voice was clear and distinct as if she had uttered words of every day import. She paused an instant, then deliberately drew from her finger the ring—the pledge of her engagement to William Talbot—which had never left that finger since he had placed it there;—with a firm hand undid the ribbon which supported round her neck the locket he had given her, containing his hair, and placing them beside the letter, said, in the same clear, calm voice—“There!

This line connects at Wheeling with the mail line for Frederick, (Maryland) at which place the Railroad cars to Baltimore and Phila, delphia, offer a cheap and rapid passage; also, with transit up and down the Ohio, by Steam. boat passage almost always in waiting, and of. fer opportunities for conveyance to Cincinnati, New Orleans, St. Louis, and intermediate points, also to Steubenville, Pittsburgh and other points in that direction. At WOOSTER,  This line leaves after the arrival of the Western and Northern Mails, for Cleveland, Norwalk, to Mansfield, and intersects the Ohio Canal at Canal Dover, where packets are constantly running in the direction of Massillon to Cleveland, and also to Columbus, Chillicothe and Portsmouth on the Ohio, and intersects at Cadiz the Steu benville line. At WOOSTER, This line intersects the Norwalk line, which line passes through Ashland to Norwalk, for Huron and Detroit, also at Wooster with the line to Mansfield, Bucyrus, and with a line at the last place for Lower Sandusky. This Line for the past ten years has been run eight months in the year in Coaches and the re- maining 4 months on horseback. The present Contract compels the Contractors to carry the Mail in Carriages, the whole year, of course no apprehension need be indulged by the pub- lic of any disappointment from season or weather.

Any three or more inhabitants of any unincorporated place, may apply in writing to one of the County Commissioners of the County in which such place is situated, whose duty it shall be to issue his warrant to one of said applicants, directing him to notify and warn a meeting of the electors of said place, within such limits as shall be described in such warrant at some specified central place by posting up notice thereof, and also of the object, in two or more public places in said unincorporated place, seven days before the day of the said meeting, and at the time and place appointed, a Moderator shall be chosen by ballot, whose duty it shall be to preside in said meeting. And three Assessors and a Clerk shall also be chosen at the same time, who shall be sworn by the Moderator or a Justice of the Peace. And the limits of all incorporations, organized, shall be described by said Assessors, so chosen, and forwarded to the Secretary of State, and by him recorded.  Sec. 2. Be it further enacted.

Enquirer of May 20th, communicates further particulars of Indian murders committed near that town. Further news from Roanoke. A company of armed men from Randolph county, hearing of the destruction of Roanoke, repaired immediately to the spot. They arrived there yesterday afternoon, and found the site of the village in possession of a company of Indians, headed by a white man. They charged them with spirit, and killed three of the Indians and their white leader. The balance succeeded in gaining the swamp, and escaped, leaving their pursuers in possession of their killed. The Western Mail is entirely stopped. We have no news from that quarter since Tuesday last, and it is impossible to say when we shall again have any communication with Montgomery, Mobile, etc. The Indians have taken all the stages attempting to go through, and murder the drivers and passengers, as many as fell into their hands. We think the contractors perfectly justifiable in stopping the mail, as it is next to impossible to get even a horseman through the nation. Mr. Russell, one of the persons supposed to have been killed in the first attack upon the stages, has since come in and the two contractors and another passenger from the last stages have also arrived.

NOTICE. WAS COMMITTED to the jail of Frederick County, on the 9th day of July inst., as a runaway, a Negro Man, who calls himself RICHARD WEDDING. He is about 45 years old, very decrepit, so much so, that he cannot stand erect, and in walking is compelled to use his hands. Had on when committed a pair of bleached cotton pants, drab cloth vest, bleached cotton shirt and old black fur hat, and says he is a shoe maker by trade and belongs to Samuel R. Strider, who resides near Harper's Ferry, in Jefferson County, Va.— The owner, if any, is hereby requested to come and have him released, he will otherwise be discharged according to law. A subscriber to the Liberator in Macon tarred and feathered, carried on a rail, ducked into the river, and taken to the whipping-post, by a mob, for presuming to take the paper. Five thousand dollars offered for the apprehension of either the editor or the publisher of the Liberator, by the Senate of Georgia. Alderman Binns presented by the Grand Jury of Scriven County, for having proposed to a Convention in Philadelphia the purchase and colonizing of all the slaves. KENTUCKY. A slave hung for attempting to commit a rape. Another for the murder of a Mrs. Dodd. Two others for attempting to poison their master. Three of her own children drowned by a female slave, in consequence of her being chastised by her master. SOUTH CAROLINA. A slave hung for having (as the Charleston papers state) accidentally set on fire a cotton factory! Peter, a slave, executed for having slightly wounded a white man. Also the slave Glasgow, for attempting to poison his master. A reward of $1500 offered by the Vigilance Association of Charleston, for the apprehension and conviction of any white person detected in circulating the Liberator in that quarter. TENNESSEE. A black girl, afflicted with the smallpox, burnt to death (supposed purposely) in a lone building. FLORIDA. A female slave shot at Pensacola by Lieut. Wm. H. Baker, of the U.S. Army. No record of the punishment of the murderer. ALABAMA.

The votes for Governor have not yet been counted. LATE FROM ENGLAND. The packet ships United States and England, arrived at New York on Saturday evening last, bringing London and Liverpool papers to the 8th ult, ten days later than before received. The political news is of but little interest. Parliament, it was presumed, would assemble about the middle of January. The Queen’s intended marriage continued to be a matter of public interest. The best informed Journals put faith in the rumor that the British Government intends a blockade of the Chinese ports. The Great Western went out in thirteen and a half days, and the news she carried out was considered better, and had a decidedly favorable effect upon American interests, unless the decline in cotton be considered an exception, which was caused by a report of the state of the crops here. Great distress existed among the operatives in the Manufacturing districts. France.—The life of the King is still in danger. Plots have been discovered, and undeniable evidence that infernal machines have been constructed. The palace of the Tuileries is turned into a fortress, strongly garrisoned; and whenever the King ventures forth, he is surrounded with such troops of guards that his movements rather resemble those of an army than of an individual. Fourteen days Later FROM CHINA.—Advices have been received from Canton to August 6th, and from Macao to the 11th, via Mazatlan.

This precious morceau was handed him by half-witted portrait painter, rejoicing in the name of Jerome W. Thompson, but whose proper appellation is John Thompson. Mr. V. read the note, smiled, and returning it to the messenger of Mars, told him not to bother him, but to be off. Mr. Thompson flared up, and informed Mr. V. with an assumption of dignity, somewhat ridi culously seated on the shoulders of a loutish bear, that he, John Thompson, should hold any insult offered to his friend as matter of personal consid eration to him, Master John Thompson. Mr. Vaneppe smiled again, told the belligerent portrait painter to leave the note, and the next moment sent a pair of police officers to settle time and place with Messrs. Dewey and Thomas. PRACTICAL BENEVOLENCE.—The Editor of the Boston Transcript, in crossing Hancock-street last week, which was flooded by a thaw, encountered a stranger on an ice bridge, over which but one could pass at a time. To retreat was impossible without plunging into the water.

Bills on London 10 percent premium. New York, 1 disc. New Orleans, 4 disc.  Money in demand at 12 to 15 percent per annum. — This will continue until the crops are worked off. [From the Circular to Bankers, April 24.] One element of uneasiness and disturbance in banking and money affairs may be regarded as neutralized, and for a time satisfactorily settled. Whatever amount of British capital may have been required for purchases of foreign corn during the last three or four months has been expended during that period. No additional sum can, we conclude, under present circumstances, be required in that channel of trade, as far as Europe is concerned; and we care not how much flour the Americans may think proper to put into our ports. The Directors of the Bank of England will not feel much anxiety at any imports of this description from the United States; they would assist the payments of the dividends on their stocks, a large amount of which will fall due in July next. It is worthy of notice that almost all the corn, and especially wheat, which has recently been sent to England and is now arriving from the continent, is on consignment.

Meetings of the directors of the several banks were immediately held, and a like course was agreed upon without loss of time. The Richmond Compiler says: This course by our banks, was one unavoidable and adopted in justice to the banks themselves, to the trade and interests of Richmond, and to the state, which is a large stockholder in them all. For some time our banks have had to supply heavy demands upon them from the North for specie; and there were in this city yesterday morning, several Northern brokers, awaiting the hour of 9 o'clock, to make large drafts upon them. Our banks did not hesitate, and they ought not to have hesitated. They are justified by our citizens. Upon the announcement of their determination all was calm, and there was a general indication of satisfaction at the event. The people die. culation is, probably! The Philadelphia banks issue notes of no less denomination than $10. The fives already issued are called in and redeemed. Also the ones put out by the Girard Bank. The suspension of the Providence banks agreed on, it is alleged, under the prevalent impression that the New York banks must go down. It is urged as a ground of action.

March 4th, 1833. On this day may the Union be cemented by the native clay of Kentucky. Our Union. The Palladium of our Liberty; palsied be the arm that attempts to destroy it. Agriculture, Manufactures, and Commerce. Three respectable citizens doing business in company: whenever they shall dissolve copartnership, we are all bankrupts. Our National Government. Pure in its principles, but degraded by demagogues and office seekers. Lafayette. The early and powerful friend of the American Independence; the champion of universal freedom. The Tariff as it is. Its benign effect on our national prosperity is evident and cheering to every honest man: confusion to the efforts of selfish and unprincipled demagogues who are striving to abolish it. Water power, steam power, horse power, dog power, any power that will drive Machinery—but none of your power that would drive us into the service of demagogues and office seekers. Daniel Webster. Mighty among the great; whose unbending integrity and powerful intellect, causes the Nullifiers to quail before him. The Massachusetts Representative Districting System. Retrenchment a good thing; but, national liberty and equal rights for individuals, societies, and towns, without unnecessary excitement toward either, better. FOR THE MASSACHUSETTS SPY. CELEBRATION AT NORTH BORO’.

In the Orders issued in 1829, by the British Government, in St. Lucia, placing all freemen of African descent upon the footing of equal rights with their white neighbors, the loyalty and good conduct of that class are distinctly acknowledged, and they are declared 'to have shown, hitherto, readiness and zeal in coming forward for the maintenance of order.' As similar Orders have been issued for Trinidad, Berbice, and the Cape of Good Hope, it may be presumed that the conduct of the free blacks and colored persons in those Colonies has likewise given satisfaction to Government. [From the (N. Y.) Emancipator.] Friend Denison: —I wish to say a few words in the first number of the Emancipator. At some future day, I may enter more deeply into the matter, but for the present, I only wish to lay down a few plain, simple propositions, which I suppose everybody can understand. Every subject has its alphabet—its first principles, its fundamental truths, which only need to be stated, in order to be understood and as sented to. These principles, drawn out into their proper consequences, and followed into all their ramifications, constitute the true the ory and practice on the subject to which they refer. With these views I submit the following:— PROPOSITIONS ON SLAVERY.

Mrs Judson's powers of conversation were of the first order, and the many affecting anecdotes that she gave us of their long and cruel bondage, their struggles in the cause of religion and their adventures during a long residence at the court of Ava, gained a heightened interest from the beautiful and energetic simplicity of her language, as well as from the certainty I felt that so fragile a flower, as she in very truth was, had but a brief season to linger on earth! Why is it that we grieve to think of the approaching death of the young and virtuous, the ready? Alas! it is the selfishness of human nature that would keep to itself the purest and sweetest gifts of heaven, to encounter the blasts and the blights of a world where we see them, rather than that they should be transplanted to a happier region, which we suppose they do not. When I left the kind Judson's I did so with regret. When I looked my last on her mild, worn countenance, as she issued some instructions to my new set of boatmen, I felt my eyes fill with prophetic tears. They were not perceived; we parted, and we never met again; nor is it likely that the wounded subaltern was ever again thought of by those who had succored him. Mrs Judson, and her child, died soon after the cessation of hostilities. Horrible. The Woonsocket, R.

THE CHARGE OF THE COURT IN THE TRIAL OF In the Oyer and Terminer for Columbia county, January Term, 1838, Judge LEWIS delivered the following charge to the Jury. Gentlemen of the Jury: Thomas T. Poke, the prisoner at the bar, is charged with the crime of murder. The indictment charges him with the murder of John Dornell by striking him upon the head with an axe on the 25th of November, 1837. To this indictment the prisoner has pleaded "not guilty," and has put himself upon his country for trial. You are that country, and it is for you, after you have heard the necessary instructions from the court upon matters of law, to determine upon his guilt or innocence. If he should be found guilty of murder of the first degree, his punishment is death. It is therefore of immense importance that we should give the case our most careful and solemn deliberation in order that we may arrive at a correct conclusion. We must guard against the commission of any errors which we may never have it in our power to recall or correct.

C. for the north, was passing over Lynch's Creek the bridge gave way and precipitated it, together with the driver, Mr. Broadfoot, and the Rev. Mr. Powers, into the water, a distance of between twenty and thirty feet. Fortunately, however, no serious injury was sustained. BANK DIRECTORS. They estimate the whole of them at the close of the year to be $10,549,905. VI. A few more explanations of other grounds for the estimates of receipts and expenditures for the next year may be useful. It will be noticed that the estimates for both are founded principally on the existing laws. Should Congress, therefore, alter the tariff, so as to increase or reduce the duties, the expected amount of receipts must of course be varied in that proportion. So it must be if Congress makes any essential change in respect to the public lands, and either passes a graduation bill, and creates new land districts whose surveys are ready for large sales, as this would increase the immediate receipts, or if, on the other hand, it should distribute the proceeds of the sales among the States, as that would diminish the revenue applicable to the purposes of the General Government, and render a resort to new taxation, an increased tariff, or a loan, indispensable, to the extent of the distribution. The estimates of the receipts from customs have been lessened somewhat, because the importation of certain articles paying a duty will, in 1841, be partially postponed to 1842, in consequence of the great reduction in the tariff on them which will take place in the latter year under the existing laws.

The volunteers who have enrolled themselves, within the last two or three days, for Florida, were passed in review last evening. They amount to 200 able-bodied men, of very fine appearance. We have every reason to believe that this number will be augmented, before the day fixed for their departure, to 900 men. U.S. General Gaines, of the U.S. Army, has made a requisition upon Gov. White, for eight companies of Militia, to be in readiness to march at a moment's warning. St. Karlin gaz. The Legislature of Louisiana has passed two appropriation bills for the relief of the citizens of Florida—the amount granted by both is $80,000. The Governor is authorized to call on the citizens to volunteer, and to incur the necessary expenses of forwarding them to Florida. The Legislature [of Louisiana,] has made an appropriation of 10,000 dollars for the purchase of swords, muskets, pistols. They have not expressed an opinion concerning the state of our relations with France. Red River Herald. We are informed that a regiment of troops is hourly expected from St. Louis, and as great a number as can be spared was to have been sent down in the Huntsville from Baton Rouge yesterday. A concentration of these forces here will probably amount to 700 men, and these joined to 800 volunteers, which it is expected will be raised here, making altogether 1000, will be despatched for Florida in the course of a few days.

I thought when he found it necessary to renew happiness to my inner way land, I should obtain it, but was mistaken; his ambition and inordinate love of wealth supplanted every other feeling. And I learnt—but too late—that I had sacrificed all my hopes of happiness to it. For weeks and months I labored with him but in vain. He sternly refused to release me from my promise, and bade me banish all thoughts of way land. I resolved to see him—tell him all, and then bury my love in oblivion. I sent him a note requesting him to meet me in the arbor, at the foot of the garden. He came—but why dwell upon that hour? Suffice it to say it was one of anguish unutterable to me. I have never seen him since. I heard the next day he had sailed for India. I have never heard from him, and suppose if he is living he is there still. In six months from that time I married Bernard Westermann, your father. I respected and obeyed him as my husband. I admired his noble mind and generous disposition, but I could not love him as I ought.

It is ordered by the court that the said Wesley Hunt, be notified that an action of debt, for the sum of thirty dollars, hath been commenced against him by the said Samuel Finley, that a writ of attachment hath been issued against him and the real estate aforesaid attached, and that unless he, the said Wesley Hunt, be and appear, (by himself or Attorney,) before this court, on or before the third day of the next term thereof, to be holden at the Court House, in the town of Bowling Green, within, and for said county of Pike, on the second Monday of July next, and plead to this action according to law, a judgment will be rendered against him, and his said real estate will be sold to satisfy the same. And it is further ordered by the court, that a copy of this amended order, be published for four weeks successively in some newspaper printed in this state; the last insertion whereof, to be at least twenty days before the next term of this court. And this cause is continued. State of Missouri, County of Pike.

Whistle away, then, over Missouri and Arkansas, for, as regards Missouri, it is whistling over a State saved, while the Whigs “sound the banjo” over a empire regained.—N. Y. Ex. Missouri, after all the boasting and bragging of the collar presses, will not probably give the Van Buren candidate for Governor over 500 majority. So says the Boonville Herald of Aug. 19th. Fated Affray.—A dreadful case of violent death occurred in this city yesterday; the unfortunate affair took place in Front st., corner of Old Slip. Thomas Brennan, a bricklayer, was engaged in laying some bricks on the gable end of the house erecting, when he requested Andrew McGrath, a hodman, to place some bricks near him for use, and the other (perhaps not understanding him) did not do as desired, when Brennan pushed his plumb-board towards McGrath, not however touching him, and proceeded on with his work, and thought nothing more of the affair; but McGrath grumbled, and getting some feet in the rear of Brennan seized a brick and hurled it at his head, which striking him near the temple, as he was in a stooping posture, precipitated him to the ground below, a distance of 50 or 90 feet, breaking his neck by the fall. The deceased had been married but four or five months —N. Y. Star.

It may be here remarked, that, hitherto, the greater part of the English Works, maps, etc. seen on the continent of Europe, whether British or American, have been supposed to be British. An important branch of commerce would thus at once be opened to the United States, the products of which would be spread through the Old World, where an opportunity would at the same time be afforded for also spreading its arts, sciences, and influence. In the next place, let a certain number of newly invented machines or instruments be given by the inventor, to be sent, namely, England, Russia, Germany, and other countries, by which means the inventor may secure himself and his native land the honor of the discovery, and the right of priority in industry. Moreover, the exhibition of these models will procure for him, wherever the instruments or machines are admired, besides enabling him to avoid the loss of time and money, and the inventor in many cases is unable to meet the expenses requisite for obtaining a patent right in other countries, so that he is often deprived a balance will remain in the former nation, to be met by the receipt of and remittance from the old world. The exchange of weights, measures, and coins, would be highly advantageous, particularly to the United States, which is, and must be always, a commercial nation.

It is ordained by the President and Selectmen of the Town of Manchester, that all owners of property lying in said town, be hereby required, at their own expense, to pave or cause to be paved, in and along the front thereof, on all the streets now graded and guttered, and that in the event of their failure so to do, then the Town Constable be, and is hereby required to do the same at the expense of the said owner or owners of said town property. Sect. 2. And be it further ordained, That if in the event, any owner or owners of the property aforesaid, shall, upon application of the Town Constable for the payment of all expenses which may accrue therefrom in the event of said failure, refuse or fail to comply with the requisitions of this Ordinance, then the said Constable shall have full power to expose for sale, at public auction, to the highest bidder, the property of said owner or owners so required to be paved as aforesaid, to satisfy all expenses for paving as aforesaid, after first having given notice thereof, for the space of thirty days in some newspaper published in said town. Proceedings of the City Council. Yazoo City, Sept. 7, 1840.

But. 'these votes, it was ascertained, if counted, would take up the bill,'' etc. The vote was then announced, ayes 74; noes 77: so the House refused to go into Committee on the Bill." By some rule of calculation taught in the new school of Locofocoism, the initiated are enabled, by the process of adding three to seventy-four, to obtain a greater sum than seventy-seven, thereby producing the extraordinary result of a majority from equal numbers. But this exploit exceeds the powers of their political arithmetic, without resorting to that species of management by which one of the deadliest enemies of the bill is made to vote in its favor. What! carry a proposition, by an equal vote, for and against it; that is to say, by a tie? The assertion was known by him who first made it, and by all who have since repeated it, to be an impudent falsehood. You may have noticed, at the time they appeared in the Statesman, and other kindred prints along the line of the road, certain letters from Washington, in which the writer predicted, several weeks before Congress acted on the bill, that it would be defeated, and charged, that if it did fail, it would be owing to the misconduct of the Western Whig, in not voting to suspend the rules to receive a motion to make the appropriation bills the special order of the day.

At advantage of introducing the goods, required for carrying on the trade, free of duty. In the Rocky Mountain expedition undertaken two or three years ago, by the American Fur Company, they sustained a loss of $60,000, not being able to compete with the Hudson Bay Co. for the reason above stated. The branch of the latter company in the Columbia has obtained the present season about one hundred packs of Beaver, worth at least $40,000; two thirds of which has been taken on the territory claimed by the United States. With this competition, the American Fur Company have found it necessary to confine their trade to the Missouri River and its tributaries—leaving the uncontrolled possession of the Rocky Mountains and the Oregon Territory to the English Company. The Hudson Bay Company now extend their trade on this side of the Mountains, even to within fifty days traveling of this city; and many of the fur hunters who were formerly in the service of the American, have found it necessary to apply for employment in the British company. This branch of trade which is entitled to the same protection from our government that our commerce receives on the ocean, and yet it is entirely neglected. If a drawback were allowed upon the goods employed in the trade, we might be able to come in competition with the Hudson Bay Company, but even this is denied.

Between the Old and the New World, there has long existed a mutual spirit of enterprise in the exchange of commercial objects. But it belongs to Mr. Vattemare to have suggested a national interchange of intellectual matter, both as regards literature and the fine arts. I hope the enlightened nations of the world will join cheerfully in bringing to consummation a project of such universal interest. DAN'L. STURGEON, Pennsylvania. June 20, 1840. *Representatives. As a member of the committee on the Library of Congress, I have had occasion to examine the project of Mr. Alexandre Vattemare, and most heartily approve of it. I sincerely hope that he may be successful in its establishment. CHAS NAYLOR, of Philadelphia. Mr. Vattemare's active benevolence, and the kind and Catholic spirit exhibited in his plan, entitle him to much higher evidence of consideration than is afforded by complying with his very moderate request. In view of these considerations, it was unanimously resolved that "it is expedient" to form a Peace Society. A Society was accordingly organized and a committee of five appointed to take a general superintendence of its concerns. The Society has held six meetings during the year. At these meetings, addresses have been delivered, and articles read from the peace publications furnished by the parent Society. We have received from Mr. Ladd a number of copies of the tracts of the London Peace Society, copies of the Calumet, and other publications on the subject. Also, a hundred copies of the "essays on the duty of females to promote the cause of Peace," a very valuable tract which we recommend to the perusal of all who wish to know how females may aid this cause. We have also received from the same source 50 copies of the "Solemn Appeal." These pamphlets have been gratuitously distributed. We shall be asked whether we embrace the doctrine of non-resistance. It would perhaps be most becoming in us to suspend our opinion, or at least the expression of it, while the wisest part of the community is so much in doubt. But our children will ask us, and we must answer them.

He has successfully demonstrated the fact that a novel may be written and a good one too without any resort to historical associations, or to legends of the past, or to the refinements and complications of a more artificial state of society, or to the passions of ruder life, or in short to anything beyond the actual events and impressions of the day, as they are developed around us and in the midst of us. Now this we hold to be no small achievement. It is the overthrow of a prejudice, the refutation of a prevalent error and betokens strength as well as originality of intellect. The author withholds his name; but if success can induce him to step forth from his concealment, we have no fears that we shall be long in ignorance of one to whom we are under obligations for so much pleasure. Com-. J- 117. Another victim to the demon Alcohol was immolated in our streets yesterday. Mr. William McPherson, about 50 years of age, father of a respectable family in Caledonia, came to town with a load of wheat, sold it, loaded several barrels of salt, and started for home. While here, he became so much intoxicated as to be incapable of managing his spirited team, and after various fantastic feats, he fell to the ground and one wheel passed over his head, crushing it in a most horrible manner, causing almost instantaneous death.

Instead of fulfilling the promises then held out, the Government now wraps itself up in its dignity to tell the people that they expect too much of it; that it is not its business to furnish exchanges; and that they may look to Europe for the manner in which, through the agency of private bankers, the commerce and business of its countries are supplied with exchange. We are advised to give up our American mode of transacting business, through the instrumentality of banking corporations, in which the interests of the rich and the poor are happily blended, and to establish bankers similar to the Houses, the Barings, the Rothschilds, the Hottinguers of Europe; houses which require years or ages to form and control, two solemn decisions as the Supreme Court of the United States; twenty years of successful experiments, and disastrous consequences quickly followed. We are also instructed, as a member of Congress, to take part in the discussion to which they gave rise, and had the opportunity of extensively knowing the opinions of members; and I declare with complete conviction that, upon neither was there one-third of the members in either House who entertained the opinion that Congress did not possess the constitutional power to charter a bank.

THE JOURNAL, SATURDAY, SEPTEMBER 12, 1840. Of The Democracy of Marion turned out on the 7th inst, in large numbers notwithstanding the unfavorable state of the weather, for the purpose of forming a Demo cratic association. Though outnumbered at the late election, they will renew their efforts, on behalf of the rights of the people, with a spirit that betokens their ultimate success, and their thorough confidence in the correctness of their principles. Several spirited resolutions were adopted, and steps were taken which if carried out will dispel the mists of deception which have been attempted to be cast over the minds of the honest Yeomanry of Marion. Judge Allen, Mr. Wells of Lincoln, Mr. Buckner of Pike, and Mr. Pearson each ad dressed the meeting. A delegation of 30 young men, were appointed to represent the county at Jefferson city, the present call. Let the young men of Old Pike follow up the example of Marion, and call a meeting for this purpose as soon as practicable. HON. HENRY CLAY AND GENERAL JACKSON.

It may be well enough to inform the gentleman that none of Mr. Van Buren's friends are getting tired of the principles set forth in his (Mr. Van Buren's) Messages, except it be a few who, like the gentleman himself, never were the friends of Mr. Van Buren, or the friends of Democracy, any longer than it suited their interest to be so, regardless of the interest of the people. We see in these instructions the part intended to be acted by the opposition, in order to deceive the people: "Talk not about who is to be our next President." Why not? Because if the people see our choice, thus early, they will go against us in our county next August, and we shall not have the pleasure of seeing Messrs. Brown and Strange instructed out of the Senate. Thus say the federal whigs, and thus they continue: We must keep the currency and public lands steadily before the people—on this point all must rally. We thus see the deception intended to be practiced upon the people of the county, by these opposition-modern whig federal leaders. The real end aimed at must not be disclosed, until by panic and pressure they get into power. Let Mr. Biddle's policy be felt (say the federal whigs); suffering must force the people to acknowledge the Banks to be their rulers.

Dunlop's troop of horse were patrolling in Berry's District, in this county, and on advancing near a house, Mr. Camper Yost and his horse were precipitated into a well upwards of forty feet deep. Strange as it may appear Mr. Yost did not receive the slightest injury. He was drawn up by a rope, by his companions, after remaining in the well probably half an hour. The horse was taken out the following day, and although considerably injured, it is thought he will recover. Mr. Yost remained seated on the saddle, until the horse struck the bottom of the well."—Rockville Press. Wheeling, (Va.) Oct. 8.

The Journal Saturday, November 23, 1839. The U. S. exploring expedition seems to have penetrated as far south as 70 degrees of latitude, during the past season when large masses of ice appearing in every direction, and the winter approaching, it was deemed imprudent to prosecute the voyage further at that time, and the expedition returned to Valparaiso and Callao, for the purpose of refitting; after which it proceeded to the Sandwich Islands, whence on the return of spring it was expected to go south again, intending to push into as high Southern latitude as the state of ice would allow. The spring being now far advanced in that region, it is supposed that the expedition has been sometime on the voyage South, which it is intended to prosecute with vigor, until forced by the return of the fall season, which will be our spring, to return towards the milder latitudes, when it is intended to visit the Columbia River, the Oregon Territory, and the neighboring coasts and islands, and return to the United States by the way of the Cape of Good Hope; which it is not expected can be accomplished, in less than two years from this time. "JUSTICE AND EQUALITY." THE FREE TRADER. Ottawa, Ill., Friday, November 19, 1841. Stupendous Election Fraud! Our exchange papers from the east come to hand this week, burdened with testimony elicited by a legal investigation now going on in New York City, developing the most stupendous election frauds committed by the whig leaders of New York, Philadelphia, and Baltimore. The disclosures there made, show a regular and magnificent system of fraudulent voting, which has been in practice for the last two years, and far outstrips anything of the kind ever heard of in this country. They lay open, too, some very singular facts connected with elections in New York, Philadelphia, and other places, and account for their unexpected results, which have hitherto been entirely inexplicable to the shrewdest politicians. We have not the room to publish the testimony which has been taken, but shall endeavor to give, in as condensed a form as possible, the leading facts connected with the affair. From the testimony it appears that, in October 1839, J. B. Glentworth, tobacco inspector of New York, at the instance of R. M. Blotehford, S. Dra per, Jas. Bowen, R. C. Wetmore, and Moses H.

We have authority to state that the number of signatures to the address of the ladies of England to the queen on behalf of the Negro apprentices, now exceeds 420,000. This is exclusive of the signatures to separate Addresses on the same subject from several large towns, such as Manchester, Hereford, and others; and, of course, does not include the signatures to the Addresses of the Ladies of Scotland and Ireland, where, we know, very large numbers have been obtained. If these were added, we believe the numbers would fall very little short, if any, of one million. This number would have been considerably exceeded had more time been given for signing; for the ladies, in a great majority of the towns, state that they could have sent three or four times the number they have sent, had that been the case. It is an interesting subject of reflection, that one million of the fairest, loveliest and best of human beings, have thus attested, by their own handwriting, how deeply they are interested in the sufferings of their feeble sisters on the distant shores of the Antilles. "A pianist, Samivel, a pianist!" said Mr. Weller, striking his son on the chest with the back of his hand, and falling back a step or two. "What do you mean?" said Sam. "A pianist for hire, Samivel," rejoined Mr. Weller in a still more mysterious manner, "as he can live on hire if he won't play, Sammy." "And what would be the good of that?" said Sam. "Let him send to my friend the cabinet maker to fetch it back, Sammy," replied Mr. Weller. "Are you awake now?" "No," rejoined Sam. "There ain't no works in it," whispered his father. "It will hold him easy with his hat and shoes on; and breathe through the legs which is hollower. Have a passage ready taken for 'Meriker. The 'Merrikin government will never give him up once they find as he's got money to spend, Sammy. Let the governor stop there till Mrs. Bardell's dead, or Mr. Dodson and Fogg's hung, which last event I think is most likely to happen first, Sammy, and then let him come back and write a book about the 'Merrikans, as 'll pay all his expenses and more, if he blows 'em up enough." Mr. Weller delivered this hurried abstract of his plot with great vehemence of whisper, and then, as if fearful of weakening the effect of the tremendous communication by any further dialogue, gave the coachman's salute, and vanished. From the Somerset N. J. Whig.

He called upon the members to organize, and made a very happy allusion to the boast of the Globe, that the administration were in the majority allotting the Whigs all the contested seats. He called upon that majority then to organize, or bear the blame. In his private opinion the rights of the people of New Jersey were at stake, and he did not believe that a majority of the House could say that the five members who had produced their credentials should be set aside and not permitted to vote. He said nothing of State sovereignty or the Governor of the State but of the people. Mr. A. spoke with much eloquence and force on this point and created quite a sensation in the House. Mr. A. said he proposed to put the question himself, and after he had concluded his remarks, there was much confusion. Mr. Rhett finally proposed that Lewis Williams become temporary chairman, "pro tem." to serve until the House was organized. Mr. W.

The Gazette of Lisbon of Oct. 3d, publishes a brief account of the battle between the forces of Don Pedro and those of Don Miguel on the 29th of Sept. It admits that the troops of Miguel were beaten back by those of the enemy but claims that the retreat was made "in good order". "Our troops," says the account, "after having given many proofs of courage, were obliged to retire and resume their former position, on account of the great strength of the enemy." London, Oct. 19. By the Paris papers of Wednesday, we learn some further particulars of the late Ministerial changes at Madrid, and of their ascertained cause. It seems now quite evident that while King Ferdinand was considered past recovery, and was about to receive extreme unction, another kind of anointment was preparing for his brother Don Carlos, by his treacherous apostolical Ministers. The Ministers with Calomarde at their head had actually formed a plot for cancelling the late act, by which his Catholic Majesty abolished the Salic Law—for setting aside the title of the infant princess of Asturias, and for proclaiming Charles V her uncle, King of Spain and the Indies.
