If the files of the Legislature were examined, and a statement of accounts allowed at the different sessions, laid before the people, consisting of bills for the repairs of the old house, merchants and mechanics bills for furniture, the greater part of which has been lost between the sessions, it would be additional evidence of the necessity there is for public buildings. The bills for the storage of public arms in Little Rock, which amounted to somewhere about one thousand dollars in the last ten years—now strange to tell, this smokehouse of Crittenden’s, which so much has been said about, would have answered as a safe repository for the public arms, and had such a house been purchased ten years ago, it would have produced a saving to the people of between $800 and $1000—Economy, however, is not the order of the day, and there are some members of society.

Such is a description of the resolutions offered today, and of the incidents that attended them. To note one-fiftieth of all, would require a volume as big as the family quarto Bible. Mr. Bell, of Tennessee, asked and obtained leave to introduce two bills, one to secure the freedom of elections; the other to prevent the abuse of Executive patronage. Neither can ever be passed, even if they are taken up, which is a very doubtful matter. They would never suit any party in power; but might answer the purposes of an opposition. Mr. Bell has introduced these bills the three last sessions successively. In the Senate, the morning hour was occupied in receiving reports and petitions. Several abolition petitions were presented by Mr. Sturgeon, of Pennsylvania, and the question of their reception was laid on the table. Mr. Linn, of Missouri, from the Select Committee to which the subject had been referred, made a very voluminous report on the Northwestern Boundary, conflicting with the claims of Russia and Great Britain. The report was written, as Mr. Linn stated, by Mr. Greenhow, of the Department of State, and is a document of very great importance. It was ordered to lie on the table, and an edition of 500 extra copies ordered to be printed. Mr. Grundy's Report on Mr. Benton's non-assumption resolutions was now called up as the order of the day.

The Message of the President, and Protocol of Mr. Fox and Mr. Forsyth, had arrived in England by the England packet ship, a few days before the sailing of the Great Western, and, to every appearance, caused no uneasiness or fluctuation in our political or commercial relations. The moderate tone of the President was respected, although the Tory papers attempted to attribute hostile views to our government. The "London Times," in a very elaborate article on the Boundary Question, comes out quite pacifically, and propounds an exchange of territory, in order to bring the matter to a termination. If the Tory opposition are favorable to a compromise, the ministry will be more so, and then all difficulties will soon be removed. In reply to a question put on the 15th ult. to Lord Palmerston, by Sir S. Canning, the former said "that both governments were actuated by a serious desire to obtain a satisfactory result." The affairs of Ireland, and the relations of France with Mexico, appear to engage more the attention of the British government and people than those of the United States. There appears also to be a strong probability that the Melbourne Cabinet has resigned on the subject of Irish affairs, but that does not at all alter the pacific position of England. A British special messenger arrived in the Great Western, and proceeded yesterday to Washington.

gates to attend the Convention to be held at Columbus, on the 22nd February. On mo tion, Mr. John Sergeant of Philadelphia, was recommended as a candidate for Vice Presi dent, to be run on the same ticket with Gen. Harrison;—The Whigs also advertised a meeting, on the 2nd inst., at Louisville, for the Purpose of nominating a candidate for the Presidency. But the friends of Van Buren rallied, returned a wing of the Republican party, and in a late num ber of the "New York Courier and Enquirer," the Whigs retired to the Court yard, and then dissented among themselves—Mr. Wilmington, the chief orator of the majority of the Committee, recommended Gen. Harrison "as the most suitable candidate."—Mr. Marshall, on the part of the minority, contended that by des ignating Gen. Harrison as "the most suitable candidate," a stigma would be cast on the fame of Henry Clay, as it would, in effect, be acknowledging that he was a broken down politician, and thereby confirming the declara tions of his enemies. It was also urged that by nominating several candidates, the Whigs were weakening their own cause; that if they could not defeat Van Buren by uniting on one candidate, it was the height of folly to suppose it could be done by dividing among several.

We say to the people, look to your agents your Representatives, in time. Their interests are at war with your principles. They are mixed in with, and are of course for the banks. What a mockery of the representative doctrine to find Philadelphia sending to the halls of the nation men who are either large stockholders in the Bank of the United States, or retained as its counsel. The same remark will apply to members from other quarters of the Union. It is a lie to call such men the Representatives of the people - they are the instruments of the bank, palmed on the community through its influence and intrigue, to do its biddings. Find men, intent on power. We again and earnestly call on the people to move in this matter. If presses, professing to advocate and sustain the cause of numbers, exhibit indifference for fear of losing patronage, the greater the necessity for the people to act. Their sentinels are either timid or corrupt. Let honest and fearless men come to the rescue, and check the insolence of monopoly. We learn from the Observer of yesterday, that W. R. Griffith, Esq. senator from Breckinridge, and Gen. James Taylor of Newport, "both decided whigs" have been appointed by the President visitors to West Point Academy; and for this act of liberality on the part of Van Buren, to the Whig party, the editor cannot avoid a sneer.

The following article from the New-Haven Palladium explains the matter fully and fairly. The Libel.—We are now prepared to prove that the charges against the Hon. W. W. Boardman of an attempt to defraud the Post Office, is a gross and malicious libel, and we have the best reasons for believing it was known to be so by the men who perpetrated it. The person who took the pamphlet referred to, and engaged to deliver it in Hartford, was Mr. Constant W. Kirkland. Owing to the late arrival of the stage in that city, Mr. K. had not time to deliver it in person, and he handed it to Mr. Rowe, of Hartford, who promised to see that it reached the person to whom it was addressed, and Mr. R. for his own convenience deposited it in the Post Office. The seal was there broken, and a few words having been found written in the letter covering the pamphlet, the Post Master, instead of asking one cent postage, (the usual charge in such cases,) had the audacity to demand thirty! and upon the gentleman's refusing to be swindled after this fashion, it was sent to New-Haven with directions to prosecute Mr. Boardman for an attempt to defraud the Government! Was there ever more consummate impudence?

“Dermot Mac Morrogh, or the Conquest of Ireland, an historical tale of the twelfth century, in four cantos, by John Quincy Adams.” This is the title of a neat pamphlet, of 106 pages, which came from the press of Carter, Hendee & Co. on Saturday. Nothing has occurred for a long time, in the history of literature, more remarkable than this. A poem—a playful, satirical, finished, polished poem, from the pen of the profound and austere statesman, the learned civilian, the deep scholar, the acute politician! And yet we really have in this poem another of those specimens so rare and so agreeable in the history of genius and effort at great versatility of talents: the union of the opposites of playful humor and profound research. The Ex-President has occasionally been known to have wooed the muses, in a few stanzas, but this is his first attempt at anything like a continuous poem. His friends might well have trembled for his fame, if they had known he was venturing on so hazardous an experiment, at an age when those who were practiced poets in their youth, lose all the fire and force of fancy, and when those who never were poets, cannot acquire the smoothness and refinement of the art.

This expectation has been fulfilled, in the nomination of General William Henry Harrison by the Harrisburg Convention. He may be justly regarded as a true exponent of the political doctrines of the party which have put him forward as their leader and representative. Before the contest between Thomas Jefferson and the elder Adams in 1798, he had been already in public life, and upon the separation of parties on the leading political questions of that day, he became a supporter of the latter. From that time to the present, nothing has appeared in his public acts evincing any change of opinion on his part in respect either to the questions referred to, or to others which have grown out of subsequent divisions upon measures involving great principles of government and policy. Indeed, those who now take upon themselves to speak for him authoritatively, and to be the expounders of his principles, have publicly declared that on all the exciting questions which formerly agitated, and still agitate the public mind, his opinions remain ** unchanged." We may, therefore, justly appeal to his former course and opinions as the criterion by which he is now to be judged. He stands confessed as a supporter of the most obnoxious federalist measures—the alien and sedition laws, and the standing army of the elder Adams; the high-toned doctrines of the younger, and the administration of both.

He will further suggest any general changes which appear to him expedient, as well as any new means deemed necessary to meet all the burdens, which, it is apprehended, may be imposed. The estimates for the ordinary receipts and expenditures in the ensuing year differ some millions from what will be actually received and expended in the present one. It is calculated, however, that the difference will be what is always most desirable,—some increase of the receipts, and a further diminution of the expenditures. The estimates for the latter, in the present year, were made less than those of 1839; and the results have corresponded. Indeed it is a cause of much gratification that the expectations heretofore cherished, of materially reducing the public burdens, have been verified to so great an extent. Thus, the expenses of 1838 fell below those of 1837; while the expenses of 1839, notwithstanding the continuance of the Florida war, were nearly eight millions below those of 1838; and it is expected that the expenses of 1840 will be from two to three millions still lower, or quite ten millions less than those of 1838. They would have been nearly twelve millions less, had not that war continued, and unusually large payments been made to Indians, under old appropriations.

The revenue accruing from imports in the first quarter of the present year was 5,344,540 dollars; the payment actually made into the Treasury from the Custom-houses for the same quarter was 4,435,386 dollars; and the payments from lands for the same time was $1,308,206. The first two months of the second quarter were producing in a full ratio to the first quarter; and the actual amount of available funds in the Treasury on the 9th day of this month was ELEVEN MILLIONS, TWO HUNDRED AND FORTY-NINE THOUSAND, FOUR HUNDRED AND TWELVE DOLLARS. The two last quarters of the year were always most productive. It was the time of the largest importations of foreign goods which pay most duty—the woollens—and the season also for the largest sale of public lands; it is well believed that the estimate will be more largely exceeded in those two quarters than in the two first; and that the excess for the whole year, over the estimate, will be full two millions of dollars. This, Mr. B. said, was one of the evidences of public prosperity which the report contained; and which utterly contradicted the idea of distress and commercial embarrassment which had been propagated from this chamber for the last six months.

In his knee sat a girl ten or twelve years old, lovely as an angel, remarkable for the beauty and nobleness of her features, and their expression of artless vivacity. She was his daughter. This family picture struck me forcibly; and Sanson must have perceived it. The fact is, that without sharing in the prejudice of the multitude, I had nevertheless, formed an idea very different from what was now before me. That little girl, above all—she strangely bewildered me. I could have wished that nothing so beautiful might have been found there; it was like sunlight on a thunder cloud, or a rose rising in its beauty between the stones of sepulcher. For several years past, M. Sanson the younger has performed duties of his father's office. Destined, for reasons which I have already explained, to succeed to that office, he is serving his apprenticeship of blood under the eye of the latter, who is obliged to be present at every execution—for the law knows no other than him, and he is personally responsible for all that passes. M. Sanson received me like a man of the world, without embarrassment or affectation, and politely inquired the object of my visit. My heart ready prepared.

If they had been a Providential infliction, they would have been borne with manly resignation and Christian fortitude; but they are aggravated by the conviction that they are the work of one mortal man, the product of one individual will, prompted by vitiated passion, or guided by erroneous views of the Public interest. The experiment, besides its influence on the general business of the country, the dismissal of thousands of the laboring classes from employment, and the check on commercial operations and other enterprises, has occasioned a very great depreciation in the value of most descriptions of property, and especially of stocks. I should be glad to see an estimate (and it would be a most interesting and not impracticable estimate) of the aggregate amount of national and individual loss produced by this most imprudent measure, in the property, business, and capital of the country. I have somewhere seen a statement that the total value of all the property, real and personal, in the single State of New York, is estimated at eight hundred millions of dollars. It would not be deemed extravagant to suppose that this property, by the operation of the recent measure of the Executive, has been subjected to an average depreciation of ten percent. Assuming the correctness of this calculation, the loss in that State would be eighty millions.

Be it known by this agreement, made and concluded this fourteenth day of June, 1839, before me Horatio Davis, a notary public, duly commissioned and sworn, in and for the parish and city of New Orleans, that in conformity to the law approved by the legislature of this state, on the thirteenth March Eighteen hundred and thirty-seven, entitled "an act to authorize limited and anonymous partnerships, and for regulating the same;" There is hereby formed a limited partnership which shall be regulated in manner and form following, viz:  1. The name and style of said partnership shall be "The steamer Brilliant Association of New Orleans." 2. This association is formed for the purpose of using the steamer called the Brilliant of New Orleans, as a passenger and freight boat, for hire, and to run between Louisiana and others of or on the coasts of the United States.  3. The amount of the capital stock of this association is thirty thousand dollars, divided into fifty shares of eight hundred dollars each. The whole of this capital has been paid in ready money, said amount being the price paid by the hereinafter named members of said association for the original cost, repairs, and alterations made to said steamer Brilliant.

Fatal Accident. —Last Sunday morning Mr. Michael Shannon, a married man, boarding with his brother John, after all the family except himself and brother J. had gone to the Catholic church to which they are attached, while looking for some article which had been mislaid, with a pipe in his mouth, turned up an empty barrel which proved to have a keg nearly full of powder under it, the head of the keg being out. A spark from his pipe fell into it, and by the explosion, the unfortunate man was perfectly flayed from his hair to about four inches of his ankles, and pieces of flesh from four to two inches in circumference fastened like wafers to the side of the room. The whole of his clothing, except the waistbands of his pantaloons, was blown off, his face perfectly charred and left black as an African’s. He presented the most agonizing spectacle we ever beheld, and survived until about four o’clock, bearing his torture with great fortitude and expressing his desire to live only until his spiritual friend and pastor could aid him to prepare for death.

Monday, February 1, On motion of Mr. Crouse, the Senate took up the bill to establish the Bank of the Scioto Valley with the pending amendment to insert a provision amending it liable to repeal by any future legislature. Mr. Vance then offered a resolution, recommitting the bill to a select committee with instructions to amend it so to give to the Supreme Court the power of declaring the charter null and void, upon the exhibition of fraud or mismanagement in the institution. Whereupon ensued a long and interesting debate, in which Messrs. Medary, McMechan, and Patterson opposed the resolution, and Messrs. Vance and James in support of it. Mr. Vance then, upon a motion being made to indefinitely postpone the resolution, asked, and obtained, leave to withdraw the resolution. The question then recurred upon the adoption of the spring provision, and being put was lost, by the following vote: AYES Messrs. Cox, Medary, McMechan, Patterson, Ravenscroft, Scott, Shephard, Spangler, Thompson, and Wellhouse - 10 NOES Messrs. Allen, Crouse, Donally, Granger, Hopkins, Houston, Howard, Hunt, James, Kendall, King, Kirby, McLaughlin, Morse, Newell, Sharp, Steele, Taylor, Vincent, Wadsworth, and Speaker - 21 The question then being on ordering the bill to be engrossed, was lost as follows: AYES Messrs. Allen, Crouse, Donally, Granger, Hopkins, Houston, Howard, Hunt, James, Kendall, King, Kirby, Morse, Vincent, and Wadsworth - 14 NOES Messrs. Cox.

It is clear that there is but a small chance in the Lords, notwithstanding the willingness of Lords Harrowby and Wharncliffe to allow it to go to a second reading. But there is now stronger evidence of a determination on the part of the Ministry to create a batch of Peers, than has any time before appeared. During his last speech in favor of the bill, on the evening of the 22nd, shortly before the question was taken in the Commons, Mr. Stanley, Secretary for Ireland, made the following remarks: "The responsibility of advising the Sovereign to create Peers was great, as had been truly observed by the members for Boroughbridge and St. Mawes; but when the two Houses of Parliament were at hopeless variance, it was undoubtedly in the power of the Crown thus to decide the difference." The Rt. Hon. Gentleman then referred to the impeachment of Lord Oxford, contending that the charge of making Peers was a very minor accusation, which Lord Oxford had met by asserting the right of the Sovereign so to exercise his prerogative ex mero motu. He met his trial—two years the charge was hanging over his head, and at last it fell to the ground unprosecuted. Whatever Ministers might in this case advise, this, he (Mr. Stanley,) would say, that they would not shrink from the responsibility. (Cheers)

That quarrel, as the reader will recollect, was brought about by Van Buren, who, in order to deprive Calhoun of his influence with the President, was guilty of one of the most mean and dirty transactions that ever disgraced any public man in this country, in searching out and publishing the course pursued by him as a member of President Monroe’s cabinet, in the confidential consultations of that body respecting Gen. Jackson's conduct in the Seminole War! The act of Van Buren’s no high-minded or honest man could overlook, and it produced a most bitter and undying hostility between the rivals. But as both are now alike hostile to the public interests, ambitious, selfish and unprincipled, they are enabled to unite in supporting a measure calculated to sap the foundation of our republican institutions, and thus promote objects that both have at heart. The bill to annex Part of Orwell to Whiting was returned by the Senate with an amendment; laid on the table. The House resumed consideration of Mr. Partridge's resolution against chartering any banks at this session; when Mr. Stark moved a substitute for it, embracing the following propositions: no bank to be chartered unless on the following conditions: 1. to secure bill holders against loss; 2. to be exempted from safety fund; 3. to be subjected to alteration or amendment at any future legislature; 4. to be subjected to repeal, when the legislature shall be of opinion that the bank has violated its charter, or so conducted as to become prejudicial to the public interests; 5. to be allowed to issue $3 for $1 paid in; 6, officers to be subjected to penal punishment for mal-conduct. Mr. Partridge opposed the substitute, and the whole subject was made the order for Friday morning next. Mr. Stark introduced a bill to provide for the security of bill holders on the closing of banks; referred to a select committee of three.

Addison. (To be continued.) SELECTED ITEMS. Ecclesiastical Council.—We learn from the Gazette, that an Ecclesiastical Council held in North Wrentham on the conduct of the Rev. Moses Thatcher, after a session of two days, determined that he had been guilty of unchristian conduct in excluding masons from the communion table, and that the excluded brethren should be again received into fellowship. The Rev. Messrs. Beecher and Codman were members of the Council.—Rattan Transcript. Temperance Anecdote. — A man was taken before a magistrate for having, while drunk, knocked down in the street a minister of religion. The prisoner was fully convicted of the offence, but at the urgent intercession of the reverend gentleman whom he had injured, was liberated on signing the teetotal pledge for a month. — At the expiration of the month he called at the house of the divine, and being introduced, expressed his gratitude for the effects of the pledge he had submitted to, and concluded with expressing the utmost sorrow at not having met and knocked down his reverence thirty years before.

At 2 o'clock this morning, that long-known and splendid establishment, the Mansion House, was discovered to be on fire, and to such an extent that it was impossible to extinguish it. We have only time to say that from the Mansion House, the fire was communicated to the magnificent new Hotel adjoining on the south, which was yet in an unfinished state, and to the Planters' and Merchants' Bank, that new, beautiful, and splendid edifice, both of which were reduced to ashes. From the new tower, no effect could be brought out of chaos—for northward to the furniture store of Gwinn and the grocery store of Geo. Davis, the circumstances reported yesterday that an armorial bearing was displayed. Here the properties of the insured, Jotham Clark's warehouse and workshop, were reported to suffer—insurance $8,000. B & Newhouse's warehouse insured. Geo. A Correspondent of the Journal of Commerce, writing from Union City, Michigan, under date of the 19th ult., says: Great ado is being made in our vicinity about the removal of the Pottawattamies. Gen. Brady with about 200 regulars and 100 horsemen are quartered at Marshall, (18 miles distant,) from whence they have been making various excursions through our forests in pursuit of the poor fellows. The Indians say that the treaty which our Government professes to be acting under, and which exchanged their lands for lands west of the Mississippi, was made by a few unauthorized Chiefs, who were cheated by the she mokimans (whites) while they (the Chiefs) were squabby (drunk.) The Indians keep out of the reach of the troops so long as they are able, but when surprised they submit to be taken without resistance. The war is a bloodless one, and doubtless will end as such. About 500 warriors, squaws and papooses are assembled on the Nottaway prairie, about 16 miles west of us, who are to take up their line of march for their new home this day. The contract for removing them has been taken by the Messrs. Godfrey, who were two French traders among them. I understand they received $55 a head for transporting them.

HORRIBLE SHIPWRECK. The Francis Spaight, of 345 tons, laden with timber, sailed from St. John's, New- foundland, on 24th November, bound for Limerick. The crew amounted to 14 men with the captain & mate; they had fine weather a few days, but it afterwards blew so hard that they were obliged to drive before the wind. At three in the morning of December 3rd, the vessel, through the carelessness of the helmsman, suddenly broached to, and in less than an hour she lay on her beam ends, the greater part of the crew saving themselves by clinging to the rigging. Patrick Cusack and Patrick Behane were drowned in the forecastle, and Griffith, the mate, in the after cabin. The Captain and Mulville got to the fore and main masts, and cut them away; the mizzen top mast went with them over the side, and the ship immediately righted. As soon as she righted, she settled down in the sea, and there was scarcely any of her to be seen except the poop and bulwarks.

Under a deep impression of the propriety of confining our declarations within moderate limits, we affirm that the value of our real estate has, within the last six months, depreciated more than forty millions; that within the last two months, there have been more than two hundred and fifty failures of houses engaged in extensive business; that within the same period a decline of twenty millions of dollars has occurred in our local stocks, including those Rail Road and Canal Incorporations, which though chartered in other States, depend chiefly upon New York for their sale; that the immense amount of merchandise in our warehouses has, within the same period, fallen in value at least thirty per cent; that within a few weeks not less than twenty thousand individuals depending upon their daily labor for their daily bread have been discharged by their employers because the means of retaining them were exhausted—and that a complete blight has fallen upon a community heretofore so active, enterprising, and prosperous—the error of our rulers has produced a wider desolation than the pestilence which depopulated our streets, or the conflagration which laid them in ashes.

Black Hawk and the Prophet. Extract of a letter to a gentleman in Albany, dated St. Louis, Missouri, Nov. 10. “ We yesterday visited Black Hawk and the Prophet, now confined at the garrison ten miles from this place. There are now 11 Indians as hostages, confined there. The description that has been given in the papers, of Black Hawk and the Prophet, is not correct, as I think. Black Hawk is nearly 6 feet high, 68 years old, has a piercing countenance, looks pleasant but imperious. The Prophet looks more savage and warlike, and is more full-faced. Black Hawk has two sons, as well-built men as you ever saw, tall and handsome; one 22, and the other 25 years old. All of them look like great warriors, and tell many stories of their exploits. One says he has killed three white men, and would have killed more if he could. They all appeared to be glad to see us, and shook hands with us as though we were old friends.” — Albany Daily Advertiser. THE GOLD DISTRICT.

Missionary Adventure.—Our neighbor of the Times is perfectly au fait as a political missionary—in other words, he is a "buster" at the business. The following incident, which occurred during his late "pilgrimage" out east, is too good to be lost. The occurrence is related by a gentleman from the place where it happened, and a gentleman whose veracity even Capt. Jones is not accustomed to doubt. We do not pretend to give the precise words of the conversation, but merely the substance of it. It appears that the Captain, upon arriving in the town of Windham, went to a public house, and inquired for the leading administration men in the town. — In reply, he was directed to a store up the street. Intent upon the duties of his mission, he proceeded thither, and upon entering the store, he addressed a gentleman at the counter, with "Is this Mr. L.?" "No, sir," was the reply, "Mr. L. is my partner; he is not in."

Tuesday morning, Nov. 19, on motion of Mr. Leffler, a select committee composed of Messrs. Several attempts were made to commit the enacting clause to a select committee or to lay it on the table subject to the order of the House—the latter motion prevailed. This is a very important resolution, as it contemplates giving the laws to the people without delay, at a very low price—too low by one half. The assembly of Upper Canada have appropriated $200,000 for the improvement of the roads in that province. It is said that schemes for railroads are before the British parliament, involving an expenditure of upwards of two hundred millions of dollars. Niles Register. A screw manufacturing company has been formed at Schenectady, which it is expected, will turn off five hundred gross per day of wood screws of all sizes, from half an inch to three inches. Ib. A French paper calls attention to the fact that in the month of August next, the city of Rome will number 2,751 years since its foundation, ab urbe condita. Ib. According to a table furnished in the New York Transcript by Mr. Sicke's, the city inspector, the number of buildings erected in the city in 1834 was 1,767, and in the following year 1,259, showing an increase in a single year of all. In a single ward, the 12th, 274 buildings were put up in 1835. In the same year twelve new churches were built. Ib. On the 2nd instant, the Hudson opened to navigation only so far as West Point. It was thought that Newburg would be reached by steamboats on Monday the 4th. Ib. Auction sales of real estate were made in New Orleans to the amount of $322,000 in two days.

But the clear-sighted and rational of all parties, regarded the prediction as a mere hoax, a "gold humbug;" and it is only occasionally that we now come across one of those gold expectants, fishing for hard money, and though he exhibits all the patience becoming such a pursuit, the only bite, or even nibble, which he can get from anything in the name of money, is from an occasional stray five-dollar Illinois bill; and even these, like angels' visits, are becoming "few and far between." At its first presentation, he affects disgust, but when informed that he can get nothing else, he concludes he had as well pocket it, consoling himself with the wise reflection that even rye bread is far better than actual starvation. But I am aware of the fact, gentlemen, that many of the friends of the present administration deny that the removal of the deposits or the destruction of the U.S. Bank had any agency in producing the present state of confusion in which our currency is involved. Shifting the responsibility from their own shoulders, they lay it all upon the people. They alone, it seems, are to blame. It is their overtrading, their extravagance, their rage for speculation, which has brought upon them the evils of which they complain. Well, now, won?!.

The last number of the North Ameri can Review contains a Review of Sparks's life and writings of George Washington, an interesting work, recently published in 12 volumes, and which is republishing in England, France and Germany. In the course of the review, which is very ably written, the following beautiful tri bute is paid to the wife of Washington: “The matrimonial connexion of Wash ington was eminently happy, and con tinued for forty years till his death. — With her intimate acquaintances, the character of Mrs. Washington was the theme of unerring praise. To friends at large, she was the object of affectionate respect; for it was known to all men, that she made the home of the Fa ther of his Country happy. Affable and courteous, exemplary in her deportment, remarkable for deeds of charity, consolations, and without vanity, she adorned private life by her domestic virtues, and with dignity and grace every station to which her husband's eminence called her.

The "Bank Commissioner," will ever be remembered by the people of Michigan, of all parties, with gratitude. We regret that circumstances have made this appeal to our city banks imperatively necessary. Our city banks have heretofore taken the issues of country chartered banks almost indiscriminately on deposit, while those of good safety fund institutions, have been, as indiscriminately, rejected and scouted. They have done so since, in convention, they solemnly pledged themselves, by resolution, to observe no such distinction. None of these remarks, however, we believe, apply to the Detroit City Bank. To the honorable Directors of the several Banks in the City of Detroit. Gentlemen, I desire to appeal to you in behalf of the Safety Fund Banks in my district, and to respectfully solicit for them that credit and confidence to which their present situation justly entitles them, and which you extend to the country chartered banks. I have labored faithfully to detect and destroy all fraudulent banks, whether in successful operation or not and equally so, to encourage and enforce a compliance with the law, and to correct the errors and improve the condition of such as appeared to have been got up in good faith, and were under the control of honest men: and have the proud satisfaction to believe that my labors have not been in vain.

Did not our adversaries indulge the confident hope that the separation of bank and State, by the adoption of the 'Independent Treasury bill," would prove the death knell of the party; and are they not now left like Paganini on the single string," to ring the changes of panic and distress? Beaten upon every question, they resort, in a land whose proudest boast is the intelligence of the people, to the meanest passions and the worst prejudices. Where is the pride of a party, which once boasted "All the talent!" When a man of such "a feeble temper" should outstrip all competitors, and "oh! shame where is thy blush," when all the decency" can boast no brighter weapon than a log cabin and hard cider!" Vain hope to deceive the people by such subterfuge. Vain such reliance upon trickery and hypocrisy! It is with feelings of honest pride that we turn from such a picture, to inquire under what banner we fight, and who is the chosen head to lead us on to glorious victory? With an unanimity, the result of undiminished confidence, the Democracy again seek to re-elect their Chief Magistrate, that distinguished statesman, Martin Van Buren, who, four years ago, was deemed by them a worthy successor of the illustrious Jackson.

About two-thirds of its columns are devoted to the best Original and Selected Literature, comprising Reviews, Essays, (Scientific and Literary,) Tales, Poems, choice extracts from New Works, Biographical and Historical Sketches, &c. &c. In this department, Critical Notices of nearly all new publications of interest will be given. The remaining columns will be devoted to a condensed abstract of the Political and General Intelligencer of the day, Domestic and Foreign. Under this head, the returns of all important Elections throughout the United States will be carefully compiled and compared with those of former Elections, so as to give a clear exhibit of the relative strength of each political party in the several States. All political movements of interest will likewise be briefly and impartially chronicled. In fine, it will be the general aim of this paper to be first useful and instructive then agreeably and entertaining. The steady support through the last three years of an average of more than 8,000 subscribers warrants the belief that the conduct of the work has not disappointed the hopes of its friends, while it has secured the decided approval of an intelligent public. Such as it has been it will continue to be, with the exception of the additional aid already secured in its Editorial department, to which it is hoped that still further strength will be added by arrangements now in progress.

Ally devoting a particular day to the service of Him who controls the destinies of nations, and in conformity with a usage which has prevailed in this state, I do hereby appoint Thursday, the thirtieth day of November next, as a day of public Thanksgiving and Prayer; and I do recommend to the people of this State, that refraining from all employments inconsistent with the duties and solemnities of the occasion, they repair to their respective places of public worship and unite in suitable acknowledgments to the "Giver of every good and perfect gift," for the many mercies he has vouchsafed to us as a people; that we may thank him for the blessings of civil and religious freedom, and all the advantages of a republican form of government, for the favorable seed time and the abundant harvest, for exemption from disease, and all national calamities; for all we can reasonably want on this earth, and for the guidance of a religion that can secure (to us) happiness hereafter; that we beseech Him to take our beloved country into His holy keeping, and that he continue it a land of liberty, virtue and intelligence. In testimony whereof I have hereunto set my hand, and have caused to be affixed the great seal of the State, this 19th day of October, A.D. 1837. STEVENS T. MASON.

ALEXANDRIA: WEDNESDAY MORNING, AUG. 3, 1836. Who from Texas.—The following is an extract of a letter from a native of this place, now attached to the Texan Army, dated Velasco, July 6th: "The Mexicans are advancing upon us, and are now, perhaps, not more than four miles from our forces stationed near our boundary line. The Mexicans number about 5000, and the Texan main army is not more than 900 strong, but it is generally thought, will amount to 3000 when all are united and the volunteers join. With these we do not fear the power of Mexico. Our Navy is about to make an attack upon Matagorda. We all hope for the best, and many are confident the war cannot last six months longer, when Texas will be free. We still have Santa Ana and Cos, with about 500 prisoners in close custody. If the Mexicans advance too far, it is thought that Santa Ana and Cos will be shot." It was asserted in the Richmond Enquirer the other day, that serious thoughts were entertain ed of nominating "the Devil" as a candidate for the President's chair. We denied the correctness of this opinion, according to all the information we had received. It will be perceived, however, that an attempt is actually about to be made to put up Mr. Benton in the same place. The Journal of Commerce is out against Mr. Kendall's "Express Mail." It thinks.

Ezra Convis, of Calhoun, as follows. Mr. Speaker: It is with the most painful emotions, that I announce to this House, one of those afflicting dispensations of Providence, in which it is difficult to acquiesce, however confidently we may believe that "whatever is, is right." Our late and highly esteemed associate, the Hon. Ezra Convis, departed this life at half-past nine o'clock, this morning. Were his character not so extensively known, and so justly appreciated, I would pay a parting tribute to his memory; but, Sir, upon this floor, where his talents as a Statesman have commanded him respect, and his virtues as a man have won him confidence, no eulogy is necessary. While we lament the loss of our friend, let us treasure up his many excellent qualities, and keep them constantly before us as patterns, worthy of imitation and let this melancholy event, deeply impress upon our minds the solemn truth, that even in the midst of our days, it is but a single step to that bourne from whence no traveller returns. On motion of Mr. Alden, Resolved, That in testimony of our respect for the memory of Gen. Ezra Convis, late a member of the House of Representatives, who departed this life this morning, the members of the House of Representatives will wear the usual badge of mourning during the remainder of the session. In Senate Thursday, March 2.

A half-breed family, named Duchamp, had murdered another half-breed, Jack Ram, for which the half-breeds of the neighborhood and some French voyagers determined to exterminate them, for which purpose they attacked a blockhouse in which the Duchamps were. The Duchamps, understanding that no quarter would be given them, became desperate and reckless of consequences, and defended themselves to the last with determination. A Frenchman contrived to set fire to the blockhouse, and the wind being high, the flames spread with great rapidity. The yells of defiance of the inmates could be heard amidst the crackling of the flames, and they could be seen calmly and deliberately watching an opportunity through the smoke and flames to bring down their opponents with their rifles, of whom they killed eight and wounded several. The Duchamps, with the exception of a few small children, were completely exterminated. At length—as if the fountains of the deep were broken up—as told in holy writ, when God destroyed the pleasant earth he made. The rain in sheets of foaming wave descends; the frightened birds had sunk into their nests, and even the lion sought a covert there. There was a crashing amid the mountain pines, and the small stream into a torrent swelled, rushing from rock to rock with angry roar. Such fury could not last—the clouds were spent; Nature grew still, and the bright sun appeared, as bright as when upon creation's dawn the voice of God proclaimed, "Let there be light"! Like virtue struggling with adversity, that sun appeared; and those same clouds, ashamed of former hate, blushed! and the sun went down behind the hills. Bridgeport, Nov. 26, 1858. M. A. W. Foreign Intelligence. Latest from Europe—By the ship Tioncer, Captain Malbury, (of Alexandria,) which sailed from Liverpool May 18th, in the afternoon, London dates to the 17th and Liverpool to the 18th have been received at the Merchants Coffee House from which we have made the following extracts: The following are some further extracts from the French papers of Saturday and Sunday. Paris, it would appear, has scarcely freed itself from one sort of irritation when it is upon the eve of being subjected to another. The great question is now whether the Archbishop of Paris will consent to bestow upon the remains of the famous Abbe Gregoire, who, by the way, is still living, the rites of burial according to the forms of the Catholic Church. The prelate is, as usual, unaccommodating; but the Government have taken the matter up, and M. Casimir Perier seems determined that the Church shall succumb. Trifling as is the cause, there are some apprehensions that this affair will lead to some serious disturbances. The French papers repeat their assurances that the great Powers are about to negotiate upon the affairs of Poland. Switzerland, too, seems to be approximating to a closer understanding with France, and to be desirous of placing itself in a situation independent of Austria. The Diet proposes to place on foot a respectable military force.

Extract of a letter from Philadelphia, dated Oct. 2nd, 1832. “The opposition have been exulting all day in this city owing to the Philadelphia Gazette having abandoned our cause and gone over to theirs. The defection is of no consequence to us; but the manner in which it was brought about, is of great importance. “This paper was for a great many years, while Relf was the proprietor, a federal paper of the old stamp. It afterward fell into the hands of Kitchen who sold it to Smith and Gouge. It was the advocate of Gen. Jackson in 1828. Of late it has been lukewarm from necessity, being a commercial paper and supported principally by merchants, the most of whom are Bank men.

They retired with Elder Barton W. Stone, a short time to arrange the business of the day. - It was concluded by them, from communications made, that the whole matter could be more satisfactorily settled by the parties, Hughes, and the aggrieved part of the congregation, by mutual acknowledgments and concessions to each other, than by the referees as judges. It was suggested to them that Elder Hughes would publicly acknowledge that his conduct had been improper, and that it might be sufficient to excite suspicion, as it in fact did in the minds of some that he now could, and did cordially forgive those who had indulged those suspicions. It was also suggested to the referees that the congregation would acknowledge that such of their members as had heard of these reports, and had spread them to the injury of Elder Hughes, and had kept them concealed from him, had done wrong. That the aggrieved in the church, would also acknowledge, that on due reflection, they considered that the grounds of their suspicion of Elder Hughes were insufficient to prove him guilty and that they were willing to abandon all suspicions, and forgive and forget those things forever; and give Elder Hughes the right hand as full confirmation of their peace and harmony. The referees returned into the house, and proposed their plan of reconciliation. It was adopted by the congregation unanimously.

They will lay bare the corruptions of the administration. It will be found that there is scarcely a public officer, who is not a defaulter, and that too, in most instances, to a large amount. Then it will be seen why the revenue from imports, postage, and the sales of the public lands is not sufficient to support the government gently. Then will the astounding facts be laid open to public view, of the enormous defalcations of the public officers, and their plundering the National Treasury, be exhibited to open day and the examination of the people. At present the National Treasury is deficient more than FIVE MILLIONS OF DOLLARS, to supply which, Treasury notes have been reissued, bearing interest. There was outstanding, more than this amount on the first of this month, according to the Report of the Secretary of the Treasury. But no administration can stand in this country, Great Britain or France, when the opposition possesses the majority in the popular branches of Congress, or of Parliament. It must go by the board. We question if the whig House of Representatives will allow the re-issuing of any more Treasury notes, saddling the government with a currency bearing an interest, and only redeemable by being made receivable in all government dues. It is complete paper currency, and has no specie basis. This is a fruitful subject, and may lead to much sound argument and demonstration of the pernicious tendency and effects of the sub-treasury scheme. More hereafter.

It is with the most painful feelings that we allude to the late riotous proceedings in Cincinnati, but our duty, as public journalists, compels us to say something in regard to them. On the 23rd of July, a large meeting of citizens was convened to consider the subject of Abolitionism, and especially what steps should be taken in reference to the publication of the philanthropist the organ of the Ohio Anti-Slavery Society, which was issued in that city. The meeting appointed a committee of twelve to see what steps could be taken with the consent of the publishers of that paper to suppress it. A correspondence was opened with the editor Mr. J. G. Birney, who declined acting in the matter, as he was not authorized by the Executive committee to make any negotiations on the subject, but he referred the whole to the committee, with whom a correspondence took place, which eventuated in their refusal to suspend the publication, for a number of reasons which are assigned at length in their reply. The committee first mentioned, then published their proceedings, and adjourned, having first recommended the citizens to abstain from violence- with what effect we shall soon see. At an early hour on Saturday evening the 30th ult.

The Governments of France and the United States have removed those difficulties upon the score of national honor, which have hitherto stood in the way of the prompt execution by France of the treaty of the 4th July, 1831, and that consequently, the French Government is now ready to pay the installment, which is due on account of the American indemnity, whenever the payment of the installment shall be claimed by the Government of the U. States. The French Government has also stated that it made this communication to that of Great Britain, not regarding the British Government as a formal mediator, since its offer of mediation had then reached only the Government of France, by which it had been accepted; but looking upon the British Government as a common friend of the two parties, and, therefore, as a natural channel of communication between them.

Mr. Naylor, of Pennsylvania, presented a resolution calling on the Secretary of the Navy, for a copy of the proceedings of the Court of Enquiry, held at the Navy Yard in that city, for the purpose of investigating the conduct of Commodore Jesse D. Elliot. It is alleged that the Court of Enquiry, of which Commodore Stewart was President, recommended a Court Martial to be held on the charges preferred by Mr. Barton; and Mr. Naylor's object, I understood to be, to get such information as will lead to an elucidation on the subject, and enable him to know why it is, if the Court of Enquiry recommended a Court Martial, that the Secretary of the Navy has not ordered one. So far so good. But if Mr. Naylor wishes to get up a new excitement against Commodore Elliot, he may miss his mark, and prove unsuccessful. Of the merits or demerits of Commodore Elliot, I know nothing; if he be guilty of all the acts that have been charged against him by officers in the ward room—if all the sickly stories of irresponsible boatswains and dismissed midshipmen are to be believed, why then he ought to be stricken from the lists of the navy.

The walk in the water was performed day be fore yesterday by Mr. McIntosh, as we predicted, in a most creditable manner. He left the steamboat opposite the Palisades, and after traversing the surface of the river for some distance, and firing his gun very leisurely, he returned to the boat, having only wet his pantaloons a little at the bottom! We hope he may repeat this exhibition, which was a very interesting one.—N. Y. Sun. At a recent meeting of the republican party in London, who are endeavoring to procure a total abolition of the stamp duties, a procession was formed, and the music, in allusion to the early struggles of the Americans on the same subject, struck up our national air of Yankee Doodle, which had a most inspiring effect on the multitude. [COMMUNICATED.] No man who has mingled with his fellow man and has observed the various influences which operate to fix a man’s station in society, can be insensible to the importance of a good name. — It is a diamond, the value of which can be fully appreciated, however, by none but the possessor. When we speak of a good name, we mean one deservedly so. He who stands fair and unsullied before the world, and has within, an approving conscience, is rich indeed.

The long pending controversy between the Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company, and the Chesapeake and Ohio Canal Company, says the Baltimore Patriot, is at length in a fair way of being terminated. The case is to be argued, upon final hearing, before the Chan- cery Court at Annapolis, in the course of the present week [October 7.] The Counsel said to be engaged are for the Rail-Road Company Messrs. Taney, Gwynn, Johnson and Latrobe. For the Canal Company, Messrs. Wirt, Jones and Cox. WHAT IS MAN? Originally dust; engendered in sin; brought forth with sorrow; helpless in infancy; extravagantly wild in his youth; mad in his manhood; decrepit in age; his voice moves pity; his last commands grief. Nature clothes the beast with hair, the birds with feathers, and the fishes with scales: but man is born naked, his hands cannot handle, his feet cannot walk, his tongue cannot speak, nor his eyes see aright: simple his thoughts, vain his desire, toys his delight. As soon as he puts on his distinguished character Reason, he burns it with wild fire passions: paints it with abominable pride; tears it with unsatiable revenge, and defiles it with avarice. His next state is full of miseries, tears torment, hopes intoxicate, cares perplex, enemies assault him, friends betray him, thieves rob him, wrongs oppress him, and dangers way-lay him.

We pronounce VALENTINE BEST a LIAR, and shall continue to do so, until he either proves true, or acknowledges the falsity of the assertion made in an extra Intelligencer, which he, the past week, was hawking about the county, like an itinerant slanderer, that we, "last fall, in company with a highly respectable and popular land lord of Bloomsburg, went to the house of John Robison, and joined with the said land lord, in urging Mr. Robison to come out in opposition to the democratic candidate," as we declare the assertion to be a willful, deliberate falsehood on the part of Valentine Best, and uttered by him for the express purpose of personally injuring ourselves and the "highly respectable and popular landlord," to whom he refers.

At the solicitation of an old friend, we publish, with pleasure, in this day's paper, a letter from Mr. O. L. Smith, of the firm of Smith, Riddle & Co. of this city, to Mr. Moffat, of the city of New York, proprietor of the "Life Pills and Phoenix Bitters." In this letter, Mr. Smith gives an account of his sufferings from a severe attack of chronic rheumatism, under which he has labored for more than three years, and of his speedy cure by the use of the aforesaid medicines. A certificate, coming from so respectable a source, cannot fail to have its weight in this community. If this medicine is found by experience to effect so immediate a cure of a complaint so painful and distressing, it would be highly valuable to persons engaged in navigating steamboats on our western rivers. From their constant exposure to the inclemency of the weather, and from the rapidity of their transition from a cold to a warm climate, and from a warm to a cold one, they are peculiarly liable to rheumatic attacks. To them this medicine may prove an invaluable remedy: one which they can always keep on hand for use as occasion may require. It is said that a single dose of three pills, or two doses at most, will relieve a common cold.

Mr. Royston presented a petition from citizens of Chicot county, for the incorporation of a rail-paid company with banking privileges, to contract a railroad from Columbia, via Old Lake, to Bayou Bartholomew, which was referred to the committee on banking. Mr. Royston, from the committee on the judiciary, to whom had been referred the claims of John K. Taylor, sheriff of Pulaski county, against various counties in this state, reported that, there being no evidence before the committee in relation to the correctness of the claims, the committee are not warranted in making a report in relation to the same, and asked to be discharged from the further consideration of the subject; which report was agreed to. The bill from the Senate, providing for a revision of the laws, was read a third time and passed. Mr. Davies, from the select committee to whom was referred the bill to establish a State Bank for the state of Arkansas, reported the same with sundry amendments. After considerable discussion and amendment, the bill was read a third time and passed. A bill for the removal of the administration on the estate of Austin Kendrick, was read a third time and passed. The hour having arrived for the election of circuit judges and prosecuting attorneys, the Senate convened in the Representative Hall, and the two houses proceeded to the election. [For result, etc., see Senate proceedings.] SENATE. Friday, Oct. 21.

This individual has shut himself up in the house for about one and a half or two years. He appears to be in perfect health, and every way capable of attending to business, if he only thought so. He has for more than a year fancied himself a teapot, and believes his nose is the spout, and is afraid to have anyone come near him for fear they will break it off. It is impossible for any of his friends to convince him to the contrary. He never appears in the street, except early in the morning, or late in the evening, and then takes care never to meet anyone. He has been known to go a mile out of his way to avoid a person, for fear of coming in contact and breaking the teapot into a thousand pieces, as he expresses it. — New Haven Herald. Canal Transportation. Important reductions have been made in the rates of toll, both on the Erie Canal of New-York, and on the Ohio Canal, which the Canal Commissioner states will materially reduce the cost of transportation between the Western country and the seaboard, by way of the Lake and the Canals. On the Erie Canal of New-York, the tolls on the staple articles of agricultural produce, such as flour, wheat, beef, pork, lard, whiskey, &c. have been reduced from 7 to 5 mills per 100 lbs.

So choose betwixt my rescue and my rare And quickly too! The hour of sacrifice Is near! Anon the immolating priest Will summon me! Devise some speedy means To cheat the altar of its victim. Do it Nor leave the task to me! The delivery of this whole passage, and the unaffected but beautiful gait and action with which it was accompanied, was enough to give the stamp of genius to Miss Vandenhoff's performance; yet she has much to acquire, particularly in the management of her voice, which is often discordant, and grates harshly on the ear. This fault she must attend to, and a few years of close application will make her a great artist. At the close of the curtain Mr. V. was loudly called for, and in a very neat speech he returned his most grateful thanks to the New York public for the kindness they had displayed towards his daughter on the occasion of her debut on the American stage. The fair beneficiary was led on, and the whole house literally rose to greet her. We never saw more enthusiasm exhibited towards any public favorite. This evening the opera of Amine is to be produced, with Miss Shirreff, etcetera. 09> Dexter A. 'existence; her girlhood had passed forever; she took no pleasure in any of her former pursuits; she had put away childish things; and nothing had arisen to supply their place, save one memory haunted out by one image. Days, weeks elapsed, and Adalbert returned not—her sleep was broken by a thousand fanciful terrors; but one fear had taken possession of her mother Ursuline's mind—that the stranger was false; and bitterly did she lament that she had ever entrusted him with the happiness of her precious child. "And yet I did it for the best," she would piteously exclaim, whenever her eye fell on the pale cheek of her daughter. "He is come, my mother!" exclaimed Theresa, bounding one evening into the cottage with a long unaccustomed lightness of heart and step. Though eager to spring down the path and meet him, yet, amid all the forgetfulness of joy, she had bethought her of her aged parent, and returned that she too might share the happiness of their meeting. They hurried out, and three horsemen were riding up the valley—one much in advance of the others. "Mother it is a stranger!" with difficulty articulated Theresa, and, sick at heart clung to her arm for support. A Wild Goose Chase: A paragraph headed "Aerial Ship" is making the circuit of the papers, and we have been given to understand that this aerial vessel, 134 feet long by 34 feet high and 25 feet wide, is constructed in the form of an air bladder or a fish, and calculated to raise a weight of 6,500 pounds, and capable of accommodating thirty persons. It was to have ascended on the 15th instant with 18 persons on board, and the adventurers expected to navigate it from Paris to London in six or eight hours.

6 per cent was charged for long-dated bills by one banking house, and to give some idea of the scarcity of money here, one joint stock bank, have requested their customers to take three months' bills for their checks instead of cash. The steam-ship Britannia has arrived, and brings news from New York to the 1st. The accounts respecting the crop are contradictory. The crop has, in different locations, sustained injury, but upon the whole we have no doubt that the quantity will be equal to last year. Home consumption is very depressed, and sales can only be effected at ruinously low prices. Handloom cloths are very hard to get off; 70's weighing 31lbs cannot be sold for more than 17s 7d to 19s 9d; 21lbs 10oz are offering at 4s 7d to 4s 11d; and 41lbs 2oz at 4s 10½d to 5s. Power-loom printers, 63's, 4s 4d to 6s 7½d; and 72's, 5s 7½d to 6s; although some weights may sell at these prices. The print trade, with few exceptions, is also very flat, and many works are only working half time. From the 'London Morning Post' of Oct. 7th. Attempt upon Louis Philippe's Life.

Wm. H. Brown, Chairman. S. C. Sherman, Secretary. Mr. Grundy - opposes the removal of the deposits as emphatically the act of the President. Mr. Grundy well knows, that such an act on the part of the President is an usurpation of power, and yet he defends it. What may we not expect when such men as Mr. Grundy defend palpable violations of the constitution? The gentleman appears greatly to admire the energy of the President, when he says, “I take the responsibility.” Has he reflected upon the “fatal import of the principle and precedent?" We are indebted for a copy of the folio issued by him, dated 10th February, by the Congress, in which it had been recommended for an amendment. It is true, that in the message of the President, which produced this in quiry and resolution on the part of the House of Representatives, it was his object to obtain the aid of that body in making a thorough ex amination into the conduct and condition of the Bank and its branches, in order to enable the Executive Department to decide whether the public money was longer safe in its hands. The limited power of the Secretary over the subject, disabled him from making the inves tigation as fully and satisfactorily as it could be done by a committee of the House of Rep resentatives, and hence the President desired the assistance of Congress, to obtain for the Treasury Department a full knowledge of all the facts which were necessary to guide his responsibility which did not belong to them, and relieve the Executive branch of the Go vernment from the duty which the law had imposed upon it. It is due to the President, that his object in that proceeding should be distinctly understood, and that he should ac quit himself of all suspicion of seeking to es cape from the performance of his own duties, or of desiring to interpose another body be tween himself and the people, in order to a void a measure which he is called upon to meet.

The nation (says Blackstone) seems to have groaned under as absolute a slavery as it was in the power of a warlike, an ambitious and a politic prince to create; when the laws were administered in an unknown tongue; when the purest laws restrained all rural pleasures; and in cities and towns the case was no better, the company being obliged to disperse, and fire and candle to be extinguished by eight at night, at the sound of the melancholy curfew: when the ultimate property of all lands and a considerable share of present profits was vested in the king, or by him granted out to Norman favorites who, by a gradual progression of slavery, were absolute vassals to the crown, and as absolute tyrants to the commons; and, to crown all, the king had always ready at his command an army of five thousand knights or milites, who were bound, upon pain of confiscating their estates, to attend him in time of invasion, or to quell any domestic insurrection." From all we learn, however, we are satisfied that the ticket nominated by the Democratic Party, has been elected by an average majority of about forty; with the exception, perhaps, of the collector; of whose election there is yet some doubt. We shall have the official return in our next. In the district composed of the counties of Green, Dane, Jefferson, Dodge, and Sauk, Lucius I. Barber of Jefferson, and James Sutherland of Green, have been elected members of the House of Representatives. In the county of Iowa, where the party lines were drawn, the whole Democratic ticket has been elected by an average majority, we learn, of about 60. The names of the successful candidates are Francis J. Dunn, David Newland, Daniel M. Parkinson, and Matthew Chilton. In Milwaukee County, D. A. J. Upham and J. E. Arnold, Esqs; have been elected to the Council, and Messrs. Brazzleton, Bund, Shepherd, Rockwell, and Ray, to the House of Representatives. Maine Election. —The vote for governor in this state has been so close, that it is impossible to say with certainty which of the candidates has been elected; or in fact, whether either has been. The question appears to be, whether there has been a choice; a majority of the whole number of votes cast, is required, and the difficulty is, whether Kent, the Whig candidate, has such majority.

In this view of our duties, we have taken occasion to inform ourselves fully of the whole transaction charged upon Judge Crawford as official misconduct and now submit it to the impartial decision of the public. We are not disposed to shield anyone improperly; neither can we bring ourselves to join in injuring any man by means of detraction and falsehood. On the morning of the election, and before the judges took their seats, Samuel Hitchcock, a resident of Mobile, and a personal friend of Judge Crawford, asked him whether he, Mr. Hv, had a right to vote at St. Stephens for a member of Congress and Governor. The Judge replied that he did not think that he had a right, inasmuch as the Constitution declares that "no elector shall be entitled to vote except in the county, city, or town in which he may reside at the time of the election." One of the judges of the election was present and read from the Constitution the provision relating to the subject, as confirmatory of the opinion expressed by the Judge. It was unknown to Judge Crawford for whom Mr. Hitchcock intended to vote, and he had at other times when asked given the same opinion. The judges of the election were not, as alleged by the correspondent of the Inquirer, 'plied' by Judge Crawford to obtain a decision on this point. The judge of the election, in whose presence Mr.

No care or expense is spared in their manufacture, Dr. Brandreth being too jealous of his own fame to trust the mixing to anyone. He attends to that department with his own hands, and guarantees the medicine to be always equal to what it ever has been. The States of Michigan and Illinois resound with the praises of this valuable medicine. Persons are often known to ride sixty miles through the woods for a box. The Brandreth Pills are indeed a sure and speedy cure for Fever and Ague, and those miserable Bilious Fevers, with which, unfortunately, these districts abound. Therefore, the Brandrethian method of curing all kinds of disease, has now no longer to contend with the blind prejudices of the greatest portion of the public; it is only with those few who still are determined "to be killed according to rule," like the people of old, who 'would have a king to reign over them.' But, thanks to the circulation of newspapers—thanks to the general diffusion of knowledge, which enables nineteen twentieths of the people to read.

To the Honorable Nathaniel Groton, Judge of Probate for the County of Lincoln: I, respectfully represent Mary Tyler and Mr. Enos Goss, both of the City and County of Providence in the State of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations, that by the Municipal Court of the said city of Providence, exercising Probate jurisdiction, on the third day of April A.D. 1850, they were appointed joint administrators of the goods and estate of Nathan Tyler late of said city, deceased intestate; that the said Nathan Tyler at the time of his decease, being within twenty years last past, was seized in fee and in mortgage of certain real estate in the Town of Bath in the said county of Lincoln, of the value of one thousand dollars, the condition of which mortgage was long since broken, and that by law the said mortgaged premises should be administered upon, and the avails thereof distributed according to law, and they therefore pray that Ebenezer Everett of Brunswick in the County of Cumberland, may be appointed administrator of the goods and estate of the said Nathan Tyler within and for the State of Maine agreeably to the law in such cases made and provided. Above six-and-twenty years ago, I publicly declared my good opinion of them, which has since been corroborated by Mr. Steele and General Tottenham, and still more by their own general good conduct. Mr. Bickell, a clergyman of the Church of England, who lived six years in Jamaica, says, 'I am well aware it has been asserted that nothing but coercion can induce a negro to labor more than barely to raise enough for his subsistence. This is extremely erroneous: witness the great number of free blacks in the towns of Jamaica: in Kingston, they are most of them good mechanics, and work as regularly and as hard as white men in this country (England.) They also conduct themselves as well, can read and write; many of them, and are more respectably clad than white men of the same class in England. In Port Royal, just the same; they are industrious and intelligent, and several of them have more, much more religion, than the low white men there, who affect to despise them. They are very anxious to get their children educated, and in Kingston, Spanish-Town, Port-Royal, and other towns, most of the free children can read and write. In Kingston, the church is thronged every Sunday morning, principally by free people of color and free blacks. There is but one church; on this account, the dissenters have four or five places of worship.

In this Situation, the General called the troops together, and addressed them in a feeling and patriotic manner, impressing upon them the necessity of remaining only a few days longer; at the same time pledging himself to be their part from his own private resources, (should Government refuse to do it.) for the time they might serve after their legal time of service had expired. "We cannot forbear acknowledging a debt of gratitude of long standing, and yet due to Gen. Harrison, for his kind and personal attention to those of our company who were wounded in the engagement on the 5th of May, at Fort Meigs; and for his general deportment towards our corps during our term of service. JOSEPH SCOTT, JOHN H. SMITH, JOSEPH MASON, WM. R. CHIEVES, JAMES PAGE, WM. P. BURTON, R. CLEMENTS." A FINE INCIDENT. The Madisonian of the 29th ult. contains a very spirited sketch of a great meeting of the electors near at Staunton, Virginia, on the ult. The political discussion was commenced by a Mr. William Taylor, brother-in-law of Thomas H. Benton. This gentleman addressed the meeting for two hours, and exhausted all the stale slanders put in circulation by a party press against the character and conduct of William Henry Harrison. Gen. Harri8on, the Whig candidate for President, replied to Mr. Taylor in a most triumphant manner.

We have received a highly complimentary communication from J. T., in reference to these lectures, but we believe our citizens are so well informed on the subject that we need not say one word to them respecting this gentleman. The club passed a resolution inviting the General to deliver a course of lectures before them at his leisure, and we feel sure that when tickets are to be purchased his audience will be quite as large. First lecture on Friday eve. A correspondent requests us to inquire how many different ways the first four letters of the alphabet may be transposed. The newsboys in New York getting out of patience waiting for the President's Message, laid hands on a kit of last year's ones and succeeded in selling several dollars' worth before the fraud was discovered. We learn that a vessel was wrecked a few days since near "Gerrish Point" — Phipsburg. All that we gather of the particulars is that she belonged to Boothbay, and no lives were lost. To make room for Congressional news (which we have compressed in as small a space as possible) and other interesting news, we are obliged to lay aside several original articles intended. The election in Boston resulted in the choice of Jonathan Chapman for Mayor by a plurality of about 1373 votes over Bradford Sumner, the Van Buren candidate.

In offering a six-month course of Lectures instead of the usual term of four months, the Faculty assert that Medical Students will fully appreciate the great advantages which they will derive from this extension of the time, while for those who cannot conveniently spend six months in the city, an attendance during their months will meet the demands of the charter. The well-known facilities of this Institution have been increased during the past season by the addition of numerous valuable preparations to its costly and extensive Anatomical Museum, and will be further aided by a full set of the celebrated physiological Models of J. Gilbert, ordered from Paris by Dr. Smith. It is believed they will be the only set in the country on this side of the mountains, and will render the course in surgery vastly more instructive than it could be made without them. The splendid chemical apparatus of the University has been materially improved and increased, and a valuable collection of Foreign and American Minerals have been placed in the Laboratory for the benefit of the class in Chemistry.

Every friend of Harrison and Tyler in the ward is invited to attend and join the club. By order. ma-27 AH E. O'DONNELL POOR, Secretary.  TWELFTH WARD.- The First Baltimore Tippecanoe Club will meet at the room over Matthew Clark's store, west Baltimore street, on THIS EVENING, the 28th inst., at 7 o'clock.  The friends of Harrison and Reform generally are invited to attend.  WM. P. STEWART, President. WM. W. MCCLELLAN, Cor. Sec. ma 28 AIM SIXTH WARD. — At a meeting of the Tippecanoe Club No. 9, held on Thursday Evening, 21st, the following resolutions were adopted, and ordered to be published: Resolved, That the members of this Club, have viewed with feelings of abhorrence and indignation, the death of Thomas H. Laughlin, who was basely murdered on the 4th inst., while marching in the Whig Procession, and in the exercise of a high and important privilege, guaranteed to every citizen by the Constitution and laws of the United States.  Resolved, That the spirit that would encourage, or justify, such an outrage, is destructive to all free governments, under whatever names, or disguise, it may assume.  Resolved, That we sincerely sympathize with the bereaved widow and orphan children of the deceased, under their severe affliction.

Phrenological works: nor shall we fail to present to our reader, such matters of interest and importance as may be found in foreign Phrenological works of standard excellence and which are not generally accessible to the American public. Our facts, we pledge ourselves, shall be bona fide and as often as practicable, we shall accompany our descriptions with illustrative cuts. The American Phrenological Journal and Miscellaneous will be issued monthly, commencing on the 2nd inst.; each number will contain at least 32 octavo pages, making a volume of not less than 384 pages; corresponding in point of mechanical execution with the best periodicals of the day. Fales, teach er of French and instrumental music; Mrs. H. Strong, teacher in the primary department.  ADDRESS OF THE HON. THOS. J. WORD To the People of Mississippi.  Fellow citizens: As soon as I ascertained the result of the April election, I set out on the business confided to me, and reached the seat of Government about the last of May. My colleague and myself claimed and obtained our seats under the November Election. We viewed the April election as illegal, and one which ought not to have been ordered. We had so declared ourselves at home; and by demanding our admission under the November Election, we have, as far as that act goes, sustained the right of the State to "fix the times, places, and manner of holding elections." But as a further vindica- tion of the rights, dignity, and laws of the State, my colleague, a few days after our admission to our seats, introduced a series of resolutions, setting forth in a clear and bold manner, the rights of the State, the dangerous innovation upon the Constitution, by the action of the House on the Mississippi election, and reading the resolution of the 6th of February last, by which your representatives were rejected, and the State disfranchised.

M. M. then endeavored to fasten on the factious, as well as on the miserable condition of the regicide, the crime he had perpetrated. M. Ledru, Alibeau’s advocate, said that when he was chosen by the President of the Court for that difficult task, he had at first hesitated, but, having at last accepted, he resolved to do his duty to the fullest extent. When he repaired to Alibeau's cell, he found him calm, and reading over the bill of indictment. Alibeau greeted him, and told him that he was guilty, and told him he could not be defended: “But,” said he. “my head alone will not suffice for my persecutors; they have pried into my whole life; they must have my honor and that of my family; it is that, and that alone, you must defend.” M. Ledru then read a note Alibeau had drawn out for his defense, etc. He was born of poor but honest parents. One of his uncles had received a sword of honor in the armies of the republic— the Star of the Brave had been placed on his breast by Napoleon, by whom he had been appointed commander of cavalry. M. Ledru related a number of facts creditable to Alibeau. On two occasions he had saved, once a young girl, and another time a fellow soldier, who were on the point of drowning.

It's 3:45 p.m. to the Editor of the Louisville Courier: It appears, by the observations of the chairs of the Presbyterian Union in Tru~la- .,.+ receipt, as well as the editor's of the Memphis Enquirer, etc., that the "Ole Colonel move" in among the doctors. This it proves strikingly true, knowing that his time is e'er short, and that an indebted American reading may be able to judge for themselves what are puffs and proper propositions. The worthy editors who are voters, therefore, should endeavor to get every letter from persons who have in any case resorted to sight in the right place or, plite or cable. The fact is, that I never had such great success within so limited a period as ten or twelve days.
