Every attentive reader of the Old Testament will remember the sinful longing of the royal Psalmist for the property of one whom he was in duty bound to protect, the measures which he took to remove the obstacle which hindered the accomplishment of his desire, and the final issue of the event. And we presume Uncle Sam would be equally incensed were an allegorical description of his own covetousness and injustice to be related to him, and equally astonished on being told—Thou art the man!—Danbury Time.  Washington, Jan. 15th, 1833. In the House of Representatives, tomorrow the report on the Mississippi contested elections, made special order of the day. The friends of the gentlemen claiming seats, in opposition to Mr. Golson and Mr. Claiborne (the sitting members) are sanguine of success. They considered the case so clear and indisputable, that no party trammels are sufficiently strong or powerful to secure a majority in favor of confirming the right of the sitting members. On this point I hazard no opinion, but simply state the fact. It is certain that some of the administration members, who have examined the Report critically, are, to say the least of it, staggered in their former opinion. I could name, if it was proper, two individuals thus circumstanced. The Mississippi Legislature have met, and you will see, probably, in the National Intelligencer, that both branches have chosen Whigs as presiding officers.

Numberless other instances have occurred similar to the above, which neither time nor space will permit me to give in detail. He will insert from one to a full set of artificial teeth, when requisite; and those entire sets of teeth are worn with great ease and satisfaction, answering every purpose of mastication; at once curing all impediments of speech, and withal ornamental, in filling up the languid look of countenance. Mr. H. feels confident the teeth (being his own manu facture) possess every requisite quality in color, size.

Just before ray fever came on, in one of her fits of intemperance, she strolled away, and was gone for three days and three nights; and to this hour, I have never known where she was all that time. It almost broke my heart. The doctor always said there was something on my mind; but I never told him or anyone else, the cause of my trouble till now. What a curse! Don't you think, sir, that something can be done to put an end to this terrible curse of intemperance? Your case is a very hard one,' said the collector after a solemn pause, 'and I wish I could point out a remedy. You need give yourself no uneasiness about the doctor's bill, for I am sure he will think no more about it, when I have told him your story. If it would not give you too much pain, and take up too much time, I should like to be informed a little more particularly, of the commencement and progress of this habit in your wife, which seems to have destroyed your domestic happiness. Johnny Hodges wiped his brow, and sat down on a bench, in his shop, and the collector took a seat by his side. Eight years ago, said Johnny Hodges, 'come the first day of next month, I was married. Polly Wilson, that was her maiden name, was twenty-three, and I was four years older.

Thus the urchins continued with boisterous glee,  Till, my toilet disposed of, they then turned to me:  "And now," said their leader, with mirth in his eye  (Though I own for my part I felt ready to cry),  "What remains to be done is confoundedly stupid,  But, to finish the job, we must bury old Cupid!"  This roused me at last, and I leaped out of bed,  When, quicker than lightning, the rascals all fled,  And left me—I will not describe my condition—  But heartily glad it was only a vision.  City.—The papers and private letters from the  United States and Canada do not add at all to the  copious information which was given from thence in  the "Morning Herald" of this day. They convey  the impression, which is responded to here by parties  who have received advices from New York, that  there will not be any hostile movements between the  government of this country and the United States,  though fears are entertained that the borderers of  both parties will occasionally come into collision.  It is, nevertheless, considered that the disputes respecting the boundary line will require the exercise  of much firmness and tact on the part of this government, in order to bring the urgent negotiation which must now ensue to a favorable issue.—Morning Herald, 21st ult.

The following is a literal copy of a transcript from the record of a Justice of the Peace. We publish it to show that "the course of justice, as little as that of love, did never yet run smooth." Benjamin Clark, June the 19th, 1855. The plaintiff charged the defendants (Trayvon and Feudalts) with breaking his Richard Brittain. watch, to his damage 45 dollars, and to taking five dollars from his pocket, totalling i dollars. This day the parties appeared, the court required a jury, and after empanelling six jurors, Dennis Ramell and Thomas Phillips were called in as witnesses for the plaintiff. Judge Bates advocated the cause of the defendants. I read the demand of the plaintiff to the jury but was much interrupted by the noise and was not able to keep order. The jury retired and on their return brought in their verdict: we find for the plaintiff 100 dollars for bodily damage and ten dol-

The reason why he was charged no interest on the bond for some five months which was due, or more, was because of his, Robinson's, paying the cash. Thought he would pay the money that he might get out of Mr. S's. clutches. He had heard that Mr. Hoagland had built a house, and when it was two-thirds done, Mr. S. would not let him have any more money. He had talked with Mr. S. about paying the money two or three days before he had paid him—no one was present. Does not know whether the payment was made by him before or after the election. No one came into the room while he was paying Mr. Suydam, was all done in 20 minutes. Would not tell where he got the money; guess it was his own business where he got the money; he worked and earned the money; had the money in his possession 18 months. He does not recollect exactly the size of the notes paid, thinks it was $5 and $10. He made the contract with Mr. S. last fall 1839, and commenced digging for the cellar 2 or 3 days before the fall election. Part of the bargain was, that Mr. S. was to advance him money to build his house. The contract was only verbal. The first money he received was an order on Mr. S. paid to Mr. J. Cheeseman, when the foundation was up, for $95.

The more we reflect on the subject, the more are we convinced of the arrant folly of getting into a war with France on the subject of the 5,000,000 Treaty. It was easy three months ago, and may not be difficult still, to avoid so great a calamity, without in the slightest degree compromising our national honor. As we have before stated, we do not perceive that the national honor is involved in the question at all. And we observe that many of the most chivalrous members of Congress from the South, take the same view of the subject that we do. Mr. Gilmer of Georgia, in a speech on Mr. Adams’ motion, spoke directly to this point.

Nevertheless, Mr. Adams has been eminently successful in his fanciful, but elegant trifling of his leisure hours. He has chosen for his theme the events which led to the possession of Ireland by England in the twelfth century, and as has always been the fact, since the days of Eve, man's downfalls of man and empire, and women is the cause of all. Dermot, King of Leinster, seizes and carries off Dervorgilla, wife of Ororlc, who in return attacks Dermot and drives him out of the kingdom. He applies to Henry of England for succor, and ravages his kingdom, which he consents to hold in vassalage, under the crown of England. Henry availed himself of this claim, and through it achieved the entire conquest of Ireland. This outline Mr. Adams has wrought up to a beautiful moral, illustrating the trite, but as he observes, false maxim, that History is Philosophy, teaching by examples an aphorism which he says has made a greater fortune in the world than it deserves. "History, as it should be written and read, is the school of morals, teaching sometimes by examples, but much more frequently by admonition." To test this truth, says Mr. A, "I would ask the young men and women of my native country, who may charge an idle evening with the perusal of the history which."

The Palace at St. Petersburg, which was destroyed by fire on the 30th December, was built by Count Stroganov, about 80 years ago, in the early part of the reign of the Empress Elizabeth, granddaughter of Peter the Great. The loss occasioned by the fire is estimated at 125 millions of francs. A splendid gallery of paintings was destroyed, in which were several chef d'oeuvres of Rubens. Nothing was saved but the children of the emperor, a bird and some few articles of silver plate, and the imperial diamond. The Empress was at the Theatre, where Taglioni was dancing when the fire broke out. The cold was so intense that the water would have frozen in the air, had no engines with furnaces been used, which kept the element from congealing. After the fire had burned for an hour, this was unnecessary, as the immense volume of flame so heated the air as to melt the ice into a river, close by.

Would you admit him into your house? Would not his arrival at any place where you might be, raise throughout your frame the same kind of shudder as if you were in the Garden of Plants, and the lion had broken loose? and yet he is a man, as well as you—and equally in want of friendship and love, which he can demand only from those circumstanced as he is. He and his are like a family of Chandala in the midst of a community of Brahmins. Do not believe, however, that the office of executioner can ever want an occupant. When Monsieur de Versailles died some years ago, without issue, there were hundreds of applications for his office. Eight of the candidates were old soldiers, several of them butchers. This fact leads to a horrible doubt. Can it be possible that all men are qualified for such an office, and that familiarity with blood is alone wanting? "I return to my visit. "I was ushered into a small room, where I saw a man about sixty, with a countenance beaming with mildness and candour, amusing himself at the piano. This was the executioner? In the same room was his son, a young man of three or four and thirty, with light hair, and a mild timid look.

We refer to a late communication in the Commercial Advertiser, under the signature of "An Irishman." He says, in reference to the expected arrival of three ships mentioned by Dr. Rockwell in his letter to the Mayor, and which are expected soon to arrive, having on board seven hundred and seventy-three passengers for this country. "The three ships in which they are expected to arrive, are, to my certain knowledge, bound by their charter party, to proceed in ballast to Amboy, and thence to Quebec." If these passengers are "principally mechanics and laboring men, always acceptable in this country," as he says, why are they landed at Amboy? Why are not such valuable citizens sent to Canada? Why are our Quarantine Laws thus openly violated, in their eager haste to empty the polluted contents of these vessels on our shores? And above all, how happens it that this "Irishman's" countrymen, as he claims they are, are so soon found begging in our streets, or applying to the commissioners of our Alms-house for relief? But it is not the pauperism of these foreigners of which we alone complain. Our political institutions are endangered by their unwarrantable interference in our governmental affairs; and their increasing numbers enhance that danger. During the last seven years, 296,359 foreigners have arrived at this port.

And be it further enacted, That any father, or mother after the death of the father, may enter in the name of each child, and as the property of such child, one tract of the public lands subject to entry at private sale, not exceeding one section, upon first filing with the register and receiver of the proper land district the affidavit of such parent as aforesaid, that said entry is made only for the use of said child, and not for sale or speculation; that said child is at least twelve and not twenty-one years of age, and that no previous entry has been made in the name of said child, which, with the quantity then sought to be entered, would exceed one section; that said parent is a citizen of the United States, and has not entered any land under the provisions of this section, which, with the quantity then sought to be entered, would exceed two sections; whereupon, on payment of the purchase money to the receiver, a receipt shall issue therefor in the name of the said child, but no patent shall issue for said land until one year after said child has attained the age of twenty-one years, and any sale, lease, or mortgage, or contract for the sale, lease, or mortgage of said land, made before the emanation of the patent shall be null and void. On motion of Mr. Cummins, the names of S. M. Rutherford and Daniel K. Mills, were added to the list of justices of the peace for Big Rock Township, Pulaski co., Mr. Cummins from a joint committee appointed to confer with Wm. S. Fulton and John Pope, Governor and ex-Governor of the Territory, made a report, accompanied with two resolutions—the first of which fixed the day of adjournment on today, (Nov. 6) which was rejected; the second, providing that John Pope be authorized to deposit all funds in his hands belonging to the Territory, in the hands of the Treasurer, and that the present Governor dispose of the same to secure the public buildings from the effects of the weather—and that the late Governor should deposit all deeds, &c. in his possession for the Territory, with the Secretary, was adopted. On motion of Mr. Cummins, the following resolution was adopted— Resolved, That the act authorizing a call of the Convention, be published in all the newspapers printed in this Territory, weekly, until the second Monday in December next, and that the publishers forward their accounts to the Auditor. On motion of Mr. Fowler, it was Resolved, That this House approve of the conduct of John Wilson, Speaker, in the indefatigable and impartial discharge of his duties in the chair, during the present session. On motion, the House adjourned sine die.

The Algerine unhesitatingly threw himself upon the Frenchman, embraced him and closely pressing him to his arms, turned to the gunner, and said "Fire! since I cannot save my benefactor, I shall die with him." All the spectators were affected at this sight; the gunner withdrew, and the people rescued the Frenchman in spite of the Dey, who, though unmoved at the scene, was unable to oppose any resistance. When a man after regularly receiving a newspaper for years, pleads that he only intended to take it for one year; or when another pleads the statute of limitations in bar of a just claim, we feel disposed to gazette these bankrupts in integrity, and expose them to the scorn of honest people.

This dispute arose out of a question of moral honesty, the accuser having charged the assailant with the commission of a robbery, which he repelled by a knock down blow. The jury found Hamilton guilty, and recommended him to the mercy of the Court. This culprit was indicted for knocking down another man, and his counsel consented to take a similar verdict, which compromise was agreed to. Simon P. Huff was charged with assaulting one Bernard I. Simpson, a Dutch dealer in spirits. It appeared that the defendant was an officer of the Marine Court, and that having an execution against Simpson, went to his store, and while the boss was away at his dinner, Huff came, and put his "paw" on a pipe of gin, and a half pipe of Rhenish Wine, without letting the owner know what his authority was. An altercation, of course, ensued, wherein Simpson got his coat torn, and was called a Dutchman. The defense was that Simpson interfered with Huff in the execution of his duty. The jury found Master Huff guilty and the court adjudged him to pay $20 or go to the tomb. This dilemma was anything but pleasant to the feelings of Huff, but after some grumbling, he made a raise, paid the money, and was once more a free man. WASHINGTON, July 3rd, 1839. Dr. Leidy, Respected Sir: Twelve months have now elapsed since you begged of me to make trial of some of your medicinal preparations. I have been strongly opposed to quackery, but having known you intimately for a long time, and knowing you to be a regular physician, I resolved at all events to make trial of your medicines, for twelve months, before I should publicly make known their effects. I have found your Blood Pills one of the most valuable purgatives I ever employed. Indeed, so pleased am I with their effects that I seldom employ any other purgative. I give them in mostly all diseases where a purgative is found necessary. It is not only as a purgative, however, that I employ them; I find in Rheumatic pains, Diseases of the Skin, and various other affections, they are equally effectual. Dr. Bechter's Pulmonary Preservatives, for Coughs, Catarrhs, Influenza, and diseases of the Breast and Lungs, is a medicine possessing all the virtues you represent it to possess.

Grinnell, all leading whigs, went on to Philadelphia to engage men to come on to New York at the election. From the testimony of Stevenson, one of Wentworth's assistants, these men were to be brought on to vote; but Mr. Blatchford says the intention was to bring them on to prevent democrats of Philadelphia from voting in New York. But, be this as it may, Wentworth, while at Philadelphia was making arrangements to get men to come on to New York to vote, when he received a letter from Bowen, containing from $200 to $500 dollars, requesting him to stop all further proceedings, and with directions to compensate the men and leaders, and hush up the matter. Mr. Blatchford says that this was done because Wentworth had gone beyond his instructions in getting men who were to vote instead of getting them to watch the polls; but Stevenson says it was because they ran out of funds, and this appears, from the subsequent movements of Wentworth, to have been the true reason, for he immediately returned to New York, had an interview with his friends, told them that an attempt to stop a fair would lead to a full exposure, and was next day again sent to Philadelphia, with about $3,000.

My views and opinions in regard to it were, however, communicated to the people of the United States, in reply to a letter received in the same year, from Junius Amis, Esq., and other citizens of North Carolina, and also through other channels. Perceiving that I cannot do justice to your inquiries in the form which you have given to them, by a general reference to the answers I have heretofore given, I will repeat the substance of them. First: That the relation of Master and Slave, is a matter which belongs exclusively to each state within its own boundary, that Congress has no authority to interfere, in any respect whatever, with the emancipation of the slaves, or in the treatment of them in any of the states; and that any attempt to do so by the government or people of any other state, or by the General Government, would not only be unauthorized, but violate the spirit of the compromise, which lies at the basis of the Federal Compact; and which is binding in honor and good faith on all who live under the protection of the Federal Constitution, and participate in its benefits. This doctrine is in strict conformity to the principle embodied in a Resolution passed by the House of Representatives of the United States, in 1790, upon the report of a Committee, consisting almost entirely of northern men.

It has been stated, the President offered to two of them, Mr. Ingham and Mr. Branch, after their removal, places of trust and honor, let it be set down to his kindness, not to their merits. He did not then know these men. He did not know how incapable they were of properly appreciating acts of kindness. He was ignorant that they had entered his Cabinet, all smiles and fair professions, with daggers concealed in their bosoms. He little knew that these persons who were admitted to his familiar intercourse, had been taking notes of his private conversations and free expressions, which had been conned over between them and prepared and carefully laid away for future uses. He did not in fact know, they had been spies upon him from the beginning of his administration; and that, finding themselves deprived of the means of longer stealing into his bosom, to hunt out and note down his thoughts, they were now ready for open, implacable and exterminating war. These things he did not then know. Recent events have disclosed them. Now he comprehends the depths of the designs of these three gentlemen. Having resigned my seat in the Cabinet, and being about to retire to my residence in Tennessee, I did not calculate that I should be detained here from my home and business, to defend himself against their unprovoked attacks.

Sir: Pellarmine was made a cardinal, as a reward for maintaining the paradoxical proposition, that, the Pope chose to forbid the exercise of virtue, and command that of vice, the Romish church would sin against conscience, did it not instantaneously abut on the former and commence the practice of this latter. Were every man who holds a similar doctrine at the present day, with regard to the head of the political church, to be rewarded in like manner, the creation of new offices, though they should spring into existence with more train virtuous room rapidity, would hardly keep pace with the increase of the emreiti who would swarm in to fill them. Liverpool Cotton Market, Aug. 11—The sales of cotton today have again been very large, the total amount being as many 11,000 bags, 3,000 of which are on speculation, and 4000 for export. The market is very firm, and the full prices of yesterday have been obtained. Liverpool Cotton Market, Aug. 14—We have again had a very large week's business done in Cotton. Speculators having purchased heavily throughout, have caused an advance in the common qualities of American of 1 to 1 1/2 d on the quotations of this week. In Brazilian and Egyptian, only a moderate business has been done and they are limited of sale at our quotations. East India descriptions have been in very good demand, and the common qualities have advanced 1d per lb. Machine for Copying Oil Pictures.—The London Morning Herald states that M. Liepmann, a painter of eminence at Berlin, has invented a mechanical process for taking, in a very short time, a copy of any painting in oil, however old, with an exactitude which can not be attained by the brush. The machine was exhibited in the galleries of the Royal Mu seum at Berlin, and, with the presence of its di rectors, 110 copies made of a portrait by Rem brandt with the greatest success. There seems to be no limit to the wonderful discove ries in the Fine Arts made in the present day. From Maravzas.—Sept. 18— The prospect for the coming crop of sugar never was better, and will probably be both abundant and early in market. The favorable season has al so produced large crops of corn, rice and other ground provisions—the supply of which will materially affect the demand for foreign rice in the course of a month, and prices must go low with you, to make shipments of the article with any hope of profit.—Charleston Patriot. Prescription of Mourning Apparel.—A meeting has been held by the citizens of Cazenovia, N. Y., when it was resolved that the wearing of mourning clothes for the dead was useless, and proceeded more from ostentation than sin cere regret. The meeting, therefore determined to use their influence to abolish the fashion from society.

The first blood drawn from the veins of a British officer in that great quarrel, said Mr. Tillinghast, Providence, in an eloquent address before a band of our surviving veterans of '70, was drawn by a shot from a Rhode Island musket upon our own waves, within sight from the tower of that temple where we have, this morning, heard the scenes and principles of the revolution so eloquently reviewed by the orator of the day. The owner of that musket still lives in honor amongst us, still characterized by that native resolution, which the lapse of sixty-seven years from that time has not been able to extinguish or abate. The first sword that waved in triumph upon the surmounted rampart of Yorktown, was a Rhode Island sword. The owner of that sword, as he clambered up the work, received upon his hands and arms the stabs of the bayonets that were aimed at his life, and, having gained the summit, and planted himself firmly there, he lifted his sword aloft in his bleeding hand, and called aloud to his friends and foes, 'Captain Stephen Olney's company forms here. The prisoner, unfortunately, bore a very bad character; and many people did not hesitate to say that, if he had not committed murder, he was not too good to do it. But what made his condition still more unfortunate was the condition of the stomachs of the jury. They had been kept out all night, and it was now near the hour of dining. Consider then, the perilous condition of the poor prisoner, his life depending on such untoward circumstances—presumptive evidence, a doubtful character, a hungry jury! He might have got over the two first; but woe to him whose life depends on the last. A comfortably filled stomach is one of the best guarantees for acts of justice, kindness, and charity. Bribe a hungry jury with one’s life!—Never. Hunger renders a man savage; and he who has a stomach to feed, has a mind in any bowels of compassion. In the case of David Dunn, seven of the jury were for hanging, and five for acquittal. Thus they had continued for some time, without ever agreeing.

In the House, there was a strong effort made by Mr. Russell, of New York, to bring the attention of that body to the fact that they were suffering in the estimation of the country from the tardiness of their proceedings. He demanded that the Committees should have an opportunity to report; but he was overruled. The subject, in order, was the proceedings of the Committee on Elections in the New Jersey case, and the motion of Mr. C. Johnson that was pending, directs the Committee to report the names of the individuals who received the greatest number of votes at the late election. It was, today, stated, in the debate, that the polls showed a clear majority of 142 for Messrs. Vroom, Dickerson, &c. and that only twelve votes of the whole number have been impugned. It is intended, no doubt, to bring the House to a vote on the motion pending, and to follow it up with a motion to admit to their seats those members who, according to the evidence before the Governor and Council of New Jersey, were entitled to the return. The House adjourned without settling any question, except some questions of order, which, by the way, have become frequent and perplexing. What shall we do, when the number of members is increased under the next apportionment?

His Ex. D. Manuel Eduardo Gorostiza, Minister Extraordinary from Mexico to the United States, came passenger in the Montezuma. It is confidently asserted (and this rumor we heard from a very creditable quarter before his arrival) that he is charged with a special mission relative to the cession of Texas to the United States. [N.Y. Paper. A New Orleans paper gives the following account of the execution of the unfortunate victims of Mejia’s treachery, at Tampico. The object of the Mexicans was not only to kill the prisoners, but to make them suffer as much as possible before this was effected—for this purpose the guns were fired within a few paces of their bodies, and aimed at those parts which would not cause instantaneous death. In this way, some of them were shot ten or fifteen times before life was extinct. Their clothes were set on fire by the wadding of the guns, and suffered to burn off of them. Among the number shot were two youths apparently about 17 years of age.—A large pit was then dug, and after these unfortunate but brave men were stripped of their clothes and boots they were indiscriminately thrown in.—A few moments before they were shot, one of them, an American, implored the guards in the presence of a priest, for a drink of water, which was refused! Natchitoches, (La.) March 9.

Dear READER, If you have a cough or cold beware Mixts of their consequence. Colds generally pro Unco imperceptibly, and insinuate themselves throughout the human system, finally settling upon the lungs, and ending in consumption. A WORD TO PARENTS. How often is youth cut down when least expect ed by the consumption, and followed to their graves by parents who are in a measure the cause of their premature death, in neglecting to remedy colds when existing in childhood, looking upon them as trifling affections, and not attracting their notice until the destroyer has commenced its work and made sure of its victim. This is not a fanciful representation, for daily numerous instances occur which prove the fact. In manhood colds terminate in the same way, but do not progress so rapidly as in youth; they should, however, in both youth and manhood, be early at tended to, and not regarded as trifling affections, for it is a delusive idea that has shortened the lives of thousands. In Pennsylvania, where the Democratic Party has always had a majority of 20 or 30,000, the Van Buren electors have succeeded by something short of 5000. In Connecticut, it has been neck and neck, and the Democratic ticket has been elected by less than a thousand majority. In New York, Maine, and New Hampshire, the success of our ticket is unquestioned. These states have 97 electoral votes and are certain for Van Buren and Johnson. In Ohio, there has been an available gain as far as heard from of several hundred votes. The Buckeyes have possibly gone against us. — Federal Delaware has again been true to her principles, and gone for the available. Maryland has gone against us by a majority of 310 4000 votes. — Vermont has also gone for the Whigs. These 3 states choose 20 electors whose votes are certain for the opposition. In Virginia, the Whigs claim a great gain over the vote of 1832, but the ratio must increase in the districts to be heard from to secure to the opposition the influence of the Old Dominion. We confess we have been a good deal disappointed in the result of the election in Pennsylvania. Although Wolf's majority in 1832 was no greater than Van Buren’s at present, there has been a great gain to the opposition over the Presidential vote of '32. Our friends confidently calculated to carry the State by a majority of at least 20,000.

As a refined and elegant repository of the belles lettres, embracing every subject within the range of polite literature and the fine arts, the New-York Mirror has received the spontaneous and universal commendation, not only of the press of the United States, but of Great Britain. The first number of a new volume of this beautiful parlor journal will be issued on the twenty-seventh day of June next, at which time, as the work is generally bound at the end of the year, it is desirable that new subscribers should commence their subscription. As it is the intention of the proprietor to print no more copies than shall be required, this early notice is given in order to prevent the disappointment that usually takes place in applications for the first number of a new volume. The New York Mirror is the oldest and unquestionably the cheapest periodical in America. Every number contains a great variety of useful, interesting, and amusing matter on every subject connected with polite literature and the fine arts; and they form at the end of the year an immense volume of four hundred and sixteen imperial quarto pages, vignette title page, table of contents, splendid engravings, and fifty pieces of popular music, arranged for the piano-forte, guitar, etc. - a library in itself, and all this is afforded at the very trifling cost of five dollars a year, a sum almost inadequate for the extraordinary equivalent rendered to subscribers.

His Excellency recommends the adoption of measures for extending the system of county poor or workhouses; the establishment of a House of Refuge for the insane; and the erection of a penitentiary at Sing Sing exclusively for females. Upon this latter subject, he remarks— The female convicts belonging to the prison at Sing Sing, are kept in a department of the Alms House in the City of New York, prepared for that purpose, at the great expense of one hundred dollars each. Their number was 49 on the 19th day of December last. The number of female convicts was, on the 17th December last, twenty-nine, who, for want of other accommodations, are confined together in one room—Among them are some of the most profligate of their sex, mingled with others, who, under proper restraint and instruction, might be reclaimed. The keepers find it beyond their power to preserve order among them. On a late visit there, I noticed the decent demeanor of a female, whose sentence of death for murdering her husband, had been commuted by me to imprisonment for three years, under a belief that she had produced the death by inadvertence, and from representations that she was of good character, but ignorant.

The Jeffersonian Republican, a new Weekly Paper, to be published at Stroudsburg, Monroe County, Pa., and Milford, Pike County, Pa., simultaneously. The whole art of Government consists in the art of being honest. Jefferson. THE JEFFERSONIAN REPUBLICAN in principle, will be all its title purports the firm and unwavering advocate of the principles and doctrines of the democratic party, delineated by the illustrious 'Jefferson: the right of the people to think, to speak, and to act, independently, on all subjects, holding themselves responsible to no power for the free exercise of this right, but their God, their Country, and her Laws, which they themselves have created. A free and untrammelled Press, conducted in a spirit worthy of our institutions, is a public blessing, a safeguard to the Constitution under which we live, and it should be cherished and supported by every true republican. Such, then, it is designed to make the paper now established, and as such, the publisher calls upon the enlightened citizens of Monroe and Pike to has arrived when the Press should take a bold and fearless stand against the evidently increasing moral and political degeneracy of the day, and endeavor, by a fair, candid, and honorable course, to remove those barriers which sectional prejudices, party spirit, and party animosity have reared to mar the social relations of men without accomplishing any paramount good.

Mr. Whipple from the committee on the resolution relative to the public lands, reported certain resolutions against dividing the public lands amongst the new States. Mr. E. R. Potter said a few words had struck him as slightly of a party character, and if those were amended, he hoped the resolutions would pass unanimously. Mr. Robbins thought this the most important business of the session, and he hoped the House would come together in the afternoon and have a fair discussion of the subject. Mr. Blake thought if this was to be discussed, we must adjourn over. He thought if the resolutions were divested of any reflections of a party character, they might pass unanimously. Mr. Robbins said he would not take up more than twenty minutes. Mr. Simmons said he hoped the House would not pass the resolutions without speaking the truth of the conduct of the administration; there was not a man in the State who would dare put his party tactics on the distribution of the public lands to the new States. And the resolutions were so assigned. Mr. Atwell read the report of Commissioners of Scituate Bank. Received and communicated to the Senate. Petition of John W. Fish, for sale of real estate, received. The House then adjourned to ten o’clock Tuesday morning.

A minute description of this Estate is unnecessary, as it is certain that persons disposed to purchase will examine it particularly: they are invited to do so, and Mr. A. Smith, the Manager, residing on the premises, is instructed to show any gentleman who desires it, every part of the Estate. I will only add, that these Lands lay between Ware and Severn Rivers, and extending to both, affording navigation to vessels of large burden, and furnishing fish and oysters of the best quality, and in great abundance. The improvements consist of a large brick Dwelling House, with four rooms on a floor, nearly new, and finished in the best style; all necessary out houses, some of them brick, and most of them new. The Low Grounds lay in one compact body, immediately in front of the house, without a single break, and every part to be seen from the dwelling at a single glance of the eye; they are finely calculated for the production of Barley, Wheat, Indian Corn, Cotton, etc.; in fact, this Estate is generally acknowledged by all who have seen it, to be the best of the same extent, and one of the handsomest in Va. The time of payment will be made to suit the purchaser, and the necessary number of hands to cultivate the Estate, together with the stock of every kind, farming utensils, etc. will be sold with it, if required. Robinson, (said note now produced) lie took up the note himself, paid the money to Mr. S. in the Bank, Mr. S. was alone in the Bank, paid the note rising 3 weeks ago, the note was discounted by the Bank, was not due when paid, was due on the 9th December inst, does not know whether Mr. S. made any entry in the books, did not see him do so, was in the Bank but a few minutes. Had a gold watch in his possession; had shown it perhaps to a dozen persons; bought it in New York; — bought it of a pedlar 18 months ago; cost 75 dollars; double case; has been to New York two weeks ago today. There was no written agreement between himself and Mr. S. in relation to the property; has applied to Mr. Edmonds to purchase lumber on credit; called on him twice; thinks he wanted about $33 worth; thinks it was in April last he called on Edmonds. He told Mr. Edmonds that he had expended all his money; perhaps may have told Mr. Edmonds that he had no money, but he could pay him if he trusted him; Mr. Edmonds told him he had spent his money at other yards. Gave as a reason that he asked Messrs. Vail Acker to trust him, and they told him they would trust him no longer than he could put his hand in his pocket and pay the money.

WYOUNG GENTLEMEN are qualified for the duties of the Counting House at Foster's Establishment, 191 Broadway, in an expeditious and superior manner. Book- keeping, Penmanship, etc. are taught on an improved plan, by which a competent knowledge of these branches may be ob tained in one third of the time usually devoted to that pur pose. Prospectuses may be had by applying at the Rooms. [from the Boston Evening Gazette.] The manner in which Book-keeping is frequently taught, conveys a very imperfect idea of the practice of merchants. The great difference between theory and practice?between the study of an art and its application, is too well known to need remark; and we think Mr. Foster's plan possesses advan tages worthy the consideration of all who wish to acquire the forms and modes of business in a thorough and effectual man ner. [from the Boston Republican.] We are personally acquainted with Mr. Foster and take great pleasure in recommending his establishment to the no tice of our fellow citizens.

The three last numbers of "A Citizen of Mississippi," addressed to the editors of the State, are unavoidably postponed. They shall appear as soon as we can possibly find room for them. "The wandering piper," with the Great Western," or the Great Pacificator," and "Love among the Roses," alias "the Father of the American System," alias the projector of the Fifty Million Bank, alias Henry Clay of Kentucky, held his last barbecue at Baltimore, where he made a speech for the Whig Congressional candidates. There ends his pilgrimage, we believe, which for folly, violence, intrusiveness, and indelicacy, can hardly find a parallel in the annals of electioneering. The running commentary upon it, too, by the people, has been worthy of remembrance. While Mr. Clay has been eating, drinking and spouting in the East, as they were before the flood, the mighty tide has come upon the party in the West, and "swept them" away. Charlatan Mercury. Monday, Feb. 4. Petition of Elijah Grover and 00 others, that the Legislature may inquire by what authority and for what cause one Tappan is confined in Augusta jail, and render him relief, presented by Mr. Levensaler of Thomaston, was referred to a select committee, with power to send for persons and papers, consisting of Messrs. Levensaler, Smiley, Nickerson, Cushman, Bradbury, Mason and Sheldon, with such as the Senate may join. Mr. Otis called up the resolve in favor of Damariscotta Bank, and tomorrow was assigned. At the request of Mr. Vose, Mr. Buxton moved a reconsideration of the vote referring the petition of Elijah Grover and 55 others to a joint select committee. Mr. Vose said the House cannot possibly have any jurisdiction in this case; our Courts of justice have ample power from time to time to examine into the case of prisoners suspected to be confined without cause. Mr. Vose stated that this Tappan is insane—that his friends had done everything in their power—they had endeavored to get him boarded in private families—to keep him in their own— but the nature of his disease prevented. They had then offered to pay his expenses at Insane Hospitals in Massachusetts, but his case had been of so long standing that he could not be received.

JUST received, a fresh supply of the above useful Medicine, direct from Dr. Lee’s Dispensary 12th mo 30th, 1833. W. STABLER. The following extract is published by Dr. Lee’s authority, viz:— Extract of a letter, written by a very respectable gentleman, residing in Baltimore, to the subscriber, who deems it his duty, though unauthorized, to lay it before the public, withholding the writer’s name, for particular reference, should any require it personally. SAM. H. P. LEE. Baltimore, August, 1828. Doctor S. H. P. Lee, New London, Conn. Dear Sir—It is now upwards of twenty years that I have used your New London Bilious Pills in my family, and always found them an efficient purgative in almost every description of disease, and believe they saved many a long doctor’s bill by their early and timely use. I never found their accustomed certainty to vary in their operation and effects until a few months past; and having, since then, been sadly disappointed in every instance of using them, I began to conclude that you had made your fortune, and abandoned their manufacture to mercenary and unfaithful hands.

On the 30th June last, the ship Byron left Liverpool for New York, heavily laden with iron, salt, etc. and having on board, in passengers and crew, 119 or 130 souls. On the morning of the 3rd of August, 34 days out in lat. 44 22, long. 48 50, a scene occurred which can never be effaced from memory. It was the watch of the first mate, a man of great fidelity; but, being indisposed, his place was taken by another. An unusual degree of levity and thoughtless security among the passengers had just given place to sleep. And now all was still, save the tread of the watch on deck, or the occasional toll of the bell to warn fishing craft, if near, of our approach; but we had more need to be warned ourselves than to give warning to others of approaching danger. About 2 o'clock A. M a hurried step awoke the writer of this sketch; and the rapid whispering of some created the suspicion that all was not right. Springing from his berth, he asked one of the men near the cabin door what was the matter. "We are in the midst of ice," said he; "Will you inform the captain and mate?" The captain was instantly on deck; he ran forward to lookout. In a moment the vessel, going at the rate of five knots, struck as if against a solid rock.

From the Cincinnati Republican. GEN. HARRISON AT FORT MEIGS. The following rapid sketch of General Harrison's remarks at Fort Meigs are fur nished by a correspondent who was pres ent, and stood by the side of the General whilst addressing the assembled thous ands. The writer furnishes but a synopsis of the General's speech, without, of course, pretending to furnish the exact language or the connection in which it was used. The venerable speaker commenced by saying: "I am not here, fellow citizens, in con sonance with my own wishes. I have ever supposed that a candidate for the Presidency of the United States should never be a man who seeks the office, but rather a man who is sought for by the people. I should still have been here in spirit, had I not been able to come nevertheless at the request of friends, at your solicitous I cannot but feel affected, fellow citizens, when I look around me.

A discussion ensued upon the plan of the Prince of Orange’s campaign, and as these gentlemen seldom concurred upon any subject, so they differed upon this, one censuring and the other approving of the Prince’s arrangements. The discussion lasted for a considerable time. When M. de Rumigny returned, the whole party rode out for the Bois de Boulogne, and a secluded spot was chosen for the determination of the quarrel. The combatants entered a thicket, and the friends withdrew to a little distance, where they remained waiting the event. After the lapse of some time, becoming impatient, they again approached the spot, but what was their surprise to see the two principals still engaged tete-a-tete in their dissertation. M. de Rumigny raising his voice, said—“Messieurs, it is impossible that two men so distinguished as you can have any inclination to cut each other’s throats without the most serious provocation. Only think of the scandal which you give to the enemies of your country, who are already too eager to triumph in our division. Gen. Lamarque, M. Sebastiani thinks himself offended, have you any objection to declare at the tribune that you had no intention of being personal.’ ‘Sir,’ replied the General with vehemence, ‘I could sooner burn all the powder in Paris.’—‘Then at least you would write a letter to the newspapers?’

But admitting that the only object of naturalized citizens in opposing a change in the naturalization laws, is an innocent desire to oblige and gratify their friends abroad, who may wish hereafter to emigrate to the United States! Are the Representatives of the American People to legislate, to please foreigners, now in a distant land? or should the benefit and gratification of their native born constituents, and the welfare and happiness of their country, be their only object, aim, and end? If one single native born American desired the repeal of the naturalization laws, and conscientiously impressed with the conviction that they are pernicious to his country, demanded constitutionally their abrogation by Congress, I hold it is as an axiom that his petition should take precedence of, and outweigh in potentiality with his rulers, all the combined exertions and solicitations of all the foreign nations of the earth, and all their naturalized agents, put together. If naturalized citizens were able to adduce any advantages which could possibly accrue to the United States by a continuance of the process of naturalization, it might, to a certain extent, contribute to extenuate their guilty interference (as a foreign party) with the desires of our native born inhabitants; but as all their objections to a change of the naturalization laws are based upon their foreign affections and predilections, they of course come under the above category.

The vote of Mr. John Baker, the individual alluded to by "Citizen," was, it is believed, the last one offered, and not more than ten minutes before the polls were closed. Yet for the purposes of "Citizen," he thinks proper to represent that it was at the "period of the day when the contest seemed to be waxing warm." Mr. Baker, we are informed, is the overseer of Dr. Alston, of Clarke county, and resided with his family upon the plantation of the Doctor in Clarke. Sometime before the election, the smallpox appeared among the negroes of the Doctor, and Mrs. Baker left there and spent a few weeks at her father’s, in Washington county; but Mr. Baker continued in Clarke as the overseer of the Doctor, and his house in Washington county was occupied by another family. It may be remarked that Mr. Baker, together with his family, is now residing in Clarke county, in the house of the late Dr. Alston. Judge Crawford returned from dinner not more than fifteen minutes before the polls were closed, and, as even his position seems to be important, he was standing outside of the house, because it was less oppressive than inside, when the vote of Mr. Baker was offered.

ANDREW JACKSON. Washington, Feb. 6, 1837. UNITED STATES AND MEXICO. The Globe, of Friday last, is filled in part with an interesting correspondence between Mr. Ellis, our late Charge at Mexico, and Mr. Forsyth, Secretary of State. We are unable to publish this correspondence in full, and can only give a brief outline of its contents. It seems that Mr. Ellis did not leave Mexico altogether upon his own responsibility, but agreeably to instructions from the Secretary of State. In July last Mr. Forsyth addressed a letter to Mr. Ellis setting forth certain causes for complaint made by the Government of the United States against the Government of Mexico.

These warriors stated to the General that they would endeavor to bring Powell to terms, who was on an island in the Ocklawaha, destitute of provisions and ammunition, with a few devoted followers. Juniper and Alligator also stated that the greater part of the Indians were humbled and thoroughly convinced that it was fruitless to contest any longer with the whites. Defeated in all their endeavors to obtain a supply of ammunition from Cuba and other places, they are, no doubt, crestfallen. The chiefs above named told General Jesup that they would, with his permission, bring in Powell by fair means, if possible; if not, they would use stratagem or force. They tendered their families as hostages, and their offer was accepted. "BETTER STILL." P.S. By the despatches forwarded by express to St. Marys, and though brought by the Ocmulgee, not opened until her arrival here (Darien), an addressed to an officer on board, contains the important intelligence that Oceola, subsequently to the foregoing news, came in to Jesup's camp with 300 followers. He ranged his men in a line and concealed himself behind a tree. When Jesup came up, he (Oceola) sprang from the tree and gave himself up as a prisoner of war, and has thus secured himself the title of hero. Darien Tel. "Invitation," with a vengeance.

The Providence Journal says: The young Whigs turned out yesterday afternoon in full strength. With the aid of shovels, spades, pickaxes, etc. soon leveled the site for the log cabin, dug the hole for the liberty pole, and prepared everything in proper style for the building which is to be erected today. We learn that some of our friends from the country towns are coming in today, to assist; all will be welcome. Massachusetts Awake. The Whig Convention held at Northampton, Mass. on Wednesday numbered 250 delegates, the largest ever held in the interior of that State. They came in processions, with music and banners through a severe snowstorm. The towns of Amherst and Hadley alone counted 500. The right spirit is at work in the Old Bay State.

The Ohio Canal is now open from Lake Erie to within four miles of Chilicothe, a distance of two hundred and fifty miles. On Monday of last week the water was let into the Columbus feeder, and the first boat arrived there from Lake Erie. The event was celebrated by a salute of twenty-four guns, a procession, a dinner, etc. Truly, the citizens of Ohio have reason to rejoice and exult in the success which has attended this stupendous work of internal improvement, by their infant state. A number of Merchants of Chilicothe have received their supplies of fall and winter goods by the way of the canal for one dollar and eighty-seven cents per hundred, including commission, etc. By the former route they paid about four dollars a hundred. Had Rail-roads or Canals been in successful operation during the late war, more money would have been saved in the transportation of cannon and ammunition, as well as troops, than would have completed a railroad from Georgia to Maine. In a few months the railroad between Philadelphia and New York will enable passengers to complete the journey in six hours. In four years a railroad can be completed from New York to Albany, on which the distance of 150 miles can easily be traveled in eight hours.

No good man can have seen without deep gratification the progressive, and latterly very rapid, amendments, on both sides, of that baneful temper a change which even the most sceptical has not questioned, nor either country shown inclination to repel. While the growth and multiplication of ties, both commercial and personal, between the two branches of the old British stock move steadily, though silently, onwards, year after year, we discover periodically the fruits of that complex, continuous, and strengthening relation, in the reception now given every six or eight months by Englishmen to the expositions made by the President and his predecessor to the legislature of the United States, intended as are those documents for the whole civilized world, have not merely created a respectful sensation throughout the British islands, virulence against the people of the Republic being now confined to the oratory of Mr. Daniel O'Connell, but have met with a cordial and brotherly hearing, and have excited sympathies in the breasts of the people of England nearly, if not completely, akin to the feelings with which considerate and upright Americans themselves.

When the decree went forth, pronounced by the Hero of the Iron Nerve, that the Bank of the United States, after the expiration of its present charter, should not be renewed; when that decree was sanctioned and sustained by a virtuous and patriotic people, resolved to restore their wounded and downtrodden Constitution, then commenced the Second War of the Revolution. The second war of revolution, only bloodless as yet, because the largest esses, open bribery, violence, and excesses, practiced in New York, New Jersey, and Pennsylvania, could not provoke an honest, self-possessed, and resolute people into similar practices, riots, and excesses and because a sufficient number of hired myrmidons could not be procured to fire on their countrymen, and protect, and cover up and conceal the fraud, corruption and profligacy which had been practiced at Harrisburg. Seven long years have we been involved in this war of revolution, and have not yet approached the beginning of the end. Seven years have nearly elapsed; during that long period many have fallen in the conflict, many passed from the scene of action; many leaders and whole masses of men have changed their position, altered their relations to each other the old landmarks have been obliterated, darkness has come over the path of the people, the dear objects of their pursuit seem to have eluded them at every step, and they are, apparently, no nearer their attainment than when they first began.

The room swam around her; the lights reeled before her eyes. It was Talbot whom she saw before her. Talbot! Yes! It was Talbot—her first, her last, her only love. It was that most guilty, most miserable of beings, who now, with bloodless lips, and fixed eyes, and heart whose pulsation seemed arrested in his bosom, sat gazing on the apparition of her whom he had injured, insulted, yet oh! amid sin and madness, he had never, never ceased to adore. This was indeed a time and a place for such a fearful recognition! They had not met, had not looked upon each other’s face since that night when he clasped her in his arms, and im printed the kiss of an affianced bridegroom on her lips, at her own chamber door. Horror, agony, and despair had rolled over either head since that remembered evening; but they had never met since then. And now they sat and beheld each other; and they knew that, let them dream as they chose, of pride, and of estrangement, and of ob livion, it was all a dream; that they loved at that moment as deeply and as fervently as they loved on the day when their hearts were plighted to each other; and they felt this, and they felt that a gulf was betwixt them, that it was guilt and madness to look upon each other; that she was a wife; and he, what was he? a reckless, and abandoned, and a miserable man.

The first link which bound these youths together was the remarkable circumstances of their having been all three born on one day, and, being all of good families, they had been constant play-fellows in childhood. Had studied at the same academy as school-boys, and had become members of the same university in their more advanced years. Through all these stages of their existence, they had exhibited the same unvarying affection for one another, and had displayed great similarity in their tastes, inclinations, and pursuits. On reaching manhood, however, circumstances led them might have been expected, to adopt different courses of life. Darnacourt, the son of a prominent physician, selected the profession of the law as the road to eminence and an opportunity to the world. St. Maure, whose father was a nobleman of decayed fortunes, chose the army as the most suitable to his birth and pretensions. De Valmont, on the other hand, preferred the captivating study of letters and the fine arts to the pursuit of any positive profession; and the circumstances of his father, a retired colonel of engineers, enabled the young for the time, at least, to indulge his tastes in this respect. Ernest de St. Maure, at the period whence this narrative takes its date, had not yet joined the army, but the imperial mandate for Brussels was then within the dominions of Napoleon I was looked for daily and.

La Grange. —The sanctity of La Grange, the residence of Lafayette—the asylum of the persecuted friends of Liberty—has been violated. The 'National,' of the 12th of March, relates that on that day Gen. Lafayette informed the Chamber of Deputies that a member of the Polish Government, the celebrated Lel ewel, who, driven from Paris at the instigation of the Russian ambassador, had found an asylum at La Grange, had been taken from it by Gensd'armes, who thus violated the sanctity of a dwelling which even the Restoration had respected. The pretext for this savage step, as the General characterized it, was that this proscribed man had been seen in the capital, notwithstanding an engagement not again to appear there. The imputation cast upon M. Lelewel of having brok en his word, Gen. Lafayette and his son both repelled peremptorily. The outrage excited the highest indignation at Paris and raised a storm in the Chamber of Deputies. Expenses of the City. In the Common Council last evening, an order was passed, making appropriations for the next financial year.

The Senate, after ascertaining that a quorum was present and appointing the initial committee, to wait upon the President in conjunction with that of the House, adjourned. In the House, the Clerk proceeded to call the roll and had got to the names of the New Jersey Members when some debate arose. The Clerk stated that two sets of claimants appeared to five of the seats from that State, and accordingly asked liberty to pass over their names until he had completed the roll. This was objected to by Mr. Maxwell, one of the New Jersey Delegation, who called for the reading of their certificates. This was done and it appeared that the whole six appeared then with regular certificates signed by the Governor of New Jersey, and impressed with the Seal of that State. Mr. Hoffman contended that the certificate alone gave a member the right to be called. Mr. Halsted, another of the New Jersey Members, took the ground assumed by Mr. Hoffman and expressed his perfect willingness to be passed over in the call if the evidence of the opposing claimants bore the authentic Seal of New Jersey. Otherwise, he would not consent to have her degraded from the list of other States; but he insisted upon having the evidence of the justice of his claims acknowledged at once.

Philadelphia Election.—In Phila- delphia the election for Governor and Representatives to Congress has gone decidedly against the Administration. The votes for Miner, the Anti-Ma- sonic and Clay candidate for Governor, were 4957; Wolf, 3493. Miner's majority, 1559. Binney and Harper, the Anti-Ma- sonic and Clay candidates for Con- gress are elected—the former by a majority of 2115 over Horn,—the latter by a majority of 1808 over Richards.

Respecting his motion, and the vote on it, I have nothing to say, as neither the one nor the other had anything to do with the subsequent fate of the Roddill. Nor have I anything to do, at present, with this letter writer, further than to remark, that it is well understood that a certain loafer, whose trade is politics, formerly a political scribbler for his party in Dayton, afterwards Clerk to the now defunct Board of Public Works, and who has subsequently been rewarded with a Clerkship in one of the Executive Departments at Washington, is the author of the calumnies against myself that have regularly been transmitted from Washington City for the last few years. He has doubtless performed with fidelity the part assigned him in the Van Buren system of political management, which is known to consist in having a corps of Executive officers, with comfortable salaries, at the seat of Government, to dragoon the representatives of the people into submission to Executive will, and to denounce and vilify them if they presume to disobey. But, it is natural to ask, how this writer became possessed of information, at that early day, which enabled him to foretell the defeat of a measure that had been steadily sustained by every administration since the days of Mr. Jefferson?

In the present state of the grain markets, and the existing position of the Corn Law question, our merchants will not make large advances on such consignments; and we should not be surprised to witness some of the more recent arrivals of wheat sent to France, instead of being warehoused for an indefinite period in England. It does appear to us not altogether unreasonable to expect that some more speedy relief may come to the Bank of England from the operations of the Corn trade than would now be in store for them, if the purchases of our merchants at the northern ports had been less extensive, because from intelligence which we obtained at Hull of the impossibility of getting an adequate supply of shipping to convey corn from Hamburg to France, and the high prices prevailing on the Continent, we should think part of the stock of grain held abroad by British capitalists will be resold, and conveyed by our ships, or those of Sweden and Denmark, to France. We are under no alarm for the effect of any such contemplated demand on the prices of grain in England; at the most it would only check a decline, which might otherwise be more sudden and decided than we imagined to be probable in the foregoing observations.

“I think him (Byron) finely bred, and one of the handsomest horses I ever saw. I never saw him run, but have heard his trainers speak highly of him.” — Extract from J.J. Skinner's letter in 1830. “Byron, of whom you have heard, was purchased by me at 2 years old for £1500. He is of the most approved blood in Virginia, and certainly the most beautiful animal in the world, and of the finest form. When three years old, Byron received an injury in one of his fore legs, which prevented him from showing any figure on the turf, though in a private trial which Col. John son saw, he pronounced him able to run with any horse of his age in the world— When let down entirely, Col. Johnson purchased him of me; his granddam B. Bounce, sold at fifteen years old for 2000 dollars, and produced, by different owners in colts actually sold, $17,000; and I now have a letter by me, offering me 1500 dollars for one of her colts two years old.” — Extract of a letter from Doctor John Page, to a gen. tleman of Hanover county, in 1830. In vain did the prisoner insist on continuing; he was taken by two of the municipal guards, and forced to sit down. He anew protested against the violence offered him, and handing out manuscript to his counsel, he said, "I confide to you this document." The President ordered it to be put into the hands of the officers of the court. M. Ledru, after some hesitation, gave it to M. Sajau, who handed it to the Recorder. M. Bonjour, the second Counsel of Alibeaud, rose and recommended his client to the humanity of the court, which Alibeaud disclaimed with indignation. The President asked Alibeaud if he had anything to add to his defense? The prisoner begged to be allowed to continue his MS. The President said that, if he abstained from the expression of regicide sentiments, he might go on with his defense. The paper was then placed in the prisoner's hand, who skipping over a few leaves, commenced reading anew. No sooner, however, had he read a few lines, in which he ascribed to the King all the sufferings of the people, and contended that it was a praiseworthy act to drain their source, by destroying their author, then the President interrupted him and ordered him to sit down. As he was paying no attention to the President's injunction, but was proceeding with his MS.

From the Niagara Frontier, it will be seen by other accounts in the preceding columns, all is apparently quiet again—the Patriots of Navy Island having abandoned that ground, given up the major part of their arms, and dispersed. It does not appear, however, but that there is still a disposition on the part of some of the troops to organize again, and proceed to the neighborhood of Fort Malden, near the western extremity of Lake Erie, where it is said a force of considerable magnitude is collecting. Letters from Detroit, and also the papers, represent that the people of that section of Michigan are in a state of great excitement on account of the movements there. A letter to the editor of the Baltimore Patriot gives these particulars: Detroit, Jan. 7, 1833. Our city for the last three days has been in great confusion, in consequence of the extensive plan of invading Canada, by certain refugees and others, having been discovered. Nothing of the movement was known save that meetings had been held and resolutions passed, but it was thought the matter would stop there. It has turned out differently, for, on Thursday night, all the arms in our City Arsenal were secretly taken away; on the same day four hundred stand were openly seized at Monroe, and on the succeeding nights our jail was robbed of all the arms and fixed ammunition which had been deposited there for safe-keeping.

There were nine District Courts of Chancery, that would average about 200 causes, but say 1000; some of which causes have 60 parties, and scarcely one less than a half dozen. Distribute these through the counties, (and this was the original scheme,) and is it unreasonable to suppose that one additional counsel on each side will be—must be employed? Suppose $20 only is given to each, the lowest to ever received, it will make $40 in each cause and the sum of $45,000 which must come out of the pockets of suitors and not to those of gentlemen who do not practice in the Superior Courts of Chancery. But there is still another expenditure to be encountered—the present Clerks of these Courts must be paid for taxing costs, levying orders and rules and transmission of records, and $2.50 for each case is a moderate estimate, and this would make $22,500 more, and an aggregate of $172,500. The interest on one-fourth of which would support four Chancellor. I rever, and Hi, must be paid by the present suitors, and when expended, their causes are just where they are at present—(for all these expenses must be incurred, before one advanced step can be taken).

To the Editor of the Liberator: Dear Sir, — I beg the indulgence of your paper to offer the following to the judgment of our brethren. In the times of the Apostles, the worship of images made with hands appeared to be the greatest obstacle in the way of the Christian religion, as slavery may appear to be at present in Democratic and Christian America. It was the custom that the meat of the beasts which were offered in sacrifice to the Idol gods, often in great numbers, should be eaten by the priests, and sold either in the temple or in the market for the maintenance of the Idol’s Temple and of the Priests. Doubtless, many Christian brethren then thought it a great sin to eat meat offered to a false god—and perhaps many thought, as Idolatry was principally supported by the profits of the sacrifices, that if all Christians refused to eat such meat, and could induce all other persons to refuse to eat it, that Idolatry would speedily fall to the ground. It was meat which was principally offered to Idols, and by refusing to eat meat of any kind, a man might be pretty sure, perhaps, not to use anything which was offered to Idols. These facts, which I believe are true, being premised, let us read the 8th chapter of 1st Corinthians, which was written for our edification, ‘upon whom the ends of the earth are come.’

It was a time when the Baptists in the United States were placed, where there were no Acts of uniformity - where there were no tithes, where there were no courts of inquisition, where there was no union of Church and State, where there was no sword of majesty placed in the hands of religious oppressors, they ought to act unshackled and fearlessly. This was the time where we spoke as we printed, and printed what we spoke, and there was "none daring to make us afraid." Mr. K., went on to say, that the Baptists wanted no new Bible, they merely wanted to have the privilege of printing and circulating it as they understood it now. Were they to stand still and not step forward and give the Scriptures unmutilated and uncorrupted to the nation of the earth? They are called upon, under existing circumstances, to have a distinct organization to accomplish the purposes for which the Bible was given to all organizations in Christian effort the most popular. In America and Europe there was no institution which had a greater hold of the popular feeling, than that connected with the circulation of the Scriptures.

The undersigned, His Britannic Majesty's Chargé d'Affaires, has been instructed to state to Mr. Forsyth, the Secretary of State of the United States, that the British Government has witnessed with the greatest pain and regret the progress of the misunderstanding which has lately grown up between the Governments of France and of the United States. The first object of the undeviating policy of the British Cabinet has been, to maintain uninterrupted the relations of peace between Great Britain and the other nations of the world, without any abandonment of national interests, and without any sacrifice of national honor.

The letters, of which we now publish the first, seem to be written in a correct spirit, and are worthy of consideration as the opinions of one who has suddenly come upon a system of religion, which, although to many in this country, from education and habit, is considered the only way to get religion, to him and to his forefathers is entirely novel. At least, the only remarkable instance in this country, was the revival in Dr. Irving's church; but that case is not a fair test in favor of it, as, unfortunately, in its progress, Dr. I. became deranged, and many of his congregation, having surrendered their reason, became unawares, sympathetically affected, and avowed themselves speakers in unknown tongues, etc. We shall continue the publication of these letters at intervals, and we can assure our readers they are well worthy of perusal. The Liverpool arrived on her passage from New York on the 20th July, in thirteen days and a half. Among other interesting news, we have the intelligence of the deaths of Sultan Mahmoud, Lady Hester Stanhope, and Admiral Sir Isaac Cothin. The correspondent of the Commercial Advertiser, at Birmingham, gives an account of the serious riots at that place. An effort was made, it appears, to burn down the town by the mob. The population of Birmingham is about the same as that of New York. The desperate characters concerned with the Chartists, render the appearance of things for a continuance of quiet and order, very unfavorable. Much valuable property had been destroyed by the mobites, and a number of persons had been arrested. The Chartists, it seems, do not confine their operations to Birmingham. They are very active in all the manufacturing towns, and the end of their proceedings no one could conjecture. The crops in England, it is said, will be fair. For wheat and all other grain the prospect is good. Should the wet weather continue, however, for a much longer period, the prospect would be less favorable. A battle between the two great armies of Turkey and Egypt, has been forced on by Ibrahim Pacha, and has resulted in the defeat and dispersion of the Turkish army. The united forces amounted to near two hundred thousand men.

Let it be the prayer of every true patriot, that they may contribute. WASIIINGTON, Dec. 11. "Resolved, That this Government is a Government of limited powers, and that by the Constitution of the United States, Congress has no jurisdiction whatever over the subject of Slavery in the several States of the Confederacy.  "Resolved, That petitions for the Abolition of Slavery in the District of Columbia, and the Territories of the U States, and against the removal of slaves from one State to another, are intended to affect the Institution of Slavery in the several States, and thus, indirectly, to destroy that Institution within their limits.  Resolved, That Congress has no right to do that indirectly, which it cannot do directly, and that the agitation of the subject of Slavery, in the District of Columbia, or in the Territories, or in any manner, with the view of disturbing, or overthrowing that institution in the several States, is equally unconstitutional, and beyond its legislative competency.  Resolved, also, That the Constitution rests on the broad principles of equality among the members of the Confederacy, and that Congress, in the exercise of acknowledged powers, has no right to discriminate, between the Institutions of one portion of the States and another, with the view of abolishing the one, and promoting the other.

His subject, of course, like nine tenths of the members of all parties who address the House in Committee of the Whole, was irrelevant. His reasons, however, for taking up the time of the House, were given. One of his colleagues, Thomas, upon a former occasion had attempted to bring the Maryland legislature into disrepute, because they had presumed to send to Congress resolutions instructing their Senators, and requesting their Representatives to vote against Expunging. Then, to use his own language, the voice of the legislature was not the voice of Maryland, and the legislature itself was no exponent of the popular will. Mark the assertion, for to this and almost this alone, Jennifer replied. Though weeks had elapsed since Thomas made his attack upon the House of Delegates, of his own State, still the present is the first opportunity which has been offered to reply, and here again Mr. Jennifer charged Mr. Thomas and Speaker Polk with communing together for the purpose of keeping him (Jennifer) from the floor. Mr. Jennifer began his speech by making a few remarks appropriate to the subject, and then commenced a reply to Thomas, condemning him out of his own mouth, for saying that the legislature of any State was no exponent of the popular will.

One day Jack came as before to the place where they used to meet, but did not find his teacher; he searched for him, and finding him busy at marbles, he waited until the game should be over. After a short time, to his great sorrow, the boy called out,—"Sorry boy, I can’t teach you any more, father and mother have both scolded me, because you have dirtied my book with your black hands." Poor Jack had not expected this, but was unwilling to be disappointed, and being very different from some idle children who are glad of any excuse to escape their lessons, he offered to pay two marbles for every lesson, and to wash his hands carefully every day. This was in vain; his teacher was either tired of the task, or afraid of being blamed about his book. All now seemed to be at an end, when Jack recollected that he had seen letters on the tombstones in the churchyard, and as these could not be hurt by his black fingers, he mentioned this plan to the boys, and offered to go on paying anyone who would teach him to read the words on the stones. The boys were struck with his anxiety to learn, and agreed that they would take it by turns to teach him, and immediately began. After continuing this method for some days, one of them offered to take him to a Sunday school.
