The Wilmington Expositor will be published weekly on a Super Royal sheet, at Two Dollars per year in advance; or Three Dollars at the end of the year. No paper will be discontinued, until all arrears are paid, unless at the discretion of the Editor. Advertisements not exceeding one square, will be conspicuously inserted three times for One Dollar, and for every subsequent insertion Twenty Cents. Those not exceeding twelve lines, will be done at the usual rates. All letters and communications to the establishment, if sent by mail, to ensure attention; must be post paid. A young fellow riding down a steep hill, and doubting the foot of it was hoggy, called out to a clown that was ditching, and asked him if it was hard at the bottom. Ay, answered the countryman, it is hard enough at the bottom, I warrant you. But in half a dozen steps the horse sunk up to the saddle skirts, which made the young gallant whip, spur, curse, and swear. Why, you son of a rascal, said he to the ditcher, did you not tell me it was hard at the bottom?

Our wants are the same as yours and we supply them in the same manner. We will take, on account everything that can be eaten by man or beast. We want wood, and from those who have nothing else, we want labor. Shall we have our pay? Bank Reform. The Ohio Statesman, the central organ of the Locofoco party in this state, gives the members of that party to understand that they are expected to oppose the passage of a "general Banking Law." It is evident from the remarks of the Statesman that the cry of "bank reform" raised by the Locofocos, previous to the election did not contemplate the enactment of any law, by the legislature then to be elected, which should throw open the business of banking to a general competition. In fact, the Statesman, since the election, expressly tells us that the "bank reform" measures are to be applied to the institutions now in existence. This application can only be made by: Repealing the charters of the banks, or by a searching investigation into their affairs. The repeal of the charters can hardly be called "bank reform," but rather "bank abolition," neither will the party in power dare to undertake such a measure without supplying the place of such abolished institutions.

Mr. B. had a further view to give of the prosperity of the country, and further evidence to show that all the distress really suffered was factitious and unnatural. It was in the great increase of money in the U. States during the last year and a half. He spoke of money; not paper promises to pay money, but the thing itself,—real gold and silver,—and affirmed that there was a clear gain of from EIGHTEEN to TWENTY MILLIONS OF SPECIE, within the time that he had mentioned. He then took up the custom-house returns to verify this important statement, and to let the People see that the country was never so well off for money as at the very time that it was proclaimed to be in the lowest state of poverty and misery. He first showed the imports & exports of specie and bullion for the year ending the 30th of September, 1833. It was as follows: Year ending September 30, 1833. IMPORTS. EXPORTS. Gold bullion, £648,267 £626,775 Silver, do. 207,840 Gold coin, 563,585 495,800 Silver, do. 6,160,076 1,722,196 $7,079,268 $2,244,861 Mr. B. having read over this statement, remarked upon it, that it presented a clear balance of near five millions of specie in favor of the United States on the first day of October last, without counting at least another million which was brought by passengers, and not put upon the custom-house books.

Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, That in all cases where any Merchant of the United States shall have given an order on a Foreign Manufacturer or Merchant, or his own Agent and Supercargo for foreign Merchandise, previous to the first day of May, one thousand eight hundred and twenty eight, and shall make it appear to the satisfaction of the Secretary of the Treasury, that the said order was given in the regular course of his business, and that it was not in the power of said Merchant to countermand the said order subsequent to the passage of the Act of the nineteenth of May, one thousand eight hundred and twenty eight, entitled “An Act in alteration of the several Acts imposing duties on imports,” and where it shall be further made to appear, in like manner, that the said Merchandise was imported previous to the first day of September, one thousand eight hundred and twenty-eight, the Merchandise so imported shall be exempted from the operation of the Act aforesaid, and be subject only to the duties to which it was liable previous to the passage of the Act. Dear Sir, I wrote you a few days since stating, briefly, the quantity of Life Pills and Phoenix Bitters. I should inform you at this season. When I accepted the agency of this medicine in June last, (although your application was accompanied by documents, attesting the great benefit of this medicine,) I had no expectation that it would equal the description given of it; nor give such general satisfaction as the experiment of a few months has proved. I can only say that all who have used it bear testimony of its great value and I know of no instance where it has been thought in the slightest degree injurious, and, even those who are afflicted beyond hope of cure, bear testimony of its salutary influence upon their general health. The great and increasing demand for this article is the best assurance of its usefulness, and I would not wish to be without it for a single day. Respectfully, yours, J. V. Rice. If the interests of the banks were alone concerned there is nothing in the bill which have passed the House of Representatives too rigid. Banks suspending under such circumstances ought so far as they are concerned to forfeit their charters and be made to wind up unless they promptly comply with the provisions of those charters; but it is hardly worth while for such a State as Pennsylvania utterly to destroy herself; upon an abstraction, "and in punishing the misconduct of certain corporations, punish herself and her citizens past all redemption. Better bear with minor evils and permit minor crimes to go unpunished, than bring upon herself the greatest of all evils dishonor and disgrace. Faith once lost, is lost at least for a century." For ourselves, we do most sincerely rejoice in this triumph of Republican principles. We should be glad to be able to promise a similar result at the election in Pennsylvania, which is to be held next week. We are not sanguine, however, of the success of that election. There are too many questions mixed up with it, which do not properly belong to either party side, to allow of any confidence in the event. At the election in November, when the single question is to be tried between two candidates for the Presidency, we entertain little apprehension of any other than a favorable result, because it is a plain question of preference between two individuals. But we shall not consider the election, now immediately at hand, a fair test of the strength of the opposition in Pennsylvania to the Baltimore nomination.—Nat. Intel. FRANCE.—Paris dates are of the 14th—one day later. The Moniteur congratulates the country on the municipal elections of the West of France, where the Legitimists have universally been set aside. This is no wonder. The legitimists form merely the rustic population; the Liberals reign uncontrolled in the towns. But this is not the case in the South of France, where the Reformist party has succeeded in obtaining the nomination of a great many of its candidates. Our republican institutions know no distinction of persons; peers from one part of Europe, and peasants from another, must alike pay the penalty of their delinquencies, and as we said yesterday we shall show them up with as little compunction as we do the poorest petty larceny rascal that pilfers for a livelihood. As soon as the worthy magistrate had mounted the rostrum this morning, he called out in his usual blunt offhand manner, "John M'Coy—stealing of meat." Pris.—Here, sir. Mag.—Where's the witness against M'Coy? Watch.—Not here, sir. Mag.—You can go, M'Coy. John M'Cabe for beating his wife. Watch.—He's made his escape, sir. Mag.—Then he's let himself go. Pat Casey and Pat Bryan, drunk and fighting in the street.—What did you two Pats break one another’s pates and the peace for. Mag.—There’s no witness—you can go. Marquis of Waterford, Lord John Beresford, Viscount Jocelyn, and the Right Honorable Colonel Dundas! Come up here all of you. What did you get drunk and carry on so for, did you want a bit of a spree too? Watchmen, what were these men doing? It appeared by the testimony of Charles Thompson, a watch, that between three and four in the morning, he was on duty in Washington street near the battery, and heard someone breaking the gas lamp.

EVANS continues to be consulted at his office as usual, in the treatment and cure of secret and delicate diseases. Our friends are more numerous, our resources more ample, and our exertions to render the Mirror the first of periodicals shall be as unflagging as ever. At home we have a list of nearly two hundred contributors, embracing most of the talent and genius of America; and we have established a permanent correspondence both in London and Paris. Four superb engravings will be given in the course of the new volume, from original designs, painted and engraved expressly for the work, by the most eminent artists. A number of curious etchings on wood will also be given. In catering for the general taste, we shall never omit to set aside a due proportion of space for the especial amusement and edification of our fair and gentle countrywomen, in the sunshine of whose favor the Mirror has so long flourished as a favorite journal. In issuing this new volume, we feel confident that while we continue to merit, we shall continue to receive, the liberal support which we take this occasion to acknowledge; and we shall commence a new volume with renewed spirit, and a steady determination to render the Mirror all that its most ardent friends could wish. Conditions: The Mirror is published every Saturday, at No. 1, Barclay Street, next door to Broadway. Proceedings of the Mobile Chamber of Commerce, in relation to Frauds in Cotton. At a meeting of the Mobile Chamber of Commerce, convened at their office on the 6th March, 1831, the following Resolutions were unanimously adopted: Resolved, 'That it shall be the duty of each member of this Chamber to report to the President, every case of fraudulently packed Cotton, which may come to his knowledge; and that the President be requested to publish in the public papers, at the expense of the Society, all the names and marks which may appear on any Cotton: And also, to publish the name of the Planter and ginner, and the place of their residence, when they shall be furnished by respectable and responsible, persons. Resolved, That this Board, in all cases when frauds are discovered in the picking of Cotton, will sustain any member prosecuting for such offence. Mobile, April 4, 1831. The unexpected and glorious victory achieved by menon of Harrison in his cause, has so com- pletely dumbfounded the Loco-focos, that they have vaporized and blustered so much about it, claiming that the entire West was annihilated. It is true that they have not entirely lost that State, and allege that Fairfield, the Loco-foco candidate for Governor, has been elected by a slim majority. It is no go; they have been used up and they may as well submit. But suppose that Fairfield has been elected, which we say is not the case; what then? Last year the Senate stood 8 Whigs to 17 Loco- focos. Now the Whigs have 9 majority. Last year the house stood 63 Whigs to 123 Locos. Now the Whigs have a majority, sufficient to secure the election of a Whig U. S. Senator. The repre- sentation in Congress last year, was 6 Locos and 2 Whigs; now the Whigs have elected 5, the Lo- cos 2, and in one district there is no choice.

From the commencement of the undertaking, I was indebted to the Baltimore, Wilmington, and Philadelphia railroad for the conveyance of the papers, which they, through Mr. Ashmead, the agent of the company, promised to do, and still do not refuse to carry them, but do refuse to leave them in time to make a connection with the evening line to New York—for what cause I cannot tell. The papers are conveyed in a box, the conductor having the key in his possession. On finding that the cars from Baltimore arrived in this city so very late, that it was impossible to connect, I immediately called on the conductor, and requested him to hand me the papers at Gray's Ferry, three miles from the city, the depot where the cars are compelled to stop to detach the locomotive, and attach the horses. He immediately informed me that he should do no such thing, for said he, as long as the editors in New York get their papers regularly, they do not care a damn about the mail. I, at the time supposed that he spoke from authority, and concluded to wait a few days and let time determine whether or not he was acting under the instruction of the company. On the next day the baggage car arrived in time to connect with the New York train, but the New York package was detained.

To this statement a certificate is also appended, from a very respectable gentleman in the same town, which says - "I have full and personal knowledge of all the facts as stated above." Fire in Saco. We learn from the Portland Courier, that a destructive fire occurred at Saco, on Thursday evening. The valuable Machine shop connected with the factories, with a grist mill, a house and barn were totally consumed. The Machine shop contained much valuable machinery and many tools, most of which were destroyed. The loss is estimated at $30,000. It is not known how the fire originated. The cars on the Richmond and Fredericksburg Railroad ran off the track on Friday night last. Several of the cars were smashed to pieces, but no lives were lost. The accident occasioned a delay of the mail. EXCITEMENT IN ALBANY. The correspondent of the New York Express states that the tenants of the Patroon Van Rensselaer have refused to pay rent any longer. "This property is very extensive, comprising almost the entire towns of Knox, Westerly, and Rensselaerville, most of which is held on long lease, a nominal rent having been paid heretofore in produce."

The site of the Town is on a dry sandy soil, pleasant to live on, and the lands immediately adjacent are of the same quality. Water of the best quality is abundantly supplied by many springs, conveniently situated around, and good well water can be obtained at from 20 to 30 feet deep. There is at this place an excellent dry landing to receive goods and ship produce, and also a ferry to cross into the Fork. At a short distance from the Town site, commences that large body of rich Black Land, which extends up Prairie Creek into the Great Cane Brake, which is now drawing universal attention. For this great body of rich land, Areola will become the principal place of deposit and of shipping the produce, as it is the nearest point on the river, and no creeks being in the way. A large warehouse is already erected to receive goods and cotton for shipment. Areola also possesses advantages as a crossing place. The river can here be crossed at almost every stage of water, the banks are high on both sides, and there is no river swamps, or creeks, or backwater in the way on either side. As to health, this situation is not surpassed by any in the country.

English and Classical School. MR. P. NICHOLS, intends opening an ENGLISH and CLASSICAL SCHOOL for Boys, at Corinthian Hall, in State St. on the 1st Monday of November next. The Hall will be re-fitted in two rooms, both airy and convenient. Mr. Nichols has engaged the services of a gentleman, whose reputation as a classical Scholar, and success in teaching, have secured him the liberal patronage of an intelligent public. Mr. Nichols will devote his whole attention to the studies in the English department and general superintendence of the School, and will endeavor by unremitting efforts, to merit so liberal a share of public patronage, as to make his profession both pleasant and permanent.

Bound to Auuintr, Ga, connecting regularly with the railroad cars to Charleston, and the steam packets to New York, Norfolk, Philadelphia, etc. Transports are at the best for this service, and the navigation presents more advantages than can be found upon any other steamboat route in the southern region. The great improvements in the route have been produced by the construction of fifty miles of new road, by the proprietors, viz : from LaGrange on the Lafayette Bayou, on arm of Santa Rosa Bay, to Bryant's Ferry, on the Chattahoochee river, ten miles above the Coward, or 14 above Cedar Bluff, whereby the navigation of the river, and the consequent descents, and more recently the inconvenience crossing at the Coward, are entirely avoided, and a fine road from Marianna direct to Bainbridge, instead of the roundabout road via Chattahoochee, decreasing the distance about forty miles, and increasing the facilities more than once ado.

His object, he said, had been to arrive at a Republic, by a general overthrow of everything. His project, he said, he had first conceived at Barcelona, which place he left for the purpose of taking the life of the King. Several witnesses, who had been on intimate terms with the prisoner, were called. — It appears that the unhappy man has been a soldier in the 15th infantry, a shopman, and, lately, a commercial traveller. All the witnesses agree in giving him an excellent character. He was described as being honest, brave, generous, and modest. On the fourth day the Court adjourned at half-past five. They resumed at a quarter to 11 o’clock on Saturday. He again exhibited the utmost composedness of demeanor. The appeal being concluded, the President observed that all "the witnesses having been heard on the preceding day, it now remained with the Attorney General to present his requi sitory. Mr. Martin Du Mond accordingly arose, and said that he might abstain from adducing further proofs of the guilt of the prisoner, his avowal being sufficient for his conviction. M. Martin then blamed the conduct of M. Colbert in the affair. He regretted that legislation did not allow him to order the arrest and prosecution of that witness for not revealing Alibeau's intention to the magistrates.

In full view of the speedy dissolution of all connection between the President and myself, I availed myself of the occasion afforded by the kindness of my fellow-citizens of Savannah, to do an act of justice to his public conduct, on a question vitally interesting to the people of Georgia. If there be any man who is incapable of understanding, or of appreciating the motive which prompted this act, I cannot envy his feelings, and will not attempt to enlighten his understanding. I returned to this city, had a conversation with the President, of which the prominent points are adverted to in my letter of resignation which immediately followed it, and having brought up the public business, which was in error, retired from office. While these occurrences were in progress, Maj. Eaton addressed to me a letter of like import with his first communication to Mr. Ingham. He called upon me to sanction or disavow the statement in the Telegraph, that my family had refused to associate with his. I answered by detailing the conversation which had passed between myself and Col. Johnson, and stated that I had subsequently expressed the same views to the President, who had disclaimed any disposition to press this requisition, referring to that which I had previously stated to have been made through Col. Johnson. The Editor of the Globe has published this detached sentence of my letter, and has made an impotent attempt to distort its meaning.

of danger, lay down and slept; when suddenly the Camp was aroused by the echo of voices and the tramp of men, who had completely surprised and finding themselves overpowered, fled in every direction. The citizens who were sleeping in their houses were surrounded before they knew of the approach of the Indians. A dreadful scene presented itself to them when they awoke. Mr. Kershaw fell dead in his own house. His widow and little orphan were saved by the presence of mind of Mr. Pearce, the partner of Kershaw; he hid them under a box on the stairs of the house. Several times did the Indians come up and take their seats upon the identical box under which they lay! When the steamboat Helena returned, the Indians, unnoticed by it, left them and ran down to the shore to attack it, while the disconsolate wife, with her little orphan in her arms, escaped to the neighboring wood. The following lines have been suggested by this dreadful scene: "Twas dawn, and the gloom of the night was retiring, When watch-worn and weary the sentinels remained; 'Twas morn, and the dies of their camp lay expiring, Nor dreamed they of danger so shortly confirmed, Through the veil of dim Henry?it came as the lightning! All silent in sleep the few volunteers lay."

The entire revenue of the town would be absorbed in the payment of her subscription, and without the authority which is now sought to be conferred upon the Common Hall, there would be no fund for the current ordinary and unavoidable expenses of the corporation. Her police would go down, her public buildings be neglected, and every act, which the health of the inhabitants and the safety of the town required, would, for the want of the necessary means, go unperformed. He repeated, that the sum of $50,000 was all that was wanted, or that the town would borrow, whatever were its powers. The bill, therefore, in effect, limited the act of the last winter. Mr. Goode proposed to add a rider: "Provided, that no loan shall be contracted by the said Common Council exceeding the sum of $50,000." After some conversation between Messrs. Macfarland, Goode and Brodrax upon the object of the rider, the rider was ordered to be engrossed. On Mr. Macfarland’s suggestion, the rider was amended, with Mr. Goode's concurrence, so as to read as follows: "Provided, That no loan shall be contracted by the said Common Council, in pursuance of the Boimann bill before referred to, exceeding the sum." Mr. Miller of Powhatan opposed the bill. After a reply from Mr. Macfarland, the question was taken on passing the bill with the rider, and carried, Ayes 62 Noes 31.

Newport, N. H. Argus. Insane Wife, and Brutal Husband.  Isabella Conner, a decently dressed woman, was brought in on information in writing, that she was insane, but not furiously mad.  The appearance of the woman, and the facts stated at the examination, created a strong degree of sympathy for her, and of indignation towards an unfeeling husband, who devoid of every attribute that entitles him to respect, and respect, and to all the charities of religion and feelings of manhood. It appeared that Mrs. Conner, has borne a respectable, matronly character, and circumspect woman. Within a year she has buried, in succession, all her children, four in number. Her maternal affections thus repeatedly agonized, proved too strong for fortitude and she became insane.

I understood from a source no less high, that the newspapers are to be as much relied upon as the books of law reports in which the cases are afterwards published; that, in fact, the newspaper report is apt to be the best, being generally the most full, as well as quite accurate. If not the latter, the newspaper giving it would soon fall into disrepute, and give way to more accurate competitors. Hence, he who keeps his daily London paper, has, at the year’s end, a volume of the law reports of the kingdom, besides all other matter; and what variety, what entertainment, what a power of original discussion and anecdote, does every paper contain! In the discussions, editorial as otherwise, there is a remarkable fearlessness. Things that in Junius’s time would have put London in a flame, pass almost daily without notice. Neither the sovereign nor his family are spared. Parliament sets the example, and the newspapers follow. Of this, the debates on the royal marriages in the course of the present month give illustrations. There are countries in which the press is more free, by law, than the English; for although they impose no previous restraints, their definition of libel is so loose that a jury may make one out of almost anything; but perhaps nowhere has the press, in point of fact, so much latitude. Everything goes into the newspapers.

Mr. Cambreleng, remarking that a bill having been reported this morning providing $10,000 for the carrying on and completing of military surveys, withdrew his amendment. Mr Holsey made a general reply to the positions assumed by Mr. Mercer and Mr. Bell. Mr. Pickens contended that the act of 1821 only authorized an appropriation of $10,000 for that one year, and not, as Mr. Mercer insisted, from year to year. Mr. P. was opposed to the continuance of the appropriation on constitutional grounds because though, if confined to military surveys only, they would come within the pale of the Constitution, but it was notorious that these surveys, as they had for years been carried on, formed the basis upon which the internal improvement system was constructed. The amount of $10,000 was but a trifle, yet he looked to the enormous expenditures involved in, and brought about by, these surveys. Mr. Garland of Va. also opposed the amendment of Mr. Mercer and regarded it both as unconstitutional and inexpedient. Mr. G then went into a general argument against the proposition as productive of works of internal improvement and involving immense appropriations often for improvements which, but for these surveys, would never have been thought of.

On motion of Mr. Erskine, the committee on the Judiciary was instructed to inquire into the expediency of providing by law that no person convicted of a crime against the State shall be imprisoned in State Prison for a less term than two years, and also that convicts in the County jails be put to hard labor, the jailers of each County being responsible for their safe custody. Mr. Dumont called up the Report of the Judiciary Committee, relative to the election of Clerks of Courts, County Attorneys and Commissioners, and remarked that he wished to offer an amendment, but did not see exactly how he could get at it. Mr. Littlefield could see no difficulty in effecting the object of the Senator. Mr. Dumont then moved to lay the Report on the table and to take up the bill. The motion to lay on the table was put and lost by a party vote. The question on the acceptance of the Report was taken by yeas and nays as follows: Yeas—Messrs Barker, Bradley, Comstock, Emery, Gross, Reagan, Holden, Littlefield, Osgood, Perkins, Prince, Shaw, Smart, and Williams—14. Nays—Messrs Belcher, Boutelle, Bowles, Dumont, Erskine, Jaques, Norton, Wm. Reed, and Steward—9.

Support your own Mechanics. —We have seen several well written articles, bearing the foregoing title in our Territorial papers. The subject is one of more than ordinary importance, and is more directly connected with our general interests than is commonly supposed. We cordially approve of the principle of supporting our own mechanics, and should be glad to see it strictly adhered to, in every town and village throughout the country. Mechanics form the most important part of a village community, and the prosperity of every town is identified with their success in business. No community can expect to thrive, which does not liberally patronize its own mechanics, but must of necessity retrograde in business and prosperity.

And if the officers have attained to a respectable degree of knowledge, it will not be difficult for them in a period of actual service, to train their men, in a short time, in all the duties of the soldier. South Carolina acts on the assumption of this idea: and all her militia laws look particularly to a body of well trained and disciplined officers. And perhaps no instrument has been more useful in accomplishing this end, than Brigade Encampments. It will be readily admitted that these assemblages are attended with much inconvenience to the officers and sergeants. But liberty can only be maintained at the expense of great sacrifices, and perpetual vigilance. Exemption from inconvenience, would be an inadequate compensation for the ease and activity of despotism, which would follow the introduction of standing armies, in the place of the Militia. Nevertheless, the Legislature should seek to render the performance of this duty as little onerous as possible, consistent with the great object to be effected. Perhaps the system bears with more oppressive weight on the sergeants, who are compelled by law to serve a limited time, no matter what may be their means. To alleviate the burden as much as possible, I would repeat the recommendation of my predecessor that rations should be issued to the officers and sergeants, of Brigade Encampments. I herewith transmit the Report of the Adjutant and Inspector General, for the year 1839.

At length the farmers and laborers from the distant townships began to arrive by companies with music, flags, and banners. One delegation numbered nearly 300 in wagons and Dearborns. In the borough, our active and spirited young men had erected one of the most characteristic "log cabins" I had ever seen, and I have seen many. It was decorated with buffalo robes, raccoon skins, deer horns, ox chains, old sickles, etc., hanging on its corners and around it. But I cannot describe all the particulars—I should fill my sheet before I was half done. There is one incident, however, which my feelings will not permit me to pass over in silence. While Mr. Washington was addressing the assembled thousands, I was tapped on the shoulder by a country friend, who called my attention by pointing to the heavens, and crying out, loud enough to be heard by the whole meeting, "a large Eagle!"

He will attend to all diseases. A letter, it is said, from the pious and philanthropic Arthur Tappan, was found on the person of one of the slaves, inviting them to the deed. "Who does the best he can, does well, acts nobly; Angels can do no more." And, in this humble confidence, the gallant chief exclaims, "I do not fear to die." We know not what are the hopes of that tribe beyond the grave; whether that dis- pensation, by which 'life and immortality are brought to light," has reached them, or whether the Chief "Thinks, admitted to that equal sky, His faithful dog shall bear him company." But the cause of the old man's confidence is worthy of note. And we may well ask.

The late revolt at Paris disclosed the existence of the Society of the Seasons. Seven members formed a week, the chief was called Sunday, four rounds formed a month, and three months a season. Four seasons formed a year and the society was governed by three chiefs who were called the three years. The names of these persons cannot be found out. The case was so palpable a one, and the proposition carried absurdity so plainly upon the face of it, that we have never thought it worth while to bestow much attention upon it. But we find that the defeat of the measure is harped upon as one of the abominations of Gen. Jackson’s administration. John Holmes treated the subject very lucidly in his Portland dinner speech, and his partisans in New-England are raising a hue and cry against those Senators and Representatives who voted against Mr. Clay’s electioneering proposition. We say electioneering, for he could have had no other motive in bringing it forward, especially at the time he introduced it. The public lands have heretofore been taken care of, managed and sold by the United States, the expense paid out of the national Treasury, and the proceeds of the sales, (deducting 5 per cent which goes to the state where the land is located) paid into the U. S. Treasury, where it has been used to discharge the public debt, and to pay the expenses of government, and from which the whole people derive an equal benefit. The sales during the last year amounted to three millions of dollars; but what advantage, we ask, would the individual States derive from such a distribution?

His forehead is low, and gives to his countenance a vulgar aspect. On being asked what use he intended to make of the pistols and poignard found on him, he replied that he carried them for the purpose of defending himself. Fortunately he was so stunned and confused by the explosion of the carbine, and his wound, and surprised by the promptitude with which he was seized, that he had neither time nor self-possession to make use of his arms. To CONTRACTORS FOR GRADUATION, MASONRY & BRICK MAKING—Proposals will be received by the undersigned at the Engineer's offices of the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company, in Baltimore, Martinsburg and Cumberland, up to 6 o'clock, P. M., on Saturday, November 14th, proximo, for the following work—on the line of said road between Harper's Ferry and Cumberland, viz:—GRADUATION AND CULVERT MASONRY of Section 1st—1st Residency, 2nd Division—Situated at the Paw Paw Bend of the Potomac, near the Canal Tunnel. Also, of 3rd Section; 5th Residency, 2nd Division—Situated at the Narrows of Break Neck Mountain, opposite the mouth of Town Creek—both of said sections having been recently abandoned by the company. On the first named a considerable amount of work has been done by the late contractors—on the latter nothing. ARCHING AND FRONT WALLS OF THE TUNNEL AT THE PAW PAW BRIDGE, opposite the Canal Tunnel. This Tunnel is about 300 feet in length, and is now in active progress; about two thirds of the heading being finished. The quantity of brick required for the arching and side walling will be about 400,000, to be burned in the neighborhood of the Tunnel, and for which the clay must be dug the present fall and winter, for molding and burning in the spring of next year. The manufacture of the bricks may be proposed for separately from the other work.

Mr. Slade, of Vermont, offered a resolution, rescinding the resolution yesterday adopted; and declaring that a traffic in slaves is carried on in the District of Columbia, and that Congress ought to prohibit it, as a disgrace to the nation, and a violation of human rights. It was inquired whether the resolution did not come within the prohibition of the rules yesterday adopted. The speaker decided that it did not. Objections were made to its reception, and the House refused to suspend the rules, yeas 55 nays 107. Mr. Calhoun, of Ky. offered a resolution, declaring that the Judiciary Committee report a bill providing punishment for such persons as may assist slaves to abscond from their masters; also to make it unlawful for any inducement or enticement to be held out by persons in non-slaveholding States for the escape of slaves; also to punish those persons in non-slaveholding States, who may harbor slaves; objections being made, Mr. C. moved a suspension of the rules: lost, yeas 49, nays 170. ELECTION OF CHAPLAIN. On motion of Mr. Grennell, the House proceeded to elect a Chaplain, viva voce. On the first vote, the Rev. Mr. Reese had 100 votes and was elected. The House then adjourned. A man named David Hart, a well-digger, was gored to death by a steer in Frederick, Md, on the 16th inst. The man had placed himself at the mouth of an alley, while another person was driving the steer down the street. The steer, seeing the man at the alley, made at him and tossed him a considerable distance, injuring him in the thigh so badly as to cause his death in a few hours. Things in Ohio.—Sixteen hundred and thirty-one names are appended to a call for a Harrison meeting, in Fairfield County, Ohio. A promising prospect for Van Buren! The Steamboat Motto, on her way up the river from Cincinnati, ran afoul of a bar at Bonnehassett's Island on the 9th instant. In attempting to back off she burst one of her boilers, which killed three men instantly and scalded nine others so badly that they died on the same day. Five or six others were wounded, some of it is supposed, and ten or twelve slightly. The execution of Thomas Winteringer, for the murder of Jesse Chrisman, took place in Reading on the 15th instant. Boon Long, his accomplice, was reprieved by the acting Governor on the 23rd of September, at which time he will be executed, should he be alive. At present he is very much indisposed.

For sore eyes, shut the eye and put the plaster directly on the eye and wear it (removing it once in twelve hours and washing off the matter on the eye with castile soap,) till the inflammation is entirely out, say from three to ten days. The proprietor never thought of applying it to sore eyes until about two years since when he applied it to a case in his family, and was astonished at the result. Since then he has applied it in many severe cases and has uniformly effected a cure and leaves the eye natural and free from any inflammatory appearance as though it had not been sore. He fully believes that almost every case of sore eyes may be cured by it in a few days except in cases of intemperance. Certificates. Mr. E. Dean, Sir, I have used your Chemical plaster in six cases of felon, all of which I cured; also several severe cases of inflammatory rheumatism with like good success. Thayer Cummings. Mayville N. Y. And such, it appears to us, will be the final result of all the discontented movements that agitate the latter. The organization of the Republic of Rio Grande may contribute to this end. The following extract of a letter from Gen. Fisher of Houston, takes a view of these movements, which is worthy of attention. "Should they fail in their undertaking in central Mexico, they will retreat from before the Central forces, and penetrate into our country for protection, for which they are anxiously looking, in which case we cannot remain neutral, but must place ourselves upon the defensive, by opposing force against force; and although they may enter our territory harmless, nevertheless, would this inspire sufficient confidence, and should the Central forces, commanded by a chieftain of honor and military and renown, as Gen. Arista is among the Mexicans, not to be intoxicated with the faint hope of not only dispersing the Independents of Rio Grande, but even of reconquering Texas? And supposing the occurrences should take place, would not in all probability the Trojans and Tyrians make a common cause against us? And is it not incumbent upon the citizens of Texas to be on the alert, and ready at a moment's warning to take the field. St. Louis Gazette. Hancock I drain Gilbert Jno Gibson Jno. C. and about 10 o'clock, when crossing one of the highest embankments, two miles this side of Franklin, from some cause not yet accounted for, the engine was run from the rails, and precipitated to the bottom of the embankment. The immense power and weight of the engine exerted instantly, together with the suspension of its progress forward, upset the passenger and baggage cars, and threw them over the embankment on the opposite side with all the passengers. The embankment is twenty or thirty feet in height, and so steep that the cars rolled to the bottom instantly and were dashed to atoms. But one passenger, we fear, out of fifteen or twenty, escaped uninjured. One unskilled infant survived; one died in a short time; and when the mail car of Wednesday hit the scene of disaster, two more were supposed to be dying. Others, though badly bruised and injured, were not thought to be dangerously so. Colonel Robert Singleton died yesterday morning of the wounds received from the explosion of the Ben Franklin's boilers. There are several others whose lives are in a very precarious condition. We are happy to learn that Captain H. A. Slade and R. G. Gordon, Esq. of this city, are out of danger, and will shortly recover from their wounds.

General Government, sufficiently consolidated to meet the wishes of any tyrant—then would the free-born sons of Columbia be “white slaves”. Indeed—then the people might talk about liberty and equality, State rights, and State sovereignties, while the great author of the usurpation, was putting the remnant of their liberties at stake, (if any they had left) upon a single game of his favorite brag. Under the influence of such feelings and fearful forebodings—with such a dominant and well-disciplined party in power, we assembled at the last State Convention. How wonderfully changed is the state of things! and how grateful do we feel for that change! The people have triumphed gloriously in the election of our venerated JACKSON to the Presidency, and the re-establishment of the Government upon principles of industry and economy and of constitutional accountability of rulers to their constituents, have been the consequences. The canker worms which have been found upon the tree of liberty, have been shaken to the ground. Rebels have been driven from the political camp; and ‘“our modern Nebuchadnezzar's turned out to grass.” Our then faith in the wisdom and patriotic fitness of our candidate for the Presidency, has been lost in sight of the just patriotic measures of the President; and our hope that he would stop the march of unconstitutional appropriation, and alleviate the burdens of the people, has ended in fruition.

"If I am not grossly deceived, the signs of the times indicate that General Harrison will not only be a very formidable but a successful competitor of Mr. Van Buren for the Presidency. The fact that he has done so much service in the field, and in the council, and held so many responsible places of trust, without the slightest imputation upon his integrity, will recommend him very strongly to the people, and carry many of the purest and most patriotic of our citizens to his support. Your neighbor, Mr. Ritchie, may flutter and flounder like a duck in a mud puddle. He may talk of his ten thousand majority, and affect the most perfect confidence, but I am perfectly satisfied Virginia is lost to Mr. Van Buren. She cannot, consistently with her principles, sustain the latitudinous and monarchical measures of this administration, and bind herself to the car of Executive power forever. Mark my prediction: Van Buren cannot get the electoral vote of Virginia." HARRISON IN ALABAMA, One of the most respectable political meetings ever held in Tuscaloosa, was held in that place on the 18th January. "Never before," says the Whig, "have we seen the same ardent spirit actuate our political friends."

The New York Herald gives the following timely items:— How to be economical.—Eat meat in small quantities. Touch no butter—it is unnecessary. Burn a single light. Sell your horses. Use as little flour as possible. Take care of the fragments. Advice for the Ladies in hard times. —Wear neat, clean, plain cambrics. Abandon high priced silks. Use neat handkerchiefs, with no expensive embroideries. Advice to Young Men. —Wear your old coat a week longer, and your old hat a month. Old hats feel best—so do old boots. "Oh! Don't you take a newspaper?" "No, never." "Oh! I can't talk with you then. You know nothing." Various Matters. Hon. Mrs. Norton.—The London papers say that a reconciliation has taken place between this lady and her husband. The witty editor of the Louisville Journal, speaking of the difficulties in which Gen. Jackson is said to be involved, says that Congress should buy his manuscripts as they did those of Mr. Madison. A Child swallowing a Cent.—A little girl aged six years at Alstead, N. H. recently swallowed a cent, and great alarm was caused, from fear the copper might prove poisonous. The little girl, however, continued as playful as ever, and complained only occasionally of slight sickness in the stomach. Nothing was done, and on the 4th day, says the Claremont Eagle, the coin came away unchanged.

After being more or less confined to the quarters mentioned, it gradually extended in every direction but to the town last, and, with the exception of the case on the 10th, to the parts of the town bordering on the port the very last. The disease, in its rage, after ravaging in a signal manner the poor, extended to all classes, attacking alike those few who had suffered absolute exclusion, and sparing thousands of those constantly exposed to its fury. A few medical men suffered from, and one student died of the disease; but the greater number had been rendered susceptible from overexertion and consequently exhaustion. The Clergy, to the number of about thirty persons,—unremitting in the zeal of their calling, carrying spiritual succor to all quarters, at all hours, with as much devotedness to the most abject and filthy as to the more wealthy friend,—to a man escaped. Also the persons in charge of the two cholera hospitals, where were carried the worst cases, the expiring, and many dead,—all escaped. The carters, too, constantly employed to carry the affected to the depots, and the numerous dead to the cemeteries,—all escaped. Yet it must not be concealed, that where one person died another case was likely to occur; but this was far from being universal, rather the reverse was the fact.

In French the recitations occupy from an hour and twenty, to an hour and forty minutes, daily, independently of the conversational exercises, incidental to social intercourse, and, to add to the facilities, there will be at regular intervals, French and Musical Soirees, for the benefit of the pupils in those departments respectively. In addition to instructing his own classes, Mr. P. will continue to exercise a supervising care over all the branches of study, examining the scholars, and seeing that justice is done to them. Pupils are charged from the time of entrance, and no deduction will be made for absence, except in cases of long protracted severe indisposition. The academical year commences on the first Monday in September, embraces forty-six weeks, and is divided into four equal terms. Pupils are not received for a period less than a term, and Pupils cannot be withdrawn except at the end of the current full term.

Officers commanding companies will cause this order to be carried into execution and returns made to the Col. as soon thereafter as possible. By order of Col. John Chesnut. J. D. MURRAY, Adjutant 23rd Reg S C M. Extract of the law governing the election for Major. That when the commission of Major shall become vacant, the Colonel, and if there be no Col the officer next in command in the said regiment, shall order each Captain or commander of a company, to call to his assistance two of his subaltern officers, or other fit and proper persons to open and hold a poll at their respective muster grounds, giving forty days notice, by advertising in three public places in the bounds of their command. The said managers shall hold the poll on one day at their muster ground, from eleven o'clock in the morning until three o'clock in the afternoon, and shall meet on the battalion muster ground, or some public house near the same, on the day following, and count over the votes and declare the election. Sir, since Congress have been in session, a mournful group of these unhappy beings, some of Virginia, had been, thirty or forty, were marched as if in derision of members of Congress, in view of your Capitol, chained and manacled together, in open daylight, in the very face of heaven itself, to be shipped at Baltimore for a foreign market. I did not witness this cruel transaction, but speak from what I have heard and believe, in this district, men, women, and children, whose feelings are incommensurate with transactions like this, are at this moment exhibited under the roof of our chamber, do you think the Senators, deliberate, could continue with that composure and calmness, which you have witnessed? Sir, all your powers could not preserve order for a moment.

We shall not proceed any farther in detailing the severities exercised in the other colonies towards the Quakers, but we refer the reader to the History of those colonies, and those times, for a particular account of that extraordinary fanaticism on the part of the first Quakers, and the still more extraordinary remedy resorted to by the Colonial Governments. Rhode Island remained inflexibly firm to her original purpose, the Quakers were allowed in common with all other sects, to promulgate their doctrines and recruit their numbers within her jurisdiction. The Society became large and respectable, especially on the Island, and a meeting house was built not long after this time in the town of Newport for their accommodation.

The following characteristic anecdote of a British sailor will be read with feelings of deep interest. The subject of it was the father of Sir T. Trowbridge, now one of the lords of the Admiralty: "A curious scene occurred on board the Sans Pareille on the morning of the 1st of June (Lord Howe's action), Capt. Trowbridge, who had been recently taken in the Castor, with his convoy bound to Newfoundland, was a prisoner on board the French ship just named, where Rear-Admiral Neuilly had his flag flying. After Lord Howe had obtained his position and had drawn his fleet in a line parallel to that of the enemy, he brought to give the signal to go to breakfast. Trowbridge knew the purpose of the signal, and telling it to the French Admiral, they took the advantage of the time allowed them for the same repast. Trowbridge, whose appetite never forsook him on these occasions, was helping himself.

On the 1st of February, in fulfillment of the Act of Congress of the 3d of March, 1807, I, the subscriber, was appointed by the Commissioners of the City of Washington to examine the buildings in the City of Washington, and assign to each the number of the lot and the square on which they stand. A draught of the City of Washington, with the squares and lots, was prepared for the purpose, and the buildings have been numbered accordingly. The numbers of the squares commence at the southeast and increase to the northwest, and those of the lots increase from south to north. The whole City, as laid out, is divided into squares, each square containing eight lots. A plan of the City and of the public appropriations which have been made thereon, will be deposited in the office of the Secretary of State, for the inspection of those concerned. A copy of the draught is filed in the office of the Commissioners, where the numbers of the squares and lots, with the number of the buildings thereon, may be known.

As the season is approaching, when most farmers begin to think and talk about pulling in their winter wheat, and many inquiries are made respecting the best manner of preparing the soil, and the proper time of sowing, I have taken the liberty to extract part of a letter I found from Hon. S. Lathrop of West Springfield, Mass, in an old volume, and from such a source it will, I think, bear the scrutiny of any of our young farmers. He says 'I have cultivated this species of grain, every year, for the last five and twenty years, and have been as successful in this as in any other branch of husbandry. My crops have averaged be tween 120 and 30 bushels an acre, and have sometimes risen higher; twice during that period, my crop has been sadly blasted. I have found the red bearded wheat the most sure. This has never failed me entirely; when the white wheat from Genessee has proved worthless. My course is briefly this. I have five fields which I stock down to clover and herdsgrass.

Presentment by the Grand Jury, —The Grand Jury, who have had before them for some days the subject of the Naturalization frauds, lately discovered to have been perpetrated in some of our courts, made a presentment yesterday, which implicates the Clerk of the Criminal Session. It is due to the public that the matter should be fully and thoroughly investigated, and the guilty, whoever they are, brought to justice. Unless this is done the purity of the ballot box can be no longer relied on, and our elections will be little better than a solemn mockery. — Phila. U. S. Gaz. Miss Liver more may accomplish nothing to be hereafter his named on the roll of fame; but the unphil love of truth and duty paramount in her own mind will bring to her its "own exceeding great reward." At the date of her letter she had scarcely reached her place of destination, and it is accordingly filled with details gratifying to her private friends, but of hardly sufficient interest for the public eye. It abounds with sentiments of the most ardent piety, and faith in Him who has hitherto protected her in her solitary pilgrimage, and who has promised to "temper the wind to the shorn lamb." It must be borne in mind that she traveled alone, apparently un patronized by any of our missionary board. At Gibraltar she was hospitably entertained by our worthy consul, Mr. Sprague, who seems not unmindful of the apostolical injunction to "entertain strangers."

Although we have done gloriously, everywhere, throughout the whole length and breadth of the State, in redeeming her so completely from the grasp of the spoilsmen, yet we must be prepared to do STILL MORE GLORIOUSLY, in the great battle for HARRISON AND REFORM, which takes place in less than SIX WEEKS! Be up then, and doing—our opponents are rallying for the contest, and will leave no stone unturned—let us rest then, only upon our arms, and once more “trust in Providence—and keep our powder dry! Thus, doing our duty, OUR WHOLE DUTY, as freemen ought always to do, we shall be again successful by a triumphant majority, and our beloved Country will be redeemed from MISRULE and CORRUPTION, and once more be governed in accordance with the principles of Democratic Republicanism, from which, our rulers for the past twelve years, have so wickedly departed. It was remarked, the week previous to our State election, that the Polls on the 15th would show conclusively whether the reports of “Changes” in different sections of the State, were true or false, and we then expressed our willingness to abide the result. We will now only ask our opponents to examine the returns carefully, and answer us, whether there be “NO CHANGES!!” - Old Massachusetts, is arming herself to the teeth for the great struggle, in which she is soon to engage.

She was almost lost in thought when the light footsteps of her mother roused her, and seating herself by her side, she thus began: ‘The events of my childhood and the unwearied assiduity with which my brother anticipated and gratified every wish, are not unknown to you, therefore I will say nothing of them but pass on to my seventeenth year. At this time I loved—yes, deeply, madly loved; and the object was every way worthy the affections of the best of our sex. He was talented and possessed a noble mind, every chord of which beat in unison with my own. And while I could not help triumphing in the number of my conquests, there was but one that ever touched my heart. My brother was proud of my accomplishments, and anticipated my marrying fashionably, and being mistress of a splendid establishment. Albeit Wayland had not wealth, and I think the most unhappy hour my brother ever knew, was the one in which he discovered my passion for him. He set himself to work to eradicate it; he knew at that time that your father loved me, and the first time I saw him angry, was when I expressed my inability to reciprocate the affection. I loved my brother, and his anger so alarmed me that I rashly promised him that I never would marry without his consent.

One dollar per square for the first insertion—fifty cents for each subsequent insertion. SPEECH OF MR. KANE, Of Illinois, In Senate, Thursday, April 24—on the motion of Mr. Poindexter, that the protest of the president of the United States against the resolutions of censure passed by the Senate, be not received; Mr. Kane rose and said: Mr. president, I am amongst those who believe that this session has been already sufficiently extended; and that the time for action upon the necessary business of the country has arrived—my sentiments upon the present subject will be therefore delivered as briefly as possible. The proposition first made, in haste and without consideration, not to receive the communication of the president has in some measure, given way to more sagacious councils; more sagacious because formed upon more reflection. Gentlemen in the opposition have trodden back their steps, and have consented to present their reasons upon record, in the shape of the resolutions now before you.

Or writhing in mid air to feel That suffocating knot.  If foes prevail, not ours to quail Or sue for grace to Spain; Our ensign to the mast we nail And fire the powder train; Nor ours to rest in earth unblest Or rot beneath the turf, Old Ocean takes us to his breast, And wraps us in his surf. And now to trowl one lusty bowl Before we mount the wave, Here’s rest to gallant Egmont’s soul, Health to the living brave! While conquest’s fame gilds Nassau's name,  That "leader of the free, No chain can bind, no threat can tame The Beggar of the Sea! When the Dutch first revolted against the yoke of Spain, the courtiers at Brussels called them in contempt "Des Gueux”—beggars. These insurgents, like the Roundheads of England, and Sansculottes in France, accepted the nickname as a title of honor, the maritime insurgents called themselves “Les Gueux de Mer”—Sea Beggars. On a temporary success of the Spaniards, the Prince of Orange and the Count of Egmont, the patriot leaders, debated what they personally should do. The Prince, who had no faith in Spanish mercy, resolved to emigrate; Egmont resolved to stay. On parting, the Count said “Adieu, Prince sans terre.” Nassau rejoined, "Adieu, Compte sans tête."

Wise Justice.—We learn from numerous quarters, inquiries whether it is the intention of the Whigs to mark their triumph by any celebration in this State. We presume that some festival of the kind will be held, not to triumph over our opponents, but to give expression to the joy which fills the whole country, and to exchange congratulations upon the happy termination of the long and arduous struggle in which we have been engaged. We have no doubt that the mode in which this is carried into effect, will be rational, and worthy of the high occasion which calls for it. We suggest to the Tippecanoe Club the propriety of taking the matter into consideration, as soon as the final result is precisely ascertained.—Providence Journal, Honor to Large Delaware.—Nobly have the Whigs of warm-hearted, gallant little Delaware discharged their duty. They have chosen the Harrison Electoral Ticket by 850 majority. They have carried EVERY COUNTY AND EVERY MEMBER OF BOTH BRANCHES OF THE LEGISLATURE!

To such it will prove of incalculable value, as the means, and in many cases, the only means of relieving their suffering and restoring them once more to health and happiness. This is not offered as a common remedy, that may per chance be equally good with many others now in use, but as one which is capable of saving life in many true cases which all the usual remedies fail. This it has done repeatedly; and this is the reputation it has obtained wherever it has been introduced. It is only about three years since this preparation was presented to the public: but in that short space of time some hundreds of persons might be found, who would solemnly declare that they believed that their lives were saved by it, and in most cases after they had tried many and perhaps all the common remedies in vain. Wherever it is known it is rapidly coming into use, and this affords the most substantial and convincing proof of its merits. The value of the Practical is most conspicuous in those instances of constitutional or scrofulous diseases, which have defied all other remedies, and particularly in those cases where mercury has been so lavishly used as to cause distressing pains in the bones, ulcers, mercurial ulcers, derangement of the digestive organs, etc.

But as from sickness and other causes, there is much time lost, and as many individuals are lazy and add nothing to the common stock of wealth, but on the contrary squander and destroy the products of others’ labor—(among which class Mr. Van Buren and his hundred thousand office holding friends hold a conspicuous station.) we will deduct from the sum of labor 578,000,000 dollars, and cull the interest and return of capital invested 1,000,000,000 dollars only. We shall then have left 1,500,000,000 dollars, as the wages and board of productive labor of every description, which is probably near the truth—to which add the returns of Capital, and we have 2,500,000,000 dollars, as the annual products of our national wealth,—This is about 7,000,000 dollars each day; thus showing that one and a half day’s labor of the people is sufficient to have paid the expenses of the Federal government for one year during the administration of J. Q. Adams, which was about 12,000,000 dollars annually—and also that 24 days work of the people is sufficient to meet the expenses of this government, this present and last year of Mr. Van Buren's administration—

It lay in that situation until the middle of March. We then ran a furrow with a small router, in the middle of the ridge, and a similar one on each side of the middle furrow nine inches from making three router furrows on each flat ridge and dropped the corn in each router furrow, double the quantity we intended to let stand. When brought to a stand, our intention was to leave the stalks three feet apart in each furrow, which would give about eight thousand five hundred stalks to the acre. When the corn was up, thinned, and had obtained the height of six inches, we put a coat of leaves, about three or four inches thick, covering the flat ridge all over and around the corn then plowed and hoed it, covering the leaves with soil. In about twelve days, we repeated a similar process and laid the corn by some time in May, and then covered the ground all over, between the rows, about six inches deep, with leaves, which were put on and covered while wet, in order that the ground might retain its moisture, to prevent the crop from firing. The rows were 80 by 62 yards, making 39 double rows, 80 yards long. We gathered and measured three of the double rows, two on each side, and one in the middle; the three rows produced ten bushels in the ears, which shelled out six bushels and a half peck.

In silence, today, the valet of Monsieur le Compte de St. Ange? 'Because he treated me with insolence,' answered Julian—'he called to me to hold his horse while he alighted; as though I had been his master's groom!' 'Was it not rather because his master was a nobleman?' sternly interrogated Monsieur le Croix. 'You have been insolent to the count, too,' resumed he. 'He threatened to apply his whip to my shoulder,' said Julian, 'and I told him he had better reserve it for his horse.' 'And had he put his threat into execution, what would you have done?' Julian was silent. 'Answer me, sir,' cried his master. Julian folded his arms and still made no reply. 'Am I to be answered?' coolly demanded Monsieur le Croix. 'I see the future traitor in you, Julian,' continued he; 'this insubordination is only mischief in the bud. 'Twill come to more and to worse.' 'May be,' said Julian. 'I command you to answer me,' impatiently exclaimed the former. 'What would you have done, had the count struck you?' 'Struck him again!' indignantly vociferated Julian, 'though my hand had been cut off the very next moment.' 'So the count thought,' said Monsieur le Croix, resuming his coolness. 'I saw it,' said Julian. 'How?' inquired his master.

The Jackson Creed in 1828. We publish from the Congressional Debates of that period the able and conclusive speech of General Alexander Smyth, a leading friend of General Jackson from the State of Virginia, and shall append a note from Gen. Jackson's first message, in concurrence with the views of all his friends at that period. Surely the momentous question has lost none of its importance by the alarming increase of Executive patronage since that period and we put the question to the right Jackson men of that day whether they will now stand by their principles or that portion of their party who have abandoned them! Of course, every reader will decide according to his own feelings of self-respect, and his own conceptions of the ultimate interest of his country. At all events, let all read the speech. So strongly did the necessity of limiting the Presidential tenure to a single term impress itself upon the public mind in that day, that Gen. S. moved an amendment to the Constitution inhibiting the President from re-election! That, requiring two-thirds, and being found impracticable, the people have no other resource but to establish the precedent, and make it binding, by the election of General Harrison specifically for a single term. The Speaker says: "The Constitution should be inviolable; but not unchangeable."

On THIS day came the plaintiff, by Orlando Ficklin, his AL attorney, and it appearing to the satisfaction of the court, that the defendant, Samuel Freeman, is not an inhabitant of this state, and he having failed to enter his appearance and give special bail according to law and the rules of this court: It is therefore ordered by the court, that the said defendant be notified that a writ of foreign attachment has issued out of our said court at the suit of the said William Mitchell against his es tate for the sum of seventy-two dollars and seventy-five cents; and that unless he appear, give special bail, and plead to the said suit in attachment on or before the first day of the next term of the said court, to be holden at the court house in Palestine, on Thursday after the third Monday of September next, judgment will be entered against him by default, and the estate attached will be directed to be sold according to law for the satisfaction thereof, together with the costs of this suit. And it is further ordered that a copy of this order be published in the Vandalia Whig and Illinois Intelligencer, a public newspaper printed at the town of Vandalia, in said state, for four weeks successively, the first insertion whereof to be made within sixty days from the date hereof, and this cause be continued till the next term of this court.

The construction of this work, in which so many of the tales are interested, seems to me to warrant the conclusion, that if the subject was properly presented to congress, no hesitation would be evinced in granting the request of the Legislature. Great excitement prevails in the councils of the nation, as well as in the minds of the people generally on the subject of re-chartering the Bank of the United States. There is doubtless no measure before congress, in which the people of all classes are more interested. The importance of having a sound circulating medium sufficient to supply all our real wants, must be apparent to everyone. No institution has so much contributed to effect this as the present Bank of the United States. Confidence has been given to it wherever its paper has circulated, because of its unfluctuating value. I am clearly of the opinion that a well-regulated National Bank is not only necessary, but indispensable, amongst an agricultural, commercial, and manufacturing people. The present charter of the United States Bank should not be permitted to expire, without first chartering a new one, or altering and amending the present one, obviating the objections which experience has pointed out. There is no doubt that a moneyed aristocracy should be guarded and limited in its powers, so as to disarm it of that baneful tendency towards controlling political power, which it is but too prone to assume.

A resolution was some time since adopted by the Senate calling for a statement of the amount which each State in the Union would, by this time, have received under a bill which passed both Houses of Congress during the Administration of Gen. A melancholy occurrence took place on board the steamer Lady of the Lake, Capt. Nichols, early on the morning of Sunday the 7th inst. Just as the boat was about leaving the Queen's wharf, at Quebec, for Montreal, the boiler burst, and all the persons, eight in number, who occupied the middle cabin, were so sadly scalded as to survive but a few hours. Their names are Wm. Ronaldson and Ellen his wife, their four children, Ellen, Archibald, Jane and Robert; Grace Purvis, their servant, (all from Leith.) and Thomas Moffat, a child. It was proved that the boiler was so much corroded as to be eaten half through—and although no criminal intent could be proved or imagined, yet such was the culpable negligence of the Captain and Engineer, that the coroner's jury levied a deodand of £50 upon the boiler. Mr. Ronaldson was a man in easy circumstances, and has a brother in Philadelphia. The Engineer was intoxicated at the time of the accident. This was the first fatal accident that had occurred upon the St. Lawrence, since the introduction of steam upon it, twenty-three years ago.—N. Y. Com. Destruction of Wall-street Church.—On Saturday afternoon, at about half-past 5 o'clock, a workman engaged in the new building now erecting adjacent to the Wall-street Presbyterian Church, observed smoke issuing from the roof of the latter edifice.

Mr. Editor—At the close of the Legislative Session in 1837, a call of the House was ordered; and members, as their names were called, were requested to state the number of days they had been absent. Charles B. Phelps was a member of that house, but was absent the entire second week of the session, attending professionally to the numerous failures which were taking place in Woodbury. Yet when his name was called, he sat mum, although within whispering distance of the clerk, and suffered his absenteeism to be made out in full! Now, he is big enough to be honest; but if he will not be so in the State Legislature, I know not why we should expect him to be so in the National Councils. Suspicion, Revenge, Suicide.—The steamboat New York, consumed herself while lying at the wharf in New Haven a few days since, because she was suspicious that the Loco Focos were going to charter her for a voyage to Salt River on the last Monday of April. Report says, “she had previously understood such was to be her destiny, and sworn to destroy herself before she would permit such a base and reckless use of her bowels, and revel in her domicile," rushing to such an act.

He was chosen President to see the laws faithfully executed, and to superintend the affairs of the people, and to perform all the duties of his high station with punctuality. The People pay him $8,500 per diem for his services, and he should remain at his post, therefore when the public affairs require it: he might have spared two or three weeks from the tail of his electioneering tour, to sign the papers for the purchasers of the public lands. There would then have been no necessity to call on Congress, as he now does, by a clause in the bill under consideration, to grant a salary or pension of $8,000 to one of his sons to sign his father's name to those patents. Mr. van Buren might have signed all the patents issued last year in one-eighth part of the period he occupied on his electioneering tour, and perhaps in less time than he spent in gallanting the Countess of Westmoreland at Saratoga. But his son would not, in that event, have been quartered at the public crib. At the late session of the legislature the Editor of the Free Press was appointed State Printer, and the Free Press consequently made the State Paper of Michigan. The earliest information of American, political and general news can be found in its columns. During the session of the Legislature competent reporters will be employed in each House, and a correct daily report of the proceedings and Debates published. The laws of the State are to be published in it forthwith upon their passage, and they are made legal evidence in its columns for six months after the close of the session at which they may be passed. The proprietors are also publishers of the laws of the United States, and every subscriber therefore who is careful enough to preserve his files will have by him, not only the proceedings and discussions of the Legislature, but also all laws of the State and the United States complete in either the Daily or Weekly sheet. Each paper will be mailed regularly to country subscribers, on the morning of publication and forwarded by the earliest mails. BAGG, BARNS & CO. Caution, — We would caution our Whig friends, particularly those in the country towns, to examine well the tickets which may be offered to them on Tuesday next —as we are assured that attempts will be made to palm off a ticket upon them, containing the names of one Whig and one Locofoco. If Dore and Thurston are elected, and the election of President and Vice President goes into the House, they will vote for Martin Van Buren. Fire.—About half-past 8 o’clock, last evening, the Providence Screw Manufactory, situated in Randall street, (North End,) was discovered to be on fire; by timely assistance, the roof only was burnt—the Screws and materials were principally removed from the building. The building and machinery were fully insured at the Manufacturer’s Mutual Insurance Office, in this city. Loss, about $1000, [Providence Journal of Tuesday. Errors of Locofocoism.—The Newark Daily Advertiser has the subjoined sensible remarks on the lamentable condition of our country, by the miserable policy and measures of the federal locofocos: A thousand fires have been extinguished, and a peaceful army of 5000 workmen disbanded in the factories of Pittsburg. This is one of the consequences of that fatal blow which first shattered the currency of the country. From that hour to this the nation has been reeling under its effects, but never with so much suffering as at the present moment. And we now present this strange anomaly, that in the midst of general health, propitious seasons and abounding harvests, the wail of distress is heard on all sides of us. Turn which way you will, melancholy evidences meet the eye of individual and public embarrassment. No class, no circle, no institution high or low, near or remote, is exempt, save alone the money changer and the pensioners on the public bounty.

Louis Gazette. Abolition in Indiana.?The joint resolution relative to the abolitionists, and condemning their efforts, passed the Indiana House of Representatives on the 31st ult., by a vote of 87. It is stated that it will likewise pass the Senate by a vote of 4 to 1. Preserved Eggs.?We published some time since a paragraph stating that eggs are kept for three months in France, in lime water. In consequence of this, a lady of East Woods, L. I., has sent us a present of a basket of eggs, of geese, turkeys, and ducks, which have been preserved a year in lime water. As far as we can discover they are as good to the taste and sight as they ever were. A two-gallon pot was filled with eggs and about a pint of slackened lime prepared for white washing was put in, and the vessel filled with water, and a board laid over. The water was never changed, and appears clear and sweet. The same lady has for several years kept eggs a few months in the same manner. "A Defaulter's Dinner.-The New York Transcript says that Mr. Samuel Swartwout has given a splendid dinner in Liverpool, to his American and English friends, the price of which was seven pounds sterling, or about 21 dollars for each man's ticket. This was truly done upon a grand scale, and must startle his plain, unsophisticated friends on this side of the water. If, however, he has saved for himself more than a million of the late public money, he can well afford to give grand dinners. He makes no default in the eating department. Fairfax County, Va.-The special election for a delegate to the General Assembly, has resulted as follows: Triball (Whig) 492, Wallace (V. R.) 428. Mr. Triball's majority would have been much greater had no local causes operated upon the election. The Mayor and the Second Municipality.-The memorial of the Council of the Second Municipality, which we published yesterday, remonstrating against Mr. Hon's bill which passed the Senate, and is in the House of Representatives, speaks the feelings of a large majority of the citizens above Canal street. The bill augments the power of the mayor and invests him with privileges, in the exercise of which, he can greatly annoy, if not frustrate the plans of the council for the advancement of the interests of the Municipality."

The necessity which General Jackson experienced at the defense of New Orleans when he was obliged to refer to the Poll-Lists to enforce the duty of defending the country upon those who had enjoyed every benefit as adopted citizens, and who had sworn fidelity to our Government; and also the desertion of a military corps, entitled the Irish Greens, of New York, during the late war; and the case of Capt. Poussain, an adopted citizen of the United States, but now a responsible officer under a Monarch in his native country; also General Bernard who was received with open arms by the American People, inducted into one of the first offices of the United States, who has returned to his native country and former allegiance, and is now nid-de-camp to the King of France. All these circumstances conspire to prove that devotion to our native country is not susceptible of being supplanted by legislative enactments. Such are the opinions of hundreds of thousands of men to whom we daily confide the protection and securities of our blood-bought institutions!!! [communicated.] Mr. Editor; A few evenings ago I took a ramble with a friend round your capitol. We met one of the musicians belonging to the marine band in this city. In case of the entire extinction of the male line of Napoleon (the children of the daugh ters being excluded), the property—valued, all expenses and legacies being deducted at 15,000,000 of Roman piastres (about three millions sterling)—will become the exclusive property of the capital of Corsica, with the reservation of the legacy of 50,000 piastres for the boys born of the female line of Napo leon. The Cardinal Fesch, brother of Madame Letizia, the Cardinal of Rome, and Torlonis, the banker, have been appointed testamenta ry executors. The body of Madame Letizia is directed to be transported from Rome to Ajaccio, where her sons are to erect a monument, bearing the inscription—“To the Mother of Napo leon.” A legacy of 30,000 piastres is given to the National church of St. Louis of the French, for the perpetual celebration, by the dignita ries of the Church, of a Mass to be sung daily, and a solemn service for the anniversary of the deaths of the Emperor Napoleon and Madame Letizia. The day her mortal remains will be taken from Rome to be conducted to Ajaccio, do nations are to be given to the poor; the 26 Curates of this town are each to have 100 pi astres for this purpose.
