CALHOUN IN 1817—AND IN 1881 Never was there a greater metamorphosis in the opinions of any individual than there has been in those of Mr. Calhoun in the last fourteen years. He now sneers at "the American System," which, as our readers are aware, comprehends Internal Improvements and the Protective Policy—as if he had not himself been one of its earliest and ablest defenders. Unfortunately for him, too, his opinions are on record, and will be made to stare him in the face, until his cheeks shall be crisped with blushes!—if a man so boldly inconsistent can blush at anything.

The majority of the Legislature who have sustained the Administration in its recent desperate courses, are alleged to be preparing an address to the people of the Commonwealth, justifying themselves and it. Is it not incumbent on the majority to follow the example? The resolutions on federal measures were put forth without, as at another great epoch, a Report and argument to sustain them. It is not too late to do this. The supporters of power are active, and are resolved upon a violent struggle at the polls, in April. Will the supporters of the laws, the constitution and its principles, as they believe themselves to be in the great existing controversy, be less active, less solicitous to justify themselves to the People, less resolved to conquer at the polls, by all fair and honorable means?—Rich. Whig. The Fire of Adversity!—It was related of the celebrated phrenologist, Dr. Spurzheim, who died in Boston a few months since, that in selecting a lady for his wife he made choice of one who had seen much trouble and had passed through uncommon scenes of calamity. His theory was that great mental suffering was necessary in the formation of the human character, to develop the highest and purest qualities of the soul. We need not say how well this corresponds with that sacred declaration—“Every son whom he loveth, therefore, he chasteneth.” From the New York Gazette.

Any man of even ordinary understanding using such language in a public manner, and at the same time pursuing such a course of consummate partiality, and proscription, as has this same "enlightened" Gov. Fairfield, must have most curious and exalted notions of the constitution of the human mind. We suppose Gov. Fairfield’s liberality amounts to about the same, with that of a worthy Frenchman, which he read tell of. The old gentleman, it appears, got possession of two apples—one very large and of excellent flavor, and the other rather an ordinary affair, whereupon with wonderful generosity, he says to his better half—3v. Snjakz your choice—assist me with this one—reserving to himself the finest one! Such, we apprehend, is the "freedom of thought and opinion" so much cried by Gov. Fairfield and his party. What hypocrisy was inditing and sending forth these excellent sentiments, so ostentatiously fine upon paper, that can be no question, if we judge by subsequent practice, that he was determined in his heart to remove from office every man, whose "thoughts and opinions" did not precisely correspond with his own! Fatal Accident: The Christian Guar- dian of August 6th, gives a distressing account of the falling of a gallery in the city of Toronto, during a late public meeting held in the market house of that place, by which nearly 100 persons were precipitated from the height of twenty feet, of whom two were instantly killed, three died a few hours after, and forty-five were severely injured, by having their legs or ribs dreadfully lacerated by the hooks of the butchers' stalls. So deep is the impression and so universal the admiration produced by C. Middleton's speech, that no one seems anxious to follow him immediately in reply, and by general consent the question is permitted to lie over for a future day. When Sheridan made his ever to be remembered speech against Warren Hastings, and a motion was made that the House should adjourn and not vote at a moment when they were transported by passion and eloquence we see a tot instance of that resistless sway which the orator holds over the feelings and judgment of men.

It will be recollected, that Hummerston was reported to have made no choice of representative to the Legislature, at the late election. After hallowing, as was supposed unsuccessfully, till near midnight the meeting was adjourned. On examining afterwards the poll book however, it was found that a mistake intentional or unintentional, We will not say which, had been made, and Clarke Rice, a sound "Whig was ascertained to have been elected. The Constable, who is the presiding officer at such meetings, we understand is a Loco Foco. The member elect has been furnished with his credentials, and has doubtless taken his seat. The Governor's Message to the Legislature, will be found in another column. It is a brief, plain, well-written business paper, and highly creditable to our Farmer Governor. It will be seen that his Excellency records his disapprobation of the course of the national administration in allowing their whole powers to be absorbed, during the late money pressure in the country, in exerting all their efforts to carry their favorite Sub-Treasury scheme and other party measures, in defiance of the public will, and in opposition to the dictates of justice and sound policy. UPPER CANADA. The jails in the Upper Provinces are all but emptied of their political prisoners. Six more were liberated from Hamilton jail, toward the end of September, giving security to keep the peace all under sentence of death and there are now but three left, namely, Peter Malcolm, Horatio Hills, and Walrath.

The following are the most remarkable bequests which have transpired: A legacy of 50,000 piastres to the mother of the Duke of Reichstadt. 150,000 piastres to each of her sons and daughters. A very considerable legacy for a pious and magnificent establishment at Rome, under the direction of the testamentary executors. The Mont de Piete, of Rome, to be the receptacle at the time of Madame Letitia's death, of her diamonds and other valuable effects, which are valued at least at ten millions of Roman piastres. A legacy in money or jewels, to the amount of 100 seipiins, at the choice of each of the testamentary executors. All the remainder of her property, in furniture, income, plate, diamonds, jewelry, pictures, and precious effects of all kinds, is willed to the eldest, or eldest commit, of the possessor or tenant, who must be the direct male descendant from Napoleon. If there should be two sons of the same age in succession, coming from father to son, both will become tenants or possessors of the majority, which is to remain in the family of Napoleon until the extinction of the descendants of the different brothers.

The humble gig of Mr. Camp had as good right to occupy the space on the broad avenue as had Mr. Van Buren's gilded coach. It was well however, that Mr. Camp escaped with mere abuses & that he had not been forthwith imprisoned for refusing to submit with Christian meekness, thus to be rode over and rode down, by this great democrat's British state coach. And here let me step aside for a moment to show you what are the real principles, the genuine feelings of men who claim to be the patent exclusive democrats of this country. About two years ago, the editor of the Globe, speaking of the officers of the Navy, said that 'It is impossible to make heroes of men who adopt the maxims and principles of cobblers and tinkers.' Here the truth slipped out, these men showed to the world with what sort of feelings they view the laboring mechanics of this country; but that article had scarcely got abroad, when the author found he had missed a figure, and in an unguarded moment had too freely exposed his real sentiments; so he tried to get out of the scrape by philosophizing a little, and after reflecting for about a fortnight, he came out with an explanation in which he said, that by cobblers and tinkers he did not mean the cobblers and tinkers of this country; oh no! not at all!

But the bill, according to the opinion of the Attorney General, was sufficiently clear and explicit. Gen. Jackson has, therefore, in the most arbitrary and despotic manner, substituted his own will for that of the Nation — for that of the people, expressed through their representatives in Congress. It has been ever thus, through a series of eight long, long years. The will of Gen. Jackson, through the whole of that period, has, by fair or foul means, constitutional or unconstitutional, displaced the popular will, and swayed the destinies of this nation with a fierce despotism which knew no dependence and acknowledged no restraint. But all this tyranny has been disguised by pretended reverence for the popular sovereignty, by protestations of love for the people. By such pretenses and such protestations have the people been made the instruments of their own debasement and enslaving. Thus has Gen. Jackson, assisted by an army of bribed dependents, lorded it over the dear people, whose voice was all the time appealed to as the voice of God. — Louisville Journal. He is "gone.—General Jackson has left Washington and has actually set out for home, although many believed he would never turn his back again on Mecca. Let us soliloquize. His friends had a happy knack of apologizing for all his measures. If he violated the laws, they said "he is very honest."

The married pair must know each other to the bottom of their souls; the mysterious veil which covered the two spouses in the primitive church must be raised in its utmost folds, however closely soever it may be kept drawn to the world. What! on account of a fit of caprice, or a burst of passion, am I to be exposed to the fear of losing my wife and my children, and to renounce the hope of passing my declining days with them? Let no one imagine that fear will make me become a better husband. No: we do not attach ourselves to a possession of which we are not secure; we do not love a property which we are in danger of losing. We must not give to Hymen the wing of love, nor make a sacred reality a fleeting phantom. One thing alone is sufficient to destroy your happiness in such transient unions; you will constantly compare one to the other; the wife you have lost to the one you have gained, and do not deceive yourself, the human heart will always incline to the past for so God has constructed the human heart. This distraction of a sentiment which should be indivisible, will erase its unity, and link today who made him the present, and the future. A Stretcher.

Too soon has the ill-fated example of St. Louis, without any of its mitigating circumstances, been followed, in the murder of a slave in Arkansas. The only account we have of the horrible transaction, is contained in the annexed extract from the Arkansas Gazette, of the 29th ult. "Oh Horror's head, let horrors accumulate." We have been informed that the slave William, who murdered his master some weeks since, (Huskey,) and several negroes, was taken by a party, a few days since, from the Sheriff of Hot Spring, and burned alive! Yes, tied up to the limb of a tree, a fire built under him, and consumed in slow and lingering torture! We have mentioned this disgraceful and barbarous outrage, that the ministers of the law may take steps to bring those implicated in the guilt of so black a crime, to punishment. The circumstances of this criminal outrage are aggravated by the fact, that the evidence against the negro was of such a character, that there was no chance of his escape from a just expiation of his crime by law—his condemnation was next to certain.—Rep. calamitous era.

He was still willing, however, to "go it again," and soon had everything fixed for a last trial. "How much do you bet this time?" said he to the Kentuckian. "I'll go the entire pile and quit," returned Kentuck. "What! the whole of it!" said the greedy knight of the thimbles, his eyes sparkling with delight in anticipation of so much booty. 'Every cent in the pile,' said the Kentuckian coolly. 'I go the whole pile or nothing.' 'Well I haven't that much money about me,' retorted the gambler, 'but there's my watch that's worth so much,' at the same time putting it in among the money which was now spread open in the handkerchief. 'There's a diamond breastpin that's worth so much,' putting that in also. In this way, and by staking what money he had, best on hand, an amount which the Kentuckian considered a fair stake. The gambler now commenced arranging his tools with greater care than usual, while the Kentuckian set to work at the different corners of the handkerchief, lifting them up-apparently to get all the money and valuables in the center. He was evidently bent upon playing a stronger game than the gambler, but one which the latter never saw into until it was too late. One by one the gambler placed his thimbles, and one by one the Kentuckian gathered each of the four corners.

He fought like a lion, and killed with his single hand, two of the three who were sent to dispatch him, while the rest of the gang were engaged in murdering his wife and children! The foundation was set up immediately after the cellar was dug. He, Mr. Suydam, advanced him money as he got along with the house. Thinks $300 about the largest amount he ever received at any one time in cash. He had the money laying by him 19 months before he paid it to Mr. Suydam. Mr. S. made the proposition to sell him the lot and he thought he would hold on to what he had and get all he could, in case sickness or something should happen to him. He calculates, counting his own work, that the house and lot was worth $2000. He had expended all that Mr. S. had furnished him with in addition to his own labor. The reason that the bond and mortgage was not canceled was because people were talking about him and saying in less than a year Mr. S. would take the house and lot away from him, so he thought he would pay it off and say nothing about it. The house is not done - put down the cellar floor about a week ago. He never had shown the bond and mortgage to any person - not even his wife - has not shown it to any person within a few days, not even his brother has seen it. From the same. It appears to be certain that Mr. Hawes has been re-elected to Congress, and that his success is a triumph of principle, honorable alike to himself and the voters of his District. He was opposed by Philip Thompson, Esq., a popular and talented man, who, though of the National Republican party, declared himself in opposition to the United States Bank, and Mr. Clay’s Land Bill. Mr. Hawes, on the contrary, although heretofore a Jacksonian, declared that he considered the Land Bill a wise and judicious measure, and the rechartering of the United States Bank essential to the safety of the public funds, and the preservation of a sound currency. On these grounds, the voters selected their representative, regardless of former party divisions, and the result shows their greater devotion to principles and measures than to party names and party men. We rejoice that Mr. Thompson has been defeated if we have been correctly informed as to his time serving course.

Tennessee.—The Legislature of Tennessee on the 26th ult. adopted a resolution instructing their Senators and requesting their representatives in Congress to oppose the passage of all laws making further appropriations for the support of the Military Academy at West Point. On the 30th, the resolution offered early in the season, against the recharter of the Bank of the United States was finally adopted. The Bank recently chartered at Memphis, is called the Farmers and Merchants Bank of Memphis. The capital stock is not to exceed $600,000. A branch is to be established at Paris. The exhibition on this occasion was one of uncommon interest, and went off with great eclat; and, indeed, it is not often we are permitted to enjoy such a treat. The number of Mr. G.'s scholars amounts to about forty; and the number of performers amounted to something like ten or fifteen—their ages varying from twelve to sixteen. The Church—I say Church, for it was in one of those good and quiet places of worship, I mean the Quaker meeting-house, where the exhibition was held, and, I believe, occupied, in part, as Mr. G.'s school-home—was filled entire, as many of the fairer part of creation were compelled to stand until the close of the exercises. The scene was truly enchanting—fathers and mothers, brothers and sisters, together with many disinterested spectators, were scarcely able to conceal those heartfelt emotions of overwhelming joy, in beholding these amateur performers in the different branches, such as French, Latin, and English. This worthy gentleman has been located but a few months in that section of the City, and his teaching has been attended with more than common success.

The LITERARY WORLD, APRIL 11, 1888. Credit Failure.—Our readers will perceive by the annexed article from the N. Y. Courier of Monday morning that a great failure, accompanied by forgery, fraud and flight, has taken place at Buffalo. The news created no slight sensation even among our peaceful and usually unexcitable population, from the circum stance probably that several of our former fellow citi zens are located in Buffalo, and have been pretty ex tensively engaged in the speculations at that place. — In addition to what is given below, we learn through other sources that the amount of the failure is from one to two millions, and that a large amount of forged paper is supposed to be afloat in New-York city as well as in many places at the west. This affair will undoubtedly seriously affect the prosperity of Buffalo for some time to come. From the N. Y. Courier, August 8. We are sorry to say that intelligence was received here on Saturday, that Mr. Benjamin Rathbun, of Buf falo, and his brother, Mr. Lyman Rathbun had ab sconded from that place in consequence of its having been discovered that the former had caused to be dis counted a very large amount of his notes of hand, with forged endorsements. Mr. Rathbun had so completely identified himself with the prosperity of Buffalo, and indeed, with all the western part of the State, that this event may with justice be considered one of public in terest.

The allegiance to, and dissolve all connection with the Masonic Institution; and I am impelled to take this course from a firm belief that Masonic principles not only justify, but require the infliction of Masonic penalties for a breach of Masonic oaths. While I resided in Belfast, the abduction and murder of Morgan was frequently a subject of conversation in our Lodge meetings, and I am sorry to be under the necessity of saying that dark transaction was justified by many of the members, among them some of our most respected citizens. I do not state these facts to injure the feelings of any individual or body of men but merely to show the demoralizing influence Masonic oaths have over the minds of the best of men; for if Masonic oaths are considered binding, the more honest and upright the man, the more scrupulous he will perhaps feel about breaking them. I have no doubt that if any Mason had been detected in writing an exposition of the secrets of Masonry in this section of the country, previous to 1826, he would have shared a similar fate to Morgan. These are my sober convictions of Speculative Masonry, and in giving them to the public, I have only discharged a duty from which a year's past experience has taught me there was no reprieve.

The gay the versatile, the Philosophic Frenchman, accommodating himself cheerfully to all the vicissitudes of life, incorporates himself, without difficulty, in our society. But, of all foreigners, none amalgamate themselves so quickly with our people as the natives of the Emerald Isle. In some of the visions which have passed through my imagination, I have supposed that Ireland was originally part and parcel of this continent, and that by some extraordinary convulsion of nature, it was torn from America, and, uniting across the ocean, was placed in the unfortunate vicinity of Great Britain. The same openheartedness; the same generous hospitality; the same careless and un calculating indifference about human life, characterise the inhabitants of both countries. Kentucky has been sometimes called the Ireland of America. And I have no doubt that if the current of the emigration were reversed, and set from America upon the shores of Europe, instead of bearing from Europe to America, every Amer ican emigrant would there find, as every Irish emigrant here finds, a hearty welcome and a happy home! But, Sir, the gentleman to whom I am about to allude, although long a resident of this country, has no feelings, no attachments, no sympathies, no principles, in common with our people. Near fifty years ago, Pennsylvania took him to her bosom, and warmed, and cherished, and honored him; and how does he manifest his gratitude?

But, continued Mr. B., the game is up; the alarm is over; the People are tired of it; the agitators have ceased to work the engine of alarm. A month ago he had said it was "the last peatime," with these distress memorials; he would now use a bolder figure, and say that the Secretary’s report just read had expelled forever the ghost of alarm from the chamber of the Senate. All ghosts, said Mr. B., are afraid of the light. The crowing of the cock,—the break of day, —remits them all, the whole shadowy tribe, to their dark and dreary abodes. — How then can this poor ghost of alarm, which has done such hard service for six months past, how can it stand the full light, the broad glare, the clear sun shine of the Secretary’s report? "Alas, poor ghost!" The shade of the "noble Dane" never quit the stage under a more inexorable law than the one which now drives the away. This report, replete with plain facts—and luminous truths, puts to flight the apparition of distress, breaks down the whole machinery of alarm, and proves that the American People are, at this day, the most prosperous People on which the beneficent sun of Heaven did ever shine! Mr. B. congratulated himself that the spectre of distress could never be made to cross the Mississippi.

Yet, so important is the great and general question, which for the last twelve or fifteen months, has been presented to the consideration of the Legislature, that I deem it proper on this, & on all occasions, to state at the risk of some repetition, perhaps, what is the nature of that important question, and briefly to advert to some of the circumstances in which it had its origin. Whatever subordinate question may have been raised touching a sub-Treasury or a Constitutional Treasury, or a Treasury in one, of in another, or in yet a third form, I take the question, the plain, the practical question to be this, viz; whether it be among the powers and the duties of Congress to make any further care of the national currency than to regulate the coinage of gold and silver. That is the question. From that, all subordinate questions, however they may have been multiplied or varied, have arisen. Now, indeed, that should have become a question at this day, might be a wonder for the wise: for it is a truth capable of proof from all the records of this Government for fifty years - from the very first day that the constitution drew breath as a practical standard for the administration of Government - it has uniformly been received and admitted as one of the duties of the Government to take care of the national currency.

It appears that the plot originated with some of the officers of the fort St. Sebastian. JAMES DRYSDALE, Veterinary Surgeon, ml9 1m* No. 18 Fifth St., near Bowery THE HUMAN HAIR.  Personal appearance is influenced by nothing more materially than a luxuriant crop of hair. The loss of the hair in most cases is occasioned solely through neglect of those means which really do most to preserve it. To prevent its de cay, and accomplish its complete restoration, is the object to which Mr. Clirehugh has for 13 years directed his attention.  The growth and organization of the hair, its structure, as con nected with the anatomy of the skin has been his peculiar study. To attain full information on this subject, he attended the lectures of the most celebrated physiologists in London and Edinburgh, dissected the different layers of the skin, exa mined the nerves, blood vessels and baths connected with both skin and hair, and by these means discovered the causes which occasion baldness, and produce gray hair.

The packet-ship Siddons had arrived at Liverpool on the morning of the 14th ultimo, bringing dates from the United States to the 26th December, which had generally been regarded as highly satisfactory. The Cunarder Caledonia has on board the letters saved from the packet-ship Pennsylvania, and those by the St. Andrew have been forwarded by the Kosciusko, which has also arrived at New York. The cargoes of the St. Andrew and the Pennsylvania, it was ascertained, would both be saved almost entirely, though thoroughly soaked. As to the ships, the opinions are quite various. Some of the accounts say they will be floated with little damage and others that they are entirely ruined. The ravages of the hurricane appear to have extended all over the island, and to have been felt in Ireland and the Isle of Man. The loss of lives both in these last mentioned islands and in Scotland appears to have been very great. Ireland, from Londonderry to Cork, and from Dublin to Galway, was swept by the tempest in an awful manner, leaving the country a scene of desolation. At Galway five persons were killed. At Athlone from 40 to 50 houses were blown down. Orchards, groves, avenues of trees in every direction were laid prostrate. Two thousand trees were blown down on one estate; on another, the estate of Lord Charleville, near Tullamore, upwards of 1,000 pounds worth of timber was destroyed.

HOFFMAN'S HOTEL BROADWAY, NEAR MAIDEN LANE, NEW YORK. This HOTEL is one most commodious in the Union, having a front upon Broadway of over 200 feet, upon Maiden Lane 50 feet, with two large entrances, each 25 feet. It is six stories high, exclusive of basement and under cellars, and con tains over 200 bed chambers, each well lighted and ventilated with grates—besides numerous private par lors with sleeping rooms attached, and public parlors, dining, reading and other rooms. There are two elegant Dining saloons, the floors of which as well as the spa cious halls in the first and second stories, together with the reading and other public rooms, are paved with Ital ian marble. This increase consisted almost entirely in the article of cotton; the increased production of which, is mainly owing to the transfer of a large amount of slave labor from the less productive lands of Maryland, Virginia, the Carolinas and Georgia, to the more fertile region of Alabama and Mississippi. This sudden transfer was principally induced by the high price of cotton in 1835 and 1836; and it has, to a great extent, ceased with the cause that produced it. This source of increased production, from its very nature, has its limit, and it is believed, that it has nearly attained that limit. It should be remembered, too, that the increase of the value of our exports in 1835 and 1836, was not owing entirely to increased production, but partly to the high price of cotton in those years. Hence we see the export fell 11 millions in 1837, and 5 millions more in 1838. I do not think that the average increase for the next three years will be as great as it has been for the last three, but, even allow that it will, and the increase in the duty to be derived from the customs will not keep pace with the reduction, under the act of the 3rd March, 1833. Considerably more than half of our imports are duty free, and the disproportion is annually increasing.

Stillwell, at that time, was worth exactly $1,342 1-2, and intended to make it $3,000,000 in a year. Why should not Barnabas Bates? One day, about this period, a gentleman met with Mr. Taylor, the partner of Taylor & White, who were the stockbrokers of Bob White. This was the Post-office, and in the presence of Barney. Now Mr. T. said to the gentleman, "Do you want to make a little money?" "Yes, I do." "Then buy Kentucky bank stock—it is par, and will run to 100 in a week." "Do you think so?" "Certainly," with a nod and wink. The gentleman did buy. Barney all this time was looking on and licking his lips. "Why," says Bates to Mr. Taylor, "if money is to be so easily made, I should like to make a little too." "Well, I'll buy some for you." "Do," said Barney—"say 100 shares." Accordingly Mr. Taylor bought 100 shares, at par, of Kentucky stock, and had them transferred as collateral security on his personal obligation, and all given to the Manhattan Bank for the advance of the $10,000. Instead, however, of the Kentucky stock rising to 100, it fell to 85, and in a short time he had to advance $200 and over to make up the deficiency.

AN AFRICAN'S REVENGE. The following thrilling tale is translated from a passage in Eugene Sue's French novel of Atar Gull. The scene is laid in Guadaloupe. It is merely necessary to premise that Atar Gull is a favorite slave, whom Colonel Willis brought from Africa several years before the event described is supposed to have taken place. Atar Gull always appeared faithful to his master, and grateful for his kindness to him, but in secret he brooded over the loss of his liberty, and resolved to be deeply revenged. Smiles shone in his countenance but deadly hatred rankled in his heart.  When Atar Gull had nearly reached the summit of the mountain, the sun had already risen, and the lofty heights of La Soufriere threw their shades to a great distance across the valleys below. Upon the authority of Mr. Jefferson, contained in the fourth volume of his works, that in 1793, when the country was convulsed upon the subject of Mr. Genet’s conduct, who was the minister of France near the government of the United States, when party violence ran high, and when his cabinet were decided as to the proper course to be pursued with regard to him, and other questions of a political nature, in a discussion which took place in his cabinet, Washington “manifestly inclined to the appeal to the people.” His own character and motives had been impeached; and during the same discussion, the father of his country complained of “the personal abuse which had been bestowed upon him,” and he “defied any man on earth to produce one single act of his, since he had been in the government, which was not done on the purest motives: that he had never repented but once, the having slipped the moment of resigning his office, and that was every moment since,” that “he had rather be in his grave than in his present situation; that he had rather be on his farm than be made emperor of the world,” “and yet, that they were charging him with wanting to be a king.” What a striking parallel is here observed between the personal abuse heaped upon President Washington and Gen. Jackson.

Manufactory. The chain of circumstances flow inevitable out of the laws of commerce. If we turn to this country we find that a large portion of its agricultural land that in the past two years the production has been greater than the consumption. This has lead to the accumulation of surplus, for which there is no market. The immediate result of this state of things will be to force them to invest in unproductive business. We are indebted largely to England. We have great quantities of food (for which she is willing on good security to lend) us the money to buy her manufactures but not to take our produce in pay. This state of things cannot long exist. The time is not far distant when a system of perfect free trade must be established between this country and the nations of Europe, as much as now exists between one State and another. The absurdity of "protecting" any particular interest by taxing the whole community in the shape of import duties, will soon be done away with.

True, oil his land is a garden; but this is only what? or nearly what? every farmer should make of all which he cultivates. What is the use of skimming over ten acres to obtain what might be secured from a single acre? Must he who does that be called a farmer, whilst he who does this should be set down as wanting in a practical knowledge of the principles of agricultural economy? The Editor actually cultivates but a single acre of land, but that he does cultivate, and makes it yield all that land can yield. Nor, small as the quantity is, is the amount of substance obtained from it unimportant in the support of a large family. One-third of an acre he devotes annually to corn? the long eared, cleveled, eight rowed yellow corn, that is not very early, and not very late. With him, it has ripened every year for the last ten years that he has cultivated it. The soil he makes rich. He applies to it, before ploughing, at the rate of eighteen or twenty cords of long manure to the acre, (or six to the third of an acre) and turns it under by the plough. He plants the hills three feet and a half apart one way and three feet the other? exactly by measuring with a line. In each hill he deposits either a shovel full of old rotted hog manure, or as much night manure as will not over stimulate the crop.

In a week after the funeral of Mr. Willis, Mrs. Egerton was informed by her husband, that letters he had received from Philadelphia, demanded his immediate departure for that city, where he would only be compelled to remain two days, and then return to his sorrow-stricken wife. Accordingly he departed in the noon steamboat, followed by the blessings of his young bride, whom he had led to believe he would shortly carry to England, and introduce into all the fashionable circles of the metropolis. Five days after his departure, as Mary sat mournfully musing on the cause of her husband's protracted stay, she received a letter from him, in which he confessed himself an impostor, having only been employed as a travelling companion to the Hon. George Egerton, from whom, during a spell of sickness under which he labored for some time in Canada, the husband of Mary freely acknowledged he had purloined his letters of introduction, together with some funds, and that the real Simon Pure was then in search of him; and to strike deeper the arrows of affliction to the heart of poor Mary, the villain had the audacity coolly to add, that he had mortgaged all Mr. Willis’s property to its full amount—had in consideration of value received, assigned all his personal property to a friend, and bidding her keep her spirits up, concluded by an eternal farewell.

Van Buren imported his British state coach, and splendid British carriage harness, about 6th of July, 1837, nothing but the breaking out of the cholera in New York prevented such a demonstration on the part of the coach builders and harness makers, in that city, as would have made it almost hazardous for this plain, hard-handed democrat, to introduce another British state coach at the port of New York. As Mr. Van Buren was traveling in his plainest style, on his Virginia tour, I presume, therefore, he did not use his British state coach on that occasion. Well, sir, let us next take Mr. Van Buren to church, where all the partakers of this frail and mortal state should appear in a state of equality. Let us see in what sort of style this worshipper goes to the house of God, and how he returns to his own house. Now be it remembered that he usually attends service at St. John's church, on the north side of Lafayette square, and directly facing the main entrance to the palace: from the palace to the church there is a very fine paved footway, the distance being about 300 yards. In order to show the plain manner in which this humble, hard-handed democrat, goes to and returns from church, (300 yards,) I will read the following description, written by a gentleman who was at that church on one occasion during the present session of Congress.

Black Hawk.-Yesterday, this Western warrior and suite arrived in town and took lodgings at Congress Hall. They appeared gratified with their journey, and were friendly and affable to those around them. When the procession passed along Third-street, opposite Congress Hall, the Indians were stationed at the windows to observe and admire this display of American strength. Black Hawk and his friends leaned upon the window, and looked down upon the dense mass below with interest, but not with amazement. His eye ran along the almost interminable line of military, as if measuring its strength and numbers, and rested with obvious delight upon their splendid equipments. The crowd, which had increased to a tremendous extent, gazed upon the singular array at the windows with silent curiosity; and at length, Black Hawk, observing this, signified his desire to make a speech. “Black Hawk,” said the old warrior, “once thought he could conquer the whites. His heart grew bitter, and his hand strong.

Pure religion, and undefiled before God and the Father, is this: to visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction, and to keep one’s self unspotted from the world.’’ Gentlemen: Whoever takes a view of the world, will find that what the greatest part of mankind have agreed to call religion, has been only some outward exercises, esteemed sufficient to work a reconciliation with God. It has moved them to build temples, slay victims, offer up sacrifices, to fast and feast, to petition and thank, to laugh and cry, to sing and sigh by turns; but has not yet been found sufficient to induce them to break off an amour, to make restitution of ill- gotten wealth, or bring the passions and appetites to a reasonable subjection. Differ as much as they may in opinion concerning what they ought to believe, or after what manner they are to serve God, as they call it, yet they all agree in gratifying their propensities.—The same passion reigns eternally in all countries and in all ages.

We can boast of no civil code. We can promise no protection but the ulterior resort of self-defense. By whom, then, shall our country be populated! By the reckless and unprincipled adventurer! Not by the hardy and enterprising pioneer of the West. By the Botany Bay refugee, the renegade of civilization from the Rocky Mountains, by the profligate, deserted seaman from Polynesia, and the unprincipled sharper from Spanish America. Well are we assured that it will cost the Government of the United States more to reduce elements so discordant to social order, than to promote our permanent peace and prosperity by a timely action of Congress. Nor can we suppose that so vicious a population could be relied on in case of a rupture between the United States and any other power. Our intercourse with the natives among us, guided much by the same influence which has promoted harmony among ourselves, has been generally pacific. But the same causes which will interrupt harmony among ourselves, will also interrupt our friendly relations with the natives. It is, therefore, of primary importance both to them and us, that the Government should take energetic measures to secure the execution of all laws affecting Indian trade and the intercourse of white men and Indians.

Something of an extraordinary nature appeared necessary to arrest the attention of a giddy people, who were ready to conclude that Christianity was a fable and futurity a dream. The revival has done it. It has confounded infidelity, awed vice into silence, and brought numbers beyond calculation under serious impressions. Whilst the blessed savior was calling home his people, and building up his church in this remarkable way, opposition could not be silent, as I have hinted above. But it is proper that I should observe here, that the clamorous opposition which assailed the work at its first appearance, has in a great measure borne down before it. A large portion of those who had fallen were at first opposers, and their example had taught others to be cautious, if it has not taught them to be wise. I have written on this subject to a greater length than I first intended. But if this account should give you any satisfaction and be of any benefit to the common cause, I shall be fully gratified. Yours with the highest esteem, George A. Baxter Rev. His appearance before the town of Far West, in the county of Caldwell county, where the Mormons were entrenched. Upon their approach the Mormons hoisted a white flag, which was shot down by Capt. Bogard, but was immediately replaced. General Atchison then sent a message with a view to learn their wishes and intentions, when six of their leaders avowed their willingness to surrender, in the expectation that the Mormons would be unharmed. The surrender was accepted, and the individuals put under guard. Their names are Joseph Smith, Sidney Rigdon, George Hinkle, Lyman Wight, Parley P. Pratt, and Mr. Knight. The Mormons assembled at Far West comprised 700 men under arms. Of this number, a small body of 153, retreated and pursued their way to the northern frontier. It is reported that subsequent to this surrender, a number of Mormons were set upon and murdered. Accounts which are now received tend to show that these deluded Mormons are a persecuted people, more sinned against than sinning, in the matter of this war; that they have merely resisted acts of oppression, which have driven them to desperation. Their error appears to have been in settling down on some of the richest lands in the state and in defense of their right to them. Upon the avarice of others, they were forced to take up arms. Mo. The Morning Air. There is something in the morning air, that, while it defies the penetration of our proud and stupid philosophy, adds brightness to the blood, brings life, and vigor to the whole frame, the redness of the lip, by the cheek, according to Dr. Marshall, is the surest marks of health.  It would be well, therefore, if you would have your heart dancing gladly like the morning breeze, and your blood flowing like an April brook, to rise early; to see the dawn, watch the sun as it, which no philosopher can, to warn him to take his walk, to breakfast on the morning air fresh with the dews of budding morn, and all fragrance of the maiden from your nerves destroying north, and from the foul air pent with coal smoke o'er the dew of the far eastern hill; But we must defend the morning air against the aspersions of those who sit in their close chambers studies and talk of the chilling fever and the unwholesome damps; of the ague; we have all the.

From the Boston Chronicle, Jan. 10. We see by an advertisement in another column that Messrs. Comstock & Co., the American Agents for Oldridge's Balm of Columbia, have deputies to sell that article in Boston and elsewhere. We know a lady of this city whose hair was so nearly gone as to expose entirely her phrenological developments, which, considering that they betokened a most amiable disposition, was not in reality very unfortunate. Nevertheless she mourned the loss of locks that she had worn, and, after a year's fruitless resort to miscalled restoratives, purchased some months ago a bottle or two of Oldridge's Balm, and she has now ringlets in rich profusion, glossy, and of raven blackness. We are not puffing; none of the commodity has been sent to us, and, indeed, we do not want any, for though we were obliged to wear a wig a year ago, we have now, through its virtue, hair enough, and of a passable quality, of our own. ROBERT WHARTON.

And who can tell but these very trials which they are called to endure, in sympathy for their persecuted brethren, may awaken the inquiry whether they have not hitherto rejected the true Christian, and whether there is any other name given under heaven among men whereby they must be saved? Or who can tell but it may be the means of interesting the Church of the Lord Jesus Christ on their behalf with something of the feeling which actuated the bosom of Paul, when his conscience bore him witness in the Holy Ghost that he had great heaviness and continual sorrow in his heart, for his brethren, his kinsmen according to the flesh, who are Israelites, to whom pertaineth the adoption and the glory, and the covenants, and the giving of the law, and the service of God, and the promises, whose are the fathers, and of whom as concerning the flesh Christ came, who is over all God blessed forever." Indeed we can regard the dealings of God with them in no other light than as among the means which he, in his infinite wisdom, sees fit to employ to accomplish his expressed and glorious designs towards them as a people.

British—Killed 53, Wounded 183—236. Spanish—Killed 19, Wounded 121—140. Total 376. From the N. O. Times, Oct. 29th. LATE FROM TEXAS. By the Pennsylvanian, arrived this day. We have been favored with the perusal of the Texas Telegraph of the 12th, which states that Gen. Houston and suit arrived at Columbia, the seat of government, of the 9th Oct. The election of Houston and Lamar as president and vice president has been confirmed, and the votes for annexing Texas to the United States were almost unanimous. The most interesting subject before the people is the appointment of a successor of Houston as commander in chief of the army. Thos. J. Rusk's claims upon Texas are spoken of, as also those of Generals Greene and Felix Houston. We have been informed, however by the Captain of the Pennsylvanian—who sailed on the 20th inst.—that the latter gentleman will be, if he has not already been appointed to the command. The Telegraph makes some allusion to an individual in the United States as worthy of command, and promises to give the name of the individual in their next number.

It is generally supposed that there are so many absentees from the city, that nothing of importance will be done tomorrow, and that the appointment of a printer will be postponed until next week. I was informed today, however, by an administration member, that there will be an effort, as soon as the House meets, to make the election. I should think the attempt would fail. N. Y. Courier. Washington, Dec. 27. As I anticipated, very little has been done in Congress today. The House met at the usual hour. Mr. Mercer’s resignation of his seat was received and read. A message was received from the President, containing the correspondence, etc. between the State of Missouri and the Territory of Iowa, on the subject of the boundary. Mr. Hoffman moved, and the resolution was adopted, that the rules and orders of the last House of Representatives be adopted for the government of the present House until the 10th of January. On motion of Mr. Wise, ordered that the Speaker appoint the usual standing committees. Having made the motion, Mr. Wise requested that the Speaker would not appoint him on any committee, and craved the indulgence of the House on this point. Ordered, that when the House adjourns, it adjourns until Monday next. The resolutions offered by Mr. Dawson, in reference to the appointment of a printer, being the unfinished business, came up in order.

All the useful arts are progressing with unparalleled rapidity; and the art of printing is coming in for its full share of the common improvement. Determined not to be outdone in anything that pertains to his profession, where there is a fair chance for exertion and enterprise, the undersigned is determined upon issuing a sheet twice the size of the Boston Weekly Times, and to take the place of that paper, and will contain eight columns more matter than its brother Jonathan. This sheet will be called the Boston Notion, and it is determined it shall fully sustain a cognomen so full of meaning, and so interwoven with the established character of our Yankee City. It will be the largest newspaper in the world without exception, and will be printed on a sheet thirty seven by fifty two inches. It will be entirely filled with reading matter, and will contain three thousand eight hundred and eighty-eight square feet of print in fine type, and a single number will contain more reading than an ordinary book of three hundred pages. These great dimensions will enable the publisher to draw largely upon the most popular periodicals and magazines of the day, both American and foreign; and as the selections will be made with great care, it is believed this paper will be a welcome NOTION in every family.

The aborigines or Iroquois believed that when men were a long time destitute of women, but happening to wander near a small lake, they saw certain animals among the branches of the tree which proved to be women. They attempted to catch them but found it impossible to get any hold of them, as they were as slippery as eels. At last they employed a number of men whose hands had been rendered rough by a kind of leprosy, and who succeeded in catching four of them by whom the world was peopled. There seems to be an idea, as well among savages as civilized men, that woman is rather a slippery animal. Cm Sun. A memorandum on the margin of the Buffalonian of September 4, says: A new Great Western, the largest boat but one of all that sails on the inland waters of the United States, was leaving Detroit, she was discovered to be in flames and was burned all but hull and engine, which may be rebuilt. She was commanded by Captain Walker who commanded the ill-fated Washington, which was burnt last summer. The Great Western was a most splendid boat, built in the style of the Mississippi boats, with high-pressure engines. She cost over $100,000. She was 800 tons burden. New York Journal of Commerce. "Exchange is no Robbery," or "Paying him in his own Coin."— The New York Sun will not vouch for the legal truth of the above saying, but the following incident goes in some ways to assure its editors that it may be true in some cases. The arrest of Elias J. Sylvester induced several persons who held tickets in the New Orleans Real Estate Lottery humbug, to consult a legal gentleman on the means of recovering the money of which they had been swindled by this nefarious transaction. One of the clients was advised to try the following experiment, which he executed with effect. He procured a number of whole tickets, blanks, of the Virginia Lottery, which had been drawn, and presented them at the counter of Sylvester for examination. Mr. S. turned to his drawing, and informed the holder that they were blanks. "So much gone, then," replied the holder — "I’ll send another hundred after that, and see what luck will follow. — Show me your wholes," continued he; and looking over the packages thrown on the counter before him, he selected 14 tickets, at $10 each, and quietly folded them up and put them into his pocketbook. Then taking out some of the 6 tickets of the G. Humbug Lottery, which he bought of Sylvester, he presented them to him with the learned saying, "Exchange is no robbery."

The difference in the productiveness of the soils between the east and the west, will be from fifty to one hundred per cent, in favor of the west. — The expenses of living, will also be less at the west. From these premises, any individual may be enabled to draw safe conclusions, of the future prospects and value of property in Wisconsin. Improved farms, which can now be purchased in this Territory, from six to ten dollars per acre, must soon by the common rules, which regulate trade, nearly if not quite reach in value, an equal number of acres in N. York or New England. If any of our eastern friends should raise the inquiry, why improved lands are now so cheap in Wisconsin, if the prospect of their ultimate value, be as flattering as we have represented; we reply, that this arises from the vast quantity of unoccupied lands in this Territory, and from the sparse ness of our population. We have a Territory capable of sustaining more than one million of inhabitants, while its present population but little exceeds thirty thousand; besides, we have no surplus capital beyond the immediate and actual wants of the people. This accounts for the cheapness of real estate property. We repeat it then, now is the time for making profitable purchases of land in Wisconsin.

The Whig returns were all made in pursuance of law, but the V. B. certificates showed the highest number of votes. This is the true state of the case; and under such circumstances what might to have been done? Every day's experience, and the laws governing the election of all deliberative bodies, declare that each house shall decide and determine all contested elections of its members. The V. B. manes are here, instead of raising a lawless mob to carry everything by brute force, should have presented the evidence of their claims to be members, and regularly contested the seats of the sitting (Whig) members, and in this way, the whole matter would have been decided according to law. But this did not suit their 'the with nil e e i.e.' re t'r there, that its origin in the conduct of W. L. Ingersoll and the ten Van Buren judges, inflating the votes of the Northern Liberties. The whole election is probably illegal, in consequence of this misconduct. But the sitting members have a right to hold their seats until the election is declared null by the proper authority. The returns of the V. B. judges are clearly illegal, because they were not made within the time prescribed by law. In equity, the Whig members, in a contest, must be ousted, on proof that they have not a majority of votes.

Besides the advantages to every family and every individual which results from an adequate supply of change and which apply to all countries, there are peculiar reasons for it in the United States, in saving the body of the people from the imposition of shinplasters every time that our paper system comes to stand. Let the supply of silver change become adequate; let it amount to two or three dollars per head for the whole adult white population; let there be fifteen or twenty millions of dollars in quarters, in ten cents, and five cents, and not only will economy and exactness in expenditures be promoted, but the shinplaster party will forever be deprived of all pretext for resorting again to their filthy and degrading substitute. On Monday, soon after it was known that Mr. Rathbun was bailed, two stout young Dutchmen came to the house, and without saying anything but "good morning," Mrs. H. came to the door and asked them what they wanted. "Does Mr. Rathbun live here?" "Yes." "Does he want to hire? We heard he was out of jail, and we didn't know but he wanted somebody to work for him." This simple anecdote speaks more for the character of the man, and the estimation in which he is held by the laboring classes, than volumes of elaborate eulogy.

Lord Palmerston, his "good vriend," sets himself to dream, in a circumambient cloud of smoke, of the beauties of cent, percent, and stagnant canals, or, as the British poet more neatly expresses it, of "the verdant mud." This happy state of repose must, however, be again interrupted next week, when the States General are to meet. Monday is the day fixed for that important event, which, coupled with the probable settlement of the French Ministry, may produce some acceleration in the settlement between Belgium and this country. Any opposition of moment to the King's policy is not anticipated here, nor can it be calculated upon from the conduct of the States since the four days of Brussels. It has been hitherto quite devoted to his Majesty, both before, as far as it was Dutch, and since that event. The Prince of Orange is expected in town tomorrow; his family are here at present. The Prince is universally liked, and in the army he is enthusiastically beloved. I believe he deserves it, for he possesses a sound head and a manly, well-tempered heart.

From a reference to the individual members who compose this family, it will be conceded, that as regards sex, age and capability for labor, they will not more than average with the whole population of the United States. With this data, calling the present population of the United States 15,000,000, we find by the rule of three, (Say,) if 7 earn $970 what will 15,000,000 970 ? 2078,571 42 that the whole value of the productive labor of the United States amounts to more than 12078 millions of dollars annually. As far as the laborer is employed in agriculture and manufacturing, it is fair to presume that those branches yield double the amount of the laborer's hire—it being customary in many parts of the country to improve land and the proceeds to be divided between the landlord and tenant in equal shares,—Allowing this rule to hold good as regards all branches of business, this sum must be doubled to give us the annual products of our national wealth.

An establishment has been purchased by order of the Legislature of South Carolina, about a mile from Charleston, to be called the Cattle Farm, the design of which is to afford to raisers of stock of every description, an opportunity of obtaining a fair price for their animals, by furnishing a place of general rendezvous, where sales may be effected with proper deliberation. Substantial pens with some covered stalls in each division, are erected on the farm, and means provided to supply each pen separately with water, and grain can be had on the place at the market prices. The pens are free of charge. This is a patriotic act of the Legislature of that State, and must be productive of essential benefit to Charleston. We seldom experience such marks of consideration from ours.—Savannah Georgian.

Deadful!—One of our western papers in giving an account of a tornado, heads it as follows: "Disgraceful Thunder Storm." Prejudice.—The following forcible and beautiful delineation of prejudice is ascribed by Hugh Worthington, a late English divine, to the celebrated Doctor Price: "Prejudice may be compared to a misty morning in October: a man goes forth to an eminence, and he sees at the summit of a neighboring hill, a figure—apparently of gigantic stature—for such the imperfect medium through which he is viewed would make him appear. He goes forward a few steps and the figure advances towards him; the size lessens as they approach; they draw still nearer, and the extraordinary appearance is gradually but sensibly diminished; and at last they meet, and perhaps the person he had taken for a monster, proves to be his brother." When at a subsequent period, the controversy occurred between the President and Vice-President, I thought I saw in this, the evidence of an intent to agitate the question, which by the agency of the personal friends of Gen. Jackson, had been before happily repressed. The connection of Mr. Crawford with this controversy, and my own relation to Gen. Jackson, forbade me to take any part in it, and I studiously avoided all interference, except to deprecate Mr. Calhoun’s publication. I left Washington on the fourth day of April, one day after Mr. Eaton had announced to the President his determination to resign, according to the statement in his (Maj. Eaton’s) letter of resignation, and not the slightest intimation was given to me of the intended change in the Cabinet. But when I saw the correspondence between the President and the several Heads of Departments, I could not doubt for a moment how, and by whom, the dissolution had been produced. I did not feel at liberty to express my views generally, until my return to Washington should enable me to dissolve my connection with the President, but to a few friends who had the right to understand my actual position, I wanted the utter impossibility of my continuance in the Cabinet, unless the President could place the retirement of my colleagues on other grounds than those which I believed to have occasioned it, and such as I could approve.

It will be recollected that the reported piracy took place on Saturday the 21st, six leagues from the Cape. Still, there is a possibility that some other ship has met the fate which was feared to have been the Susquehanna’s. In addition to the firm belief of the respectable pilots who reported the piracy, already published, we annex the statement of Capt. Baxter, of the schooner Meridian, arrived at Providence. This, taken in connection with the fact that the pilots are not certain that it was the Susquehanna they saw near the piratical looking schooner, having previously lost sight of her, renders the matter still dubious as to the fate of some other ship. Alarm has also been increased by a statement made by Capt. Baxter of the schooner Meridian, arrived at Providence, from James River, that on Saturday, 21st inst., about four o’clock, 50 miles E. by S. of Cape Henlopen, saw a vessel on fire, enveloped in smoke, a fore topsail rakish looking schooner near her, about 7 or 8 miles to windward [eastward] of him.—Boston Traveler. How few there are of all those who vote, that are governed in their selection of candidates by a pure spirit of patriotism, and how much fewer who give their votes for or against any particular one of the issues that are so pompously made up by the papers for their consideration. A drunken Irishman is dragged up to the polls, between two respectable office holders, on the election of, perhaps, a constable. Hurrah for Jackson! I vote for Pat Jackson, because he is an Irishman." This brute's vote is duly chronicled as the suffrage of the "free, unbought elector," in favor of the Sub Treasury. "Hurrah for the French and the Whig," screams a Frenchman in Detroit, staggering under the weight of a full cargo of rusty pork and stale beer, distributed free, gratis, for nothing, by the patriotic Whig committee. "I go for the Whigs; they protect the Catholic." Here is a victory "over office holders and governmental corruption" the proper subject for a hurrah, and a glorification.

A hundred and fifty people fill the room! no response,--ten to nine Maria takes the lead --but no answering "done" draw out the document till the men run up to declare, and in a terrible struggle passed, and came on hell. Never was such a deafening shrill heard on a track all over rose up. Gray-headed men danced and shouted like merry Andrews, and losers, as well as winners, seemed frantic with excitement. Goal yard was now drawn, and the Pole being distanced in the previous heat, Malzro and Maria had it all to themselves. As good as any. The field kept getting more select, Each thicket served to thin it. Twelve miles had been run, yet the mare looked better than when she started. Maria Black is, without doubt, the very best running nag ever imported! and Malzure as game a young as Bertralud ever got. The fourth heat.--Not a jot of the excitement was lost when the two started for the fourth heat, nor for three miles were they ever wider apart than just to save their heads from striking. At every jump the spur was given to the horse, and in the last mile the whip flew merrily about his flank, yet they entered the quarter stretch on a lap. But here the English lady thought it time to take off too tenacious a author, and with a rush and fresh air her first quarter.

Early in the morning of yesterday, Colonel Webb, of New York, called at my lodgings while I was yet in bed, and stated that on the evening preceding, Mr. Cilley, of Maine, had accepted a challenge from Mr. Graves, of Kentucky, and that they were to fight at eighty paces with rifles. Col. Webb said that the proposed meeting grew out of a correspondence between Mr. Graves and Mr. Cilley, founded upon a letter from Col. Webb to Mr. Cilley, asking for an explanation of certain language used on the floor of the House of Representatives, and of which letter Mr. Graves was the bearer. He said that it was utterly impossible that any meeting could be permitted to take place between Messrs. Graves and Cilley until Cilley had first met him, and that he was determined to force such meeting upon Cilley, be the consequences what they might, and that in pursuance of that determination, he had secured the services of on the evening previous, shortly after the acceptance of Mr. Graves's challenge by Mr. Cilley, and now called to ask my co-operation in the following proceeding, viz.

It would appear that there is at present a superabundance of money in New York, and other Atlantic cities; far greater than is adequate to the immediate wants of the community. This to a western man seems passing strange. What capital lying dormant, begging for five per cent interest on proper securities? Immense sums of money hoarded up, for the reason that the owners cannot turn them to a profitable account? Eastern capitalists have but a limited knowledge of the world after all. If they would only take a week's journey west to Wisconsin, they would suddenly be introduced into a wide and extensive theatre of operations, where capital to almost an unlimited extent might be readily employed in safe and profitable investments. Instead of five and six percents, twenty and twenty-five percents might be realized; instead of being pent up in cities, surrounded with banterers and competitors, here they might conduct their business operations, free from the perplexities incident to crowded towns and markets. No country elsewhere presents more rare inducements for the employment of money than this; lands as productive as the valley of the Nile, situated in a climate partaking of the blandness of Italy, yet pure as the mountains of Switzerland, are proffered to the emigrant, for a pecuniary consideration, comparatively small.

The Secretary shall within ten days thereafter, deliver to the tax collector, a fair and true copy thereof, with an additional column showing the amount of taxes due from each and every person, and file another like copy in his office, for the information of any whom it may concern. Charles S. Pattison is hereby appointed tax collector for the present year, for which he shall be allowed eight per cent on all taxes by him collected; and from the proceeds of personal property sold, or from the owners thereof, for a levy, for keeping the property and selling the same, such allowances as are made by law to constables for the like services; and from the owners of real estate reported for nonpayment of taxes, on the sale thereof, fifty cents for each delinquent reported. The tax collector shall proceed to collect the taxes as soon as he shall receive the tax list, and at least once in every month, he shall pay to the Secretary, all taxes or other corporation money by him collected. At the first meeting of the Mayor and Aldermen, after the 15th day of July next, he shall make a report of all real estate in arrear for taxes, stating the numbers of the lots and residence of the owners where known, and of whom and when he demanded payment. We perceive by the following extract from the report of the proceedings of the Maine Legislature, in the Kennebec Journal, that an order was lately passed authorizing the purchase of two bibles and two dictionaries for the use of members: "Mr Hobbs, of Eastport, introduced an order to appoint a committee to inquire into the expediency of establishing a library for the use of the Legislature. Mr Hobbs said that the present State Library consisted only of exchanged laws of other states, and reports of decisions. He had been unable to find books to which he wished to refer for the purpose of legislation. The order passed, and Messrs. Hobbs, Ide, Shaw, Bradbury, McMillan, Chadwick, Bunker, Lames, Woodbury, and Holmes of Alfred, were appointed. "Mr Holmes, of Alfred, introduced an order directing the purchase of a bible and dictionary for the use of members. Mr Welles moved that the order be laid on the table.

Directly she perceived the smell of fire, went up stairs and opened the door, and found it full of smoke, from the burning bed. The fire was soon extinguished, when it was found that the wretched inmate had been murdered by the infliction of several blows with a hatchet on the right temple. The deed was probably done while she was asleep, and she died without a struggle. Robinson's cloak was found in an adjoining yard, with a piece of twine tied to the tassel, and a hatchet covered with blood in another yard, with a piece of twine tied to the handle corresponding with the piece on the cloak. The hatchet has also been identified by the porter of Mr. Hoxie as one belonging to the store. The cloak was identified by the circumstances of a tassel having been broken off and sewed on in a particular manner. The alarm was given to the watch and the police, and early on Sunday morning Robinson was arrested in his bed. He denied all knowledge of the affair, or having been at the house that night. His clothes were found marked with whitewash from climbing the yard fences. The motive suggested for the deed is that he was weary of her, and altercations had arisen, in which she threatened to expose his conduct to a young lady whom he was expecting to marry.

REWARD - Runaway from the subscriber, about the 10th December last, my Negro Man STEPHEN, who sometimes calls himself Shepher. Stephen is about 30 years of age, five feet two or three inches high, of dark complexion, still black beard, and whiskers, has lost one of his upper front teeth, and a small gap in his under teeth, which are short in front. He has several scars, one on his forehead just above his nose, two small scars or marks on his right breast, a little below his neck, a small one on the back of the left wrist on the joint, and a small lump on the left breast just below the neck. Stephen was raised in Lunenburg county, about 15 miles below the Court House.

We cannot refrain giving our readers the entire following heart-stirring account of the late Convention of the people at Columbus, Ohio. It is estimated that more than 20,000 persons were present on the occasion. Thus, the good cause progresses in the whole West. [Correspondence of the Boston Atlas.] Columbus, Ohio, Feb. 24, 1840. THE OHIO STATE CONVENTION of the friends of HARRISON and TYLER came off here on the 21st and 22nd. There was probably never before so great a turnout, such unanimity of thought, such universal enthusiasm, at any political meeting in the United States. In giving a description, there is no danger of exaggeration. You must bear in mind that Columbus is near the center of the State, from 100 to 175 miles from either boundary—that the weather was in transition from winter to spring—that the roads were all kind, deep mud, nothing but interminable unmitigated mud—that money was hard to be got for any purposes—that Harrison has been derided as the "LOG CABIN CANDIDATE," fond of "HARD CIDER," and so forth. On the morning previous to the first day of the Convention, it began steadily to rain, and the people began to come in by delegations. The Hotels were all filled by 10 o’clock in the forenoon. Soon crowds collected along the streets and about the public houses.

The splendid patronage awarded to the Philadelphia Saturday Courier, induces the editors to commence the publication, under the above title, of a quart o edition of their popular journal, so long known as the largest Family Newspaper in the United States, with a list of near TWENTY-SIX THOUSAND SUBSCRIBERS. The new feature recently introduced of furnishing their readers with new books of the best literature of the day, having proved eminently successful, the plan will be continued. Six volumes of the celebrated writings of Captain Marryatt, and sixty-five of Mr. Brook's valuable Letters from Europe, have already been published without interfering with its news and miscellaneous reading. The Courier is the largest and richest folio newspaper ever issued in this country, containing articles in Literature, Science and Arts; Internal Improvement; Agriculture, in short every variety of topics usually introduced into a public journal. Giving full accounts of sales, markets and news of the latest dates. It is published at the low price of $2. For this small sum subscribers get valuable and entertaining matter, each week enough to fill a common book of 200 pages, and equal to 52 volumes a year, and which is estimated to be read, weekly, by at least two hundred thousand people, scattered in all parts of the country from Maine to Florida, and from the seaboard to the lakes.

How exciting! The Detroit Advertiser, it is rumored, has pulled the wool over the eyes of the Democrats of Monroe county, so completely, that but very few of them can see at all. Now, Mr. Whitney, that is too bad! Office of the Georgian, Savannah, August 6, 1836 11 A.M. Another Battle A Brilliant and Gallant Action. Yesterday afternoon an express arrived in town with intelligence that a battle had taken place in the morning at 6 o'clock, at Ridgeley's Mill, near the mouth of Black Creek, between a detachment of U.S. troops, 15 in number, under command of Lt. Herbert, and a party of 25 Indians. Lieut. Herbert left here on Wednesday last to escort 40 led horses to Garey's Ferry, and reached that place in safety the next day. He was returning in the steamboat Express to Picolata. When the boat arrived opposite the mill, he landed in a small boat with nine of his men, with a view of going to Mr. Travers' plantation, whose house had been burned the day previous, and making an examination. On landing, he discovered fresh moccasin tracks, and took up the pursuit. He soon fell in with a party of fifteen or twenty Indians, whom he immediately attacked. He was joined by the remainder of his command as speedily as possible, and at the same time the enemy were reinforced by 25 warriors.

Joel Clough, the murderer of Mrs. Hamilton, was executed at Mount Holly, N. J. the 20th of July. Upwards of twelve thousand people are said to have been present at his execution. He was accompanied to the scaffold by bishop Doane, and several other clergymen. At his request, Mr. Doane read his confession and a farewell letter to his mother, the latter of which produced a powerful sensation.

Her six sisters had all married respectable farmers and me- chanics, and were well to do in the world. But Molly, who was the beauty and pride of the family, resolved to look a little higher than her sisters. She would not take up with the hum- drum fellows, the farmers and me- chanics of Toppingtown, not she. She wondered that her sisters had no more respect for themselves than to marry such coarse, unmannerly critters. For her part, she meant to have a man that was somebody. Before she was fairly eighteen, she began to be wooed. Her beauty, the respectability of the Deacon, the Dea- con's wife, and indeed the whole fami- ly, early procured her plenty of suit- ors. But they were not Miss Molly's taste; and though she felt some little vanity in being wooed, even by farm- ers and mechanics, she was not to be thus won. Her first lover was Joshua Plough- share. He was a sober, industrious, moral young man of twenty-three, well- to-do, and resolved on getting a wife. He was quite taken with Molly Gray, insomuch that he never passed her in the street or saw her at church, but his heart beat as though it would fly out of his jacket. "She's an 'all fired handsome girl, that," said he to himself, and if I could only marry her, I should be the hap- piest fellow in all Toppingtown.

COMMUNICATIONS. [For the Liberator.] MRS. CHILD'S APPEAL. Mr. Editor:—I have recently given Mrs. Child's 'Appeal' a pretty attentive perusal, and I wish to avail myself of an early opportunity to say to others, in her own language, 'Read it.' If I were to judge from my own feelings, I should presume to say that those who read this book, will feel well repaid for the time which they spend in so doing.

During his speech, and while commenting upon the charge of the want of courage and good conduct of General Harrison, and vindicating him most successfully upon the testimony of Croghan and others, his eyes happened to rest upon the old veteran, who had been in Wayne's army in '93, '94, and '95, and who is a man of irreproachable character. The old soldier was attracted by the discussion, and stood a most attentive listener in the midst of the crowd. When the Gen. espied him, he threw down the book from which he was reading and exclaimed, "away with your books, I see a living witness amongst you, an old soldier and an honest man! Stand forth, John Inse! I'll tay you! Is General Harrison a brave soldier?" The effect was electrical every eye was bent on the honest old German soldier for a moment it seemed as if the respiration of every man in the house was suspended, that the response might be distinctly heard. The old man was evidently taken by surprise. For an instant he paused his eye seemed to kindle, and his chest to dilate, as he responded in the most emphatic manner, "As brave as ever was." The Dutch accent seemed to give additional force to the words, and no one who was not an eye witness to the scene, and of all the accompanying circumstances, can form an idea of the effect produced.
