The New Bedford Gazette of the 12th inst. in speaking of the flagitious attacks of many of the whig editors upon the venerable Ex-President Adams, says:—The base ingratitude evidenced by a few partisan striplings and unprincipled men toward this 'thing as it does on an unreal basis. In regard to the most slight cause of justification. As a public man, what has he done, or what left undone, deserving the curse roundly pronounced upon his venerable and bald head by the school-boy politicians of the day? We ask in sincerity—and the meagre apology for a reply, clad in all the bitterness of Billingsgate, teems forth,—not from the high-toned presses of the complaining party—but from the cringing and sycophantic issues, whose object is noise and bustle, and the height of whose ambition is, a little notoriety. The high-minded portion of the press, who differ with Mr. Adams in sentiment, do not hesitate to declare their nonaccordance with him—but yet maintain regards for his person and past services, which forbid abuse. It is not to be supposed that such a free man as Mr. Adams, will pledge his heart, soul and limb to any party, unless it be one based on his own peculiar measures of policy.

Several of the leading members having delivered their sentiments on the subject, Mr. Hughes (the member for Oxford) rose to address the House. This gentleman, for what reason I am at a loss to guess, generally meets with a very unfavorable reception; on this occasion it exceeded anything I ever before witnessed. The moment he pronounced the word “Sir,” addressing himself of course to the Speaker, he was assailed with the most tremendous uproar and confusion. Such a variety of sounds, and so discordant, hardly ever before greeted human ear. Mr. Hughes’ voice was at once drowned amidst the babel of sounds. Lord Brougham (then Mr. Brougham,) once compared the House to a menagerie; the application of the term would certainly have been most appropriate in this case. Had a blind person been that night conducted into the house, and not told what the place was, he would assuredly have supposed that he was in some zoological establishment. The Morning Post of the following day thus described the scene: — “The most confused sounds, mysteriously blended, issued from all corners of the house. One honorable member near the bar repeatedly called out “Read,” (to the member endeavoring to address the House) in an exceedingly bass and hoarse sound of voice.

While the receipts from the customs, between the present time and June, 1842, will be sufficient to defray the expenditures of the Government? It is contended, in the Report of the Committee, that the reduction of the receipts from the customs will not be in proportion to the reduction in the duties, because, as the imported articles will be cheaper, when the duties are lower, a greater amount will be consumed, and consequently imported, and though the rate of duty may be less, the gross receipt will be as large. This is unquestionably true to some extent. But it must be borne in mind, that the amount of importations to this country is dependent upon the amount of exports. By a calculation which I have made, it appears, that the annual average increase of domestic exports, from 1823 to 1834, is less than five millions. For the year, ending in September, 1831, the domestic exports are 41 millions. In 1833, do. do. 61 do. Do. 1836, do. do. 70 do. Do. 1837, do. do. 84 do. Do. 1838, do. do. 90 do. It will be seen that the increase in '35 and '36 of our exports was very great.

After the appointment of a few committees, to perform the unimportant details of their business, the Electors adjourned to meet tomorrow morning at 9 o'clock, when their principal business will be to receive the reports of their committees, and adjourn, sine die. The business would probably have been concluded today had not the credentials necessary to be appended to the returns, required additional certified copies of the appointment of Mr. Burrows, the making out of which, by the Governor, required some additional time. There being three separate returns for each of the offices of President and Vice President, and one copy being ordered for each, would make the whole number required six none of which were received when the College adjourned. No business of a political character beyond that for which they were immediately assembled to perform, was transacted; and I do not apprehend that they will do anything worthy of notice tomorrow. If they should, however, it will be communicated hereafter. Gen. Harrison at Lexington, Ky. A friend has furnished us with the annexed extract of a letter from a merchant of Kentucky, dated Lexington, Nov. 26th, 1840. "Old Tip arrived in town on Monday last, and was received by a detachment of volunteers, hundreds of citizens on horseback, on foot, and in vehicles of every description, with the most lively demonstrations of popular regard. It was comparatively a triumphal entry into the literary and scientific metropolis of Kentucky.

Fellow-citizens, the Presidential election of 1840 will probably be carried on with more than wonted zeal by our adversaries, if we may judge by the note of preparation. Rendered desperate by continued defeat, they are marshalling their forces for another struggle. Every device which cunning can suggest, every means of success, however unworthy, are resorted to with more than characteristic recklessness. To the timid, they conjure up before their heated imaginations all the gorgeous and chimeras dire; the sordid those whose "only care is to increase their store"; the delusive promise of unmeasured abundance is lavishly extended. To the enemies of popular rights, the flattering suggestion is applied, that their success will be the beginning of a better system, the dawning of a new era. You are aware that the concerted wisdom of the Whig party, which met recently in convention at Harrisburg, so famed for its skill in "nicely discerning," have determined to try the experiment once more, of overthrowing the great Democratic party of the country; and the instrument by which a "consummation so devoutly wished" by the n is to be effected, is no less a personage than William Henry Harrison.

To the Editor of the Bath Telegraph: Friend Clarke:—We had a fine day, and a proud day for the friends of Harrison in Old Penobscot. F. O. J. Smith has acquired new laurels and carried dismay into the ranks of the locos here. They have boasted that they should be able to show him a turncoat and a traitor by comparing what he would say today with what he has said heretofore, and they were actually at the expense of printing 5000 copies of his Portland speech in 1834, which were industriously circulated among the assembled thousands this morning.

He picked up a small stone from the hill, and throwing it gently about ten yards, said, "It is well, well, well, after all, thank God! no grave stone has been raised during my absence! If I am not deceived I shall find my parent living and in good health," continued he, with a sigh, "may I also find their love. It is hard, sir, when the heart of a parent is turned against his own child." He drooped his head upon his breast for a few moments and was silent, and, hastily raising his forefinger to his eyes, tried to dash away a solitary tear. Then turning to me, he continued, "You may think, sir, this is weakness in a soldier; but no, sir, the heart beats beneath a red coat. My father, whose name is Carroll, and I are estranged from each other; he is a wealthy farmer in the neighborhood. There was a time when our hearts were warmed towards each other, and he grew in liking towards me. But alas! his only daughter threw herself away, the daughter of a wealthy shepherd. Our alliance greatly offended my father. He never forgave us, and we fled together. I could

According to a recent review in New Grenada, our government has guaranteed her sovereignty and independence but it has, in point of fact, we have paid a large bonus for the privilege of holding war, and if there are any advan tages to be derived from it, the government should at least endeavor to recompense itself for the responsibility of the guarantee. The favor of the Tehuantepec route, claim for the consideration of the right of way, the same protection for Mexico that has been grant ed to New Grenada. It is hardly necessary to say, that there is a wide difference between the responsibility of the two cases. Even now, the condition of Mexico is unsettled, and if the recent accounts be true, the territory through which the projected road would pass, is in pos session of a rebel chief. A guarantee of the sovereign ty and independence of Mexico for twenty years, might cost the government a larger sum than the whole expense of a road connect ing the two oceans, over the continent. Such a contract ought to be considered seriously before being signed. Virginia Legislature. The Washington (Penn.) Press states that on Thursday morning, the citizens in the neighborhood of the residence of Mr. Charles Greer, of Richills, were aroused from their slumbers by the appearance of fire, and on reaching the spot, found the residence of Mr. Greer wrapped in flames, and the bodies of Mr. and Mrs. Greer burnt to a cinder. It is supposed that the old couple had been murdered in their bed by some demon in human form in order to possess himself of Mr. G's money, and that after committing the robbery, to cover over the villainy, had set fire to the house. Riot at Salem.—The Journal learns, from Salem that a disgraceful riot occurred at the exhibition of Sandu, Lent & Co's. Circus, in that city, Tuesday evening. It seems that more tickets had been sold than the immense canvass would accommodate, and the seats being overloaded, broke down. At this the crowd became exasperated and fell to destroying whatever property they could lay their hands on, which of course resulted in a general melee between the audience and the company. Several arrests have been made.

It the treaty were entirely unobjectionable, facts have been disclosed which show that with justice to others, and honor to herself, the United States cannot at present annex Texas to the Union. It is shown clearly, that Mexico has not relinquished her designs of subverting the territory of Texas. There is a written armistice existing between Mexico and Texas; the nature of which is, a cessation of hostilities between the two countries, for a specified time, hostilities to be renewed at the end of that time, at the pleasure of Mexico. It is not nor never has been the policy of this country to add to her territories by conquest. Republicanism and freedom are not generally very efficiently spread by the sword; and the good, the protection of our Union might afford to that country, would be vastly overbalanced, by the laxness of principle manifest in the mode of acquiring it. It is clear that under the circumstances, if we take Texas, we walk with our eyes wide open, into a war with Mexico. By the armistice, Mexico impliedly declares her purpose of again invading Texas; we are fully acquainted with that fact, and by annexation, of course we take precisely the position of Texas, and take her difficulties upon ourselves. Very few, we imagine, under the circumstances, can seriously urge the immediate annexation.

Resolved, That it was the duty of John E. Harmonson, member of the House of Representatives for the Third Congressional District, at such a crisis in the affairs of the country, to have represented his District in Congress, and if unable, it was his duty to have resigned. He did neither; and reviewing his course we are compelled to believe that love of pay and mileage, outweighed his affections for his country. Resolved, That Millard Fillmore, President of the United States, by his messages to Congress; and by his signatures to the Compromise Bills, has wholly redeemed the pledges which his southern friends made for him; that he has the confidence of the South, and deserves well of his country. Resolved, That under all conceivable circumstances, Louisiana will be steadfast to the Union. Resolved, That copies of these resolutions and preamble be forwarded to Henry Clay, Lewis Cass, Daniel Webster, Henry S. Foote, Solomon W. Downs, and Millard Fillmore, President of the United States, and that copies be forwarded to the Baton Rouge Gazette, Plaquemine Sentinel, Donaldson Vigilant, and the various papers printed in New Orleans for publication. After the names were read, a motion for their adoption was made, and the same were adopted unanimously. Hon.

Humors of the House of Commons.—There was a good deal of fun in the House of Commons on Friday night, when the House sat late, and the members had most of them come down after dinner to be present at the division. Poor Mr. Law, the Recorder, had got an enormous brief, by way of speech, and every now and then, whilst expressing some very strong feelings, he was obliged to refer with his eye glass to his papers before he could finish his sentiment. The following is a specimen of the honorable gentlemen’s speech: “ I now give the Right Honorable Baronet this open intimation of my opinion, that when—that (hang it, where the deuce have I got to now?) Let me remind the House, which I do most solemnly, that—that—(hang it, who has taken up my spectacles?) The principle of this measure is, I boldly assert to mislead us—to take us we know not where. (There !

The Afghanistan War. Lord Palmerston having been accused, on the hustings at the recent election, of improperly bringing on the Afghanistan war, entered into the following explanation and defence of that sanguinary contest. It is interesting (says the Georgetown Advocate) as showing, from an authentic source, the cause and the motives of the English conduct:  "But Mr. Harney says, too, that we behaved very ill in Afghanistan. We are charged with being the author of a great and deplorable calamity in the country. It is perfectly true that a most afflicting catastrophe happened in the mountains of Afghanistan, and that several thousands of brave men—some of whom were British born subjects of this country, and others British born subjects of India—perished in the most melancholy and barbarous manner. But why was it we went originally into Afghanistan? It was because the King of Persia was threatening an inroad into Afghanistan, and, in conjunction with persons in authority there, was evidently, and without the slightest disguise, contemplating the invasion, and, if possible, the subjugation, of our Eastern Empire.

Duff Green in England, as connected with commercial relations between the United States and the British Government. Gen. Green, who is still in London, has written a long letter, which is published in the Boston Post, which amongst other things, discloses some facts connected with the project of regulating the import duties of the two countries by Treaty. He states that having procured a copy of the census of the United States before it was published here, he proceeded to Europe, and published in that city and in Paris, a series of essays on the resources of the United States. Owing to the favorable impression made by these, he became acquainted with several leading men in England and France. Information in relation to the American trade was given by him, to aid the Board of Trade in England to make up their report on that subject, and Lord Riaon asked his opinion upon the practicability of making a treaty of commerce with this country on terms of reciprocity. Lord Aberdeen himself, in an interview with Green, authorized him to assure the President that the British desired to enter into such a treaty, and the details of the proposed arrangement were laid before Mr. Green, at the London Board of Trade. Green returned to the United States and communicated his budget to the President and several members of Congress.

Mr. Asa C. Johnson of Potosi, to Miss Mary Wilson of the former place. AN ACT to authorize the Election or appointment of officers in the Territory of Wisconsin. Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, That the Legislative Assembly of the Territory of Wisconsin shall be, and are hereby, authorized to provide, by law, for the election or appointment of sheriffs, judges of probate, justices of the peace and county surveyors, within the said Territory, in such way or manner, and at such times and places as to them may seem proper; and after a law shall have been passed by the Legislative Assembly for that purpose, all elections or appointments of the above named officers, thereafter to be had or made, will be in pursuance of such law. Sec. 2. And be it further enacted, That the Members of both Houses of the said Legislative Assembly of the Territory of Wisconsin, shall upon the expiration of the terms of service for which the present members have been elected, be hereafter elected to serve for the same terms of service as that for which the members of the Legislative Assembly in Iowa, are now elected. Approved, March 3, 1843.* ELECTION LAW. An Act to provide for the election of sheriffs, judges of probate, and justices of the peace.

We are pained to learn that four Hawaiian passengers from Hong Kong on board the Enigma died through the reprehensible negligence of somebody in not furnishing the vessel with sufficient water for even a short passage to these islands. We learn from Capt. Hubertson, who chartered the Enigma, that after the Brig was ready for sea, with provisions and water on board, seventeen native passengers were put on board by the owners of the vessel, against the wish and protest of his agents in China, and also of the Captain, (Stavers,) who commanded her. That she sailed with a supply of water for forty-five days only, which was further reduced, by stress of weather, to but thirty-six days' supply; and as the vessel was sixty-six in making the passage, they were put upon so short an allowance as to cause sickness, and the death of the four men alluded to above. No blame, certainly, can attach to Capt. Hubertson, but it would seem to fall upon the owners who forced the passengers on board. But we cannot refrain from expressing the opinion that with such a necessity of proceeding to sea with an inadequate supply of water, the captain should either have declined the command, or should have put into some port on the way for a supply.

The corresponding Secretary of the Historical Society has received replies from several individuals and Societies, whose opinions were solicited in reference to the adoption by this country of a national name. The replies in general discountenance the proposed change. Chancellor Kent declares his judgment and taste decidedly against any attempt at innovation. Reverend and Dear Brethren, The subject of Common School Education has ever excited a wakeful interest in the free States of this country, and especially in New England. The clergy have always stood on the most forward rank in promoting this great cause, and on their zealous endeavors and agency, its advancement must continue, materially, to depend. It cannot, we think, be denied that Vermont, notwithstanding her indomitable spirit of enterprise, is pursuing less efficient means to improve Common School Education than the other States of New England, or the adjacent State of New York.

Mr. Niles ridiculed the idea of alarm at the growth of British power on this continent. He contended that we had nothing to fear from any power on earth and had no more reason to fear England than we had to fear Mexico. Whatever could possibly be done for Yucatan, he was ready on his part to do cheerfully. On motion the Senate then adjourned. House. The Speaker announced that the first thing in order on his table was the motion previously pending to reconsider the passage of the bill for paying the regiment of Texas six months volunteers, which was carried. So the bill again came up before the House for consideration. Mr. Burt, of South Carolina offered an amendment to the bill upon which considerable debate ensued, in which Mr. Burt, Mr. McKay, of North Carolina, and others, participated. Mr. Kaufman, of Texas, moved to refer the bill with the amendments to the committee on military affairs, and went on to speak at considerable length in its favor. After concluding his speech, he withdrew his motion, and the bill again came up on its passage, when it was read a third time and passed.

We have heretofore had occasion to censure O'Connell for the vile and abusive epithets which he applied to this country, at the moment when he was pocketing the money sent by our citizens. His last speech was worse than any which preceded it, and excited universal indignation; for, however desirous the citizens of the United States may be to see Ireland restored to her rights and privileges, they are not disposed to sanction the conduct of a man who would devastate their country with fire and sword. INSOLENCE AND FOLLY. At the meeting of the Repeal Association in Dublin on the 7th April, O'Connell made a speech, in which, after praising Sir Robert Peel, he said—"To America I say, don't dare attack England; they are conciliating Ireland; and you have three millions two hundred thousand slaves among your inhabitants." At the next meeting of the Association on the 14th, the amount of the week's rent was announced to be £700 4s. 11d.—£250 of which were from Boston; £100 from Illinois; and £44 16s. from Philadelphia. It is thus that Americans place in the hands of the turbulent and insolent O'Connell the means of scourging their own backs. SAVINGS BANK AT LOWELL. Her cargo is thoroughly Mexican and her invoice is in the language of that country. Governor Hill's speech to the extra session of the legislature. It was delivered on the 19th instant. We regret that it did not reach us in time for entire publication this week. It is a patriotic and able document, and equal to the exigencies of the times. The following extract: So long as we were permitted to hope, that there existed no fixed or meditated design, to deprive us of our acknowledged and essential rights, our usage was that of entreaty And supplication. Our course that of forbearance and conciliation but with the undeniable evidence now before us, of a settled determination to despoil us, regardless of constitutional guarantees, of at least third of our territory; a continuance of such language and such a course, were degradation and shame. Difficult and embarrassing then, as the position undoubtedly is, and however repugnant its contemplation with painful languid it, we have left us no choice, but to meet it. It must be met boldly and resolutely and determined. Not by further supplication or discussion with the Federal authorities. Not by renewed appeals to their generosity and sympathy.

Accounts from Vienna via Warsaw, to the 16th of August, say that the Hungarian Diet, having surrendered their powers to Gorgey, had dissolved itself. A meeting, including Kossuth, Gorgey, and Bern, took place, when it was to their minds to put an end to the war, as sanguinary and useless. Gorgey, addressing the Council of War, protested that he had no hopes for the cause of the Hungarians. All resistance was vain. Nothing but utter ruin would attend the prolonging of the strife. The war party, headed by Bern, Kossuth, and leading members of the Parliament, had nothing left but to hasten to Osstirva. It is stated that Kossuth carried with him the insignia of the Hungarian Emperor.

A young man named Morris Simmons, one of the Texan Rangers, while riding in advance of a train going from Monterey to Camargo, with a few companions, was fired upon by a party of Mexicans near Cerralvo some ten days since and shot in the thigh. He fell from his horse, and as the Mexican who shot him came out of the chap arral to rob him, he shot him dead with his five shooter. The rest of the party fled, and the ac count we have received states that Simmons' companions were carried out of reach by their horses becoming unmanageable. His leg was amputated, but no hopes were entertained of his recovery. This young man, in company with a companion, ran the gauntlet of lancers from Sal tillo to Rinconada, carrying the order for Gen Marshall to march on with his two heavy pieces of ordnance. It is the band of the 2nd Kentucky Regiment entering the town on their way home, and a joyful occasion for them all.

The English Cabinet has just sent an order to the Ionian Islands to prepare an expedition for Italy. Two regiments will be immediately conveyed to Ancona. Lord Palmerston has given notice of this resolution to the Cabinet of Vienna. The real motives of this resolution are these: Lord Palmerston became acquainted with an understanding between France and Austria, he knew that the Cabinet of Vienna had assured itself of the perfect neutrality of the Cabinet of the Tuileries on the Italian question at the moment at which England pressed Austria to make common cause with her in a protest against the marriage of the Duke de Montpensier. By this bold proceeding, which obliges Austria to stop, Lord Palmerston effects two objects at the same time—that of making Austria abandon the policy of temporization with respect to Spain, and of obliging France to make a demonstration. Louis Philippe and M. Guizot have returned to Paris to occupy themselves with this grave affair. The council is to assemble today. England has not waited for events: she has foreseen them; she acts. The fighting was over just then, and after various detours, we succeeded in reaching the barricade where fifteen minutes before a bloody struggle of a short duration had cost the lives of some brave men, and they had already commenced pulling down the stones, under the protection of a body of troops. The houses and shops had been entered as in February by the mob, for procuring arms, and "Armes donnees'' was written with chalk on all the doors and shutters in the neighborhood. I stopped to talk with an old woman who kept a milk shop within a stone’s throw of the fight, who had ventured to put her head out of her half-opened door. She gave me some particulars, and I expressed a hope that all was finished. An ouvrier, who was passing at the moment, heard my remark, and said, fiercely: “No it is not finished—you will have more of it to-night. You will see 1” A glance at his physiognomy showed a man boiling over with rage and disappointment. An artist might have sketched his features to make a successful picture of Satan on the day he completed that nine days’ voyage, which Milton gives him, from Heaven to the unmentionable place. We hurried towards the residence of my banker who lived in that and everywhere knots of ouvriers were to be seen debating with each other angrily, and manifesting feelings not to be mistaken.

Treaty.—This right was established in the cession of Louisiana and Florida, and cannot now be questioned, without menacing the organization of the government and the Union; for, by virtue of this power, three States and several Territories now compose a part of the republic. In 1842, we acquired territory by treaty, and attached it to the States of New York and Vermont. There was then no disputed boundary, for the call was for a certain parallel of latitude—a mere question of measurement which, when made, placed this territory within the undoubted limits of Canada; in consequence, we ceded to Great Britain, for tress erecting at Rouse’s Point, and the ground it occupied, which was a part of the territory which we acquired by the treaty of 1842. The question of the power of annexation by treaty is settled, and incorporated into the very existence of the government and of the Union.

It is a well-known fact that Mr. Clay's Lexington propositions have not responded to in more than two or three instances, and that meetings in opposition to them have been general. The Pea Patch case. After about thirty years of litigation, the Pea Patch case is at last decided—the title of the United States having been confirmed. In the meanwhile, we have thrashed John Bull on land and water, settled two boundary questions with him, whipped sundry Indian tribes, threatened France, and made her "pay up," swallowed Texas and overrun Mexico. All this time one Anglo-Saxon has kept Uncle Sam at bay—driving him off his own premises and like a good fellow not giving up until all hope was gone. —Delaware Republican, The Washington correspondent of the New York Mirror thus describes Mr. Clayton of the United States Senate. The Hon. John M. Clayton, of Delaware, who has spoken so eloquently and boldly today, is apparently about fifty-five years of age. He wears the capacious waistcoat of an alderman, and the white cravat and benign expression of a clergyman. His hair is white and full, his voice strong and agreeable, his action is graceful and earnest, and his style direct and clear.—He is a great admirer of Old Rough and Ready, and has named his beautiful residence in Delaware Buena Vista.

I have conversed with many other southerners within the last two days, and begin to believe there is trouble ahead of a portentous character. New York, Nov. 28. Father Mathew left this city this morning for Philadelphia, en route for the South where his physicians recommended him to go. Washington, Dec. 5-7, r, a. There being no choice on the 3rd ballot today, Mr. Johnson, of Tenn., offered a resolution, that on the next vote, any individual receiving a plurality of votes, should be declared elected. Mr. Holmes moved to lay the resolution on the table, which was carried—yeas 110, nays 7. The House then proceeded to vote viva voce, and on the 4th ballot, Cobb received 89 votes, Winthrop 98, scattering 35. The House then adjourned without electing. In the House today, on the 3rd ballot the vote stood Winthrop 100, Cobb 66, Potter 17, Richardson 25, Tuck 8, Gentry 4, scattering 3. On the 4th ballot, Winthrop 100, Cobb 63, Potter 18, Tuck 8, Richardson 23, Gentry 5, scattering 3. No choice, and the House adjourned. The Legislature of Ohio is organized. Officers elected all democrats. Memphis, Dec. 6. Hates from Texas of Nov. 12th, state that Gen. Brooke, has received important news that the Comanches, Wichitas, and other powerful tribes, had leagued together to make war on the whites.

In connection with this subject, we notice a statement that the Michigan Central Railroad from Detroit west, will be pushed through to Chicago without delay. From these demonstrations, our citizens, and the citizens of the Lakeshore generally will see the necessity for their own welfare of having a road to connect, within our boundaries, the Lake and the Rock River, or the Mississippi. There can be no doubt that without some improvement of this kind, the Rock River country will be connected with this southern road, without delay, and the trade of this valley in the territory will be lost to us, in a great measure. The Hon. Wm. B. Sheldon, of Janesville, once a citizen of Milwaukee, and well known to old residents of the territory, died at Buffalo, last week. His remains will be returned to Janesville for interment. The last Gospel Herald, published at Voree, contains the ground plan of the temple of the Latter-Day Saints, which it is intended to erect at that place. It consists of a main building surrounded by a court. The main building is to be 70 feet by 100, two stories high. The enclosure, or court, is composed of 12 buildings, connected by a wall, pierced by four entrances or gateways to the main building.

I was present at the opening of the Court on Monday and observed an aged negro dressed in a long black coat, small clothes and stockings, knee buckles and other characteristics of fashion "sixty years ago." As the Chief Justice entered the floor, the old fellow rose and was greeted by the head of the judiciary with as much civility as if he had been the "chief executive magistrate." The Chief Justice and the District Judge shook him cordially by the hand and made many inquiries for his health. On inquiry, I found that he was the slave and favorite body servant of the late Chief Justice Marshall, whose memory he cherishes in most affectionate remembrance. He is but a nominal slave now, preferring like many others here, not to be free. He dresses in precisely the style of his former distinguished owner, and insists on being at the door of the court room, as in days of yore, to render his services to the esteemed successor of his old master. He takes the hat of the Chief Justice, fills his glass of water and does several other offices as proudly as if he were the titled chamberlain of a king, and is treated with as much consideration by the very urbane and popular jurist, as his old master could have been.

He says it's now absolutely necessary that some of the large dissenting churches should reunite with them, and make a stout defence against their "common enemy." He calls upon and most earnestly on the Methodists, who, he argues, do not differ from his church in any point which should now be considered essential. He smoothes the path by withdrawing the name of Dissenters, heretofore used, and substituting "Separatists" as a milder term, and one of less reproach.—Extracted from the Lon. M. Mag. A Philadelphia Quaker.—The checkerboard plan of Philadelphia, and the uniformity of its buildings, are so much taste, for which, in our humble opinion, William Penn and his followers would receive utter condemnation by any jury of architects. But if Quaker heads are not cast in the same mould as Michael Angelo's, their hearts are generally developed to the greatest amplitude of Christian charity. This remark might be fully illustrated by pointing to certain hospitals, asylums and like institutions; but it is our present purpose merely to prove its applicability in a particular case. A certain "Friend," whom we very well know, was recently at a distant place of summer resort. He stepped into the post office one morning, and while there, the postmaster asked him whether he knew any English people staying at the hotel. "Why does thee ask?" said the Quaker.

I [ A squadron of the 2nd dragoons, to serve under Gen. Worth's orders until relieved by other cavalry, propose to assign Lieut. Kearny's company 1st dragoons to his squadron or two of the Kentucky "horse to his division, as soon as they can be brought forward. I shall also reinforce him by a volunteer force, probably a brigade. The division at Saltillo covers the direct route to San Luis Potosi. The other route would be covered by a force at Parras, which would at the same time command the abundant supplies to be drawn from the neighboring country—the richest of the north of Mexico. Brig. Gen. Wool being in position at Monclova, and it being fully ascertained that he cannot march thence on Chihuahua, even were it desired, I have ordered him to move on Parras. The State of Coahuila will then be completely occupied and covered, and in case active operations towards the interior should hereafter be determined on, we shall be in position to march on San Luis, Zacatecas, or Durango. Brig. Gen. Wool will at once abandon his communications by San Antonio and Lavaca, and adopt that by the Rio Grande and Monterey. I am, sir, very respectfully, your obedient servant, Z. TAYLOR, Maj. No account is made of the multitude of small dealers who have gone down with liabilities ranging from ten to one hundred thousand dollars, which would doubtless swell the whole amount to not less than seventy-five millions. The great weight of these immense losses will fall upon parties having no immediate business relations with this country, but the failures to meet business engagements to so great an extent must be felt in all branches of trade, and indirectly their consequences will be felt throughout the commercial world. The Boston Atlas says: The letters of our London Correspondent contain many details in relation to monetary and manufacturing affairs, which are not in the accounts we publish today. The state of trade in the manufacturing districts is very much depressed. The free trade system which was forced upon the nation by the Cotton Shipping interest has reacted upon them with dire effect; some of the mill owners are running their mills at a weekly loss of five hundred pounds sterling. The chief item of political interest is the position of the Pope as a temporal prince. The demonstration of Austria upon the Papal dominions has, unexpectedly to Austria, been resisted with so much firmness by the Italian States, especially Sardinia, that, backed, as it was understood such resistance would be by England, the Austrian government has faltered in its designs, and it is to £800 per Annum in Advance. 5s if not paid within 6 months.

The Pennsylvanian of the 16th inst., learns that there is a letter in Philadelphia dated at Mexico on the 17th of Jan. which states that the order to the column under Gen. Patterson, to move to San Luis Potosi, has been counter manded, owing to the assembling of a force of Mexicans at Quatitlan, twelve thousand in number. Gen. Cadwallader would, however, move on with his command in the design upon the Pacific coast. Santa Anna again in the field.

It were the honest last; but they were carefully calcined in a close iron cylinder, and considered worth one cent per pound for animal carbon. Remember now that for lard and tallow, foreign markets are ever open the duty, if any, being quite small: lard, for instance, being taxed about one half cent per pound, when shipped to England from New York city, and only about one fourth that sum when introduced through the Colonies. Allow me to mention one thing more the sugar of the West. Yes, the corn-stalk Sugar. I know there is much incredulity on this subject. Experiments this year have, however, settled the point, that the inhabitants of the interior can supply themselves with sugar from the cornstalk alone. At Indianapolis in Indiana, I found an enterprising citizen, Mr. Blake, who had prepared several acres of the stalk, boiling with five kettles the juice expressed. From 21 gallons of juice he obtained 7 gallons of crystallizable syrup. Another gentleman near Lafayette thought he should get one thousand pounds of sugar to the acre. From 600 to 1000 pounds per acre may be confidently expected. It is an old saying, that you cannot take off the cream and then make butter; nor can you raise corn and make sugar from the same. I am asked to state the process I will cheerfully do it.

We were not aware at the time where the blame did lie, but thought it most probably the case that they were not printed in time. We have since learned that they were printed, and that the fault of non-distribution was with the territorial librarian, whose duty it was made by law to distribute them. We make the remark now, that the blame may not fall where it does not belong. Interference in elections.—Gov. Doty has been guilty of active interference in our late delegate election. Not content with fair means, he resorted to foul, in his endeavors to defeat the election of Dodge. Extracts of letters are given in the Wisconsin Enquirer showing that he descended to electioneer personally, by statements not possessing the recommendation of truth; that he hired men to electioneer, and that he distributed Madison Expresses among the people. Will our whig administration give us a practical illustration of its professions, by turning Gov. Doty out of office? Mr. Ewing’s veracity.—Mr. Ewing says, in his letter of resignation to President Tyler; “The bill which I reported to Congress with your approbation, at the commencement of the session, had the clause relating to agencies, and the power to deal in exchanges as strongly developed as the one you have now rejected, and equally without the assent of the States."

What question of a great diversity of opinion has prevailed. According to one view of it, the western limit of Texas was the Nueces; according to another, it extended to the Rio Grande, and stretched from its mouth to its source. A majority of the committee, having come to the conclusion of recommending an amicable adjustment of the boundary with Texas, abstain from expressing any opinion as to the true and legitimate western and northern boundary of that State. The terms proposed for such an adjustment are contained in the bill herewith reported, and they are, with inconsiderable variation, the same as that reported by the Committee on Territories. "According to these terms, it is proposed to Texas that her boundary be recognized to the Rio Grande, and up that river to the point commonly called El Paso, and running thence up that river twenty miles, measured thereon by a straight line, and thence eastwardly to a point where the hundredth degree of west longitude crosses the river; being the southwest angle in the line designated between the United States and Mexico, and the same angle in the line of the territory set apart for the Indians by the United States. If this boundary be assented to by Texas, she will be quieted to that extent in her title. And some do suppose that, in consideration of this concession by the States, she might, without any other equivalent, relinquish any.

In pursuance of the public message, a bill was ordered to the prudent discussion Committee of the Whole in the House of Representatives, by one of the partisans of the Executive, for making an appropriation of Two Millions of Dollars to enable the President of the United States to negotiate a Treaty of Peace with Mexico, including most plainly the acquisition, by force or purchase, or by both means conjointly, of a greater or less portion of Mexican territory. This bill was debated both before and after the recess on Saturday, and all was going merrily as marriage bells towards its consummation, when the apple of discord was thrown into the midst of the majority by a motion of Mr. Wilmot, of Pennsylvania—the gentleman whom the government paper eulogized, the other day, as "the bold and fearless and truly able Wilmot," and in regard to whom we were given to understand, by the same authority, that it was "a singular coincidence that he is a native of the same town and county in the State as Mr. Walker."

Court Intelligence. General Sessions, Dec. 3 — Before Recorder Tall- madge, and Aldermen Hart and Henry. M. C. Patterson, Esq., District Attorney. Trial of Henry Perisho concluded. — The trial of the accused, for a constructive grand larceny, the particulars of which were given in the last number of the Mail, was resumed on opening the court this morning. On the part of the defense, which was ably conducted by Thomas Warner, Esq., testimony was adduced to show that Stoffriell was at the rooms of the accused for some time on the occasion of the alleged difficulties be- tween the parties; that Stoffnell received the piece of silk, (which was sealed up,) willingly, as security for the money advanced for the purpose of paying the Cus- tom House dues. The prosecution called witnesses to prove that Stoffnell was assaulted by the accused, and received the silk with great reluctance, and under the threat, that if he did not take that he would get nothing at all; and that under these circumstances, he, Stoffnell, received the silk, and conveyed it without breaking open the seal, to Mrs. May, and which, on being exam- ined, was found to contain 60 yards, worth about 80 cents per yard, and 27 yards worth about 85 cents per yard.

A party of twenty-seven of the boys of the Farm School, on Thompson’s Island, accompanied by a teacher and boat-keeper, went out yesterday morning on a fishing excursion. After being out nearly all day, while returning, about 4 o’clock P.M., the boat was capsized by a flaw of wind, when between Fort Independence and Thompson’s Island, a quarter or half a mile from the latter, and out of all on board only three of the boys are known to be saved—Frederick W. Simmons, W. Wallace and G. F. Gould. We saw the first named last evening. He states that the boat sank soon after it capsized. The boatman, Mr. Oakes, threw a chair and the topmast of the boat to some of the boys, to help to save them. The teacher gave a shriek soon after they went over, and immediately sank. He got into the box, and his companions, who were good swimmers, held on to the sides; and though it was turned over several times, they were fortunate enough to regain their position each time. One of the boys, Isaac Major, swam nearly to Thompson’s Island, when he became exhausted, began to cry out, and went down. The H.B. Foster was hailed by one of the boys after he had been about half an hour in the water, when the captain humanely dispatched a boat to their relief. They were much chilled when taken from the water.

Mr. McDowell then quoted from the "Union," some allusions to Judge Wick's "scathing attack" upon the Ohio delegation. Mr. McD. knew not what the writer intended by his remarks, but if he (Ritchie) insinuated that his colleague (Drinkerbott) had sufficient power over him (Mr. McDowell) to induce him to abandon his principles, because he had been disappointed in getting an office, he threw back the imputation with the contempt it deserved, and branded it with the falsehood it uttered. I stand not here to be lashed by the man who is an unmitigated slanderer. As to the gentleman from Indiana, he should wait until he heard from him before he replied. If he had attempted to whip him in, or had used any language warranting such an inference, he should read him a lecture that he would remember. TARIFF BILL. Mr. Houston of Ala. moved that the House go into Committee of the Whole upon the Tariff Bill. The motion was agreed to, and a very eloquent and animated speech was made by Mr. Bently of Penn. in exposition of the "Sromnrdum," as he called it, which had been practiced in the canvass of 1832 upon this very question of the tariff, and particularly upon the Kane letter.

On this the ayes and noes were called, and the motion was rejected by a vote of 101 to 60. Mr. Wise moved to amend the resolution so as to go into an election of Clerk immediately after choosing Speaker. Mr. Barnard objected, and after some remarks for and against the amendment, it was rejected, and the original resolution adopted. Messrs. Underwood, J. C. Clarke and Allison were designated as tellers. On counting the votes it appeared that John White of Ky was chosen. Whole number of votes, 221. Necessary to a choice, 111. Mr. White received, 121. John W. Jones, 61. Henry A. Wise, 8. Joseph Lawrence, 5. Messrs. Uriel's, Clifford and W. Cost Johnson, each received one. Mr. White was conducted to the Chair by Mr. Triplett, of Kentucky and Gen'l Keim, of Pennsylvania. On taking the Chair, the Speaker delivered a brief and appropriate address. The members were then sworn in. Mr. Wise then moved that the House proceed to the election of a Clerk. The motion prevailed and Messrs. Wise, Randolph and Good were requested to act as tellers. Mr. Wise nominated Matthew St. Clair Clarke. Mr. Randall nominated F. O. J. Smith. Mr. H. Ourland and Dr. Mason of Virginia were nominated by two new members whose names could not be learned.

In a word, we look with pride and satisfaction upon the intelligence, sagacity, and persevering industry that has marked each step in the progress of this great work, and shall contemplate with high pleasure the general approval that will be awarded to those by whom it has been so admirably contrived and conducted. The Works of John Adams. We learn from the Transcript that the second volume of the collected works of the second President has been issued in Boston, in advance of the first, by Messrs. Little & Brown. The first will be devoted to a life of John Adams, commenced by his son, and concluded by his grandson, the editor of these interesting papers. The volume now given to the public forms a large and elegantly printed octavo of five hundred and forty-two pages, embellished with a well-engraved likeness of John Adams when a young man, and a view of the house in which he and John Quincy Adams were born. The contents are, first, a diary, with passages from an autobiography; second, notes on debates in the Continental Congress in 1776 and 1778; third, an autobiography continued; fourth, an appendix containing, among other valuable matter, the original draught of the declaration of rights and grievances, made by the Congress of 1774.

With the addition of thick boots and a cape, with a tasteful touch of material, might be made a very picturesque and page-like costume, without infringing greatly on the inherent rights of the other sex. And then the infinite advantages over casual dress, in rambling over rocks, leaping mountain streams, making way through the forests and over marshy places, or in skimming the main in the meadows. Medical men have said and made it decided that regular exercise in the open is of incalculable benefit to woman; it is absolutely necessary to her physical well-being. It is our keeping of ourselves shut up in close rooms, with too much indoor care and labor on hands, which is to our country an entire generation of pale, complaining, nervous, or to use the words of a plain old friend, "fiddle-faddling" women. It is this Mohammedan sentiment, that feminine shrinking from the bold and healthful exercises of their English sisters, which renders the beauty of fair Americans so proverbially ephemeral. Beauty is not a fragile, rouged and powdered ball-room belle; but a ruddy, blooming, vigorous nymph of the mountain, bounding, sparkling, in the pure, free, healthful exercises of the country.

Something has been done, but what, where and when are yet matters of painful uncertainty. There must be some truth in all that has been said, but how much must be left to the hopes and fears of the public to guess. Smithsonian Institute. —Messrs. James Dixon of Washington City and Gilbert Cam eron of our City have taken the contract for erecting the edifice of the Smithsonian Institute at Washington. — It is to be 500 feet long, about 100 wide, the main building two stories high, and the towers 100 to 140 feet. The Museum and Grand Hall on the second floor will be nearly 500 feet long and 27 to 30 feet high. The whole is to be built of Potomac Free-stone, (obtained near Washington) is to be rendered fire-proof and fin ished in the richest and most thorough manner. The estimated cost is about $210,000. W. V. Tribune. A Good Man. —A Mr. Peter Miller died at Easton, Penn, at the 81st year of his age. He left no family and his estate, valued at half a million, is appropriated to various public objects—one of which is to invest a large sum, the income of which is to be loaned on good security to young farmers and mechanics purchasing property, and entering into business in the country. "We are indebted to O'Reilly's line for intelligence of the arrival of the Washington. It is strange that the news of the arrival of the Europa, by the line, which we published on the 2nd inst., was not received in New Orleans, until the 5th, notwithstanding Morse's line was in connection with Baltimore. This fact tends to strengthen the charge made by the New Orleans papers, that their dispatches have been suppressed for purposes of speculation. So far, Mr. O'Reilly has proved himself the superior of Morse, in enterprise and usefulness to the community.  Jackson Mississippian.  Gold in Brooklyn.—A gentleman of this city had a piece of virgin gold presented to him yesterday morning, which he would not sell for $5000. This is what we call a very handsome New Year's present. So does Mrs. Gold and the nurse. — Brooklyn Daily Advertiser.  The New York Globe adds: "That's not bad, cousin, but we should not wonder if the purse should declare that same lump of 'virgin gold' to be spurious, in a week or two." Squaring men in the lace, with a recorded vote in each House upon Spangler's bill, and two recorded votes, in the Senate, upon Bamen's, in which the party voted in solid phalanx, they come before the peo ple, and talk of their desires for an honest political apportionment of their ef forts to promote such an end, and justify their secession from the House their annihilation of the Legislature authori ty, upon the mere engrossment in One House of a bill, which by their standard, worked no greater injustice in the higs than they sought to work to the democracy of the state. And this, let it be borne in mind, is all based upon the rule which they have laid down; the calculations are all predicated upon the vote of 11 It, which the democracy averred then, as they contend now, was so coupled with pipe laying and fraud, as to furnish no fair criterion of the political character of the State. This averment the federalists admit; for they are not willing to trust even the vote for Governor, in forming congressional districts for the State. What is the result of an abandonment of that test? Why, as you reduce the whig strength, for the frauds by which they so largely increased it, you approximate the democratic bill more nearly to the standard, and increase the disparity the excess claimed in all the federal propositions.

So far as the boundary is doubtful, that doubt can only be removed by some act of Congress, to which the assent of the State of Texas may be necessary, or by some appropriate mode of legal adjudication, but in the meantime if disturbances or collision arise or should be threatened, it is absolutely incumbent on the Executive Government, however painful the duty, to take care that the laws be faithfully maintained; and he can regard only the actual state of things as it existed at the date of the treaty, and is bound to protect all inhabitants who were then established, and who now remain north and east of the line of demarcation, in the full enjoyment of their liberty and property according to the provisions of the 9th article of the treaty; in other words, all must be now regarded as New Mexico which was possessed and occupied as New Mexico by citizens of Mexico at the date of the treaty, until a definite line of boundary shall be established by competent authority. This assertion of duty to protect the people of New Mexico from threatened violence or from seizure, to be carried into Texas for trial for alleged offences against Texan laws, does not at all include any claim or power on the part of the Executive to establish any civil or military government within that Territory.

In 1815 they had amounted only to about $40,000, and for 1842 it is proposed to appropriate $475,000. Even the additional expenditures which would be incurred in consequence of the Bankrupt Law could not require so enormous a sum if the courts did a day's work in a day. The practice of merely opening and closing the courts, without performing any judicial functions, or of sitting merely two or three hours, and then fully paying the officers, witnesses, &c. caused, in his opinion, the astounding growth of the expenses of the United States Courts. Hoping that attention being called to the abuse would superinduce its correction, he moved a reduction of the appropriation to a sum, which he deemed amply sufficient for the judiciary under proper attention to duty. Mr. Adams asked if the chairman of the Committee of Ways and Means had inquired what charges had been made by the District Attorney and Marshal of Connecticut for the expenses incurred on account of the Amistad negroes. The expenditures on this account he conceived would make a large proportion of the amount paid for the judiciary. Mr. Fillmore had made no such inquiry. The debate on Mr. Ingersoll's amendment was continued by Messrs. Cooper of Georgia, Barnard, and by the mover.

I have a good opportunity of knowing the feelings of the people, and my opinion is that we are farther off from peace than ever. Another letter writer says: Now we are tranquil, but there is a sad tranquility, as we fear new disturbances. — Our precarious situation will not change for the better until peace be concluded or the Americans send about 15,000 more men. The army of invasion is really small, for figure to yourself 7,000 men in a city of upwards of 200,000 hostile souls, and in Puebla less than 1,500 to maintain a population of more than 50,000. This is really too little. The Mexican Congress was to meet in Queretaro on the fifth of October. It was not supposed that they would agree upon anything. Herrera, with 4,000 men, and Gomez Farias were at Queretaro. Parades is supposed to be in Guadalajara. Not only was Gen. Rea at Puebla—but Santa Anna also. His force, after its junction with that of Gen. Rea, amounted to 30,000 men, and it was quartered in the convent of St. Carmen. The Americans under the command of Col. Childs, were strongly fortified in a convent of the same city [the Mexican correspondent before alluded to, says:—“Gov. Childs has fortified himself on the heights of Loretto, which commands the city”] awaiting the arrival of reinforcements, in the utmost tranquility. At intervals bombs were thrown into the city. Such was the report in Vera Cruz on the 2d inst.

In the mean time, this is a reason with me for not desiring the annexation of Texas to this Union, because, if we go to war for that annexation, I entertain serious apprehensions that this will become a practical question. If we shall go to war with Mexico, and, which necessarily follows, go to war with Great Britain, to annex Texas to this Union, the day is not remote when, in one of the slave States, an invading army from abroad may be combined with an insurrection of the slaves and with a civil war, and the danger still further heightened by an irruption of that whole body of Indians, whom you have accumulated and compressed together as if for the very purpose of organizing them for a hostile movement upon our frontier. I put this possibility to the Representatives of the Southern States as presenting a most serious and painful consideration to my mind. The gentleman from Virginia makes very light of this. He talks to us about the star of Texas, which he calls the “lone star of liberty.” Liberty and Texas! The star of Liberty illumined by the radiance of slavery restored! Yes, that is the star which is to lead us on to victory.

The medicine. Dr. A H. Marriner, of Tarboro, North Carolina, writes under letter date of Feb 11, 1876, that he has used Wild Cherry, in his practice the last eight or nine months, and considers it the best preparation of the kind he ever saw, and knows of none so deserving of public patronage. Dr. Wm. A Shaw, of Washington, N. C., writes under date of May 1, 1876, as follows: "I have heard of many cases of decided benefit from its use, especially in Asthma and Chronic Cough, of spasmodic diameter. I have used the wild cherry a great deal in practice, and with marked good results in those cases of great nervous irritability and irregularity, in which physical medicines fail, of wild cherry, is in, and uniform." Medical men are justly distrustful of patent medicines in general, and candidor must discriminate between quackish humbugs and nostrums and those medicines which have proved salutary, and in many attested cases curative. Dr. Hoffman, Lebanon, Pa., cured a child of asthma with it, after he had declared he could do no more with his medicine, and the child must die. Dr. Fredejoh.cil'Samfordic, says he cured liver complaint of four years' standing, that would not yield to the usual remedies. Abraham Skillman, M.D., of Windbrook, N. J.

Most of those present at the 3 o’clock meeting moved in a body to Kensington, at the corner of Second and Master streets, and proceeded to organize a meeting, but they had scarcely nailed up their flag—the same that is spoken of above—when a number of boys made an attack upon the Hibernia hose house. A number of shots were then fired from houses in the vicinity toward the meeting, and the Native Americans, after dispersing for a moment, rallied and attacked the hose house, took out the carriage, ran the hose off the reel, and then broke the apparatus up. An old engine, the property of the Washington hose company, which was also in the house, was broken up likewise. The Native Americans then (between 4 and 5 o’clock) took possession of the market house, and for the next three hours, the shots were frequent from the houses in its western vicinity. Men were seen lying upon the roofs of a row of houses fronting the market; and in every place of concealment near the same place, others were occasionally discovered. About five o’clock another attack was made upon the hose house, and a large new bell found in it was brought out into the open square and shattered to pieces.

Indeed, there is as much difference between the appearances of the two places, and the relative civilization of each, as between a town of New-England, and one of Barbary. The huts of the islanders are built of cane; open at the sides for a free circulation of air, and thatched only on the roofs. A screen of tapa or mats protects the dwellers from the weather. The floors are mostly of mother earth, damp and cold. On that account they pay more attention to making rude bedsteads and seats than the Hawaiians, but the best of their houses which I saw, would not compare, in neatness or comfort, with the generality of those of the latter. It was evident, however, that improvements were making even here. Papeete boasts a good hotel, with its usual appurtenances of bowling alleys, and a billiard-room. Business is rapidly increasing, owing mainly to its having become a very general resort for whalers for these few years past. The chief trade is with Sydney. Sugar, but in small quantity, is the chief export. But no criterion of the capabilities of the soil can be formed from its present productiveness. Notwithstanding all the benevolent labors which have been expended upon the Tahitians, it must be confessed, that as a body, they continue corrupt and indolent.

Willi branches in an expanding through the States, both south and west, have been fostered by the government, and a wealthy gentleman of the city has been heard to say, "if he were superstitious Mars & Co. would purchase the charter, or take up the stock, and 'go ahead'." We cannot afford to jest! But what will the abolitionists say to our starting drills? The exports of the cotton crop, and our ships would be at the wharves, our factories would be in motion, and the commerce Two millions of dollars. We also correspond with will be developed, and the wealth of the nation. But it must not be forgotten, that the wheel of fortune will continue to revolve, and 'what is the nation's gain today, may be her loss tomorrow.' We have already seen the desolation of our country, and our ships lying idle at the wharves, our factories silent, and the commerce of the country at a stand-still, the cotton crop from the bosoms of our children, and swarms of emigrants crowding the streets. Indeed, on the trade of England alone, in cotton, thirty millions of dollars are annually given.

The indefatigable President of the Milwaukee and Mississippi Rail Road Company, returned from the East, a day or two since, with propositions of a very favorable character, from responsible houses in New York, to furnish the rails, locomotives, etc., for the entire line of road from this city to Whitewater, 55 miles. These propositions are easily within the reach of the Company, with such aid as our city can, and we doubt not will lend, and with the cordial and concurrent action of the country. If they shall be accepted on our part, the road will be completely stocked to Whitewater, and in full operation to Waukesha this fall. Its construction thus far ensures its speedy completion to the Mississippi. The enterprise is one of great moment to our whole state, and of vital importance to Milwaukee. The question of its success or failure rests at present with our city. To such a question there can be but one decision, and that heartily favorable to the prosecution and completion of the great work. We understand that a public meeting is to be held on Friday evening with a view of submitting the matter to the public judgment. We hope to see on that occasion a spirit manifested which will put the success of the enterprise beyond a peradventure. —Milwaukee Sentinel. A very pleasing meeting took place a few days since at Indianapolis. Some weeks ago.

From Key West.—Letters from Key West, received at Charleston by the Isabel, state that General Gonzales, Col. Pickett, Col. O’Hara, and Maj. Hawkins, and Capt. Lewis, of the steamboat Creole, engaged in the recent expedition to Cuba, have been arrested by the U.S. Marshal, under the law prohibiting the fitting out or setting on foot armed expeditions against a foreign power at peace with the United States, and are to be sent to this city for trial as soon as circumstances will permit.

Pending the reading, Mr. DICKINSON moved that the reading be suspended, the previous orders postponed, and the Senate resume the consideration of the special order of yesterday. "NOTICES." The Senate then proceeded to the consideration of the special order of the day—the joint resolution notice to Great Britain terminating the joint occupancy of Oregon, and the several amendments offered thereto. Mr. ALLEN resumed and concluded his remarks, which were mainly directed to the maintenance of the position assumed by him yesterday, that Great Britain dare not single-handed go to war with the United States on the subject of Oregon. The honorable senator concluded at 3 o'clock, p.m., having occupied, altogether, three hours and eighteen minutes. A full report of his speech has been prepared by the reporter, and will speedily be published. When Mr. A. resumed his seat, a motion to adjourn was made and adopted. We hear that one or two small purchases have been made at two dollars and fifty cents, which perhaps will be the opening price. The low prices of the barreled pork now on hand and a knowledge of the large number of hogs in the country induce operators to be timid and cautious in their offerings. We think it probable, however, that the first sales at least will not be made at less than two dollars and a half; but, after the nad shall have fully commenced, prices will most likely sink to two dollars and twenty-five cents, or possibly two dollars. A considerable number could, we believe, be sold at the present time at two dollars and twenty-five cents. Madison (Wis.) Banner. Pork. We have been frequently asked the price of pork in this market. It will embrace all the prominent commercial and literary publications of the Union, the leading Reviews and Magazines of Great Britain and France—papers from London, Paris, Liverpool, and Havre, and care will be taken to obtain the best publications relating to Commerce, Agriculture, and the Arts. In addition to these, subscribers will have the use of the Library of the Society, consisting of nearly 8000 vols., which it is anticipated will be largely increased during the coming winter. The Society are satisfied that such an institution will prove of great benefit to our community, that it will open to every trade and profession, sources of information, and authorities for reference, not otherwise to be procured, but at great personal expense. Mobile is almost the only city of any commercial importance in the Union that is without an institution similar to the one now proposed. The citizens of the commercial cities of the North have freely given of their abundance, and have succeeded in the establishment of Lyceums, Reading Rooms, and Libraries that afford to their inhabitants rational amusement, sources of improvement formerly accessible to the wealthy only, and the earnest information of all advances made by other countries in the arts and sciences.

Creeks, Seneca, Iroquois, Osages, Shawnee, Caws, Pawnees, Sioux, Sacs, Foxes, Piankeshaws, Pottawatomies, Wyandots, etc., are to be present, it is said, in order to form a grand confederacy, or to become united as one tribe. The ostensible object of which is said to be to oppose any further emigration of the Whites west, to prevent the erection of any more forts in their country, and never more to part with another foot of their land, either by force or treaty, at the peril of their lives and the total extinction of their race. The Dilantin- inform our correspondent that by this confederacy the Indian tribes will be enabled to bring a force of 40,000 warriors into the field. Ross, the Chief of the Cherokee, is said to be at the head of this affair, and to defray all necessary expenses of the Council. MEXICO AND TEXAS, The war between these two neighboring republics has recommenced in earnest. San ta Anna has followed up the declaration of hostilities contained in his letter to General Hamilton, by prompt and decided action. It has been "a word and a blow" with him, for the very next mail after the one which brought us his threat of an invasion, conveys the intelligence that this threat has been fulfilled. A Mexican army, some 15,000 strong, is in full march for the capital of Texas. The frontier posts of Antonio and Goliad have already fallen before the invaders.

CHAPTER I. OF THE DUTIES, LIABILITIES, AND COMPENSATION OF MARSHALS. Sec. 1. Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, That the marshals of the several districts of the United States, including the District of Columbia and the Territories, are hereby required respectively to cause all the inhabitants so enumerated, and to collect all the other statistical information within their respective districts, in the manner provided for in this act, and specified in the instructions which shall be given by the Secretary of the In terior, and in the tables annexed, and to return the same to the said Secretary, on or before the first day of November next ensuing, omitting from the enumeration of the inhabitants Indians not taxed; also, at the discretion of said Secretary, any part or all the statistics of the Territories ex cept those of population: Provided, however, And if the time assigned for making the returns shall prove inadequate for the Territories, the said Sec retary may extend the same: Provided further, If there be any district or Territory of the United States in which there is no marshal of the United States, the President shall appoint some suitable person to discharge the duties assigned by this act to marshals. The political parties will make a desperate effort to save themselves from the destruction their corruption has brought upon them; and the efforts of the abolitionists to maintain and enforce the right should be proportionately great. Arrangements should be made at the coming meeting to continue the anti-slavery agitation by the lips of the living speaker, and to extend more widely the circulation of the Society's paper The Anti-Slavery Bugle. Let none of the friends who can be there, absent themselves for any light cause, for the presence of all, and the counsel of all is desirable. Besides the friends of Liberty in the West who will be present on the occasion, Henry C. Wright and Chas. C. Burleigh are expected to be in attendance, and perhaps other representatives of the East. Whatever may be thought of the necessity of the Church of the present day propagating its religion in Pagan lands, none can deny that the Missionary enterprise has developed some of the noblest traits of character that adorn humanity. Catholics and Protestants have gone forth to preach their peculiar views of the requirements of the Gospel, and have manifested a devotion and self-sacrificing spirit worthy of emulation.

The editor of the American, we imagine is only “blowing for the boys.” It must be confessed that the editor of the American disposes of our prominent changes at Pittsburg, in a very summary manner and a very satisfactory manner too, for such as are willing to take his ipse dixit. We know however, that the men alluded to are not all liberty men, but a majority of them are laboring for Polk and Dallas. Senator Archer’s letter on the subject of Nativeism, will be found on the outside of today’s paper. We ask every naturalized citizen, and indeed every citizen of liberal mind to read it. Senator Archer is a prominent Whig of Virginia, and he tells distinctly what he will do should his party come into power. But whatever Senator Archer may strive to do, and however he may expect to be sustained by his party, we do not believe that his sentiments are those of the mass of the party.

I am glad to see, by the following, that there are plenty of the genuine "little shining straight-backed fellows" (as Jimmy Morrison described them) still left:  From Lastres, a province of Asturias, I've learned that so great a number of sardines have entered the bay at that place, that twenty-two lighters, belonging to the port, and as many more from Gijon and Candas, have been used to carry off the innumerable quantities of this fish. In fact, almost every day a quarter of a million of sardines entered the port, and some days twice that number; so that neither the extraordinary rivalry of penquetos, argoyanos, and other craft, which were employed in carrying them off, pickled and fresh, nor the great pains taken to catch the fish at Santander, Bilbao, and San Sebastian, could seriously diminish their prodigious abundance. Latest from Washington.—We have been favored with the perusal of a letter from a distinguished gentleman at Washington, dated 11th inst. We make the following extract:— “The bank bill, as you will have seen, is before the President. We look with strong hopes for a veto—many are sanguine in their expectations—I am not so much so. Yet, I think we shall have a veto—and if we should, it will complete the prostration of federal whiggery already commenced. “The news from Indiana is cheering. The statement yesterday was that we had gained nineteen members in twenty-one counties heard from. “If a veto comes you shall hear from me by the first mail, if it be but a line.”

It is in view of this natural feeling of the heart that the system of leasing is so universally unpopular, and it is from the lack of facilities in acquiring such a home that many have left the Islands. Had the three hundred men who have left for California held each one hundred acres of land, we question much if many of them would have gone. We understand that a foreign house of great industry and wealth are preparing to sell off all their property and leave the Islands, alleging as a reason, that they can procure no properties on the Islands in which to make a safe and profitable investment for their children. This is a singular reason, and, if true, we cannot blame them for going elsewhere. It is a melancholy fact, that innumerable obstacles still exist in the way of transferring real estate. Of the law respecting foreigners holding real estate we have before given our views. It appears to have been framed in order to guard against some fancied danger, but has operated only as a clog to advancement, and will, if persisted in, multiply troubles. The emigration of so many foreigners cannot but be a source of regret to His Majesty and all who are true friends of the nation, while the rich lands of the Kingdom lie waste and uncultivated. The present is a crisis big with the fate of the Islands.

If we look into the condition of many of the States when the constitution was framed, we will find it could never have been adopted had it forbidden the acquisition of the only outlet of all the products of the West. The waters of western Maryland, and of western New York, commingle with those of the Ohio and Mississippi. There stood Pittsburgh at the head of the Ohio; and one-third of Pennsylvania is intersected by streams which water a part of the great valley. Virginia then included Kentucky; three-fourths of her territory was within the great valley, and the Ohio and Mississippi itself were its boundary for more than a thousand miles. North-Carolina then included Tennessee, and was bounded for hundreds of miles by the river Mississippi; and Georgia then embraced Alabama and Mississippi, and was not only bounded for several hundred miles by the great river, but advanced to within a few miles of the city of New Orleans. Is it possible that all these States, in forming the constitution, could have intended to prohibit forever the acquisition of the mouth of the Mississippi, then in the hands of a hostile and despotic foreign power?

Lucy was soon transformed into the most gentle creature I ever saw - her trim person was set out in a neat calico frock, her feet, which I thought rather out of taste, had crept into a modest, mincing pair of moroccos - her face so meek and winning that you would suppose passion never deformed it. Evening and pleasure came on apace, we had a rousing fire. I went out into the yard to hear the animating music of the sleigh bells as the guests came in. A young spark here bantered me for a trade; he had all the self-confidence of one of the quick-made men I have told you of. The first crop of beard had not been reaped from his face, though he looked as if he had been lately cradled. "Stranger, I just lack four bits, to pay my ball expenses. I’ve been catching Prairie chickens for Squire Wisacre, but lack four bus yet. Will you buy my razor? Ann says I’ve no more beard than the inside of her hand; I want enough to pay my expenses.” You sicken me, Master Spark, look, and there’s the heel of your pumps sticking out of your coat pocket. I'd rather help you to buy a spelling book. I came to this country to set good examples, young man." How insulting to challenge this quick proceeding... Master Spark's eyes looked like two glowworms. "Stranger, you haven’t."

About seventy names from the ranks of our Dayton Democrats are already on a list to invite them here for that purpose. There are many in our city who dare not come out. They are convinced of their party folly in ultra Bank opposition, but have not moral courage sufficient to declare it. The produce market is fairly supplied but the demand for most articles is inactive. The Cotton trade is languid at fair prices. Cash abundant at 61. Great business is exhibited in Breadstuffs, and higher prices have been paid. In cured provisions there is a moderate business, with steady prices. Advices from the manufacturing districts are unsatisfactory. COTTON. The Committee's quotations for fair Cotton remain unaltered. For the present, there appears to be a stop to an advance in prices, nor is it likely that a further rise will take place unless encouraging accounts from India should be received. SUMMARY.
