We have no doubt that the ghost of the United States Bank is the most extraordinary sight that ever "revisited the glimpses of the moon." The ghost evoked by the Witch of Endor was a remarkable one, and that of poor Banquo, which frightened the shivering Macbeth so awfully, was another singular apparition; but the ghost of the dead and buried "monster," the monster which Gen. Jackson slew, is by far more extraordinary than either. It haunts every household in the nation from Mr. Benton up and down, from morn to eve, from eve to dewy morn, without ceasing. It is continually shrieking in their ears that it will be revenged on them for slaughtering it so cruelly. It comes before every mother's son of them at the "witching hour" of midnight, as Hector's ghost did before the wakeful Achilles, foretelling the doom of their party. We advise the Locos, on retiring at night, to take enough laudanum to ensure soundness to their slumbers, and then, if the grim ghost of the bank should stalk into their rooms and tweak their sweet noses, they will happily be beyond awaking. In the course of only two or three years after this most deservedly popular remedy had introduced itself into the sick chamber, and its fruits become widely known, up springs several others into the market and with a view to give currency to their new inventions, they take the name of Wild Cherry to add to their patched up nostrums, thinking to borrow a reputation from that already established and to help out still further with the scheme of deception, by one of the vendors the name of Dr. Wistar is attached making it appear that this popular and highly respected Physician was the author of this preparation: such is not the fact. Another individual comes on with what he calls the Syrup of Wild Cherry, clinging still closer to the original name, that individuals might even be deceived, when they went to purchase, if the name of Dr. Swayne was not always kept in view. Those who apply for these latter day preparations of Wild Cherry, let me tell them, are tampering with their constitutions, and taking draughts from the hands of inexperience, which they would have to repent, on some future day, had I not put them on their guard.

While the hopes of our adversaries are ebbing, our own are at high tide; and with united ranks we shall next August storm and carry the I had almost said the strong hold of Whiggery. Here in the West is where the danger lies. Give us a man whom we know we can trust, and we will rally around him and bear him on to victory. David S. Reid we do know, and we are not afraid to trust him. I do not expect many, if any of our Western counties will be represented in Convention; but I have seen and talked with prominent and well-informed Democrats in several counties, and all are unanimous that Mr. Reid would make a better run than any other man. If we could be fairly represented in Convention, I should not trouble you with this communication; and my only design in making it is that the Democracy in other portions of the State may know our wishes and compare them with their own. If our first choice is not the choice of the Convention, then we are willing to concede our preferences and unite on the man offered us, provided he be a true Democrat, embodying the principles of his party. But with David S. Reid to bear our banner, we feel assured that we can "roll down" some Democratic thunder from the mountains that will carry dismay into the Whig ranks. A WESTERN DEMOCRAT.

A medical practitioner in Paris, states that in two instances of approaching dissolution by inhalation of Chloroform, he recalled life by thrusting two fingers deep into the throat, down to the larynx and esophagus, a sudden movement of expiration followed, and recovery took place.  Lola Montes, that masculine female, who has made so much noise in Europe, upset one throne and numberless heads, was at the last accounts in a most forlorn condition and furious mood. Her lover the youthful Heald, after having made several unsuccessful efforts at escape, succeeded on Christmas day in giving her the dodge. They were in Spain. She pursued him and sent forth various agents after him. But all in vain. Heald had secreted himself in an English steamer, and has arrived safely in London. Mrs. Partington on Education. "For my part I can't perceive what on earth education is coming to. When I was young, if a girl only understood the rules of subtraction, provision, multiplying, replenishing, and the common denominator, and knew all about the rivers and their obituaries, the covenants and dormitories, the provinces and the umpires, they had education enough. But now they have to study botany, Algebra, and have to demonstrate suppositions about sycophants, of circuses, tangents and Diagonals of pa rallelograms, to say nothing about the ox hides, cowsticks, and abstruse triangles." And the old lady was so confused with the technical names that she was compelled to stop. Napoleon's Tomb.

In the older States, and in many favorable locations in the new, lands are sold for from 50 to $100 per acre, and improved farms for $200 to $500 per acre; and none of these will yield a greater net revenue than lands in this kingdom, which can now be bought for from $5 to $20 per acre. If we are incorrect in the above statement, we should be happy to be corrected, as our belief is founded less on personal knowledge than on information from others. But as we have it from good authority, we shall adhere to the opinion, until corrected by indubitable evidence. If, then, lands in Louisiana, and other sugar-growing States, are worth $100 per acre, and improved estates from $200 to $500, most certainly lands in this kingdom, capable of producing the same crops, are not exorbitantly high at from $5 to $20. We confess that the wheat and corn-growing lands of the world are the best adapted to men of small means, who have their own hands merely to rely upon, without capital, but we are equally clear in the opinion that.

The corrected paragraph: He gave one triennial acceptance for 10,000 pounds, and received two or 200 pounds for them; he purchased a dressing case to pawn, and his friends pocketed the advance on the pledge; he takes a picture off a dealer's hands at a nominal value of 1,000 which turns out to be worth 20. It is not merely the money he fancies he holds that proves un substantial; the luxuries purchased with this shadow come turn out to be equally unreal. He lives in a world of appearances which have no reality or mocking shadows of pleasure which he cannot taste. And this tantalizing condition will be Lord Huntingtower's throughout life. A large portion of his mimical debts are declared to be not binding, but enough remain behind to absorb the property he may hereafter become heir to. Colonel Copeland too is a Tantalus. Lord Huntingtower's acceptances enable him to raise a part of the sum they were ostensibly worth, but it was all needed to meet claims that were falling due. These had accumulated to such an amount by allowing comparative small debts to be called larger in order to obtain a prolonged term of payment. A plausible man was the Colonel inventive in devising ways and means to meet financial difficulties; but in proportion as the sum he raised increased, the claims to be liquidated by him increased also. He realized nothing.

The House of Representatives has again adopted the one-hour rule, which will greatly expedite business for the remainder of the session. The practice into which so many of the members had got of speaking not for the purpose, or with any expectation, of influencing the votes of members in the House, but for the purpose exclusively of influencing the elections at home, led to the useless consumption of so much of the time of the House that this or some other remedy became indispensable. During a recent visit to the principal northern cities, nothing which we saw or heard seems to us more remarkable than the wonderful progress of the Temperance reformation. In the city of New York, the first signature to the Washington pledge was made in March 1841. At that time the number of licensed houses in the city for retailing spirituous liquors was nearly 3000. It is now less than 1300 and a license is never refused when applied for.

Letters from Cuba state that the island is in the greatest commotion, but make not the slightest mention of insurrectionary movements there, except in the single instance in which the Colonel and several officers of one of the regiments were murdered. The Captain General was using the most energetic measures to suppress all attempts at rebellion that might occur in any part of the island. By an order of the Captain General the Falcon was prohibited from receiving any passengers at Havana. No United States papers were allowed to pass through the Post Office. The commander of the sloop of war Albany, now at Round Island, has been requested by the consul at Havana to proceed there immediately. California. —The population of California, according to the St. Louis Union, will be 100,000 by the time the emigrants now on their way will all reach there. The Placer Times estimated it at 60,000, but in that estimate it only allowed 8,000 for overland emigration, while the Union says the number going by the way of Independence, St. Joseph, and Council Bluffs, was by a very accurate estimate at those points, made at 35,000. But great numbers went also by the Arkansas river and Santa Fe. So that the whole population by the 1st of October must be set down at 90,000.

Our readers are aware that the people of New York have just voted in favor of a Convention to reform the State's Constitution. We have already shown what bold ground the Democrats of New York city have taken against a movement to be made in that Convention, to strike off all legal impediments in the way of Negro suffrage, and to allow the Negroes full and equal rights in their elections.

"Talk to me of a man eating a peck of dirt in a lifetime," said he, "why I have only been swallowing the sediment for a month, and am already chuck full of sandbars, snags, rafts, islands, and sunken flat-boats, and was never healthier in my life."

Accounts from Turin state that the Chamber of Deputies will grant the indemnity of 75,000,000 livres demanded by the government of Austria. The Emperor of Austria has extended to the end of September the amnesty hitherto offered to his Lombard and Venetian subjects, and also to deserters from the Austrian army. The Austrian government, in return for the services rendered by it to the Pope and the Dukes of Parma and Modena, has required that the navigation of the Po shall be free. The governments of Parma and Modena have consented to the demand of the Austrian government, and it is not doubted but that the Pope will withdraw his opposition. New York, Sept. 7. Southern Whigs have any positive reason to hope for anything from General Taylor's veto. We are satisfied, as we have all along said, that he will waive the exercise of the veto. Else, why was the first article written? Why did he put forth as the only article of the creed, his virtual determination not to exercise? In his message, he refused to say one word in behalf of the rights of the South, to sacrifice the right of the South to party confidence in his favor. He will surely disappoint them and how then can they excuse themselves to their indignant constituents? It is our most anxious desire that this whole question should now be divested of all party connections. If we know ourselves, if we know our party, it is their wish, as is our own. We most solemnly repudiate every possible motive to promote our party, or to injure General Taylor, by this movement. But we have no doubt. "For a long time, we have been convinced, that there is no breed of any in this country to the cultivation of Tea. “Hear me swear,” said Augustus—“by yonder moon, I swear that you shall have half a dozen catfish for breakfast tomorrow; let the oath be registered!” CHAPTER VII. The Perjury. "Take them—I implore thee, take them.” Blithely the young wife rose on the morrow, to a breakfast which she deemed would be happy—Augustus came—in his right hand he held a string of fish; but were they catfish? Stand aghast, ye heavens, when you hear it; they were perch! Julia caught one glance—her delicate system could not stand the shock. 'Perch?' she wildly cried, and sank lifeless upon the ground. CHAPTER VIII. The Denouement. "Man’s Inhumanity to woman makes countless thousands mourn.” Sadly they bore her to her long home; flowers were strewed over her grave, and the face of all her acquaintances wore a lugubrious aspect. But there was one whom that sad sight struck down to the earth—it was the perjured Augustus. Wildly he rushed forth, and sought consolation in the bowl— it was not there— and goaded to frenzy, one day he walked calmly down to the Navy-yard, and enlisted in the marines.

The Thorn plant should be of a proper size before they are planted, so that when they are leaned at an angle of 45 degrees, the main branch should tip off the stake and be cut even therewith. The brush or limbs should be tied closely with a stalk in order to put out from the leaning stalk, and grow upwards above the stake, and form a perfect live fence. It is important to cut off the sprouts that come from the cut at the ground. Cut them off near the ground unless the edge is weak and needs them, but do not let them grow. The sap from the roots will nourish and finally kill them. We must depend upon the stalk for the fence the sap must pass through them and grow up healthily. By this way, the hedge is all the time becoming stronger with the trifling expense of occasional work, yearly for each one hundred rods. The price for making a hedge per rod is $1.50 including cutting, setting, and replanting except trimming. Now is not this the cheapest fence together considering its durability, we can make? It may be asked, is not the Osage Orange better?

The Nation's Government has faithfully paid every dollar of the revolutionary debts and the debts of the last war, amounting to upwards of five hundred millions, all incurred in defence of human rights against European tyrannies; none of the States are delinquent, and only two or three of the States are so from present inability, and not inclination. But what is the European side of the account? Fraud, repudiation, for times almost innumerable. England once refused to pay more than a fourth of a national debt, and has frequently repudiated the stipulated interest. France annihilated the whole debt due at the commencement of the revolution of 1789, and an enormous amount contracted afterwards. Austria has repudiated nearly a thousand millions, and Russia and Denmark have followed the fraudulent example. Yet these Europeans, stained with the guilt of enormous debt, contracted for criminal wars, and the additional guilt of beheading the leaders, taunt us with dishonesty for the delay of the interest, which becomes necessary under our arrangements for full and honest payment! Integrity. We quietly take such imputations from Europeans.—Phila. Ledger. Cass cannot, from numerous reasons—among which I will only mention two, viz: his avowing himself a Harrison man, and his determination not to make any removals on succeeding to the Presidency, expressed in a recent letter to one of his friends—be the candidate of the Democratic party. Gen. Cass, will, in this case, be a most formidable aspirant to the Presidency, and it is possible that Mr. Webster himself will go for him. More anon!” There is as much probability that the Whigs will take Mr. Van Buren as their candidate for the Presidency as that they will take Gen. Cass; and the idea that Mr. Clay and his friends give so much importance to the result of the New York State election at this time, (so remote from the Presidential election,) as that he will withdraw himself as a candidate for Chief Magistrate at that election, or that his friends will consent to his taking that course, is absurd, too silly to be seriously repeated except by the Locofocos.

These principles have been recognized for many years by Mexico, and by this republic; and it is absurd in Mexico now to attempt to recall her unequivocal assent to these doctrines, and ask to be permitted to change the well-settled law of nations, and oppose the reannexation of Texas. It is an admitted principle of the law of nations that every sovereignty may cede the whole or any part of their territory, unless restrained by some constitutional in-tertiary, or by the same sovereign power which imposed the limitation. There is, however, no such limitation in the constitution of Texas, which is a single central government, with the same authority to make the cession, as appertains to France or Spain, in the transfer of Louisiana or Florida.

Mount Hecla.—The latest advices from Iceland, state that the eruption of Mount Hecla continued with great violence. The lava ran from the Southwest crater in a large stream, and had already covered a space of three miles, and heaped up a mass in a plain at the foot of the mountain, thirty or forty cubits in height. No farms had yet been destroyed by the lava, but the ashes which had fallen into the meadows had already exercised most pernicious effects upon the cattle, especially the cows. In some districts many have perished, much uneasiness is felt at the duration of the eruptions, as the lava when it receives fresh supplies, always approaches nearer the cultivated lands. Evils of Rum Drinking.—On Thursday morning, the 28th ult., at Philadelphia, three persons were found dead by intemperance, as follows: William Crawford, aged 44, suffocated in a lime kiln; John Paterson, aged 35 years, and Patrick McCullough, 30 years of age, died from the effects of liquor. The American Arctic Expedition.—The expedition so nobly conceived by Mr. Henry Grinnell, of New York, for the rescue of Sir John Franklin and his brave men from a horrible death, is going forward with energy. Two vessels have been procured for the purpose, one of 144 tons, the other of 91 tons, and are fitting up for their perilous enterprise, by such additions to their strength and other appliances for the safety and comfort of officers and crew as the case admits of. John Allyn, and died in 1725, aged 61. Wm. S. PORTER. MACCONNELL AND GREELEY. MacConnell, the drunken member of Congress, is a perfect half horse and half alligator. We alluded to his discreditable course a few days since. His disgraceful conduct was once spoken of in just terms of reprehension, in the Tribune. Mac was very indignant at this liberty taken with him, and swore he would whip Greeley the first time he saw him. Soon after, Mr. Greeley was in Washington, and was pointed out to him; whereupon, throwing himself in his way, and facing him boldly, he asked if his name was Greeley? "Yes," was the reply. "The editor of the Tribune?" "Yes." "Well, then, I'm going to have satisfaction out of you. You said I was a drunkard, a blackguard, and a disgrace to the House." "Yes, Mac, I did say so, and you know it is true—you know you are drunk now." "That’s a fact," said Mac. "And you know, Mac," said Greeley, "that you have talents enough to make a respectable man."

A little incident occurred at Niagara Falls last October which I intended to have sent you at the time, but omitted to do so, and perhaps it is not too late yet, being a matter of fact. A certain gentleman who was on a visit to our villa while rambling about the Falls, frequently made use of his quizzing glass, holding it up before an American lady's face, in rather an awkward manner, as most cockneys do. While thus gazing, a gentleman from Cleveland happening to come near him remarked: "Sir, you do not understand the use of that instrument you have stuck before your eye." This very much offended the Englishman, who, turning around very angrily, said, "What's that you say, sir?" "I say you do not understand the use of that instrument you hold before your face." The Englishman immediately thrust his hand into his pocket, taking his card, and handed it to the Cleveland who took it and after reading it over aloud, very coolly replied, "Ah? I suppose this is your name?" "Yes, that's my name."

The Ninth Regiment.—From conversation with some of the officers of the Ninth Regiment, we are satisfied that many of them have reason to complain against the usage of the Government. Many of them have sacrificed all they have for the benefit of their companies, besides enduring great hardships, without any prospect of being repaid. The whole war from beginning to end has been one series of outrages, not only upon the Mexicans, but upon our gallant officers and men who have been engaged in it; outrages for which the people will hold those in power responsible. Mr. Du Pont, of Florida, offered a resolution declaring that it is not expedient for this convention to declare in advance what particular measures passed by Congress would call for forcible resistance, but that this matter should be left to each State. Mr. McClelland, of Florida, offered a series of resolutions in regard to the abolishing of the slave trade in the District of Columbia, declaring that such a measure would be a violation of the spirit of this Government. Mr. Pearson, of Florida, offered a resolution declaring that no portion of this Government excels the South in devotion to the Union, but she will resist further aggressions on the part of the North at all hazards. Mr. W. H. Polk, of Tennessee, offered a resolution as an amendment of the Alabama resolutions yesterday, declaring in favor of the compromise of the Committee of Thirteen in the Senate. Mr. Campbell, of Alabama, moved to lay the amendment on the table. Mr. Polk spoke eloquently and forcibly in favor of his amendment. After some further debate, Mr. Polk withdrew his motion to amend the Alabama resolutions, and offered it as a separate proposition. All the above resolutions were referred to the Committee on Business. The Committee on Resolutions did not report this morning. The Committee adjourned till 10 o’clock tomorrow morning. There was much less excitement manifested than yesterday or Tuesday. Nashville, Friday, June 7.

He has taken entire stories written by his contemporaries, and given them to the world as his sole work. All the plays, save four, which he has sold to the theatres, are proved to be from the pens of his collaborators, and the four, which are Henri III., Christine, Charles VII., and Don Juan de Marana are cooked up from numerous authors, whole scenes being taken in some instances, while characters and incidents are appropriated unblushingly and without compunction. The most popular of his novels are here given to the authors who wrote them. Even Monte Cristo, Les Trois Mousquetaires, and Vingt Ans Apres are the work of others, and the pet of the Parisians is left bare indeed. 'Monte Cristo' is in two parts: and these two parts have different authors, P. A. Florentino for the first, and M. Aug. Maquet for the second. From a work published in Paris, in 1837-8, bearing the title Memoirs tires des Archives de la Police de Paris, pour servir a l'histoire de la morale et de la police depuis Louis XIV., jusqu 'a nos jours, par J. P. Proudhon, Archiviste de la Police, Dumas has copied, and 'textuellement', two episodes: namely, Francois Picard, histoire contemporaine, and Lafante de Verteuil, ou un Crime de Famille.

Some use fictitious names, while others use their own; some of these unprin cipled men publish counterfeit certificates, others have induced their brothers and other interested persons to abuse the use of their names, to which they put Esquire, to give them respectability. One makes medicine and puffs it with respectable names procured by using Dr. Townsend’s Sarsa parilla, published them as cured by his study. He also publishes the certificate of a person who styles himself as Dr., who mends shoes, dyes wells, and doctors horses by turns for a livelihood. (From the Independence Express. Nov. 17th.) Overland itlaitfMm Oregon?Arrival of Dr. White, direct from Oregon?Capital Mes sengers to the U.S. Congress?Through Many Days. We had the pleasure on Saturday evening last of taking by the hand our old friend, Dr. Elijah White, Sub-Agent of Indian Affairs for the Territory of Oregon, who had just arrived with h party of only three men, Messrs. Chipman, Brown and Saxton, all claiming to be citizens of Willamette? two of whom, Oras Brown and Charles Saxton, had accompanied him some time previously, on an interesting and important exploring expedition, the re sults of which will soon come before the public, of ficially. They left the beach of the Pacific on the 30th of July, some forty miles from the Umpqua river, and arrived in the Colony about the 10th of August.? London correspondence of the Jour, of Commerce ANIMAL MAGNETISM. A most extraordinary surgical operation has been performed, the particulars of which will be found detailed in a couple of columns of the London Morning Herald of the 20th ult. James Wombell, 42, a laboring man, had suffered for a period of about five years with a painful affection of the left knee joint. He was admitted into the hospital at Wellow, in Nottinghamshire, and it was decided that amputation should take place above the knee joint, and it was accordingly done whilst the patient was under the influence of mesmeric sleep! On the 1st of October, this wonderful operation was performed, as given in the words of the mesmerizer, one Mr. W. Topham, a lawyer of the Middle Temple, London: “I again mesmerized him in four minutes. In a quarter of an hour, I told Mr. W. Squire Wood (the operator) that he might commence. I then brought two fingers of each hand gently in contact with Wombell’s closed eyelids, and there kept them, still further to deepen the sleep. Mr. Wood, after one earnest look at the man, slowly plunged his knife into the center of the outer side of the thigh, directly to the bone, and then made a clear incision round the bone, to the opposite point, on the outside of the thigh. The stillness at this moment was something awful.

Fitcnavao (Mass.) Railroad. This road will be opened in Waltham, six miles from Boston, on Monday next, and omnibus conveyance will be provided for all passengers from Boston to the depot in Charleston at 6 cents each, as a regular charge. The Boston Post says "The engines for this road destined erelong to be extended to Uxbridge, are made by Messrs. Hinkley & Drury of this city, and the first on their contract has been delivered, and generally approved, As the Worcester railroad have concluded to reduce their fare to Waltham, via omnibus, to 20 cents, the rate upon the Waltham line, now opened, will undoubtedly be equally low, to meet this unexpected change of policy on the part of the Worcester, which has hitherto opposed the idea of low fares." The same paper adds the following notice of a Railroad Convention for the extension of this route to Burlington, and we perceive that other Boston papers are all alive on the subject. Will the inhabitants of the northern part of the state and Vermont turn a 'cold shoulder' to their own interests and neglect to advocate the extension of the Concord Railroad by a nearer and much more favorable route? ib, Railroad Convention. The friends of the extension of the Boston and Fitchburg railroad to Brattleborough, Vt., Champlain and Canada, are requested to meet in convention at Brattleboro Tuesday December 6th.

She brings one million dollars in gold dust, and four hundred thousand dollars in the hands of passengers. The accounts from California continue encouraging. The health of the miners is generally good. There had been large arrivals from Europe and the United States. Eighty thousand persons were there. The Constitution (or the Misssissipi) had been adopted. Prodigious Murder. Roe, a young man of very good character, was shot at his house in South Creek district in this county, a few days since, by Moses Ernal, of Craven county. The wife of Roe, to whom he had been married about 10 months, was wakened about 3 o'clock in the morning by a noise at the door. She got up, opened the door, and the moon shining very bright, saw Ernal holding a handkerchief over his face; but in attempting to dodge around the corner of the house the handkerchief fell; and she saw his face and knew him!

Several towns are the licensing board for other several towns, and they may license not more than two persons in each to sell liquors for medicinal, chemical and mechanical purposes only. The selectmen may vacate all licenses, by them granted, when they deem fit. No innkeeper or grocer shall have a right to sell liquors, even for medical pur- poses. Licenses shall be signed by a majority of the selectmen, and recorded in the town clerk's office. A person obtaining a license shall not be required to pay anything therefor. Innkeepers and grocers to be licensed by the selectmen, without any fee, and under the same regulations and restrictions as others. Instead of granting a medical l icense as above named, to one or two persons, the town may, by vote at the March meeting, authorize the selectmen to appoint one or two agents to purchase and sell liquors for medical purposes; said agents to pay into the town treasury all money received for said liquors, and the town shall pay such agents a reasonable compensation for doing the busi- ness. Penalty for selling over twenty gal- lons, fifty dollars—under twenty, twenty dollars.

The Argus publishes a most conclusive and overwhelming refutation of the story, that 47 more votes were thrown for Mr. Fessenden in Auburn, than were counted for him. It is true that 47 votes for Mr. Fessenden on one of the counts, were put down by the clerk and afterwards erased. For they were erased for the purpose of adding 13 other ballots, and then 50 was put down instead of 47. Mr. Littlefield of Auburn has a memorandum of counts, exactly corresponding with that of the clerk. — Both show the erasure of the 47 votes, and the explanation of Mr. Littlefield, sustained by one of the selectmen, and all the circumstances in the case, will satisfy everybody, that they were not lost, but are included in the 60 votes for Mr. Fessenden, put down immediately after wards. Let me ask you, sir, if there is not a manifest inconsistency in orthodox churches, disciplining behavior for horse-racing and dancing, and yet permitting their ministers to defend the barbarous and cruel slavery laws of Greece and Rome! Is it not grossly inconsistent to denounce intemperance or gambling, and yet palliate the infamous African slave trade, to send out missionaries to convert the heathen, and yet defend the worst heathenism the world ever saw; to pray to Almighty God that he would bless our missionaries, and break down the system of caste, which enthralls the millions of India, and yet do all in our power to uphold and perpetuate the tyranny of color, which exists in our own midst! What think you? In your speech at the Presbyterian church, you were pleased to introduce the Apostle Paul, who when standing on Mars-hill, he confronted the most daring opposers of religion - the old Epicureans and Stoics - and yet in that great sermon, with the Athenian slave in full view, he did not condemn slavery. Why, my dear sir, that sermon, rightly understood and practically carried out, would have severed the collar of every manumitted dog that chained beneath the Athenian Portico. It would have transformed every human chattel into a man and brother.

Dry beef in the settlement is at 40 per lb.; at the Placer $1 to $2 per lb.; pork beef and pork, $75 to $100 per bbl.; flour $5 to $7 per bbl.; coffee, sugar and rice 50 cents to $1 per lb. As washing is fifty cents to a dollar a garment, many prefer throwing away their used up clothes to paying the washerwoman; that is, if they intend returning to the settlements soon, where they can purchase more. As to sharing, I have never seen a man at the Placer who had time to perform that operation. They do not work on Sundays, only brush up the tent, blow out the mercury or line black sand from the week’s work. Horses that can travel only one day, and from that to a week, are from $100 to $300. Freight charge by launch owners for three days run, $75 per bbl. Wagoners charge $100 per load, twenty to fifty miles on a good road. Corn, barley, peas, and beans $10 a bushel. Common pistols, any price; powder and lead very dear. I know a physician who, in San Francisco, purchased a common made gold washer at $20 or $30, made of 70 or 80 feet of boards.

The Senate came to order at 9 o’clock. The Governor in the chair. A resolution was received from the House of Representatives appointing a committee to examine and audit the amount of the Quartermaster General, which was concurred in, with the addition of Messrs Lawson and Durfee. The Governor laid before the Senate a letter from Alexander Vattemare, upon the subject of international literary exchanges. A message was received from the House inviting the Senate to join it for the purpose of proceeding in the trial of the docket of private petitions, and the Senate joined accordingly. The Senate continued in the House the whole day, upon the trial of the petition of McNeil. After which, the Senate adjourned. House.—The House met at 9 o’clock. Mr. L Brown after some observation moved that the Committee appointed by the House, relative to the acts of the canvassers of the town of Glocester be discharged from the further consideration of the petition. Mr. Sherman, one of the committee, made some remarks in opposition to the motion, to which Mr. Brown replied. The vote was then taken, and the motion rejected. The two Houses then joined for the purpose of continuing the trial of private petitions.

The afternoon of yesterday's session was taken up by Mr. Benton, in an elaborate speech, on the subject of a Road from the Mississippi river to the Pacific Ocean. He obtained leave to bring in a bill for that object, which was read by its title and referred to the Committee on Military Affairs. The bill contemplates a great National Central Road, from St. Louis to San Francisco, with a branch to the Columbia river. It is to be a railroad, where the character of the country will admit of the construction of such a road, and when that is not the case, it is to be Macadamized or made of such materials as may be most suitable. A proportion of the proceeds of the sales of the public lands of the United States is to constitute the fund out of which the road is to be made. A strip of land a mile wide is to be reserved along the route, and military posts are to be established at various points for its preservation. After disposing of this subject and the transaction of some further unimportant business, the Senate went into Executive Session and then adjourned. A. Norton, A.M., Professor of Mathematics in Delaware College, has just been published by Messrs. Wiley & Putnam, New York. There is no mistaking the signs of the times; the importance of the moral, political, and financial crisis which impedes, is everywhere arresting the attention of the democratic party, and the conviction becomes enforced upon them, that of all our leaders, Mr. Calhoun is the man best qualified to avert the evils with which the frame of our government and of society is threatened - and that he is the pilot who can best brave the approaching storm, and guide the old racked and tempest-tossed republican ship to her safe and accustomed moorings. Our northern counties are earnestly reflecting upon these matters, their old prejudices are being lost sight of - the worth of the man they have long since known, and nowhere has our progress been more rapid, than among the indomitable democracy of the Tennessee Valley. Soon will they respond to the acclamations which South Alabama is uplifting, and when the solemn voice of our State is spoken, every county in our border, every community within our limits, will send back a mingled echo, swelling with the name of "Calhoun." It is not the mere politicians alone who are eager, desirous of being borne into power upon the top of the mighty wave which they see gathering, but the people, the honest-hearted, and intelligent Southern farmers, the plundered and outraged agriculturists, whose indignation is now finding utterance.

I lie House then went into Committee of the Whole, and took up Hill No. 11, authorizing the President to call out 60,000 Volunteers, and to appropriate $10,000,000 of money for their support. The Hill was read in Committee of the Whole when a motion was made that the Committee rise, which was agreed to. Mr. Hnnkerhoff offered a Resolution to end the debate upon the Hill in two hours, which was agreed to, and the House returned to the Committee of the Whole, Mr. Hopkins of Va. in the Chair. Correspondence called for in the House was now read and occupied a long time—nearly all the time given to the debate. The correspondence covered letters between the Executive Department and the officers of the Army and Navy; and also between the officers at Matamoras and the Mexicans at Matamoras. A half hour was given to the discussion only, when Mr. Rimkerhoff, of Ohio, said he presumed that no one would wish to carry on a defensive war alone. It would be a second edition of the Florida war, but far worse in its results.

Railroad Accident. The Camden and Amboy line, which left here at 6 A. M. yesterday, did not reach Philadelphia till a late hour last evening. The detention was caused by some difficulty at the drawbridge over the Rancocus. The creek is considerably swelled in consequence of the rains. The Philadelphia Inquirer thus explains the cause of the detention: While crossing the bridge over the Rancocus Creek, it was discovered that the "draw" was not let completely down. The danger was imminent, as there was not sufficient time to prevent the train from reaching the open draw, such was the headway of the locomotive. The conductor, brakeman, and engineer behaved with the utmost coolness, and contrived to detach the cars just at the very moment that the engine touched the aperture or chasm. Another instant and the locomotive was plunged into the creek, which is broad and deep, and disappeared beneath the waters, the cars remaining, and the numerous passengers saved, as if by a providential forethought and coolness on the part of the individuals we have named from frightful danger, injury, wounds, and death. As soon as intelligence reached Camden, the "States Rights" steamboat was dispatched to Rancocus Creek, for the purpose of bringing away the passengers and baggage. She returned that evening. N. Y. A Severe Rebuke. The Tuscaloosa Observer, the new Democratic paper at the late seat of government in Alabama, administers the following severe but just and merited rebuke to the slanderers of "Rough and Ready:" Two members of the House of Representatives in Congress have been bad enough, or foolish enough, to commence a malignant warfare on a war-worn veteran, who has led our armies to uninterrupted victory through the miasmata and marshes of the Rio Grande. They dare to charge him with inefficiency, when he never fired a shot which did not tell; and with delay.

It is beautifully situated on the rising ground, about 1 ½ miles North of the State House in Newport, facing on the main road, and commands a very extensive view of the harbor and bay. The main building is 40 by 37 feet, and contains 8 rooms, including drawing rooms, and attached, in the rear, is an addition containing the kitchen and wash-room below, and servants’ lodging rooms above,— with a good cellar the whole size of the house. There is also a good coach house and other outbuildings; an excellent well of water, and brick cistern, both connected with the house by lead pipes. The lot consists of 31 acres of good land, handsomely laid out, and having on it a great number of ornamental trees. The house is but three years old, and was built and finished with first rate materials and in modern style. This establishment is admirably calculated for a gentleman’s residence, and if immediate application is made it will be sold at a great bargain—much below the first cost,— or a tenant will be received at a moderate rent. From all parts of the Union we have the most charming prospect of success. In this State all will be right. The democracy of the "Prairie State" are coming up nobly for their principles. Col. C. Lansing and Mr. E. Hollister, presidential electors, are doing good service in northern Illinois. They have taken hold like men laboring in a good cause, and their services have had the most beneficial effect. If Illinois does not give a great increase on her usual democratic majority for Cass and Butler, then put us down as poor hand at guessing the result. Our Candidates. We have been decidedly of the opinion ever since the adjournment of the Baltimore convention, that a stronger ticket than was selected could not have been presented to the American people for their suffrages. Generals Cass and Butler have endeared themselves to every true American by their faithfulness to their country. They have proved their devotion in the halls of legislation and on the field of battle. If ever the democracy had reason to be proud of their ticket, it is now. At least they have one which the true democrat can support cordially and cheerfully.

The original contract for the construction of an Iron Bridge on the Potomac, at the Point of Rocks, has been annulled, and Mr. Abraham Bowers, the constructor of the Bridge at this place, has contracted for the whole work. The superstructure of the Bridge is to be of wood. The Bridge at this place is now passable for everything, the Ferry Boat having ceased running entirely. This announcement will be received with great joy by everyone who has ever suffered the inconvenience and delay at the river from high waters and other causes. Brilliant Succession. Tuesday, the 10th of September, was the anniversary of the victory of Commodore Perry on Lake Erie. Wednesday, the 11th of September, was the anniversary of the victory of McDonough on Lake Champlain. And Thursday, the 12th of September, was the anniversary of the battle which was fought at North Point, and which saved Baltimore from the British troops. There is not an American heart which does not exult over these achievements, performed under the flag of this glorious Union. Union. And Thursday, the 12th of September, was also the day on which was fought the great battle of the Union. On that day the last of the compromise measures was passed, and the Disunionists defeated. ? About 80 public meetings in favor of Temperance are held weekly in the city, and The average number of new signatures to the pledge per night at this time is about 100. We attended one of the meetings. It numbered probably 2000—certainly over 1500, and was one of the most animated meetings we ever saw anywhere; whilst at the same time everything was done decently and in order. In Brooklyn, a town of about 20,000 inhabitants, across what is called East River from the city, the quantity of grain consumed in distilleries eighteen months ago was estimated to equal the total amount consumed in the city of New York, for food by man and beast. Now it is reduced to almost nothing. The distilleries have all been discontinued except two. During our absence we conversed with persons from all parts of the country, and the changes which they represented to have taken place almost everywhere exceeded our expectations almost as much as did those in New York, and in some places even more. The first Washington Temperance Society was formed in Baltimore in April 1840. To its influences are all these changes to be ascribed, and that in the brief space of little more than two years. 'Mrs. Smart,' said Katherine, who saw that something must be done to bring the present state of affairs to as early a conclusion as possible, and had risen and set the tray with some refreshments, 'you have a long way to ride back, and the days are getting very short now; pray make a hearty lunch before you start off.' Mrs. Smart hastened to take Katherine at her word. 'I suppose you haven't got a chicken, Miss,' she, however, inquired almost immediately. 'No, we have not,' said Katherine. 'We have yet only just what we could get into our boxes, coming by the mail.' 'Oh!' proceeded Mrs. Smart, 'you can get anything you want at the township. They have everything at the stores, from a needle to an anchor. He's an old Jew, Miss, that keeps it. Such an ugly old man! I wonder the prisoners some of these nights don't break in and murder him, and take all he's got. But there, it's no use talking, the devil's children will have the devil's luck.'

Thus it will be ready to be placed in a position for deadly action, in a very short time. The cable by which it is to be maneuvered may be at least 5 miles long, so that the balloon at a suitable elevation would leave the ship, or land position, which acts as the retaining point, out of reach of the Castle guns, and under the cover of our own batteries. The man of war balloon, hovering a mile above the Castle like a cloud of destruction, would be entirely out of danger of the enemy guns, since they could not be made to bear on an object immediately above them. The position of the balloon as to height, and distance from the retaining point, could be easily maintained by keeping a proper eye to its ballasting. As it would become lightened by the discharge of shells and torpedoes, an adequate quantity of gas can also be discharged.

He may not care much about that, for it is probably settled that in the next Presidential race (horse-race no longer sir,) he is to ride behind Mr. Clay as candidate for Vice Presidency. What light Southern man is to ride en croupe behind Mr. Webster; what Northern man with Southern principles, or what Southern man with Northern principles, behind Gen. Cass I do not care to inquire. One thing I do know, sir. Only one of the three can be elected, all the five understrappers of that committee will be provided for. What then does Mr. Foote care for Mississippi? About as much as she will henceforth care for him. But General Taylor's plan? Sir, don't talk to me about Gen. Taylor. "What portion have we in David? Neither have we inheritance in the son of Jesse. To your tents oh Israel! Now see to thine own house David." General Taylor will be pretty sure to see to that, and to his Sugar plantation too. Whatever else he neglects, he will spare no pains to prevent anything which may lead to the independence of Cuba, to her admission into this Union, and to the loss of two cents and half in the pound, in the price of his sugar which he must submit to, whenever the sugar of the West Indies is admitted free of duty. To a man like him.

Late from Mexico!  Philadelphia, Oct. 13, 8 o'clock, P. M. The Spanish paper at New Orleans, La Patria, has rumors from the city of Mexico, which are generally discredited, to the effect that the American troops in the city, have had severe conflicts with the Mexicans, and owing to the forces of the latter being superior, the American loss was very severe.  It is said that Gen. Scott withdrew from the city. Santa Anna then advanced from Guadaloupe, with an army of 10,000 fighting men, and the fighting was resumed.  The conflict was going on desperately at the last accounts.  Gen. Rea is also said to have obtained complete possession of Puebla. but the American troops occupied the heights in the neighborhood of the city, a commanding position, and were pouring a destructive fire upon the enemy.

After many eloquent but inflammatory remarks as to what would be the course of the South in a certain contingency, he said: if there was any attempt to abolish slavery in this District, and to interdict it by bill in the Territories, he, for one, was ready for a dissolution of the Union. Nay, if such injustice was to be practiced upon the South, he would glory in a dissolution of the Union. As to the organization of the House, that was of small moment. What did they want with an organization, if the slavery question was not to be settled? [His speech was frequently cheered by Southern men.] Mr. Baker, of Illinois, made a very patriotic and eloquent speech, in which he declared himself to be a Wilmot proviso man. He expressed his sorrow at hearing the remarks that fell from the gentleman from Georgia. He did not believe that that gentleman was in earnest when he said he was ready for a dissolution of the Union. He did not believe that any portion of the South was ready for such an act if all should happen which that gentleman apprehended. The Union, he said, could not be dissolved. That it would not be. That the patriotic masses, North and South, would rise up to prevent it. [The gentleman from Georgia, Mr. Toombs, said they were in earnest, and would teach the North that they were.]

We are requested to announce Mr. Joshua M. Lanesdale as a candidate for Treasurer of Marion county. Marion County Democrat & Press: Please announce Samuel T. Black as a candidate for Treasurer of Marion county, and oblige MANY VOTERS. Congressional Convention, What say the Democrats of the Fifth Congressional District? Shall we have a District Convention? We suggest that each county composing the District at once take measures to appoint delegates to attend such a convention, to nominate a candidate for Congress to be elected by the ardent democracy of the district. We have no choice as to place or time, so far as we are personally concerned. But as near as we can learn, a majority are in favor of holding such convention. At 11:30 a.m. On Saturday the 16th of May. What says the Volunteer and the Examiner? It must not be idle. Every blow struck must tell. Then up and on, every county act at once and meet, send your proceedings to the nearest papers, and have them published. Now is the time to begin, and when warm weather comes, we will both vote and then crow!! Did Marion pledge herself to do as well, if not better, for the nominee, than any other county in the district. Which county accepts the challenge? Oh! Marion, Awake!

Custom-house, Honolulu, May 5, 1849. NOTICE. NOTICE is hereby given to Owners and Captains of Hawaiian Coasting Vessels, that it is their duty to give notice to the Collector of the Port from which they are to sail, of the time of their sailing, and of their destination, in order that the MAILS may be in readiness to deliver to them; and it is also their duty to call for the Mails, before the hour of four o'clock, P. M., on the day of sailing. In case of neglect to give the above notice, and call for the Mails, the license of the vessel is liable to forfeiture. CHAS. R. BISHOP, Collector General of Customs. Honolulu, July 7, 1849. if. 9. [COMMUNICATED.] At a Meeting of the Stockholders of the Bank of Virginia, in the city of Richmond, on Wednesday, January 11th, 1843, on motion of Mr. Tazewell Taylor, of Norfolk, the following resolution was unanimously adopted: Resolved, That the Stockholders of the Bank of Virginia, in general meeting assembled, hereby express to John Brockenbrough, on his retirement from the Presidency of this Institution, their sentiments of confidence in his integrity and of respect for him as a man. Extract from the Proceedings. J. W. PEGRAM, Secy. The Stockholders of the Bank of Virginia have resolved that the salary of President of the Mother Bank shall be reduced to $2,000.

CALHOUN AND BENTON. We don’t know that Mr. Calhoun would thank us for patting his name in conjunction with that of Tom Benton. They “move in different spheres,” Mr. Calhoun says, and we were aware of that before. But at present they are in pretty close quarters, giving each other as fraternal a hug as St. George and the Dragoon—Mr. Calhoun, of course, standing for St. George, and Benton for the “varmint.” All who have read Mr. Calhoun’s reply will agree that it is masterly. Nothing that could come from Calhoun could be common-place. While we cannot always agree with him, we never cease to admire his ingenuity; his knockdown logic; the consciousness and yet clearness of his style; and his marvelous powers of condensation.

Mr. Pond gave notice of a motion for leave to introduce a bill providing for the proper observance of the Sabbath day, for the suppression of gambling, and for other purposes. Mr. Setzer, in pursuance of notice, asked, obtained leave and introduced No. 31, H of R, a bill fixing the time of the annual meeting of the Legislative Assembly, which was read the first time. Mr. Wilkinson, in pursuance of notice, asked, obtained leave, and introduced No. 32, H of R, a bill to locate a Territorial road from Point Douglass to St. Paul, which was read the first time. No. 1, C F, memorial to Congress for an appropriation to construct a military road from Fort Snelling to the mouth of the Sioux river on the Missouri, was read the second time. And on motion of Mr. Babcock, the House resolved itself into the committee of the whole House for the consideration of the said memorial. Mr. Pond in the chair. And after some time passed therein, the committee rose and by their chairman reported the same back to the House without amendments. On motion of Mr. Trask, Ordered, That the said memorial be read a third time tomorrow.

The question of the distribution of the proceeds of the sales of the public lands among the several States, which under other circumstances, was recommended by strong justice and sound public policy, will not come up for the consideration or action of Congress for many years to come, since, by common consent, the proceeds of the land sales are to be applied to the payment of the large national debt which has been created by the late Mexican war. You need not be told, fellow citizens, that the condition of the manufacturing interest, involving the labor and prosperity of the millions engaged in that branch of national industry, is very much depressed and calls for the adoption of a more just and patriotic policy, which, while it supplies the necessities of the General Treasury, shall discriminate in favor of the industry of our own country. It is the high and imperative duty of our government to protect the labor of our own citizens against the ruinous competition of the low-priced, pauper labor of other less favored lands. The tariff of 1846, which was the result of a lamentable error of 1844, is working mischief to all branches of business and to all the great interests of the country.

THE GOLD FEVER IN NEW YORK. “How is your health?” said we to a bilious looking friend, that we met, after missing him for some time. ‘Twenty-three carats fine.’ ‘Your health?’ said we again. ‘Worth sixteen dollars an ounce.’ We said nothing more and left him, perfectly well convinced that he was troubled with the California fever. ‘What’s the price of United States stock?’ said we to a celebrated Wall Street broker we jostled against a moment after. ‘Thirteen pounds of pure gold in one lump!’ ‘New York Sixes?’ ‘Perfectly inexhaustible, and more mines discovered.’ We trembled for the few shares we possessed, and passed on. Presently we came across a famous divine, who preached every Sunday against the greedy thirst for gold and mammon. ‘That was a noble discourse of yours last Sunday, Doctor. If we remember aright the text was, ‘It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God!” ‘Establish a mint, sir—establish a mint.'

And now John Tyler, walking in the footsteps of Mr. Van Buren, has dared to twain and outrage the feelings of the country, by removing him! Should not the people speak out in relation to this last and grossest act of the treacherous Executive? This removal is calling out justly severe comments from the press. The New York Commercial Sms notices this appropriate act of Tylerism. Alderman Wasson, a full-blooded Loco, takes the office. "What an outrage is this! Solomon Van Rensselaer, the hero of the Miami, under that old fire-eater, Gen. Wayne, and the companion in that battle of William Henry Harrison, of whom, till his death, he was the bosom friend;—Solomon Van Rensselaer, who was shot through the body at the Miami, yet kept his horse, fighting till the blood spurted from his nostrils; Solomon Van Rensselaer, who was riddled like a sieve by the bullets of the enemy in storming the heights of Queenstown; this same Solomon Van Rensselaer turned out of the Albany Post Office by—John Tyler! We blush for the dead while we record the fact. Why should a man love his country, or shed his blood for it, when that country treats its scarred and war-hardened soldiers thus?" The Tribune says Gen. Jackson always refused to have this gallant old patriot turned out throughout his whole eight years.

Taylor's victories have been felt more here, in Europe, even than at home. For two weeks (between the sailings of the steamers) our suspense was great; but, when the news did come, each American strutted out as large as life. The papers which had predicted Taylor's capture as certain were sorely used up. Taylor's despatches have been greatly admired for their terseness, dignity and modesty. The greatest compliment yet paid to Taylor I heard of in London. When the steamer of May 16th brought England the news of Taylor's difficult position, the Duke of Wellington met Mr. , and the subject was introduced. "Why," said the Duke, "does he not do this and that?" (mentioning the steps he thought Taylor ought to take). When the steamer of June 1st brought the news of his victories, Mr. met the Duke again. "Your Grace," said he, "must have been advising Gen. Taylor for you see he has done exactly what you said he ought to do." "So he has," said the Duke, as much gratified as if he had won the victories himself. As I get this from Gen. , to whom Mr. related it, it is undoubtedly authentic; exact in substance, and, I think, also verbatim. In Bordeaux I met a whole-souled American, a 'live Yankee' shipowner, of New York, Mr. Holmes. He is of the stuff which, when abundant in a nation, makes it great.

Whale: We have no change to notice in the market which remains very dull. Whalebone: Sales of 10,000 lbs Polar at 36 cts; and 24,000 lbs do, understood to be at the same price.

Within the limits of our own circulation, not a man enters, for in everything else: the local Press is the true exponent of the popular feeling of the people among whom it circulates, and he who neglects to support it, and fancies he is supporting his party by patronizing distant Journals, might as well keep his money in his pocket, for all the benefit it confers. TREMENDOUS INUNDATION! "Immense Loss of Property" A Loss of Life!! Cleveland, Feb. 23, 3 P.M. Three o'clock this morning, the large reservoir, two miles from Messilon, covering over two thousand acres, and ninety feet above the level of the town, was cut open by some person, flooding the whole town, totally carrying away Reynold's brick warehouse, Skinner's factory, and part of the stone block. Tremont House completely surrounded, and in great danger.

There were about forty passengers on board; those in the cabin at the moment were not injured but of those who were on deck, and about the boiler, none escaped without injury. The wreck floated down to Black Rock pier, and was secured near the ferry landing. Small boats at once went to the rescue of the passengers blown overboard, some of whom got upon cakes of ice, and floated down the river till they were taken off. The number of persons who are known to have lost their lives is twelve—seven passengers and five of the crew. Many are wounded. Of the number lost overboard, nothing certain can be ascertained. The current being swift and filled with floating ice, there is no probability of recovering the bodies, which will find their way over the falls. Gentlemen who witnessed the explosion from the shore, say they saw a number in the water, only five of whom were rescued.  The WiLMOT Proviso in Michigan.—The Michigan House of Representatives, on the 19th ult., passed resolutions sustaining the course of Gen. Cass on the Slavery question. Resolutions offered by Mr. Leech, were rejected, 26 to 37. Thus are repealed the instructions of the last Legislature to Gen. Cass and his associates in the U.S. Senate, to vote in favor of the Wilmot Proviso.  A Question for the Electors.—In the State of Rhode Island, the Locofocos refused to nominate a regular ticket.

M. & F TAYLOR. rpo COUNTRY MERCHANTS AND DEAL ERS GENERALLY. — W. BAYSE is now receiving per schr. Hamilton, from New York, his Spring supply of GROCERIES. His purchases were made with an especial eye to the wants of country merchants. In his opinion nothing is to be gained by them (all things considered) by going further north for articles in his line, and under no circumstances can they be losers by examining this market as they pass through. W. B. considers it his interest to put his customers on the best footing, otherwise he could never calculate on having a permanent trade.

Almost something like unto an Odyssey Journey or," the distance only of about eight hundred miles, coursed by the voyage, during a great portion of the time hailed by vigorous sailing winds, then by squalling gales, which (to one unaccustomed to sea-faring) would seem for a time to put to flight all hopes of a sight of the diggings, and then, Oh! what calms, and lulls, which seemed almost to annihilate the little remaining pa- tience necessary to a continuance of the voy- age; and during the last fifteen days to hear nothing but- "blessed land of gold," "little travel hereafter," "steamship always," "cool drink of Tennessee water," and "Oh! just one morsel of mamma's nice cake and fresh buttermilk," from the mouths of an hundred worn-out wayfarers to the far- famed "diggings."

On returning to St. Mary's, one of them took the next steamer to Savannah, intending, he said, to telegraph his partners in regard to his gold mine, and also to see or write to Mr. Nightingale to notify him. A man remained in an open boat of a schooner, in Cumberland Sound, came for him. Yesterday some negroes, left in charge of the Dungeness place, reported to the collector of this port that a schooner had laid off the place some three days, but was now gone, and they had discovered a complete path, made by tramping backwards and forwards of men's feet, from where the vessel was anchored to this spot on the beach where the men first alluded to had disappeared and here, on the sand hill, they found a very large hole dug, a strong oak stick which had been used as a pry, and a broken spade. The collector of the port and a party of gentlemen went yesterday to the beach, and found everything as the negroes had represented. There was but one digging, and the party had evidently known exactly in what spot to dig in for whatever it was that they sought. The collector has taken measures to ascertain if the headwinds have prevented this schooner from going over the bar at St. Andrews, and if so, will promptly inspect the vessel to ascertain what these singular movements mean. The schooner is the Belknap, Capt. Dexter, of Charleston.

The House then proceeded to a fourth ballot, as follows: Frank B. Combs 25, Brown 17, Buckner 16, Hardy 35. After this ballot, Mr. Buckner, under the rule which had been adopted, was dropped. Mr. Hardy then withdrew his name, leaving but two candidates, Mr. Combs and Mr. Brown. Mr. Combs then rose, and, stating that he had no disposition to run against a Whig, also withdrew his name. Mr. Pratt, (Democrat,) of Scott, was then put in nomination; and Mr. Buckner, of Christian, was again nominated, when a fifth ballot was had as follows: Fifth Ballot: Brown 38, Buckner 28, Pratt 27. No choice being made, a motion to adjourn until next day was made and carried. In the House, on Saturday, Mr. Buckner of Christian, was elected Speaker on the first ballot.

DIED, At his residence, Cherry Point, King George County, Va., on Saturday, 28th October, Mr. JOHN T. FITZHUGH, aged 31 years. He was a truly upright and honest man, a kind and affectionate father and husband, a humane and indulgent master, and a good neighbor. While many friends and relatives deeply deplore his death, to his wife and children the blow will prove severe and irreparable. LADIES' FAIR—The Ladies of the "Providence Sewing Society," Fairfax Court House, Virginia, contemplate holding a Fair, on Monday, the 9th instant, (the first day of the Superior Court,) at the house of Captain R. Wilcoxen, the proceeds of which are to be applied to the Episcopal Church, now erecting near said town. A variety of useful and fancy articles will be offered—refreshments and a warm dinner served up.

CATJg irtTrTfiR —We refer the sugar planters in general to a circular from Mr. John P. Bryan, His newly invented cane-cutter is actually what he claims it to be, a cane cutter in reality. It will be seen by the card of the highly respectable sugar planters below, that it has been tried to their satisfaction. Mr. Josiith Barker is now taking off his crop with this machine, where it can be seen at any time, by those gentlemen who would like to test its qualities personally. Mr. Bryan will also transport it to the plantation of any planter wishing it; he will also expose it at the next fair. This machine will cut as much cane in seven minutes as nineteen hands can do in the space of an hour. Come and see the instrument and judge for yourselves.—[H. R. Gazette of Nov. 4] BRYAN'S PATENT CANE CUTTER. Mr. John P. Bryan has the honor to announce to the sugar planters of Louisiana, that he has invented a Cane Cutter to work by horse power; and that it is now in successful operation at "the" plantation of Mr. Jesiah Barker, at Manchac, in the parish of East Baton Rouge, where all are respectfully invited to witness its powers.

Philip Olivarius, a monk of Orval, in the year 1544, predicted, it is said, all the remarkable events of the present century. The following lines have long been current in Germany: "I would not be a king in 1848; I would not be a soldier in 1849; I would not be a grave digger in 1850; but I would be whatever you please in 1851." Arms in Ireland.— The New York Herald learns from authentic sources that the quantity of ammunition and arms in Ireland, secreted in places known only to the leaders of the people, is immense—sufficient to last for a prolonged warfare, in case the matter be not decided at once when the blow shall have been struck. O'Flaherty, who was announced as the companion of Mr. Meagher in the intended visit of the latter to this country, arrived in the Europa. He is a young gentleman of talent and high position among the leaders of the popular movement in Ireland, from several of whom, including the gallant and eloquent Meagher, he brings the most unreserved letters. Curious Event—It is said that a high legal functionary of New York is about to lead to the altar of political hymen, the daughter of one of the Generals who is a candidate for the office of President of the United States.

Upon win one inane to the next legislature to amend the present Charter of this City. Not knowing what amendments are contemplated, I have thought that this would be a fit opportunity to suggest the propriety of adding a clause in the Charter, authorizing the authorities of the City to establish a Hospital; with power to charge the County for all parishioners taken care of and attended to during their illness, that are not citizens of the Corporation; and to make such other provisions as in their judgment, from time to time, the good of the institution might require. Some twelve years since Mr. John Rex bequeathed the sum of eight thousand dollars, with twenty-five acres of land, having on it two good dwellings, just at the Southwest of the City, for the purpose of building a Hospital for the poor of the town. This money I learn has been, and is still at interest, and must necessarily have accumulated at simple interest alone, to the amount of fourteen thousand seven hundred and sixty dollars. If it is the intention of the authorities ever to carry out the wishes of the deceased, it is now high time that some steps should be taken to practically fulfill them.

Possessed of remarkable vigor and firmness, his opinions were at once clear, firm, and unyielding; while his Christian principles and grace gave a sweetness and charm to his life, that kept him from sourness or bigotry, and made him the friend and favorite of the sincere, the honest, and the good. No man had a higher character in this community for cheerful, solid piety, for honesty, industry, and Christian benevolence, than the subject of this notice. Throughout his long life, his thoughts and affections were "on things above, not on things on the earth" and hence the purity and simplicity of his character, the high estimation in which he was held by neighbors and friends, and his fellow-Christians of all denominations. The secret of all this was, undoubtedly, in the nature and power of his early religion which he chose for his portion in the morning of his life when only eighteen years of age. And for sixty-two years as a member, and for forty years as a ruling elder, in the Newton Presbyterian Church, his walk and conversation have been as becometh the gospel of that Saviour, in whose atoning blood, and through the regeneration and sanctification of whose Spirit he trusted alone for eternal life. The Scotch-Irish family, from which Mr. Torbert was descended, has been identified with the church of their choice in this place, for more than a hundred years.

The Bey sent for the Vizier. "God is great," said he, "Mahomet is his Prophet, I am the friend of justice and of the King of the French. Let the crier shout through the city that I shall send for any Jew seen tomorrow without a cotton cap." Alas! Alas! There are twenty thousand Jews in Tunis, but not one cap-maker. Luckily, however, there was a French vessel in port, with twenty thousand caps, and on the next day each Jew in Tunis wore one of them. The Captain called again to thank the Bey for his kindness. "How can I show you how I honor you?" said he. "Do not thank me yet; you do not know all I intend to do for you." The Bey again sent for the Vizier. "Vizier, Mahomet is God's Prophet, let the crier shout through the city that I shall send for any Jew in whose possession a cotton cap shall be found." The Vizier left, and the French Captain soon followed him. When the latter reached his vessel, all the Jews of the city came on board, and each sorrowfully laid down a cotton cap upon the deck. The Captain was not ungenerous; however, he repurchased the cotton caps, at two sols apiece, and sailed for China. There, however, he was not very lucky; the Chinese would not buy, and he brought them back to Marseilles.

The "pole" being up, the company consisting of just seventy-five, was marched to the woods and Giesy was called on to address the "mighty multitude." Emanuel mounted the rostrum and then, O! shades of hickory, but wasn't Cicero cast in the background. Wonder if the Heavens didn't thunder and the earth rock when that child was born. "Fellow Democrats" said he, "we are now in the midst of one of the brightest and most glorious campaigns the democracy ever witnessed, and we're determined to show the coons before the Ides of November are passed that fur won't sell." He paused, wiped the sweat from his brow, and no doubt expected the air would be rent by the cheers of his audience. But no "murmur of applause was heard," and for about two hours this prodigy continued to fatigue both himself and audience. Mr. Whitman, who had made his appearance during this time, was next called for, and after telling the whigs not to bet on Ohio, he told the democrats that Ford and Weller would have a pretty even race of it and maybe they'd better not bet. He then told them he had been up north lately and had spent a night under the roof of Gen. Cass, "and there," said he, "the old man was in fine health and spirits, calmly and quietly awaiting the hour when he would be called by his countrymen to the highest office in their gift."

Buchanan, Rives, and Simmons delivered their views. THE DON AND THE BRITISH MAIL. From the following, it would appear that a difficulty has lately occurred between the officers having in charge the mail on board of the steam packet "Tay," and the Spanish mail officer at Turks Island. A good deal of feeling appears to have been excited, and it is far from impossible that the occurrence may lead to ill blood between the two governments. The Don does not seem to fancy the running of a British mail through his possessions without his sanction, and were it not for the utterly impoverished and prostrated condition of the Spanish nation, there might be some chance of a flare up.

Lake Erie is connected with Lake St. Clair by the deep and navigable strait of Detroit, 25 miles long. Lake St. Clair is connected with Lake Huron by the navigable strait of St. Clair, 32 miles long. Lake Huron is connected with Lake Michigan by the deep and wide strait of Mackinaw, and with Lake Superior by the strait of St. Mary, 46 miles long. Not including the extensive Bay of Georgian, itself 120 miles long, and averaging 45 miles in width. Yankee Thoughtfulness.—An acquaintance of ours was up in Connecticut one day last winter to visit a friend, who was a manufacturer. The shaft of the manufacturer's water-wheel had been broken that afternoon, under a great accumulation of ice, and he was in great trouble, for he had searched long to find a suitable stick, and knew not where to find another. If he could find one, it would be green, and unfit for immediate use. Very early in the morning, while the day had scarcely dawned, the manufacturer and his guest were at the mill, to see what should be done. A farmer, who lived two or three miles off, was already looking on. As the manufacturer looked painfully at his catastrophe—"Bad break, that, Mr. —said the farmer. "Yes, very bad," said the manufacturer. "Can't mend that stick, can you?" said the farmer.

No changes have been made as yet in the Post Office, War or Navy Departments; those that have taken place having been made in that immense and ramified establishment, the Treasury. Changes will occur in the other Departments soon in all probability. The Cabinet meet daily at the White House at 11 A.M. and separate at 1 P.M. The residue of the day is spent by the heads of the Departments in attention to public business. The President is in good health, I learn, although prevented by the rush of visitors from taking the outdoor exercise he greatly desires. He was observed on the day before yesterday in attendance at the Presbyterian Church on 4 1-2 street, as also was Mr. Webster. His recent circular to the office-holders about election interference is nearly universally well spoken of here, being entirely reasonable and proper. The Secretary of the Treasury and Postmaster General are observed to be greatly thronged with visitors, but are believed to be successfully progressing with the business of their respective Departments. I apprehend the Post Office Department will be relieved from debt by the first of December next. N.Y. Express. A MEAN OBJECTION. The State of Connecticut pays her Governor $1,100 a year. We like that.

Howland & Aspinwall, who "have already embarked so largely in the trade of the northwest coast, have just contracted for the construction of two or more steamships, of large dimensions, to be used in the California trade, In connection with the Oregon, Panama, Tennessee, and Carolina, &c. They will register about 1800 tons, and will rank in the same class with the Empire City, of Howard & Son's line. One steamer. The other about the same. They will be commenced forthwith, and with the design of having them completed in nine months. We understand that Messrs. Smith & Dimon and Wm. H. Webb are the contractors. " Messrs. Perrine, Patterson and St;ck, of Williamsburgh, have commenced building a large steam propeller, to run on the Pacific, in a line to be formed, in part, of the steamers Commodore Preble and Gen. Warren one of which sailed some time since for her destination, and the other will probably clear today. This vessel will be 215 feet long, 39 broad and 18 deep; she will be completed in about three months. The same builders have a large steamship now in progress, for Messrs. Howard & Son's new Pacific line. She is to be put together with extraordinary strength, being more easily moulded than any steamer yet built. She is also to be coppered throughout, and fastened by diagonal iron bracing. She is built uniform.

Age should not destroy the child. The child is the original, and man is merely a superstructure upon the boy. It is an unfortunate sign for a man's happiness, when he has forgotten his boyish feelings. And yet, how rarely we find it otherwise? Parents, in exercising authority over their children, forget that they were young. They expect those whose hearts throb with the warmth and disinterestedness of youth, to think and act with the same cold, calculating, and selfish feelings which predominate in their breasts. And these feelings are the result of experience, they say! Verily, it is dearly bought, at the sacrifice of all the finer sensibilities and generous impulses of the human heart. The Most Solemn. The London Court Journal makes the following remarks on Tom Thumb, the Dwarf: Strange are the honors accorded to this little being whose only recommendation was that he amused those who treated him so ill. We can conceive that idlers should repair to the place where this little deformity is exhibited to while away a half hour in satisfaction of a frivolous curiosity; but when we hear the tales related by the man who gains his livelihood by exhibiting this libel on humanity, of the reception given to General Tom Thumb by great European potentates, we are struck with astonishment at reflecting on this strange freak of fortune.

Captain Larner, of the bark Xl-lvr, which arrived yesterday from Hiraina, reports that the ship Alia from New York, bound to Vera Cruz, with troops, was lying on the shore 60 miles south, and that a few days previously four officers of the U.S. army went ashore for the purpose of purchasing a few small stores; that they were allowed to pass by the sentinels and other city officers, and that after they had obtained what they wanted, and returning to the ship they were arrested.

The two Governments having already agreed, through their respective organs, on the terms of annexation, I would recommend their adaption by Congress in the form of a joint resolution, or act, to be perfected and made binding on the two countries, when adopted in like manner by the Government of Texas. In order that the subject may be fully presented in all its bearings, the correspondence which has taken place, in reference to it, since the adjournment of Congress, between the United States, Texas, and Mexico, is herewith transmitted. The amendments proposed by the Senate to the Convention concluded between the United States and Mexico on the 8th of November, 1843, have been transmitted through our Minister, for the concurrence of the Mexican Government; but, although urged thereto, no action has yet been had on the subject; nor has any answer been given which would authorize a favorable conclusion in the future.
