From a careful enquiry at the different warehouses, we have obtained the amount of last year's crop shipped from this place to be 175,000 bushels.  On Tuesday last, the day on which we made inquiries of the receipts at the different warehouses, were 7000 bushels. This however, is hardly an average day's receipts, although 10,000 bushels having been frequently received since the new crop began to come in.  If our plank road could be extended a distance compatible with the wants of the farming community, before the fall rains set in, our receipts could not average less than 20,000 bushels per day for some months, for as yet, not one eighth of the Farmers have threshed their wheat or begun to think of a market. A Curiosity in Commercial Transactions. A letter from Liverpool, dated Nov. 10th, received in this city by the last steamer, has been shown to us, which mentions the extraordinary fact of the sale of a lot of cotton thirty-two years old, which has been lying all this time, in a warehouse in that city. The letter says: 400 bales of Cotton are now on sale, the remainder of a lot of 600 bales purchased in 1817. 100 bales were sold yesterday at 6 5-8d. They originally cost 17d per pound, and it is estimated that the loss from beginning to end is about 10,000 or say equal here to about 200,000. This act shows the extraordinary progress of the cotton trade during the past thirty or forty years: the price of the staple in 1817, having been 17d per pound, while now a tremendous excitement is created if the price reaches 6 5-8 or 6 3-4d, per pound. Mr. President and Members of the Senate, and of the House of Delegates: Prior to the organization of the State Government, it has been customary for the Executive unit, annually, to the Legislature, a report, detailing the manner in which the government had been conducted since the adjournment of the preceding Legislature, and indicating the adoption of such measures as union would best conserve the honor and the interest of the people. The time set apart for discharge of duties has been occupied with domestic affairs, and this Message will be consequently less extensive than will be satisfactory to you, as to myself. Gentlemen, our country an enduring union is the surest criterion of national greatness. Mindful of the station I occupy, I am anxious to continue and adopt such measures, as in my judgment will tend to promote the public credit, and prosperity of our common constituency, and maintain the financial condition of the State well the same.

Whereas there is now pending a suit in Equity in the Supreme Court of the United States in favor of the State of Rhode Island against the State of Massachusetts, wherein Rhode Island claims of Massachusetts jurisdiction over a certain territory about four miles wide and about twenty miles long; and whereas it has been suggested with a view it is feared to alarm the owners and occupants of said territory, who own and occupy said territory under titles granted originally by said State of Massachusetts, that in case the said State of Rhode Island succeeds in said suit against said State of Massachusetts, she will be entitled to disturb said owners and occupants under said titles - that they do not consider that said State of Rhode Island has any right in law or in equity, to disturb any of said owners or occupants in the enjoyment of their said titles under said State of Massachusetts, or in any way or manner to interfere in relation to any private title to said territory, and in order to quiet the apprehensions of said owners and occupants: Resolved, That the Committee upon the Judiciary, be instructed to bring in a Bill at the next session of this Assembly, remising and releasing to said owners and occupants, all right, title and interest which said State of Rhode Island may have, or be supposed to have, in and to any and all said lands, tenements and hereditaments, and confirming said owners and occupants in their present titles. True copy— witness, Henry Bowen, Sec’ry.

The Tribune, from which these particulars are collected, also announces the presence of Mr. Parrott, U.S. consul at Mazatlan, at the Astor House in New York. Mr. Parrott left Mazatlan on the 10th April, after his return from an exploration of the gold region. He reached Mexico on the 9th of May, Vera Cruz on the 17th, and Mobile on the 21st. He reports having met companies continually during his overland journey. The road to the Pacific is lined with them, and they are all, so far as he saw, doing well. Capt. Hutton's company sailed from San Bias in the Cayuga, April 13. Capt. Earle's was at Guadalajara on the 19th. There were four or five vessels at Mazatlan, and two or three at San Bias, for California. The price of passage varied from $50 to $250, according to the accommodations. Mr. P. tells a story of a cabin boy who deserted one of Hargous' ships at Vera Cruz and walked the whole way to San Bias, where he arrived safe and sound on his way to California. The Tribune is indebted to the courtesy of Mr. Parrott for much new and interesting information concerning the general wealth of California. His visit to the placers was undertaken for the purpose of informing himself on these points, and his revelations are even more astonishing than any which have previously reached us.

The previous threats, the way laying of himself and Connell on the streets, the combination of three men attacking him, their renewing of weapons at the neighboring grocery are facts which, if correctly reported to us, indicate plainly the existence of a plan to assassinate. There was premeditation, deliberation, passion and perversity in making the attack upon him; and these things vastly account for the immense indignation excited in his hundreds of friends on last Saturday. Ever fearless and unhesitating in the performance of what he believed to be his duty, and believing the attempt to gain possession of the bank to be a design to defraud the creditors and a portion of the stockholders, he stoutly resisted it on their behalf. For years, this bank has embroiled our citizens, and injured the character of Memphis abroad; and now, it seems that an organized system of ruffianism and violence is established, to deter attorneys from the performance of their duty, and overawe public sentiment. It will have but a sorry chance for success. Another extract from the same: Gen. Coe was much easier at dark yesterday evening, although his condition is still very critical. The hundreds of friends whom he has made by his bold, frank, chivalrous character, most ardently hope that his recovery will disappoint the malice of assassins. CANDIDATES.

It will be seen that he refers to two commissions, and long and protracted negotiations which had escaped the observation of the Union. He says, "by the 12th article of the treaty of Utrecht, his most Christian majesty ceded to the Queen of Great Britain all Nova Scotia, or Acadie, with its ancient boundaries. A great part of the ceded territory was in the possession of the Indians, and the extent of the cession could not be adjusted by the commissioners to whom it was referred. The treaty of Aix la Chapelle, which was made on the principle of the status ante bellum, did not remove this subject of controversy. Commissioners for its adjustment were appointed, whose very able, elaborate, though unsuccessful arguments in favor of the title of their respective sovereigns show how entirely each relied on the title given by discovery, to lands remaining in possession of the Indians. After the termination of this fruitless discussion, the subject was transferred to Europe, and taken up by the cabinets of Versailles and London. This controversy embraced not only the boundaries of New England, Nova Scotia, and that part of Canada which adjoined those colonies, but also our whole western country. France contended not only that the St. Lawrence was to be considered as the centre of Canada, but that the Ohio was within that colony.

They landed at Plymouth on the 22nd day of December, 1620. "and here hung in number about twenty, they rendezvous this evening, but a storm rising, it blows and rains hard all night - continues so tempestuous for two days that they cannot get on board, and have nothing to shelter them." Their Pastor, Mr. Robinson, did not accompany them, but remained with the greater number of his flock in Holland, intending to come, yet ever unable to accomplish his desire. He is here represented in an attitude of prayer, at their final leave-taking. Robinson is described as “a man of learned, polished, and modest spirit, pious and studious of the truth, largely accomplished with gifts and qualifications suitable to a shepherd over his flock of Christ.” He died in Leyden, 1625, in the 50th year of his age. His widow and children came over to Plymouth colony, & his son Isaac lived to the age of ninety, who is mentioned by Prince as “a venerable man.” In vain we seek to Governor Carver is described as a man of great prudence, integrity, and firmness of mind. He had a good estate in England, which he spent in the emigration to Holland and America. He was taken sick in the field, while they were engaged in their planting, and died in the month of April, 1621. Many able pens have been employed in portraying his character.

Having been well received at Tidore, they loaded spices there, and without effecting any permanent settlement, one of the ships, La Trinidad, took her departure eastwards, towards Panama, and the other, as we have already seen, westwards, by the Cape of Good Hope. But the flattering accounts received induced the Emperor to persevere. In 1524, a new expedition was fitted out under Estevan Gomez, but was dispersed by bad weather. In 1525, Father Loyasa was dispatched from Corunna with seven ships and 450 picked men. Loyasa did not live to reach his place of destination; after many difficulties and hopes, the fleet reached Tidore on the 41st December, 1526, very opportunely to assist the chief of that Island, against whom the Portuguese had declared war, for his previous kind reception of the Spaniards. Several partial actions were fought with the Portuguese, but finding their number reduced to 120 men, the Spaniards built a fort and intrenched themselves. They were relieved in 1526 by Don Alvaro de Saavedra, Commanding a new expedition of three ships built and equipped on the west coast of Mexico, by the Viceroy, acting under orders from Spain. Polk's father was a tory has been proven false, the federal press, ever on the scent of calumny and detraction, have changed the person, and now charge his grandfather with the crime that they charged his father. This second coinage is equally false as the first. 'I say, the whole Polk family, North Carolina did not contain a purer set of patriots during the days of the Revolution. Joe Smith.—The Mormon Prophet is said to have made use of the following language recently to an audience of 2,000 Mormons. After taking a glass of water, he said: "May all the nigger drivers, kidnappers and mobbers of Missouri be placed in a stone canoe with an iron paddle, the canoe placed in the middle of the sea, a shark swallow the canoe, the devil swallow the shark, the devil sunk in the northwest corner of hell, the door locked, the key lost, and a blind man hunting for it."

And nought to thee is human pride. The rich and poor lie side by side, In thine overshadowing gloom. And there the out-cast wanderer sleeps, Where princely treasures, heaps on heaps, Adorn this coral tomb. Thou hast a mournful voice to me, Thou reckless, ever murmuring sea! A sad funereal wail, As if thy strange, repentant waves, Were sighing o'er the hidden graves, Of those, whom they have slain. Roll on, roll on, thou mighty deep! I hear the wrathful tempest sweep, I hear thy billows moan. And if perchance my grave may be Within thy bosom—singing o'er me That mournful dirge-like tone. N. G. R. Foreign Items. A Funny Railroad Accident. On Saturday evening as the last train of cars from Lowell was approaching this city two of the hindermost cars accidentally parted from the train in Medford, about five miles distant. The occurrence was not discovered, however, until after the conductor had stopped in the city and returned to the depot, when he was astonished to find that two of his cars, containing some 15 passengers each were “among the missing". He instantly dispatched a locomotive on the return track, and the lost cars, with their population, were brought into the city after a detention of about an hour and a half by this both amusing and vexatious oversight. — Boston Journal. News of the Day. “To point the very age and body of the times." A telegraphic dispatch from Syracuse, New York, states that the regular “Democratic" State convention met on Monday at noon. A committee was appointed to nominate the per- manent officers of the convention. They re- ported in favor of Hon. S. Beardsley of Oneida as president, together with the usual number of vice presidents and secretaries. The report was unanimously adopted. On motion, the conven- tion proceeded to ballot for candidates for gov- ernor, lieutenant governor, and canal commis- sioners. Finally, the nominations of Chancel- lor Walworth for governor, and Charles O'Con- nor for lieutenant governor, were made unani- mously. For canal commissioners.

I was informed that some of the first received were from the remote State of Wisconsin, and we were permitted to peruse letters from the agents at California and New Mexico, who make earnest promises of the speedy and judicious execution of the trusts committed to them. In this office there are forty persons employed, and, novel as is the duty before them, we doubt that any department of the Government can exhibit a more systematic routine of operations. The work of analyzing and compiling for publication the information contained in the returns is progressing well. The first object to be effected is the ascertainment of the population, the second the arrangement of the population into the respective classes, as to sex, age, color, place of nativity, etc. The social statistics is also a feature to be elicited at an early day, comprising as it does an account of the aggregate wealth, educational, religious and other institutions, with the particulars of pauperism, crime and wages—subjects for the philanthropist, moralist, and Christian, to dwell upon. The name of every person who died within the year ending June 1, 1850, is given, with the age, color, sex, occupation, condition, place of birth, time afflicted, and name of disease.

1 also hereby make known and give notice that the following act of Assembly was passed by the general Assembly of the Commonwealth on the 27th day of February, A. D. 1819, to wit: "An Act relative to voting at elections in the counties of Adams, Dauphin, York, Lancaster, Franklin, Cumberland, Bradford, Centre, Greene, and Erie. Section 1. Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania in General Assembly met, and it is hereby enacted by the authority of the same, That it shall be lawful for the qualified voters of the counties of Adams, Dauphin, Lancaster, York, Franklin, Cumberland, Bradford, Centre, Greene, and Erie, from and after the passage of this act, to vote for all the candidates for the various offices to be filled at any election on one slip or ticket: Provided, That the office for which every candidate is voted for, shall be designated, as required by the existing laws of this Commonwealth. Section 2. That any fraud committed by any person voting in the manner above prescribed, shall be punished as similar frauds are directed to be punished by the existing laws of this Commonwealth.

The local laws have now a majority of two thirds in both Houses—so that they have it in their power to remove all the abuses of which they have for many years complained, and to bring about all their promised reforms. So that we shall henceforth hear no more complaints about monopolies of any kind—no more about the banks, college faction, imprisonment for debt, downtrodden rights and privileges of working men—no more about sumptuary or temperance laws—no more oppressive registry laws—no more voting by professors or students in colleges, or by the Presbyterian clergy. The people will watch the proceedings of the Legislature very closely, and compare the professions and practices of those who have all power to fulfill their promises—and in the end will weigh the men and the measures in the balances and award the former their deserts, and this is all the whigs desire.—N. H. Pal., April 23. From Mexico.—The New Orleans papers of the 19th contain later dates from Mexico, letters from Vera Cruz state that it was generally believed Santa Anna had succeeded in negotiating a loan of $7,000,000 with England on a pledge with the peninsula of California. It is quite improbable, however, that this should be correct. Business was very dull in Vera Cruz. The yellow fever was carrying off several persons daily. Rumors were afloat that Santa Anna intended soon to declare himself Emperor.

Lewis Carusi has the honor to inform the citizens of Washington and vicinity, as also his former pupils, that his dancing academy will be re-opened on Tuesday, 25th October. L. C. will instruct in waltzing, galopade, Polka, the L'edua Waltz, and all the other fashionable dances as practiced at the assemblies, in a style peculiar to the society of Washington, and to the most select circles in the United States. L. C. also announces that his "soiree dansante" will take place as soon as the subscription list is filled, of which a due notice will be given. Private lessons to classes, single persons, academies, at individual residences, and at the saloon. Classes for young ladies on Tuesdays and Thursdays from 3 till 5 o'clock, p. m. For youths, same days from 5 till 7 p. m. For young gentlemen, same days from 7 till 9 p. m. The saloon will be rented for cotillion parties, concerts, balls, exhibitions, lectures, etc., on the most liberal and pleasing terms. Oct. 4—3taw2w NEW AND ELEGANT SILKS—The subscribers have this day received a large invoice of dress silks, which have been selected with great care from the New York markets within the last week. They name: 10 pieces very rich changeable Brocade silks, 10 do. do. Ottoman satins, 15 do. do. plaid and striped Glace silks, and 5 do.
